Running compensation handlers continue to completion regardless of any interrupting event in the surrounding scope of the compensation throw event that invoked them at the start

c.text: Incorrect. The Interrupting compensation handlers section lists several in-process interruption sources, including terminate end events and interrupting event subprocesses, not only external cancellation of the instance.

a.text: Correct. The page states a compensation handler is terminated when the invoking compensation throw event is interrupted, for example by a terminate end event, an interrupting event subprocess, or interruption of the embedded subprocess containing the throw event.

d.text: Incorrect. The page explicitly states that compensation handlers can be interrupted by these scope-level events; they do not always run to completion under all circumstances in the surrounding scope.

b.text: Incorrect. Non-interrupting event subprocesses do not interrupt the throwing scope; the documented interruption sources are interrupting events such as a terminate end or an interrupting event subprocess in scope.

explanation: Compensation handlers stop when the compensation throw event that invoked them is interrupted. This happens on process cancellation, when a terminate end event is entered, when an interrupting event subprocess is triggered, or when an embedded subprocess containing the throw event is interrupted.

scenario: Apex Bank models a fraud-review embedded subprocess in Camunda 8.8 that groups 5 tasks inside a parent process. The team must respect the start event rules of the embedded subprocess to keep the model deployable on the engine.
question: Which start event constraint applies to an embedded subprocess?

options.text: One signal start event activates the subprocess and a none start event is required only when boundary events are attached to it
One timer start event marks the subprocess entry and a none start event can be added when the subprocess is invoked manually
Exactly one none start event is required and other start event types are not allowed inside the embedded subprocess at all
One message start event is required and additional none start events may be added for alternative entry points to the subprocess

d.text: Incorrect. An embedded subprocess does not accept a message start event; the only valid start event inside it is a single none start event.

c.text: Correct. An embedded subprocess must have exactly one none start event at its entry, and other start event types are not allowed inside it.

b.text: Incorrect. Timer start events are not permitted inside an embedded subprocess; the only allowed start event is a single none start event.

a.text: Incorrect. Signal start events are not permitted inside an embedded subprocess; the only allowed start event is a single none start event, regardless of boundary events.

explanation: An embedded subprocess must have exactly one none start event at its entry. Other start event types such as message, timer, or signal are not allowed in the subprocess body, which keeps the activation semantics aligned with the parent scope.

scenario: Catalyst Bank models a Camunda 8.8 onboarding process and groups the KYC steps into an embedded subprocess that runs across 12000 customers per month. The reviewer asks for the start event configuration of the embedded subprocess to comply with BPMN execution rules in Camunda 8.8.
question: Which start event configuration is valid inside the embedded subprocess in this Camunda 8.8 model?

options.text: Exactly one timer start event, optionally followed by additional timer start events used as alternative entry points to the embedded subprocess in the same diagram
Exactly one none start event, with no other start events of any type defined inside the embedded subprocess in this Camunda 8.8 diagram of the parent
One none start event together with one signal start event used to re-enter the subprocess after a broadcast signal from a different process instance in the cluster
One none start event together with one message start event so the subprocess can be entered either directly or by a correlated message in the same scope

a.text: Incorrect. The embedded subprocesses page states that an embedded subprocess must have exactly one start event, and that start event is required to be a none start event in the diagram.

d.text: Incorrect. Only one start event is allowed in an embedded subprocess, and it must be of type none; message start events are not permitted inside an embedded subprocess.

b.text: Correct. The embedded subprocesses page states that an embedded subprocess must have exactly one none start event, and that other start events are explicitly not allowed inside the embedded subprocess.

c.text: Incorrect. Additional start events are not allowed inside an embedded subprocess; the rule is exactly one start event, and it must be of type none in the diagram.

explanation: Embedded subprocesses are constrained to a single none start event. When the subprocess is entered, that start event activates and the subprocess stays active until its last contained element completes, at which point the outgoing sequence flow is taken.

scenario: Pinnacle Healthcare models a Camunda 8.8 admissions process with one subprocess shape that conceals nested logic for 9000 customers per month. The team wants the diagram to stay readable by hiding internal steps inside one activity shape while keeping the same diagram file.
question: Which statement about a collapsed embedded subprocess in this Camunda 8.8 model is supported by the documentation?

options.text: A collapsed embedded subprocess can reuse a different already deployed process definition through the drill-down link in Modeler from inside the parent diagram of the process
A collapsed embedded subprocess can be created by collapsing an existing expanded embedded subprocess in the same Camunda Modeler diagram of the parent process file
A collapsed embedded subprocess hides its internal details for display only, and the drill-down link in Modeler only opens the same embedded subprocess within the diagram
A collapsed embedded subprocess is fully supported by Optimize, including drill-down into the nested elements of the subprocess from the Optimize UI for the parent process

a.text: Incorrect. The Collapsed subprocesses section states the drill-down link only opens the embedded subprocess within the same diagram; to reference another process, use a call activity instead.

d.text: Incorrect. The page contains a caution that collapsed subprocesses are currently only partially supported by Optimize, and drill-down into them in Optimize is not possible from the UI.

b.text: Incorrect. The page states you cannot collapse an existing expanded subprocess in your model; the collapsed or expanded form is chosen when the subprocess is added to the diagram.

c.text: Correct. The Collapsed subprocesses section states they serve purely display purposes, hiding internal details inside the activity shape, and the drill-down link only opens the same embedded subprocess within the diagram.

explanation: Collapsed embedded subprocesses are a display-only convenience in Modeler. They cannot be created from an already expanded subprocess, they are only partially supported by Optimize, and the drill-down link does not reference a different process. Use a call activity for reuse.

scenario: Catalyst Bank handles 4000 requests per hour through a payment process in Camunda 8.8. A developer wants the catch error boundary event to use a FEEL expression so the errorCode is selected at runtime based on the current request type for routing handlers.
question: Which value form does the errorCode of an error catch event accept in Camunda 8.8?

options.text: A static value only; FEEL expressions are accepted on error throw events but not on the catch event errorCode at any time
A FEEL expression that is evaluated when the throwing job fails and then matched against the active error reference of the scope
A static value or a FEEL expression; the engine resolves whichever form is configured on the catch event reference at runtime
A FEEL expression that returns the errorCode when the catch event is activated and is used to match the thrown error from the scope

b.text: Incorrect. Error catch events do not evaluate any FEEL expression for the errorCode; the matching uses a static value defined on the referenced error in the model.

d.text: Incorrect. The errorCode on a catch event is never derived by evaluating an expression at activation time; only a static value is accepted on catch events.

c.text: Incorrect. Catch events accept only a static errorCode value; expression-based errorCodes are valid solely on error throw events in Camunda 8.8.

a.text: Correct. The errorCode of an error catch event must be a static value; FEEL expressions for the errorCode are accepted only on error throw events in Camunda 8.8.

explanation: In Camunda 8.8, the errorCode of an error catch event must be defined as a static value. Expression-based errorCodes are accepted only on error throw events, where the expression is evaluated when the event is reached and used to throw the error.

scenario: Lattice Insurance reviews a Camunda 8.8 process where a credit scoring service becomes unavailable for 5 minutes at a time. The architect must decide which class of reactions the BPMN model should encode with explicit error events rather than handling generically.
question: Which classification of reactions does Camunda recommend modeling with explicit error events in the process?

options.text: Business reactions are modeled with error events in the process while technical reactions are handled generically by retries or incidents
Only reactions whose root cause is a business rule failure are modeled; reactions to any technical exception stay outside the BPMN flow always
Technical reactions such as automatic retries are modeled with error events while business reactions are left to incidents and operators
Both business and technical reactions are modeled with error events to keep the visual model complete for business personnel reviewing it

c.text: Incorrect. Retries are not modeled with error events; the Camunda 8.8 guidance is the opposite, with technical reactions handled generically by retries or incidents rather than visual elements.

a.text: Correct. Business reactions belong in the process model and are expressed with error events, while technical reactions such as retries or incidents are handled generically outside the visual model.

d.text: Incorrect. Modeling every technical reaction with error events bloats the visual model and confuses business personnel, which Camunda 8.8 explicitly discourages.

b.text: Incorrect. The classification turns on the reaction rather than the root cause; even a technical issue can warrant a business reaction modeled with error events.

explanation: Camunda 8.8 recommends classifying error handling by the reaction rather than the root cause. Business reactions are modeled in the process with error events, while technical reactions like automatic retries and incidents are handled generically outside the visual model to keep diagrams clear.

scenario: Lumen SaaS uses a Camunda 8.8 onboarding process where the next step depends on whichever event arrives first for 4000 customers per day. The team adds an event-based gateway after a user action so the flow waits for either a message correlation or a timer in the same scope.
question: Which behaviour does the event-based gateway exhibit when the process instance waits at it in Camunda 8.8?

options.text: The gateway must have exactly one outgoing sequence flow connected to one intermediate catch event of type timer that decides whether the process continues or terminates the scope
The gateway invokes every intermediate catch event after it in parallel and merges all received events at a downstream parallel join gateway in the same flow scope of the model
The gateway waits until one of the connected intermediate catch events is triggered, then takes the outgoing sequence flow of that event so no other events of the gateway can be triggered afterward
The gateway evaluates a conditionExpression on each outgoing sequence flow and takes the first one whose condition evaluates to true at the time of activation in the same scope

d.text: Incorrect. The event-based gateway routes by which event triggers first, not by evaluating sequence-flow conditionExpressions; the gateway has no condition expressions on its outgoing flows in this model.

b.text: Incorrect. The gateway does not run all events in parallel; it waits for the first triggered event and then disables the others, so there is no parallel join behind it in the diagram of the model.

c.text: Correct. The event-based gateways page states the process instance waits until one of the events is triggered, then takes that event's outgoing sequence flow, and no other events of the gateway can be triggered afterward.

a.text: Incorrect. The page states an event-based gateway must have at least two outgoing sequence flows, each connected to an intermediate catch event of type timer, message, or signal in the same scope.

explanation: An event-based gateway has at least two outgoing flows, each ending in a timer, message, or signal intermediate catch event. The process instance waits at the gateway; the first event to trigger wins, its outgoing flow is taken, and the others become inactive.

scenario: Nimbus SaaS reviews the BPMN XML of a Camunda 8.8 payment confirmation flow that splits 1800 transactions per hour into a message-driven branch and a 1 hour timer branch. The reviewer focuses on which intermediate catch event types are wired to the event-based gateway in the XML.
question: Which intermediate catch event types are wired to an event-based gateway in the BPMN XML examples for Camunda 8.8?

options.text: bpmn:intermediateCatchEvent elements with conditionEventDefinition, dataInputEventDefinition, or terminateEventDefinition for routing the gateway by data and termination signals
bpmn:intermediateCatchEvent elements with linkEventDefinition, messageEventDefinition, or terminateEventDefinition for routing the gateway by link relationships and message correlation
bpmn:intermediateCatchEvent elements with messageEventDefinition, timerEventDefinition, or signalEventDefinition for routing the gateway by external messages, timers, and signals
bpmn:intermediateCatchEvent elements with errorEventDefinition, escalationEventDefinition, or compensationEventDefinition for routing the gateway by exceptions and compensation events

a.text: Incorrect. The XML examples wire timer, message, and signal intermediate catch events to the gateway; conditional and data-driven event definitions are not listed in the Additional resources section.

d.text: Incorrect. Error, escalation, and compensation events are not supported as event-based gateway branches; the documented event definitions on the gateway are timer, message, and signal.

b.text: Incorrect. Link event definitions are not used with the event-based gateway in this section; the documented set is timer, message, and signal intermediate catch events on the outgoing flows.

c.text: Correct. The Additional resources XML wires intermediate catch events with messageEventDefinition, timerEventDefinition (with timeDuration), and signalEventDefinition as branches of the event-based gateway in the model.

explanation: Each outgoing sequence flow of an event-based gateway connects to an intermediate catch event of type timer, message, or signal. The XML example shows a messageEventDefinition, a timerEventDefinition with timeDuration PT1H, and a signalEventDefinition wired to the gateway.

scenario: Kestrel Bank models a Camunda 8.8 mortgage application process where a global event subprocess must react to multiple incoming reminders without terminating the main flow, handling 900 loans per day across 3 tenants in the same parent process model.
question: Which event subprocess configuration in Camunda 8.8 allows the subprocess to be triggered multiple times without terminating the containing scope?

options.text: A non-interrupting event subprocess that starts with an error start event can be triggered any number of times while the containing scope keeps running normally in the same instance
An interrupting event subprocess can be re-triggered after each completion as long as no other event subprocess of the same scope has been triggered earlier in the same instance
A non-interrupting event subprocess in the same scope can be triggered multiple times during the lifetime of its containing scope without terminating active instances of that scope
An interrupting event subprocess with a timer start event can fire multiple times per process instance, with the containing scope restored each time the subprocess completes its work

c.text: Correct. The event subprocesses page states that a non-interrupting event subprocess can be triggered multiple times while its containing scope remains active, contrasting with interrupting subprocesses which can be triggered only once.

b.text: Incorrect. An interrupting event subprocess can be triggered only once and terminates active instances of its containing scope, so it cannot be re-triggered later in the same parent process instance.

a.text: Incorrect. The page lists error as one allowed start event type but does not change interrupting semantics; only non-interrupting event subprocesses can fire multiple times in the same containing scope.

d.text: Incorrect. Interrupting event subprocesses cannot fire multiple times; the containing scope is terminated, not restored, when an interrupting event subprocess is triggered in the same instance.

explanation: Event subprocesses behave like boundary events but live inside the scope. Their start event is timer, message, error, signal, or escalation. Non-interrupting variants can be triggered multiple times while the containing scope stays active; interrupting variants run once and terminate that scope.

scenario: Apex Retail routes 25000 orders per day through Camunda 8.8. An exclusive gateway directs each order to a domestic or express handler based on conditionExpressions. During a configuration change, both outgoing conditionExpressions evaluate to false for some orders and no default flow is configured on that gateway.
question: What does the engine do at the exclusive gateway when none of the outgoing conditionExpressions evaluate to true and no default flow is defined?

options.text: The process instance ends silently at the exclusive gateway and the parent scope completes without an error indication for the operator.
The process instance proceeds along the first outgoing sequence flow defined in the BPMN XML, ignoring the unsatisfied conditions.
The exclusive gateway forks the unsatisfied flows into a single concurrent token that continues into the next downstream activity.
An incident is created at the exclusive gateway because no outgoing sequence flow can be taken and no default flow exists to absorb the token.

c.text: Incorrect. An exclusive gateway selects exactly one outgoing flow when entered; it never forks tokens onto multiple branches, which is the role of an inclusive or parallel gateway.

b.text: Incorrect. The engine selects the first flow whose conditionExpression is fulfilled, not simply the first flow in XML order; with all conditions false there is no flow to take and no fall-through behaviour.

a.text: Incorrect. The engine does not silently end the process at a stuck exclusive gateway; the documented behaviour is to raise an incident so the situation can be investigated and resolved.

d.text: Correct. The documentation states that when no conditionExpression is fulfilled and the gateway has no default flow, the engine creates an incident at the exclusive gateway because no outgoing path can be taken.

explanation: Exclusive gateways select the first outgoing flow whose conditionExpression is fulfilled, falling back to the default flow when no condition matches. If there is no default flow and no condition is fulfilled, the engine raises an incident at the gateway.

scenario: Meridian Marketplace fulfils 50000 orders per day in Camunda 8.8. After a diverging inclusive gateway splits each order into the selected fulfilment paths, a converging inclusive gateway downstream must join those active branches before the invoicing step runs for the same order.
question: Which condition does the documentation list for a converging inclusive gateway to complete and continue past the join?

options.text: All incoming sequence flows have been taken at least once, or no path exists from any active flow node to the gateway excluding paths already taken.
All incoming sequence flows have been taken exactly once and every other token in the process instance has reached an intermediate wait state.
The default outgoing flow has been taken at least once, signalling that all other diverging branches have completed their work for the instance.
Any single incoming sequence flow has been taken, which immediately passes a token onward like an exclusive gateway join would do for tokens.

a.text: Correct. The documentation states that a converging inclusive gateway completes when all incoming sequence flows have been taken at least once, or when no path exists from any active flow node to the gateway excluding the incoming paths already taken.

b.text: Incorrect. The documented condition is at least once, not exactly once; and there is no rule requiring every other token in the process instance to be at an intermediate wait state for the join to complete.

d.text: Incorrect. A converging inclusive gateway is not pass-through after a single incoming flow; it waits for all selected paths, which is precisely what distinguishes it from an exclusive joining gateway.

c.text: Incorrect. The default flow is a property of the diverging side of an inclusive gateway and is taken when no condition is fulfilled; it has no role in the completion condition of the converging gateway.

explanation: A converging inclusive gateway completes either when all incoming sequence flows have been taken at least once, or when no path remains from any active flow node to the gateway excluding incoming paths already taken.

scenario: Vertex Foods, a meal-kit retailer, processes 6000 orders per day in Camunda 8.8. A diverging inclusive gateway sends each order into pasta, steak, or salad preparation flows based on a courses variable, with salad chosen as the fallback path taken when no other course is selected by the customer.
question: Which BPMN XML attribute on the inclusiveGateway element identifies the outgoing sequence flow that is taken when no condition is fulfilled?

options.text: The defaultFlow attribute on the sequence flow marks it as the fallback path of the gateway when no other outgoing condition matches.
The default attribute on the inclusiveGateway element references the id of the outgoing sequence flow used when no condition is fulfilled.
The conditionExpression child on the sequence flow with no name is interpreted as the default outgoing sequence flow of the gateway.
The isDefault attribute on the conditionExpression element marks the outgoing sequence flow as the default flow of the inclusive gateway.

c.text: Incorrect. A sequence flow without a conditionExpression is eligible to be the default outgoing flow, but it is the default attribute on the gateway that actually points to it; having no condition is not sufficient on its own.

b.text: Correct. The documented XML places a default attribute on the bpmn:inclusiveGateway element, and that attribute carries the id of the outgoing sequence flow that is taken when no other condition is fulfilled.

a.text: Incorrect. The documented XML does not use a defaultFlow attribute on the sequence flow; the default outgoing flow is declared on the gateway element itself through its default attribute.

d.text: Incorrect. The documented XML does not use an isDefault attribute on a conditionExpression; the default outgoing flow is selected by the default attribute on the inclusiveGateway element.

explanation: The documented XML shows that a diverging inclusive gateway points to its default outgoing sequence flow through the default attribute on bpmn:inclusiveGateway, which carries the id of the chosen fallback sequence flow.

scenario: Kestrel Marketplace ships 4500 orders per day through a Camunda 8.8 inclusive gateway with three outgoing flows for pasta, steak, and salad. The XML must declare a default outgoing sequence flow so an instance never raises an incident when no condition matches.
question: How is the default outgoing flow declared in the XML for an inclusive gateway?

options.text: The bpmn:sequenceFlow chosen as default carries an extensionElement that flags it as default so the engine picks it instead of raising an incident later
The bpmn:inclusiveGateway carries a default attribute whose value is the id of the outgoing sequence flow taken when no outgoing condition is true
The bpmn:inclusiveGateway carries a defaultFlow child element whose value is the name of the outgoing flow taken when every condition evaluates to false
The bpmn:inclusiveGateway carries a fallbackRef attribute whose value is the id of a catching event that fires when each outgoing condition evaluates to false

c.text: Incorrect. The documented XML uses a default attribute on the gateway element, not a defaultFlow child element, and the value is a sequence flow id rather than a flow name.

d.text: Incorrect. There is no fallbackRef attribute on the inclusive gateway; the documented mechanism for fallback routing is the gateway's default attribute pointing at one sequence flow id.

a.text: Incorrect. The default flow is declared on the gateway itself through the default attribute and not through an extensionElement on the sequence flow element.

b.text: Correct. The documented XML shows bpmn:inclusiveGateway with a default attribute whose value is the id of the outgoing sequence flow taken when no other outgoing condition evaluates to true.

explanation: The documented XML representation places a default attribute on the bpmn:inclusiveGateway element whose value is the id of the outgoing sequence flow taken when none of the other outgoing conditions evaluates to true.

scenario: Quanta Telco, a telco operator, orchestrates 20000 instances per day of its provisioning process in Camunda 8.8. To tame a busy diagram with many crossing sequence flows, the modeller introduces paired link events to express a retry loop between two sections of that same process at the root level.
question: Which documented rule restricts where a paired throwing and catching link event can be used in a Camunda 8.8 process?

options.text: Throwing link events can connect to a catching link event only when both events are attached to the boundary of the same activity.
Throwing and catching link events must sit on different scopes, allowing a task to link from the root process to a subprocess directly.
Throwing and catching link events must share the same scope, existing together on the root process level or within the same subprocess.
Throwing link events can target multiple catching link events as long as each catcher shares the throwing event's parent process scope.

b.text: Incorrect. The documentation states the opposite, that link events share the same scope limitation as sequence flows and cannot cross from a root process level into a subprocess or vice versa.

a.text: Incorrect. Link events are intermediate events placed within the sequence flow, not boundary events on an activity, so the boundary attachment described is not how paired link events are used.

c.text: Correct. The documentation explicitly limits link events to a single scope: a throwing link event and its paired catching link event must both exist on the root process level or together within the same subprocess.

d.text: Incorrect. A throwing link event cannot link to multiple catching link events; the only multiple-to-one direction supported is several throwing link events targeting one catching link event.

explanation: Paired link events act like a throw-and-catch shortcut for a sequence flow and inherit the same scope restriction. They must coexist on the root process level or together within the same subprocess and cannot cross scope boundaries.

scenario: Granite Manufacturing, a manufacturer of industrial valves, tracks 1500 tasks per week of preventive maintenance in Camunda 8.8. The process model represents a physical inspection step performed offline by a technician, with no UI or worker integration, so the modeller chooses a BPMN manual task to document that step inside the flow.
question: Which runtime behaviour does the Camunda 8.8 engine apply to a BPMN manual task element when the process instance reaches it?

options.text: The engine creates a user task entry in Tasklist for the manual task and waits for a human to complete it through Tasklist.
The engine activates a job worker subscription using the configured taskDefinition type and waits for a worker to complete the job.
The engine treats the manual task as a pass-through activity that completes automatically the moment the process instance arrives at it.
The engine blocks the manual task until an external client calls a complete-manual-task API exposed by the Zeebe gateway to the team.

a.text: Incorrect. A manual task does not generate a Tasklist entry; user tasks are the actionable BPMN element assisted by Tasklist, while manual tasks are documentation-only and behave as a pass-through inside the engine.

b.text: Incorrect. Manual tasks have no taskDefinition type and no job worker subscription; that mechanism is used by service tasks, not by manual tasks, which the engine completes automatically.

d.text: Incorrect. There is no complete-manual-task API; manual tasks are handled as pass-through activities and the engine continues the process immediately, without any external completion call.

c.text: Correct. The documentation states that the engine handles a manual task as a pass-through activity, automatically continuing the process at the moment the process instance arrives at the task.

explanation: A BPMN manual task models a step performed by a human outside the process engine, such as a physical inspection. In the engine it behaves as a pass-through activity that automatically continues the process the moment the instance arrives at the task.

scenario: Sable Hospital documents 200 tasks per day that staff perform outside the Camunda 8.8 engine, such as physically filing a chart. The modeler adds a manual task element to record the step in the diagram and asks how the engine actually handles it at runtime.
question: How does the Camunda 8 engine treat a manual task when a process instance arrives at it?

options.text: A manual task is handled as a receive task, so the engine waits for a correlated message with the configured name before continuing the outgoing flow
A manual task is handled as a pass-through activity, so the engine continues the process the moment the instance arrives at the manual task element
A manual task is handled as a user task, so the engine creates a Tasklist entry that an assigned user must claim and complete from a worklist inbox
A manual task is handled as a service task, so the engine creates a job with the configured job type and waits for a job worker to complete the job

c.text: Incorrect. The documentation distinguishes manual tasks from user tasks; user tasks create Tasklist entries while manual tasks have no engine-driven assignment or completion step.

d.text: Incorrect. Manual tasks do not create jobs; the documented behavior is a pass-through activity, while service tasks are what create jobs that workers must complete.

b.text: Correct. The documentation states that within the engine and BPMN model a manual task is handled as a pass-through activity, automatically continuing the process the moment the instance arrives.

a.text: Incorrect. Manual tasks do not subscribe to messages; that role belongs to receive tasks and intermediate message catch events, not to the documented manual task element.

explanation: The manual task is documented as a pass-through activity for the engine and BPMN model: it offers modeling visibility for offline work but does not block the process flow.

scenario: Forge Manufacturer processes 12000 records per day in a Camunda 8.8 order subprocess that must run once for each element of an order line collection. The team needs the BPMN marker category that maps to a for-each-style iteration over a fixed collection.
question: Which marker category executes a subprocess once for every element of a collection?

options.text: The ad-hoc marker executes the subprocess once each time an operator picks a contained activity, with the runtime activity selection unknown at design time
The loop marker executes the subprocess on each clock tick of the engine until a configured termination condition becomes true at the start of a tick
The multi-instance marker executes the subprocess once for every element of a given collection, supporting parallel or sequential creation of instances
The compensation marker executes the subprocess once each time an upstream activity raises a compensation event for a previously completed step inside the scope

d.text: Incorrect. Compensation is for undoing already completed activities and is triggered by compensation events, not by iterating a collection like a programming language foreach loop.

a.text: Incorrect. Ad-hoc subprocesses allow flexible activity selection at runtime rather than a deterministic iteration over the elements of a predefined collection.

b.text: Incorrect. There is no clock-tick loop marker in the supported markers list; the for-each semantics belong to the documented multi-instance category.

c.text: Correct. The documentation explains that parallel multi-instance markers execute a subprocess multiple times, once for each element of a collection, like a foreach loop in a programming language.

explanation: The documented markers overview identifies multi-instance as the for-each marker that runs a subprocess once for every element of a given input collection.

scenario: Apex Logistics ships 12000 orders per day in Camunda 8.8 through a fulfilment process whose engineers must execute the same packaging subprocess once for every line item on a customer order before the warehouse hand-off step can run.
question: Which BPMN marker executes the subprocess once for every element of the configured collection?

options.text: The multi-instance marker executes the subprocess once for each element of the configured collection like a for each loop
The ad-hoc marker executes the contained activities in any order or any number of times under runtime control flow
The loop activity marker executes the subprocess repeatedly while a configured Boolean condition still evaluates to true
The compensation marker executes the subprocess to undo a previously completed activity through compensation handling

a.text: Correct. The markers page lists multi-instance as the marker that executes a subprocess once for each element of a given collection, matching the documented for each pattern from BPMN workflow patterns.

d.text: Incorrect. The compensation marker is one of the listed markers but it triggers compensation handlers for already-completed activities, not collection-driven repetition of a subprocess.

b.text: Incorrect. The ad-hoc marker is one of the listed markers but it allows contained activities to run in ad-hoc order, not once per element of a configured collection.

c.text: Incorrect. The markers page does not list a loop activity marker among the currently supported markers, and the for each pattern is implemented via the multi-instance marker.

explanation: The Camunda 8.8 markers page enumerates multi-instance, compensation and ad-hoc as the supported markers, and assigns the for each semantics to the multi-instance marker which runs the subprocess once per collection element.

scenario: Stellar Bank ingests 3000 transactions per hour in Camunda 8.8 with a message start event keyed by accountId, and the architects must decide what happens when a second transaction message arrives for an accountId that already drives an active instance, given that the new message is published with a time-to-live of 600 seconds.
question: What does Zeebe do with the new message when an instance triggered by the same correlationKey is still active?

options.text: Zeebe discards the new message at once without buffering even when its time-to-live exceeds zero on the publish call
Zeebe does not correlate the new message and buffers it until the active instance completes when its time-to-live exceeds zero
Zeebe correlates the new message only after the BPMN process ID version has been incremented by redeploying the model
Zeebe correlates the new message immediately and starts a second concurrent process instance alongside the active one

d.text: Incorrect. The message start events section states that when an instance triggered by the same correlationKey is active, the message is not correlated and no new instance is created, so a second concurrent instance is not started.

c.text: Incorrect. Redeploying a new process version does not change how an in-flight correlationKey collision is handled; the buffering rule depends on the message time-to-live, not on the process version.

a.text: Incorrect. The documentation explicitly states that the message is buffered when its time-to-live is greater than zero, so discarding it at once contradicts the documented behaviour.

b.text: Correct. The message start events section says the new message is not correlated and is buffered when time-to-live exceeds zero; once the active instance ends, the buffered message starts a new instance of the latest version.

explanation: On a correlationKey collision the engine suppresses correlation for the new message and buffers it if time-to-live is greater than zero, then correlates the buffered message once the active instance completes or terminates.

scenario: Vanta Retailer publishes 4000 order-shipped events per day in Camunda 8.8 from a fulfilment process and must clarify how Zeebe handles an intermediate message throw event placed just before the end event of the customer order flow, since the platform team plans to register a dedicated job worker for the outbound publication.
question: How does Zeebe process an intermediate message throw event so the process instance can continue?

options.text: Zeebe correlates the thrown message to a matching catch event in the same cluster and continues once correlation succeeds in the broker
Zeebe publishes the payload itself to a configured Kafka topic and waits for the broker acknowledgement before the instance continues
Zeebe routes the thrown message through an inbound connector that must acknowledge receipt before the process instance continues running
Zeebe creates a job of the configured job type and waits for a registered job worker to complete it before the instance continues

b.text: Incorrect. The message throw events section does not describe Zeebe publishing payloads to Kafka itself; integration with a Kafka topic is the job of an external job worker that the platform team would register.

d.text: Correct. The message throw events section states that on entering the throw event Zeebe creates a corresponding job and waits for its completion, which a job worker requesting that job type must process before the instance continues.

a.text: Incorrect. The note on the message throw events section says these events are not processed by Zeebe to correlate to a catch event; correlation is the role of message catch events, not message throw events.

c.text: Incorrect. Inbound connectors deliver messages into a process; the throw event is outbound and is handled through the job worker mechanism rather than an inbound connector callback.

explanation: Intermediate message throw events behave like service or send tasks: Zeebe issues a job of the configured type and the instance waits for a job worker to complete it before flow continues, with no internal correlation to a catch event.

scenario: Kestrel Healthcare orchestrates 200 instances per hour of a patient onboarding flow in Camunda 8.8 and engineers want to apply the multi-instance marker to several activities and events in the current model, but they must first confirm which element type in the BPMN spec does not accept this marker according to the documentation.
question: Which BPMN element from the current model does not support the multi-instance marker?

options.text: A call activity that delegates to another deployed process cannot carry the multi-instance marker on the activity element
An embedded subprocess that wraps a sequence of activities cannot carry the multi-instance marker on the subprocess element
A service task with a configured job type and worker cannot carry the multi-instance marker on the task element in the model
An intermediate throw event with a JobWorker implementation cannot carry the multi-instance marker on the event element

a.text: Incorrect. The multi-instance page lists call activities among the supported activities that can carry the marker, so a call activity delegating to another deployed process can be marked multi-instance.

d.text: Correct. The note on the multi-instance page states that events with a JobWorker implementation, such as intermediate throw events, do not support the multi-instance marker on the event element.

c.text: Incorrect. Service tasks are explicitly listed among the supported activities for the multi-instance marker, so a service task with a configured job type can be marked multi-instance in the model.

b.text: Incorrect. Embedded subprocesses are explicitly named as one of the supported activities for the multi-instance marker, so an embedded subprocess can be marked multi-instance on the subprocess element.

explanation: Camunda 8.8 supports the multi-instance marker on supported activities like service tasks, receive tasks, embedded subprocesses and call activities, but explicitly excludes events with a JobWorker implementation such as intermediate throw events.

scenario: Aurora Bank runs 80 tasks per loan application as a parallel multi-instance subprocess in Camunda 8.8. The risk team wants to terminate any still-active instances and complete the multi-instance body as soon as 50% or more of the audit instances have finished, so they need a FEEL completionCondition that uses the BPMN multi-instance properties.
question: Which FEEL completionCondition expresses 'complete when 50% or more of the instances already completed'?

options.text: = numberOfTerminatedInstances / numberOfInstances >= 0.5 evaluates true once at least half of the created instances have been terminated by the engine at runtime
= numberOfCompletedInstances / numberOfActiveInstances >= 0.5 evaluates true once the completed count reaches half of the currently active instances at evaluation
= numberOfCompletedInstances / numberOfInstances >= 0.5 evaluates true once at least half of the originally created instances of the multi-instance body have completed
= numberOfActiveInstances / numberOfInstances >= 0.5 evaluates true once at least half of the originally created instances remain active in the multi-instance body

d.text: Incorrect. numberOfActiveInstances counts instances still running, not those that have completed, so this expression triggers on the active share and does not match the documented 50%-completed rule for the multi-instance body.

a.text: Incorrect. numberOfTerminatedInstances counts instances ended early rather than those that completed successfully, so the ratio does not express the 'already completed' threshold the team wants to enforce.

b.text: Incorrect. Dividing completed by active uses a moving denominator that shrinks as instances finish, so the ratio does not match 50% of the originally created instances defined by numberOfInstances.

c.text: Correct. The docs give exactly this completionCondition as the example for 'complete the multi-instance body when 50% or more of the instances already completed', dividing numberOfCompletedInstances by numberOfInstances.

explanation: BPMN multi-instance bodies expose numberOfInstances, numberOfActiveInstances, numberOfCompletedInstances, and numberOfTerminatedInstances for use in the completionCondition, and the 50% threshold is expressed as numberOfCompletedInstances / numberOfInstances >= 0.5.

scenario: An ACME warehouse team models 1500 jobs per hour as a sequential multi-instance service task in Camunda 8.8. The modeler is inspecting the BPMN XML to confirm that the loop runs one instance at a time and wants to know which attribute on the BPMN element controls sequential versus parallel execution in the multi-instance loop characteristics block.
question: Which XML attribute marks the loop characteristics as sequential on a multi-instance service task?

options.text: The isSequential attribute set to true on the bpmn:multiInstanceLoopCharacteristics element marks the multi-instance service task as sequential in the BPMN XML
The inputElement attribute on zeebe:loopCharacteristics set to sequential controls whether the multi-instance service task runs instance by instance or in parallel
An ordering attribute set to sequential on the bpmn:multiInstanceLoopCharacteristics element configures the multi-instance service task to run one instance at a time
A sequential attribute set to true inside the zeebe:loopCharacteristics extension element configures the multi-instance service task to run instance by instance

a.text: Correct. The XML representation in the docs shows bpmn:multiInstanceLoopCharacteristics with isSequential set to true on a sequential multi-instance service task; this BPMN 2.0 attribute selects sequential rather than parallel execution.

d.text: Incorrect. The zeebe:loopCharacteristics extension element configures inputCollection, inputElement, outputCollection, and outputElement, not the sequential flag, which lives on the outer bpmn:multiInstanceLoopCharacteristics element.

c.text: Incorrect. There is no ordering attribute on bpmn:multiInstanceLoopCharacteristics; the BPMN 2.0 spec uses the boolean isSequential attribute, which the source XML sets to true for the sequential example.

b.text: Incorrect. inputElement on zeebe:loopCharacteristics names the local variable that holds each item from the inputCollection; it does not control sequential or parallel execution of the multi-instance loop.

explanation: The XML representation in the multi-instance docs shows that bpmn:multiInstanceLoopCharacteristics carries the isSequential boolean attribute, which is set to true to make the multi-instance body run one instance at a time rather than in parallel.

scenario: Quanta SaaS provisions 1500 tenants per day in Camunda 8.8 by iterating a multi-instance service task over a tenant list. The platform engineer reviews how the multi-instance body relates to the inner activity so workers are scheduled correctly.
question: What does the multi-instance body do at activation time?

options.text: The multi-instance body activates and waits for an external job worker to publish each element of the inputCollection before any inner activity instance is created
The multi-instance body activates and creates one inner activity instance per partition of the running Zeebe cluster, independent of the inputCollection size
The multi-instance body activates and creates a single inner activity instance that is then re-entered once for each element of the inputCollection in turn
The multi-instance body activates and creates one inner activity instance for every element of the inputCollection, either sequentially or in parallel

d.text: Correct. The documentation states that when the activity is entered, the multi-instance body is activated and one instance for every element of the inputCollection is created, either sequentially or in parallel.

c.text: Incorrect. The documented behavior is to create one instance per element rather than re-entering a single instance; re-entry semantics are not part of the multi-instance body description.

a.text: Incorrect. The multi-instance body evaluates the inputCollection expression locally on activation; it does not subscribe to a job worker to obtain the collection elements one at a time.

b.text: Incorrect. The number of instances is driven by the size of the inputCollection, not by the number of partitions in the running Zeebe cluster as suggested by the option.

explanation: The multi-instance body activates and creates one inner activity instance per element of the inputCollection, with the chosen execution mode determining whether instances run sequentially or in parallel.

scenario: Zenith Wholesaler iterates 50 orders per day in Camunda 8.8 through a multi-instance service task whose inputCollection FEEL expression evaluates to an array of order items. Each instance must access only the current item without leaking its value into sibling instances.
question: How is the current iteration element exposed inside each multi-instance activity instance?

options.text: The inputElement variable names a local variable in the instance that stores the current element of the inputCollection so the instance accesses only that element
The inputElement variable names a system variable that holds the current loopCounter so each instance can compute its own index inside the inputCollection array
The inputElement variable names a job header that the worker reads at runtime to fetch the current element from the inputCollection by a separate remote lookup
The inputElement variable names a process variable that holds the entire inputCollection so each instance can read neighbouring elements by index inside the iteration

d.text: Incorrect. The documentation describes inputElement as the current element, not the whole inputCollection; access to neighbouring elements by index is not part of the documented contract.

b.text: Incorrect. loopCounter and inputElement are distinct documented variables; inputElement holds the current element of the inputCollection rather than the iteration index.

a.text: Correct. The documentation states that the inputElement is stored as a local variable of the instance under the given name, holding the current element of the inputCollection for that iteration.

c.text: Incorrect. The current element is exposed as a local variable on activation, not as a job header that the worker must read and resolve through a remote lookup.

explanation: The inputElement is stored as a local variable of each instance and holds the current element of the inputCollection so each iteration can access only its own element of the collection.

scenario: Meridian Bank runs a parallel multi-instance task over 30 customers per batch in Camunda 8.8 and worries about race conditions on a shared variable named status that several worker callbacks update concurrently inside the batch.
question: How do input variable mappings on a multi-instance activity reduce race conditions across parallel instances?

options.text: An input mapping creates a local variable in the instance scope so worker updates stay inside the instance and do not overwrite any sibling instance variable of the same name
An input mapping creates a process-level variable in the root scope so each instance can read the same value at activation time without conflicting with sibling instance writes
An input mapping creates a global header at the multi-instance body level so every instance writes back through the header rather than through ordinary process variables
An input mapping creates a copy on write proxy at the cluster level so concurrent writes by sibling instances are merged through a last-writer-wins strategy by the broker

b.text: Incorrect. Putting the variable into the root scope exposes it to every parallel instance and reintroduces the race condition, which is the exact problem the local variable scope prevents.

a.text: Correct. The documentation explains that input mappings can create new local variables in the scope of an instance, restricting visibility so that variables modified inside the instance do not affect sibling parallel instances.

c.text: Incorrect. Multi-instance scoping uses local instance variables rather than a custom global header mechanism; there is no documented body-level header that workers write through.

d.text: Incorrect. There is no copy-on-write proxy or last-writer-wins merging documented for multi-instance variables; isolation is achieved through local instance scope rather than a broker-level strategy.

explanation: Input variable mappings on a multi-instance activity can create local variables in the instance scope, which prevents parallel instances from clobbering each other's values through the shared process scope.

scenario: Junction Bank reviews 8 tasks per batch through a parallel multi-instance user task block in Camunda 8.8. The product owner wants the batch to complete the moment at least half of the instances finish, so a FEEL completionCondition must encode that 50% threshold against the documented multi-instance properties.
question: Which FEEL completionCondition expresses stopping when at least half of the multi-instance instances complete?

options.text: An expression like = numberOfTerminatedInstances >= 0.5 stops the body when half or more of the instances have been individually terminated by a job worker callback
An expression like = count(numberOfCompletedInstances) >= 0.5 stops the body when the completed count function returns a value of at least 0.5 as a fractional result
An expression like = numberOfCompletedInstances / numberOfInstances >= 0.5 stops the body when half or more of the instances are already in the completed state at evaluation
An expression like = numberOfActiveInstances < numberOfInstances / 2 stops the body when fewer than half of the instances remain in the active state at evaluation

d.text: Incorrect. The documentation expresses the 50% threshold through numberOfCompletedInstances divided by numberOfInstances, not through a separate comparison of the active instance count.

a.text: Incorrect. numberOfTerminatedInstances counts terminations rather than completions, so it would not fire on instances that finished normally without being explicitly terminated.

c.text: Correct. The documentation lists this exact form, = numberOfCompletedInstances / numberOfInstances >= 0.5, as the way to complete the body when 50 percent or more of the instances are complete.

b.text: Incorrect. numberOfCompletedInstances is a numeric property, not a list to count; wrapping it in count() does not produce the documented half completion semantics.

explanation: The documented multi-instance properties include numberOfCompletedInstances and numberOfInstances, and the standard half-completion expression is = numberOfCompletedInstances / numberOfInstances >= 0.5.

scenario: An online marketplace correlates payment events to open orders in Camunda 8.8 across 2000 orders per hour. The order process waits at a receive task named Money collected and correlates incoming messages by the order identifier stored in the orderId process variable, so the modeler must define the message name and correlationKey expression on the referenced bpmn:message element.
question: Which two properties must the message referenced by a receive task define for a published message to correlate?

options.text: The message name as an expression that resolves to a number and the correlationKey as a static value compared at the time the message is published
The message name as a static value and a correlationKey expression that evaluates to a boolean or a list at activation of the receive task instance
The message type as one of system or user and a correlationKey expression that resolves to a string evaluated only when the matching message is published
The message name as a static value or expression that resolves to a string and a correlationKey expression that resolves to a string or a number value

b.text: Incorrect. The docs require correlationKey to resolve to a string or a number, not a boolean or a list, and a message name can also be supplied as a FEEL expression rather than only as a static value.

a.text: Incorrect. The correlationKey is an expression that runs when the receive task activates, not a static value matched at publish, and the message name is not constrained to numeric expressions.

d.text: Correct. The docs state the message must define a name (usually static, but expressions are allowed if they evaluate to a string) and a correlationKey expression that evaluates to a string or a number on activation of the receive task.

c.text: Incorrect. Camunda 8 messages do not have a system or user type attribute on the bpmn:message element; the required parts are the message name and the correlationKey expression on the referenced message.

explanation: A bpmn:message referenced by a receive task must declare a name, which is typically a static value but may also be a FEEL expression that resolves to a string, and a correlationKey expression that resolves to a string or a number when the receive task activates.

scenario: Vanta Telco correlates inbound payment messages to 3000 orders per day in Camunda 8.8 at a receive task. The correlationKey expression references a process variable orderId, and the engineer must know exactly when the engine evaluates this expression.
question: When is the correlationKey expression of a receive task evaluated?

options.text: The correlationKey expression is evaluated on activating the receive task and must result in a string or number that is matched against the published message correlation key
The correlationKey expression is evaluated on publishing the message by the client and the resulting value is stored alongside the message name as instance metadata
The correlationKey expression is evaluated on deploying the process and the resulting value is stored on the message subscription as a static routing key for every instance
The correlationKey expression is evaluated on completing the receive task and the resulting value is used to discard any further messages that share the same name

c.text: Incorrect. The documentation evaluates the correlationKey on activation per instance rather than once at deploy time, since the key normally depends on per-instance variable values.

b.text: Incorrect. The publishing client provides a correlation key value alongside the message; the receive task evaluates its own correlationKey expression on activation to match against that value.

d.text: Incorrect. The correlationKey is evaluated on activation rather than completion; completion is when the receive task finishes after a message has already been correlated to it.

a.text: Correct. The documentation specifies that the correlationKey expression is evaluated on activating the receive task and must result in a string or number, which the engine then matches against the value of the published message.

explanation: At a receive task the correlationKey expression is evaluated on activation and must return a string or number; the engine then matches that value against the correlation key carried by a published message.

scenario: A fintech enriches transaction records via job worker implementation script tasks in Camunda 8.8 at 9000 jobs per hour, using the community Zeebe Script Worker to evaluate scripts. The platform team needs to confirm how Zeebe handles a job worker script task at runtime so they can size workers and decide which side owns script execution end to end.
question: How does a job worker implementation script task get its work processed at runtime in Camunda 8.8?

options.text: A boundary script worker is attached to the script task and intercepts the job creation event so the process instance continues to the next element immediately after activation
Zeebe executes the script itself using an embedded engine for the configured script language and writes the result of the evaluation back to a process variable directly on the script task
The script task behaves like a send task by publishing a message of the configured type and waits for an external worker to publish a reply message correlated by a configured key
Zeebe creates a job of the configured job type on entering the script task and a subscribing job worker picks up that job processes it and completes it so the process instance can continue

b.text: Incorrect. The docs note explicitly that jobs for script tasks are not processed by Zeebe itself; a job worker must be provided, which is exactly the difference from the FEEL implementation evaluated by the engine.

c.text: Incorrect. Job worker script tasks are based on jobs and job workers, not on BPMN message publication; they behave like service tasks rather than send tasks at runtime.

a.text: Incorrect. Boundary events do not implement script task work; a job worker implementation script task creates a job and the process instance waits for the worker to complete that job.

d.text: Correct. The docs state that on entering the script task Zeebe creates a corresponding job of the configured job type and waits for completion; a subscribing job worker processes the job and completes it, then the process instance continues.

explanation: Job worker implementation script tasks behave like service tasks: they require a configured job type, Zeebe creates a job on activation and waits, and a subscribing job worker is responsible for executing the script and completing the job so the process instance can continue.

scenario: Lumen Fintech evaluates 10000 calls per day against risk-scoring FEEL expressions inside Camunda 8.8 script tasks. The operations team needs the runtime behavior when a FEEL evaluation fails so they can monitor and resolve the right signal.
question: What happens when a FEEL expression on a script task fails to evaluate successfully?

options.text: An incident is raised at the script task, and once the incident is resolved the FEEL expression is evaluated again at the same script task before the flow continues
The job is failed with a backoff retry until the configured retry count reaches zero, then the engine completes the script task with a null result and moves the flow forward
An incident is raised at the parent scope, and once the incident is resolved the process continues at the next activity placed after the failing script task element
The script task is silently skipped and the configured resultVariable is written with a null value so downstream gateways treat the missing path as default at runtime

c.text: Incorrect. The documented incident is raised at the script task itself rather than at the parent scope, and the engine retries the expression at the same script task on resolution.

a.text: Correct. The documentation states that if the FEEL expression evaluation is unsuccessful, an incident is raised at the script task, and when the incident is resolved the script task is evaluated again.

b.text: Incorrect. The FEEL-based script task does not run a job worker with retries; it evaluates the FEEL expression inline, and a failure produces a documented incident rather than retry decrement.

d.text: Incorrect. A failed FEEL evaluation does not silently skip the script task or write a null result; the engine raises an incident that must be resolved before the process can continue.

explanation: A failed FEEL evaluation at a script task raises an incident on that script task, and when the incident is resolved the FEEL expression is re-evaluated against the current scope.

scenario: Vector Logistics calculates 5000 route prices per day in Camunda 8.8 across 5000 jobs per day. A modeler chooses the inline FEEL implementation for a BPMN script task to compute the sum of two variables a and b, and the result must be written into a process variable named sum.
question: Which extension element and attributes are used in the BPMN XML to declare this inline FEEL script?

options.text: A camunda:script element with a scriptFormat attribute set to feel and a result variable defined on the surrounding bpmn:scriptTask element itself
A zeebe:taskDefinition element of type feel paired with a zeebe:header carrying the FEEL formula under a reserved expression header key value
A zeebe:ioMapping output element with a source FEEL expression and a target variable that receives the result of the inline script evaluation step
A zeebe:script element with an expression attribute carrying the FEEL formula and a resultVariable attribute naming the target process variable

d.text: Correct. The inline FEEL form of a script task is declared as a zeebe:script extension element with an expression attribute that carries the FEEL formula and a resultVariable attribute that names the process variable receiving the evaluated value.

b.text: Incorrect. A zeebe:taskDefinition is used for the job worker implementation of a script task, not for declaring an inline FEEL expression body on the activity.

c.text: Incorrect. The ioMapping construct handles variable transformation around an activity; it is not the documented mechanism for declaring an inline FEEL script body.

a.text: Incorrect. The documented Camunda 8 form uses the zeebe:script extension element with expression and resultVariable attributes, not a camunda:script element from a different namespace.

explanation: The inline FEEL implementation of a script task is captured by the zeebe:script extension element. Its expression attribute holds the FEEL formula and its resultVariable attribute names the process variable that stores the evaluated result.

scenario: Aurora Marketplace publishes 4500 order events per day to a Kafka topic named payment from Camunda 8.8. A modeler must configure the BPMN send task so the right pool of workers picks up the job and so the static Kafka topic name travels with each job without being passed as a process variable.
question: How should the modeler configure the send task to route jobs to the correct workers and carry the static topic name?

options.text: Configure the topic on the BPMN messageRef of the send task and let Zeebe correlate the outgoing publication based on the resolved message name at activation
Set the job type on the task definition to payment and rely on the topic header being injected by Zeebe automatically based on the configured job type name
Set the job type on the task definition to a value such as kafka and add a task header that carries the static topic name to the subscribed job workers
Leave the task definition empty and put the topic value into an input variable mapping so that the worker reads the topic from the activated job payload variables

c.text: Correct. A send task defines a job type the same way a service task does so that the right workers subscribe to the job, and task headers pass static parameters such as the Kafka topic name to those workers.

b.text: Incorrect. There is no automatic topic header injection in Zeebe; static parameters such as the topic name must be declared explicitly via task headers on the send task.

d.text: Incorrect. Without a task definition there is no job type for workers to subscribe to, and variable mappings transform variables rather than carry static configuration values.

a.text: Incorrect. Send tasks are job-based outbound publication elements; they do not use messageRef-driven correlation to deliver static configuration to workers.

explanation: Send tasks declare a job type via the task definition so the right workers subscribe, and task headers carry static parameters such as the Kafka topic name. Variable mappings transform dynamic variables but are not the place for static configuration.

scenario: Apex Bank routes 7500 jobs per day for service tasks in Camunda 8.8 to different worker pools depending on a process variable priorityGroup. The modeler needs the job type to switch between order-gold and order-standard at activation time and does not want to set retries explicitly on the task.
question: How should the service task taskDefinition be configured to satisfy these requirements?

options.text: Leave type empty and set retries to a static value of zero so the engine falls back to a worker pool resolved from the process variable priorityGroup at runtime
Set type to the literal string order-gold and configure retries as a FEEL expression evaluating to the string priorityGroup so the broker maps it to a pool at activation
Set type to a FEEL expression prefixed by equals that evaluates to a string such as order- concatenated with priorityGroup, and omit retries to take the default of three
Set type to a FEEL expression that evaluates to a number derived from priorityGroup and rely on a global default of ten retries when retries is omitted from the task

c.text: Correct. The required type can be a static value or a FEEL expression prefixed by equals that evaluates to a string at activation, and the optional retries property defaults to three when it is omitted from the task definition.

b.text: Incorrect. The retries property must evaluate to a number, not a string, and using a static literal type would defeat the requirement to switch job type per priority group at activation.

a.text: Incorrect. The type property is required on a service task definition, and retries cannot be used as a fallback to resolve which worker pool handles the job.

d.text: Incorrect. The type expression must evaluate to a string rather than a number, and the documented default for retries is three, not ten, when the property is omitted from the task.

explanation: A service task taskDefinition requires a type that resolves to a string, either statically or via a FEEL expression prefixed by equals evaluated on activation. The optional retries property defaults to three and must evaluate to a number.

scenario: Beacon Logistics runs the order-fulfillment process in Camunda 8.8 with a signal start event named order placed. After version 4 has been live for 2 weeks and started 18000 process instances, the team deploys version 5 of the same BPMN process ID with the same signal start event and now wonders which version will be started by future broadcasts of the order placed signal.
question: Which subscription behavior applies to the signal start event after the new version is deployed?

options.text: The signal subscription is reopened against the oldest deployed version of the process ID, so future broadcasts start version 1 of the process instead of the newly deployed version 5
The signal subscription for version 4 is deleted on deployment and a fresh subscription is opened for version 5, so future broadcasts start only version 5 of the process
The signal subscription stays bound to version 4 until that version is undeployed, so future broadcasts continue to start version 4 instances even after version 5 is deployed
Subscriptions for both version 4 and version 5 stay active in parallel, so each broadcast of the order placed signal starts one instance of each deployed version of the process

b.text: Correct. Signal subscriptions only exist for the latest version of a process definition. Deploying a new version of the same BPMN process ID deletes the old signal subscription and opens a new one for the newly deployed version.

d.text: Incorrect. Signal subscriptions do not coexist across versions; only the latest version of the BPMN process ID holds an active signal start subscription at any time.

c.text: Incorrect. The subscription does not remain bound to the older version once a newer one is deployed; the older subscription is deleted in favor of the latest version.

a.text: Incorrect. Subscriptions track the latest deployed version, not the oldest, so version 1 would not receive new signal-triggered starts after version 5 is deployed.

explanation: Signal start event subscriptions only exist for the latest version of a process definition. Deploying a new version of the same BPMN process ID deletes the previous signal subscription and opens a new subscription for the new version.

scenario: Lyric Marketplace operates an order-fulfillment process in Camunda 8.8 that fans out to two concurrent tasks B and C handling roughly 9000 orders per day. When task C completes a terminate end event is reached at the process level, and the team needs to predict precisely what happens to the still-active token at task B in the same flow scope.
question: What does the engine do when the process instance reaches the terminate end event at the process level?

options.text: The engine terminates all element instances in the same flow scope as the end event, including the active task B, and the process instance completes without taking outgoing flows
The engine completes the token at task C and leaves task B active until it completes naturally, after which the process instance finally ends and reports completion
The engine terminates only the token that reached the end event and lets task B continue independently as a detached element instance until a separate cancel is issued
The engine compensates the already-completed task C and waits for task B to complete before considering the process instance terminated by the end event

a.text: Correct. When a process instance reaches a terminate end event, the engine terminates all element instances in the same flow scope as the end event, so any concurrent token such as the one on task B is cancelled along with the rest of that scope.

b.text: Incorrect. The terminate end event does not wait for concurrent activities to finish naturally; it actively cancels the other element instances in the same flow scope on arrival.

d.text: Incorrect. The terminate end event terminates element instances in the flow scope; it does not trigger compensation of already-completed activities such as task C.

c.text: Incorrect. A terminate end event affects the whole flow scope rather than just the arriving token, so concurrent activities like task B are cancelled rather than left running as detached instances.

explanation: A terminate end event is the only kind of terminate event. When a process instance reaches it, all element instances in the same flow scope are terminated, which is the documented way to cancel concurrent flows that are no longer required.

scenario: Stellar Logistics runs a Camunda 8.8 release pipeline that pushes 4000 orders per release into the carrier feed at a single absolute instant. The team models a timer start event that must fire exactly once at the planned date 2026-10-01T12:00:00Z and then never repeat for that deployment.
question: Which child element of bpmn:timerEventDefinition configures a one-time absolute moment for the release timer start event?

options.text: A timeDuration child with an ISO 8601 duration like PT24H configures a one-time absolute moment for the release timer start event
A timeDate child with an ISO 8601 instant like 2026-10-01T12:00:00Z configures a one-time absolute moment for the release timer start event
A timeCycle child with an ISO 8601 repeating interval like R3/PT1H configures a one-time absolute moment for the release timer start event
A bare timerEventDefinition with no child element at all configures a one-time absolute moment for the release timer start event

a.text: Incorrect. The documented timeDuration child carries a relative ISO 8601 duration such as PT10M for an intermediate catch event waiting that long, not an absolute calendar instant for a one-time start.

b.text: Correct. The documented timer start example uses a timeDate child holding an ISO 8601 instant such as 2019-10-01T12:00:00Z, which schedules the start event to fire exactly once at that absolute moment.

c.text: Incorrect. The documented timeCycle child takes an ISO 8601 repeating interval such as R3/PT1H, which is used for non-interrupting boundary timers, not for a single absolute start instant.

d.text: Incorrect. The documented XML always includes a child element under timerEventDefinition; omitting it leaves the timer unconfigured rather than scheduling a one-time absolute instant.

explanation: Timer start events scheduled at a fixed calendar moment use a timeDate child of timerEventDefinition holding an ISO 8601 combined date and time, per the documented XML representation.

scenario: Helios Healthcare runs a triage process in Camunda 8.8 for 5000 cases per day. The team needs a timer boundary event on a long-running review activity: one variant must cancel the review after 5 minutes if it is still active, and a second variant must only notify support after PT1H without cancelling the review.
question: Which combination of timer boundary event variants correctly satisfies both requirements?

options.text: An interrupting timer boundary event with a time cycle of PT5M for the cancel case and a non-interrupting timer boundary event with a time date of PT1H for the notify case
An interrupting timer boundary event with a time duration of PT5M for the cancel case and a non-interrupting timer boundary event with a time duration of PT1H for the notify case
A non-interrupting timer boundary event with a time duration of PT5M for the cancel case and an interrupting timer boundary event with a time cycle of PT1H for the notify case
An interrupting timer boundary event with a time date of PT5M for both the cancel case and the notify case, distinguished only by the configured number of repetitions

b.text: Correct. Interrupting timer boundary events terminate the attached activity when the timer fires and accept a time duration or time date. Non-interrupting timer boundary events fire without cancelling the activity and accept a time duration, time cycle, or time date.

c.text: Incorrect. A non-interrupting variant would not cancel the review, so it cannot satisfy the 5-minute cancellation requirement; the cancel case requires the interrupting variant.

a.text: Incorrect. An interrupting timer boundary event accepts a time duration or time date, not a time cycle, so a PT5M cycle on the interrupting boundary is not the documented configuration.

d.text: Incorrect. A PT5M value is a duration rather than a date, and a single interrupting variant cannot satisfy the non-cancelling notify requirement regardless of the repetition count.

explanation: Interrupting timer boundary events must use a time duration or time date and they terminate the activity when fired. Non-interrupting timer boundary events accept time duration, time cycle, or time date and leave the activity in place.

scenario: Cascade Logistics models an order-processing flow in Camunda 8.8 that ships 4500 orders per day. The team wants a reminder on an activity named process-order that fires up to three times, every hour, without cancelling the activity, and a developer is editing the BPMN XML to add the corresponding boundary timer event extension elements.
question: Which XML pattern correctly defines a non-interrupting boundary timer event with a time cycle of R3/PT1H?

options.text: A bpmn:intermediateCatchEvent with attachedToRef process-order, containing a bpmn:timerEventDefinition whose bpmn:timeDuration child has the value R3/PT1H instead of a cycle
A bpmn:startEvent with attachedToRef process-order, containing a bpmn:timerEventDefinition whose bpmn:timeDate child has the value R3/PT1H to represent the recurring trigger
