c.text: Correct. The BPMN primer states the sequence flow defines the order in which steps happen, drawn as an arrow connecting two elements with the arrow direction indicating the order of execution as the token advances.

d.text: Incorrect. The BPMN primer describes catching events as making a token wait for something to happen; they do not define the overall order of execution between the steps.

explanation: The BPMN primer defines sequence flow as the core concept that controls execution order: an arrow between two elements whose direction shows where the token moves next once the current step is complete.

scenario: Atlas Insurance runs a Camunda 8.8 underwriting process that evaluates 8000 policies per day with a deployed DMN decision named riskScore on a business rule task. Operations is rehearsing how the engine behaves when riskScore evaluation fails because of a missing input variable and needs to plan their incident response runbook.
question: What happens at the business rule task in Camunda 8.8 when the DMN decision evaluation is unsuccessful?

options.text: The business rule task is skipped and a default outgoing sequence flow is taken so the process instance can continue without manual action
The process instance terminates and the business rule task is removed from the active instances list permanently for that run
An incident is raised at the business rule task and the decision is evaluated again once the incident has been resolved by an operator
The business rule task completes with an empty result variable and the process instance moves to the next sequence flow as normal

b.text: Incorrect. The business rule task documentation states an incident is raised on a failed DMN evaluation; the process instance is not terminated and remains at the business rule task.

d.text: Incorrect. The business rule task does not complete on a failed DMN evaluation; the engine raises an incident at the task instead of moving the token onward.

a.text: Incorrect. There is no default-flow fallback for a failed DMN evaluation on a business rule task; the engine raises an incident at the task instead of taking a default outgoing flow.

c.text: Correct. The Camunda 8.8 business rule task documentation states that if the decision evaluation is unsuccessful, an incident is raised at the business rule task, and once the incident is resolved the decision is evaluated again.

explanation: When a Camunda 8.8 business rule task evaluates a deployed DMN decision unsuccessfully, the engine raises an incident at that task. The process instance waits at the business rule task until the incident is resolved, after which the decision is evaluated again.

scenario: Atlas Bank deploys 12 microservices behind a BPMN flow in Camunda 8.8. Each underwriting case must run a deployed DMN decision named credit_band, and the business rule task must always evaluate the most recent deployed version so that 4500 loans per day pick up the latest scoring rules without redeploying the calling process.
question: Which bindingType value on the called decision selects the most recent deployed version of credit_band at activation time?

options.text: The bindingType attribute set to latest selects the most recent deployed version of credit_band whenever the business rule task is activated
The bindingType attribute set to deployment selects the version of credit_band that was deployed with the current calling process definition
The bindingType attribute set to current selects the in-progress draft version of credit_band that is staged in the connected modeler workspace
The bindingType attribute set to versionTag selects the deployed version of credit_band that matches the configured versionTag attribute value

a.text: Correct. The latest bindingType resolves the called decision to the most recent deployed version at the moment the business rule task is activated, which matches the requirement to always pick up the newest scoring rules.

b.text: Incorrect. The deployment bindingType pins the called decision to the version that was deployed alongside the current calling process definition, which would freeze credit_band at parent deployment time.

d.text: Incorrect. The versionTag bindingType selects the latest deployed version annotated with a specific tag, which requires every release to carry the right tag and does not always pick up the newest version.

c.text: Incorrect. There is no current bindingType for a business rule task. The documented bindingType values are latest, deployment, and versionTag.

explanation: When bindingType is omitted or explicitly set to latest, a business rule task resolves the called DMN decision to the most recent deployed version at activation, which is the documented way to keep evaluating against the newest rules.

scenario: Vertex Fintech processes 2500 transactions per day through a BPMN flow in Camunda 8.8. Instead of evaluating a DMN model, the team implements risk scoring inside an external Python service and wants the business rule task to behave like a service task so that the existing pool of job workers can pick up scoring jobs of type risk-score.
question: Which extension element configures a business rule task in Camunda 8 to dispatch a job to a custom job worker instead of evaluating a DMN decision?

options.text: The zeebe:taskHeaders extension element with a type attribute of risk-score dispatches the job to the registered Python job worker for scoring
The zeebe:calledDecision extension element with a decisionId of risk-score dispatches the job to the registered Python job worker for scoring
The zeebe:taskDefinition extension element with a type attribute of risk-score dispatches the job to the registered Python job worker for scoring
The zeebe:calledElement extension element with a processId of risk-score dispatches the job to the registered Python job worker for scoring

b.text: Incorrect. The zeebe:calledDecision extension element links the business rule task to a deployed DMN decision through decisionId and does not dispatch a job to an external job worker.

a.text: Incorrect. Task headers pass static key-value parameters to the job once a job type has been defined. They do not declare the job type that workers subscribe to.

c.text: Correct. The zeebe:taskDefinition extension element with a type attribute makes the business rule task behave like a service task and creates a job that workers can claim by that type.

d.text: Incorrect. The zeebe:calledElement extension element belongs to call activities and references a child BPMN processId, not a job worker type for a business rule task.

explanation: A business rule task with a job worker implementation defines its job type through zeebe:taskDefinition. It then behaves like a service task and creates a job that any worker subscribed to the configured type can pick up.

scenario: Polaris Logistics models a shipping decision in Camunda 8.8 for 7000 shipments per day. The team writes BPMN XML by hand and needs the business rule task to call a deployed DMN decision named shipping_box_size and merge the outcome into a variable called boxSize, without specifying a binding type so that the latest deployed decision version is used by default.
question: Which XML snippet inside the business rule task correctly configures the called decision in Camunda 8?

options.text: A zeebe:calledElement child element with processId set to shipping_box_size and resultVariable set to boxSize is placed inside the extension elements
A zeebe:taskHeaders child element with decisionId set to shipping_box_size and resultVariable set to boxSize is placed inside the extension elements
A zeebe:taskDefinition child element with type set to shipping_box_size and resultVariable set to boxSize is placed inside the extension elements
A zeebe:calledDecision child element with decisionId set to shipping_box_size and resultVariable set to boxSize is placed inside the extension elements

a.text: Incorrect. The zeebe:calledElement element is used by call activities to reference another BPMN process by processId. It does not configure a DMN decision call from a business rule task.

c.text: Incorrect. The zeebe:taskDefinition element configures a job worker implementation. Its type attribute names a job type rather than a DMN decision identifier.

b.text: Incorrect. The zeebe:taskHeaders element passes static key-value parameters to job workers. It does not declare decisionId or resultVariable for a DMN call.

d.text: Correct. The zeebe:calledDecision element with decisionId and resultVariable is the documented XML pattern for a business rule task that evaluates a deployed DMN decision.

explanation: When a business rule task evaluates a deployed DMN decision, the XML uses a zeebe:calledDecision child element under bpmn:extensionElements. The decisionId and resultVariable attributes wire the BPMN element to the deployed decision and the result variable.

scenario: Oasis Insurer needs to evaluate a deployed DMN decision named premiumRating inside a Camunda 8.8 quote pipeline that handles 9000 policies per day. The team is choosing which BPMN element invokes the internal DMN decision engine when the process instance arrives at that step in the model.
question: Which BPMN element invokes the internal DMN decision engine when a process instance arrives at the task?

options.text: A receive task with a referenced decision name triggers the internal DMN engine when a correlated message arrives on the configured event subscription
A service task with a decision-typed extension element triggers the internal DMN engine when a registered job worker polls the orchestration cluster
A user task with a configured form key triggers the internal DMN engine when the assignee submits the form for evaluation by the decision engine
A business rule task with a called decision evaluates the decision on the internal DMN engine when the process instance arrives at the task

c.text: Incorrect. User tasks are for human work and form completion; they do not invoke the internal DMN engine when the assignee submits the form.

a.text: Incorrect. Receive tasks wait for a correlated message on a subscription and do not invoke any DMN decision when the message arrives.

d.text: Correct. The business rule task is the BPMN element that, on activation, evaluates a called decision using the internal DMN engine and continues the process instance with the result.

b.text: Incorrect. Service tasks create jobs for workers; they do not call the internal DMN engine through a decision-typed extension element in Camunda 8.

explanation: A business rule task models the evaluation of a business rule and, when the process instance arrives, runs the called DMN decision on the internal DMN engine before the instance continues.

scenario: Pinnacle Bank wants its Camunda 8.8 loan rating process, handling 6000 loans per day, to pin the called DMN decision to the exact decision version that was deployed alongside the running process version, so a later DMN redeployment cannot change loan rating logic for instances already in flight.
question: Which bindingType on the business rule task pins the called decision to the version deployed together with the running process version?

options.text: Setting bindingType to latest pins the called decision to the version deployed together with the currently running process version on the orchestration cluster
Omitting bindingType on the business rule task pins the called decision to the version deployed together with the currently running process version on the cluster
Setting bindingType to versionTag pins the called decision to the version deployed together with the currently running process version on the orchestration cluster
Setting bindingType to deployment pins the called decision to the version deployed together with the currently running process version on the orchestration cluster

a.text: Incorrect. The latest binding evaluates the latest deployed version of the decision at activation time, so redeployments would shift the version used by in-flight instances.

c.text: Incorrect. The versionTag binding evaluates the latest version that carries a specific version tag, not the version deployed together with the process version.

b.text: Incorrect. When bindingType is omitted, latest is used as the default, so the called decision is not pinned to the version deployed with the process.

d.text: Correct. The deployment binding pins the called decision to the version deployed together with the currently running version of the process, isolating in-flight instances from later DMN redeployments.

explanation: The bindingType attribute on a business rule task controls which decision version is evaluated. The deployment value pins evaluation to the decision version that was deployed alongside the running process version.

scenario: Quantum Insurer runs a Camunda 8.8 underwriting workflow handling 5000 policies per day. A business rule task evaluates a DMN decision and writes a result, and the team wants to confirm the default behavior of the variable defined by resultVariable when no output mapping is configured on the task.
question: How is the variable defined by resultVariable on a business rule task propagated by default when no output mapping is configured?

options.text: The variable defined by resultVariable is discarded by default and only kept inside the business rule task scope unless an output mapping is configured for it
The variable defined by resultVariable is propagated only to a parent call activity by default when the business rule task completes without an output mapping configured
The variable defined by resultVariable is stored as a deployment-scope variable by default and made accessible to every process instance that uses the same decision
The variable defined by resultVariable is merged into the process instance by default when the business rule task completes without any output mapping defined

d.text: Correct. By default, the variable defined by resultVariable is merged into the process instance when the business rule task completes; output mappings only customize that default behavior.

a.text: Incorrect. The result variable is not discarded; the default behavior merges it into the process instance, and output mappings only refine where the value lands.

b.text: Incorrect. There is no parent call activity routing by default; the result variable lands in the current process instance scope on completion.

c.text: Incorrect. Camunda 8 has no deployment-scope variable storage shared across instances; result variables are written into the running process instance.

explanation: The result of a business rule task is exposed through the resultVariable property and, by default, merged into the process instance on completion. Output mappings can be added to customize that default behavior.

scenario: Tessera Fintech runs a Camunda 8.8 fraud-scoring workflow on 22000 transactions per day. A business rule task is implemented with a job worker instead of an inline DMN decision, and the platform team needs to confirm how such a task behaves during process execution and how it differs from a service task.
question: How does a business rule task with a job worker implementation behave compared to a service task in Camunda 8.8?

options.text: The business rule task with a job worker implementation skips creating any job and uses task headers as inline scripts that the engine evaluates on activation of the task
The business rule task with a job worker implementation behaves exactly like a service task, differing only in its visual marker and semantic role for the model
The business rule task with a job worker implementation evaluates an inline DMN decision and additionally creates a job for the worker on each evaluation pass at runtime
The business rule task with a job worker implementation runs in parallel with an inline DMN evaluation and merges the worker output with the decision result on completion

c.text: Incorrect. A job worker implementation replaces the DMN evaluation for that task; the task does not run an inline DMN decision alongside the job.

b.text: Correct. A business rule task with a job worker implementation behaves exactly like a service task at runtime; the differences are the visual task marker and the semantic intent for the model.

d.text: Incorrect. There is no parallel DMN evaluation alongside the worker; the worker handles the task in place of DMN evaluation when this implementation is chosen.

a.text: Incorrect. The task still creates a job for the registered worker; task headers pass static parameters and are not evaluated as inline scripts by the engine.

explanation: When a business rule task uses zeebe:taskDefinition, it creates a job on activation and waits for a worker to complete it, behaving like a service task while keeping the business rule marker and semantic.

scenario: Meridian Bank runs a loan origination process in Camunda 8.8 where a call activity launches a parallel multi-instance credit check for 600 loans per day. The team observes variables produced inside child instances overwriting each other in the parent scope and wants only the variables declared in the call activity output mappings to flow back into the parent.
question: Which attribute on the call activity should the team disable so that only the variables defined in output mappings are propagated back to the parent process?

options.text: The propagateAllChildVariables attribute should be set to false so that only declared output variables flow from the child process back to the parent
The propagateAllChildVariables attribute should be set to true so that only declared output variables flow from the child process back to the parent
The propagateAllParentVariables attribute should be set to false so that only declared output variables flow from the child process back to the parent
The localScope attribute on the call activity should be set to true so that only declared output variables flow from the child process back to the parent

c.text: Incorrect. The propagateAllParentVariables attribute controls which variables are copied from the parent into the child process when the call activity is activated, not what is propagated back on completion.

a.text: Correct. Setting propagateAllChildVariables to false makes the call activity propagate only the variables declared in its output mappings back to the parent process when the call activity completes.

b.text: Incorrect. The attribute is already true by default, which copies every child variable back to the parent on completion. That is the very behaviour producing the overwrites the team is trying to avoid.

d.text: Incorrect. There is no localScope attribute on a call activity. Child-to-parent propagation is controlled by propagateAllChildVariables together with the output mappings.

explanation: When propagateAllChildVariables is left at its default of true, every variable of the created child process instance is propagated back to the call activity scope. Disabling it confines propagation to whatever the call activity output mappings explicitly declare.

scenario: Orion Logistics models call activities in Camunda 8.8 for 4500 shipments per day. The team writes BPMN XML by hand and needs a call activity that always pins the called process child-process-a to the version that was deployed together with the parent definition, so that one coordinated release advances both processes in lockstep without surprises.
question: Which XML configuration on the call activity pins it to the version of child-process-a deployed together with the parent?

options.text: A zeebe:calledDecision with decisionId child-process-a and bindingType set to deployment is placed inside the extensionElements of the call activity
A zeebe:calledElement with processId child-process-a and bindingType set to latest is placed inside the extensionElements of the call activity
A zeebe:calledElement with processId child-process-a and bindingType set to versionTag is placed inside the extensionElements of the call activity
A zeebe:calledElement with processId child-process-a and bindingType set to deployment is placed inside the extensionElements of the call activity

b.text: Incorrect. The latest bindingType resolves the called process to the most recent deployed version at activation time, which would let the child drift ahead of the parent release rather than stay pinned.

c.text: Incorrect. The versionTag bindingType selects the latest deployed version annotated with a specific tag and also requires a versionTag attribute, so it does not pin the child to the parent's deployment.

d.text: Correct. The deployment bindingType pins the call activity to the version of the called process that was deployed alongside the current parent process definition, advancing both together.

a.text: Incorrect. The zeebe:calledDecision element belongs to business rule tasks that evaluate DMN decisions. A call activity uses zeebe:calledElement to reference a child BPMN process.

explanation: A call activity is configured through the zeebe:calledElement extension element. Setting its bindingType to deployment pins the called process to whichever version was deployed together with the currently running parent process definition.

scenario: Vertex Logistics deploys a parent shipping workflow and a child labelling process to Camunda 8.8 together. Every parent process instance that starts in this release must invoke exactly the labelling process version contained in the same deployment, even after 12 deployments add newer child versions to the same cluster.
question: Which bindingType value on the called element pins the child invocation to the version included in the parent deployment?

options.text: Setting bindingType to versionTag without a versionTag attribute on the zeebe:calledElement binds the call activity to the matching parent versionTag at runtime
Setting bindingType to deployment on the zeebe:calledElement of the call activity binds it to the child process version contained in the same parent deployment
Setting bindingType to latest on the zeebe:calledElement of the call activity binds it to whichever child process version is most recently deployed at runtime
Omitting bindingType on the zeebe:calledElement of the call activity binds it to the child process version that lives in the same parent deployment package

b.text: Correct. The Additional resources XML shows bindingType="deployment" on zeebe:calledElement, which ties the call activity to the child process version contained in the same deployment as the parent process definition.

c.text: Incorrect. The latest binding tracks the most recently deployed child process at runtime rather than the version contained in the parent's own deployment, so a later deployment of the child would change which version runs.

a.text: Incorrect. The versionTag binding requires both bindingType="versionTag" and an explicit versionTag attribute on the called element; it does not implicitly track the parent process version.

d.text: Incorrect. When bindingType is omitted on the zeebe:calledElement the binding defaults to latest, not deployment, so omitting the attribute does not lock the call to the parent deployment.

explanation: Pinning a call activity to the version in the parent's own deployment requires setting bindingType="deployment" on the zeebe:calledElement. When bindingType is omitted the default is latest, and versionTag binding requires an explicit versionTag attribute.

scenario: Apex Logistics runs a Camunda 8.8 procurement workflow that reserves inventory across 8 warehouses for each purchase order. After payment rejects 2000 transactions per day, the model must undo the inventory reservations recorded by the earlier completed service task while leaving running activities untouched.
question: Which BPMN element pairs the completed service task with a handler that runs the reversal logic in Camunda 8.8?

options.text: A terminate end event placed after the completed service task is associated with a reversal handler that runs the reversal logic for the same completed task in the scope
A signal boundary event attached to the completed service task is associated with a compensation handler that runs the reversal logic for that task in the same scope
A compensation intermediate throw event attached to the completed service task runs the reversal logic for that task directly without a separate handler activity defined
A compensation boundary event attached to the completed service task is associated with a compensation handler that runs the reversal logic for that task in the same scope

b.text: Incorrect. Signal boundary events react to broadcast signals; they are not the BPMN element that wires the compensation activity to its reversal handler in the compensation pattern documented for Camunda 8.

d.text: Correct. The compensation events page states that a compensation boundary event is attached to the activity to compensate, and the compensation handler associated with that boundary event is the activity that reverts the effects of the original task.

c.text: Incorrect. A compensation intermediate throw event triggers compensation in its scope; it does not itself host the reversal logic and it does not attach to the completed activity as a boundary event.

a.text: Incorrect. Terminate end events end a scope without invoking any reversal handler; the documented compensation pattern wires a boundary event on the activity to an associated compensation handler.

explanation: BPMN compensation pairs the compensation activity, which carries a compensation boundary event, with a compensation handler activity. When a compensation throw event fires in the scope, that handler is what actually performs the reversal of the original task.

scenario: Nimbus Insurance models a Camunda 8.8 claim settlement parent process that uses a call activity to invoke a child reserves process. After a parent compensation throw event fires, the modelling team needs the child reserves work to be reverted as well across 4500 claims per month.
question: Which approach reverts the effects executed inside the child process when the parent compensation throw event activates?

options.text: A compensation boundary event attached to the call activity in the parent process invokes a parent-side compensation handler that performs the child reversal logic instead
A compensation intermediate throw event placed inside the parent embedded subprocess invokes the child process compensation handlers via the call activity calledElement reference
A compensation boundary event attached to the start event of the child process invokes the child compensation handlers whenever the parent compensation throw event fires later
A compensation throw event placed in the child process is triggered automatically when the parent compensation throw event fires inside the parent flow scope of the call activity

d.text: Incorrect. The Compensating embedded subprocesses section states that compensation handlers of child processes are not invoked from the parent; triggering of compensation stops at the call activity boundary.

c.text: Incorrect. The page states triggering of compensation stops at the call activity, so a boundary event inside the child process is never reached by a parent compensation throw event.

b.text: Incorrect. Compensation is broadcast within its scope and stops at the call activity boundary; it does not cross into the child process via the call activity reference.

a.text: Correct. The Compensating embedded subprocesses section directs you to attach a compensation boundary event on the call activity itself so that a parent-side compensation handler can revert the effects of the child process.

explanation: Compensation triggering does not cross call activity boundaries. To revert effects executed inside a child process, attach a compensation boundary event on the parent's call activity and let its parent-side handler perform the reversal logic.

scenario: Forge Bank models a Camunda 8.8 funds transfer process where two specific completed activities must be reverted in a strict order rather than in parallel, covering 750 transactions per day. The team adds compensation intermediate throw events to trigger each reversal one after the other.
question: Which configuration on a compensation intermediate throw event restricts compensation to one specific activity?

options.text: The triggerScope property on the compensation intermediate throw event is set to the id of the activity that should have its compensation handler invoked in the scope
The errorRef property on the compensation intermediate throw event references the activity that has the compensation boundary event in the same scope of the throw event
The activityRef property on the compensation intermediate throw event references the activity that has the compensation boundary event in the same scope of the throw
The compensationCode property on the compensation intermediate throw event is set to the id of the activity that should have its compensation handler invoked in the scope

a.text: Incorrect. The Triggering compensation for a specific activity section names activityRef as the property to use; there is no triggerScope property defined for compensation throw events.

c.text: Correct. The compensation events page states that on a compensation intermediate throw or end event the activityRef property identifies the specific activity to compensate, and that activity must have a compensation boundary event in the same scope.

d.text: Incorrect. compensationCode is not the documented property; the property that targets a specific activity for compensation on the throw event is activityRef in the same scope.

b.text: Incorrect. errorRef is used by error events, not by compensation events; targeted compensation uses activityRef on the compensation throw event in the same scope of the activity.

explanation: By default a compensation throw event broadcasts to all handlers in its scope. To target one activity, set activityRef on the throw event to the id of an activity in the same scope that carries a compensation boundary event.

scenario: Zenith Insurance models a Camunda 8.8 policy renewal process that runs 2000 policies per day. The team wants an error event subprocess inside the main flow to trigger compensation of previously completed activities in the outer scope when renewal fails, without changing the outer process layout.
question: Which placement is valid for a compensation throw event that compensates activities in the outer scope from inside an error event subprocess?

options.text: A compensation intermediate throw event placed at the boundary of the outer scope is invoked from the error event subprocess via a thrown error code reference on activation
A compensation intermediate throw event or compensation end event placed inside the error event subprocess broadcasts compensation in the outer scope of the event subprocess
A compensation intermediate throw event placed in a separate error end event of the outer scope is invoked when the error event subprocess sends a correlation message
A compensation intermediate throw event placed in the outer scope is invoked automatically whenever any error event subprocess inside that same outer scope is triggered later

a.text: Incorrect. The Triggering compensation from an event subprocess section places the compensation throw event itself inside the event subprocess, not at the boundary of the outer scope of the process.

c.text: Incorrect. The page describes the compensation throw event living inside the event subprocess and broadcasting to the outer scope, not signalling another throw event in the outer scope by message.

b.text: Correct. The page states that an interrupting or non-interrupting event subprocess can contain compensation intermediate throw events or a compensation end event, and these can broadcast the compensation within the outer scope of the event subprocess.

d.text: Incorrect. Compensation does not fire automatically when an event subprocess triggers; the event subprocess itself must contain a compensation throw event or a compensation end event for the broadcast.

explanation: Compensation throw events can be hosted directly inside an interrupting or non-interrupting event subprocess. From there they can target a specific activity or broadcast compensation within the outer scope of the event subprocess.

scenario: Ridgeway Insurance issues 1200 policies per day in Camunda 8.8. A service task named Issue Policy reserves a policy number, and when a downstream payment step fails the team needs that reservation reversed before the process continues. The model has a compensation intermediate throw event later in the flow and the team must decide which construct actually performs the reversal.
question: Which BPMN construct performs the reversal of the Issue Policy service task when the compensation throw event fires?

options.text: A compensation intermediate throw event placed after Issue Policy itself releases the reserved policy number when the process reaches that throw event
A boundary error event attached to Issue Policy catches a thrown BPMN error and runs an inline error handler that releases the reserved policy number
A compensation boundary event attached to Issue Policy releases the reserved policy number directly when the compensation throw event is reached in the flow
A compensation handler activity linked to the compensation boundary event on Issue Policy releases the reserved policy number when compensation is triggered

b.text: Incorrect. Boundary error events react to thrown BPMN errors raised while the attached activity is active. They are not what reverses a previously completed task when a compensation throw event later fires.

a.text: Incorrect. The compensation intermediate throw event triggers compensation. It does not itself perform the reversal; the work is delegated to the compensation handler associated with the boundary event.

c.text: Incorrect. The compensation boundary event only associates the handler with the compensation activity. The boundary event itself does not execute the reversal logic when compensation is thrown.

d.text: Correct. The compensation handler activity, linked to the compensation boundary event on the compensation activity, is what runs the reversal logic when the compensation throw event fires.

explanation: Compensation in BPMN combines three parts. The compensation activity performs the original work, a compensation boundary event attached to it associates a handler, and the compensation handler activity is what actually undoes the original work when a compensation throw event fires.

scenario: Helios Bank books 12000 transactions per hour through an embedded subprocess containing booking steps in Camunda 8.8. A compensation throw event lives inside that subprocess to roll back accounting entries when reconciliation fails.
question: Which compensation handlers does the throw event invoke?

options.text: Compensation handlers attached to completed activities anywhere in the parent process scope are invoked from inside the embedded subprocess
Only compensation handlers attached to completed activities inside the same embedded subprocess that contains the throw event are invoked
Compensation handlers attached to active or terminated activities inside the embedded subprocess are invoked together with completed ones
Compensation handlers reached through any called child process via call activity are invoked transitively from the embedded subprocess

b.text: Correct. When a compensation throw event sits inside an embedded subprocess, the engine invokes only the compensation handlers within that subprocess and any nested subprocesses, not handlers outside it.

a.text: Incorrect. A throw event located inside an embedded subprocess does not reach compensation handlers outside that subprocess; handlers in the parent scope are not invoked.

c.text: Incorrect. Compensation handlers are invoked only for activities that completed; if the activity is active or terminated, its compensation handler is not invoked.

d.text: Incorrect. Compensation triggering stops at a call activity, so handlers inside a called child process are never invoked unless a compensation boundary event is attached to the call activity itself.

explanation: Compensation throw events that reside inside an embedded subprocess invoke only the compensation handlers attached to completed activities within that same subprocess and its nested subprocesses. Handlers outside the subprocess and inside child processes reached through call activities are not triggered.

scenario: Orion Insurance models a Camunda 8.8 batch claim reversal process. The claim processing activity is multi-instance over an input collection of 200 claims per batch. The team wants every individual claim reversal to be executed by the compensation handler rather than one combined reversal in the scope.
question: Which configuration causes the compensation handler to run once for each item in the original input collection?

options.text: Mark only the compensation activity as multi-instance, then the compensation handler is automatically invoked once for each completed item from the input collection in the scope
Mark the compensation handler as a parallel gateway with one outgoing flow per item in the original input collection of the compensation activity in the surrounding scope
Mark the compensation handler with a sub-process loop counter that increments once for each completed instance of the compensation activity in the surrounding scope
Mark the compensation handler itself as multi-instance and use the same input collection as the multi-instance compensation activity it reverts inside the same scope

d.text: Correct. The Multi-instance activity as compensation handler section states the handler is invoked only once by default, and to run it per item the handler itself can be marked as multi-instance, optionally reusing the same input collection as the activity.

a.text: Incorrect. Marking the compensation activity as multi-instance is the original setup; the page is clear that the handler must itself be marked multi-instance to run per item.

b.text: Incorrect. A compensation handler is an activity with a compensation marker; a parallel gateway is not an activity and cannot serve as the compensation handler in this pattern.

c.text: Incorrect. There is no documented loop-counter sub-process pattern; the documented per-item behaviour requires the handler to be marked multi-instance over the original input collection.

explanation: By default a multi-instance compensation activity is paired with a handler that runs once. To revert each item, the handler is also marked multi-instance, commonly reusing the same input collection as the compensated activity.

scenario: Helios Insurance models a claims-validation process in Camunda 8.8 where 2000 claims per day can throw a businessRule error. The architect must decide whether an error boundary event can remain attached to a service task without cancelling that activity at runtime.
question: Which interrupting behavior is required for error boundary events and error event subprocesses?

options.text: They are non-interrupting by default and become interrupting only when the activity declares a corresponding error reference at design time
They may be non-interrupting so the attached activity continues running while the catch path triggers a parallel handler branch
They are interrupting only when attached to a service task; for user tasks they remain non-interrupting under the default behavior
They must be interrupting; the process leaves the regular path and follows the catching error event flow once the error is caught

d.text: Correct. Error boundary events and error event subprocesses must be interrupting; the process instance does not continue along the regular path and instead follows the catching error event flow.

b.text: Incorrect. A non-interrupting error boundary event is not a valid configuration in Camunda 8.8; the regular path is always abandoned when the error is caught.

c.text: Incorrect. The interrupting nature of an error catch event does not depend on the type of attached activity; error catch events are always interrupting.

a.text: Incorrect. Error boundary events are interrupting by default and there is no flag that switches them based on whether the activity declares the error reference.

explanation: Camunda 8.8 error boundary events and error event subprocesses are always interrupting. When the error is caught, the host scope is terminated and execution continues along the outgoing sequence flow of the catching error event rather than the regular activity path.

scenario: Aurora Bank models a Camunda 8.8 payment service in which the throw side computes the BPMN error code at runtime from a process variable for 600 transactions per hour, while the catch side defines a fixed code that the modelling team owns in the same scope of the process.
question: Which combination of errorCode definitions on throw and catch events is valid in BPMN error events for Camunda 8.8?

options.text: An errorCode defined as a FEEL expression on a throw event together with another FEEL expression for errorCode on the catching boundary event in the same scope of the model
A static errorCode value on a throw event together with a FEEL expression for errorCode on the catching event subprocess start event in the same scope of the parent
An errorCode defined as a FEEL expression on a throw event together with a static errorCode value on the catching boundary event in the same scope of the process model
A FEEL expression for errorCode on both the throw event and on every catching boundary event so the codes can be computed dynamically at runtime in this Camunda 8.8 model

c.text: Correct. The Defining the error section states that throw events may define errorCode as an expression or a static value, while catch events require a static value, so an expression on throw plus a static value on catch is the documented combination.

a.text: Incorrect. The page states that for error catch events errorCode must be a static value; using a FEEL expression on the catching side is not allowed in Camunda 8.8.

b.text: Incorrect. Although a static value on the throw event is allowed, FEEL expressions for errorCode are not allowed on any error catch event, including catch events inside an event subprocess.

d.text: Incorrect. The catch side of an error definition does not accept FEEL expressions for errorCode; expressions are only supported on the throw side of the BPMN error event.

explanation: BPMN errors in Camunda 8.8 require an errorCode. Throw events may set errorCode from a FEEL expression or a static value, while catch events require a static value. A catch event may also omit the error reference entirely to catch all errors.

scenario: Stellar Insurance runs a Camunda 8.8 claim validation process where a job worker validates 1800 claims per hour. The worker has fetched a job and detected a business rule violation that should follow the modelled error path rather than return as a generic technical incident on the task.
question: Which mechanism is documented for the worker to throw a BPMN error for the currently processed job in Camunda 8.8?

options.text: The worker completes the job with a special errorCode field on the result payload, which the engine interprets as throwing the matching modelled error code on the task
The worker calls a gateway error end event REST endpoint directly so the engine maps the response payload to a modelled error code on the running process instance scope
The worker calls the throw error client command for the active job, which throws the BPMN error and disables the job so other workers cannot activate or complete it again
The worker calls a generic fail job command with the errorCode embedded in the failure message, which the engine then converts into a BPMN error event on the task

b.text: Incorrect. The page describes a throw error client command tied to an active job, not a direct REST call to an error end event endpoint of the gateway in the orchestration cluster.

c.text: Correct. The Throwing the error section states that a worker can use the throw error client command while processing a job; this throws the BPMN error and disables the job so it cannot be activated or completed by other workers.

a.text: Incorrect. There is no errorCode field on the standard complete job payload; the documented path is the dedicated throw error client command on the gRPC and REST job APIs of the cluster.

d.text: Incorrect. Fail job is the retry path for technical failures; the documented BPMN error throw is the throw error client command, not the fail job command in the orchestration cluster API.

explanation: A worker raises a BPMN error from a running job by calling the throw error client command on the orchestration cluster API. The command throws the configured BPMN error and disables the job so it is not re-activated by other workers.

scenario: Helios Payments models a Camunda 8.8 settlement process that handles 8000 transactions per day. Inside one embedded subprocess the team needs to catch a specific business error from a service task while also providing a fallback handler for any other thrown error in the same scope.
question: Which catch event configuration is allowed inside the same embedded subprocess in Camunda 8.8 error events?

options.text: One non-interrupting boundary error event on the service task together with one interrupting boundary error event on the same task for the same matching errorCode value
One boundary event on the service task referencing the specific errorCode together with one catch-all error event subprocess inside the same embedded subprocess scope
Two boundary events that both reference the same specific errorCode, attached to the same service task in the embedded subprocess for redundant routing of the same error
Two catch-all error event subprocesses inside the embedded subprocess, both with no errorRef so that any thrown error matches either one of the two catch-all subprocesses

c.text: Incorrect. The Catching the error section states it is not possible to define multiple error catch events with the same errorCode in a single scope, so two boundary events with the same code are not allowed.

d.text: Incorrect. The page states it is not permitted to have multiple error catch-all events in a single scope, so two catch-all event subprocesses cannot coexist inside the same embedded subprocess.

b.text: Correct. The Catching the error section allows a specific errorCode catch event and an error catch-all event to coexist in the same scope; when both could match, the specific errorCode catch event is prioritized.

a.text: Incorrect. The page states that error boundary events and error event subprocesses must be interrupting, so a non-interrupting boundary error event is not a supported configuration in Camunda 8.8.

explanation: In a single scope you may pair one error catch event for a specific errorCode with one error catch-all event; the specific match wins when both could fire. All error boundary events and error event subprocesses must be interrupting.

scenario: Quanta SaaS runs a Camunda 8.8 billing process that processes 25000 invoices per day. A small share of unhandled BPMN errors raises incidents, and the operations team wants to know which incidents can be resolved through the UI and which ones cannot in the same process model.
question: Which statement about incidents raised by unhandled BPMN errors in Camunda 8.8 matches the documentation?

options.text: All unhandled error incidents can be resolved through the operations UI, which ignores the error and continues the process from the next sequence flow in the same scope
An unhandled error incident attached to a task can be resolved in the UI and the job is then re-enabled, while an incident attached to an error end event cannot be resolved by a user
An unhandled error incident attached to an error end event can be resolved through the operations UI by editing the process variable that defined the errorCode at runtime
An unhandled error incident attached to a task and an incident attached to an error end event are both resolved by re-deploying a new process version that adds a catch event

a.text: Incorrect. The Unhandled errors section explicitly states that the incident attached to an error end event cannot be resolved by a user, so not every unhandled error incident is resolvable from the operations UI.

c.text: Incorrect. Editing a process variable does not resolve the incident on an error end event; the page states this incident cannot be resolved by a user because the failure is in the process model itself.

d.text: Incorrect. The documented resolution path for the task-attached incident is to resolve it directly, which ignores the error and re-enables the job; re-deploying a new version is not the resolution path on this page.

b.text: Correct. The Unhandled errors section states that resolving the incident attached to a task ignores the error and re-enables the job for activation, while an incident attached to an error end event cannot be resolved by a user.

explanation: Unhandled errors raise incidents on the originating element. For a task-attached incident, resolving it ignores the error and re-enables the job. For an incident on an error end event, the failure is in the process model and a user cannot resolve the incident.

scenario: Apex Fintech is reviewing the BPMN XML of a Camunda 8.8 payment process that must catch an unknown error code from a job worker for 4500 transactions per hour. The reviewer focuses on the boundary event XML attached to the collect-money task in the same scope of the model.
question: Which XML structure correctly defines an error catch-all boundary event on the collect-money task in Camunda 8.8?

options.text: A boundaryEvent on collect-money with a generic eventDefinition element and a special errorCode attribute set to the wildcard value of asterisk on the bpmn:error element
A boundaryEvent on collect-money with an errorEventDefinition that has no errorRef attribute, so the boundary event catches every thrown error code reaching this scope
A boundaryEvent on collect-money with an errorEventDefinition referencing a bpmn:error whose errorCode attribute is the wildcard value of asterisk in the diagram
A boundaryEvent on collect-money with an errorEventDefinition that references an errorRef pointing to a bpmn:error definition with an empty errorCode attribute on the error

d.text: Incorrect. The Additional resources XML shows the catch-all variant with an errorEventDefinition that has no errorRef at all; an errorRef pointing at an empty errorCode is not the documented pattern in BPMN XML.

b.text: Correct. The Additional resources section shows an error catch-all boundary event whose errorEventDefinition element is present without any errorRef attribute, so it catches every thrown error in scope of the boundary.

a.text: Incorrect. The XML uses bpmn:errorEventDefinition, not a generic eventDefinition with a wildcard errorCode attribute; catch-all behaviour comes from omitting errorRef on the errorEventDefinition.

c.text: Incorrect. The page defines catch-all behaviour by omitting errorRef on the errorEventDefinition, not by using an asterisk errorCode on a referenced bpmn:error in the BPMN XML representation.

explanation: An error catch-all boundary event in BPMN XML is expressed by an errorEventDefinition with no errorRef attribute attached to the task via attachedToRef. The catch event then matches any thrown error within its scope hierarchy.

scenario: Ridge Insurance models a Camunda 8.8 claim review process where 1500 claims per day might exceed agreed handling times. The throw side wants to compute the escalationCode at runtime from a process variable, while the catch side needs deterministic routing inside the same parent flow scope.
question: Which escalationCode configuration is allowed for escalation events in this Camunda 8.8 model?

options.text: A FEEL expression for escalationCode on the throw event together with a static escalationCode on the catching boundary event in the same parent flow scope of the process
A FEEL expression for escalationCode on the throw event together with multiple catch-all escalation boundary events on the same parent flow scope of the process at runtime
A FEEL expression for escalationCode on both the throw event and on every catching boundary event so the codes can vary per process instance at runtime in the same scope
A static escalationCode on the throw event together with a FEEL expression for escalationCode on the catching boundary event in the same parent flow scope of the process

c.text: Incorrect. The Defining an escalation section states that for escalation catch events escalationCode must be a static value, so a FEEL expression on the catch event is not allowed in this scope.

d.text: Incorrect. The page only allows static values on catch events; a FEEL expression on the catch side of escalationCode is not supported in any escalation catch event configuration.

a.text: Correct. The Defining an escalation section states that throw events may define escalationCode as an expression or a static value, while catch events must use a static value, so this combination is documented and allowed.

b.text: Incorrect. The escalation events page states that multiple catch-all escalation events in a single scope are not permitted, regardless of how escalationCode is defined on the throw side of the event.

explanation: An escalation event needs an escalationCode. Throw events may set it from a FEEL expression or a static value, while catch events require a static value. A catch event may omit the escalation reference entirely to catch every escalation in its scope.

scenario: Meridian Insurance models a Camunda 8.8 underwriting process where a long-running activity might raise an escalation that the parent scope must catch. The team handles 2400 claims per day and asks which catching configuration follows the documentation inside the same parent flow scope.
question: Which escalation catch configuration is documented as allowed within the same parent flow scope in Camunda 8.8?

options.text: A non-interrupting escalation catch event together with an incident automatically raised by the engine whenever no escalation catch event matches the code in the same scope
Two escalation boundary events on the same activity that both reference an escalation with the same escalationCode for redundant routing in the same parent flow scope
Two escalation catch-all event subprocesses in the same parent flow scope so both react to every escalation that reaches the scope from a child activity at runtime
One escalation catch event for a specific escalationCode together with one escalation catch-all event in the same parent flow scope, where the specific code wins on match

c.text: Incorrect. The Catching the escalation section states it is not permitted to have multiple escalation catch-all events in a single scope, so two catch-all event subprocesses cannot coexist there in the same parent.

b.text: Incorrect. The page states it is not possible to define multiple escalation catch events with the same escalationCode in a single scope, so two boundary events with the same code are not allowed in the same parent.

a.text: Incorrect. The page explicitly states that unlike error events, an uncaught escalation does not raise an incident; the process simply continues without escalating in the same parent flow scope.

d.text: Correct. The Catching the escalation section allows a specific escalationCode catch event and an escalation catch-all event in the same scope; when both could match, the catch event referencing the matching escalationCode is prioritized.

explanation: Escalations are caught by boundary events or event subprocesses in the nearest parent flow scope. Within one scope you may pair one specific escalationCode catch with one catch-all; the specific code wins. If nothing matches, no incident is raised and the process continues.

scenario: Sable Insurance reviews the BPMN XML of a Camunda 8.8 underwriting process. The reviewer must distinguish a non-interrupting escalation boundary event from an interrupting one for an activity processing 1800 claims per day in the same scope of the process model file.
question: Which XML detail in the Additional resources section of escalation events marks a boundary escalation event as non-interrupting?

options.text: The boundaryEvent element on the activity sets cancelActivity to false in addition to carrying an escalationEventDefinition with the escalationRef attribute on the boundary
The boundaryEvent element on the activity uses a dashed border attribute on its diagram representation and a static escalationCode on the bpmn:escalation element of the model
The bpmn:escalation element referenced by the boundary event sets a special non-interrupting attribute alongside the escalationCode attribute value on the same element of the model
The escalationEventDefinition element on the boundary event carries an interrupting attribute set to false along with the usual escalationRef attribute on the boundary in the model

a.text: Correct. The XML examples in Additional resources show a non-interrupting escalation boundary event by setting cancelActivity="false" on bpmn:boundaryEvent together with an escalationEventDefinition that has an escalationRef in the model.

d.text: Incorrect. There is no interrupting attribute on escalationEventDefinition in the documented XML; the boundary event uses cancelActivity on bpmn:boundaryEvent to indicate non-interrupting behaviour in the model.

c.text: Incorrect. The non-interrupting flag lives on the boundary event element via cancelActivity, not on the bpmn:escalation referenced element in the BPMN XML representation of the model.

b.text: Incorrect. The visual dashed border is a diagram convention, not a serialised non-interrupting attribute on the boundary event in the BPMN XML examples for escalation events of Camunda 8.8.

explanation: In the BPMN XML for escalation events, a non-interrupting escalation boundary event is expressed by cancelActivity="false" on the bpmn:boundaryEvent attached to the activity, while still carrying an escalationEventDefinition that references a bpmn:escalation.

scenario: Aurora airline routes 9000 events per day through a booking process in Camunda 8.8. An escalation throw event must raise an escalationCode that depends on the fare class detected at runtime so that different catch handlers can be triggered downstream.
question: Which value forms does Camunda 8.8 accept for the escalationCode on an escalation throw event?

options.text: A static escalationCode value only; FEEL expressions cannot be configured for the escalationCode on the throw event in this version
A FEEL expression only; static escalationCode values are accepted only on the escalation catch event reference of the enclosing scope
A static value combined with a FEEL expression that returns a fallback escalationCode if the static value cannot be resolved at runtime
A static value or a FEEL expression; the expression is evaluated when the throw event is reached and the result is used to throw the escalation

a.text: Incorrect. Camunda 8.8 allows the escalationCode on a throw event to be defined as a FEEL expression in addition to a static value.

b.text: Incorrect. Static values are valid on escalation throw events as well as on catch events; FEEL expressions are not the only accepted form on the throw side.

d.text: Correct. An escalation throw event accepts the escalationCode as either a static value or a FEEL expression; the expression is evaluated when the event is reached and the result is used to throw the escalation.

c.text: Incorrect. There is no fallback combination of a static value with a FEEL expression on the throw event; the escalationCode is configured as one form.

explanation: An escalation throw event in Camunda 8.8 accepts the escalationCode either as a static value or as a FEEL expression. When configured as an expression, the engine evaluates it once the event is reached and uses the result to throw the escalation, while catch events require a static value.

scenario: Zenith logistics processes 1500 shipments per hour in Camunda 8.8. A throw escalation with code DELAY may be raised by a service task even when no parent scope models a matching catch event, and operations want to confirm the engine behavior in that situation.
question: What does the Camunda 8.8 engine do when no escalation catch event matches a thrown escalationCode?

options.text: An incident is raised in Operate against the throwing element so an operator can resolve the unmatched escalation manually before the flow continues
The escalation is not caught and no incident is raised; the process simply continues without escalating along the regular outgoing flow of the throw event
The process instance is terminated immediately because an unmatched escalation behaves the same way as an unmatched BPMN error at the root scope
The engine retries the throwing escalation event a configurable number of attempts before raising an incident on the throw event itself

a.text: Incorrect. Unlike error events, an unmatched escalation does not raise an incident in Camunda 8.8; the process is not blocked by the throwing element.

c.text: Incorrect. An unmatched escalation does not terminate the process instance; only errors that go uncaught propagate up to the instance level for handling.

d.text: Incorrect. The engine does not retry escalation throw events; if no matching catch event exists in any parent scope the escalation is simply uncaught.

b.text: Correct. When no escalation catch event matches the thrown escalationCode, the escalation is not caught and no incident is raised; the process continues without escalating along the regular flow.

explanation: Unmatched escalations differ from unmatched errors in Camunda 8.8. If no catch event matches the thrown escalationCode in any parent scope, the escalation is silently uncaught, no incident is raised, and the process continues along its regular outgoing flow from the throw event.

scenario: Zenith Logistics handles 4000 shipments per day in Camunda 8.8. The team attaches a boundary escalation event to a fulfillment subprocess that should react to any escalation raised inside that scope, not just one specific escalation code, so the carrier can intervene whenever an internal escalation is thrown.
question: Which XML configuration turns the boundary escalation event into a catch-all that handles every escalation from the attached activity?

options.text: The boundary event sets cancelActivity to true so it catches every escalation regardless of escalation code from the scope.
The boundary event omits the escalationRef on the escalationEventDefinition so it catches any escalation thrown from the activity.
The boundary event sets escalationRef on the escalationEventDefinition so it catches only the matching escalation code.
The boundary event references a wildcard escalation element so it catches any escalation thrown from the attached activity.

c.text: Incorrect. Setting escalationRef binds the boundary event to one specific escalation element and its code, so it catches only that escalation rather than every one thrown by the attached activity.

b.text: Correct. The documented catch-all boundary escalation event is modelled by an escalationEventDefinition with no escalationRef attribute, so it reacts to any escalation thrown inside the attached activity scope.

a.text: Incorrect. The cancelActivity attribute decides whether the boundary event interrupts the activity; it does not change which escalations are matched on the escalationEventDefinition.

d.text: Incorrect. The bpmn:escalation element identifies a single named escalation by code; there is no wildcard escalation reference, so this is not how a catch-all boundary event is expressed.

explanation: An escalation boundary catch-all event is built by attaching an escalationEventDefinition without an escalationRef. With no reference set, the boundary event matches any escalation thrown from inside the attached activity scope.

scenario: Tessera Insurance models a Camunda 8.8 claim handling process where a sub-step throws an escalation visible to the parent scope without halting the main path. The flow processes 3000 claims per day and must keep the activities after the throw point alive at runtime.
question: How does execution at the location of the escalation throw event behave in Camunda 8.8 once the escalation is caught in a higher flow scope?

options.text: The outgoing sequence flow of the throw event is taken only when the catching event in the higher scope is configured as a non-interrupting catch event for the same code
The outgoing sequence flow of the throw event is also taken when an escalation is caught, with execution continuing at the throwing location alongside the catching higher flow scope
The outgoing sequence flow of the throw event is taken only if no catch event in a higher flow scope reacts to the escalationCode thrown by the throw event in this scope
The outgoing sequence flow of the throw event is skipped, with escalations transferring execution into the higher catching scope instead of the throwing scope of the throw event

c.text: Incorrect. The escalation events page states escalations are non-critical and execution continues at the location of the throw; the outgoing flow is taken whether or not the escalation is caught in a higher scope.

b.text: Correct. The escalation events overview states that unlike an error, an escalation event is non-critical and execution continues at the location of throwing, so the outgoing sequence flow of the throw event is also taken.

d.text: Incorrect. Escalations do not transfer execution into the catching scope; the throwing scope keeps going, and the catching scope takes its own outgoing flow as well in the same instance.

a.text: Incorrect. The outgoing flow of the throw is taken whenever the escalation is non-critical, independent of whether the catch event in the higher scope is interrupting or non-interrupting at runtime.

explanation: An escalation event is non-critical: when an escalation is thrown, the catch event in a higher flow scope reacts to it while execution at the throwing location continues. Both the throw event and the catch event take their outgoing sequence flows.

scenario: Helios Insurance runs 8000 claims per day through a Camunda 8.8 claim-handling subprocess. Inside that subprocess, a cancellation flow must start whenever a CancelClaim message arrives mid-flight, so the modeller adds an event subprocess to react to that message while the parent scope is active.
question: Which start event configuration is required for the event subprocess that handles the CancelClaim message?

options.text: An event subprocess has exactly one start event of type timer, message, error, signal, or escalation, with no incoming sequence flow.
An event subprocess has one start event of type conditional or compensation, depending on whether it should be interrupting or non-interrupting.
An event subprocess has exactly one start event of type none and uses incoming sequence flows to wire it to the parent scope.
An event subprocess has one start event for each supported event type so it can be triggered from any of the listed sources.

c.text: Incorrect. The documentation lists timer, message, error, signal, and escalation as the supported start event types for an event subprocess; none is not part of that set, and the start event is triggered by its event rather than by sequence flows.

a.text: Correct. An event subprocess must have exactly one start event, and that start event must be one of the documented types: timer, message, error, signal, or escalation, triggered when the containing scope is active.

d.text: Incorrect. The documentation requires exactly one start event in an event subprocess, not one per supported type; the chosen type matches the trigger the subprocess should react to.

b.text: Incorrect. The documented set of start event types for an event subprocess is timer, message, error, signal, and escalation; conditional and compensation start events are not part of that set.

explanation: An event subprocess must contain exactly one start event chosen from the documented set: timer, message, error, signal, or escalation. The solid or dashed border of that start event then indicates whether the event subprocess is interrupting or non-interrupting.

scenario: Granite Insurance models a Camunda 8.8 claim review process whose embedded event subprocess must read process variables of the containing claim scope and add its own working variables that should not leak when the subprocess completes, across 900 claims per day in the same scope.
question: Which variable behaviour applies to an event subprocess in its containing scope in Camunda 8.8?

options.text: The event subprocess can only read local variables of the containing scope but it cannot modify them, and any input mappings define global variables visible to the outer scope at runtime
The event subprocess can create new global variables in the containing scope via output mappings on the start event, and its own local variables are propagated by default to the containing scope
The event subprocess has its own isolated variable scope and cannot read variables of the containing scope unless explicit signal correlation copies values into it on activation at runtime
The event subprocess can access and modify local variables of the containing scope, and input mappings create local variables only visible inside the event subprocess that disappear when it completes

a.text: Incorrect. The Variables section states the event subprocess can both access and modify local variables of its containing scope, and input mappings create variables local to the event subprocess in this scope.

d.text: Correct. The Variables section states the event subprocess is inside the scope, so it can read and modify the containing scope's local variables, and input mappings create new local variables only visible inside the event subprocess.

b.text: Incorrect. The page states that by default the event subprocess's local variables are not propagated; output mappings must be defined on the event subprocess to propagate values to the containing scope at completion.

c.text: Incorrect. The page contrasts the event subprocess with a boundary event by stating the event subprocess is inside the scope and can therefore access all local variables without explicit correlation copying values into it.

explanation: Event subprocesses sit inside the containing scope, so they can read and modify its local variables directly. Input mappings define variables local to the event subprocess; by default these are removed on completion, and output mappings are required to propagate values back to the containing scope.

scenario: Catalyst Insurance models a Camunda 8.8 claim routing process that evaluates 6000 claims per day at an exclusive gateway with four outgoing flows. The reviewer must understand how the engine evaluates the conditionExpressions and the role of the default flow under Camunda 8.8 semantics in this scope.
question: How does an exclusive gateway in Camunda 8.8 select an outgoing flow when multiple conditionExpressions could be true at once?

options.text: The gateway evaluates conditionExpressions in the order they appear in the BPMN XML, takes the first true one, and falls back to the default flow only when no condition is fulfilled in scope
The gateway evaluates conditionExpressions in the order they appear in the BPMN XML and takes the first true one, but it must always have a default flow defined to avoid creating an incident
The gateway evaluates every conditionExpression in parallel and picks the outgoing flow whose condition evaluates to true with the highest priority attribute on the outgoing sequence flow
The gateway evaluates conditionExpressions in alphabetical order of their sequence flow ids and takes the first true one, then takes the default flow when no condition is fulfilled at runtime

c.text: Incorrect. The exclusive gateway page does not describe parallel evaluation or a priority attribute on sequence flows; evaluation follows the order of conditions in the BPMN XML of the model.

b.text: Incorrect. The default flow is optional from the perspective of evaluation order; without it an incident is raised only when no condition matches, and the page does not require a default flow to always be present.

a.text: Correct. The Exclusive gateways page states conditionExpressions are evaluated in the order they are defined in the BPMN XML, the first satisfied condition wins, and the default flow is taken only when no condition is fulfilled.

d.text: Incorrect. The page specifies BPMN XML order of definition, not alphabetical order of sequence flow ids, as the evaluation order on the exclusive gateway in the same scope of the model.

explanation: An exclusive gateway evaluates conditionExpressions in the order they appear in the BPMN XML and takes the first flow whose condition is true. If no condition matches, the gateway takes its default flow; if there is no default flow either, an incident is raised.

scenario: Aurora Bank routes 5000 transactions per hour through an exclusive gateway in Camunda 8.8. The XML must declare a sequence flow that the engine takes whenever no other outgoing condition expression evaluates to true, so that no incident is raised on a normal path.
question: Which XML attribute on bpmn:exclusiveGateway designates the fallback sequence flow when no condition evaluates to true?

options.text: The default attribute on bpmn:exclusiveGateway names the sequence flow id that is taken when no other condition expression evaluates to true
The fallbackFlow attribute on bpmn:exclusiveGateway points to the sequence flow id that runs once each other path has been visited at least once
The else attribute on bpmn:exclusiveGateway references the sequence flow id that runs whenever the gateway fails to evaluate a condition
The conditionExpression attribute on bpmn:exclusiveGateway names the sequence flow id that runs when every other condition expression returns false

a.text: Correct. The exclusive gateway XML carries a default attribute whose value is the id of the sequence flow taken when no other outgoing conditionExpression evaluates to true, as shown in the documented snippet.

d.text: Incorrect. The conditionExpression lives on individual outgoing sequence flows, not on the gateway element itself, and it defines a single path condition rather than naming a fallback.

b.text: Incorrect. There is no fallbackFlow attribute in the documented exclusive gateway XML; the documented mechanism for a fallback is the gateway's default attribute pointing at one sequence flow id.

c.text: Incorrect. The documented XML uses default for the fallback sequence flow id; else is only a sample sequence flow id and not a reserved attribute name.

explanation: The documented XML representation of an exclusive gateway uses a default attribute on the gateway whose value is the id of the sequence flow taken when no outgoing conditionExpression evaluates to true.

scenario: Beacon Insurance routes 4000 claims per day through a Camunda 8.8 process. Each claim must follow exactly one outgoing branch chosen from the value of a routing variable, so the modeling team needs the BPMN gateway category that selects a single data-driven path.
question: Which gateway type routes the token along exactly one outgoing sequence flow based on data?

options.text: An inclusive gateway routes the token along zero or many outgoing sequence flows depending on which condition expressions evaluate to true
An event-based gateway routes the token by waiting for whichever catching event reference receives an external trigger first in time
A parallel gateway routes the token by generating tokens on every outgoing sequence flow regardless of any data value attached to the flows
An exclusive gateway routes the token along exactly one outgoing sequence flow chosen by evaluating the data-driven conditions on the flows

c.text: Incorrect. A parallel gateway generates new tokens on every outgoing path so several flows run concurrently, which is the opposite of selecting a single data-driven branch.

d.text: Correct. The exclusive gateway chooses one outgoing sequence flow out of many based on data, which matches the documented role of the BPMN exclusive gateway in Camunda 8.

a.text: Incorrect. An inclusive gateway may take several outgoing flows at once whenever multiple conditions hold, so it does not guarantee exactly one branch.

b.text: Incorrect. An event-based gateway routes on event arrival, not on a data condition, so the selected branch depends on external events rather than process data.

explanation: The documented gateways overview names the exclusive gateway as the element that chooses one sequence flow out of many based on data, in contrast to the parallel, inclusive, and event-based variants.

scenario: Granite Healthcare orchestrates 3500 claims per day with a Camunda 8.8 process whose branches fan out through a diverging inclusive gateway and later rejoin at a converging inclusive gateway. The clinical team needs to know when the join is considered complete so the next activity runs only once.
question: When does a converging inclusive gateway complete and continue the outgoing flow?

options.text: The converging inclusive gateway completes when the first incoming sequence flow arrives and immediately cancels every other branch that has not yet reached the gateway
The converging inclusive gateway completes when only one incoming sequence flow has been taken and the rest of the incoming flows are still pending in other branches
The converging inclusive gateway completes when every incoming sequence flow has been taken at least once or no active flow node can still reach the gateway along an untaken path
The converging inclusive gateway completes when a configurable timer attached to the gateway expires regardless of how many incoming sequence flows have arrived

b.text: Incorrect. A single incoming flow is not enough to close the join; the converging inclusive gateway must account for every incoming path that can still be reached by an active flow node.

a.text: Incorrect. The converging inclusive gateway does not cancel sibling branches when one flow arrives first; it waits for the remaining reachable paths to complete or for them to become unreachable.

c.text: Correct. The documented join rule is that the gateway completes when all incoming sequence flows have been taken at least once or when no active flow node can still reach it along an untaken incoming path.

d.text: Incorrect. The converging inclusive gateway does not own a timer; completion is driven entirely by token arrival and reachability of the remaining incoming sequence flows.

explanation: The documented behavior of a converging inclusive gateway is that it completes either when every incoming sequence flow has been taken at least once, or when no active flow node can still reach it through an untaken incoming path.
