d.text: Correct. The docs show that for a non-job incident, such as a condition expression that did not return a boolean value, the client calls newSetVariablesCommand on the element instance and then newResolveIncidentCommand on the incident key.

explanation: For incidents created during process execution that are not job-related, the docs note the cause is usually a process instance variable, for example a condition expression that did not return a boolean. The fix is to update the variables on the element instance and mark the incident as resolved, after which the process instance continues.

scenario: Keystone Bank correlates 7500 payment confirmation messages per hour to running process instances in Camunda 8.8 using a FEEL expression =orderId on a message catch event. An operator updates orderId on one process instance 2 hours after the subscription was opened and expects the new value to take effect on the open subscription.
question: What happens to an open message subscription on a catch event when the referenced correlation variable is updated on the same process instance?

options.text: The subscription is closed immediately on the variable update and a fresh subscription is opened with the new correlation key on the next poll
The subscription re-evaluates the correlation key expression on each variable update and rebinds the subscription to the new correlation key
The subscription keeps the original correlation key value computed at activation and ignores subsequent updates to the referenced variable
The subscription is correlated to every process instance whose current variables match the updated correlation key on each variable change

a.text: Incorrect. The documented behavior is that a subscription is closed only when the corresponding element or its scope is left, not when the referenced variable is updated on the instance.

b.text: Incorrect. The message name and correlation key expressions are evaluated on activating the message catch event; the subscription is not re-evaluated when the variable changes later.

d.text: Incorrect. Correlation matches a published message to an open subscription by message name and correlation key; updating a variable does not retroactively rebind any subscriptions to other instances.

c.text: Correct. The docs state that after a subscription is opened, it is not updated when the referenced process variable is changed; the subscription retains the correlation key from activation.

explanation: Message subscriptions are based on the message name and the correlation key evaluated when the catch event activates. Once opened, the subscription holds those evaluation results and is not updated when the referenced process variable later changes. The subscription closes only when the element or its scope is left.

scenario: Catalyst Payments publishes 3000 settlement messages per hour in Camunda 8.8 with a default time-to-live of 3600000 ms. For one synchronous integration the team wants the message to be correlated only if a matching subscription already exists, otherwise the message must be dropped right away without sitting in the buffer.
question: What does setting the timeToLive on a published message to zero do in Camunda 8?

options.text: It disables buffering of the published message so it is discarded immediately if no proper subscription is open at publish time
It schedules a delayed publication so the broker holds the message for the default buffering window before any correlation attempt
It enables buffering indefinitely so the published message stays in the buffer until any matching subscription opens later for it
It promotes the published message to a priority lane so the broker correlates it ahead of any earlier published matching messages

c.text: Incorrect. A TTL of zero turns off buffering; indefinite buffering is not the documented behavior. Buffering only applies while the TTL is positive, and the message is discarded once the TTL elapses or if no subscription is open.

a.text: Correct. The docs state buffering is disabled when TTL is set to zero, and if no proper subscription is open the message is discarded. There is no waiting window for a future subscription to open.

b.text: Incorrect. timeToLive controls how long the message is buffered for correlation, not a delayed publication time. The broker does not delay publishing the message.

d.text: Incorrect. timeToLive does not affect ordering or priority; ordering among buffered messages follows FIFO based on publish time, not on the TTL value.

explanation: A message TTL specifies how long the broker buffers the message for correlation. Setting TTL to zero disables buffering, so the message is correlated only to a subscription already open at publish time; otherwise it is discarded. Buffered messages match subscriptions in FIFO order based on publish time.

scenario: Orion Logistics publishes a shipment-arrived message that is awaited by both an inbound-handling process and a billing process in Camunda 8.8. Each process has 1 open subscription for the message name and correlationKey order-9001 across 12 instances of each definition on the cluster.
question: How is a single published message correlated when subscriptions are open across two different BPMN process IDs?

options.text: The message is correlated to only one subscription chosen by the cluster regardless of how many distinct BPMN process IDs subscribe to it
The message is correlated to only the newest BPMN process ID version on the cluster, so subscriptions from older deployed versions are skipped
The message is correlated to none of the subscriptions when two different BPMN process IDs both subscribe to the same message at the same time
The message is correlated to all matching subscriptions across the different BPMN process IDs that have an open subscription on the cluster

a.text: Incorrect. Single-correlation applies within a BPMN process ID across its versions; across different process IDs the docs state the message is correlated to all matching subscriptions.

b.text: Incorrect. The docs do not select by newest version across different process IDs; the once-per-process rule is scoped to one BPMN process ID across its versions.

d.text: Correct. When subscriptions are opened for different processes, the docs state the message is correlated to all the subscriptions, one per BPMN process ID.

c.text: Incorrect. The docs do not discard the message when two process IDs subscribe; cross-process correlation is documented as allowed and applied to each matching subscription.

explanation: Message cardinality is scoped per BPMN process ID. Within one process ID across all versions the message is correlated only once, even if multiple subscriptions are open. When subscriptions exist for different processes, the message is correlated to all the matching subscriptions, one per process ID.

scenario: Aurora Insurance, an insurer running 3000 claims through Camunda 8.8, correlates payout-ready messages to long-running claim instances. Each publish sets a correlationKey of the claim number, leaves the messageId unset, and uses a TTL of 24 hours. The receiver is an intermediate message catch event inside the claim instance.
question: How does the broker treat such a publish (correlationKey set, no messageId, TTL greater than 0) against an intermediate message catch event?

options.text: The message is dropped if no subscription is currently active because intermediate catch events ignore TTL and only consume messages that are already waiting at the subscription point
The message is correlated during its lifetime if a matching subscription is active, and any new equal message that arrives in the meantime is buffered alongside it until the lifetime ends
The message is correlated only at the exact moment the subscription first becomes active, with no buffering and no waiting, regardless of the configured TTL value on the publish call
The message creates a new process instance keyed on the correlationKey if no instance is active, and otherwise buffers against that instance until the configured TTL window expires

a.text: Incorrect. The correlation overview table is explicit that with TTL greater than zero the message waits in the buffer for its lifetime, so a subscription that activates later within that window can still consume it.

d.text: Incorrect. Only message start events create new process instances; intermediate catch events correlate into existing instances and never start a new one in any row of the overview table.

b.text: Correct. The overview table row for correlation key set, no messageId, TTL greater than 0, intermediate event states that the message is correlated during its lifetime if a matching subscription is active and that new equal messages are buffered.

c.text: Incorrect. TTL is exactly the lifetime during which the broker holds the message and waits for a matching subscription; treating it as a single instantaneous match would contradict the documented overview behavior.

explanation: The messages overview table maps every combination of correlation key, message ID, and TTL onto either a start event or an intermediate event behavior. With a correlation key set, no message ID, TTL greater than zero, and an intermediate event, the broker correlates during the lifetime and buffers any new equal messages.

scenario: Stellar Bank publishes 'fraud_review' messages to a Camunda 8.8 message start event for fraud-suspect onboarding flows. Each publish sets a correlationKey of the customer id, sets a messageId, and uses a TTL of 10 minutes (PT10M). Roughly 25% of publishes are retries that carry the same name, correlation key, and message id triple.
question: Which combination of conditions, per the correlation overview table, must hold for the broker to start a new process instance from such a publish?

options.text: An instance with that correlationKey is currently active, and no other equal message with the same name and messageId has ever been published since the process was deployed
No instance with that correlationKey is active at the moment of the publish, and the messageId value is unique across the cluster regardless of message name or correlationKey value
No instance with that correlationKey set at start is active during the lifetime of the message, and there is no equal message already buffered for that name, correlationKey, and messageId
An instance with that correlationKey was active at some point during the last lifetime window, and the buffer holds no message of the same name regardless of its messageId value

c.text: Correct. The overview table row for correlationKey set, messageId set, TTL greater than 0, and a start-event receiver states that a new instance is started only when no instance with that correlation key set at start is active during the lifetime and no equal message is already in the buffer.

b.text: Incorrect. Uniqueness is defined by the triple of name, correlationKey, and messageId together; the messageId alone is not evaluated as globally unique across the cluster.

d.text: Incorrect. The required condition is the absence of an active instance with that correlationKey during the lifetime, not the past presence of one, and the buffer check also considers the messageId.

a.text: Incorrect. The table requires the absence, not the presence, of an active instance with that correlationKey, and the buffer comparison is against the current buffer, not the full deployment history.

explanation: For a start-event receiver with correlationKey set, messageId set, and TTL greater than zero, the overview table requires two conditions: no instance with that correlation key set at start is active during the lifetime, and no equal message (same name, correlationKey, and messageId) is currently buffered. Only then is a new instance started.

scenario: Vertex Logistics aggregates package-scan events per shipment in Camunda 8.8. For one delivery the team expects 6 scan messages within 12 hours, and they want exactly one process instance per shipment that absorbs every scan and ends only after the last scan has been collected and processed downstream.
question: Which message pattern matches publishing messages with a TTL greater than zero and a correlationKey that groups events per entity?

options.text: The non-interrupting boundary pattern, where the engine spawns a child token for each arriving scan and the parent token closes when the configured boundary timer event eventually expires
The single-instance pattern, in which TTL of 0 and a correlation key identifying the entity guarantee that duplicate publishes are discarded while the original process instance is still running
The message aggregator pattern, where the first message creates a process instance and following messages with the same correlation key correlate into that instance during its lifetime
The buffer-reset pattern, in which every new message with the same name clears earlier buffered messages and replays them against the receiver in their original chronological order

b.text: Incorrect. The single-instance pattern is defined with TTL equal to zero and is aimed at creating exactly one instance per correlation key by discarding later equal publishes, not at aggregating multiple distinct events into one instance.

a.text: Incorrect. The messages page does not document a non-interrupting boundary aggregation pattern; aggregation across messages is achieved through the correlationKey and a TTL greater than zero.

c.text: Correct. The message aggregator pattern publishes messages with TTL greater than zero and a correlationKey grouping per entity; the first message creates a new process instance and following messages correlate into that instance during its lifetime.

d.text: Incorrect. There is no documented buffer-reset pattern; the message buffer rejects equal incoming messages by uniqueness rules rather than clearing or replaying earlier ones.

explanation: Among the documented message patterns, the aggregator combines a correlationKey that groups events per entity with TTL greater than zero. The first message creates a process instance and later messages with the same correlation key correlate into that instance during its lifetime, which is the textbook behavior for batching events per shipment.

scenario: Tessera Insurance, an insurer running 800 active claim instances on Camunda 8.8, calls the message correlation endpoint from a synchronous claims widget. The team needs to know whether a 'claim_filed' publish reached either a message start event or an intermediate catch event, and which process instance accepted it, before the widget renders its next screen.
question: Which behavior is documented for the message correlation endpoint compared to the publish message endpoint?

options.text: The endpoint correlates synchronously without buffering, returns a single process instance key on success, and prioritizes creating a new instance via a message start event over correlation with existing instances

The endpoint correlates synchronously without buffering, returns the full list of every process instance key that the message reached, and never starts a new process instance from a message start event
The endpoint buffers the message for up to the configured TTL and asynchronously returns every process instance key whose subscription correlated to the message within that lifetime window
The endpoint behaves like the publish endpoint except it returns 204 No Content when no subscription was found and otherwise echoes back the correlationKey value that was used for routing

c.text: Incorrect. The correlation endpoint does not support message buffering; any message published through it is either immediately correlated or not at all, because of the synchronous nature of requiring a response.

a.text: Correct. The correlation endpoint runs synchronously, does not buffer, returns one process instance key on success, and the response always prioritizes the creation of a new process instance via a message start event over correlation with an existing instance.

b.text: Incorrect. The response carries only one process instance key even when the message correlated to multiple instances, and start-event creation is explicitly prioritized rather than disabled by the endpoint.

d.text: Incorrect. The endpoint returns a process instance key on success rather than a 204 with the correlationKey; its response shape is documented to include the process instance key, not simply echo the routing value.

explanation: The message correlation endpoint differs from publish in three documented ways: it does not buffer messages, it returns one process instance key on success, and it prioritizes creating a new instance via a message start event over correlating with an existing message catch event or receive task.

scenario: Aurora Wholesaler delivers 11000 orders per day in Camunda 8.8 and an architect must plan message correlation with set correlation key, set message ID and a positive TTL when the message arrives on an intermediate catch event inside the fulfilment process on the broker.
question: Which behavior does the correlation overview document for a message with set correlation key, set message ID and a positive TTL on an intermediate event?

options.text: The message is correlated to all matching subscriptions, ignoring the message ID since intermediate events do not perform any uniqueness check on the broker at all
The message is correlated during the lifetime of the message if a matching subscription is active and there is no equal message already in the buffer on the broker
A new process instance is started for every published message because intermediate events accept duplicate messages without consulting the buffer at correlation time
The message is held without correlation until the matching subscription is explicitly resubscribed via an operator API call from the operations team on the broker

a.text: Incorrect. The overview table applies the uniqueness check to intermediate events too when a message ID is set, rejecting equal messages already in the buffer on the broker.

b.text: Correct. The overview row for set correlation key, set message ID and TTL > 0 on an intermediate event says the message is correlated during its lifetime if a matching subscription is active and there is no equal message in the buffer.

c.text: Incorrect. Intermediate events never start a new process instance; they correlate only to existing subscriptions, as the overview rows for intermediate events make clear in every TTL configuration.

d.text: Incorrect. The broker polls the buffer when a subscription opens; there is no operator-driven resubscribe API documented in the messages page for intermediate events at all.

explanation: For set correlation key, set message ID and TTL > 0 on an intermediate event, the overview row states the message is correlated during the lifetime of the message if a matching subscription is active and there is no equal message in the buffer, combining buffering and uniqueness in a single row.

scenario: North Telco serves 9000 customers in Camunda 8.8 and an architect must decide between an empty correlation key with a message ID set and TTL of zero on a start event versus the same combination on an intermediate event for inbound activation events in the onboarding process.
question: Which behavior does the correlation overview document for a message with empty correlation key, set message ID and TTL of zero on a start event?

options.text: The message is correlated to an existing instance with a matching correlation key, even though the correlation key is the empty string in this start-event configuration
The message is buffered indefinitely on the broker because a set message ID always extends the TTL of zero to the cluster's longest configured retention window
A new process instance is started if there is no equal message in the buffer, combining the empty correlation key with the message ID uniqueness check on the broker
The message is rejected because a message ID cannot be combined with the empty correlation key on start events in any TTL configuration documented in the table

c.text: Correct. The overview row for empty correlation key, set message ID and TTL of zero on a start event states a new instance is started if there is no equal message in the buffer, applying the message ID uniqueness check on the broker.

b.text: Incorrect. A TTL of zero disables buffering; the overview never extends TTL based on the message ID setting or any cluster retention value on the broker side at all.

a.text: Incorrect. The row is for a start event, so it documents instance creation, not correlation to an existing instance, and the empty correlation key does not match arbitrary instances.

d.text: Incorrect. The overview explicitly defines this combination; the empty correlation key plus a message ID is a legitimate row in the table, not a rejection case on the broker.

explanation: The overview row for empty correlation key, set message ID and TTL of zero on a start event reads: a new instance is started if there is no equal message in the buffer. The message ID still drives the uniqueness check, even though the correlation key is the empty string in this configuration.

scenario: Beacon, a SaaS provider running on Camunda 8.8, exposes an internal 'Start onboarding' API that must respond in under 200 ms even when downstream service tasks run for several minutes. The BPMN begins with a none start event and handles roughly 4000 onboarding requests per day across customer tenants in production.
question: Which Camunda 8.8 command starts a process from a none start event and responds with the process instance key before execution begins?

options.text: DeployResource with a fresh BPMN payload creates a single instance as a deployment side effect, returns its process instance key, and continues execution after the deployment commit completes

CreateProcessInstance creates a new process instance and immediately responds with the process instance key, while execution of the process occurs asynchronously after the response is sent to the client
PublishMessage with the start event's message name creates an instance synchronously, returns the process instance key, and waits for the first user task to be activated before returning the response
CreateProcessInstanceWithResult creates the instance, blocks the response until execution is complete, and returns the final result variables alongside the process instance key in the same call

b.text: Correct. CreateProcessInstance starts a process from a none start event, immediately responds with the new process instance key and definition identifiers, and the execution of the process continues asynchronously after that response is sent to the client.

d.text: Incorrect. CreateProcessInstanceWithResult deliberately delays the response until execution is complete, which would break the sub-200 ms latency budget the onboarding API depends on.

c.text: Incorrect. None start events are started by an explicit create command, not by publishing a message; only message start events are reached via publish or correlate calls.

a.text: Incorrect. DeployResource publishes BPMN resources to the cluster and does not create a process instance as a side effect; instance creation requires a separate command.

explanation: CreateProcessInstance is the documented command for a none start event: it creates a new instance and immediately responds with the process instance key, and the execution of the process occurs asynchronously after that response is sent.

scenario: Lumen Marketplace runs a short Camunda 8.8 process that scores 1500 risk-check requests per hour for merchant onboarding. Each instance finishes in under 2 seconds and the client widget needs the scoring variables returned in the same HTTP response so the operator can immediately approve or hold the merchant.
question: Which Camunda 8.8 command is intended for short-running processes whose result variables must be returned on the same call that created the instance?

options.text: CreateProcessInstance creates the instance asynchronously and forwards the final result variables to a callback URL that the client configured on the original create request payload
CreateProcessInstanceWithResult automatically resends the same request when no response arrives, which guarantees the client always receives the final scoring variables exactly one time
CreateProcessInstanceWithResult executes the process synchronously and returns the resulting variables together with the process instance key once the process execution has completed
PublishMessage with awaitCompletion set to true correlates against a message start event and returns the final result variables in a synchronous response once the process has finished executing

a.text: Incorrect. CreateProcessInstance responds immediately with the process instance key before execution starts and is therefore asynchronous; the documentation does not describe a callback URL field for delivering result variables.

b.text: Incorrect. The documentation explicitly warns that when the client resends the command it creates a new process instance, so retries do not provide exactly-once delivery of the original outcome.

d.text: Incorrect. PublishMessage does not have an awaitCompletion flag; awaiting completion and returning result variables is a property of CreateProcessInstanceWithResult, not of message publication.

c.text: Correct. CreateProcessInstanceWithResult synchronously executes the process and returns the result variables together with the process instance key when execution is complete, which is the documented use case for short-running processes that collect information.

explanation: CreateProcessInstanceWithResult lets a client synchronously execute a process and receive the outcome variables on the same call that created the instance, sent only once execution finishes. It is documented as suited to short-running processes and processes that collect information, with retries warned to produce new instances.

scenario: Helios Bank runs a settlement process in Camunda 8.8 across 7000 transactions per day. The platform team needs to start one process instance every 4 hours from a timer start event, and also needs separate instances created whenever a payments message arrives that matches a message start event on the same process.
question: Which behavior describes how Camunda 8 timer and message start events create process instances?

options.text: Timer start events queue all triggers and create one batched instance per timer interval, while message start events buffer messages and create one instance per batch arrival
Timer start events require a paired message correlation key to create instances, while message start events can also trigger periodically when no matching message has arrived
Timer start events create an instance when the timer is triggered and can fire periodically, while message start events create one new instance per new matching message published
Timer start events fire once at deployment and create a single instance, while message start events create one instance per process definition version after the first message arrives

a.text: Incorrect. Each new matching message produces a new instance; there is no documented batching of timer triggers or message arrivals into single combined instances.

d.text: Incorrect. Timer start events can fire periodically rather than only at deployment, and each new matching message creates its own instance for message start events.

b.text: Incorrect. Timer start events do not require a message correlation key, and message start events do not fire periodically without a matching published message arriving.

c.text: Correct. The instance is created when the timer is triggered, timers can also fire periodically, and a new instance is created for each new matching message published.

explanation: Timer start events create an instance when triggered and can fire periodically; message start events create one new instance per matching published message.

scenario: Lumen Bank starts 22000 instances per day in Camunda 8.8 from a none start event using the CreateProcessInstance command on the v2 process-instances REST endpoint, and a developer must confirm the timing of the gateway response relative to actual process execution on the broker.
question: When the CreateProcessInstance command is used for a process with a none start event, when does the gateway respond to the client?

options.text: After the first service task completes, so the response carries an initial variable snapshot for the calling client to read on success of the call
Only after the process instance reaches a wait state such as a message catch event or user task to keep latency bounded for the caller in a known window
Immediately with the process instance ID, with the execution of the process occurring after the response is sent back to the calling client by the gateway
Only after the process instance ends, with CreateProcessInstance returning once execution is complete with all final variables for the caller to read

c.text: Correct. The Create and execute asynchronously section states that CreateProcessInstance immediately responds with the process instance ID, and that the execution of the process occurs after the response is sent.

a.text: Incorrect. The async create command does not wait for any service task; it returns the process instance ID immediately and execution continues afterwards on the broker side.

b.text: Incorrect. CreateProcessInstance returns immediately without waiting for any wait state; that variant is not described in the asynchronous section of the process instance creation docs.

d.text: Incorrect. Waiting for the end of execution is the behaviour of CreateProcessInstanceWithResult, not the plain CreateProcessInstance command described in the async section.

explanation: The Create and execute asynchronously section documents that CreateProcessInstance returns the process instance ID immediately and that the execution of the process occurs after the response is sent. This decouples creation from execution and gives the caller a key to track the new instance.

scenario: Nimbus Marketplace runs 15000 orders per day in Camunda 8.8 through a short, deterministic checkout process and needs the API caller to receive the resulting process variables once execution finishes inside a single REST round-trip on the v2 process-instances endpoint.
question: Which Camunda 8 command lets a client receive the process variables when execution completes synchronously?

options.text: CreateProcessInstance via the v2 process-instances endpoint with awaitCompletion left at its default, then polling the instance status from the calling client
CreateProcessInstanceWithResult, which synchronously executes the process and returns the results via a set of variables when execution is complete
CompleteJob with the variables payload on the start activity, then a follow-up GetProcessInstance call to read the final variables from the broker
BroadcastSignal with the start event name and a result-subscription header so the gateway streams the variables back to the original caller asynchronously

a.text: Incorrect. The plain CreateProcessInstance command returns immediately with only the process instance ID; obtaining result variables requires awaitCompletion set to true or the CreateProcessInstanceWithResult RPC.

b.text: Correct. The Create and await results section states that CreateProcessInstanceWithResult allows a client to synchronously execute the process and receive the results via a set of variables when the execution is complete.

c.text: Incorrect. CompleteJob completes an activated job and has no role in starting a process instance; there is no documented pattern of pairing it with a follow-up read for synchronous results.

d.text: Incorrect. Signal broadcasts do not return process variables; result streaming via a signal header is not a documented Camunda 8 API in the process instance creation page.

explanation: The Create and await results section explains that CreateProcessInstanceWithResult synchronously executes the process and returns the result via a set of variables once execution is complete. The REST equivalent sets awaitCompletion to true on the v2/process-instances endpoint, returning the variables in the response body.

scenario: Zenith Manufacturer routes 11000 orders per day in Camunda 8.8 through CreateProcessInstanceWithResult and an architect must enumerate the failure modes in the docs that can stop a result response from reaching the client even when the process execution has completed successfully on the broker.
question: Which scenario can prevent a CreateProcessInstanceWithResult client from receiving a response even when the process has completed successfully?

options.text: A second client resending the same command, which makes the gateway respond on the duplicate call only and discard the original response back to the first client
A boundary error event raised on a service task inside the process instance, which forces the gateway to suppress the result payload before sending it back
Failover of the broker node processing the process to another node, with the request registered on the first node and the new node not sending the response
A misconfigured timer cycle expression on an intermediate event, which silently rolls back the response before the gateway sends it back to the client

b.text: Incorrect. Boundary error events are part of normal BPMN flow and do not change whether the result response is sent; the documented failure modes are infrastructure-level events, not BPMN handling.

d.text: Incorrect. A bad timer expression would cause an incident, not silently roll back the response; the docs list connection, network, failover and gateway failures as the response-blocking scenarios.

c.text: Correct. The docs explicitly list failover, where the node processing the process crashes and another node continues processing without sending the response because the request is registered on the first node.

a.text: Incorrect. Resending the command creates a new process instance, not a response swap; the docs treat a resend as a new instance creation, not as a way to recover the original response.

explanation: The Create and await results section enumerates four scenarios in which the client may not receive a response even though execution completed successfully: connection timeout against gRPC deadlines, network connection loss at any step in the chain, failover of the node processing the process and gateway failure between the cluster and the client.

scenario: Aurora Health, a hospital, is rehearsing two parallel notification branches of its 25-task admission workflow on a Camunda 8.8 sandbox covering 1200 instances per day. The QA team needs to activate both branches at the same time inside a single created process instance for one targeted test.
question: How can two parallel branches be activated within the same created process instance for a targeted test?

options.text: Provide a single startInstructions entry whose elementId is a comma-separated list of the two branch element ids; the engine parses the list and activates each branch.
Provide one startInstructions entry per branch but submit each as a separate CreateProcessInstance command; the gateway correlates them by processDefinitionKey at runtime.
Provide a single startInstructions entry pointing at the closest upstream joining gateway; the engine then fans out automatically to each downstream branch element.
Provide multiple startInstructions entries on the same command, one per branch element id; the engine activates each named element when the process instance is created.

d.text: Correct. The documented behavior is that multiple startInstructions entries can be provided to start the process instance at more than one element; the engine activates each named element on creation.

a.text: Incorrect. Each startInstructions entry references a single elementId; the engine does not parse comma-separated lists inside the elementId field.

c.text: Incorrect. Targeting a gateway is not the documented mechanism for activating multiple branches; multiple startInstructions entries with distinct elementIds are.

b.text: Incorrect. Each CreateProcessInstance call produces a separate process instance, so two calls cannot place two parallel branches inside the same single instance.

explanation: Multiple startInstructions entries can be supplied on a single CreateProcessInstance command to start the same process instance at more than one element, which is how parallel branches are activated together in a test.

scenario: Helios Telecom, a telco, is reviewing the Camunda 8.8 documentation before allowing engineers to use start and runtime instructions across 1500 instances per day on its test cluster. A platform lead asks how Camunda positions this capability for use in production systems.
question: How does Camunda position start and runtime instructions for production use of process instance creation?

options.text: As an advanced feature recommended only for testing purposes; the none start event is the defined beginning of the process and most instances should start from there.
As the default mechanism for production process creation; the none start event is reserved for engine-internal scheduler invocations and not available to clients.
As a connector-only feature usable solely from inbound connectors and not from the CreateProcessInstance gateway commands in production deployment pipelines.
As a legacy compatibility shim retained only for parity with Camunda 7 start-anywhere semantics, and not recommended for production use in new process designs.

a.text: Correct. The documentation labels this an advanced feature and recommends using it only for testing purposes, since the none start event is the defined beginning of a process and most instances should start there.

b.text: Incorrect. The default mechanism for creating a process instance is to start at the none start event; user-defined start elements are explicitly positioned as an advanced testing feature.

d.text: Incorrect. Start and runtime instructions are not described as a legacy compatibility shim; they are a current Camunda 8 capability flagged as advanced and recommended only for testing purposes.

c.text: Incorrect. Start and runtime instructions are supported for the CreateProcessInstance commands, not restricted to connectors; they are simply recommended only for testing purposes.

explanation: Camunda labels starting or ending a process instance at user-defined elements as an advanced feature recommended only for testing purposes, because the none start event is the defined beginning of a process and most instances should start there.

scenario: Quill Press, a publisher, is evaluating Camunda 8.8 tags as a way to carry routing keys across 900 instances per day in its editorial workflow. A developer asks what filtering semantics the search API uses when multiple tags are supplied together in a single query against the process instance endpoint.
question: What filtering semantics does the process instance search API use when multiple tags are supplied in a single query?

options.text: Exact-set semantics: an instance is returned only if its tag set equals the requested set, and any additional tags attached to the instance exclude it from the search results.
OR semantics: an instance is returned if it contains any one of the requested tags, and prefix wildcards on tag values are honored to widen results across each search query.
AND semantics: an instance must contain every requested tag and may contain additional tags; substring matching and wildcard matching on tag values are unavailable in the search API.
XOR semantics: an instance is returned only when it contains exactly one of the requested tags, and tag casing is normalized to lowercase before comparison at search time.

c.text: Correct. Search filtering uses AND semantics: an instance must contain all requested tags, it may also contain additional tags, and partial or wildcard matching is not supported on tag values.

b.text: Incorrect. The documented semantics are AND across requested tags, not OR, and the search API does not support prefix wildcards for partial tag matching.

d.text: Incorrect. Tags are case-sensitive strings; the search API does not normalize casing, and the filter is AND across requested tags rather than XOR.

a.text: Incorrect. Additional tags on an instance do not exclude it from results; the filter only requires that the requested tags are all present on the instance.

explanation: Tag search uses AND semantics: an instance must carry every requested tag but may carry more, and Camunda does not provide partial or wildcard matching on tag values in process instance queries.

scenario: Stratus Cloud, a tenant on its own dedicated Camunda 8.8 deployment, is planning a migration that touches an active call activity inside an embedded subprocess across 1300 instances per day in its onboarding process. The architect wants to confirm the migration tool's official support matrix covers each of the subprocess element types in their model.
question: Which subprocess element types are listed as supported by the Camunda 8.8 process instance migration tool?

options.text: Embedded subprocess and call activity are listed as supported, while event subprocess is intentionally excluded from the Camunda 8.8 process instance migration tool support matrix.
Call activity and event subprocess are listed as supported, while embedded subprocess migration first requires a manual process instance modification before migration runs.
Embedded subprocess and event subprocess are listed as supported, while call activity migration is delegated entirely to the called process's own separate migration plan.
Embedded subprocess, call activity, and event subprocess are each listed as supported subprocess elements in the Camunda 8.8 process instance migration tool support matrix.

d.text: Correct. The supported BPMN elements section lists embedded subprocess, call activity, and event subprocess as supported subprocess elements for migration in Camunda 8.8.

a.text: Incorrect. Event subprocess is included in the supported subprocess elements list; it is not excluded from the migration tool support matrix.

c.text: Incorrect. Call activity is supported by the migration tool itself; the called process instance is unchanged when migrating the call activity but the call activity element is listed as supported.

b.text: Incorrect. Embedded subprocess is directly supported by the migration tool without requiring a manual modification step beforehand.

explanation: Camunda 8.8's migration tool lists embedded subprocess, call activity, and event subprocess as the supported subprocess element types in its support matrix.

scenario: Sigma SaaS is auditing which BPMN task types its 2400 instances of the Camunda 8.8 contract-renewal process may migrate between. The platform team needs the canonical list of task elements covered by the migration tool before approving the upcoming model rollout.
question: Which task elements are listed as supported by the Camunda 8.8 process instance migration tool?

options.text: Service task, user task, manual task, send task, business rule task, and script task are listed as supported, with receive task explicitly excluded from the migration support matrix.
Service task, user task, receive task, and send task are listed as supported task elements, while business rule task and script task are explicitly excluded from the support matrix.
Service task, user task, receive task, send task, business rule task, and script task are each listed as supported task elements in the Camunda 8.8 migration tool support matrix.
Service task, user task, receive task, send task, business rule task, script task, and manual task are all listed as supported task elements in the migration tool support matrix.

c.text: Correct. The supported BPMN elements section lists service task, user task, receive task, send task, business rule task, and script task as the supported task elements for migration in Camunda 8.8.

b.text: Incorrect. Business rule task and script task are included in the supported tasks list, not excluded; this answer misstates the support matrix for these two task types.

a.text: Incorrect. Manual task is not listed in the supported tasks for migration, and receive task is listed as supported, so this combination misrepresents the matrix.

d.text: Incorrect. Manual task is not listed among the supported task elements for migration; the supported set covers service, user, receive, send, business rule, and script tasks only.

explanation: Camunda 8.8's migration tool lists service task, user task, receive task, send task, business rule task, and script task as the supported task elements in its support matrix.

scenario: Halcyon Investments, a broker, is migrating 850 instances of its Camunda 8.8 trade-execution process to a redesigned routing block that uses event-based decisions for downstream branches. The team needs to confirm which gateway elements are listed as covered by the migration tool's official support matrix.
question: Which gateway elements are listed as supported by the Camunda 8.8 process instance migration tool?

options.text: Exclusive gateway and event-based gateway are each listed as supported gateway elements in the Camunda 8.8 process instance migration tool support matrix for active instances.
Exclusive gateway and complex gateway are listed as supported, while event-based gateway is replaced by an equivalent multi-instance marker on the target side during migration.
Parallel gateway and inclusive gateway are listed as supported gateway elements, while event-based gateway is excluded from the Camunda 8.8 migration tool support matrix entirely.
Exclusive gateway and parallel gateway are listed as supported, while event-based gateway requires a separate process instance modification before any migration command is issued.

a.text: Correct. The supported BPMN elements section lists exclusive gateway and event-based gateway as the supported gateway elements in the Camunda 8.8 migration tool support matrix.

c.text: Incorrect. Parallel and inclusive gateways are not listed as supported gateway elements in the documented migration support matrix; exclusive and event-based gateways are.

d.text: Incorrect. Event-based gateway is supported directly by the migration tool; no separate process instance modification is documented as a prerequisite.

b.text: Incorrect. Complex gateway is not listed as supported, and event-based gateway is itself supported rather than swapped for a multi-instance marker during migration.

explanation: Camunda 8.8's migration tool lists exclusive gateway and event-based gateway as the supported gateway element types in its support matrix.

scenario: Driftwood Marketplace is establishing a Camunda 8.8 migration policy after 4 production incidents in 2 weeks linked to large migration commands. Leadership asks what general posture the documentation recommends for applying migration against running production process instances.
question: What general posture does Camunda recommend when applying process instance migration in production?

options.text: Treat migration as a strictly read-only diagnostic tool: run it exclusively through Operate, and let active elements and process variables be reset to defaults during the migration command.
Treat migration as a powerful but risky tool: test on a non-production cluster or with Camunda Process Test, and prefer multiple smaller migrations over one large migration when in doubt.
Treat migration as a routine deployment step: apply the largest possible migration in one shot to minimize cluster downtime, and skip non-production rehearsals to release faster on schedule.
Treat migration as an automated step that the engine validates against a declared SLA contract, blocking any mapping instruction whose application would change visible execution paths.

b.text: Correct. The documentation calls migration a powerful tool but says to use it with care, to test on a non-production cluster or with Camunda Process Test, and to prefer multiple smaller migrations over a single big migration when in doubt.

c.text: Incorrect. The documentation recommends caution and small steps, not the largest possible single migration; skipping non-production testing contradicts the explicit guidance to test first.

a.text: Incorrect. Migration is not a read-only diagnostic; it changes the process model the running instance follows, and variables are not reset to defaults during migration.

d.text: Incorrect. There is no SLA-contract validation that blocks mapping instructions; the documentation places the responsibility on the operator to apply suitable migrations.

explanation: Camunda recommends treating migration as a powerful but risky operation: validate on a non-production cluster or with Camunda Process Test, and prefer breaking changes into multiple smaller migrations rather than one large one.

scenario: Apex Retail has 600 instances of its Camunda 8.8 fulfillment process waiting on task C after a parallel joining gateway. The team is considering a migration that maps active task C onto task A, which sits before that same parallel joining gateway in the source model, and wants the documented risk.
question: What documented risk arises if active task C is mapped back to task A located before the parallel joining gateway?

options.text: The migrated process instance is rolled back to the start event automatically; the parallel joining gateway forbids any retrograde mapping of an active element during process instance migration.
The migrated process instance creates a duplicate of task C as a sibling of task A; the engine inserts a copy whenever a mapping instruction creates a backward flow on a join.
The migrated process instance can become stuck on the parallel joining gateway; the gateway still requires both of its incoming flows to complete before the instance can continue.
The migrated process instance silently completes immediately; the parallel joining gateway treats any retrograde mapping as an implicit TERMINATE_PROCESS_INSTANCE instruction.

c.text: Correct. The documentation warns that mapping task C back to A or B leaves the process instance stuck on the parallel gateway, because the gateway still expects both incoming flows to complete.

a.text: Incorrect. The instance is not rolled back to the start event automatically; the documented outcome is that it gets stuck on the parallel joining gateway.

d.text: Incorrect. The instance does not silently complete; retrograde mappings are not interpreted as terminate instructions by the engine.

b.text: Incorrect. The engine does not duplicate the mapped element as a sibling; the documented effect of this kind of mapping is that the instance becomes stuck on the parallel join.

explanation: Mapping an active task back to a task located before a parallel joining gateway can leave the migrated instance stuck on the gateway, because the join still requires both of its incoming flows to complete before the instance can move on.

scenario: Stellar Insurance operates a claims process in Camunda 8.8 with 6000 instances. After a bug fix to the process definition, the platform team must migrate running instances to the new version, and an active user task currently assigned to an adjuster must remain with that adjuster after migration when no implementation migration occurs.
question: What does Camunda 8 process instance migration aim to preserve about a running process instance state?

options.text: Migration pauses the source instance and starts a shadow instance on the target, so user task assignments are duplicated until an operator chooses which copy to keep active
Migration replays the instance from the start event on the target definition, so all completed elements run again and active assignments are cleared during the replay phase
Migration creates a new instance on the target definition and copies only the variables, so any active user task assignment is dropped and must be reclaimed manually by a user
Migration fits the instance to the target definition with minimal interference, so an active user task remains assigned to the same user when no implementation migration occurs

b.text: Incorrect. Migration does not replay completed elements from the start event; it changes the definition of a running instance while preserving as much existing state as possible.

d.text: Correct. Migration is designed to fit a running instance to a different definition with minimal interference, so an active user task keeps its assignee when no implementation migration occurs.

c.text: Incorrect. Migration does not create a new instance and copy only variables; it changes the process model of the same running instance with its execution state preserved.

a.text: Incorrect. Migration does not pause the source and start a shadow instance; the same instance continues on the target definition with its existing execution state preserved.

explanation: Process instance migration fits a running instance to a different definition with minimal interference, so attributes like an active user task assignment are preserved when no implementation migration occurs.

scenario: Keystone Bank deploys a new version of its order-process in Camunda 8.8 that adds a user task B between an active service task A and the end event. The platform team must update 3500 instances so each instance continues at service task A and then flows to user task B once service task A completes.
question: How does a migration plan keep service task A active in the target while introducing the new downstream user task B?

options.text: Provide a runtime instruction terminating the instance after service task A; the engine recreates the instance on the target so it begins at user task B from the start event
Omit any mapping instruction for element A; the engine auto-detects matching IDs across versions and continues at A by name, then routes to user task B after completion
Provide a mapping instruction from source element A to target element B; the engine moves the active token to user task B and skips service task A in the target definition
Provide a mapping instruction from source element ID A to target element ID A; the engine keeps the active instance at service task A and reaches user task B once A completes

b.text: Incorrect. Migration requires an explicit migration plan with mapping instructions; element IDs are not auto-detected across versions by name without those instructions being supplied.

c.text: Incorrect. Mapping A to B would move the active token to user task B and skip the still-needed work at service task A on the new definition before reaching B.

d.text: Correct. A mapping instruction from source ID A to target ID A keeps the active instance at service task A, and on completion it flows to the new user task B in the target.

a.text: Incorrect. Runtime instructions terminate an instance after an element; they do not migrate state to a new definition or restart execution at a chosen target element.

explanation: A mapping instruction from source ID A to target ID A keeps the active instance at service task A so it flows to the newly inserted downstream user task B on completion in the target definition.

scenario: Zenith Logistics is migrating 2800 instances in Camunda 8.8 to a new version where the active service task A no longer exists. The new definition contains only a service task C, but a team member proposes mapping the active service task A to a user task in the target definition instead of mapping A to C.
question: Which rule governs element type compatibility in a Camunda 8 process instance migration mapping?

options.text: Element type mapping is not allowed across types; an active service task may only be mapped to a service task in the target and cannot be mapped to a user task in the target
Any element type may be mapped to any other element type; the engine reinterprets the active token to fit the target element type without restriction during the migration
Element type mapping is allowed when source and target carry the same job type; the engine bridges service tasks and user tasks using the shared job type as glue at runtime
Element type mapping requires a runtime instruction in addition to the mapping instruction; the engine then converts between service tasks and user tasks as needed at runtime

b.text: Incorrect. The documented rule explicitly forbids mapping an element to an element of a different type during process instance migration, so unrestricted reinterpretation does not apply.

d.text: Incorrect. Runtime instructions do not unlock cross-type mapping; the type rule applies to the mapping instruction itself in the migration plan and rejects type changes.

c.text: Incorrect. A shared job type does not enable mapping across element types; the engine still rejects mapping a service task to a user task in the target definition.

a.text: Correct. The documented rule forbids mapping across element types, so an active service task may only be mapped to a service task in the target process definition.

explanation: Migration mappings cannot cross element types; an active service task may only be mapped to a service task in the target process definition and not to a user task.

scenario: Catalyst Bank has 1200 instances stuck in Camunda 8.8 because a user task condition referenced the wrong variable in the deployed process definition, raising one incident on each instance. The platform team must repair the running instances by correcting the definition and applying the fix to the affected instances.
question: What is the documented sequence of steps to correct the mistake in the running process instances?

options.text: Roll back the deployed definition to the previous version, cancel the stuck instances, then redeploy the fixed definition and recreate the instances on the new corrected version
Resolve the incident on each instance first, then modify the deployed definition in place, then redeploy the same version id, then restart the instances from the start event again
Create a new process version in Modeler with the corrected condition, deploy the new version, migrate the affected instances to the new version, then resolve the incidents
Cancel each stuck instance using process instance modification, redeploy the same definition with a forced flag, then recreate the instances and trigger them at the user task element

c.text: Correct. The documented steps are: create a new process version with the fix in Modeler, deploy it, migrate the affected instances to the new version, then resolve the incidents.

b.text: Incorrect. Definitions are not modified in place at the same version id, and resolving incidents before applying the fix would not clear the underlying mistake in the model.

a.text: Incorrect. Rolling back the deployed definition and cancelling instances is not the documented procedure; migration to the corrected new version is the documented repair path.

d.text: Incorrect. Cancelling stuck instances via modification is not the documented procedure here; migration to the corrected new version is the documented way to repair the instances.

explanation: Correct a mistake by creating and deploying a new process version with the fix, migrating the stuck instances to that new version, and then resolving the incidents on those instances.

scenario: Aurora Bank runs 4200 instances per day on Camunda 8.8 and is migrating an active service task that lives inside an embedded subprocess to a redesigned process definition where both the subprocess and the inner task have new ids. The platform team needs to know which mapping instructions are required so the active element inside the subprocess can be migrated cleanly.
question: Which mapping instruction is required to migrate an active element that is located inside a subprocess?

options.text: Provide a mapping from the source lane id to the target lane id so the engine can resolve the parent scope during migration
Provide a mapping from the source process id to the target process id so the engine can resolve the parent scope during migration
Provide a mapping from the source subprocess id to the target subprocess id so the engine can resolve the parent scope during migration
Provide a mapping from the source pool id to the target pool id so the engine can resolve the parent scope during migration

c.text: Correct. The documentation states that a mapping instruction must be provided from the process instance's subprocess id to the target subprocess id to migrate subprocesses, in addition to the mapping for the inner active element.

b.text: Incorrect. A process definition id mapping is not how scope migration is configured for subprocesses; the subprocess element itself requires its own mapping instruction in addition to the inner element.

d.text: Incorrect. Pools are not the scope that the documented mapping instruction targets; the subprocess element id mapping is what allows the active inner element to be migrated.

a.text: Incorrect. Lanes are visual swimlanes and are not the scope used for migration mapping; the subprocess element id mapping is what the engine requires.

explanation: When migrating an active element inside a subprocess, two mapping instructions are needed: one for the active inner element and one for the parent subprocess id. Without the subprocess id mapping the engine cannot resolve the new parent scope.

scenario: Meridian Bank runs 5000 instances per day on Camunda 8.8 and is preparing a migration where several catch events exist on both the source and target process definitions. The platform team is reviewing what a mapping instruction between a source catch event and a target catch event actually guarantees before deciding which events to map.
question: What does providing a mapping instruction between a source catch event and a target catch event ensure?

options.text: The catch event in the target process is rebuilt and a fresh event subscription is opened against the target on completion of the active element scope
The catch event in the source process is replayed and any unprocessed messages are redelivered against the target catch event during the migration step
The event subscription in the source process is preserved after the migration and the engine carries it forward against the target catch event element
The event subscription is closed when the migration finalizes and a new subscription is opened only when the next instance reaches the catch event element

a.text: Incorrect. A mapping does not rebuild the target catch event nor open a fresh subscription; the documentation states the existing source subscription is preserved across migration.

c.text: Correct. The documentation states that providing a mapping between catch events ensures the event subscription in the source process is preserved after the migration.

b.text: Incorrect. Mapping does not replay or redeliver historical messages; the documented guarantee is that the existing subscription is preserved across the migration.

d.text: Incorrect. Mapping preserves the subscription rather than closing it; closing the subscription is what happens when no mapping is provided for a source catch event.

explanation: A mapping instruction between source and target catch events guarantees that the existing event subscription is migrated rather than closed, so the in-flight instance keeps reacting to the same events through the new definition.

scenario: Forge Bank operates a Camunda 8.8 service task that creates jobs with the static type send_mail. A new version of the process definition changes the job type to a FEEL expression =order.next_step. The platform team migrates 15000 instances of the existing process to the new definition and wants to know how previously activated jobs will behave once the migration has completed on the target definition.
question: What happens to an existing service task job after migrating its process instance when the target job type expression differs?

options.text: The existing job keeps every property it had before the migration including its original job type and the new expression is not evaluated for it
The engine cancels the existing job and recreates a fresh job using the new job type expression from the target service task definition
The engine reevaluates the new job type expression and updates the existing job to the resolved type before the worker can poll it again
The migration command is rejected because a change in the job type expression of a target service task counts as an unsupported difference

c.text: Incorrect. The docs are explicit that expressions of active elements are not reevaluated and static values are not adjusted across the two process definitions during migration.

b.text: Incorrect. Migration does not recreate jobs of active elements. Cancellation and recreation would only happen via process instance modification, not as part of a normal migration.

a.text: Correct. The docs state that jobs, expressions, and input mappings of active elements are not recreated or reevaluated, so the job keeps all its previous properties including its original job type.

d.text: Incorrect. A different job type expression is not a rejection reason; the migration succeeds and simply leaves the existing job untouched on the new definition.

explanation: During migration the engine does not recreate jobs, reevaluate expressions, or reapply input mappings of active elements. An already activated service task job keeps its previous properties, including its job type, even when the target definition changes the expression.

scenario: Lattice Insurance runs Camunda 8.8 across 6000 instances per day. The platform team is preparing a process redesign that may introduce different BPMN gateway types in the target process definition. Before committing to the design the architecture group needs to know which gateway types are explicitly listed as supported by the process instance migration coverage matrix in the docs.
question: Which set of BPMN gateways is listed as supported by process instance migration coverage in Camunda 8.8?

options.text: Exclusive gateways, parallel gateways, and inclusive gateways are listed as supported while event-based gateways are absent from the gateways list
Exclusive, parallel, inclusive, and event-based gateways are all listed as supported gateway types in the documented BPMN coverage for migration
Exclusive gateways and parallel gateways are listed as supported while inclusive gateways and event-based gateways are absent from the gateways list
Exclusive gateways and event-based gateways are the gateway types listed as supported by the process instance migration BPMN coverage matrix

c.text: Incorrect. The docs do list event-based gateways in the supported gateways group; parallel gateways are not part of the supported gateways list under the migration coverage matrix.

a.text: Incorrect. Neither parallel gateways nor inclusive gateways appear as supported in the gateways list of the migration coverage matrix.

b.text: Incorrect. Only two gateway types are listed in the supported gateways group; parallel and inclusive gateways are not part of that list.

d.text: Correct. The supported BPMN elements section of the docs lists exactly two gateway types under Gateways: the exclusive gateway and the event-based gateway.

explanation: The BPMN coverage matrix for process instance migration limits the Gateways group to two entries: exclusive gateway and event-based gateway. Other gateway types are not in the supported gateways list even when they appear elsewhere in process definitions.

scenario: Lattice Bank runs 9000 instances of a loan onboarding process on Camunda 8.8. The original model uses an embedded subprocess, a call activity, and a parallel multi-instance service task. The operations team wants to roll out a redesigned definition using the process instance migration tool and needs to confirm which of the elements they have actually fall inside the supported BPMN element set listed in the docs.
question: Which set of in-flight elements in the Lattice Bank loan process is covered by the migration tool's supported BPMN elements list?

options.text: The embedded subprocess, the call activity, and the parallel multi-instance service task are all listed under supported subprocesses, tasks, and markers for the migration tool
The call activity and multi-instance marker are supported but embedded subprocesses fall outside the supported subprocess types listed by the migration tool
Only the service task is supported because subprocesses and call activities are documented as unsupported by the migration tool for in-flight token migration
The embedded subprocess and call activity are supported but parallel multi-instance markers are explicitly excluded from the supported markers section for the migration tool

a.text: Correct. The supported BPMN elements section lists embedded subprocesses, call activities, and event subprocesses under subprocesses; service tasks under tasks; and both parallel and sequential multi-instance markers, so all three Lattice Bank elements are covered.

d.text: Incorrect. The markers section lists both parallel and sequential multi-instance as supported, so parallel multi-instance is not excluded from the migration tool's coverage.

c.text: Incorrect. Embedded subprocesses and call activities are both listed as supported subprocess types for the migration tool, alongside event subprocesses, in the docs.

b.text: Incorrect. Embedded subprocesses appear in the supported subprocesses list together with call activities and event subprocesses, so they are inside the migration tool's coverage.

explanation: The process instance migration page lists supported elements by group. Subprocesses include embedded, call activity, and event subprocess; tasks include service, user, receive, send, business rule, and script; markers cover parallel and sequential multi-instance, so a process built from these is fully covered.

scenario: A fintech operator on Camunda 8.8 is reviewing the supported BPMN elements table for the process instance migration tool before migrating 4000 instances. The team wants to know exactly which gateway types appear in the supported set so they can pre-check that their target definition only uses gateways covered by the tool.
question: Which gateway types are listed under the supported BPMN elements for the process instance migration tool?

options.text: Exclusive gateway and inclusive gateway are the gateway types listed under supported BPMN elements for the migration tool in the documentation
Exclusive gateway and parallel gateway are the gateway types listed under supported BPMN elements for the migration tool in the documentation
Exclusive gateway and complex gateway are the gateway types listed under supported BPMN elements for the migration tool in the documentation
Exclusive gateway and event-based gateway are the gateway types listed under supported BPMN elements for the migration tool in the documentation

b.text: Incorrect. The supported gateways section pairs the exclusive gateway with the event-based gateway, not the parallel gateway, so this combination does not match the documented list.

d.text: Correct. The supported BPMN elements section for the migration tool lists exactly two gateway symbols under Gateways: exclusive gateway and event-based gateway.

a.text: Incorrect. The inclusive gateway does not appear in the supported gateways list of the migration tool; the second supported gateway in the docs is the event-based gateway.

c.text: Incorrect. The complex gateway is not part of the supported gateways list for the migration tool; the second supported gateway is the event-based gateway.

explanation: Under Supported BPMN elements, the Gateways subsection shows only two symbols marked as implemented for migration: the exclusive gateway and the event-based gateway. Other gateway types are not enumerated as supported by the migration tool on this page.

scenario: An insurer running Camunda 8.8 plans to migrate 7000 in-flight claim process instances. The architect reads the Use at your own risk section and wants to apply the documented recommendation that reduces the chance of leaving instances in an unreachable state during the rollout window of 2 hours.
question: What does the migration documentation recommend to reduce the risk of leaving process instances in an unintended state?

options.text: Rely on the engine to detect unreachable states automatically and roll back any migration that would leave an instance stuck on a parallel gateway
Migrate in multiple smaller steps and consider testing the migration on a non-production cluster or with Camunda Process Test before applying it
Disable variable migration during the rollout so that no global or local variables move with the instances into the new definition
Apply the entire migration as one large command so the engine atomically reaches the new target state across all in-flight instances in one operation

d.text: Incorrect. The docs recommend the opposite of one big change; a tip in the section states that it is often safer to migrate in multiple smaller steps rather than in one big migration.

c.text: Incorrect. The section states that both global and local variables are migrated automatically; disabling variable migration is not the suggested risk mitigation.

b.text: Correct. The Use at your own risk section recommends testing on a non-production cluster or with Camunda Process Test and notes that migrating in multiple smaller steps is often safer than one big migration.

a.text: Incorrect. The docs explicitly state that the process instance does not detect these situations and that it is up to the operator to apply suitable migrations.

explanation: The Use at your own risk section warns that the engine does not detect unreachable states created by migration. It recommends testing on a non-production cluster or with Camunda Process Test and breaking the change into smaller steps when in doubt.

scenario: Polaris Brokerage has 2200 instances of its Camunda 8.8 client-onboarding process stuck on an outdated process definition after a regulatory requirement change. The team wants to fit those running instances to a different process definition while preserving as much of the current execution state as possible.
question: Which Camunda 8 operation fits a running process instance to a different process definition with minimal disruption to its state?

options.text: Process instance reassignment, performed by republishing the deployment with the same processId so the engine automatically rebinds every running instance to the new model.
Process instance migration, invoked through the gateway MigrateProcessInstance RPC or its REST equivalent, which targets each affected running instance and changes its process model in place.
Process instance redeployment, performed by deleting each affected running instance and creating a fresh instance against the new process definition through the gateway.
Process instance modification, invoked through the gateway ModifyProcessInstance RPC on each affected instance to switch the underlying process definition pointer to the new version.

b.text: Correct. Process instance migration is documented as the operation that fits a running process instance to a different process definition while interfering as little as possible with state, invoked via the MigrateProcessInstance RPC or REST.

d.text: Incorrect. Process instance modification is for repairing a broken process instance without changing the process definition; for switching to a different definition, migration is the documented operation.

c.text: Incorrect. Deleting and recreating instances loses execution state, which is the opposite of the documented migration goal of minimal state interference.

a.text: Incorrect. Redeploying a process does not retroactively rebind running instances; the documented mechanism for switching running instances to a new definition is process instance migration.

explanation: Process instance migration is the documented Camunda 8 operation for fitting a running process instance to a different process definition while interfering as little as possible with its current execution state.

scenario: Glade Travel, an operator, needs a migration plan for 1800 instances of its Camunda 8.8 itinerary process after a model redesign. The platform lead wants to know what the mapping instructions in the migration plan actually express about source and target elements before signing off on the rollout.
question: What does a mapping instruction in a process instance migration plan express?

options.text: It expresses which source element id maps to which target element id, so the engine knows where to migrate any active element instances of that source on the target process definition.
It expresses which process variables to copy from source to target, leaving the engine to discover element-to-element correspondences automatically by element name in both definitions.
It expresses a list of completed historical elements to keep on the target side, so the Operate timeline stays consistent after the process model change is applied to the instance.
It expresses a FEEL expression evaluated by the engine at migration time, and whichever target element id that expression returns becomes the new home of the active element instance.

a.text: Correct. A mapping instruction provides a source-to-target element id pair so that any active element instances of the source element are migrated to the corresponding target element in the target process.

b.text: Incorrect. Mapping instructions describe element-to-element mappings between the source and target processes, not variable copies; variables are migrated automatically.

d.text: Incorrect. Mapping instructions use explicit source and target element ids, not FEEL expressions evaluated at migration time to pick a target element.

c.text: Incorrect. Mapping instructions concern the current source-to-target element relationship for active instances, not a list of historical completed elements to preserve.

explanation: A mapping instruction in a migration plan pairs a source element id with a target element id so the engine can migrate active element instances of the source onto the corresponding target element.

scenario: Halcyon Investments, a broker, has Camunda 8.8 instances stuck at user task A because a gateway condition mistakenly uses a string variable instead of the boolean one, generating an incident across 80 instances in production. The team wants the documented recovery sequence using process instance migration.
question: What is the documented sequence for correcting the mistake using process instance migration?

options.text: Cancel the stuck instance, fix the model in Modeler, deploy a new process version, and create a fresh process instance against the corrected definition with the same variables as the original instance.
Delete the deployed process definition, fix the model in Modeler, redeploy under the same process id, and let the engine retroactively rebind the stuck process instance on the next scheduled retry.
