Resolve the incident first to unblock the instance, then fix the model in Modeler, deploy the new process version, and let the existing instance pick up the fix automatically on the next retry attempt.
Fix the model in Modeler by creating a new version, deploy the new process version, migrate the stuck instance to the new version, and finally resolve the incident on the migrated process instance.

d.text: Correct. The documented steps are to correct the mistake in Modeler with a new version, deploy that version, migrate the stuck process instance to the new version, and then resolve the incident on the migrated instance.

c.text: Incorrect. Resolving the incident first does not change the broken condition in the deployed model; the documented order is to deploy the fixed version and migrate the instance, then resolve the incident.

a.text: Incorrect. The documented approach is to migrate the existing process instance to the corrected version, not to cancel and recreate it, so that the in-progress execution state is preserved.

b.text: Incorrect. Deleting a deployed process definition is not part of the documented sequence; the fix uses a new process version and process instance migration to apply it.

explanation: Process instance migration can correct incidents caused by a mistake in the model: deploy a new corrected version, migrate the stuck instance to it, then resolve the incident so the instance can continue.

scenario: Magnolia Retail, a retailer, wants to migrate 700 instances of its Camunda 8.8 returns-handling process. Service task A inside Subprocess A must move to service task B inside Subprocess B in the new model. The team is writing the migration plan and wants to know which mapping instructions are required.
question: Which mapping instructions are required to migrate the active service task located inside the subprocess?

options.text: A mapping instruction from element id A to element id B, plus a mapping instruction from subprocess id A to subprocess id B, both provided in the same migration plan for the running instance.
A mapping instruction from element id A to element id B and a parent mapping from subprocess id A to an event subprocess id B; the scope must broaden during migration of active elements.
A mapping instruction from subprocess id A to subprocess id B only; the engine then migrates the contained active service task by matching its source id to a target id of the same shape.
A mapping instruction from element id A to element id B only; the engine derives the subprocess mapping automatically from the BPMN parent relationship between source and target elements.

a.text: Correct. To migrate an active element inside a subprocess, the migration plan must contain mappings for both the inner element and the containing subprocess, here A to B and Subprocess A to Subprocess B.

d.text: Incorrect. The engine does not derive subprocess mappings from BPMN parentage; the migration plan must explicitly map the source subprocess id to the target subprocess id.

c.text: Incorrect. Mapping only the subprocess does not move the active inner element to its new id; both the element and subprocess mappings are required.

b.text: Incorrect. You cannot migrate an active embedded subprocess to an event subprocess; the scope cannot change in this way during process instance migration.

explanation: Migrating an active element inside a subprocess requires both an element-to-element mapping and a subprocess-to-subprocess mapping in the migration plan so the engine can move the inner active step onto its new container.

scenario: Borealis Bank is migrating 950 active Camunda 8.8 instances from job worker user tasks to Camunda user tasks on a cluster running version 8.8.20. The platform lead needs to know which attributes on the source job worker user task are preserved on the target Camunda user task after migration.
question: Which source attributes does the target Camunda user task preserve when migrating from a job worker user task?

options.text: Candidate groups, the current assignee, the priority, and any pending incidents from the source task; due date, follow-up date, and form references are reset to default values.
Candidate groups, candidate users, due date, follow-up date, the embedded form definition, and the current assignee from the source task, along with the priority value carried on the job.
Candidate groups, candidate users, due date, follow-up date, and the form id or form key from the source task, along with the customHeaders set on the job worker task during execution.
Candidate groups, candidate users, due date, follow-up date, and the current assignee from the source task; the form id, form key, and customHeaders are dropped on migration.

c.text: Correct. The documented preserved attributes are candidate groups, candidate users, due date, follow-up date, and the form id or form key from the source task, plus the customHeaders set in the job.

b.text: Incorrect. Embedded forms are not supported, so the embedded form is not preserved, and the current assignee is not preserved either; the initial assignee from the target definition is used instead.

d.text: Incorrect. The current assignee is not preserved; the target task is assigned to the initial assignee defined in the target user task definition, while form id and form key are preserved.

a.text: Incorrect. The current assignee and pending incidents are not preserved into the target Camunda user task; due dates, follow-up dates, and form references are preserved when present.

explanation: Migration from job worker user tasks to Camunda user tasks preserves candidate groups, candidate users, due date, follow-up date, the form id or form key, and customHeaders, while assignee and priority follow the target definition.

scenario: Quanta Fintech is migrating 1100 instances of its Camunda 8.8 disbursement process. The new process model adds a new message catch event on the existing service task and removes an old timer catch event that is no longer needed. The team plans the catch-event mapping instructions inside the migration plan.
question: How are the new target catch event and the removed source catch event handled by the mapping instructions during migration?

options.text: The unmapped new target catch event is ignored at migration time, and the unmapped source catch event keeps its subscription open as a fallback so no events are lost during migration.
The unmapped new target catch event causes a new subscription to be opened on migration, and the unmapped source catch event has its existing subscription closed during the migration.
The unmapped new target catch event requires a manual SubscribeEvent call after migration, and the unmapped source catch event is automatically rolled into the new target catch event.
The unmapped new target catch event raises an incident during migration, and the unmapped source catch event is automatically converted into a boundary event on the active scope task.

b.text: Correct. If a target catch event is not the target of a mapping, a new subscription is opened on migration; if a source catch event is not mapped, its subscription is closed during migration.

a.text: Incorrect. Unmapped target catch events are not ignored: new subscriptions are opened for them. Unmapped source catch events do not stay open as a fallback; their subscriptions are closed.

c.text: Incorrect. No manual SubscribeEvent call is needed; subscriptions for unmapped target catch events are opened automatically as part of the migration.

d.text: Incorrect. Unmapped target catch events do not raise an incident, and source catch events are not converted into boundary events; the engine opens or closes subscriptions based on whether mappings are present.

explanation: Catch event handling during migration is driven by mapping instructions: mapped catch events have their subscriptions migrated, unmapped target catch events get new subscriptions, and unmapped source catch events have their subscriptions closed.

scenario: Forge Bank is migrating 240 loans that are currently waiting at user task Approve with a one-week timer boundary event. 5 days have already elapsed on the timer. The new process version adds a follow-up task after the timer fires, and the team wants the remaining two days of the timer preserved so the migrated instances fire when the original deadline would have triggered.
question: Which mapping instructions must the migration plan provide to preserve the timer subscription so the instances only wait the remaining two days?

options.text: Map the active user task A to A and add a variable instruction setting elapsedDuration to PT120H so the engine resumes the timer from the recorded value in the target process
Map the timer boundary event Timer1 to Timer2 only and omit the user task mapping so the engine migrates the timer subscription without touching the active user task scope
Map the active user task A to A only and omit the timer mapping so the engine keeps the existing timer subscription and counts the elapsed five days against the new target duration
Map the active user task A to A and map timer boundary event Timer1 to Timer2 so the engine migrates the timer subscription and the elapsed five days carry over to the target boundary event

d.text: Correct. Providing mappings for both the active element and the catch event migrates the timer subscription. The five days already elapsed are preserved, so the migrated instance waits only the remaining time before the timer fires on the target boundary event.

c.text: Incorrect. Without a mapping for the catch event, the existing timer subscription is closed and a new one is opened against the target boundary event. The timer effectively restarts and the previously elapsed time is lost.

b.text: Incorrect. The migration plan must map every active element. Omitting the user task mapping causes the validation step to reject the migration command, so no subscription migration takes place at all.

a.text: Incorrect. There is no elapsedDuration variable contract in process instance migration. Mapping the catch event itself is what preserves the subscription and remaining duration; no variable instruction is involved in catch event migration.

explanation: Mapping a catch event during process instance migration preserves its event subscription, so a timer boundary event that has already advanced keeps its progress on the target boundary event. The active element it is attached to must also be mapped so the validation step accepts the plan.

scenario: Aurora SaaS runs 8000 claims per day through a process where each claim waits at user task Review with a 7-day timer boundary event. 3 days have already elapsed for the in-flight claims. The team is rolling out a new process version that adds a follow-up task after the timer fires and they do not want to restart the timer for any of the running claims during migration.
question: What does mapping the existing timer boundary event to the target timer boundary event do during a process instance migration?

options.text: It migrates the timer subscription to the target boundary event so the elapsed three days are preserved and the timer fires after the remaining time on the new boundary event
It promotes the timer subscription into a global signal subscription so any claim that hits the target boundary event fires the timer immediately on migration completion
It removes the timer subscription entirely so the user task continues without any timer boundary and the team must publish a message to advance the migrated claim
It cancels the timer subscription and opens a fresh subscription on the target so the timer restarts and waits the full duration defined on the target boundary event

a.text: Correct. When a catch event is mapped during migration, the associated subscription is migrated to the target catch event. For a timer, that preserves the time already elapsed, so the migrated instance waits only the remaining duration before the timer fires.

d.text: Incorrect. Cancelling and reopening the subscription is what happens when the catch event mapping is omitted. A mapping between source and target catch events explicitly preserves the existing subscription rather than restarting it.

b.text: Incorrect. There is no promotion of timer subscriptions into signal subscriptions during migration. Catch event subscriptions stay the same type and are simply rebound to the target catch event when a mapping is provided.

c.text: Incorrect. Mapping the boundary event keeps the timer attached and preserves the subscription. Removing a subscription happens only when the source catch event has no mapping and the target has no corresponding event.

explanation: Providing a mapping instruction between a source and target catch event during migration tells the engine to migrate the existing subscription. For timer boundary events this means the previously elapsed duration carries over to the new boundary event.

scenario: A logistics platform processes 1200 shipments per day through a process that contains a message event subprocess whose start event listens for the message shipment-cancelled. The team is migrating 320 instances to version 12 of the process and needs to keep the same message name in flight so existing message correlations still reach the migrated instances after the migration completes.
question: Which behavior occurs when the source message event subprocess start event is mapped to the target start event during a migration?

options.text: The message subscription is duplicated, opening a second active subscription on the target start event so the same message can correlate twice into the migrated process instance
The message subscription is paused until the next deployment refresh, after which it is reopened with the original message name and any buffered messages are delivered
The associated message subscription is migrated to the target start event, preserving the message name and any in-flight correlation for the running process instance
The message subscription is converted into a signal subscription that broadcasts to every running instance of the target process definition rather than correlating by name

c.text: Correct. Mapping a catch event during migration migrates the associated subscription. For a message event subprocess start event, that preserves the message name and any in-flight correlation, which is the documented way to keep the same message name across the migration.

a.text: Incorrect. Migrating a catch event subscription rebinds the existing subscription to the target; it does not create a second active subscription on the target. Only one subscription is associated with the migrated catch event.

d.text: Incorrect. Catch event subscriptions do not change type during migration. A message subscription stays a message subscription and continues to be correlated by message name and correlation key on the target.

b.text: Incorrect. There is no pause-and-replay phase during migration. Subscriptions are migrated, removed, or added in the same transactional migration command, not buffered between deployment refreshes.

explanation: Mapping a message event subprocess start event during migration migrates the associated message subscription to the target start event. This is the documented mechanism for keeping the same message name available to in-flight instances after the migration.

scenario: An e-commerce retailer processes 6000 orders per day through a process where service task A currently has no boundary events. In version 9 the team adds a non-interrupting message event subprocess with message start event M to the same process. They want all 220 in-flight orders waiting at service task A to begin listening for the new message right after migration.
question: What happens to event subscriptions when active service task A is mapped to A in the target and the target process adds a new message event subprocess start event M?

options.text: An existing subscription named M is reused if one is already cached at the broker; otherwise the migration fails with a missing-subscription rejection during validation
No new subscription is opened for start event M because the migration plan did not explicitly request the new subscription via a dedicated activate instruction
A new message subscription is opened for the target start event M during migration because that target catch event is not the target of any mapping instruction in the plan
A new subscription is opened only after the next deployment refresh runs because the migration command itself does not register subscriptions for newly added catch events

c.text: Correct. If a catch event of the target process is not the target of a mapping instruction, a new subscription is opened during migration. The non-interrupting message event subprocess start event M therefore receives a fresh message subscription on the migrated instance.

b.text: Incorrect. The migration command does not have a separate activate instruction for catch events. Adding a new subscription is driven by the presence of an unmapped target catch event in the target definition, not by an explicit activation step.

a.text: Incorrect. The engine does not cache message subscriptions across process definitions. For newly added target catch events, a new subscription is always opened during migration rather than reused.

d.text: Incorrect. Subscriptions for newly added target catch events are opened by the migration command itself within the same transactional step. They do not depend on a later deployment refresh to start receiving events.

explanation: Adding a catch event during migration is handled implicitly: when a target catch event has no mapping pointing to it, the engine opens a new subscription for it as part of the migration. This is how new boundary or event subprocess start events become active on migrated instances.

scenario: A SaaS provider rolls 1800 instances back from a process version containing a message event subprocess to an earlier version that no longer has that subprocess. Service task A remains active in both versions and the team needs the message subscription removed during migration so the rolled-back instances no longer correlate the message that the older version does not handle.
question: Which migration plan removes the existing message subscription when migrating service task A to the version that no longer contains the message event subprocess?

options.text: Provide only a mapping from active service task A to A so the source message subscription is closed because no target catch event is referenced as the destination of a mapping
Provide a mapping from active service task A to A and add a variable instruction unsubscribe set to true so the engine closes the orphan message subscription on activation
Provide no mappings at all so the engine drops every event subscription on the source process instance and migrates the active element using its default element identifier
Provide a mapping from active service task A to A and a mapping from the source message start event to a special tombstone target id so the broker removes the subscription

a.text: Correct. A catch event in the source process whose mapping is not provided has its associated subscription closed during migration. Mapping only the active service task A is therefore sufficient to remove the orphan message subscription from the rolled-back instance.

d.text: Incorrect. There is no tombstone target id in the migration command. Removal of a source subscription is signalled implicitly by simply not providing a mapping for that source catch event in the migration plan.

c.text: Incorrect. The migration plan must map every active element. Without a mapping for the active service task A, the validation step rejects the migration command and no subscriptions are touched at all.

b.text: Incorrect. The migration command does not expose an unsubscribe variable instruction. Subscription removal is controlled solely by omitting the catch event from the migration plan, not by a variable side channel.

explanation: If a catch event in the source process is not mapped, the engine closes its subscription during migration. So removing an event subscription is achieved by simply leaving its source catch event out of the migration plan while still mapping every active element.

scenario: A fintech runs 3000 instances per day. In some instances service task A charge-customer is currently active; in others it has already completed. The target process definition adds a new compensation boundary event to task A. The team must choose the migration plan that ensures a compensation subscription exists for as many instances as possible after migration completes.
question: Which mapping correctly migrates the in-flight instances so a compensation subscription is created for the instances still active on service task A?

options.text: Provide no mapping for service task A so the engine adds a default compensation subscription to every active element instance in the source process regardless of its element id
Map service task A to B so the engine retroactively attaches the new compensation boundary event to every prior execution of A and opens compensation subscriptions for already-completed instances

Map service task A to A so a compensation subscription is opened on completion of the active element A in the target process; instances that already finished A receive no new compensation subscription
Map service task A to A and add a variable instruction compensateOnMigration set to true so the engine immediately opens compensation subscriptions for already-completed instances of A

c.text: Correct. When a compensation boundary event is added during migration, a new compensation subscription is opened only for activity instances that are currently active or have not started yet. Mapping A to A is enough; finished instances of A do not receive a new compensation subscription.

b.text: Incorrect. The engine never opens compensation subscriptions retroactively for activity instances that have already completed. Mapping A to B would also change the active element instead of preserving its identity for the migration.

a.text: Incorrect. A migration plan must map every active element. Without a mapping for service task A the validation step rejects the command, so no compensation subscriptions are opened during migration at all.

d.text: Incorrect. There is no compensateOnMigration variable contract in process instance migration. Compensation subscriptions for already-finished activities cannot be opened by any migration plan, regardless of variable instructions provided.

explanation: Compensation boundary events follow a special rule during migration: a new compensation subscription is opened only for activity instances that are currently active or have not started yet, never for activity instances that already finished before the migration ran.

scenario: An airline updates a process that contains a joining parallel gateway named gateway1 with one taken incoming sequence flow named flow1, by inserting a new element C before the gateway. That creates a new target gateway named gateway2 and a new target sequence flow named flow2. Roughly 350 instances are waiting at gateway1 with flow1 already taken and element B still active in a user task.
question: Which set of mapping instructions correctly migrates the waiting instances to the updated target process definition?

options.text: Map active element B to target B, map gateway1 to gateway2, and map the taken sequence flow flow1 to target sequence flow flow2 so the joining gateway state is preserved on the target
Map active element B to target B and map gateway1 to gateway2; the taken sequence flow does not need a mapping because the gateway state already carries the taken-flow record forward
Map gateway1 to gateway2 only and rely on the variable scope of the joining gateway to carry both the active element B and the taken sequence flow forward to the target definition
Map only active element B to target B and let the engine auto-infer gateway and sequence flow mappings using the BPMN element names that appear in both definitions during migration

a.text: Correct. Migrating a waiting joining parallel gateway requires mappings for the active element, the joining gateway itself, and each taken incoming sequence flow. All three instructions together let the engine reconstruct the joining state on the target gateway.

d.text: Incorrect. The engine does not auto-infer gateway or sequence flow mappings from element names. Every active element must be mapped explicitly, and joining gateways with taken flows additionally need explicit mappings for the gateway and the taken flow.

c.text: Incorrect. A joining gateway has no variable scope that carries the active element forward, and the active element must be mapped on its own. Mapping the gateway alone leaves the active user task and the taken sequence flow unmapped.

b.text: Incorrect. The taken incoming sequence flow must be mapped explicitly because the joining gateway needs to know which target flow each already-taken source flow corresponds to. Without that mapping the migration plan would be rejected.

explanation: Migrating a joining parallel gateway that has already collected taken sequence flows requires mapping the active element, the joining gateway, and each previously taken incoming sequence flow. The target gateway must also have at least as many incoming sequence flows as the source.

scenario: A healthcare network must roll back 700 instances from version 14 to version 12 of the same bpmnProcessId after a defect is discovered in version 14. The platform team is unsure whether downgrading is even possible and whether a mapping from the source process id to the target process id is required for the migration plan to be valid.
question: Which statement correctly describes whether a process instance can be migrated from a newer version back to an older version of the same process definition?

options.text: The process instance can be migrated only to a different bpmnProcessId entirely; same-id downgrades are blocked by the validation step because the version property would otherwise move backward

The process instance can be migrated only forward to a newer version of the same bpmnProcessId; downgrades require a cancel and recreate workflow because the engine refuses to migrate to an older version
The process instance can be migrated to an older version only if a process-id mapping is provided that maps the source bpmnProcessId to the target bpmnProcessId so the engine accepts the downgrade

The process instance can be migrated to any deployed target definition, including an older version of the same bpmnProcessId, and no mapping is required from the source process id to the target process id

d.text: Correct. A process instance can be migrated from an older version to a newer version of the same process definition, from a newer version back to an older one, or to a different process definition altogether. No mapping from the source process id to the target process id is required.

b.text: Incorrect. Migration is direction-agnostic with respect to versions of the same bpmnProcessId. The engine explicitly supports migrating from a newer version back to an older version without a cancel and recreate workflow.

c.text: Incorrect. Mapping instructions never need to map the source bpmnProcessId itself to the target bpmnProcessId. The process instance migration command does not include a process-id mapping concept.

a.text: Incorrect. Migration to a different bpmnProcessId is allowed, but it is not the only option. Migration within the same bpmnProcessId is supported in both directions, and the version property is updated as part of the migration step.

explanation: Process instance migration can move an instance from an older to a newer version of the same definition, from newer to older of the same definition, or to a different process definition entirely. No mapping from the source process id to the target process id is needed.

scenario: Apex Logistics migrates 900 instances. Each instance currently has a service task with an active job whose job type is send_mail. The target process definition changes the job type expression to = order.next_step, and the platform team expects the worker pool listening for the new job type to start picking up jobs immediately after the migration command completes.
question: What happens to the active job's properties when the process instance is migrated to a target process definition that defines a different job type expression?

options.text: The job type expression is reevaluated against the current process variables during migration so the active job picks up the new job type from order.next_step automatically
The job retains its original properties including the job type send_mail because expressions are not reevaluated and jobs are not recreated during process instance migration
The active job is canceled and a fresh job with the new job type is created on the migrated service task so the worker pool listening for the new type starts picking up jobs immediately
The job type is updated in place but the existing job remains queued so the original worker keeps processing it until completion before any worker for the new job type takes over

b.text: Correct. Migration does not recreate jobs, reevaluate expressions, or reapply input mappings for active elements. The active job keeps all its properties, including the job type send_mail, after the migration completes on the target definition.

a.text: Incorrect. Expressions on active elements are not reevaluated during migration. The job type that was resolved at activation time is preserved across the migration, regardless of the target definition's expression.

c.text: Incorrect. Migration explicitly does not cancel and recreate active jobs. Recreating the job would require process instance modification to terminate and reactivate the service task, not migration.

d.text: Incorrect. The job is not updated in place; it keeps all its original properties. There is no transitional state where the job type changes but the existing job continues to drain through its original worker.

explanation: Process instance migration does not recreate jobs, reevaluate expressions, or reapply input mappings for active elements. Existing jobs and variables continue with the same values, so changing a job type expression in the target definition does not affect already-active jobs.

scenario: A telecom operator with 1100 instances migrates each one to a new process definition that changes the bpmnProcessId and version. The platform team wants to understand precisely which top-level properties of the process instance itself are updated as part of the migration step that handles the outermost active element of the instance.
question: Which properties on the process instance are updated when the engine migrates the outermost active element during a process instance migration?

options.text: Only the processDefinitionKey is updated on the process instance while bpmnProcessId and version are left untouched so external systems can still resolve the original definition lookup
The processDefinitionKey is updated and a new process instance key is generated so the migrated instance receives a fresh identifier across the Operate and Tasklist audit tables
The processDefinitionKey, bpmnProcessId, and version properties of the process instance are updated to the target process definition, and global variables are migrated as well
The processDefinitionKey, bpmnProcessId, and version are updated and the global variables are reset to the target definition defaults so the migrated instance starts with clean data

c.text: Correct. After validation, the migration of the outermost active element updates the process instance's processDefinitionKey, bpmnProcessId, and version properties to the target process definition. The global variables of the process instance are migrated as part of this same step.

a.text: Incorrect. All three properties processDefinitionKey, bpmnProcessId, and version are updated to the target definition; leaving bpmnProcessId or version untouched would leave the migrated instance pointing at the wrong definition for runtime resolution.

b.text: Incorrect. The process instance key is a stable identifier that does not change during migration. Only the process definition properties are updated, so audit references to the same instance remain consistent across the migration.

d.text: Incorrect. Global variables are migrated, not reset to target defaults. Existing variables continue to exist with the same values they had before migration, which is also true for any user tasks and jobs on active elements.

explanation: When the migration of the outermost active element runs, the engine updates the process instance's processDefinitionKey, bpmnProcessId, and version to the target definition. Global variables are migrated as part of the same step and keep their existing values.

scenario: A fintech startup runs 250 instances that contain nested subprocesses with multiple active child instances inside them. The platform team needs to predict the traversal order the engine uses when migrating these active child elements during the execution step of a process instance migration command, so they can reason about audit logs.
question: Which traversal order does the engine use when migrating active child instances within a process instance during the execution step of a migration command?

options.text: The engine migrates active child instances in the lexicographic order of their elementId properties to provide a deterministic traversal that operators can correlate with audit logs
The engine migrates active child instances depth-first, descending the deepest active subprocess branch before backtracking and migrating sibling branches at the higher levels
The engine migrates active child instances in the random order produced by the underlying state store iterator because element ordering is not part of the public migration contract
The engine migrates active child instances breadth-first, migrating each direct active child first before recursing into the active children of each child instance in turn

d.text: Correct. The execution of the migration is performed in a breadth-first manner. The first active child instance of the process instance is migrated, followed by the next active child instance, before the active child instances of each child instance are migrated in turn.

b.text: Incorrect. Depth-first traversal would descend the deepest branch first, which is not the documented behaviour. Migration handles all direct active children before recursing into the children of each child.

c.text: Incorrect. The traversal order is documented and deterministic: a breadth-first walk of the active element tree. It is not delegated to a state store iterator with random ordering.

a.text: Incorrect. The order is determined by the structure of the active element tree, not by lexicographic comparison of elementId values. Element ids do not influence traversal ordering during migration.

explanation: Migration of active child instances is breadth-first: the engine migrates direct active children of the process instance first, then descends one level and migrates the active children of each child instance, until the entire active subtree has been migrated.

scenario: A logistics platform migrates 600 instances where each active service task has an associated active job, an active boundary event subscription, and one or two local variables in scope. The team must confirm which entities tied to each active element are migrated alongside the active element itself when the migration's execution step runs.
question: Which entities associated with an active element are migrated alongside the active element itself during the migration execution step?

options.text: Only the associated event subscriptions are migrated together with the active element; jobs are canceled and recreated and local variables are merged into global variables on migration
The associated jobs, incidents, local variables, and event subscriptions contained in each active element are migrated together with the active element to the target process definition
Only the associated local variables are migrated together with the active element; jobs are kept untouched but moved to a holding queue and event subscriptions are dropped during migration
Only the associated jobs are migrated together with the active element; incidents and local variables are reset to defaults and event subscriptions are reopened against the target definition

b.text: Correct. While traversing each active element, the migration also migrates the jobs, incidents, local variables, and event subscriptions contained in that active element. These entities have their processDefinitionKey, bpmnProcessId, and elementId properties updated to the target definition.

d.text: Incorrect. Incidents and local variables are not reset during migration; they are migrated with the active element. Event subscriptions are not blindly reopened either; they are migrated, removed, or added according to the mapping instructions.

a.text: Incorrect. Jobs are not canceled and recreated during migration. They are migrated together with the active element, and local variables stay local rather than being merged into global variables of the process instance.

c.text: Incorrect. Jobs are migrated together with the active element, not moved to a separate holding queue. Event subscriptions are not dropped either; they follow the catch event mapping rules of the migration plan.

explanation: For each active element migrated, the engine also migrates the jobs, incidents, local variables, and event subscriptions contained in that element. All of these entities have their processDefinitionKey, bpmnProcessId, and elementId properties updated to point at the target.

scenario: A telco operator team plans a migration that converts a parallel multi-instance body into a sequential multi-instance body for the same activity, expecting around 50 orders to switch execution order. The architect needs to confirm whether this kind of change between parallel and sequential multi-instance markers is permitted by the migration tool in Camunda 8.
question: Which statement correctly describes the supported direction of multi-instance body migration between parallel and sequential markers?

options.text: A sequential multi-instance body can be migrated to a parallel multi-instance body only when fewer than 10 child instances have already started in the current execution window
A parallel multi-instance body can be migrated to a sequential multi-instance body when the input collection variable is migrated separately as a global variable before the migration command runs
It is not possible to migrate a parallel multi-instance body to a sequential multi-instance body, nor to migrate a sequential multi-instance body to a parallel one
A parallel multi-instance body can be migrated to a sequential multi-instance body only if the loop cardinality is fixed at deployment time and matches between source and target

c.text: Correct. It is not possible to migrate a parallel multi-instance body to a sequential multi-instance body, and the reverse direction is equally not allowed. The multi-instance marker type must remain the same across source and target for a migration plan to be valid.

d.text: Incorrect. Loop cardinality has no influence on this restriction. The multi-instance marker conversion is not allowed at all, regardless of whether the loop cardinality is fixed or dynamic in the source and target.

a.text: Incorrect. The number of already-started child instances is not the gating factor. Migration between sequential and parallel multi-instance markers is unsupported in both directions, independent of how many child instances exist.

b.text: Incorrect. Pre-migrating the input collection variable does not unlock multi-instance marker conversion. The marker type itself cannot change during a migration, regardless of how variables are prepared beforehand.

explanation: Multi-instance body migration has a strict limitation: the marker type cannot change. A parallel multi-instance body cannot be migrated to a sequential one and a sequential multi-instance body cannot be migrated to a parallel one.

scenario: A SaaS provider plans to migrate 200 instances and asks whether the user task implementation can be changed from a Camunda user task back to a job worker user task as part of the migration plan in Camunda 8.8. The platform team needs the supported direction of user task implementation change at version 8.8.20.
question: Which direction of user task implementation change is allowed by mapping instructions during migration since Camunda 8.8.20?

options.text: Both directions of user task implementation change are allowed, provided the source and target user tasks share the same form key and candidate group expression
A Camunda user task can be migrated to a job worker user task, but a job worker user task cannot be migrated to a Camunda user task using mapping instructions in the plan
A job worker user task can be migrated to a Camunda user task, but a Camunda user task cannot be migrated back to a job worker user task using mapping instructions
Neither direction of user task implementation change is allowed; mapping instructions can never change the user task implementation type at any patch level of Camunda 8.8

c.text: Correct. Since Camunda 8.8.20 mapping instructions can change the user task implementation from a job worker user task to a Camunda user task, but not the other way around. Migrating a Camunda user task back to a job worker user task is not supported.

b.text: Incorrect. The supported direction is the reverse: job worker user task to Camunda user task, not Camunda user task to job worker user task. Mapping a Camunda user task back to a job worker user task is rejected by validation.

a.text: Incorrect. Only one direction is supported and it does not depend on form key or candidate group equivalence. The constraint is purely about the implementation type direction, not about the matching of other user task properties.

d.text: Incorrect. Before 8.8.20, no user task implementation migration was possible, but starting with 8.8.20 the job worker user task to Camunda user task direction is explicitly supported by mapping instructions.

explanation: Mapping instructions can change the user task implementation only from a job worker user task to a Camunda user task, and only since Camunda 8.8.20. The reverse direction is not supported, and before 8.8.20 no implementation change was possible.

scenario: Aurora, a logistics provider on Camunda 8.8, runs a dispatch process with an interrupting message event subprocess containing task C. Across 3500 shipment instances, task A is complete and task B is active; the external system that publishes the interrupting message has been down for 8 hours. The operator wants to use a modification to activate the interrupting event subprocess and replicate the documented effect on task B.
question: What is the documented effect on task B when Aurora activates the interrupting event subprocess by modification?

options.text: The engine activates the event subprocess, terminates task B, and also re-activates task A so the process replays from the beginning of the event subprocess flow at Aurora
The engine terminates task B automatically once the interrupting event subprocess is activated by modification and also clears any other running activities inside the dispatch process at Aurora
The engine rejects the activation because an interrupting event subprocess can only be activated by the real message; modification targets are limited to non-interrupting subprocesses at Aurora
The engine activates the event subprocess and enters its start event, but task B keeps running, so both task B and task C are active until task B is terminated explicitly at Aurora

b.text: Incorrect. The docs explicitly state that activating the interrupting event subprocess by modification does not interrupt the process instance or terminate task B automatically.

c.text: Incorrect. The Activate an interrupting event subprocess section describes using modification to activate exactly this construct, so it is supported, not rejected.

d.text: Correct. The docs state the event subprocess becomes active and enters its start event, but the activation does not interrupt the process instance or terminate task B, so both tasks B and C are active.

a.text: Incorrect. The docs do not describe re-activating task A; only the event subprocess is activated and task B remains running until explicitly terminated by an additional instruction.

explanation: Activating an interrupting event subprocess via modification activates the subprocess and enters its start event, but the interrupting semantics do not fire. Task B keeps running, so to simulate true interruption an additional terminate instruction for task B is required.

scenario: Zenith Bank runs a card dispute process on Camunda 8.8 with a non-interrupting message event subprocess containing task C. Across 4200 dispute instances, task A completed and task B is active; the upstream messaging system has been down for 3 hours and cannot deliver the message payload. The operator wants to activate task C and supply the variables that the missing message would have provided as local to the event subprocess.
question: How does the modification command set the message variables so they behave as local to the non-interrupting event subprocess at Zenith Bank?

options.text: Include a variable instruction that defines a scope inside task C at Zenith Bank, so the engine writes the variables as local to task C before its input mappings execute
Include a variable instruction without a scope on the modification command at Zenith Bank, so the engine writes the variables globally in the root scope of the dispute process instance
Issue a separate set-variables command after activating task C at Zenith Bank, so the engine writes the variables only after the input mappings of task C have executed

Include a variable instruction whose scope is the event subprocess flow scope on the activation at Zenith Bank, so the engine writes the variables as local to the event subprocess before activating task C

b.text: Incorrect. Omitting the scope writes the variables globally in the root scope of the process instance, which is not local to the event subprocess as the operator requires.

c.text: Incorrect. The docs describe variable instructions on the modification itself; the engine sets the variables before activating the element so they are available during input mappings and subscriptions.

a.text: Incorrect. The variable scope must be a flow scope of the activating element; setting variables local to task C itself is not the documented mechanism for making them local to the event subprocess.

d.text: Correct. The docs state that a variable instruction can define a scope, that the scope must be a flow scope of the activating element, and that scoped variables are written as local to that scope, for example the event subprocess.

explanation: Variable instructions on a modification set variables before the target element is activated, so they are available for input mappings and event subscriptions. With a scope, variables are local to that flow scope of the activating element; without a scope, they are written globally to the root scope.

scenario: Orion Bank runs 4000 instances per day in Camunda 8.8 inside a payments orchestration. After 2 hours of stuck workers, operations sends a modification command that contains both activation and termination instructions to repair a batch of in-flight process instances and needs to know exactly how the engine sequences and commits the instructions to preserve subprocess context.
question: How does the engine sequence and commit activation and termination instructions inside a single modification command?

options.text: The engine applies all activation instructions first, then all termination instructions, and rejects the whole command if any single instruction fails to apply to the instance.
The engine applies activations first and terminations second and commits each instruction independently without rolling back outcomes from a failed instruction.
The engine applies activations and terminations in the client-provided order and commits each instruction independently to the partition log of the instance.
The engine applies all termination instructions first, then all activation instructions, and rejects the whole command if any single instruction fails to apply to the instance.

a.text: Correct. The engine applies all activation instructions first and then all termination instructions, and it treats the modification command transactionally: if any single instruction cannot be applied, the engine rejects the command and leaves the process instance unchanged.

d.text: Incorrect. The documented order is activations before terminations, which keeps a scope alive when a termination would otherwise empty it before activations have run.

c.text: Incorrect. The engine does not commit each instruction independently and does not honour a client-provided order across activations and terminations; it applies the two phases in a fixed sequence and atomically.

b.text: Incorrect. The modification command is transactional and applies all or nothing; partial application after a failed instruction is explicitly ruled out by the documented behaviour.

explanation: A modification command runs activations first, terminations second, and is transactional. The phased order keeps scopes alive long enough for new activations to attach, and the all-or-nothing commit guarantees the instance stays in its prior state on rejection.

scenario: Lumen Bank operates a payments orchestration in Camunda 8.8 across 3000 transactions per day. An on-call engineer wants to use process instance modification to repair 12 instances after a partial outage of PT2H, but a senior architect cautions that the engine does not detect every unsafe modification and recommends rehearsing the command on a non-production cluster first.
question: What outcome does the documentation say the engine does not detect when an operator modifies a process instance?

options.text: Unreachable outcomes like parallel-gateway deadlocks, missing variables, or skipped interruptions inside a modification command are left undetected by the broker.
The engine validates that all variables expected at the activated element are present and blocks the modification until missing variables are supplied.
The engine rejects an activation that would deadlock a parallel joining gateway, surfacing the conflict to the caller as a rejection on the command.
The engine detects when activating an element inside an interrupting event subprocess would skip an interruption and prevents the modification automatically.

c.text: Incorrect. The documentation gives the example of activating task A again and leaving the process instance stuck on the parallel gateway, which shows that the engine does not reject deadlock-inducing activations on its own.

b.text: Incorrect. The documentation gives the example of activating task D without setting the variables a message would have provided, which shows that the engine does not block activations due to missing variables.

d.text: Incorrect. The documentation gives the example of activating task E inside an interrupting event subprocess, where the existing task C is not interrupted and variables can be overridden, which shows that the engine does not detect a skipped interruption.

a.text: Correct. The page calls out three unsafe outcomes, parallel-gateway deadlock, missing variables, and skipped interruption, and states explicitly that the engine does not detect these situations, so it is up to the operator to apply suitable modifications.

explanation: Process instance modification is powerful but unguarded against unreachable states. The engine will not refuse activations that create parallel-gateway deadlocks, leave required variables unset, or bypass an interruption; testing on a non-production cluster or with Camunda Process Test is the documented safeguard.

scenario: Forge Bank has 75 loan approval process instances stuck on message catch event B after 2 hours of downtime in an external publishing system. The team must repair the stuck instances using the modification command to advance execution past the catch event onto the next service task C without waiting for the missing message correlation to arrive.
question: Which pair of modification instructions repairs a process instance stuck on a message catch event B by moving execution to the next task C?

options.text: Terminate the active instance of the catch event B and activate the element C so the process instance leaves the stuck catch event and continues on the next task in the same scope
Republish the missing message correlation through the modification command so the catch event B fires immediately and the regular sequence flow advances to the next task C
Terminate the entire process instance and recreate it starting at element C using the create-and-start-at-user-defined-element API to bypass the stuck catch event entirely
Activate the element C and leave the catch event B subscription open so the instance proceeds along the new branch while still listening for the missing message in the background

a.text: Correct. Process instance modification moves the token from the stuck catch event B to the next task C by issuing two instructions in one command: terminate the active instance of catch event B and activate element C. The process instance can then continue along the regular flow.

d.text: Incorrect. Leaving the catch event B subscription open would let a late message still trigger the original branch, doubling the work. The documented repair is to terminate the catch event instance and activate the next element together.

b.text: Incorrect. The modification command does not republish messages. Repair is performed by terminate and activate instructions, not by injecting a message correlation into the stuck catch event from the modification command itself.

c.text: Incorrect. Cancel and recreate works as a fallback for unsupported cases of migration, but for an in-flight instance stuck on a catch event the documented repair is the terminate-plus-activate modification pair, which preserves the instance key.

explanation: To repair a process instance stuck on a message catch event, modification combines two instructions in one command: terminate the active catch event instance and activate the next element. The token effectively moves past the stuck point and the instance can continue.

scenario: A SaaS architect plans to use the modification command on 120 instances. Each command contains 3 activation instructions and 2 termination instructions. The team needs to confirm the deterministic order in which the engine applies the instructions inside one command so they can reason about subprocess lifecycle and avoid accidental scope termination.
question: Which order does the process instance use when applying activation and termination instructions within a single modification command?

options.text: It interleaves activation and termination instructions in the order they appear in the modification command so the caller can control fine-grained ordering of operations explicitly
It applies all termination instructions first to free element slots, then applies all activation instructions so newly activated elements do not collide with elements about to be removed
It applies activation and termination instructions in parallel against the engine state, relying on transactional locking to resolve any conflicts that surface during the modification batch
It applies all activation instructions first, then applies all termination instructions afterward, so the subprocess or process instance still has an active element when the terminations run

d.text: Correct. The process instance applies all activation instructions first, then applies all termination instructions. This ordering matters when the modification terminates the last active instances of a scope while also activating an element there, because the activated element keeps the scope alive.

b.text: Incorrect. Termination-first would risk terminating the last active instance of a scope before any new element is activated, which would end the subprocess or process instance prematurely and reject the activation.

a.text: Incorrect. The order is fixed by the engine, not by the order of instructions provided in the command. Callers cannot interleave activations and terminations to control the sequence within a single modification command.

c.text: Incorrect. Modification instructions are not applied in parallel. They are applied serially in the documented order, activations first and then terminations, within a single transactional command.

explanation: Within a single modification command the engine applies all activation instructions first and then all termination instructions. This order ensures that any newly activated element keeps a scope alive even when terminations would otherwise remove the last active element of that scope.

scenario: A healthcare provider's process instance has an embedded subprocess with two concurrently active instances after a parallel split. The operations team wants to activate task C inside that subprocess across 25 instances for stuck patient cases via process instance modification, and the architect must explain how the engine resolves which subprocess instance to use when the enclosing scope is ambiguous.
question: What is the engine's behavior when activating a nested element if the enclosing subprocess has more than one active instance?

options.text: The engine activates a new copy of the element inside every active subprocess instance, so the same modification is duplicated across the existing branches automatically by the runtime
The engine selects the most recently created subprocess instance based on activation timestamp and activates the element there, so operators get a deterministic placement strategy by default
The engine creates a new subprocess instance and activates the element inside it, alongside the existing two active subprocess instances, to keep parallel branches isolated from each other
The engine rejects the modification command because it cannot decide in which active subprocess instance to activate the element, so the activation instruction is not applied at all

d.text: Correct. If the subprocess has more than one active instance, the engine cannot decide where to place the nested element. The activation instruction is not applied and the modification command is rejected, so the process instance state remains unchanged.

c.text: Incorrect. The engine does not create a new subprocess instance alongside existing ones in this situation. It rejects the command instead, leaving the operator to choose a more specific path through additional instructions.

a.text: Incorrect. The engine never fans out a single activation instruction across multiple active subprocess instances. Activation targets exactly one element in one scope; duplicate activation across branches is not part of the modification contract.

b.text: Incorrect. There is no most-recent-instance heuristic. The engine refuses to guess which subprocess to use and rejects the activation instruction outright when more than one active instance exists.

explanation: When activating a nested element, the engine needs exactly one active instance of the enclosing subprocess. If multiple active subprocess instances exist, the engine cannot decide where to place the element and rejects the modification command.

scenario: Lattice Bank has a process instance waiting on user task B after completing task A. Operators must activate an interrupting message event subprocess that contains task C across 30 instances because the external publisher is offline. The team wants to confirm whether activating the interrupting event subprocess via modification automatically terminates task B as the BPMN semantics suggest.
question: What is the side effect on task B when an interrupting event subprocess containing task C is activated through a modification command?

options.text: Task B is not terminated automatically; the activation makes the event subprocess active and starts task C, and a separate terminate instruction is required to interrupt task B
Task B is terminated automatically the moment the interrupting event subprocess becomes active because the subprocess interrupting flag propagates through the modification command
Task B is paused for the duration of the event subprocess and resumes automatically once task C completes, so no explicit termination instruction is required from the operator
Task B is moved into the event subprocess scope and continues executing alongside task C inside the event subprocess until either of them completes and ends the scope

a.text: Correct. Activating an interrupting event subprocess through modification does not interrupt the process instance or terminate task B; both task B and task C are active after the activation. To simulate interrupting behavior, the modification must include a terminate instruction for task B.

b.text: Incorrect. The interrupting flag of the event subprocess is not propagated through modification activation. The activation only starts the event subprocess and its start event; it does not terminate sibling tasks like B automatically.

c.text: Incorrect. Modification does not pause sibling tasks while an event subprocess runs. Both task B and task C are active concurrently after the activation, until terminated or completed independently.

d.text: Incorrect. Task B is not re-parented into the event subprocess scope. It remains in its original scope while task C runs in the event subprocess, so the two tasks live in separate scopes after the activation.

explanation: Activating an interrupting event subprocess via modification does not by itself interrupt the process instance or terminate sibling tasks. To get interrupting behavior, the modification must include an explicit terminate instruction for the sibling task that should be interrupted.

scenario: Brightline Telco runs a Camunda 8.8 order-fulfilment process at 12,000 instances per hour. The team plans to use the modification command to terminate task C inside a non-interrupting message event subprocess. Task C is currently the only active element inside that event subprocess, and the surrounding embedded subprocess has no other active children.
question: Which cascading effects does the modification command produce when task C is terminated in this configuration?

options.text: The engine terminates task C, then the event subprocess because it has no remaining active instances, and then the embedded subprocess for the same reason.
The engine terminates task C and the event subprocess, but leaves the embedded subprocess alive until a subsequent termination command is issued explicitly for it.
The engine terminates only task C and leaves both the event subprocess and the embedded subprocess open so a new message can re-trigger them later on.
The engine rejects the termination because task C is the only active instance and a modification cannot terminate the last active instance of a subprocess.

a.text: Correct. Per the documented rules, terminating the last active instance inside a subprocess terminates that subprocess, and this rule cascades upward when the parent then also becomes empty.

c.text: Incorrect. The docs describe automatic upward termination when a subprocess has no remaining active instances, so the event subprocess does not stay open after task C is terminated.

b.text: Incorrect. The cascading rule applies recursively; once the event subprocess terminates, the same condition empties the embedded subprocess and terminates it as well.

d.text: Incorrect. The last-active-instance restriction applies only to child process instances created by a call activity, not to an embedded subprocess inside the same process instance.

explanation: The termination cascade described in the modification docs removes any subprocess that no longer has active instances after its last child is terminated. The only exception is the last active instance of a child process instance, which must be cleared by terminating the call activity that created it.

scenario: Vector Energy authored a Camunda 8.8 outage-handling workflow with 800 instances live at any time. To repair one instance, an operator submits a single modification command containing one activation instruction for a new compensation task and one termination instruction for the last active task in a parallel branch of that instance.
question: How does the engine order these two instructions and what happens if one of them cannot be applied?

options.text: The engine applies both instructions concurrently in a non-deterministic order and commits whichever succeeds while silently ignoring the one that fails.
The engine applies the activation instruction first, then the termination instruction, and rolls back the entire command if either instruction cannot be applied.
The engine applies the termination instruction first, then the activation instruction, and rolls back the entire command if either instruction cannot be applied.
The engine applies the instructions in the order they were submitted and commits each one independently, leaving residual state behind if a later instruction fails.

b.text: Correct. The docs explicitly state activations are applied first and then terminations, and that the command is transactional: either all instructions succeed or none do.

c.text: Incorrect. The documented order is activations first; reversing it would risk terminating the last active instance before the new branch from the activation exists.

a.text: Incorrect. The engine applies the activation and termination phases sequentially, not concurrently, and never commits a partial result of a modification command.

d.text: Incorrect. Submission order does not drive execution; activations always run before terminations and the whole command is all-or-nothing rather than per-instruction.

explanation: A modification command's instructions are applied in two phases: first all activations, then all terminations. This ordering ensures the process instance still has an active element when an activation replaces a soon-to-be-terminated one, and the whole command is applied transactionally as all or nothing.

scenario: Northgate Hospital migrated its Camunda 7 patient-intake process to Camunda 8.8 and now needs to repair 35 instances. The on-call engineer wants to use a modification command to activate a boundary error event attached to a user task so the process picks up a compensation path, instead of waiting for a new error from the worker.
question: What will Camunda 8.8 do when this modification command is submitted?

options.text: The engine applies the modification by creating a new token on the outgoing sequence flow of the boundary event without firing the boundary event itself.
The engine applies the modification and triggers the boundary event, but only if the attached user task is still active at the moment the command is received.
The engine applies the modification and synthesises a synthetic error variable so the boundary error event treats the activation as if it were raised by the worker.
The engine rejects the modification because activating a boundary event is one of the documented limitations of the modification command in Camunda 8.8.

d.text: Correct. The docs list boundary events among the BPMN element types that cannot be activated via a modification command, so the engine rejects the command rather than applying it.

b.text: Incorrect. The restriction on boundary events is unconditional in the documented limitations and does not depend on whether the host activity is still active at the time.

a.text: Incorrect. There is no documented workaround that converts a boundary event activation into a sequence-flow token; activating sequence flows is also explicitly disallowed.

c.text: Incorrect. Modification cannot fabricate an error to fire a boundary error event; the only documented behaviour for activating boundary events is outright rejection.

explanation: The documented list of activation limitations includes boundary events, start events of a process or subprocess, events of an event-based gateway, elements inside multi-instance subprocesses, and sequence flows. Submitting any such activation causes the modification command to be rejected outright.

scenario: Quantum Retail operates a Camunda 8.8 returns process and an SRE plans to issue an experimental modification command on a live cluster to re-activate a task earlier in the flow on roughly 120 instances stuck since a 48-hour worker outage that affected the downstream system.
question: What guidance does the Camunda 8.8 documentation give about validating this modification before it is applied to production instances?

options.text: Validate the modification automatically by relying on the engine's reachability check, which guarantees that the resulting state could be reached by normal execution.
Validate the modification by running it on a single instance first; the engine then rejects subsequent commands when it observes an unintended state in that instance.
Validate the modification by enabling the engine's built-in dry-run flag, which simulates the modification without changing any state on the target cluster at all.
Validate the modification on a non-production cluster or by using Camunda Process Test, with the engine not detecting unintended situations created by the modification.

d.text: Correct. The docs state the engine does not detect unintended modification outcomes and recommend testing the planned modification on a non-production cluster or with Camunda Process Test.

c.text: Incorrect. The modification API has no documented dry-run flag; modifications are applied directly to the targeted process instance and there is no simulate-only mode.

b.text: Incorrect. The engine does not analyse the result of a previous modification to validate later ones; the docs say it has no detection of unintended states at all.

a.text: Incorrect. The docs explicitly warn that modification can create states that are not reachable by regular execution, and the engine does not perform a reachability check.

explanation: Modification is a powerful repair tool but the engine itself does not detect unintended results. The documented mitigation is to validate the planned modification on a non-production cluster or with Camunda Process Test before issuing it against live production instances.

scenario: Atlas SaaS runs 2400 instances per day in Camunda 8.8 across a marketplace settlement process. A platform engineer is documenting how signal subscriptions appear when processes with signal catch events and signal start events are deployed across 6 tenants, so the operations team understands which broadcasts will start new instances and which will resume waiting ones.
question: Which statement describes how the engine creates signal subscriptions in Camunda 8.8?

options.text: Deploying a process with a signal catch event creates a subscription, and encountering a signal start event at runtime fires a matching broadcast across the cluster.
Encountering a signal catch event creates a subscription that waits for a matching broadcast, and deploying a signal start event also creates a subscription that starts an instance.
Encountering a signal catch event publishes a signal that broadcasts to all process instances listening on that signal name across cluster partition leaders.
Deploying a process with a signal start event creates a subscription that only fires for already running process instances that wait on that signal name.

a.text: Incorrect. The subscription lifecycle is reversed here: catch events create subscriptions at runtime when the instance reaches the event, and start events create subscriptions at deployment time; encountering a start event at runtime does not occur.

c.text: Incorrect. A catch event creates a subscription that waits for a broadcast; it does not publish a signal. Broadcasting is performed by throw or end events and by the broadcast API or RPC channels documented separately.

b.text: Correct. A signal catch event creates a subscription at runtime that waits for a matching broadcast, and deploying a process with a signal start event creates a subscription that, when triggered, starts a new process instance.

d.text: Incorrect. A signal start event subscription starts a new process instance when triggered; it does not target already running instances, which is the role of catch event subscriptions.

explanation: Signal subscriptions are created in two ways. Catch events create per-instance subscriptions at runtime that wait for a matching broadcast, and signal start events create deployment-time subscriptions that start a new process instance when triggered.

scenario: Helio Travel runs a Camunda 8.8 booking platform that needs to inform 7,500 instances of the in-flight booking process about a partner-airline outage. The integration team prefers to broadcast the outage from an external monitoring system rather than embedding it inside any of the existing BPMN models in the catalogue.
question: Which Camunda 8.8 mechanism listed in the docs satisfies this requirement to broadcast the signal from an external system?

options.text: Call the Orchestration Cluster REST API Broadcast signal endpoint or invoke Zeebe's BroadcastSignal RPC from the external monitoring system to broadcast the signal.
Configure a signal start event in every booking process and rely on Operate's incident UI to publish the signal across all running instances at once from the console.
Issue a Zeebe job-batch activation command targeting every running booking process so the worker can throw the signal back into the engine through its own response.
Insert a signal catch event into each booking process so the external system can correlate against it the way it would correlate a message by correlation key.

a.text: Correct. The docs list the Orchestration Cluster REST API Broadcast signal endpoint and Zeebe's BroadcastSignal RPC as two of the documented ways to broadcast a signal from outside a running process.

b.text: Incorrect. A signal start event creates new process instances when triggered; Operate's incident UI is not a documented mechanism for broadcasting signals across running instances.

d.text: Incorrect. Signal catch events subscribe to broadcasts but do not themselves broadcast a signal, and correlation by key is the mechanism for messages rather than for signals.

c.text: Incorrect. Job activation is for retrieving and completing jobs and is not a documented way to broadcast a signal into the engine from an external system or worker.

explanation: The docs enumerate three broadcast paths for signals: a signal throw event or signal end event inside a process, the Orchestration Cluster REST API Broadcast signal endpoint, and Zeebe's BroadcastSignal RPC. The latter two are the natural fit when an external system needs to broadcast.

scenario: Helix Marketplace runs a Camunda 8.8 cluster with 24 partitions handling 250 orders per second. The team is considering broadcasting a daily clearAllCarts signal to reset every running checkout instance and wants to understand the performance impact before rolling the broadcast out to production.
question: What performance impact does broadcasting this signal have on the cluster according to the documented signal cardinality?

options.text: The signal is broadcast to a single leader partition that then distributes execution work to peers via a batched fan-out, capping the traffic per broadcast at a constant value.
The signal is broadcast to all 24 partitions with traffic that scales linearly with partition count, and triggers all matching subscriptions which can keep many processes executing.
The signal is broadcast only to the partition that owns each affected process instance, so the per-broadcast traffic is independent of the total number of partitions in the cluster.
The signal is broadcast only to instances on the same node as the broadcaster, so the impact stays local and does not scale with the cluster's overall partition count at all.

b.text: Correct. The docs warn that signals are broadcast to all partitions with traffic that scales linearly with partition count, and that triggering all matching subscriptions can lead to continued execution of many processes.

c.text: Incorrect. Broadcasts are not partition-scoped to the owning instance; the docs explicitly state that broadcasts reach all partitions in the cluster regardless of which one owns an instance.

a.text: Incorrect. There is no documented leader-only fan-out for signals; each broadcast hits every partition directly and the traffic therefore does not stay capped at a constant value.

d.text: Incorrect. Broadcasts are cluster-wide and not local to the node where the broadcast was issued, so the impact scales with the total partition count rather than staying on one node.

explanation: The signal docs caution that broadcasting cluster-wide produces network traffic that scales linearly with the number of partitions and triggers every matching subscription. Both effects matter when planning broadcast frequency and timing for signals in production-sized clusters.

scenario: Forge Bank stores 60000 customers per day in Camunda 8.8 inside a deposit-onboarding process. A modeler is choosing variable names that comply with Camunda 8.8 rules so that FEEL expressions in gateways and input mappings can read them safely across 250 instances per minute during peak hours of the onboarding flow.
question: Which proposed variable name complies with the Camunda 8.8 variable naming rules?

options.text: The name order-number, a kebab-case identifier under 492 characters that maps to an order field in the JSON payload of the instance.
The name customer_id, a snake_case alphanumeric identifier under 492 characters that does not collide with a FEEL keyword or operator.
The name 1stOrder, intended as a short alphanumeric identifier mapping a customer's first order in the JSON payload of the instance.
The name order number, a whitespace-separated alphanumeric identifier under 492 characters that maps to a JSON property of the instance.

c.text: Incorrect. A variable name may not start with a number, so 1stOrder is rejected by the documented rules; the suggested alternative is to lead with a letter, for example firstOrder, to satisfy the naming restrictions.

b.text: Correct. customer_id is an alphanumeric identifier using the underscore symbol in snake_case, stays well below the 492-character limit, and does not collide with any FEEL literal, keyword, or operator listed in the variable naming rules.

a.text: Incorrect. The documentation explicitly disallows kebab-case names because the dash is parsed as the subtraction operator, so order-number cannot be used as a variable name.
