date part(date and time("2012-12-25T11:00:00")); the docs document date part() as the FEEL conversion function that returns the date component from a date and time value on the conversion functions page

b.text: Incorrect. The conversion functions page does not document a to date() function; the function that extracts the date component from a date and time value is date(from: date and time).

a.text: Correct. The conversion functions page documents date(from: date and time): date as a signature of date() that extracts the date component from the given date and time, and shows date(date and time("2012-12-25T11:00:00")) returning date("2012-12-25").

d.text: Incorrect. The conversion functions page does not document a date part() function; the function that extracts the date component from a date and time value is date(from: date and time).

c.text: Incorrect. The conversion functions page does not document an as date() function; the function that returns a date from a date and time value is date(from: date and time).

explanation: The date() function has two signatures on the conversion functions page. date(from: string): date parses a calendar date string, and date(from: date and time): date extracts the date component from a date and time value. The page shows date(date and time("2012-12-25T11:00:00")) returning date("2012-12-25").

scenario: Apex Telco processes 9000 events per day on Camunda 8.8 and stores a created field as a date and time value such as date and time("2012-12-25T11:00:00") on the process instance. A modeller needs the documented FEEL call from the conversion functions page that extracts only the time component time("11:00:00") from that date and time value on the process variable.
question: Which call on the conversion functions page extracts the time component from date and time("2012-12-25T11:00:00")?

options.text: time(date and time("2012-12-25T11:00:00")); the docs document time(from: date and time): time as a signature of time() that extracts the time component from the given date and time value and show it returning time("11:00:00")
as time(date and time("2012-12-25T11:00:00")); the docs document as time() as the FEEL conversion function that returns a time from a date and time value on the conversion functions page

to time(date and time("2012-12-25T11:00:00")); the docs document a to time() conversion function that ingests a date and time value and returns its time component as a FEEL time value on the conversion functions page

time part(date and time("2012-12-25T11:00:00")); the docs document time part() as the FEEL conversion function that returns the time component of a date and time value on the conversion functions page

c.text: Incorrect. The conversion functions page does not document a to time() function; the function that extracts the time component from a date and time value is time(from: date and time).

d.text: Incorrect. The conversion functions page does not document a time part() function; the function that extracts the time component from a date and time value is time(from: date and time).

b.text: Incorrect. The conversion functions page does not document an as time() function; the function that returns a time from a date and time value is time(from: date and time).

a.text: Correct. The conversion functions page documents time(from: date and time): time as a signature of time() that extracts the time component from a date and time value, and shows time(date and time("2012-12-25T11:00:00")) returning time("11:00:00").

explanation: The time() function has two signatures on the conversion functions page. time(from: string): time parses a time string, and time(from: date and time): time extracts the time component from a date and time value. The page shows time(date and time("2012-12-25T11:00:00")) returning time("11:00:00").

scenario: Helix Fintech transforms FEEL output across 6000 transactions per day in Camunda 8.8. A DMN rule earlier in the flow produced a list of contexts of the form [{key:"a", value:1}, {key:"b", value:2}] via get entries(); a script task must assemble that list back into a single context for the next decision input.
question: Which FEEL function constructs a context from a list of key-value pair contexts and is the reverse of get entries()?

options.text: Call get value(context, keys) which takes a list of contexts each with a key entry and a value entry and returns the assembled context
Call context merge(contexts) which takes a list of contexts each with a key entry and a value entry and returns the assembled context
Call context put(context, key, value) which takes a list of contexts each with a key entry and a value entry and returns the assembled context
Call context(entries) which takes a list of contexts each with a key entry and a value entry and returns the assembled context

d.text: Correct. context(entries) accepts a list<context> where each element has a string key entry and a value entry; it returns the assembled context and is documented as the reverse of get entries().

c.text: Incorrect. context put adds a single key and value to one existing context and returns the updated context; it does not interpret a list of {key, value} contexts and is not the reverse of get entries().

a.text: Incorrect. get value reads a value from a single context at a nested path defined by keys; it does not assemble a context from a list of key-value pair contexts.

b.text: Incorrect. context merge unions a list of whole contexts; it treats each list element as a full context to merge, not as a single {key, value} pair to assemble into one entry.

explanation: context(entries) constructs a context from a list of contexts that each carry a string key entry and a value entry; it is the reverse of get entries() and returns null if any element is not a context or lacks the required entries.

scenario: Helix Insurance validates filing dates on 7000 claims per day in Camunda 8.8. An incoming claim has a filedOn string of "2024-06-31" and a FEEL expression in a DMN rule evaluates date(filedOn) to convert the value before a downstream age-of-claim comparison. The DMN team needs to know how that conversion behaves for an invalid calendar date.
question: What does the FEEL expression date("2024-06-31") return for this invalid calendar string?

options.text: It returns date("2024-07-01") and rolls the day forward into the next month before completing the conversion
It returns date("2024-06-31") and stores the literal string in the resulting value without calendar validation
It returns date("2024-06-30") and clamps the day to the last valid day of June before completing the conversion
It returns null because the string is not a valid calendar date and June only has 30 days, so parsing fails

c.text: Incorrect. The date(from) function does not clamp invalid days to the last valid day of the month; the documentation states that an invalid calendar date such as 2024-06-31 yields null.

d.text: Correct. The docs state that date(from: string) returns null when the string is not a valid calendar date and cite 2024-06-31 as the example because June has only 30 days.

a.text: Incorrect. The date(from) function does not roll an invalid day forward into the next month; the documented behavior for an invalid calendar date is to return null.

b.text: Incorrect. The function parses the string into a date and validates it as a calendar date; an invalid string such as 2024-06-31 yields null, not an unchecked literal value.

explanation: date(from: string) parses a string into a date and returns null when the string is not a valid calendar date; the docs cite 2024-06-31 as an invalid example because June has only 30 days.

scenario: Helix Logistics combines a delivery date with a daily cutoff time of T23:59:00 for 8000 shipments per day in Camunda 8.8. A FEEL expression in a script task builds the deadline timestamp from date("2012-12-24") and time("T23:59:00") and the routing rule depends on the exact returned value of that call.
question: What value does the FEEL expression date and time(date("2012-12-24"), time("T23:59:00")) return?

options.text: It returns time("T23:59:00") on its own and drops the date argument from the resulting value entirely
It returns date and time("2012-12-25T23:59:00") and advances the date component by one day in the combined value
It returns date and time("2012-12-24T23:59:00") and combines the date component with the given time argument
It returns date and time("2012-12-24T00:00:00") and defaults the time portion to midnight in the combined value

d.text: Incorrect. The function uses the provided time argument; it does not default the time portion to midnight when a non-null time value is passed as the second argument.

b.text: Incorrect. The function does not advance the calendar day; it takes the date component of the first argument as-is and combines it with the time argument unchanged.

c.text: Correct. date and time(date, time) returns a date and time value that consists of the date component of the first argument combined with the time argument; the docs show this exact call returns date and time("2012-12-24T23:59:00").

a.text: Incorrect. The function returns a date and time value, not a bare time; the date component of the first argument is preserved in the combined result alongside the time.

explanation: date and time(date, time) returns a date and time that consists of the date component of the first argument combined with the time argument; for date("2012-12-24") and time("T23:59:00") it returns date and time("2012-12-24T23:59:00").

scenario: Helix Fintech books 11000 transactions per day in Camunda 8.8 with timestamps in Europe/Berlin but reports them in America/Los_Angeles local time. A FEEL expression in a script task must convert @"2020-07-31T14:27:30@Europe/Berlin" into the equivalent Los Angeles local time of 05:27:30 before the value reaches the downstream reporting rule.
question: Which FEEL call converts @"2020-07-31T14:27:30@Europe/Berlin" to America/Los_Angeles local time as 05:27:30?

options.text: Call date and time(date, "America/Los_Angeles") which adjusts the time to 05:27:30 to match the local time of the target zone
Call at timezone(date, "America/Los_Angeles") which adjusts the time to 05:27:30 to match the local time of the target zone
Call date and time(date, "America/Los_Angeles") which keeps the wall clock at 14:27:30 and only relabels the zone of the returned value
Call timezone(date, "America/Los_Angeles") which adjusts the time to 05:27:30 to match the local time of the target zone

c.text: Incorrect. The docs state that date and time(date, timezone) adjusts the time to match the local time of the target timezone when the input has a different zone; it does not preserve the original wall clock.

d.text: Incorrect. There is no built-in timezone(date, timezone) function in FEEL; the documented Camunda Extension for this conversion is date and time(date, timezone).

b.text: Incorrect. There is no at timezone(date, timezone) function in FEEL; the documented Camunda Extension for converting a value to a given timezone is date and time(date, timezone).

a.text: Correct. The Camunda Extension date and time(date, timezone) returns the value at the given timezone and adjusts the time to match the local time of that zone; the docs show @"2020-07-31T14:27:30@Europe/Berlin" becomes 2020-07-31T05:27:30@America/Los_Angeles.

explanation: The Camunda Extension date and time(date, timezone) returns the given date and time value at the given timezone; when the input has a different timezone, it adjusts the wall-clock time to match the local time of the target zone.

scenario: Atlas Bank batches 5000 loans per day into Camunda 8.8 FEEL processing windows. A FEEL expression on a service task input mapping must split a list of application ids into consecutive sublists of size 50 where the final sublist may be smaller than 50 when the total count is not divisible by 50.
question: Which FEEL function returns consecutive sublists of a list each of a given size where the final sublist may be smaller?

options.text: chunk(list, size) returns sublists of exact size and discards any trailing elements smaller than the given chunk size value
partition(list, size) returns consecutive sublists of the list each of the given size and the final sublist may be smaller than size
split(list, size) returns sublists by index ranges and pads the final sublist with null entries when the input list is shorter
slice(list, size) returns one sublist starting at position size and continuing through the end of the input list value

b.text: Correct. partition(list, size) is the documented FEEL function that returns consecutive sublists each of the given size, and the final sublist may be smaller when the input length is not a multiple of size.

c.text: Incorrect. There is no split(list, size) function in FEEL; partition is the documented mechanism, and it does not pad the final sublist.

a.text: Incorrect. There is no chunk(list, size) function in FEEL, and partition does not discard the trailing elements; it returns them as the final shorter sublist.

d.text: Incorrect. There is no slice(list, size) function in FEEL with this semantic; partition returns consecutive sublists, not a single tail sublist.

explanation: partition(list, size) returns consecutive sublists of the list of equal size, and the final sublist may be smaller when the input length is not divisible by size; if size is less than 0 it returns null.

scenario: Lattice Bank renders 8500 transactions per day into a comma-separated audit string in Camunda 8.8. A FEEL expression on a service task input mapping must turn a list of string transaction ids into one string with a delimiter between each element.
question: Which FEEL built-in produces a single delimited string from a list of strings using a chosen separator?

options.text: The concatenate function takes two lists of strings and returns a flattened list joined into a single delimited element
The list contains function checks whether the delimiter appears in the list and returns a string with the matching elements only
The flatten function takes a nested list of strings and reduces it to one string by inserting the delimiter at each level
The string join function with two arguments takes the list and the delimiter and returns one string containing the delimiter between each element

d.text: Correct. The two-argument form of string join takes a list of strings and a delimiter and returns one string with the delimiter between each element, matching the documented signature in the FEEL list functions reference.

b.text: Incorrect. The list contains function returns a boolean indicating membership of an element in the list and does not assemble a delimited output string.

a.text: Incorrect. The concatenate function appends lists end to end and returns a list, not a delimited string built from list items.

c.text: Incorrect. The flatten function reduces nested list structure into a single list and does not insert a delimiter or yield a string result.

explanation: The two-argument form of string join in FEEL takes a list of strings plus a delimiter and returns a single string with the delimiter between each element, ignoring any null items in the list.

scenario: Aurora Insurance formats a list of 3 policy codes into a JSON-like literal across 4200 claims per day in Camunda 8.8. The FEEL expression must wrap the joined output between a leading and trailing marker while keeping a comma separator between each code.
question: How does the four-argument string join function in FEEL assemble a list of strings into a single output?

options.text: It returns a list of strings where each element is wrapped in the prefix and suffix and is separated from the next entry by the delimiter argument
It returns one string that starts with the prefix, places the delimiter between each element, and ends with the suffix as documented in the list functions reference
It returns one string that starts with the suffix, places the prefix between each element, and ends with the delimiter to terminate the value
It returns one string built by concatenating the prefix and the suffix once and ignoring the delimiter unless the list has fewer than 3 entries

c.text: Incorrect. The roles of prefix and suffix are reversed in this statement; the documented behaviour places the prefix first and the suffix last around the joined elements.

b.text: Correct. The four-argument string join returns one string that starts with the prefix, places the delimiter between each element, and ends with the suffix, as shown by the example producing [a, b, c] from the list a, b, c.

a.text: Incorrect. The function returns a single string value rather than a list of wrapped elements; only the outer prefix and suffix appear once around the whole result.

d.text: Incorrect. The delimiter is always placed between elements regardless of list size and the prefix and suffix are added once around the entire joined value.

explanation: The four-argument form of string join in FEEL prepends the prefix, joins the list of strings with the delimiter between each element, and appends the suffix, producing one final string value.

scenario: Forge Bank batches 12000 transactions per day into chunks of 2 for parallel downstream workers in Camunda 8.8. A FEEL expression must produce consecutive non-overlapping sublists of the chunk size from the input list of transaction ids on a service task input mapping.
question: Which FEEL list function returns consecutive non-overlapping sublists of a chosen size from an input list?

options.text: The chunk function returns overlapping sublists of the chosen size centred on each element and pads the last group with null to the requested size
The split function returns a list of pairs of consecutive elements and discards any final element that does not fit the requested chunk size
The slice function returns a single sublist between two indices and requires the caller to iterate to produce successive chunks of the chosen size
The partition function returns consecutive sublists of the same size where the final sublist may be smaller if the list length is not a multiple of the size

b.text: Incorrect. FEEL does not define a list-chunking split function; split is a string function that splits a string on a delimiter rather than a list.

c.text: Incorrect. FEEL list expressions support index access and filtering but do not expose a built-in slice function for producing successive chunks.

d.text: Correct. The partition function in the FEEL list functions reference returns consecutive sublists of the requested size, with the final list possibly smaller, as shown by the example producing the sublists 1, 2 and 3, 4 and 5 from 1 through 5 with size 2.

a.text: Incorrect. The documented partition function returns non-overlapping sublists with no padding; an overlapping or padded chunk function is not defined in FEEL.

explanation: Partition takes a list and a size and returns consecutive sublists of that size, where the final sublist may be smaller than the requested size and a negative size yields null.

scenario: Lattice Insurance evaluates 8000 claims per day in Camunda 8.8 using DMN FEEL inputs. A FEEL expression on a DMN input entry must return true only when every flagged compliance check on a claim is true, and the same expression must return true when the list of compliance checks for a claim is empty.
question: Which FEEL function returns false if any element of a boolean list is false and returns true when the list is empty?

options.text: every(list) returns false if any element is false and returns the first false element from the input boolean list
all(list) returns false if any element is false and returns true when the input boolean list is empty for the claim
and(list) returns false if any element is false and returns null when the input boolean list is empty for the claim
any(list) returns true if any element is true and returns false when the input boolean list is empty for the claim

d.text: Incorrect. any(list) returns true when at least one element is true and returns false for an empty list, so it does not match the required semantics for the compliance check.

b.text: Correct. all(list) returns false if any element of the list is false, otherwise returns true, and for an empty list it returns true, matching the DMN compliance check requirement.

c.text: Incorrect. The function and() was the previous name and has been replaced by all(); it should no longer be used and is not the documented FEEL function in Camunda 8.8.

a.text: Incorrect. There is no every(list) function in FEEL with this semantic, and all() does not return the first false element; it returns a boolean result.

explanation: all(list) returns false if any element of the given boolean list is false; otherwise it returns true, and for an empty list it returns true. It replaced the previous and() function, which should no longer be used.

scenario: Forge Fintech screens 12000 transactions per day in Camunda 8.8 using FEEL on a business rule task. A FEEL expression must raise a fraud signal when at least one of several boolean indicators on a transaction is true, and the same expression must return false when the list of indicators on a transaction is empty.
question: Which FEEL function returns true if any element of a boolean list is true and returns false when the list is empty?

options.text: or(list) returns true if any element is true and returns null when the input boolean list is empty for the transaction
some(list) returns true if any element is true and returns the first true element from the input boolean list
any(list) returns true if any element is true and returns false when the input boolean list is empty for the transaction
all(list) returns true if any element is true and returns true when the input boolean list is empty for the transaction

d.text: Incorrect. all(list) returns false if any element is false and returns true for an empty list; it does not implement the requested fraud-signal semantics for at least one true element.

a.text: Incorrect. The function or() was the previous name and has been replaced by any(); it should no longer be used and is not the documented FEEL function in Camunda 8.8.

c.text: Correct. any(list) returns true if any element of the given list is true, otherwise returns false, and for an empty list it returns false, matching the fraud-signal requirement on a transaction.

b.text: Incorrect. There is no some(list) function in FEEL with this semantic, and any() returns a boolean result rather than the first matching element of the input list.

explanation: any(list) returns true if any element of the given boolean list is true; otherwise it returns false, and for an empty list it returns false. It replaced the previous or() function, which should no longer be used.

scenario: Vertex Logistics emits 7000 shipments per day in Camunda 8.8 through a FEEL output mapping on a service task. The mapping must concatenate the list of tracking codes for one shipment into a single comma separated string for downstream auditing, and must silently skip any null entries inside the tracking code list.
question: Which FEEL call concatenates a list of strings into one comma separated result while ignoring null items?

options.text: string join(list, ", ") joins the list into one result with the delimiter between each element and ignores null items in the list
string join(list, ", ", "[", "]") joins the list into one result with the delimiter between each element and wraps the result
string join(list, "?", true) joins the list into one result with the delimiter between each element and trims null items from the list
string join(list) joins the list into one result with a fixed comma between each element and ignores null items in the input list

a.text: Correct. string join(list, delimiter) joins a list of strings into a single string containing the delimiter between each element, and null items in the list are ignored for the result.

d.text: Incorrect. The single-argument string join(list) inserts no separator between elements; a fixed comma is not added by that form and the comma must be supplied as a delimiter argument.

b.text: Incorrect. The four-argument string join adds a prefix and suffix to the result; the scenario only requires the delimiter form without surrounding brackets.

c.text: Incorrect. There is no boolean trim argument on string join; the documented overloads take only list, list+delimiter, or list+delimiter+prefix+suffix.

explanation: string join(list, delimiter) joins a list of strings into a single string with the delimiter inserted between each element. Null items are ignored; a non-string non-null item causes the function to return null.

scenario: A logistics operator running Camunda 8.8 emits 9000 shipments per day and renders the tracking codes for each shipment as a single audit string formatted like [a, b, c]. A FEEL output mapping on a service task must concatenate the codes with ', ' between elements and must wrap the final string with '[' at the start and ']' at the end.
question: Which FEEL call concatenates a list with a delimiter between elements and wraps the result with a prefix and a suffix?

options.text: string join(list, "[", ", ", "]") joins the list with the prefix as the second argument and uses the delimiter and suffix as positional arguments
string join(list, ", ", "[", "]") joins the list with the delimiter between elements and prepends the prefix and appends the suffix to the result
string join(list, ", ") joins the list with the delimiter between elements and prepends a fixed '[' and appends a fixed ']' to the result
string join(list).wrap("[", "]") joins the list with a chained call that adds the prefix at the start and the suffix at the end to the result

c.text: Incorrect. The two-argument string join only inserts the delimiter between elements; it does not prepend a prefix or append a suffix to the result string.

b.text: Correct. string join(list, delimiter, prefix, suffix) joins the list with the delimiter between elements, starts the result with the prefix, and ends it with the suffix, producing strings such as [a, b, c].

a.text: Incorrect. The documented argument order is list, delimiter, prefix, suffix; the delimiter is the second argument, not the third, so the swapped order does not produce the documented result.

d.text: Incorrect. FEEL has no chained .wrap call on the result of string join; the prefix and suffix must be supplied directly to the four-argument string join overload.

explanation: string join(list, delimiter, prefix, suffix) joins a list of strings into a single string starting with prefix, with delimiter between each element, and ending with suffix. Null items are ignored; a non-string non-null item causes the function to return null.

scenario: Vertex Bank rounds interim balances for 6500 transactions per day in Camunda 8.8. A FEEL expression on a business rule task must round the value 5.5 with scale 0 using a half-down rule, returning 5 rather than 6 to align with the downstream ledger.
question: Which call returns 5 when rounding 5.5 with scale 0 using FEEL round half down?

options.text: round half down with arguments 5.5 and 0 returns 5 because the round-half-down rule rounds the midpoint toward zero at the requested scale
round half down with arguments 0 and 5.5 returns 5 because the first argument is treated as the scale and the second as the value to round
round half down with arguments 5.5 and 0 returns 6 because the round-half-down rule rounds the midpoint upward toward positive infinity at the requested scale
round half down with arguments 5.5 and 0 returns null because the half value cannot be represented exactly when the scale argument is zero

c.text: Incorrect. The round-half-down rule rounds the midpoint toward zero, so the documented example for 5.5 at scale 0 returns 5 rather than 6.

d.text: Incorrect. The function handles 5.5 at scale 0 directly and returns 5 in the documented example; it does not produce null for this input.

b.text: Incorrect. The function signature in the FEEL numeric reference is round half down with the number first and the scale second; the order is fixed.

a.text: Correct. The numeric functions reference shows round half down of 5.5 and 0 returns 5 because the half value rounds toward zero rather than away from zero at the chosen scale.

explanation: FEEL round half down takes the value first and the scale second and rounds the midpoint toward zero, so 5.5 at scale 0 returns 5 and -5.5 at scale 0 returns -5.

scenario: Apex Bank trims an internal prefix from a transaction reference field in Camunda 8.8 across 9200 transactions per day. A FEEL expression with two arguments must keep the substring starting at position 3 of the value foobar so that the downstream worker sees only the tail of each reference.
question: What is the result of the FEEL two-argument substring call on the value foobar with start position 3?

options.text: The expression returns the value bar because the start position is 0-based and the function returns the remainder of the string from that index to the end
The expression returns the value obar because the start position is 1-based and the function returns the remainder of the string from that position to the end
The expression returns the value foo because the start position counts characters to remove from the end before returning what remains
The expression returns the value oba because the start position is 1-based and the function returns three characters beginning at that position

b.text: Correct. The two-argument form returns the substring starting at the 1-based position to the end, so position 3 of foobar yields obar as shown in the documented example.

d.text: Incorrect. Without a length argument the function returns the substring to the end of the input rather than a fixed-length window of characters.

c.text: Incorrect. The start position selects where the result begins, not how many characters to drop from the end of the input.

a.text: Incorrect. FEEL strings are 1-based, so position 3 of foobar begins at the third character o rather than at the index 3 of a 0-based string.

explanation: The two-argument FEEL substring returns the characters from the 1-based start position to the end of the input, and a negative start counts from the end; substring of foobar at 3 is obar.

scenario: Beacon Bank extracts a fixed window of 3 characters beginning at position 2 from a customer reference for 7400 transactions per day in Camunda 8.8. A FEEL expression on a service task input mapping must apply the three-argument substring on the literal value foobar and pass the result to the downstream worker.
question: What is the result of the FEEL three-argument substring call on the value foobar with start position 2 and length 3?

options.text: The expression returns the value oob because the start position is 1-based and the length counts the characters to include starting at that position
The expression returns the value bar because the start position is 0-based and the length counts characters beginning at the start index of the string
The expression returns the value foo because the length is applied first to truncate the input before the start position is read from the trimmed value
The expression returns the value oobar because the length argument is ignored when it is smaller than the remaining characters from the start position

b.text: Incorrect. FEEL substring positions are 1-based, so starting at 2 selects the second character o rather than the third character b as in a 0-based scheme.

a.text: Correct. The three-argument substring returns length characters from the 1-based start position, so foobar at position 2 with length 3 yields oob in line with the documented behaviour.

d.text: Incorrect. The length argument is always honoured when given; it bounds the number of characters returned from the start position.

c.text: Incorrect. The function reads the start position first and then returns up to length characters; it does not truncate the input ahead of applying the start position.

explanation: The three-argument FEEL substring returns up to length characters from the 1-based start position; foobar at start 2 length 3 yields oob, mirroring the documented substring semantics.

scenario: Catalyst Bank screens free-text memos for keyword matches in any letter case across 11000 transactions per day in Camunda 8.8. A FEEL expression on a gateway condition must return true when the input FooBar matches the pattern foo using a documented case-insensitive flag.
question: How does the three-argument FEEL matches function evaluate the input FooBar against the pattern foo with the flags i?

options.text: The function returns true because the i flag enables case-insensitive regular expression matching and the pattern foo therefore matches the leading three characters
The function returns false because the supplied input begins with an uppercase F and the pattern foo can only match a fully lowercase prefix of the input string
The function returns null because the third argument must be a list of single flag characters and a plain string value such as i is rejected at evaluation time
The function returns true only when the pattern matches the entire input from start to end and otherwise returns null when the flags argument is provided

b.text: Incorrect. The pattern is treated as a regular expression and the i flag relaxes letter case, so FooBar against foo with i matches and yields true.

c.text: Incorrect. The flags argument in the documented signature is a string and accepts one or more of the characters s, m, i and x, so the value i is valid.

a.text: Correct. The matches function returns true when the pattern is found in the input under the given flags; the i flag turns on case-insensitive matching, so FooBar against foo with i yields true as in the documented example.

d.text: Incorrect. The function returns a boolean for partial matches as well; it does not require a whole-string match and it does not return null when valid flags are passed.

explanation: FEEL matches returns true when the regular expression pattern matches the input under the given flags; the i flag enables case-insensitive matching, so FooBar against foo with i evaluates to true.

scenario: Delta Bank reformats raw account numbers into a US-style phone-like grouping for an audit report across 13000 transactions per day in Camunda 8.8. A FEEL expression on an input mapping must transform the value 0123456789 into the value (012) 345-6789 using the three-argument replace function with regex capture groups.
question: Which replace call reformats 0123456789 into the value (012) 345-6789 using FEEL regex capture groups?

options.text: Call replace with the value, a pattern of three capture groups for three, three and four digits, and a replacement that places $1, $2 and $3 inside the punctuation of the target string
Call replace with the value, a single pattern that matches the whole input as one group, and a replacement that uses the same group reference three times to repeat the digits
Call replace with the value, a pattern of three groups each capturing a fixed-width run of digits, and a replacement that uses placeholders 1, 2, 3 without the leading dollar character
Call replace with the value, a pattern matching any sequence of digits, and a replacement that lists the literal target string without any capture group references

c.text: Incorrect. The documented replacement syntax uses dollar-prefixed references such as $1; a plain digit without the dollar character is treated as a literal character rather than a capture group reference.

d.text: Incorrect. A pattern that matches all digits as one match without grouping cannot place the captured digits into the punctuation of the target format and would replace the entire string with a static value.

b.text: Incorrect. A single group matching the whole input would yield the original digit sequence on each repetition and would not split the value into three runs of three, three and four digits.

a.text: Correct. The example in the string functions reference uses three capture groups for runs of three, three and four digits and references them with $1, $2 and $3 inside the formatted output to produce (012) 345-6789 from 0123456789.

explanation: The three-argument FEEL replace uses a regular expression pattern and lets the replacement access capture groups with $1, $2 and so on, which is the documented mechanism for the digit-grouping example.

scenario: Echo Bank masks any token resembling SSN inside memo lines for 5400 transactions per day in Camunda 8.8. A FEEL expression on a service task input mapping must perform a case-insensitive replace using a regex pattern and a replacement value, so the four-argument replace function is required to pass the documented flags string.
question: Which form of the FEEL replace function applies a regular expression with a flags string for options such as case insensitivity?

options.text: The four-argument replace function takes input, pattern, replacement and a boolean for whether the replacement string supports capture group references such as the placeholder $1
The four-argument replace function takes input, pattern, replacement and a maximum number of substitutions to perform across the input and ignores any regex flag characters

The four-argument replace function takes input, pattern, replacement and a flags string of characters such as i, m, s or x and applies the regex under those options as documented in the string functions reference
The four-argument replace function takes input, pattern, replacement and a separate fall-back string and uses the fall-back string when the pattern fails to find any match in the input

c.text: Correct. The string functions reference documents the four-argument replace that accepts input, pattern, replacement and a flags string of characters such as i, m, s or x that control regex options including case insensitivity.

b.text: Incorrect. The four-argument form takes a flags string for regex options rather than a numeric cap on the number of substitutions; all matches are replaced.

d.text: Incorrect. No fall-back string parameter is defined for replace; when the pattern does not match, the function simply returns the original input unchanged.

a.text: Incorrect. Capture group references in the replacement are always supported in the three-argument and four-argument forms and are not controlled by a boolean flag.

explanation: The four-argument FEEL replace adds a flags string that controls regex options such as s, m, i and x; the i flag enables case-insensitive matching alongside the standard regex behaviour.

scenario: Forge Bank breaks a CSV memo line into individual tokens for 16000 transactions per day in Camunda 8.8. A FEEL expression on an input mapping must split a string at every occurrence of a regular expression delimiter and pass the resulting list of substrings to the downstream worker.
question: What does the FEEL split function return when it is applied to a string and a delimiter pattern?

options.text: It returns the first substring of the input that appears before the initial match of the delimiter and discards any text that follows the first occurrence
It returns a list of substrings produced by splitting the input string at every match of the delimiter regular expression, as documented in the string functions reference
It returns the count of matches of the delimiter found inside the input string, which the caller can then use to size a downstream list of placeholders
It returns a single string with every match of the delimiter replaced by an empty value, preserving the original character count of the input string for auditing

d.text: Incorrect. The split function does not replace matches; it produces a list of substrings rather than a single modified string of equal length.

b.text: Correct. The documented split function returns a list of substrings produced by splitting the input at every match of the delimiter regular expression in line with the string functions reference.

c.text: Incorrect. The function does not return a count; it returns a list of substrings, and the caller can apply the list count function separately if the size is needed.

a.text: Incorrect. All segments are returned in the result list, not only the segment before the first delimiter match.

explanation: FEEL split takes a string and a delimiter regular expression and returns a list of substrings produced by splitting the input at every match of the delimiter.

scenario: Granite Bank pulls every transaction reference that begins with 12 from a free-text memo for 8800 transactions per day in Camunda 8.8. A FEEL expression on a service task input mapping must apply the extract function on the literal references are 1234, 1256, 1378 with the pattern 12 followed by zero or more digits.
question: What does the FEEL extract function return for an input that has multiple regex matches in the input string?

options.text: It returns a list of every match of the regex pattern in the input string, or an empty list when the pattern does not match anywhere in the input
It returns a boolean indicating whether the regex pattern matches at least once anywhere inside the input string and not the matched characters themselves
It returns the input string with the matched substrings highlighted by surrounding marker characters so the worker can read which segments were detected
It returns only the first match of the regex pattern in the input string and discards any further occurrences that appear later in the input text

d.text: Incorrect. Extract returns all matches of the pattern across the input rather than only the first one, as illustrated by the example returning 1234 and 1256.

c.text: Incorrect. The function does not modify the input or add highlighting characters; it returns a separate list of matched substrings.

a.text: Correct. Extract returns a list of strings containing all matches of the regular expression pattern in the input and returns an empty list when no match is found, in line with the string functions reference.

b.text: Incorrect. A boolean check is the role of matches; extract returns the matched substrings rather than a true or false value.

explanation: FEEL extract takes an input string and a regular expression and returns a list of every substring that matches the pattern, or an empty list when no match is found.

scenario: Nimbus Bank processes 60000 records per day in Camunda 8.8. A FEEL input mapping on a service task must extract a 4 character window from an account reference starting at the third character of the reference, and must safely return whatever characters remain when only 2 characters remain after the start position.
question: Which substring call returns a 4 character window starting at the third position and returns the remaining characters when fewer than 4 remain?

options.text: substring(value, 3, 4) returns a window of length 4 starting from position 3 using one based indexing and returns the remainder when fewer remain
substring(value, -3, 4) returns a window of length 4 starting from the third position from the end and returns null when fewer characters remain
substring(value, 4, 3) returns a window of length 3 starting from position 4 using one based indexing and returns null when fewer characters remain
substring(value, 3, 4) returns a window of length 4 starting from position 3 using zero based indexing and pads the result with spaces when short

d.text: Incorrect. substring uses one based indexing where the first index is 1, not 0; treating the third character as index 2 with zero based indexing does not match the documented start position rule, and the function does not pad short results with spaces.

c.text: Incorrect. The order is substring(value, start position, length); a length of 3 from position 4 returns three characters from the fourth character, which does not satisfy the four-character requirement at position 3.

a.text: Correct. substring uses one based indexing where the first character is at position 1, so substring(value, 3, 4) returns up to 4 characters starting at the third character, and when fewer than 4 remain it returns all characters from the start position to the end.

b.text: Incorrect. A negative start position counts from the end of the value, not from the front; substring(value, -3, 4) starts three characters before the end rather than at the third character.

explanation: substring(string, start position, length) uses 1 as the first index and -1 as the last index. If length is greater than the remaining characters from start position, the function returns all characters from start position through the end.

scenario: Apex Healthcare validates 3000 records per day in Camunda 8.8 using a FEEL rule on a business rule task. The rule must determine whether a patient identifier string matches a regular expression in a case insensitive manner, so the comparison ignores letter casing differences between the identifier and the configured pattern.
question: Which FEEL matches call returns true when the input matches the pattern under case insensitive comparison?

options.text: matches(input, pattern, "x") returns true when the input matches with the comments flag enabled inside the regular expression
matches(input, pattern, "i") returns true when the input matches with the case insensitive flag enabled during the comparison
matches(input, pattern, "s") returns true when the input matches with the dot all flag enabled across newline characters
matches(input, pattern, "m") returns true when the input matches with the multi line flag enabled at line boundaries

c.text: Incorrect. The s flag enables dot-all mode so the . metacharacter matches newline characters; it does not change letter casing behavior of the comparison.

d.text: Incorrect. The m flag enables multi-line mode so anchors match at internal line boundaries; it does not enable case insensitive matching of the input.

a.text: Incorrect. The x flag enables comments mode inside the regular expression; it does not affect case sensitivity of the comparison between input and pattern.

b.text: Correct. The i flag in matches(input, pattern, flags) enables case insensitive comparison, so matches("FooBar", "foo", "i") returns true under the documented FEEL behavior.

explanation: matches(input, pattern, flags) returns true when input matches the regular expression pattern. The flags string can include s (dot-all), m (multi-line), i (case insensitive), and x (comments).

scenario: Forge Bank prints 12000 invoices per day in Camunda 8.8. A FEEL expression must reformat raw 10-digit account references like 0123456789 into the customer-facing form (012) 345-6789 on each statement using the three-argument replace function from the FEEL string built-ins page.
question: What does the three-argument replace function return when applied to the account reference in this FEEL expression?

options.text: Returns a new string with only the first match of the pattern rewritten, while later matches and capture groups are left in the input as written
Returns a new string with every match of the pattern rewritten using the replacement, where $1 and $2 expand to the captured digit groups
Returns the list of substrings that match the pattern in the input string, without performing any rewrite of the original characters
Returns the original input unchanged unless a fourth flags argument is supplied to enable group expansion in the replacement output

b.text: Correct. The three-argument replace rewrites all occurrences of the pattern with the replacement, and $1, $2, and so on expand to the corresponding capture groups from the regular expression, so the documented example (\d{3})(\d{3})(\d{4}) with replacement ($1) $2-$3 emits the formatted account reference.

a.text: Incorrect. replace rewrites every match of the pattern at once and not only the first hit, so claiming that later occurrences remain in the input misrepresents the documented behavior of the three-argument form.

c.text: Incorrect. Returning the list of matching substrings is the documented behavior of extract, while replace constructs a single new string in which every pattern match has been swapped out for the replacement value.

d.text: Incorrect. The three-argument replace already supports capture-group expansion through $1 style references in the replacement, and no flags argument is required to enable that behavior on the input.

explanation: The three-argument FEEL replace rewrites every match of the pattern with the supplied replacement string, and the replacement can refer back to capture groups using $1, $2, and so on to splice the matched digits into a formatted output such as (012) 345-6789.

scenario: Aurora Logistics scans 7200 shipments per day in Camunda 8.8. A FEEL expression must collect every reference code starting with 12 from a free-text field such as references are 1234, 1256, 1378 and return a list like [1234, 1256] for downstream routing, drawing on the documented FEEL string built-ins.
question: Which FEEL string function returns the list of all matches of a regular-expression pattern in the supplied input string?

options.text: The split(string, pattern) function returns a list of substrings produced by cutting the input at every position where the pattern hits the text
The matches(string, pattern) function returns a boolean alongside the matching substrings, indicating whether at least one pattern hit was found
The find(string, pattern) function returns just the first matching substring from the input and a null value when no part of the pattern hits
The extract(string, pattern) function returns a list containing every substring of the input that matches the supplied regular expression pattern

b.text: Incorrect. The matches function on the FEEL string built-ins page returns only a boolean that reports whether the pattern matches the input, and it does not return a list of the matching substrings drawn from the supplied text.

a.text: Incorrect. The split function cuts the input around every pattern hit and returns the surrounding pieces, which is the inverse of what extract does when it collects only the matches themselves from the supplied input.

d.text: Correct. The extract function returns a list of all substrings in the input that match the supplied regular expression pattern, and it returns an empty list when the pattern does not match anywhere in the input, matching the documented references-are-1234-1256-1378 example.

c.text: Incorrect. There is no FEEL function called find in the documented string built-ins page, and returning a single substring would not satisfy the requirement to collect every reference that starts with 12 from the shipment field.

explanation: The FEEL extract function takes an input string and a regular expression pattern, returns a list containing every substring of the input that matches the pattern, and returns an empty list when nothing matches, exactly the behavior described on the FEEL string built-ins page.

scenario: Lattice Insurance enrolls 4500 policies per month in Camunda 8.8. A FEEL expression must label each renewal cohort with the English name of the month derived from the policyStart value, which is supplied as a date such as 2019-09-17 or as a date and time value at noon, drawing on the FEEL temporal built-ins.
question: What does month of year(date("2019-09-17")) return when used to label the renewal cohort in this FEEL expression?

options.text: It returns the numeric month index 9 as a number aligned to the Gregorian month position in the calendar year used by FEEL temporal functions
It returns the full English month name September as a string from the Gregorian calendar, regardless of any engine locale that might be configured
It returns the three-letter ISO short code Sep as a string abbreviation of the Gregorian month that contains the supplied policyStart value
It returns the month name resolved from the engine locale at evaluation time, so the cohort label adapts to the language configured on the broker

a.text: Incorrect. The documented signature is month of year(date): string and the documented example evaluates the September date 2019-09-17 to the literal value September, so the function returns a string and not a numeric month index.

d.text: Incorrect. The documentation explicitly notes that month of year always returns the English name of the month, so the result does not vary with any engine locale configuration when labeling the renewal cohort.

c.text: Incorrect. The example output in the documentation is the full English month name September and not a three-letter abbreviation, so a Sep result would not match the documented behavior of the month of year function.

b.text: Correct. The function returns the month of the year from the Gregorian calendar and always returns the English name of the month, so a September date such as 2019-09-17 yields the string value September for the cohort label exactly as documented.

explanation: month of year accepts a date or a date and time value and returns the English name of the month from the Gregorian calendar as a string, so a September date such as 2019-09-17 evaluates to the literal value September regardless of how the engine is configured.

scenario: Pinnacle Bank schedules 9000 loans per month in Camunda 8.8. A FEEL expression on a process timer must compute the renewal due date as the last day of the month of each contract, where contract.startedAt is supplied as a date and time value such as 2022-10-16T12:00:00, drawing on the FEEL temporal built-ins.
question: What does last day of month(date and time("2022-10-16T12:00:00")) return when used to compute the renewal due date in this FEEL expression?

options.text: It returns the number 31, representing how many days are contained in the calendar month of the supplied input date value
It returns the date value 2022-11-01, representing the first day after the last day of the month containing the input value
It returns the date value 2022-10-31, representing the final calendar day of the month that contains the supplied input value
It returns the date-time value 2022-10-31T12:00:00, preserving the original time component on the last day of the input month

c.text: Correct. The function takes the month of the supplied date or date and time value and returns the last day of that month as a date, so the documented date and time 2022-10-16T12:00:00 yields the date value 2022-10-31 exactly as shown in the FEEL temporal built-ins example.

d.text: Incorrect. The documented signature returns a date and not a date and time value, so the original time component is dropped and the result for the October input is the date 2022-10-31 rather than 2022-10-31T12:00:00.

a.text: Incorrect. The function returns a date value rather than a numeric day count, so although October contains 31 days, the documented result is the date value 2022-10-31 and not the standalone number 31 by itself.

b.text: Incorrect. The function returns the last day of the current month and not the first day of the following month, so the documented result for an October input is 2022-10-31 and not the date 2022-11-01.

explanation: last day of month takes the month of the supplied date or date and time value and returns the last day of that month as a date, so an October input such as 2022-10-16T12:00:00 evaluates to the date value 2022-10-31 regardless of the original time component.

scenario: Harbor Bank routes 7000 transactions per day through a weekday-only batch in Camunda 8.8. A FEEL expression on an exclusive gateway condition must read the day name from the booked date and compare it to a literal weekday list, using the documented day of week function on the date value 2019-09-17.

options.text: It returns the ISO-8601 ordinal date code formatted as 2019-W38-2 and lets the caller derive the day name from the trailing digit of the resulting string

It returns the English name of the day as a string, so the documented call on 2019-09-17 yields the value Tuesday in line with the temporal functions reference
It returns the integer 2 because the Gregorian calendar numbers the days of the week starting at 1 for Sunday and the input is the third day of the week

d.text: Incorrect. The function returns a string rather than an integer; the temporal functions reference shows the result is the English day name.

b.text: Incorrect. The documented function always returns the English name of the day regardless of any cluster locale configuration.

a.text: Incorrect. ISO week dates are produced by other temporal functions; day of week returns a plain English day name as a string.

c.text: Correct. The temporal functions reference documents that day of week returns the English name of the day, so the call on 2019-09-17 yields Tuesday.

explanation: FEEL day of week takes a date or date and time value and returns the English name of the day of the week as a string, regardless of any locale configuration.

scenario: Iris Insurance buckets 5200 claims per day by ISO calendar week in Camunda 8.8. A FEEL expression on a business rule input must derive the ISO week number from the claim date, using the documented week of year function applied to the value 2019-09-17 so the output drives downstream routing.
question: What does the FEEL week of year function return for the input date 2019-09-17?

options.text: It returns the integer 260 because the function returns the ordinal day of the year, from which the caller divides by 7 to derive the week of year
It returns the string Week 38 of 2019 because the function formats the ISO week number alongside the year using a fixed text template before returning it
It returns the integer 37 because the function counts the number of full weeks that have ended in the calendar year before the input date value
It returns the integer 38 because the temporal functions reference defines the Gregorian week number within the year following the ISO 8601 calendar rule

d.text: Correct. Week of year returns a number that follows the ISO 8601 calendar rule, so the call on 2019-09-17 yields 38, as shown in the documented temporal functions example.

b.text: Incorrect. The function returns a number rather than a formatted string; any text template must be assembled by the caller from the numeric result.

c.text: Incorrect. The function returns the ISO week containing the input date, not the count of full weeks already ended before that date.

a.text: Incorrect. The function returns the ISO week number directly rather than the ordinal day of the year, which would require a different temporal function.

explanation: FEEL week of year takes a date or date and time value and returns the Gregorian ISO 8601 number of the week within the year, returning 38 for 2019-09-17.

scenario: Junction Bank labels each ledger entry with the calendar month name for 9300 transactions per day in Camunda 8.8. A FEEL expression on an input mapping must apply the month of year function on the value 2019-09-17 so the worker receives a stable text label across all environments.
question: What does the FEEL month of year function return for the input date 2019-09-17?

options.text: It returns the integer 9 because the temporal functions reference defines month of year as the 1-based ordinal of the month within the calendar year
It returns the localized month name based on the cluster default locale, so the result varies by deployment for the same input date value
It returns the integer 36 because the function counts the months that have passed since the FEEL epoch year of 2017 up to the input date value
It returns the string September because the documented temporal function always returns the English name of the month for a date or date and time input

a.text: Incorrect. The function returns a string rather than an integer ordinal; an ordinal month number requires a different expression on the date value.

d.text: Correct. The temporal functions reference shows month of year returns the English name of the month, so the call on 2019-09-17 yields September.

c.text: Incorrect. FEEL does not define an epoch-relative month counter; month of year is bounded to the calendar year of the input.

b.text: Incorrect. The documented function always returns the English month name regardless of any locale setting at the cluster level.

explanation: FEEL month of year returns the English name of the month for a date or date and time input, so month of year of 2019-09-17 returns September.

scenario: Lumen Insurance schedules a month-end reconciliation timer for 6700 policies per day in Camunda 8.8. A FEEL expression on a timer date must compute the last calendar day of the month containing the bookedDate input value, so the documented last day of month function is used on the value 2022-10-01.
question: What does the FEEL last day of month function return when applied to the input date 2022-10-01?

options.text: It returns the date 2022-11-01 because the function returns the first day of the month following the month of the input date for downstream period boundary checks
It returns the date 2022-10-01 because the function returns the input date unchanged when the input is already the first day of the month value
It returns the date 2022-10-30 because the temporal functions reference defines the result as the last weekday of the month containing the input date value
It returns the date 2022-10-31 because the function takes the month of the input value and returns the last calendar day of that month as a date

c.text: Incorrect. The function returns the last calendar day of the month rather than the last weekday; weekend filtering is not part of the documented behaviour.

a.text: Incorrect. The function stays inside the month of the input value and does not roll forward into the first day of the next month.

b.text: Incorrect. The function ignores the day component of the input and always returns the last day of the input month rather than echoing the input back.

d.text: Correct. The temporal functions reference documents that last day of month takes the month of the given date and returns the last day of that month, so the call on 2022-10-01 returns 2022-10-31.

explanation: FEEL last day of month takes a date or date and time value and returns the last calendar day of the month containing that value, so the call on 2022-10-01 yields 2022-10-31.

scenario: Meridian Bank assembles a small context value with 2 entries on a service task input mapping for 9100 transactions per day in Camunda 8.8. The FEEL context literal sets entry a to 2 and then needs to compute entry b from entry a inside the same literal so that the worker receives a derived value.
question: How does a FEEL context literal handle a later entry that references an earlier entry by name in the same literal?

options.text: The later entry can reference earlier entries only when the context literal is wrapped in an additional parent context and the entries are accessed through a dotted path
The later entry cannot reference earlier entries in the same literal because each key in a context literal is evaluated against the surrounding scope and never against its siblings
The later entry can only reference an earlier entry through the get value function and a string key, with direct name references between sibling entries in the same context literal disallowed
The later entry can reference the previously declared entry by name inside the same context literal, so a context that declares a as 2 and then b as a times 2 evaluates b to 4

d.text: Correct. The context expressions reference documents that previous entries can be accessed inside the same context literal, and the worked example shows a as 2 with b as a times 2 evaluating to a as 2 and b as 4.

b.text: Incorrect. The reference explicitly states that previous entries can be accessed by name within the same context literal during evaluation.

c.text: Incorrect. Direct name references between sibling entries in the same context literal are supported, so wrapping the access through a helper function is not required.

a.text: Incorrect. No outer wrapping context is needed; sibling references in the same context literal are evaluated against the entries already declared.

explanation: Inside a FEEL context literal, later entries can refer to earlier entries by name, which lets the literal compose derived values during evaluation.

scenario: North Bank reads a single nested attribute from a 2-level context value across 10000 transactions per day in Camunda 8.8. A FEEL expression on an exclusive gateway must access the entry b of the context value a, where a itself is a nested context, using the documented dotted path syntax.
question: How does FEEL access a nested entry of a context value through a dotted path expression?

options.text: The expression uses square brackets and a string key, so accessing the entry b inside the context a requires writing the value as a square-bracket lookup with the literal key in quotes
The expression uses a dotted path such as a.b that returns the entry with the key b of the context a, and the path can be chained when the value is itself a context value
The expression uses a call to the get value function with a string key, and direct dotted path access between context entries is disallowed in the FEEL grammar
The expression uses an arrow path such as a then b that returns the entry with the key b of the context a, with dot syntax reserved for filter expressions on list values

a.text: Incorrect. The documented get entry or path syntax uses a plain dot between the context and the key name rather than a square-bracket string lookup.

b.text: Correct. The context expressions reference documents that a.b returns the entry with the key b of the context a and that the path can be chained when the value is itself a context, for example a.b.c.

d.text: Incorrect. The FEEL grammar uses the dot as the path separator for context entries, not an arrow keyword form.

c.text: Incorrect. The dotted path is the documented direct way to access a context entry; get value is an additional helper rather than the only allowed mechanism.

explanation: FEEL accesses a context entry by name with a dotted path such as a.b, and the path can be chained when the value is itself a context for deeper access.

scenario: Oasis Insurance groups 2 validation checks on each journal entry across 5500 records per day in Camunda 8.8. The documented validate-data example assembles a context with named check entries plus a final result list that filters the checks by their violation count.
question: How does the documented validate-data FEEL context example expose only the failing checks at the top level?

options.text: It declares each check with an error key and a violations key, then computes a result entry as a literal of the failing error texts, hard-coded for each kind of journal entry validation
It declares each check with an error key and a violations key, then computes a result entry as a list filter over the check names that keeps only the entries whose violations list is non-empty
It declares each check inside a list rather than a context, and the result entry is the index of the first check whose violations entry is null in the documented example
It declares each check as a function value, and the result entry calls each function in sequence and collects the return values into a single list of error strings for the caller

b.text: Correct. The documented example wraps the named checks inside a context literal and computes the result entry as a list filter over the named checks where the predicate keeps entries with a non-empty violations list (count greater than zero), exposing only the failing checks at the top level.

a.text: Incorrect. The result entry is computed dynamically from the violations of each named check rather than from a hard-coded list of error strings.

c.text: Incorrect. The documented example wraps the named checks inside a context literal rather than a list, and the final result entry is a list filter over the named checks using the count of violations greater than zero as the predicate, not a null check on a list of checks.

d.text: Incorrect. The checks are context entries with error and violations keys, not function values; the result is built by filtering the named checks by violation count.

explanation: The context expressions reference shows a validate-data context that defines named checks with error and violations entries, then uses a list filter over the named checks where the predicate compares the count of violations against zero to expose only the failing checks.

scenario: Vertex Bank summarizes 22000 transactions per day in Camunda 8.8. A modeler writes a FEEL context literal that defines key a with value 2 and key b with value a * 2, expecting the result to be the context {a:2, b:4} as shown in the FEEL context expressions reference for the Literal section.
question: How does a FEEL context literal allow the entry for key b to reference the value of entry a inside the same expression?

options.text: Earlier entries are visible to later entries inside the same literal, so the expression for b can read a directly during a single evaluation of the context
The reference is rewritten by the engine into a lookup against the surrounding variable scope, so a must already be defined outside the literal to satisfy b
Only entries whose key is a quoted string can be referenced by later entries, while entries declared with a name key remain private to their own row in the literal
The literal is evaluated twice, with a second pass recomputing b once every earlier entry has been resolved as a variable into the surrounding scope

d.text: Incorrect. There is no documented two-pass evaluation of a FEEL context literal; the documented behavior is that previous entries are simply in scope while later entries are being evaluated as part of a single pass over the literal.

a.text: Correct. The FEEL context expressions reference states that inside the context the previous entries can be accessed, so a single evaluation of the literal lets the expression for b read the freshly defined value of a and return the documented result {a:2, b:4}.

b.text: Incorrect. The reference resolves against the entries already defined within the same context literal and not against the surrounding variable scope, so there is no requirement that a be defined outside the literal for the example to evaluate.

c.text: Incorrect. The documentation states that the key of a context entry is either a name or a string, and both forms are equally accessible to later entries inside the same context literal.

explanation: Inside a FEEL context literal the previous entries are in scope while later entries are being evaluated, so an expression such as b: a * 2 can read the value just defined for a in the row above and the literal evaluates to the documented context {a:2, b:4}.

scenario: Nimbus Insurance audits 6500 claims per day in Camunda 8.8. A FEEL context expression defines two checks (documentType and ledgerType) where each check records an error message and a violations list, and the modeler must return only the checks whose violations list is non-empty as the final result of the surrounding context, following the validate-data example.
question: Which FEEL pattern from the documented validate-data example keeps only the checks that produced at least one violation?

options.text: Use a for comprehension over check1 and check2 that yields each check whose violations list reports a matching collection entry to the final result
Use a sort by count(violations) over the list of checks so that checks whose violations list is non-empty are pushed to the front of the final result
Use a filter [check1, check2][count(violations) > 0] over the list of checks to keep only those whose violations list contains at least one entry
Use a fold over [check1, check2] that accumulates the first check whose violations list is non-empty and discards every later check from the final result

a.text: Incorrect. The documented validate-data example does not use a for comprehension at the top level to keep the failing checks; it relies on the filter notation [check1, check2][count(violations) > 0] for the result entry of the surrounding context.

b.text: Incorrect. The documented example does not sort the checks by their violation counts; it filters the list of checks to keep only those whose violations list contains at least one matching collection entry from the journal.

c.text: Correct. The documented validate-data example assigns result to [check1, check2][count(violations) > 0], which filters the list of checks down to those whose violations list has at least one entry, matching the audit requirement on the Nimbus journal.

d.text: Incorrect. The documented filter keeps every check whose violations list is non-empty rather than only the first one, so a fold that discards later matches would not return the full set of failing checks from the journal.

explanation: The validate-data example in the FEEL context expressions reference builds a context with two checks and assigns result to [check1, check2][count(violations) > 0], a filter expression that returns only the checks whose violations list is non-empty.

scenario: Pinnacle Bank computes an interim balance on each ledger row for 14000 transactions per day in Camunda 8.8. A FEEL expression on a business rule task divides by a measured rate that happens to be zero on an outlier row, and the engine should map the resulting not-a-number computation to the documented FEEL value.
question: Which value does the FEEL data types reference assign to not-a-number, positive infinity and negative infinity number computations?

options.text: It assigns the largest representable number for positive infinity and the smallest representable number for negative infinity, and not-a-number falls back to the zero numeric value
It assigns a boolean true for any non-finite numeric outcome so the caller can pass the result into a boolean expression and branch on whether the computation overflowed
It assigns the special string NaN for not-a-number and the string Infinity for positive and negative infinity so the caller can branch on those text values directly
It assigns the FEEL value null because the data types reference states that not-a-number and positive or negative infinity are all represented as the null value in FEEL

c.text: Incorrect. The data types reference explicitly maps these non-finite outcomes to null rather than to special string values such as NaN or Infinity.

d.text: Correct. The Number entry of the data types reference states that not-a-number, positive infinity and negative infinity are all represented as null in FEEL, which is what downstream comparisons see for those rows.

a.text: Incorrect. FEEL does not surface a largest or smallest representable number value for infinity; the documented mapping is null.

b.text: Incorrect. Non-finite numeric outcomes do not become a boolean; the documented mapping is the FEEL null value, which can then be checked with a null comparison.

explanation: The FEEL data types reference notes that not-a-number, positive infinity and negative infinity are all represented as the FEEL null value, which lets the caller branch on null in downstream logic.

scenario: Quantum Bank rounds an interim balance to 4 decimal places per ledger row across 17000 transactions per day in Camunda 8.8. A FEEL expression on a business rule task multiplies the principal by a fractional rate and stores the result in a Number variable that downstream Java workers later deserialize from the engine state.
question: Which Java type does the FEEL data types reference name as the runtime type that backs a Number value at evaluation time?

options.text: The runtime type is the primitive double, so any FEEL number is bounded by the IEEE 754 double precision floating-point range and the corresponding rounding rules
The runtime type is the class java.lang.Long, so a FEEL number that has a fractional part is truncated to an integer before being stored as a Java numeric value
The runtime type is the class java.lang.String, so the engine carries the decimal representation as text and converts it to a numeric form only when an arithmetic operator is applied
The runtime type is the class java.math.BigDecimal, as recorded on the Number entry of the data types reference, so the engine uses arbitrary precision decimals for FEEL numbers

d.text: Correct. The Number entry of the data types reference records the Java type as java.math.BigDecimal, which means FEEL number values use arbitrary precision decimal arithmetic on the engine side.

a.text: Incorrect. The documented Java type for FEEL Number is java.math.BigDecimal rather than the primitive double, so IEEE 754 limits do not bound a FEEL number.

b.text: Incorrect. FEEL numbers can carry a fractional part without truncation; the documented backing type java.math.BigDecimal preserves the fractional component.

c.text: Incorrect. The data types reference records a numeric Java type for Number values rather than a string carrier, so arithmetic is applied to decimal values directly.

explanation: The FEEL data types reference records that the Number type is backed by java.math.BigDecimal at runtime, giving FEEL arithmetic arbitrary precision decimals without the IEEE 754 limits of a double.

scenario: Ridge Bank captures a fixed booking date such as 2017-03-10 on every entry for 6800 transactions per day in Camunda 8.8. A FEEL expression on a business rule input must convert the source text into a Date value using the documented constructor and yyyy-MM-dd format.
question: Which constructor and string format does the FEEL data types reference document for the Date type without any time component?

options.text: Wrap the literal source text in a string literal alone, with the engine auto-detecting a Date value from any string that resembles the format dd-MM-yyyy on parsing
Use the date and time constructor with a string in the format yyyy-MM-dd plus a midnight time component, so the value is rounded down to a Date by the engine on assignment
Use the date constructor with a string in the format yyyy-MM-dd, as documented in the Date entry of the FEEL data types reference for date values without a time component
Use the date constructor with three numeric arguments for year, month and day taken from the variables of the same name, with the string form reserved for date and time inputs

a.text: Incorrect. The engine does not auto-detect a date from a plain string; an explicit date constructor is required, and the documented format is yyyy-MM-dd rather than dd-MM-yyyy.

c.text: Correct. The Date entry of the data types reference shows the date constructor accepting a string in the format yyyy-MM-dd and gives the worked example date of 2017-03-10 for a date value without a time component.

b.text: Incorrect. The Date type has its own constructor and format; the date and time constructor returns a different type and is not the documented path for a pure date value.

d.text: Incorrect. The documented Date entry uses a single string argument in the yyyy-MM-dd format; the numeric three-argument form belongs to a different conversion function in the built-in functions reference.
