explanation: The FEEL Date type is constructed by calling the date function with a string in the format yyyy-MM-dd, which carries a calendar day without any time component and is backed by java.time.LocalDate.

scenario: Sable Bank stores an opaque text reference such as the value valid on every booking entry for 7600 transactions per day in Camunda 8.8. A FEEL expression on an input mapping must build a String literal that can span multiple lines and include escaped characters such as a backslash or a newline character.
question: How does the FEEL data types reference describe the syntax for a String literal value?

options.text: It uses a sequence of characters enclosed in single quotes, supports only the escaped backslash and forbids any whitespace character such as a newline inside the literal value
It uses a sequence of characters enclosed in angle brackets, supports only the Unicode escape form for special characters and disallows any multi-line content inside the literal

It uses a sequence of characters enclosed in double quotes that may contain escaped characters such as a backslash, quote, newline, tab and Unicode escape, and the literal can span multiple lines as documented
It uses a sequence of characters enclosed in backticks, supports escaped quotes only and treats any unrecognized escape sequence inside the literal as a fatal evaluation error

a.text: Incorrect. The documented String literal uses double quotes rather than single quotes and supports a broader escape set than the single backslash.

d.text: Incorrect. Backticks are not the documented delimiter for a FEEL String literal, and the escape set covers more than only escaped quotes.

c.text: Correct. The String entry of the data types reference documents a sequence of characters enclosed in double quotes that can contain escaped characters such as backslash, single and double quote, newline, carriage return, tab and Unicode escapes, and the literal can span multiple lines.

b.text: Incorrect. Angle brackets are not the documented delimiter for a FEEL String literal, and the documented literal supports multi-line content.

explanation: A FEEL String is a sequence of characters enclosed in double quotes that may contain escaped characters such as backslash, quote, newline, carriage return, tab and Unicode escapes, and the literal can span multiple lines.

scenario: Tessera Bank documents 12 inline FEEL expressions on a service task input mapping across 7500 transactions per day in Camunda 8.8. The team wants short authoring notes alongside each expression so reviewers can read the intent without leaving the modeler, using the comment syntax described in the FEEL expressions introduction.
question: Which comment forms does the FEEL expressions introduction document inside an inline expression value?

options.text: It documents no comment forms inside an inline FEEL expression value, so any explanatory text must be placed in a separate documentation field on the modeling element
It documents an end-of-line comment introduced by double slash and a block comment between slash-star and star-slash, matching the documented Java-style comment forms inside the expression body
It documents an XML-style comment introduced by less-than-exclamation-dash-dash and closed by dash-dash-greater-than, which the engine strips before parsing the expression
It documents a hash-prefixed end-of-line comment style and a triple-quoted block comment form, both of which can appear before, after or inside the expression body

d.text: Incorrect. The introduction does not document a hash-prefixed line comment or a triple-quoted block form; those are not part of FEEL.

b.text: Correct. The FEEL expressions introduction documents Java-style comments inside an expression: an end-of-line comment introduced by double slash and a block comment delimited by slash-star and star-slash.

c.text: Incorrect. XML-style comment delimiters are not part of the FEEL grammar and are not documented in the expressions introduction.

a.text: Incorrect. The introduction explicitly documents comment forms inside the expression body, so explanatory text can sit alongside the expression.

explanation: The FEEL expressions introduction documents Java-style comments inside an expression: double-slash to the end of the line, or slash-star to star-slash for block content, both usable around or between expression parts.

scenario: Umbra Bank reads the third element of a list of 4 line items on a service task input mapping for 12000 transactions per day in Camunda 8.8. The FEEL expression must access the element at the documented 1-based index without falling back to an off-by-one error on the downstream worker.
question: How does the FEEL list expressions reference define index access into a list value?

options.text: The list index is 1-based and a negative index returns null, with no tail-relative addressing documented in the FEEL grammar for list expressions on a positional access
The list index is 1-based and an out-of-range index raises an evaluation error that the engine surfaces as an incident on the activity using the list expression
The list index is 1-based and a negative index counts from the end of the list with -1 selecting the last element, while an out-of-range index returns the null value
The list index is 0-based and a negative index counts from the end of the list with -1 selecting the last element, while an out-of-range index returns the null value

c.text: Correct. The list expressions reference documents that the index starts at 1, that -1 selects the last element with negative addressing counting from the end, and that an out-of-range index returns null, as shown by the worked examples on the list 1, 2, 3, 4.

d.text: Incorrect. FEEL lists use a 1-based index rather than a 0-based one, and the caution box in the reference highlights that contrast with other languages.

a.text: Incorrect. Negative index access is documented and supported, with -1 returning the last element and -2 returning the next-to-last.

b.text: Incorrect. The reference shows that an out-of-range index returns null rather than raising an evaluation error or an incident.

explanation: FEEL list indexing is 1-based, negative indices count from the end with -1 selecting the last element, and an out-of-range index returns the null value rather than raising an error.

scenario: Vanta Bank builds a list of 3 nested pair lists on a service task input mapping in Camunda 8.8 across 13000 transactions per day. The FEEL literal must hold each pair as its own list value while still presenting a single outer list to the downstream worker for further filtering.
question: Which property of FEEL list literals do nested lists such as the literal containing 1, 2 and 3, 4 and 5, 6 illustrate?

options.text: List literals embed inner lists only when the count of inner lists equals the count of outer elements, with the engine zipping the two structures together during evaluation
List literals embed inner lists only when each inner list is wrapped inside a context literal first, with the engine flattening any bare inner list at the outer literal boundary
List literals require all elements to share the same scalar type, so a literal that holds inner list values is rejected at evaluation time as a heterogeneous container
List literals allow elements of any type, including other list literals, so an outer literal can embed inner list values without flattening, as documented in the list expressions reference

c.text: Incorrect. The list expressions reference states that elements can be of any type, so a list literal is allowed to mix scalar and list elements in the same value.

d.text: Correct. The Literal entry of the list expressions reference states that the elements can be of any type and gives a worked example with three nested inner pair lists embedded in an outer list literal.

b.text: Incorrect. Inner lists do not need to be wrapped in a context literal; the documented example embeds inner lists directly without any wrapping value.

a.text: Incorrect. The reference does not constrain the count of inner lists to match the count of outer elements; nested lists embed freely without any zip rule.

explanation: FEEL list literals allow elements of any type, including other list literals, so an outer literal can carry nested inner list values without any flattening or wrapping requirement.

scenario: Wren Bank groups a list of 6 invoices by the customer person across 6000 invoices per day in Camunda 8.8. The FEEL expression on a business rule input must reproduce the documented group-list example so each person appears once with their own list of invoices in the output.
question: Which FEEL expression form does the list expressions reference document for grouping a list of invoices by the person field?

options.text: Apply the group by built-in function to the list of invoices with the key person, with the list expressions reference exposing a dedicated grouping function for context-valued list elements
Apply a sort by expression on the invoices ordered by the person field, with the reference treating grouping as a sort step that the caller then partitions in code
Apply a for expression iterating p over distinct values of invoices person and return the invoices filtered by person equal to p, which yields one inner list per distinct person value
Apply a filter expression on the invoices that keeps the first invoice of each distinct person and discards the rest, with grouping reduced to a unique-by-key filter on the list

a.text: Incorrect. FEEL does not expose a dedicated group by built-in in this context; the documented example uses a for expression combined with distinct values and a filter on the list.

d.text: Incorrect. A unique-by-key filter only returns one representative invoice per person rather than the full list of invoices per person required by the documented example.

c.text: Correct. The Group list example in the list expressions reference iterates p over distinct values of invoices person and returns the invoices filtered by person equal to p, producing one inner list per distinct person value in the documented result.

b.text: Incorrect. A sort by step orders the list but does not partition it into one sublist per person; the documented example uses distinct values and a filter rather than a sort.

explanation: The list expressions reference groups a list by iterating distinct values of the key field with a for expression and returning the list filtered by each key value, producing one inner list per distinct key.

scenario: Helios Insurance keeps a FEEL list of 3000 claims sorted by submission time inside a process variable and needs a gateway expression that reads the most recently submitted claim, which sits at the end of the list, in Camunda 8.8.
question: Which FEEL expression returns the last element of a list named claims regardless of its length?

options.text: Index claims with claims[-1] so the engine counts from the end of the list and returns the last element of the list
Index claims with claims[0] so the engine reads the first slot of the list and returns the most recent submission record
Index claims with claims[length(claims)] so the engine treats the count as the trailing slot and returns the recent submission
Index claims with claims[count(claims)+1] so the engine reads past the end and returns the most recent submission record

b.text: Incorrect. The FEEL list index starts at 1, not 0; the docs note that a[0] returns null because index 0 is outside the valid range of a list.

d.text: Incorrect. FEEL list indexing returns null when the index is out of range; adding one to the element count points past the last element and yields null.

c.text: Incorrect. The documented mechanism for the last element of a FEEL list is the negative index -1, which counts from the end; relying on a length-based positive index is not the documented pattern in the chunk.

a.text: Correct. FEEL negative indexes count from the end of the list, so a[-1] returns the last element per the language guide; for [1,2,3,4][-1] the result is 4.

explanation: FEEL list indexes start at 1 and negative indexes count from the end, so a[-1] returns the last element of list a; if the index is outside the range the result is null, including a[0].

scenario: Aurora Bank wants to group 6 invoices currently held in a FEEL variable by their person attribute in Camunda 8.8 so that downstream rules can produce one sub-list per payer and total invoice amounts per person without writing custom worker code.
question: Which FEEL expression groups the invoices list by the person attribute into a list of sub-lists?

options.text: for p in distinct values(invoices.person) return invoices[person = p] iterates the unique person values and filters each subset
some p in invoices.person satisfies invoices[person = p] tests whether any person value yields a matching invoice sub-list
every p in distinct values(invoices.person) satisfies invoices[person = p] tests whether every person yields a sub-list
for p in invoices.person return invoices[person = p] iterates every person occurrence and filters the matching invoices each pass

a.text: Correct. The documented grouping pattern uses 'for p in distinct values(invoices.person) return invoices[person = p]' to iterate unique persons and filter the invoices for each, yielding a list of sub-lists.

d.text: Incorrect. Iterating invoices.person directly walks every entry and produces duplicate sub-lists; the docs use distinct values to iterate unique persons exactly once.

b.text: Incorrect. The 'some' quantifier returns a boolean indicating that at least one element satisfies the condition; it does not materialize a list of grouped sub-lists.

c.text: Incorrect. The 'every' quantifier also returns a boolean covering all elements; it is not the documented mechanism to produce grouped sub-lists from a list.

explanation: The FEEL grouping example computes distinct person values and, for each, filters the original list with invoices[person = p], yielding a list of sub-lists where each contains the invoices for one person.

scenario: Apex Insurance schedules premium reviews across 12000 policies per day in Camunda 8.8 using FEEL temporal arithmetic. A developer must compute a future review date by adding a duration value to a starting date value inside a service task input mapping, and needs to know the result type defined by the Addition operator.
question: Which result does the FEEL addition operator produce when adding a date value and a duration value together?

options.text: A days-time-duration value computed from the calendar gap between the two operands, ignoring the supplied duration in the calculation
A number value equal to the count of days inside the duration, with the original date discarded by the operator on evaluation
A null value because FEEL forbids mixing dates and durations in additive expressions across all timezone-related operand combinations
A date value produced by shifting the original date forward by the duration operand, as in date("2020-04-06") + duration("P1D")

a.text: Incorrect. The addition table maps date + duration to a date result, not to a days-time-duration; only subtracting two dates yields a days-time-duration value.

d.text: Correct. The Addition table shows that adding a date and a duration produces a date, illustrated by date("2020-04-06") + duration("P1D") evaluating to date("2020-04-07").

b.text: Incorrect. FEEL addition with a date operand returns a date; converting a duration to a number of days would require dividing by another duration, not adding to a date.

c.text: Incorrect. FEEL explicitly supports date + duration, time + days-time-duration, date-time + duration and duration + duration in the Addition table; only mismatched types fall back to null.

explanation: The FEEL temporal Addition operator is defined for several type pairs, including date + duration, time + days-time-duration, date-time + duration and duration + duration. Adding a date and a duration shifts the date forward and returns a new date; the docs show date("2020-04-06") + duration("P1D") evaluating to date("2020-04-07").

scenario: Pinnacle Logistics tracks 9000 shipments per day in Camunda 8.8 and a developer must compute the number of days between a planned pickup date and the carrier confirmation date using a FEEL subtraction expression inside a DMN decision input column on the routing decision.
question: Which result type does the FEEL subtraction operator return when both operands are date values?

options.text: A date-time value at midnight on the later date, with the timezone copied from the system default of the Zeebe broker at runtime
A days-time-duration value capturing the elapsed days between the operands, as in date("2020-04-06") - date("2020-04-01") = duration("P5D")
A date value equal to the earlier of the two operands, with the later operand silently dropped from the calculation by the operator
A years-months-duration value rounded down to whole months between the two date operands at evaluation time by the FEEL engine

c.text: Incorrect. FEEL never silently drops an operand; subtracting two dates returns a duration result that represents the gap, not one of the input dates.

d.text: Incorrect. Two date operands produce a days-time-duration, not a years-months-duration; the years-months result only arises when subtracting two years-months-duration values.

b.text: Correct. The Subtraction table shows that date - date returns a days-time-duration, illustrated by date("2020-04-06") - date("2020-04-01") evaluating to duration("P5D").

a.text: Incorrect. FEEL subtraction of two dates returns a duration, not a date-time; timezone defaults and broker settings play no role in this operator's result type.

explanation: The FEEL Subtraction operator maps date - date to a days-time-duration. The docs show date("2020-04-06") - date("2020-04-01") evaluating to duration("P5D"), giving the elapsed-days gap directly without any timezone or rounding intervention.

scenario: Beacon Fintech publishes SLA dashboards across 20000 transactions per day in Camunda 8.8 and uses a FEEL division expression on two duration values to compute throughput multipliers inside a service task input mapping for a downstream reporting worker.
question: Which result type does the FEEL division operator return when dividing two days-time-duration operands of the same family?

options.text: A days-time-duration value equal to the remainder of the two operands after integer-style division performed internally by the FEEL engine
A null value since the division table does not allow two duration operands of the same family as inputs to the FEEL division operator
A years-months-duration value computed by converting both operands to months using a 30-day approximation inside the FEEL engine at runtime
A number value equal to the ratio of the two durations, as in duration("P5D") / duration("P1D") evaluating to 5 in the FEEL engine

d.text: Correct. The Division table maps days-time-duration / days-time-duration to a number, illustrated by duration("P5D") / duration("P1D") evaluating to 5.

a.text: Incorrect. The result of dividing two days-time-duration values is a number, not a remaining duration; the division table documents no integer-remainder semantic.

c.text: Incorrect. Cross-family conversion is not part of the division table; same-family duration / duration yields a number, and 30-day approximations are not documented.

b.text: Incorrect. The Division table explicitly allows duration / duration within the same family and returns a number, with examples such as duration("P1Y") / duration("P1M") evaluating to 12.

explanation: FEEL Division is defined for duration / duration within the same family, returning a number, and for duration / number, returning the same duration type. So duration("P5D") / duration("P1D") yields 5, while duration("P5D") / 5 yields duration("P1D").

scenario: Atlas Telco posts 6000 events per day in Camunda 8.8 and a developer needs a FEEL expression to detect whether today is a weekend so a customer-care subprocess can skip non-business days inside an exclusive gateway condition on the escalation routing flow.
question: Which FEEL expression correctly checks whether the current day falls on a weekend, according to the temporal examples page?

options.text: day of week(today()) in ("Saturday","Sunday") returns true when the weekday name matches one of the two weekend labels at evaluation time
weekday(today()) in (6,7) returns true since FEEL numbers Saturday and Sunday as 6 and 7 in the same expression style as JavaScript Date
is weekend(today()) returns true on Saturday and Sunday using a dedicated FEEL helper provided by the temporal library inside Camunda 8
today().weekend returns true on Saturday and Sunday using a boolean weekend property exposed by the date value at evaluation time inside FEEL

a.text: Correct. The Check for weekend example uses day of week(today()) in ("Saturday","Sunday") to detect a weekend day directly from the weekday name returned by the function.

b.text: Incorrect. The documented example uses the day of week function and string day names rather than the weekday property and numbers; that alternative is not what the page shows.

d.text: Incorrect. FEEL exposes properties such as weekday, year and month on dates, but there is no boolean weekend property in the documented properties table for date values.

c.text: Incorrect. There is no is weekend built-in function shown in the FEEL temporal examples; the documented pattern is day of week(today()) tested against the weekend names.

explanation: The Check for weekend example shows day of week(today()) in ("Saturday","Sunday") as the FEEL expression for detecting a weekend, combining the day of week function (which returns a weekday name) with a list membership test against the two weekend labels.

scenario: Orion Insurance computes the elapsed interval between a claim's opened date and closed date across 5000 claims per month in Camunda 8.8 with a FEEL expression closedDate - openedDate, where both variables are held on the instance as FEEL date values.
question: What FEEL type does the expression closedDate - openedDate evaluate to when both operands are date values?

options.text: It evaluates to a date because subtracting one date from another aligns the calendar offset and yields a date result type
It evaluates to a years-months-duration because the operator infers the calendar span between the two date values
It evaluates to null because FEEL defines subtraction only when the second operand is a duration value of either kind
It evaluates to a days-time-duration because the FEEL subtraction table maps date minus date to a days-time-duration result

a.text: Incorrect. The FEEL subtraction table maps date minus date to a days-time-duration; subtracting two dates does not yield a date result.

b.text: Incorrect. FEEL date minus date yields a days-time-duration, not a years-months-duration; calendar months and years are not produced by this operator pair.

d.text: Correct. The FEEL subtraction table defines date minus date as yielding a days-time-duration; for example, date("2020-04-06") minus date("2020-04-01") equals duration("P5D").

c.text: Incorrect. FEEL does define subtraction between two date values and the result is a days-time-duration; the operator is not restricted to durations on the right side.

explanation: The FEEL temporal subtraction operator is defined for several type pairs, including date minus date, which produces a days-time-duration capturing the elapsed days between the two date values.

scenario: Aurora Bank computes 5000 transactions per day in Camunda 8.8 using FEEL temporal arithmetic. A reporting flow divides one days-time-duration value (the elapsed wait) by another days-time-duration value (the SLA window) to produce a normalized ratio for the operations dashboard.
question: When FEEL divides one days-time-duration value by another days-time-duration value, what is the result type?

options.text: The result is null because dividing one duration by another duration is undefined for the FEEL division operator at runtime
The result is a years-months-duration that the engine derives from the two days-time-duration operands during evaluation
The result is a days-time-duration that captures the difference between the two operand durations as a single duration value
The result is a number representing the ratio between the two days-time-duration operands as a dimensionless numeric quantity

a.text: Incorrect. The FEEL division operator defines dividing days-time-duration by days-time-duration; the result type is documented as number, not null. Null is returned only when the operand types do not match a defined combination.

c.text: Incorrect. The result type for days-time-duration divided by days-time-duration is number, not days-time-duration. A duration result occurs when dividing a duration by a number, not by another duration.

d.text: Correct. Per the FEEL temporal expressions division table, dividing days-time-duration by days-time-duration yields a number; the same rule applies to years-months-duration divided by years-months-duration.

b.text: Incorrect. years-months-duration is produced only when years-months-duration is divided by a number; mixing days-time-duration operands does not produce a years-months-duration result in FEEL.

explanation: FEEL division between two durations of the same kind yields a number representing the ratio. days-time-duration divided by days-time-duration produces a number, and years-months-duration divided by years-months-duration produces a number. Dividing a duration by a number returns a duration of the same kind.

scenario: Stellar Logistics dispatches 1800 shipments per day with Camunda 8.8 and uses FEEL to suspend non-urgent batch jobs across the weekend. The scheduler evaluates a single FEEL expression on each check to decide whether today is a Saturday or a Sunday and skip the run accordingly.
question: Which FEEL expression matches the documented example for checking whether today is on a weekend?

options.text: Comparing day of week(today()) against the list of strings Saturday and Sunday using the in operator on a list
Comparing day of week(today()) against the duration P2D from the previous Friday using the equals operator on a value
Comparing day of week(today()) against the numbers 6 and 7 from the weekday range using the in operator on a list
Comparing weekday(today()) against the list of integers 1 and 7 from the day-of-week range using the in operator

c.text: Incorrect. The documented example compares day of week(today()) to the string list ("Saturday","Sunday"), not to numeric weekday indices like 6 and 7.

a.text: Correct. The Check for weekend example evaluates day of week(today()) in ("Saturday","Sunday") and returns true when the day-of-week name is one of those two strings.

b.text: Incorrect. The example does not use a duration value or an equals comparison; it uses the in operator against a list of two day-name strings.

d.text: Incorrect. The example calls day of week, not weekday, and the list contains day-name strings rather than integer indices 1 and 7.

explanation: The Check for weekend example in the FEEL temporal expressions docs uses day of week(today()) in ("Saturday","Sunday"). day of week returns the day-of-week name as a string, and the in operator checks membership in the two-item list of weekend day names.

scenario: Harbor Bank operates 26000 transactions per day in Camunda 8.8 and a process instance is stuck after a job worker has failed with no retries left. The operations team needs to confirm what an incident represents in Camunda 8 and which of the listed situations creates one for this kind of failure mode.
question: Which statement correctly describes what an incident is in Camunda 8 and which situation creates one in this case?

options.text: An incident is a successful retry event raised when a job worker reactivates a failed job within its retry budget without any operator interaction needed
An incident is a Zeebe log record written for every error the broker observes, including transient network errors that recover automatically without intervention
An incident is a BPMN history attribute exported to Operate after the process instance completes, recording every caught error event along the way
An incident is a problem in process execution that leaves the instance stuck and requires user interaction, created when a failed job has no retries left

a.text: Incorrect. A successful retry is the opposite of an incident; the docs define an incident as a problem in process execution that requires user interaction, not a self-healing retry event.

d.text: Correct. The incidents concept page defines an incident as a problem in process execution that leaves the instance stuck and lists a job that has failed with no retries left as one of the situations that create one.

b.text: Incorrect. The docs explicitly note that not all errors lead to incidents; unexpected errors in Zeebe do not always result in incidents, and transient errors are not always represented.

c.text: Incorrect. Incidents are runtime indicators of stuck process instances, not historical attributes set after completion; they are also distinct from caught error events, which do not create incidents.

explanation: The incidents concept page defines an incident as a problem in process execution that leaves a process instance stuck and requires user interaction. The listed creation triggers include a failed job with no retries left, a non-boolean condition, a timer expression with an unexpected type, a decision that cannot be evaluated and an uncaught BPMN error.

scenario: Granite Insurer has 1200 claims stuck on incidents in Camunda 8.8 because a job worker failed with malformed variables on the activated job. The operations developer must resolve each incident through the Java client by repairing the variables, restoring retries and clearing the incident on the broker.
question: Which sequence of Java client commands resolves a job-related incident caused by malformed variables according to the incidents docs?

options.text: newCreateProcessInstanceWithResultCommand to replay the activity, then newCompleteJobCommand to mark success, then newResolveIncidentCommand last on the key
newResolveIncidentCommand alone, with updating variables and incrementing retries done implicitly by the broker on resolve in Camunda 8.8 anyway
newPublishMessageCommand then newCancelProcessInstanceCommand then newDeployResourceCommand, executed in order against the broker by the operator
newSetVariablesCommand for the element instance, then newUpdateRetriesCommand on the job, then newResolveIncidentCommand on the incident key

c.text: Incorrect. Publishing a message, cancelling the instance and deploying a resource are unrelated to fixing the variables on a stuck job; the docs prescribe set-variables, update-retries and resolve-incident in sequence.

b.text: Incorrect. The broker does not implicitly update variables or retries on resolve; the docs explicitly require the client to update the variables, increase the remaining retries and then mark the incident as resolved.

d.text: Correct. The docs show the Java client calling newSetVariablesCommand on the element instance, newUpdateRetriesCommand on the job and newResolveIncidentCommand on the incident, in that order, to recover a job-related incident.

a.text: Incorrect. CreateProcessInstanceWithResult creates a new instance and complete-job completes a job at the worker; the docs prescribe set-variables, update-retries and resolve-incident for an existing stuck instance.

explanation: For a job-related incident caused by bad variables, the incidents docs walk through three Java client steps: newSetVariablesCommand against the element instance key, newUpdateRetriesCommand against the job key with a positive count, and newResolveIncidentCommand against the incident key. After resolve, the job can be activated by a worker again.

scenario: Helios Retailer routes anonymous 'kiosk opened' events into a Camunda 8.8 message start event during a marketing campaign that produces about 1200 events per hour. The publishing service sends each message with an empty correlationKey, no messageId, and a TTL set to 0, and the retailer wants to confirm what the broker will do for every such publish.
question: What does the broker do when a message with an empty correlationKey, no messageId, and TTL set to 0 is published against a message start event?

options.text: The broker drops the message because an empty correlationKey is reserved for intermediate catch events that subscribe explicitly to the empty string value
The broker starts a new process instance for every such publish without any uniqueness check or correlationKey collision check against active instances
The broker promotes the publish into a synchronous correlate call and returns the new process instance key over the same connection before continuing execution
The broker queues the message until a process instance with an empty correlationKey is created and only then correlates the queued publication to that instance

a.text: Incorrect. The correlation overview table lists both start events and intermediate events as valid receivers for empty correlation keys; the publish is not dropped because the key is empty.

d.text: Incorrect. With an empty correlationKey, no messageId, and TTL of zero against a start event, the documented behavior is to start a new instance immediately, not queue against future instances.

c.text: Incorrect. Publishing a message is a fire-and-forget action and the publish endpoint does not switch into a synchronous correlate response based on the value of the correlation key.

b.text: Correct. The overview table row for empty correlation key, no messageId, TTL set to 0, start event states simply that a new instance is started, with no buffer or active-instance check applied.

explanation: When the correlation key is the empty string, no message ID is set, and TTL is zero, the messages overview table prescribes that a new process instance is started for a message start event receiver, without any uniqueness or active-instance check.

scenario: Wren Bank processes 14000 transactions per day in Camunda 8.8 and a developer must understand how the broker correlates incoming messages to process instances using subscriptions on message catch events to deliver payment confirmations from the upstream webhook.
question: Which two pieces of information does a Camunda 8 message subscription contain to route a published message to a process instance?

options.text: The message name and the correlation key, evaluated when the message catch event activates and used to match incoming messages on the broker
The tenantId and the deploymentKey, allowing the broker to scope correlation by tenant before evaluating the message body content on arrival
The processDefinitionId and the BPMN element name of the catch event, which the broker hashes to find a matching subscription record
The processInstanceKey of the waiting instance plus the broker node ID, written into the subscription by the catch event when it activates

d.text: Incorrect. The subscription does not carry the process instance key as a routing field; it contains the message name and correlation key the broker uses to find matches across processes.

c.text: Incorrect. Process definition and element identifiers are not used to route incoming messages; the docs specify the message name and the correlation key as the subscription fields.

b.text: Incorrect. Tenant scoping is a separate concern; subscriptions themselves match on message name and correlation key as defined in the Message subscriptions section of the messages page.

a.text: Correct. The Message subscriptions section states that correlation is based on subscriptions containing the message name and the correlation key, evaluated when the message catch event activates.

explanation: The Message subscriptions section defines a subscription as containing a message name (defined statically or via an expression) and a correlation key expression. Both are evaluated when the catch event activates, and the broker uses them to route published messages to process instances.

scenario: Beacon Bank operates 9000 transactions per day in Camunda 8.8 and a developer needs to know when a message subscription opens and closes during the lifetime of a message catch event in a running process instance to debug a stuck correlation.
question: When is a message subscription opened and closed during a process instance's lifetime according to the messages concept page?

options.text: A subscription is opened at process definition deployment time and closed only when the same definition is undeployed from the cluster gateway by the operator
A subscription is opened when a worker activates a job on the catch event and closed when the worker calls CompleteJob through the gateway to mark success
A subscription is opened when the process instance awaits the message, for example on entering a message catch event, and closed when the element or its scope is left
A subscription is opened when the broker publishes a message that matches the catch event and closed as soon as that single message is correlated to the instance

c.text: Correct. The Message subscriptions section states that a subscription is opened when a process instance awaits a message, for example when entering a message catch event, and closed when the element or its scope is left.

a.text: Incorrect. Subscriptions are per-instance runtime entities, not deployment-time entries; deploying or undeploying a definition does not by itself open or close subscriptions on instances.

d.text: Incorrect. The subscription is created when the catch event activates and waits for a message, not when a message arrives; otherwise no message could ever be correlated to an instance.

b.text: Incorrect. Catch events are not job-worker tasks; the subscription lifecycle is tied to the activation and exit of the catch event itself, not to CompleteJob being called by a worker.

explanation: Subscriptions open when the catch event activates and the instance awaits the message, and close when the element or its enclosing scope is left. Once opened, a subscription is not updated, even if the process variable referenced by the correlation key expression changes later.

scenario: Orion Carrier publishes 7000 events per day in Camunda 8.8 from a shipping platform and developers need to allow these messages to arrive before the matching catch event activates by configuring the time-to-live on the publish-message command in the v2 messages endpoint.
question: Which behavior does setting a positive time-to-live on a published message enable in Camunda 8 message buffering?

options.text: The message is held in a transactional outbox on the publisher and resent on each timer tick of the TTL until the broker acknowledges it back to the caller
The message is broadcast to every active process instance with a matching definition once the TTL expires at the end of the buffering window on the broker
The message is delivered repeatedly every TTL milliseconds until a subscription accepts it, simulating an at-least-once notification pattern between systems
The message is buffered for the TTL duration and can be correlated to a subscription that opens later within that window on the same broker node

c.text: Incorrect. TTL does not trigger periodic redelivery; it specifies a window during which the buffered message can be correlated to a subscription that opens later.

d.text: Correct. The Message buffering section states a message has a TTL that specifies for how long it is buffered, and within this time the message can be correlated to a process instance.

b.text: Incorrect. Messages are not broadcast at TTL expiry; the buffer is polled when a subscription opens, and the first published matching message is correlated in FIFO order.

a.text: Incorrect. The TTL governs broker-side buffering, not publisher retransmission; there is no documented outbox-based resend mechanism inside the messages page on TTL.

explanation: Message buffering lets the broker hold a published message for the supplied TTL. When a matching subscription opens within that window, it polls the buffer and the first published matching message (FIFO order) is correlated. A TTL of zero disables buffering and discards unmatched messages immediately.

scenario: Quanta Payments runs 12000 transactions per day in Camunda 8.8 across 3 deployed versions of the same payment process and a developer must understand how a single published message is correlated when several subscriptions match on the same BPMN process across these versions.
question: How does Camunda 8 correlate one published message when several subscriptions exist on the same BPMN process across multiple instances or versions?

options.text: The message is correlated to every open subscription on every instance of the process, allowing fan-out across all versions in parallel by the broker
The message is correlated only once to a given process (by BPMN process ID) across all versions of that process, so only one of the matching subscriptions receives it
The message is correlated only to the instance that has been waiting the shortest amount of time, applying a freshness rule per process definition on the broker
The message is correlated to the newest version of the process only, with subscriptions on older versions skipped by the broker silently at correlation time

a.text: Incorrect. The cardinality section states a message is correlated only once to a process across all versions, not fanned out to every matching subscription on that process at the same time.

d.text: Incorrect. Version is not the selector; correlation is per BPMN process ID across all versions and only one of the matching subscriptions is chosen by the broker.

b.text: Correct. The Message cardinality section states a message is correlated only once to a process based on the BPMN process ID across all versions, so only one of multiple matching subscriptions receives it.

c.text: Incorrect. The docs do not describe a freshness rule based on waiting time; selection is per BPMN process ID across versions with no time-based tie-breaker documented in the section.

explanation: Message cardinality states that a message is correlated only once to a process (by BPMN process ID) across all versions. If multiple subscriptions on the same process are open, only one of them receives the message. When subscriptions exist on different processes, the message is correlated to all of them.

scenario: Sable Bank publishes 13000 transactions per day in Camunda 8.8 from an at-least-once webhook source and developers need to deduplicate the messages so the same payment notification is not correlated twice when the upstream publisher retries the same correlation key.
question: Which Camunda 8 message attribute provides idempotency so the same message is processed at most once per name and correlation key?

options.text: The processInstanceKey on the publish command, which the broker uses to dedupe across instances at the gateway level before correlation happens at all
The timeToLive value, which transparently doubles as a deduplication window for any duplicates that arrive inside that same TTL on the broker
The correlation key alone, with identical correlation keys always rejected by the broker as duplicate correlations within a single process at runtime
The optional message ID, with a message rejected when a message of the same name, correlation key and ID is already buffered on the broker

a.text: Incorrect. Publishing does not target a specific process instance directly; the documented deduplication mechanism is the optional message ID, not a process instance key on the publish command.

b.text: Incorrect. TTL only controls buffering duration; it does not perform any deduplication of messages with the same name and correlation key on its own without a message ID set.

c.text: Incorrect. The correlation key alone is not a deduplication key; the docs require the same name, correlation key and message ID together to trigger rejection of a buffered duplicate.

d.text: Correct. The Message uniqueness section states a message is rejected and not correlated if a message with the same name, the same correlation key and the same ID is already buffered.

explanation: Message uniqueness uses an optional message ID. When set, the broker rejects any new message whose name, correlation key and ID match a message already in the buffer. The uniqueness check is disabled when no message ID is provided on the publish call.

scenario: Echo Insurance processes 16000 claims per day in Camunda 8.8 and an architect must plan how a message with a set correlation key, no message ID and a positive TTL is handled when it arrives on a message start event of the claims intake process from the upstream broker.
question: Which behavior does the correlation overview document for a message with set correlation key, no message ID and a positive TTL on a start event?

options.text: The message is correlated to the most recently completed instance with the matching correlation key, reactivating it for the duration of the TTL window
The message is rejected as duplicate because the correlation key alone is sufficient to identify a duplicate even without a message ID being provided at all
A new instance is started if no instance with the correlation key set at start is active during the lifetime of the message, and new equal messages are buffered
A new instance is always started for every message, with a positive TTL guaranteeing buffering and letting every message create its own instance in parallel each time

c.text: Correct. The overview table shows that with correlation key set, message ID not set and TTL > 0 on a start event, a new instance is started if no instance with that key is active during the lifetime of the message, and equal messages are buffered.

d.text: Incorrect. The same row in the overview table guards against duplicate active instances with the same correlation key; not every message starts a new instance, even with a positive TTL set.

a.text: Incorrect. The overview never reactivates completed instances; start-event correlation creates a new instance or buffers the message, but does not revisit historical instances at all.

b.text: Incorrect. Without a message ID the uniqueness check is disabled; the row only guards against an active instance sharing the correlation key, not against duplicate publications.

explanation: The Message correlation overview table for set correlation key, message ID not set and TTL > 0 on a start event documents that a new instance is started if no instance with that key is active during the lifetime of the message, with equal messages buffered behind the active instance.

scenario: Junction Bank starts 8000 transactions per day in Camunda 8.8 from a start event configured with an empty correlation key and a TTL of zero on the publish-message command, and an architect must confirm the documented behavior in this configuration on the broker.
question: Which behavior does the correlation overview document for a message with an empty correlation key, no message ID and TTL of zero on a start event?

options.text: The message is buffered indefinitely until an operator routes it manually to a matching start event subscription on the relevant process definition from Operate
The message is rejected because an empty correlation key implies a missing field and the broker refuses any publish-message command without a populated key value
A new instance is started without any uniqueness or buffering constraints, with the table entry simply recording that a new instance is started in this configuration
The message is correlated to the oldest active instance of the same process across all versions, regardless of the correlation key being the empty string here

b.text: Incorrect. The overview explicitly allows an empty string correlation key as a documented value, with rows describing the resulting behavior; the publish-message command does not reject it on the broker.

a.text: Incorrect. With TTL of zero the message is not buffered; the overview row says a new instance is started without any buffering, uniqueness check or operator routing step.

c.text: Correct. The overview row for empty correlation key, message ID not set and TTL of zero on a start event states simply that a new instance is started, with no uniqueness or buffering condition listed.

d.text: Incorrect. Intermediate-event semantics that correlate to active instances do not apply to start-event rows; the row for a start event always creates a new instance instead of correlating.

explanation: The Message correlation overview row for an empty correlation key, message ID not set and TTL of zero on a start event documents the simplest behavior: a new instance is started, with no buffering or uniqueness condition required on the publish or the broker side.

scenario: Yarrow Insurance writes 5000 policies per day in Camunda 8.8 and a publish-message call uses an empty correlation key, no message ID and a positive TTL targeting an intermediate event in the underwriting process that subscribes to the empty string correlation key on activation.
question: Which behavior does the correlation overview document for a message with empty correlation key, no message ID and a positive TTL on an intermediate event?

options.text: The message is correlated to every process instance that has an active intermediate event subscribed to any correlation key, with the empty string acting as a wildcard
The message is correlated during the lifetime of the message if a matching subscription to the empty string is active, and new equal messages are buffered behind it
A new process instance is started for every message because a positive TTL is treated as a start-event-style trigger inside the overview table for intermediate events
The message is discarded immediately because intermediate events cannot subscribe to an empty correlation key at any TTL value above zero according to the broker rules

a.text: Incorrect. The empty string is not a wildcard; the overview row only matches subscriptions whose correlation key is also the empty string, not subscriptions to any correlation key.

d.text: Incorrect. The table explicitly defines this configuration for intermediate events; the message is correlated during its lifetime when an empty-string subscription is active on the broker.

c.text: Incorrect. Intermediate event rows in the overview never start a new process instance; only start-event rows describe instance creation in the table for messages.

b.text: Correct. The row for empty correlation key, message ID not set and TTL > 0 on an intermediate event states the message is correlated during its lifetime if a matching subscription to the empty string is active, with equal messages buffered.

explanation: The overview table maps empty correlation key, no message ID and TTL > 0 on an intermediate event to: correlated during the lifetime of the message if a matching subscription to the empty string is active, with new equal messages buffered behind it on the broker side.

scenario: Meridian Retail collects 8000 events per day in Camunda 8.8 from a customer notification stream and an architect must pick the documented message pattern that aggregates many messages with the same correlation key into a single ongoing process instance per customer entity.
question: Which Camunda 8 message pattern aggregates multiple messages with the same correlation key into a single ongoing process instance?

options.text: The Single instance pattern with TTL of zero and a correlation key per entity, which silently discards every message except the first one received per key on the broker
The Message buffering pattern with TTL of zero and no correlation key, which collects messages on the broker until the operator manually publishes them to a process instance
The Message aggregator pattern with TTL > 0 and a correlation key that groups messages per entity, where the first message creates the instance and the rest correlate to it
The Message uniqueness pattern with a message ID per event, which batches duplicate messages on the broker into a synthetic aggregate event for a single process instance

a.text: Incorrect. The Single instance pattern enforces exactly one instance and discards later messages with the same correlation key; it does not aggregate them into the ongoing instance at all.

c.text: Correct. The Message aggregator pattern publishes messages with TTL > 0 and a correlation key that groups them per entity; the first message creates the instance and the following messages are correlated to that same instance.

b.text: Incorrect. There is no documented pattern that buffers without a correlation key for later operator routing; buffering is a building block, not the aggregator pattern itself on the broker.

d.text: Incorrect. Message uniqueness is a deduplication mechanism, not an aggregation pattern; it rejects duplicates rather than combining them into a single ongoing process instance.

explanation: The Message aggregator pattern is the documented solution for collecting and batching messages. Messages are published with TTL > 0 and a correlation key that groups them per entity. The first message creates a new process instance; later messages with the same key are correlated to that same instance until it ends.

scenario: Catalyst Insurance handles 6000 transactions per day in Camunda 8.8 and a developer publishes a message expecting an immediate response confirming whether correlation succeeded, choosing the correlate-message endpoint over the publish-message endpoint on the v2 messages API.
question: Which difference between the correlate-message and publish-message endpoints does the Message response section document?

options.text: The correlate-message endpoint does not support message buffering, so a published message is either immediately correlated to a subscription or not correlated at all
The correlate-message endpoint returns every process instance key the message correlated with, providing a complete fan-out report for the caller in the response body at once
The correlate-message endpoint accepts an extra timeToBuffer field that increases the buffer beyond the cluster default while still confirming success on the call to the client
The correlate-message endpoint internally retries the correlation for the message TTL, guaranteeing eventual success whenever a matching subscription opens within the window

c.text: Incorrect. The docs note the correlate-message endpoint does not support buffering; there is no documented timeToBuffer field that extends the buffer for synchronous correlation on the broker.

b.text: Incorrect. The response returns only one process instance key; the docs explicitly state that any additional keys for messages correlated to other instances are not part of the response.

a.text: Correct. The Message response section states the correlate-message endpoint does not support message buffering, so any message published using this endpoint is either immediately correlated or not correlated at all.

d.text: Incorrect. The synchronous endpoint does not retry across the TTL window; the docs describe it as immediately correlated or not correlated, with no implicit retry behaviour on the broker.

explanation: The Message response section explains that publishing a message is fire-and-forget. The correlate-message endpoint works like publish, but does not support message buffering: a message is either immediately correlated to an open subscription or not at all. If correlation succeeds, the response returns one process instance key only.

scenario: Vertex Insurance runs an order-process in Camunda 8.8 across 4500 claims per day. For a smoke test, the QA team needs to launch process instances directly at the ship_parcel element using a start instruction so the input mappings of that element apply as usual when activation occurs.
question: Where does a Camunda 8 start instruction begin execution by default relative to the targeted element?

options.text: At the parent scope of the targeted element, so the scope is initialized first and the targeted element then activates without any input mappings
After the targeted element completes, so any input mappings on that element are skipped and execution proceeds at the next outgoing sequence flow
At the boundary events of the targeted element, so attached interrupting boundaries are activated first and then the targeted element starts
Before the targeted element, so the input mappings of that element are applied as usual when the targeted element is activated by the engine

b.text: Incorrect. By default a start instruction begins before the targeted element, not after it, so the element's input mappings still apply during activation rather than being skipped.

d.text: Correct. The documented default is that a start instruction begins before the targeted element, so the input mappings of that element are applied as usual at activation time.

c.text: Incorrect. Start instructions target the element itself, not its attached boundary events; activation begins before the targeted element with its input mappings applied as usual.

a.text: Incorrect. Start instructions do not initialize a parent scope as a separate prior step; they activate before the targeted element with that element's input mappings applied.

explanation: Start instructions begin before the targeted element by default, so the element's input mappings are applied as usual during activation when the process instance is created at a user-defined element.

scenario: Nimbus Logistics tests an order-process segment in Camunda 8.8 to validate the ship_parcel branch across 2000 shipments per day. The QA team must end created instances after a specific element completes, and another scenario splits into 3 parallel branches where the instance should end when any specified element completes or terminates.
question: How are runtime instructions used to terminate a process instance after multiple parallel flow elements complete?

options.text: Provide a single runtime instruction listing the element IDs in an array; the engine terminates the instance after the first listed element completes its work
Provide a runtime instruction on the parent scope only; the engine terminates the instance when the scope's join gateway completes for every parallel flow
Provide multiple runtime instructions, one per element ID; the engine terminates the instance when any specified element completes or terminates in any flow
Provide a runtime instruction targeting a sequence flow between the flows; the engine terminates the instance when that sequence flow is taken at runtime

a.text: Incorrect. The documented form provides multiple runtime instructions, each naming one element ID, not a single instruction listing many IDs together in one array.

b.text: Incorrect. Runtime instructions name specific element IDs, not a parent scope or join gateway; each instruction targets the completion or termination of one element.

c.text: Correct. Multiple runtime instructions can be supplied with one element ID each, and the instance ends when any specified element completes or terminates in its flow.

d.text: Incorrect. Runtime instructions cannot target sequence flows; the documented limitation explicitly excludes starting or ending at a sequence flow during instance creation.

explanation: Multiple runtime instructions, each targeting one element ID, terminate the process instance when any specified element completes or terminates, which fits scenarios with several parallel flows.

scenario: Forge Bank invokes a short-running order-confirmation process from a synchronous REST handler over 14000 transactions per day in Camunda 8.8. The client must receive the resulting variables in the same response once the instance finishes, and the team is choosing the documented API command for that pattern.
question: Which Camunda 8 command returns process variables to the client once the executed process instance completes?

options.text: Call CreateProcessInstance and poll the gateway every 200 ms with GetProcessInstance until state is COMPLETED, then read the resulting variables from the response.
Call CreateProcessInstanceWithResult and supply a webhook URL; the gateway posts the resulting variables to that URL after the process execution has completed.
Call CreateProcessInstance and have a job worker on the end event publish the resulting variables back to the original client via a custom gateway callback channel.
Call CreateProcessInstanceWithResult on the gateway; the response is sent when the process execution completes and includes the resulting variables to the client.

d.text: Correct. CreateProcessInstanceWithResult allows synchronous execution: the gateway sends the response, including the variables, once process execution completes, which fits short-running processes and information-collection use cases.

a.text: Incorrect. Polling is not the documented mechanism for returning variables on completion; CreateProcessInstanceWithResult exists precisely so the client can await results in the original request without polling.

c.text: Incorrect. Workers do not publish variables back to the original client through the gateway; CreateProcessInstanceWithResult is the documented command that returns variables on completion.

b.text: Incorrect. The documented response delivery is synchronous on the original CreateProcessInstanceWithResult call; the gateway does not post results to a client-supplied webhook URL after completion.

explanation: CreateProcessInstanceWithResult lets the client synchronously execute a process and receive the variables in the response when execution completes, which is the documented pattern for short-running and information-collection processes.

scenario: Helix Insurance plans to use start instructions in a Camunda 8.8 integration test that processes 800 claims per night. A developer asks whether the input mappings declared on the targeted element are honored when an instruction starts the instance at that element of the process model.
question: What is the documented default behavior for input mappings when a startInstructions entry targets an element?

options.text: The instruction starts after the targeted element by default, so the input mappings of that element are skipped and only output mappings run on its completion.
The instruction starts before the targeted element by default, so the input mappings of that element are applied as usual when it is activated on creation.
The instruction routes through the none start event first, so input mappings on the targeted element are applied twice during creation of the process instance.
The instruction toggles input mappings off for the targeted element, requiring teams to redeclare them as a separate runtime instruction on the same command.

b.text: Correct. By default a startInstructions entry starts before the given element, which means the input mappings of that element are applied as usual when the engine activates it on creation.

a.text: Incorrect. The default position is before the element, not after; input mappings are applied as usual rather than skipped on activation.

d.text: Incorrect. Start instructions do not toggle input mappings off and runtime instructions only describe where to terminate the instance, not how mappings run.

c.text: Incorrect. Start instructions activate the targeted element directly; there is no documented re-entry through the none start event that would re-apply input mappings.

explanation: A start instruction defaults to starting the process instance before the targeted element, so that element's input mappings are applied as usual when the engine activates it during process instance creation.

scenario: Driftwood Marketplace ingests 4500 messages per hour into Camunda 8.8 from its vendor pricing pipeline. Each update message should automatically create one instance of the price-sync process, without any external service explicitly calling the CreateProcessInstance command from a client.
question: Which BPMN start event creates exactly one new process instance for each incoming pricing update message published to the cluster?

options.text: A message start event whose message name matches the published message name; for each new message a new process instance is created by the engine on receipt.
A signal start event whose signal name matches the broadcast signal; all subscribed processes share a single instance created once per broadcast across the cluster.
A timer start event configured with a 1-second interval; the engine creates one instance per tick and the worker correlates each pricing update message inside.
A conditional start event referencing a process variable mutated by an inbound connector; the engine creates one instance per change event detected on the variable.

a.text: Correct. A process with a message start event can be started by publishing a message whose name matches the start event name, and for each new message a new process instance is created on the cluster.

b.text: Incorrect. Signals broadcast to subscribers and do not implement per-message one-instance semantics the way a message start event does for each published message name.

c.text: Incorrect. A timer start event creates instances when the timer triggers, not per inbound message; correlation through a timer does not match the per-message semantics required here.

d.text: Incorrect. The documented implicit-creation events are message and timer start events; a conditional start event is not the documented mechanism for per-message instance creation.

explanation: A message start event is the documented mechanism for implicit per-message instance creation in Camunda 8: each new message whose name matches the start event creates a new process instance.

scenario: Vantage Mortgage, a lender, wants to mark each Camunda 8.8 mortgage-origination instance with up to 12 routing labels covering channel, region, experiment, and trace id across 7000 loans per day. Operators ask which labels survive end-to-end on jobs and how many labels may be attached at creation time.
question: Which statement correctly describes how process instance tags behave at creation and downstream in Camunda 8.8?

options.text: At most 16 unique tags may be attached at creation; they are mutable through a SetTags command on the running instance, and the broker stores them only on the root process instance.
At most 10 unique tags may be attached at creation; they are immutable after creation, and the broker propagates them onto every activated job for downstream routing decisions.
At most 50 unique tags may be attached at creation; they are immutable but only the first 10 propagate to activated jobs and the remaining tags stay on the process instance only.
At most 10 unique tags may be attached at creation; they may be replaced after creation through an UpdateInstanceTags RPC, and tags are not exported by the default exporters.

b.text: Correct. The documented limits are a maximum of 10 unique tags per process instance with duplicates ignored; tags are immutable after creation and are inherited by all jobs created from that instance for downstream routing.

a.text: Incorrect. The cap is 10 unique tags, not 16, and tags are immutable after creation: they cannot be added, changed, or removed via any SetTags command.

d.text: Incorrect. Tags cannot be replaced after creation through any RPC, and starting in 8.8 they are exported with the process instance and with job entities by the default exporters.

c.text: Incorrect. The maximum is 10 unique tags, not 50, and all attached tags are inherited by jobs; there is no documented partial-propagation rule that caps job inheritance at 10.

explanation: Process instance tags in Camunda 8.8 are capped at 10 unique values per instance, are immutable after creation, and are inherited by all jobs created from that instance so downstream workers can route or prioritize without inspecting variables.

scenario: Beacon Insurance routes its Camunda 8.8 claim instances by attaching tags such as priority:high and region:emea for around 3500 claims per day. An auditor asks where these tags are visible to consumers in the platform and where they are intentionally not visible across the runtime surfaces.
question: Where are process instance tags exposed to consumers in Camunda 8.8?

options.text: In Operate, Tasklist, and process instance search responses, with the broker hiding tags from activated job payloads to protect potentially sensitive routing metadata.
In process instance search responses and in activated job payloads, but not in Operate or Tasklist; tags remain API and client-only metadata in Camunda 8.8 deployments.
In Operate timeline events only, with downstream workers required to call a separate GetInstanceTags RPC after job activation to obtain the tag set for routing decisions.
In Tasklist task detail panels and inbound connector payloads only, with the process instance search API filtering tags out of responses by default for performance reasons.

b.text: Correct. Tags are included in process instance search responses and on activated job payloads, but they are intentionally not shown in Operate or Tasklist; they are API and client-only metadata in Camunda 8.8.

a.text: Incorrect. Tags are not displayed in Operate or Tasklist; they are exposed on activated job payloads rather than hidden from them, which is the opposite of what is documented.

d.text: Incorrect. The search API exposes tags in responses by default; it does not filter them out, and Tasklist does not display them in task detail panels.

c.text: Incorrect. There is no GetInstanceTags RPC; tags are propagated automatically onto activated job payloads so workers can read them without an additional call.

explanation: In Camunda 8.8, tags are exposed in process instance search responses and on activated job payloads but are intentionally not shown in Operate or Tasklist, making them API and client-only metadata for downstream consumers.

scenario: Forge Bank is planning Camunda 8.8 migrations that include both parallel and sequential multi-instance loan-checks subprocess steps across 4200 loans per day. The team needs to confirm which multi-instance marker variants are listed as supported by the migration tool.
question: Which multi-instance marker variants are listed as supported by the Camunda 8.8 process instance migration tool?

options.text: Only the parallel multi-instance marker is listed as supported, while the sequential multi-instance marker requires a manual process instance modification before migration.
Only the sequential multi-instance marker is listed as supported, while the parallel multi-instance marker is currently excluded as an experimental feature in the migration matrix.
Neither multi-instance marker is listed as supported; multi-instance subprocesses must be expanded into static parallel branches by hand before any migration is attempted.
Both the parallel multi-instance marker and the sequential multi-instance marker are listed as supported markers in the Camunda 8.8 process instance migration tool support matrix.

d.text: Correct. The supported BPMN elements section lists both the parallel and the sequential multi-instance markers under the markers section as supported by the Camunda 8.8 migration tool.

a.text: Incorrect. Sequential multi-instance is supported by the migration tool; it does not require a manual process instance modification as a prerequisite to migration.

b.text: Incorrect. Parallel multi-instance is supported in the migration matrix and is not flagged as experimental or excluded; both marker variants are supported.

c.text: Incorrect. Multi-instance subprocesses do not need to be expanded into static branches before migration; both parallel and sequential markers are supported directly.

explanation: Camunda 8.8's migration tool lists both parallel and sequential multi-instance markers as supported in the markers section of its supported BPMN elements matrix.

scenario: Lattice Insurance is preparing a Camunda 8.8 migration that introduces a new compensation handler on a service task in its claims-settlement process across 700 claims per day. The team needs to confirm which compensation event variant is listed as covered by the migration tool support matrix.
question: Which compensation event variant is listed as supported by the Camunda 8.8 process instance migration tool?

options.text: Compensation boundary event is listed as supported by the Camunda 8.8 migration tool support matrix; other compensation event variants are not marked as supported in the matrix.
Compensation end event is listed as supported in the migration tool matrix, while compensation boundary event is intentionally excluded from process instance migration plans in 8.8.
Compensation start event, compensation boundary event, and compensation end event are each listed as supported in the Camunda 8.8 migration tool support matrix for active instances.
Compensation event subprocess and compensation boundary event are listed as supported in the migration tool support matrix, while compensation throw events are excluded entirely.

a.text: Correct. In the supported events table, only compensation boundary event is marked as supported for compensation; no other compensation event variants are marked as supported in the matrix.

c.text: Incorrect. Compensation start event and compensation end event are not marked as supported for migration; only compensation boundary event is shown as supported in the matrix.

d.text: Incorrect. Compensation event subprocess is not marked as supported in the migration matrix; only the compensation boundary event variant is shown as supported.

b.text: Incorrect. Compensation boundary event is the supported variant, not the excluded one; compensation end event is not marked as supported in the migration matrix.

explanation: In Camunda 8.8's migration tool support matrix, compensation boundary event is the only compensation variant marked as supported; other compensation event variants are not listed in the supported set.

scenario: Lattice Insurance runs 8000 policies per day on Camunda 8.8. A claim review user task in the source process has a timer boundary event with a duration of one week, and the active task has already been waiting for five days. The redesign keeps the same user task but changes the timer boundary event duration to two weeks, and the migration team maps both the user task and the timer boundary event between source and target.
question: What is the resulting wait behavior of the migrated timer after the mapping instruction Timer1 to Timer2 is applied?

options.text: The timer subscription is migrated and the elapsed five days are preserved so the target timer fires after the remaining nine days of waiting
The timer subscription is closed and a new timer subscription is opened against the target with a duration of two weeks from the migration time
The timer subscription is migrated and the engine resets the elapsed time so the target timer counts the full two weeks from the migration time
The timer subscription is closed and a new timer subscription is opened against the target with a duration of one week from the migration time

b.text: Incorrect. Providing a mapping between the catch events migrates the existing subscription rather than closing it and opening a fresh two-week timer against the target.

d.text: Incorrect. The mapping migrates the existing subscription rather than closing it; the target duration is also two weeks, not one week, in the redesigned process.

c.text: Incorrect. The documentation states that mapping the catch events ensures the timer is migrated and the duration is preserved, not reset against the target definition.

a.text: Correct. The documentation states that mapping between active elements and timer boundary events migrates the timer subscription and preserves the elapsed duration, so a two-week target timer fires after the remaining nine days.

explanation: Mapping a timer boundary event between source and target migrates the existing subscription and preserves the elapsed duration. With five days already waited against a two-week target timer, the timer fires after the remaining nine days.

scenario: Forge Bank runs 12000 transactions per day on Camunda 8.8. The redesigned process keeps a message event subprocess with the same message name as before, and the migration team wants the message subscription preserved across the migration so that in-flight instances continue to react to the same message events without interruption.
question: Which mapping instruction preserves the message subscription for a message event subprocess across migration?

options.text: Map a user task in the source process to the same user task in the target process so the engine preserves the message name across migration of the instance
Map the process definition id of the source to the process definition id of the target so the engine preserves the message name across migration of the instance
Map the parent subprocess of the source to the parent subprocess of the target so the engine preserves the message name across migration of the instance
Map the start event of the source event subprocess to the start event of the target event subprocess so the engine preserves the message name across migration

d.text: Correct. The documentation states that to keep the message name the same for a message event subprocess, the start event of the event subprocess must be mapped when migrating the process instance.

b.text: Incorrect. Mapping process definition ids is not how event subscriptions are preserved; the documented mechanism is to map the start event of the event subprocess between source and target.

c.text: Incorrect. Mapping a parent subprocess does not preserve a message subscription on its own; the start event of the event subprocess must be mapped to keep the message name.

a.text: Incorrect. User task mappings are unrelated to preserving a message event subscription; the start event of the message event subprocess must be mapped between source and target.

explanation: Preserving the message name on a message event subprocess across migration requires mapping the start event of the event subprocess from source to target, so the message subscription is migrated rather than closed.

scenario: Atlas Logistics runs 7500 shipments per day on Camunda 8.8. The redesigned process adds a message event subprocess with a message start event named M to handle escalations, and active instances are currently waiting at a service task A. The migration team provides a single mapping from service task A to service task A in the target and wants to know the effect on the message subscription after migration.
question: What happens to the message subscription on the new event subprocess start event after migration with the mapping A to A?

options.text: The process instance keeps no message subscription and the engine requires an explicit mapping of the new event subprocess start event before it can be activated
The process instance keeps only its prior subscriptions and the new message event subprocess in the target stays inactive until the next instance is started fresh
