The process instance is newly subscribed to the message event subprocess start event after migration because a new subscription is opened during the migration step
The process instance opens both old and new subscriptions and waits for either path, with the engine cancelling the duplicate after one of the messages is received

a.text: Incorrect. The documentation states that the new message event subprocess start event becomes a target of a new subscription during migration even when only the active element A is mapped to A.

b.text: Incorrect. The new message event subprocess does not stay inactive for the in-flight instance; a new subscription is opened on migration when the catch event is not the target of a mapping instruction.

c.text: Correct. The documentation states that after migrating active element A with mapping A to A, the process instance is newly subscribed to the added message start event because a new subscription is opened during migration.

d.text: Incorrect. There is no duplicate-subscription behavior described; only the new subscription is opened for the added message event subprocess start event during migration.

explanation: When a catch event is added in the target and not the target of a mapping instruction, the engine opens a new subscription during migration. The active service task only needs to be mapped to itself for the new message event subprocess to take effect.

scenario: Helios Bank uses Camunda 8.8 to orchestrate 8000 loans per day. A long-running approval is waiting at a joining parallel gateway with one incoming sequence flow already taken from element A, while element B is still active at a user task. The platform team plans to migrate the running process instance to a newer process definition that inserts an extra element C in front of the joining parallel gateway.
question: Which mapping condition must hold to migrate a process instance waiting at a joining parallel gateway?

options.text: Map the source joining parallel gateway to a target joining gateway that has at least the same number of incoming sequence flows as the source
Map the source joining parallel gateway to a target exclusive gateway that reproduces the routing logic with the same outgoing sequence flows
Skip a mapping for the source joining parallel gateway because joining gateways are never treated as active elements during instance migration
Map the source joining parallel gateway to any target gateway of a matching type regardless of the number of incoming sequence flows present

a.text: Correct. The migration docs require the joining gateway to be mapped to a target joining gateway, and the target must have at least the same number of incoming sequence flows as the source so the partially completed join can continue to receive its remaining tokens.

d.text: Incorrect. A matching gateway type alone is not enough; the documented rule additionally constrains the target to have at least as many incoming sequence flows as the source joining gateway.

b.text: Incorrect. The documented scenario maps joining parallel and inclusive gateways to gateways of the same joining role, not to an exclusive gateway, so the incoming flow count rule still applies.

c.text: Incorrect. A joining gateway that has taken incoming flows and is still waiting is an active element and the docs require a mapping instruction similar to other active elements.

explanation: When a joining parallel or inclusive gateway is partially complete, migration treats it as an active element. The plan must map it to a target joining gateway that has at least the same number of incoming sequence flows so the remaining tokens can still arrive.

scenario: Aurora Insurance runs Camunda 8.8 with 12000 claims per day. After a hotfix in process definition v5 introduces an unintended branch, the platform team must move running process instances back to v3 while keeping their state. Separately, the integration team wants to relocate other running process instances onto a completely different process definition that consolidates two related products.
question: Which directions of process instance migration are supported between source and target process definitions?

options.text: Migration is allowed between any versions of the same definition and to an entirely different process definition without a version restriction
Migration is allowed only from an older version to a strictly newer version of the same process definition with a matching bpmnProcessId
Migration is allowed only between two versions of the same process definition that share the same processDefinitionKey value across the deployments
Migration is allowed only between two adjacent versions of the same process definition when the version numbers differ by exactly one step

b.text: Incorrect. The docs explicitly state that migration is supported from an older version to a newer version, from a newer version to an older version, and to a different process definition altogether.

a.text: Correct. The documented freedom is broad: a process instance may be migrated from older to newer, from newer to older within the same definition, or to a completely different process definition without further version constraints.

d.text: Incorrect. The docs impose no adjacency or step size on versions; any pair of versions of the same definition is allowed, and the target can also be a different definition.

c.text: Incorrect. The docs do not require the source and target to share the same processDefinitionKey; migration to a different process definition altogether is explicitly listed as supported.

explanation: Process instance migration places no direction or step restriction on version selection. The same definition can be migrated forward or backward across any versions, and the target can also be a completely different process definition with a different bpmnProcessId.

scenario: Vertex Logistics handles 9000 shipments per day in Camunda 8.8. Migration coordinators submit a migration plan that maps every active element of a running process instance except for one active service task whose elementId was renamed in the target process definition and was therefore overlooked in the plan that engineers prepared.
question: What outcome occurs when one mapping instruction is missing for an active element during process instance migration?

options.text: The migration is rejected transactionally and the process instance remains on its current process definition without any change in state at all
The migration completes and an incident is created on the unmapped active element so an operator can pick a target element after the fact
The migration completes by auto-mapping the unmapped active element to a target element that shares the same elementId in the target definition
The migration completes for the mapped active elements and the unmapped active element is left attached to its original source process definition

d.text: Incorrect. Migration is transactional, so partial outcomes are not possible. A missing mapping for any active element fails the whole command rather than leaving one element behind.

c.text: Incorrect. The engine does not auto-match by elementId during migration. If a mapping is missing the migration command is rejected with an explanatory message.

b.text: Incorrect. Migration does not create an incident on the unmapped element; instead the command itself is rejected up front and no state change takes place.

a.text: Correct. The docs state that migration runs in a transactional manner: either all active elements are migrated or none. A missing mapping for any active element rejects the command and leaves the instance unchanged.

explanation: Process instance migration is transactional. If any step fails, including a missing mapping for an active element, the entire migration is rejected with an explanatory message and the process instance stays on its current process definition.

scenario: Apex Insurance runs Camunda 8.8 across 7000 policies per day. After a process redesign the platform team prepares a migration plan that introduces two extra active review tasks on every running process instance so that auditors can intervene before payout. The architecture group must decide whether process instance migration alone is able to change the count of active elements.
question: Which limitation applies to the number of active elements during process instance migration in Camunda 8.8?

options.text: The number of active elements may be increased during migration by adding mappings that reference newly introduced target elements in the plan
The number of active elements is reconciled by the engine at validation, so any net difference is accepted as long as mappings exist for them
The number of active elements cannot be changed by migration, and process instance modification must be used to add or remove active elements
The number of active elements may be reduced during migration when two source elements are mapped to a single shared target element in the plan

a.text: Incorrect. Migration cannot add new active elements. The docs direct users to process instance modification when the count of active elements needs to change between source and target.

c.text: Correct. The docs list this as a hard limitation: the number of active elements cannot be changed by migration. Process instance modification is the documented mechanism for altering the count of active elements.

d.text: Incorrect. The plan can only map each sourceElementId once and the active element count must stay the same, so two source elements cannot collapse into a single target element via migration.

b.text: Incorrect. The validator enforces the unchanged active element count rather than reconciling differences. Any mismatch leads to a rejection rather than acceptance.

explanation: Process instance migration preserves the count of active elements. To add or remove active elements teams must use process instance modification, which is the documented alternative for changing the structure of a running instance.

scenario: Beacon Bank runs Camunda 8.8 for 4000 loans per day. Many instances are waiting on a message catch event named PaymentReceived. The platform team plans a migration to a target process definition that maps PaymentReceived to a renamed equivalent and wants to know what must be true about the source message subscription before the migration command can succeed against the cluster.
question: Which condition on a source message subscription must hold for a process instance migration across a mapped message catch event?

options.text: The message subscription for the source message catch event must be fully distributed across the cluster before the migration is started
The message subscription must be paused by the client on the broker before the migration command is accepted by the gateway in the cluster
The message subscription must be deleted by the client and then recreated on the target catch event right after the migration completes
The message subscription must already have at least one buffered message waiting in the broker queue before the migration is initiated

b.text: Incorrect. The docs do not introduce a pause operation on subscriptions; the requirement is about full distribution across the cluster, not about pausing the subscription.

d.text: Incorrect. The docs do not require a buffered message to be present before migration; the catch event can be migrated whether or not a message has already arrived.

a.text: Correct. The catch event limitations explicitly state that the message subscription for the source message catch event must be fully distributed before the migration can proceed.

c.text: Incorrect. The client does not delete and recreate the message subscription; the engine migrates the subscription itself when a mapping instruction is provided for the catch event.

explanation: Migration of a message catch event depends on the source message subscription being fully distributed across the cluster. Once that is true, a mapping instruction for the catch event allows the engine to migrate the subscription onto the target element.

scenario: Cobalt Energy, a utility, redesigned its Camunda 8.8 outage-response process across 600 incidents per day: the old exclusive gateway and service tasks A and B are removed and replaced by a single new service task C. The active instances are currently waiting on service task A and must continue on the new service task C.
question: Which mapping instruction lets the active service task A continue on the new service task C after migration?

options.text: A mapping instruction from source element id A to target element id C combined with one from element id B to element id C, in order to keep both source service tasks alive after migration.
A single mapping instruction from source element id A to target element id C, so that the active service task A is migrated onto the new service task C in the redesigned target process.
A mapping instruction from source element id B to target element id C; only the inactive sibling task may be remapped to a new replacement service task during a process instance migration.
A mapping instruction from source element id A to target element id C_user_task; the migration tool will widen the type from service task to user task during the active instance migration.

b.text: Correct. A single mapping instruction from source element id A to target element id C migrates the active service task A onto the new service task C in the target process, which is exactly the documented pattern for this redesign.

a.text: Incorrect. Element B is inactive and is not present in the target; mapping it adds no value, and only the active element instance of A needs a mapping to C in this scenario.

c.text: Incorrect. Mapping instructions migrate active element instances; the inactive sibling task B does not need a mapping, but the active task A does.

d.text: Incorrect. Mapping cannot change the element type, so an active service task can only be mapped to another service task such as C; widening to a user task is not allowed.

explanation: Active element instances can be migrated to a new target element by providing a source-to-target mapping instruction, provided both elements are of the same type, so service task A maps cleanly to service task C.

scenario: Forge Bank wants to reset a one-week SLA timer attached to user task Approve for 400 loans so that the migrated instances wait a fresh two weeks rather than only the two days that remain from the original timer. The new process version updates the timer boundary event duration but keeps the user task element id unchanged across source and target.
question: Which mapping instructions must the migration plan contain to cancel the existing timer subscription and start a new two-week timer on the target boundary event?

options.text: Map active user task A to A and omit any mapping for the timer boundary event so the engine closes the existing subscription and opens a new one on the target boundary event
Map active user task A to A and add a variable instruction resetTimer set to true so the engine recreates the timer subscription using the target two-week duration on activation
Map active user task A to A and provide a separate cancel-and-recreate operation for the timer boundary event so the engine performs both steps within the same migration transaction
Map active user task A to A and map timer boundary event Timer1 to Timer2 so the engine migrates the timer subscription and the new two-week duration applies retroactively to the elapsed days

a.text: Correct. Omitting the mapping for the timer boundary event closes the existing subscription and opens a new one on the target boundary event during migration. The new timer then runs the full target duration, achieving the desired reset behaviour for the in-flight instances.

d.text: Incorrect. Mapping the catch event preserves the existing subscription, which keeps the previously elapsed duration. The migrated instance would still fire after the remaining two days rather than waiting the full two weeks of the target definition.

b.text: Incorrect. There is no resetTimer variable contract in process instance migration. The documented way to reset a catch event subscription is to omit the catch event mapping while keeping the mapping for the active element it is attached to.

c.text: Incorrect. The migration command does not expose a separate cancel-and-recreate operation for catch events. Cancel and recreate behaviour is achieved simply by leaving the catch event out of the mapping instructions in the migration plan.

explanation: When a catch event is left out of the migration plan but the active element it is attached to is mapped, the engine closes the source subscription and opens a new one on the target. This is the documented mechanism for resetting a timer boundary event to the target definition's duration.

scenario: Lattice Insurance is migrating 2500 claims that are waiting at an intermediate signal catch event attached to an event-based gateway. The team has renamed the signal in the target process definition from claim-paid to claim-settled and wants the in-flight instances to start subscribing to the new signal name once the migration completes.
question: Which mapping choice for the intermediate signal catch event causes the subscription to be reopened against the renamed signal in the target process?

options.text: Map the intermediate signal catch event to itself and add a variable instruction signalName set to claim-settled so the broker rebinds the subscription to the new signal at runtime
Omit the mapping for the intermediate signal catch event so the source subscription is closed and a new one is opened against the target catch event with the renamed signal name
Provide no mappings for any element and rely on the broker to auto-rename signal subscriptions whenever a new deployment is detected within the migrated process instance
Map the intermediate signal catch event to the renamed target catch event so the subscription is migrated and the broker rewrites the signal name to match the target definition

b.text: Correct. To update a signal name for an intermediate signal catch event during migration, the mapping for that catch event must be omitted. The engine then closes the source subscription and opens a new one on the target catch event using the renamed signal name.

d.text: Incorrect. Mapping a catch event preserves the existing subscription and does not rewrite the signal name. The migrated instance would continue to be subscribed to the original signal name from the source process definition.

a.text: Incorrect. Migration does not provide a signalName variable instruction to rebind a subscription. Subscription identity for a catch event is decided by mapping or omitting the mapping, not by setting a variable.

c.text: Incorrect. The migration plan must also map every active element, and there is no auto-rename behaviour at deployment time. A migration command without the required element mappings would be rejected by the validation step.

explanation: To update the signal name on an intermediate signal catch event after migration, the catch event mapping must be omitted. The engine then closes the existing subscription and opens a new one on the target catch event with the renamed signal.

scenario: A bank operates 2000 loan applications per day through a process that contains a joining parallel gateway with two incoming sequence flows. The team plans a migration that adds a third incoming sequence flow on the target gateway and needs to confirm what conditions the target gateway must satisfy for the migration of an in-flight joining gateway instance to be accepted.
question: Which condition must the target joining gateway satisfy for a joining parallel gateway migration to be accepted by the engine?

options.text: The target gateway must have exactly the same number of incoming sequence flows as the source gateway so the engine can pair every flow to a target flow on a one-to-one basis
The target gateway must be converted into an inclusive gateway before migration so the engine can re-evaluate the conditions on the remaining incoming sequence flows after migration
The target gateway must have at least the same number of incoming sequence flows as the source joining gateway so all previously taken flows can still be represented in the target
The target gateway must have fewer incoming sequence flows than the source gateway so the engine can collapse pending sequence flows into a single waiting branch on migration

c.text: Correct. For migrating a joining parallel or inclusive gateway, the target gateway must have at least the same number of incoming sequence flows as the source gateway. This guarantees that every previously taken sequence flow can be represented on the target.

a.text: Incorrect. The target gateway is allowed to have more incoming sequence flows than the source; the constraint is at least the same number, not exactly the same. Process changes that add new incoming flows are explicitly supported.

d.text: Incorrect. Fewer incoming sequence flows would lose information about flows that have already been taken on the source gateway. The migration rule requires at least as many incoming sequence flows on the target, not fewer.

b.text: Incorrect. There is no requirement to convert a joining parallel gateway into an inclusive gateway before migration. Mapping instructions cannot change the element type, so the gateway must remain the same type across source and target.

explanation: When migrating a joining parallel or inclusive gateway, the target gateway must have at least the same number of incoming sequence flows as the source. The gateway and the relevant taken sequence flow must also be referenced by mapping instructions in the migration plan.

scenario: A bank tries to migrate 12 loans from version 3 to version 4 of the same process definition so that a corrected end event configuration is applied retroactively. The operations team needs to confirm whether these terminal process instances are even eligible for the process instance migration command in Camunda 8.
question: Which process instances can be migrated using the process instance migration command in Camunda 8?

options.text: Only completed process instances can be migrated because the engine needs a known terminal state to verify that no further sequence flows will be taken during migration
Banned and terminated process instances can also be migrated as long as the migration plan provides mappings for every previously active element id at the time of termination
Active and completed process instances can both be migrated as long as the target process definition is deployed and the source definition still exists in Zeebe
Only active process instances that have not yet been completed, terminated, or banned can be migrated to another process definition with the migration command

d.text: Correct. Process instance migration can only migrate active process instances, that is, existing process instances that have not yet been completed, terminated, or banned. Already-finished instances cannot be migrated to a new definition for retroactive fixes.

c.text: Incorrect. Completed process instances cannot be migrated; the migration command rejects them. The deployment status of source and target definitions is necessary but not sufficient on its own to make a terminal instance eligible.

a.text: Incorrect. Completed instances are explicitly not eligible for migration. The active state of the instance, not a terminal state, is the prerequisite for the migration command to be accepted by the engine.

b.text: Incorrect. Banned and terminated process instances are not eligible for migration regardless of how complete the mapping instructions are. The active state of the instance is checked up front during validation.

explanation: Process instance migration applies only to active process instances. Instances that are already completed, terminated, or banned cannot be migrated, even if the source and target process definitions are deployed and well-formed.

scenario: An insurance broker is migrating 80 claims and discovers that two source catch events in the migration plan have been mapped to the same target catch event id by mistake. The engineering team needs to know how the engine handles this case during validation and which limitation of the migration plan is being violated by the duplicate target.
question: Which validation rule does a migration plan violate when two catch events in the source process are mapped to the same catch event in the target process?

options.text: The migration plan must include a deduplication directive that lists the duplicate sources; with that directive the validation accepts mapping two source catch events to one target id
Each catch event in the target can only be the target of a mapping instruction once, so mapping two source catch events to the same target id is rejected by the migration validation
The migration plan is accepted but the second mapping instruction is silently dropped during execution so only the first source catch event is migrated to the target catch event
The target catch event must declare a multi-source attribute set to true; without that attribute, the validation rejects mapping two source catch events to a single target id

b.text: Correct. Two catch events in the source cannot be mapped to the same catch event in the target. Each catch event in the target can only be the target of a mapping instruction once, so the migration plan is rejected by the validation step before any migration happens.

d.text: Incorrect. There is no multi-source attribute on catch events that would unlock mapping two sources to a single target. The rule that each target catch event can be the destination of only one mapping is unconditional.

a.text: Incorrect. The migration plan format has no deduplication directive that would permit mapping two sources to one target. The validation rule rejects the plan regardless of any additional directives the caller might add.

c.text: Incorrect. The engine does not silently drop one of the duplicate mappings during execution. The whole migration command is rejected with an explanatory message and no state changes are applied to the process instance.

explanation: Two catch events in the source process cannot be mapped to the same catch event in the target, and each target catch event can be the destination of at most one mapping instruction. The migration is rejected during validation if either rule is violated.

scenario: Meridian Insurance runs a claims process on Camunda 8.8 with an embedded subprocess containing tasks A, B, and C joined by an inclusive gateway. Across 2500 claim instances, task A completed and the engine waited on task B; task C did not start because the worker for A returned an unexpected value. The operator wants to activate task C inside the same running subprocess instance with one modification command.
question: How does the engine handle the activation of task C nested inside the existing embedded subprocess instance at Meridian Insurance?

options.text: The engine creates a new instance of the embedded subprocess and activates task C inside the new instance, leaving task B running in the original subprocess instance at Meridian Insurance
The engine activates task C inside the existing instance of the embedded subprocess so task B and task C are both active in the same subprocess instance at Meridian Insurance
The engine rejects the modification command because nested activation inside an already running embedded subprocess instance is not allowed by the modification API at Meridian Insurance
The engine activates the start event of the embedded subprocess again and then activates task C, applying input variable mappings of the subprocess once more at Meridian Insurance

a.text: Incorrect. The docs state the engine activates the element in an existing instance of the subprocess; a new subprocess instance is created only if no active instance exists.

b.text: Correct. The docs describe exactly this case: activating a nested element places it in the existing instance of the embedded subprocess, so task B and task C run together in the same subprocess instance.

d.text: Incorrect. Even when the engine has to create a new subprocess instance, the docs state it does not activate the start event of the subprocess or apply input variable mappings; here the existing instance is reused.

c.text: Incorrect. Nested activation is supported; the command is only rejected when the subprocess has more than one active instance and the engine cannot decide which to target.

explanation: Activating a nested element targets the existing instance of the surrounding embedded or event subprocess. If no active instance exists the engine creates one without firing its start event or input mappings. The command is rejected only when multiple subprocess instances exist.

scenario: Vertex Insurance models a loan adjudication flow for its lending arm and runs 2500 claims per day in Camunda 8.8. An operations engineer is preparing a runbook for process instance modification and needs to know which element types the engine refuses to activate, so the team avoids submitting commands that the engine would reject at apply time.
question: Which element type is rejected by the engine when used as the activating element in a modification command?

options.text: A boundary event attached to a service task element in the top-level process scope of the instance
An intermediate timer catch event placed in the top-level process scope of the running instance
A user task element placed in the top-level process scope that is not currently active in the instance
A service task element placed in the top-level process scope that is not currently active in the instance

c.text: Incorrect. A user task in the top-level scope is a regular activity and can be targeted by activation instructions; the documented limitations do not call out plain user tasks as forbidden activation targets.

d.text: Incorrect. A service task in the top-level scope is a regular activity and can be activated by a modification command; the documented limitations do not list plain service tasks as forbidden activation targets.

a.text: Correct. The documented limitations explicitly list boundary events among the element types the engine rejects as activating elements, alongside process and subprocess start events, events of an event-based gateway, multi-instance children, and sequence flows.

b.text: Incorrect. A regular intermediate timer catch event in the main flow is a plain catch event and is not in the rejection list; only events that belong to an event-based gateway are called out as forbidden activation targets.

explanation: Process instance modification refuses certain activating elements: process or subprocess start events, boundary events, events that belong to an event-based gateway, elements inside a multi-instance subprocess, and sequence flows. Regular activities and standalone catch events remain valid targets.

scenario: A logistics dispatcher's process contains an embedded subprocess that currently has no active instance. Operators must activate task C inside that subprocess across 40 shipments using the modification command. The architect needs to understand what the engine does when the enclosing subprocess does not yet have any active instance to host the nested element activation.
question: What does the engine do when activating a nested element if the enclosing subprocess does not currently have any active instance?

options.text: It activates the requested element at the process root scope and re-parents it to the subprocess only after the next deployment refresh runs across the affected process instances
It rejects the modification command because a subprocess instance must already exist before a nested element can be activated, so the operator has to activate the subprocess separately first
It creates a new subprocess instance first, including any event subscriptions for its boundary events, then activates the requested element inside the new subprocess instance

It creates a new subprocess instance and additionally activates the subprocess start event with its input variable mappings so the regular activation path of the subprocess is fully replayed end to end

c.text: Correct. If the subprocess does not have an active instance, the process instance creates a new instance of the subprocess first, including creating event subscriptions for boundary events. The requested element is then activated inside the new subprocess instance.

b.text: Incorrect. The engine does not reject the command in this case. Creating the missing subprocess instance is part of the standard nested element activation flow, so no separate activation step is required from the operator.

a.text: Incorrect. The element is not placed at the root scope. It is always activated inside an instance of its enclosing subprocess; the engine creates that subprocess instance on the fly if one does not already exist.

d.text: Incorrect. The newly created subprocess instance does not activate the subprocess start event or apply input variable mappings. It differs from a regular subprocess activation in exactly that way during modification-driven nested activation.

explanation: When a nested element is activated through modification and the enclosing subprocess has no active instance, the engine creates a new subprocess instance first, including its boundary event subscriptions, but it skips the start event and input variable mappings of the subprocess.

scenario: Mercer Insurance runs a Camunda 8.8 claims process handling 5,000 claims per day. After the upstream message service failed, an SRE issues a modification command to activate a follow-up task inside a non-interrupting message event subprocess and includes variable instructions for the missing message payload without specifying any scope on those instructions.
question: Where will the process instance store the variables that were supplied with the modification command in this situation?

options.text: The process instance rejects the modification because variable instructions without an explicit scope are not allowed when activating elements inside event subprocesses.
The process instance creates the variables on the activity scope of the activating task itself, so only that task and not its peers can read them.
The process instance creates the variables as local variables of the activating event subprocess scope, hiding them from the surrounding process scope.
The process instance creates the variables globally in the root scope of the process instance, making them visible to every active and future element.

d.text: Correct. The docs state that if no scope is defined on a variable instruction, the process instance sets the variables globally in the root scope of the process instance before activating the element.

c.text: Incorrect. Variables are only created as local to a scope when the variable instruction explicitly names that scope, and the modification in this scenario provides no scope at all.

b.text: Incorrect. The activity scope of the activating task is only used when the variable instruction names a flow scope of that element; the documented default is the root scope.

a.text: Incorrect. The modification command accepts variable instructions without a scope and treats them as global root-scope variables rather than rejecting them for missing a scope.

explanation: When a variable instruction inside a modification command omits the scope, the process instance places the values in the root scope so they are globally visible. A scope is only used when explicitly defined, and it must be a flow scope of the activating element. Variables are set before activating the element so they are available for input mappings.

scenario: Sentinel Bank operates a Camunda 8.8 loan-origination process orchestrating 4,200 applications per day. An operator wants to terminate a call activity in one running instance to recover after the downstream credit-bureau service stayed down for PT3H, but the call activity has already produced a child process instance with two active jobs inside it.
question: What does the modification command do to the child process instance when the operator terminates the parent call activity?

options.text: The engine refuses to terminate the call activity until every active element inside the child process instance has finished or been terminated individually first.
The engine keeps the child running and only detaches it from the parent, so the child becomes an orphan that completes on its own schedule.
The engine terminates the child process instance entirely, including its currently active element instances, as part of terminating the call activity.
The engine terminates only the call activity boundary in the parent and leaves the child process instance untouched until its message subscription expires later.

c.text: Correct. The docs state that if the terminating element instance is a call activity, the engine terminates the child process instance, including any active element instances inside that child.

b.text: Incorrect. Terminating the call activity does not detach the child; the child process instance is terminated as a direct consequence of terminating its parent call activity.

a.text: Incorrect. Termination of a call activity does not require the child to be drained first; the engine cascades the termination into the child process instance automatically.

d.text: Incorrect. There is no documented mode that leaves the child running after the parent call activity is terminated by a modification command on the parent instance.

explanation: When a modification terminates a call activity, the documented rule is that the child process instance is terminated along with it. This is also the recommended way to clear a child, because the child's own last active instance cannot be terminated directly by a modification command.

scenario: Beacon Brokerage broadcasts 200 events per day in Camunda 8.8 to fan out price-update signals across 35 microservices subscribed to its trade-routing processes. A platform engineer is documenting the supported broadcast channels so the integration team picks a mechanism the orchestration cluster actually exposes for the brokerage's external systems.
question: Which set of mechanisms does the documentation list for broadcasting signals in Camunda 8.8?

options.text: A signal boundary event, the Tasklist broadcast signal HTTP endpoint, or Zeebe's BroadcastMessage RPC of the orchestration cluster
A signal catch event in a process, the Orchestration Cluster REST publish signal endpoint, or Zeebe's PublishMessage RPC of the gateway
A signal throw event or signal end event, the Orchestration Cluster REST broadcast signal endpoint, or Zeebe's BroadcastSignal RPC
A signal start event in a process, the Zeebe BroadcastJob RPC, or the Tasklist broadcast signal HTTP endpoint of the cluster

d.text: Incorrect. A signal start event creates a subscription rather than broadcasting a signal, the Zeebe RPC for broadcasting is BroadcastSignal and not BroadcastJob, and Tasklist does not expose a broadcast signal endpoint.

c.text: Correct. The documentation lists three broadcast channels: a signal throw event or signal end event in BPMN, the Orchestration Cluster REST broadcast signal endpoint, and Zeebe's BroadcastSignal RPC on the gateway service.

b.text: Incorrect. A signal catch event receives a signal rather than broadcasting one, and Zeebe's RPC for signals is BroadcastSignal; PublishMessage is the messaging RPC and is not used for signal broadcast.

a.text: Incorrect. A signal boundary event reacts to a broadcast rather than producing one, and Zeebe's RPC is BroadcastSignal; BroadcastMessage does not exist as a Zeebe gateway RPC for signals.

explanation: Signals can be broadcast from a signal throw or end event in BPMN, from the Orchestration Cluster REST broadcast signal endpoint, or from the BroadcastSignal RPC on the Zeebe gateway service. Catch, start, and boundary events subscribe rather than broadcast.

scenario: Forge Bank runs 60000 transactions per day in Camunda 8.8 across 8 clusters of process applications. A platform engineer is sizing capacity for a new global broadcast that triggers all running loan-origination processes, and the team needs to know how a signal broadcast scales with the partition count and matching subscription count of the cluster.
question: What is the cardinality and partition behavior of a broadcast signal in Camunda 8.8?

options.text: The broadcast triggers all matching subscriptions on the local partition only, and remote partitions receive a deferred replay through the export stream.
The broadcast triggers all matching subscriptions and is sent to every partition, with network traffic scaling linearly with the partition count of the cluster.
The broadcast triggers a single matching subscription cluster-wide and is sent to one partition selected by least-loaded routing on the gateway service.
The broadcast triggers only the first matching subscription per partition and is sent to a single owner partition selected by a hash of the signal name.

b.text: Correct. A broadcast iterates over all matching subscriptions and is sent to every partition; the documented performance caution notes that this network traffic scales linearly with the number of partitions in the cluster.

d.text: Incorrect. Signal broadcast does not select a single owner partition by hash and does not stop at the first matching subscription; all matching subscriptions on all partitions are triggered.

a.text: Incorrect. Broadcasts are sent to all partitions immediately, not just the local one with a replay; the documented traffic pattern is per-partition fan-out at broadcast time.

c.text: Incorrect. A broadcast does not pick a single subscription cluster-wide; the documented behaviour fans out to all matching subscriptions across all partitions of the cluster.

explanation: A broadcast signal triggers every matching subscription and is sent to every partition. Both factors affect performance: many subscriptions can spawn many process executions, and partition fan-out makes network traffic scale linearly with the partition count.

scenario: Lattice Insurance models a Camunda 8.8 claims-handling process that already publishes 18,000 messages per day to single instances. Architects now want to notify every running claim that a regulatory deadline has passed using a single platform-level event rather than correlating thousands of individual messages by key.
question: How do signals differ from messages in this Camunda 8.8 scenario according to the documentation?

options.text: A signal is correlated to a single process instance through a signal key, while a message is broadcast to all running process instances that subscribe by name.
Both signals and messages are broadcast to all instances by default, with the difference being that signals also persist for new instances created later in the day.
A signal is broadcast and triggers all matching signal events at once, while a message is correlated to a specific process instance through correlation keys.
Both signals and messages are correlated by a single key to one process instance, but signals are kept on a separate partition for higher throughput per second.

c.text: Correct. The docs describe signals as a similar concept to messages but explain that messages are correlated to a specific process instance while signals trigger all matching signal events with a single broadcast.

a.text: Incorrect. This reverses the documented behaviour; messages are the per-instance correlated concept, while signals are the broadcast concept that fans out to all matching events.

d.text: Incorrect. Messages are not broadcast and signals are not correlated by a single key to one instance; the two concepts differ in delivery rather than in partition placement.

b.text: Incorrect. Messages are explicitly correlated to one process instance, and the docs do not describe signals as persisting beyond active subscriptions for future instances created later.

explanation: Messages and signals are both event-style triggers, but the docs single out the key difference that messages are correlated to a specific process instance, while a single signal broadcast triggers every matching signal event at the same time across the cluster.

scenario: Pinnacle Bank automates a loan origination process in Camunda 8.8 across 8000 transactions per day. A backend developer joining from Camunda 7 is learning how process variables are represented and scoped on a process instance so the team can design payloads that downstream services consume safely across embedded subprocesses and call activities of the origination flow.
question: Which statement describes how a process variable is represented and scoped on a Camunda 8 process instance?

options.text: A variable holds a name and a JSON value, but every variable is global to the process instance regardless of where on the BPMN it is set.
A variable holds a name and a typed Java value, and its visibility is determined by the process definition compatibility mode of the deployment.
A variable holds a name and a binary blob value, and its visibility is determined by deployment-level access control lists on the orchestration cluster.
A variable holds a name and a JSON value, and its visibility is determined by the variable's scope inside the process instance of the deployment.

c.text: Incorrect. The documentation describes a variable as having a name and a JSON value rather than a binary blob, and visibility is determined by variable scope on the process instance rather than by deployment-level access control lists.

b.text: Incorrect. Camunda 8 variables carry JSON values rather than typed Java values; the engine is language-agnostic and does not key visibility off a compatibility mode of the process definition.

a.text: Incorrect. Variables are not automatically global to the process instance; the documentation states that the visibility of a variable is defined by its scope, which depends on where in the BPMN it is set.

d.text: Correct. The documentation defines a variable as a name and a JSON value whose visibility is defined by the variable scope on the process instance, and authors can leverage and customise that scope when automating a BPMN process.

explanation: Camunda 8 variables are part of a process instance and consist of a name plus a JSON value. Visibility is determined by the variable's scope on the instance, which authors can leverage and customise when modelling BPMN or orchestrating human tasks.

scenario: Stellar Bank stores currency amounts as process variables across 9000 transactions per day in Camunda 8.8. The team needs to understand how non-integer numeric values are represented internally so they can decide whether to store high-precision balances as JSON numbers or as strings.
question: How are non-integer numeric process variables stored according to Camunda 8.8?

options.text: As BigDecimal values providing arbitrary precision suitable for currency and high-precision calculations across the process payload
As fixed-point decimal values constrained to 8 fractional digits matching the Camunda 7 numeric variable storage contract for processes
As 64-bit signed integers truncated to the nearest whole number, with fractional digits stored separately in a sidecar variable scope
As IEEE-754 double-precision values providing roughly 15 to 17 significant decimal digits, with no arbitrary BigDecimal precision available

d.text: Correct. The documentation specifies that non-integer numbers are stored as IEEE-754 double-precision values, giving roughly 15 to 17 significant decimal digits, and explicitly states there is no arbitrary BigDecimal precision in process variables.

a.text: Incorrect. BigDecimal-style arbitrary precision is not the variable storage model in Camunda 8. The docs recommend storing such values as strings or in an external data store when high precision is required, rather than as numeric process variables.

c.text: Incorrect. Integers and non-integers are distinct cases in the documentation. Integers are bounded to the 64-bit integer range, while non-integers use IEEE-754 doubles. No truncation or sidecar scope is documented.

b.text: Incorrect. There is no Camunda 7 fixed-point decimal contract carried over to Camunda 8.8. Non-integer numeric variables use IEEE-754 double-precision according to the variables concept page in the documentation.

explanation: Camunda 8.8 stores process variables as JSON values. Integer numbers are bounded to the 64-bit integer range and non-integer numbers use IEEE-754 double-precision, which provides roughly 15 to 17 significant decimal digits. For arbitrary precision or very large numbers, store the value as a string or in an external data store rather than as a numeric process variable.

scenario: Helios Insurance starts a process instance carrying a JSON quote document averaging 1.6 MB per request across 4000 policies per day in Camunda 8.8. Operations is sizing the payload conservatively and wants to know the documented safety guidance for variable payload size in commands or events that include variables.
question: What is the rule-of-thumb safe variable payload size for commands or events in Camunda 8.8?

options.text: Approximately 1.5 MB per command or event, leaving headroom for follow-up records that the engine may append to temporarily duplicate the variable payload
Approximately 8 MB per command, doubling the 4 MB hard limit thanks to internal compression applied to the variable payload at submission time
Approximately 256 KB per command, matching the gateway request quota documented for Zeebe ingress traffic shaping applied across the cluster
Approximately 4 MB per command, matching the hard payload limit, and the broker never appends follow-up records that duplicate the variable payload

d.text: Incorrect. 4 MB is the absolute payload limit including engine-internal data, not a safe rule of thumb. The docs explicitly warn that less than 4 MB is available for variables alone and that the broker may append follow-up records within the same batch.

a.text: Correct. The documentation states that approximately 1.5 MB is considered safe for commands or events that include variables, given that the engine may append follow-up records that temporarily duplicate the variable payload within the same batch.

c.text: Incorrect. There is no 256 KB gateway quota documented as the safe payload size for variables. The rule-of-thumb safety threshold cited in the variables concept page is approximately 1.5 MB per command or event.

b.text: Incorrect. The documented payload limit is 4 MB and there is no doubling via compression. Operators should target around 1 MB and validate with a production-like test case to stay comfortably below the effective limit.

explanation: The process instance payload is limited to 4 MB total, including engine-internal data. As a rule of thumb, approximately 1.5 MB is considered safe for commands or events that include variables, because the engine may append follow-up records that temporarily duplicate the payload within the same batch. Operators should target around 1 MB and validate under production-like load.

scenario: Aurora Logistics models a shipping process with an embedded subprocess in Camunda 8.8. The team handles 12000 shipments per day and needs to reason about how a variable named priority defined inside the subprocess interacts with a same-named variable in the root scope of the process instance.
question: How does a variable defined in a subprocess scope behave when a variable with the same name already exists in a higher scope?

options.text: It is silently merged with the higher-scope value into an array variable, with same-named variables in nested scopes concatenated by the engine at activation
It replaces the higher-scope variable globally for the lifetime of the process instance, with the engine resolving variable names from the deepest scope upward
It is rejected at deployment as variable name collisions across nested scopes are validated and blocked by the Zeebe deployment service
It covers the higher-scope variable inside the subprocess, so activities in that scope observe only the local value while outer activities still see the original

c.text: Incorrect. Deployment does not reject same-named variables across nested scopes. The docs describe scope-based visibility and shadowing at runtime, not a deployment-time validation rule that blocks repeated variable names across nested scopes.

b.text: Incorrect. A variable in a deeper scope does not replace the higher-scope variable globally. It covers the higher-scope value only inside its own scope, leaving the higher scope intact for activities outside the subprocess.

d.text: Correct. The variables concept page states that when a variable in a deeper scope has the same name as one in a higher scope it covers the higher-scope variable, so activities in the deeper scope observe only the local value while outer activities see the original.

a.text: Incorrect. There is no implicit concatenation or array merge for same-named variables across scopes. Visibility is controlled by scope nesting and shadowing, as described in the variables documentation page.

explanation: Variable scopes define visibility. A variable in a deeper scope with the same name as one in a higher scope covers the higher-scope value inside the deeper scope only. Outer activities continue to observe the original higher-scope variable, because shadowing is scope-local rather than global to the process instance.

scenario: Meridian Bank models a payments process in Camunda 8.8 with multiple service tasks across 16000 transactions per day. The team defines ioMapping extension elements with source and target FEEL expressions and wants to predict the resolved value when the source expression references a process variable property that does not exist.
question: What value does a variable mapping produce when its source expression references a variable or nested property that does not exist?

options.text: The variable mapping produces the literal string undefined and the engine logs a warning record in the event store for operators to review
The variable mapping produces the previous value of the target variable in the parent scope so the original value remains preserved on the target
The variable mapping resolves to null when the referenced variable or nested property does not exist, and the same applies when the source is left empty
The variable mapping produces an empty string and the engine raises a deployment-time validation error referencing the missing path expression

d.text: Incorrect. There is no deployment-time validation that blocks missing source paths in variable mappings. The variables concept page states that the value resolves to null at runtime when the referenced variable or nested property does not exist.

a.text: Incorrect. The engine does not produce a literal undefined string for missing variables. The documentation specifies that missing source variables or properties resolve to null, the same behavior as omitting the source entirely.

b.text: Incorrect. The mapping does not fall back to a prior value in the parent scope. A missing source variable or nested property resolves the value to null according to the variables documentation, not to any cached previous value.

c.text: Correct. The variables concept page states that if the referenced variable or nested property does not exist the value resolves to null, and the same applies when the source expression is not provided at all on the mapping.

explanation: Variable mappings have source and target expressions. The source defines the value, usually by accessing a process variable. If the variable or nested property does not exist, or if no source is provided, the value resolves to null. The target then references the location where the resolved value is stored on the scope.

scenario: Forge Insurance models a claim triage process in Camunda 8.8 with input mappings on a service task that handles 8000 claims per day. The team needs to predict where the resulting variable lives after the input mapping executes and how it interacts with surrounding scopes for downstream activities.
question: Where is a new variable placed when an input mapping is applied to a Camunda 8.8 service task or subprocess?

options.text: The new variable is created as a local variable in the scope of the element where the input mapping is defined, leaving outer scopes untouched at activation
The new variable is materialized as a custom header on the activated job for the worker to read and is never persisted as a process variable record on the scope
The new variable is created on every parallel branch of the process instance simultaneously, regardless of the scope where the input mapping was defined on a task
The new variable is created in the root process instance scope so that downstream activities outside the element can also observe it immediately after activation

a.text: Correct. The Input mappings section states that applying an input mapping creates a new local variable in the scope where the mapping is defined. Outer scopes do not observe the new variable unless propagation is configured separately on the element.

d.text: Incorrect. Input mappings do not write to the root process instance scope by default. They create a local variable in the scope of the element where the mapping is defined, which keeps the new value isolated to that element scope.

c.text: Incorrect. The new variable is scoped to the element where the input mapping was defined, not broadcast across parallel branches. Parallel branches each have their own scopes and do not share locals from sibling elements.

b.text: Incorrect. Input mappings do not produce custom headers on the activated job. They create a local variable in the element scope and may use either FEEL expressions or static values for the source of the mapping.

explanation: Input mappings can be defined on service tasks, script tasks, business rule tasks, call activities, user tasks, send tasks, and subprocesses. When applied, they create a new local variable in the scope where the mapping is defined. The source may be a FEEL expression or a static value, and an empty source maps the target to null.

scenario: Vertex Bank models an authorization service task in Camunda 8.8 across 22000 transactions per day. The job returns a results object with status and transactionId. The team defines a single output mapping that maps status to paymentStatus but does not define any mapping for transactionId, and wants to predict which variables are merged into the parent process scope.
question: Which variables are merged into the parent scope when at least one output mapping is defined on a service task?

options.text: Only the variables produced by the configured output mappings are merged into the parent scope, while results variables without a mapping remain local and are not merged
All results variables are merged into the parent scope, and any output mappings are applied in addition to produce extra derived variables alongside them on the parent
Only the original results variables are merged into the parent scope, while output mappings overwrite them in place but never add new variables to the parent scope
All results variables and all output mapping targets are merged twice into the parent scope, first as locals and then as merged copies sharing the same name pairs

b.text: Incorrect. When at least one output mapping is defined, results variables without a mapping are not merged into the parent scope. The mappings determine what propagates upward, not the raw results variables alongside the mapped outputs.

a.text: Correct. The Output mappings section states that if one or more output mappings are defined, results variables are first set as local variables, then the mappings are applied to create new variables in the scope, and only those mapped variables are merged into the parent scope.

c.text: Incorrect. Output mappings do not overwrite results variables in place at the parent scope. Mapped results become local variables in the task scope, then the mapping outputs are merged into the parent scope as new variables.

d.text: Incorrect. There is no double-merge of results and mappings into the parent scope. Results variables become local to the task scope, and only the configured mapping outputs are merged upward into the parent scope.

explanation: When one or more output mappings are defined, results variables are set as local variables in the task scope. The output mappings then create new variables in that scope, and those new variables are merged into the parent scope. Results variables that have no mapping are not merged. If no output mappings are defined, all results variables are merged into the process instance.

scenario: Catalyst Insurance configures user tasks in Tasklist for claims agents across 5000 claims per day in Camunda 8.8. The team wants the task tile to display a short descriptive line so reviewers can distinguish similar approvals at a glance, and wants to know which reserved variable controls this display on the tile.
question: Which reserved process variable name controls the descriptive line shown on a task tile in Camunda 8.8 Tasklist?

options.text: A reserved variable named taskTileSubtitle that is parsed by Tasklist and rendered as the secondary descriptive line on the task tile for the assigned user
A reserved variable named userTaskHeaderLabel that is set as a custom header on the activated job and rendered as the descriptive task line on the tile
A reserved variable named camundaTaskDescription that is read from the user task element documentation and rendered as the descriptive line on the tile
A reserved variable named taskContextDisplayName whose value is rendered as the descriptive line on the task tile in Tasklist for the assigned user

a.text: Incorrect. Tasklist does not read a reserved variable named taskTileSubtitle for the descriptive line. The reserved variable for the descriptive line on a task tile is taskContextDisplayName according to the variables concept page in the documentation.

c.text: Incorrect. There is no reserved variable named camundaTaskDescription in Camunda 8.8, and the task documentation field is not rendered as the descriptive line on the task tile in Tasklist for assigned users.

d.text: Correct. The Context variable section identifies taskContextDisplayName as the reserved variable whose value is shown on the task tile, and notes that the name is reserved exclusively for this purpose and should not be reused elsewhere.

b.text: Incorrect. Custom headers on a user task are not rendered as the task tile label. The reserved variable taskContextDisplayName carries the descriptive line shown on the tile according to the variables concept page.

explanation: Context variables describe the context of a task and group variables into a descriptive label. The reserved variable name taskContextDisplayName is used exclusively for this purpose, and its value is rendered on the task tile in Tasklist. Context variables are not currently available in Tasklist V2 according to the migration notes.

scenario: Tower Bank is migrating a payments process handling 40 transactions per second to Camunda 8.8, and the new development team wants to formalise how variables are represented in the engine before they start designing input and output mappings for service tasks in the redesigned model.
question: Which statement matches the Camunda 8.8 documentation about how variables are represented inside a process instance?

options.text: A variable has a name and a JSON value, but its visibility is fixed to the activity that created it and cannot be observed by other activities in the process at all.
A variable has a name and a JSON value, is part of a process instance, and its visibility is determined by the variable's scope inside that running instance.
A variable has a name and a typed value declared upfront in the BPMN diagram, and its visibility is global to every running process instance across the cluster.
A variable has only a numeric identifier and a binary payload, and its visibility is configured per-deployment in the cluster's namespace settings rather than at runtime.

b.text: Correct. The docs define variables as part of a process instance with a name, a JSON value, and visibility governed by the variable's scope inside the instance.

c.text: Incorrect. Variables are stored as JSON values without an upfront BPMN type declaration, and their visibility is per-instance scope, not cluster-global across all running instances.

d.text: Incorrect. Variables have a name and a JSON value, not numeric identifiers or opaque binary payloads, and their visibility is governed by the variable's scope inside the instance.

a.text: Incorrect. Variables can be observed by activities in the defining scope and in nested scopes, depending on the documented scope rules, not only by the activity that created them.

explanation: Per the variables overview, each variable belongs to a process instance and consists of a name and a JSON value. The variable's scope, not the activity that created it, controls which parts of the running process instance can read it.

scenario: Helio Bank stores a precise monetary balance with up to 28 significant decimal digits in a Camunda 8.8 reconciliation process running 6,000 transactions daily. The architect plans to store this balance directly as a process variable of type number and asks whether the engine will preserve the full precision in payload.
question: What does the Camunda 8.8 variables documentation say about storing a 28-digit precise decimal directly as a number variable?

options.text: Non-integer numbers must be wrapped in a BPMN extension element to enable the engine's arbitrary-precision mode, after which the 28-digit decimal can be stored as a number variable.
Non-integer numbers are stored as arbitrary-precision BigDecimal values inside the engine, so a 28-digit precise decimal is preserved exactly without any further configuration in the model.
Non-integer numbers are stored as 128-bit decimals after Camunda 8.8, which is sufficient to keep about 34 significant digits and therefore the 28-digit decimal stays exact in payload.
Non-integer numbers are stored as IEEE 754 doubles with roughly 15 to 17 significant decimal digits, so a 28-digit precise decimal should be kept as a string or in an external data store.

d.text: Correct. The docs state non-integer numbers are stored as IEEE 754 doubles with roughly 15-17 significant decimal digits and recommend strings or an external store for arbitrary-precision values.

b.text: Incorrect. The engine does not store non-integer numbers as BigDecimal; the documented precision is limited by the IEEE 754 double representation rather than arbitrary precision.

c.text: Incorrect. There is no documented 128-bit decimal mode in Camunda 8.8 variables; non-integer numbers remain IEEE 754 doubles with the limited significant-digit range stated in the docs.

a.text: Incorrect. The docs describe no BPMN extension element that switches the engine to arbitrary-precision number storage for variables; the IEEE 754 double representation always applies.

explanation: Number variables follow the JSON model: integers fit a 64-bit range, while non-integers use IEEE 754 doubles with about 15-17 significant decimal digits. For higher precision, the docs recommend storing values as strings or moving them to an external data store entirely.

scenario: Crestline Insurance has a Camunda 8.8 process where the root scope already has a variable named riskLevel for each of the 3,000 claims processed daily. A worker completes a job inside a nested subprocess and returns riskLevel together with a brand-new variable claimNotes. The team needs to predict where each variable will end up after the merge.
question: Where do riskLevel and claimNotes reside after the job completes in the nested subprocess?

options.text: The engine updates the existing riskLevel only in the nested subprocess scope and creates claimNotes there as well, so neither of the two changes is visible once the subprocess completes.
The engine rejects the job completion because a worker is not allowed to write to a variable that already exists in a higher scope of the same process instance during a single merge.
The engine updates the existing riskLevel in the root scope where propagation stops, and creates claimNotes as a new variable in the root scope because no parent scope already has it.
The engine creates both riskLevel and claimNotes as local variables in the nested subprocess scope, leaving the existing root-scope riskLevel untouched until the subprocess scope ends.

c.text: Correct. Propagation walks each variable up until it finds a scope already containing the name; riskLevel stops at the root scope, and claimNotes finds no match so it is created in the root scope.

d.text: Incorrect. Variables returned by job completion are propagated to higher scopes by default; only an explicit local-variable mechanism would keep them confined to the nested subprocess scope.

a.text: Incorrect. riskLevel's update stops in the root scope where the name first exists, and claimNotes is created in the root scope rather than in the nested subprocess scope by default.

b.text: Incorrect. There is no rule that rejects job completion when a returned variable matches a higher-scope variable; the engine simply handles the propagation according to the documented merge rules.

explanation: Variable propagation on job completion walks each returned variable from the activity scope toward the root scope. It stops at the first scope that already contains a variable with the same name and updates it; if no scope contains it, the engine creates the variable in the root scope.

scenario: Northstar Insurance defines a Camunda 8.8 service task with three sequential input mappings for an intake step that handles 12,000 claims per day. The second mapping's source expression references the target variable that the first mapping created, and the third mapping has no source provided at all in the ioMapping configuration of the element.
question: Which behaviour does Camunda 8.8 produce for these three input mappings as configured?

options.text: The engine evaluates the mappings in alphabetical order by target name, lets each later source see all earlier targets, and raises an incident on the third mapping because the source was omitted.
The engine evaluates the mappings in reverse order to optimise reuse, lets the second source see the third target's value, and assigns an empty string default to the third target variable.
The engine evaluates the three mappings in parallel, so the second source sees the source state from before the first mapping ran, and the third mapping is skipped silently with no effect.
The engine evaluates the mappings in the defined order, lets the second source access the first target, and assigns null as the value of the third target because no source was supplied.

d.text: Correct. The docs state mappings are evaluated in their defined order, that later source expressions can access earlier target variables, and that an omitted source resolves to null for the target.

c.text: Incorrect. Mappings are evaluated sequentially in the defined order, not in parallel, and an omitted source maps the target to null rather than being skipped silently by the engine.

a.text: Incorrect. The evaluation order is defined by the mapping order in the model, not by alphabetical target name, and a missing source resolves to null without raising any incident on the element.

b.text: Incorrect. The engine evaluates mappings in their defined order rather than in reverse, and a missing source resolves to null, not to an empty string default for the target variable.

explanation: Variable mappings consist of paired source and target expressions evaluated in the order declared in ioMapping. A later source can read a target created by an earlier mapping, and any unresolved source - including an omitted one - resolves to null for the corresponding target variable.

scenario: Vertex Bank is configuring two input mappings on a single Camunda 8.8 service task processing 9,000 transactions per hour. The first mapping has source =customer and target sender.name, and the second mapping has source =iban and target sender.iban, with process variables customer: "John" and iban: "DE456" already defined.
question: Which local variable does the engine create on the service task in this case, per the input-mapping examples in the variables docs?

options.text: A single local variable sender with the value {name: 'John', iban: 'DE456'}, built from the two nested-property targets that share the same root variable name in the defined order.
A local variable sender = 'John' because the second mapping fails when its target reuses the same root variable, leaving only the first mapping's effect on the activity scope of the task.
Two separate local variables sender.name = 'John' and sender.iban = 'DE456' stored with literal dotted names rather than combined into one nested object variable on the task scope.
A local variable sender = 'DE456' because the last mapping always replaces earlier mappings that share the same root variable name in the same ioMapping block, regardless of nesting.

a.text: Correct. The docs' input-mapping examples show that nested-property targets build a single object variable, so combining sender.name and sender.iban produces one sender object with both fields populated.

c.text: Incorrect. Targets like sender.iban are not stored as flat dotted names; the engine creates or updates a nested property on the single root variable sender on the task scope.

b.text: Incorrect. The second mapping does not fail; the docs example shows the engine extending the existing sender object with the iban field rather than aborting the second mapping.

d.text: Incorrect. Successive mappings to nested properties of the same root variable extend that variable rather than overwriting the entire root with the last mapping's value during evaluation.

explanation: When an input mapping's target uses a nested property like sender.name, the engine creates or updates that property on the root variable. The example in the variables docs shows that two such mappings combine into a single object sender containing both nested fields after evaluation.

scenario: Lattice Bank reviews a Camunda 8.8 subprocess inside a payment process handling 250 transactions per second. A developer suggests defining a single output mapping with target name customer.email to surface a value from a local variable in the subprocess up to the parent scope of the process instance before close-out.
question: Which guidance from the Camunda 8.8 variables documentation applies to using a target like customer.email in an output mapping?

options.text: Avoid using output mappings or result variables that contain a period because doing so updates a property of an existing variable in the task scope and may produce confusing process behaviour.
Always use periods in target names because Camunda 8.8 treats them as the canonical way to merge subprocess-local variables into nested objects in the parent scope of a process.
Camunda 8.8 rewrites the period to an underscore at deployment time, so customer.email is stored and merged as customer_email without any side effects in the parent scope of the process.
Periods in target names require an additional ioMapping flag to be set in the BPMN element; otherwise the engine rejects the deployment with a validation error at startup of the cluster.

a.text: Correct. The docs explicitly advise avoiding output mappings or result variables whose names contain a period because doing so updates a property of an existing variable within the task scope and can lead to confusing behaviour.

b.text: Incorrect. The docs warn against periods in output-mapping targets rather than recommending them as a canonical way to build nested objects in the parent scope of a process instance.

c.text: Incorrect. The engine does not silently rewrite periods to underscores; it preserves the target string as written and applies it to nested properties of an existing variable on the task scope.

d.text: Incorrect. There is no documented ioMapping flag that enables or disables periods in target names, and the deployment is not rejected solely for using a period inside a target name.

explanation: A period in an output-mapping target updates a nested property of an existing variable in the task scope rather than creating a clean new variable. The docs caution against this pattern because the resulting variable layout in the process instance can be confusing or produce unexpected results.

scenario: Helix Insurance moves a workflow handling 12,000 claims per day to Camunda 8.8 and now needs a single expression language across its BPMN diagrams, DMN tables, and Camunda Forms. The architect researches whether one language covers all three modeller surfaces and where the underlying standard for that language originates.
question: Which language is used to define expressions across BPMN diagrams, DMN diagrams, and Forms in Camunda 8.8 and where is it specified?

options.text: FEEL is used only in DMN diagrams, while BPMN and Forms use a Camunda-proprietary JavaScript dialect that ships with the modeller separately from the OMG DMN specification.
Groovy is used to define expressions in BPMN, DMN, and Forms because Camunda 8.8 wraps every expression in a JVM-side scripting engine for portability across the modeller surfaces.
FEEL is used to define expressions in BPMN diagrams, DMN diagrams, and Forms, and is specified in the DMN specification of the Object Management Group standards body.
JUEL is used to define expressions in BPMN and Forms, while FEEL is used only in DMN, with both languages standardised by the Object Management Group jointly as part of DMN.

c.text: Correct. The docs state that Camunda uses FEEL to define expressions in BPMN diagrams, DMN diagrams, and Forms, and that FEEL is specified by the OMG in the DMN specification document.

a.text: Incorrect. The docs describe FEEL as the expression language across BPMN, DMN, and Forms; there is no documented Camunda-proprietary JavaScript dialect substituting it inside BPMN diagrams.

d.text: Incorrect. The Camunda 8.8 docs do not describe JUEL as the BPMN expression language for Camunda 8; FEEL is consistently used across the three modeller surfaces in the platform.

b.text: Incorrect. Camunda 8.8 does not use Groovy as the general-purpose expression language across these surfaces; FEEL is the documented choice across BPMN, DMN, and Camunda Forms.

explanation: FEEL is the single expression language Camunda uses across BPMN diagrams, DMN tables, and Forms. It is specified by the Object Management Group as part of the DMN specification, which gives implementations a common syntax and semantics to evaluate expressions consistently.
