explanation: DMN modeling in Camunda 8.8 Desktop or Web Modeler begins in the Decision Requirements Diagram view, where elements are added by dragging them from the palette on the left side of the canvas onto the diagram.

scenario: Catalyst Logistics maintains a Camunda 8.8 DRD with 8 decision tables that drive 2000 routing decisions per day. New team members want to move between those tables and the linked literal expressions without going back to the DRD canvas every single time. The platform serves 12 tenants and processes 8400 transactions per day.
question: Which feature lets a modeler jump between decision tables or literal expressions inside the same decision requirement diagram?

options.text: The right-click menu of the DRD canvas lists existing decision tables and switches the view to the chosen one
The properties panel on the right side lists decision tables and opens them with a single click from the panel
The palette on the left side of the canvas lists existing decision tables and opens them when one is dragged out
The Overview on the left side of the modeler is opened to switch between decision tables and literal expressions

d.text: Correct. The docs state that the modeler can jump between decision tables or literal expressions in the DRD by opening and using the Overview on the left side.

b.text: Incorrect. The properties panel on the right side is used to edit names and IDs of the selected element; the docs do not describe it as a navigator between tables.

c.text: Incorrect. The palette on the left is described as the source of DMN elements for the DRD canvas, not as a list of existing decision tables to open.

a.text: Incorrect. The docs describe right-clicking inside a cell for delete, copy, or insert operations on rules, not as a navigator across decision tables.

explanation: The Overview on the left side of the modeler is the documented navigator that lets a modeler jump between decision tables and literal expressions inside the same DRD without returning to the canvas each time.

scenario: Forge SaaS maintains a Camunda 8.8 DRD that runs 7500 pricing decisions per day. A modeler has just dropped a fresh BKM element onto the canvas and named it, and now wants to turn it into an executable BKM that uses a FEEL literal expression for the actual logic. The platform serves 12 tenants and processes 8400 transactions per day.
question: Which step makes a freshly added BKM element executable using a literal expression in Camunda 8.8?

options.text: Drag a literal expression element from the palette and drop it on top of the BKM element on the modeler canvas
Click the blue literal expression icon on the BKM element before naming it and before adding any parameters
Select Change type and pick Literal expression from the context menu of the BKM element on the modeler canvas
Open the properties panel and toggle the executable flag for the BKM element before assigning a result type

c.text: Correct. The docs list this as a step in the creation flow: after naming the BKM, select Change type from its context menu and pick Literal expression to make it executable as a FEEL literal expression.

b.text: Incorrect. The blue literal expression icon is what opens the FEEL editor after the BKM has already been switched to a literal expression via Change type, not the step that enables execution.

a.text: Incorrect. The docs describe morphing the BKM element itself via Change type; they do not describe dragging a separate literal expression element on top of an existing BKM.

d.text: Incorrect. The docs do not describe an executable toggle in the properties panel; the documented mechanism is Change type and the literal expression icon on the BKM.

explanation: To make a BKM executable, the modeler opens the BKM element's context menu, selects Change type, and picks Literal expression. The blue literal expression icon then opens the FEEL editor where parameters and the expression body are defined.

scenario: Aurora Healthcare runs a Camunda 8.8 DMN model that schedules clinic visits for 1500 users per day across four regional centers. One decision-table input column must capture a clock time-of-day value such as 14:30 separately from any calendar date so the FEEL rules can compare opening slots against the booked time.
question: Which DMN data type listed in the Camunda 8.8 supported types table represents a clock time of day without a date component?

options.text: The dayTimeDuration type maps to FEEL Days-Time-Duration and represents an elapsed clock interval rather than a time of day
The dateTime type maps to FEEL Date-Time and is the only listed option for representing instants and clock times together
The yearMonthDuration type maps to FEEL Years-Months-Duration and represents the calendar-aware time fraction within a day
The time type maps to the FEEL Time data type and represents a clock time of day without any associated calendar date

b.text: Incorrect. The supported types table lists dateTime mapped to FEEL Date-Time as a combined instant, while time is also listed separately as the clock-only type without a calendar date component.

a.text: Incorrect. The dayTimeDuration entry maps to FEEL Days-Time-Duration and represents an elapsed duration on the days and time scale rather than a clock time of day value.

c.text: Incorrect. The yearMonthDuration entry maps to FEEL Years-Months-Duration and covers calendar month and year deltas, not a clock time of day value used inside a single day.

d.text: Correct. The Camunda 8.8 DMN data types table lists time as mapping to FEEL Time, which is the entry intended for a clock time of day value without any associated calendar date component.

explanation: Camunda 8.8 lists time as a DMN data type mapping to FEEL Time, which is the standalone clock time of day without an associated calendar date, distinct from dateTime and from the two FEEL duration types.

scenario: A fintech team prototypes a Camunda 8.8 DMN decision that handles 300 requests per minute during a two week pilot integration. The modeler wants one decision-table input column to accept values without committing to a specific FEEL type because the upstream payload shapes are still changing across releases.
question: Which DMN data type from the Camunda 8.8 supported types table serves as a wildcard accepting values of any FEEL type?

options.text: The dateTime type accepts arbitrary values because FEEL Date-Time is the broadest of the temporal entries in the supported types table
The Any type is listed as a wildcard for any type and lets a decision-table input column accept values of any FEEL data type
The string type accepts arbitrary values because the FEEL String mapping coerces other types into text automatically during evaluation
The number type accepts arbitrary values because the FEEL Number mapping covers integers, decimals, and boolean coercions together

c.text: Incorrect. The string type maps to FEEL String, the textual type, and the data types table does not describe automatic coercion of arbitrary values into string when used as a decision-table input type.

b.text: Correct. The Camunda 8.8 DMN data types table lists Any with the description wildcard for any type, which is the entry that lets a decision-table input column accept values of any FEEL data type.

a.text: Incorrect. The dateTime type maps to FEEL Date-Time, a temporal type for instants, and the data types table does not list it as a wildcard accepting values of arbitrary FEEL types.

d.text: Incorrect. The number type maps to FEEL Number for numeric values, and the data types table does not list it as a wildcard accepting values of other FEEL data types.

explanation: The Camunda 8.8 DMN data types table includes Any with the explicit note that it is a wildcard for any type, intended for cases where an input or variable should accept values regardless of their FEEL data type.

scenario: Lattice Insurance models a Camunda 8.8 DMN decision table for SLA checks across 2500 cases per day. The modeler needs an input column that captures the time elapsed since case intake, expressed in days, hours, minutes, and seconds rather than in calendar years and months. The platform serves 12 tenants and processes 8400 transactions per day.
question: Which DMN type name is associated with the FEEL Days-Time-Duration type for that input column in Camunda 8.8?

options.text: dateTime is the DMN type name associated with the FEEL Days-Time-Duration type for elapsed time spans
dayTimeDuration is the DMN type name associated with the FEEL Days-Time-Duration type for time spans
time is the DMN type name associated with the FEEL Days-Time-Duration type for elapsed time spans
yearMonthDuration is the DMN type name associated with the FEEL Days-Time-Duration type for time spans

b.text: Correct. The DMN data types table in the docs pairs the DMN type name dayTimeDuration with the FEEL Days-Time-Duration type, which is the right fit for days, hours, minutes, and seconds elapsed since case intake.

d.text: Incorrect. The docs pair yearMonthDuration with the FEEL Years-Months-Duration type, not the Days-Time-Duration type used for day, hour, minute, and second spans.

a.text: Incorrect. The docs pair dateTime with the FEEL Date-Time type, which represents a point in time, not a duration of elapsed time.

c.text: Incorrect. The docs pair time with the FEEL Time type, which represents a time of day, not a duration measured in days, hours, minutes, and seconds.

explanation: The DMN data types table maps the DMN type name dayTimeDuration to the FEEL Days-Time-Duration type. For an input that records elapsed time in days, hours, minutes, and seconds, dayTimeDuration is the documented choice.

scenario: Catalyst Bank built a Camunda 8.8 DMN decision that computes outstanding warranty time in whole years and months for 6500 contracts per quarter. The modeler must pick a DMN type for the warranty-remaining output column, and another modeler keeps suggesting dayTimeDuration because both are durations. The platform serves 12 tenants and processes 8400 transactions per day.
question: Which DMN type name matches the FEEL Years-Months-Duration type used for the warranty-remaining output column?

options.text: dayTimeDuration is the DMN type name associated with the FEEL Years-Months-Duration type for this output
dateTime is the DMN type name associated with the FEEL Years-Months-Duration type for the warranty output
yearMonthDuration is the DMN type name associated with the FEEL Years-Months-Duration type for this output
Any is the DMN type name associated with the FEEL Years-Months-Duration type for the warranty output column

c.text: Correct. The DMN data types table pairs yearMonthDuration with the FEEL Years-Months-Duration type, which models durations expressed in years and months, matching the warranty-remaining output.

a.text: Incorrect. The docs pair dayTimeDuration with the FEEL Days-Time-Duration type, which models durations in days, hours, minutes, and seconds, not years and months.

b.text: Incorrect. The docs pair dateTime with the FEEL Date-Time type, which is a point in time rather than a duration in years and months.

d.text: Incorrect. The docs describe Any as a wildcard for any type; it is not the documented pairing for the FEEL Years-Months-Duration type.

explanation: The DMN data types table maps the DMN type name yearMonthDuration to the FEEL Years-Months-Duration type. Durations measured in years and months use yearMonthDuration, while day, hour, minute, and second durations use dayTimeDuration.

scenario: Apex Marketplace runs a Camunda 8.8 DMN decision that may return either a numeric discount amount or a string promotion code, depending on which rules match for 8000 checkouts per day. The modeler does not know the FEEL type of the output ahead of evaluation but still wants a valid DMN type definition on the output column. The platform serves 12 tenants and processes 8400 transactions per day.
question: Which DMN type name should the modeler set on the output column to accept any FEEL type at runtime?

options.text: string is the DMN type name that acts as a wildcard accepting any FEEL type for the output column at runtime
Any is the DMN type name that acts as a wildcard accepting any FEEL type for the output column at runtime
wildcard is the DMN type name that acts as a wildcard accepting any FEEL type for the output column at runtime
Object is the DMN type name that acts as a wildcard accepting any FEEL type for the output column at runtime

b.text: Correct. The DMN data types table lists Any as a wildcard for any type, so setting the output column to Any lets the column hold either the numeric discount or the string promotion code returned by the rules.

a.text: Incorrect. The docs pair string with the FEEL String type only, so a string output column would not accept the numeric discount values produced by some of the matched rules.

d.text: Incorrect. The DMN data types listed in the docs do not include a type named Object; Object is not one of the documented DMN type names for Camunda 8.8.

c.text: Incorrect. The documented type names do not include wildcard as a value; the wildcard behavior is provided by the Any type listed in the data types table.

explanation: Camunda 8.8 lists Any as the DMN type that acts as a wildcard for any FEEL type. When an output can be either a numeric discount or a string promotion code, Any is the documented choice; string, Object, and wildcard do not fit the table.

scenario: HelixHealth, a healthcare network, processes 1200 claims per day and wants to externalize its eligibility rules out of the claims process so that business analysts and developers can collaborate on the same artefact. The architects evaluate DMN in Camunda 8.8 as the modelling notation for the rule set.
question: Which characterisation of DMN does the Camunda 8.8 overview page support for this analyst-and-developer collaboration?

options.text: DMN is a modelling approach owned by the OMG that is intended to be modelled and executed in a language both business analysts and developers can understand together
DMN is a Camunda-proprietary modelling notation that is executable only on the Camunda decision engine and has no governing standards body outside the Camunda organisation
DMN is a developer-only XML schema standardised by the OMG that is intended to be hand-written by IT staff rather than modelled in a shared diagram with business roles
DMN is an OMG-standardised notation but its executable form is reserved for IT teams while business analysts work in a separate text-only requirements document for the rules

c.text: Incorrect. The DMN overview presents DMN as a shared modelling approach rather than a developer-only XML schema, with analysts and developers working in the same diagram and the same rule tables.

a.text: Correct. The overview states that DMN is owned by the OMG and that decisions are modelled and executed using a language both business analysts and developers can understand, which is exactly the use case.

b.text: Incorrect. The overview attributes DMN ownership to the Object Management Group, not to Camunda; Camunda is named only as an example decision engine that executes the resulting tables.

d.text: Incorrect. The overview keeps modelling and execution in the same DMN artefact rather than splitting analysts and developers across separate documents, so this division contradicts the page.

explanation: The DMN overview frames DMN as an OMG-owned modelling approach that yields executable decisions both business analysts and developers can understand, with Camunda acting as one of the decision engines that runs the resulting decision tables.

scenario: Northwind Retailer ships a Camunda 8.8 DMN model that classifies claim severity for 3200 orders per day. The architect renames the decision element id to align with team conventions for the new release, and must pick a value the deployment will accept and that also lets a dependent decision keep referencing the result through the DRG.
question: Which decision id value is permitted by the DMN id naming rule for this decision element?

options.text: The decision id value claimHandler is set on the decision element so it can be referenced by other DMN files later
The decision id value claim handler is set on the decision element so it can be referenced by other DMN files later
The decision id value claim.handler is set on the decision element so it can be referenced by other DMN files later
The decision id value claim-handler is set on the decision element so it can be referenced by other DMN files later

d.text: Incorrect. The kebab-case form contains the dash operator, which the DMN id rule forbids; if used, the decision result cannot be accessed in a dependent decision.

a.text: Correct. The camelCase form is alphanumeric only and is explicitly recommended by the docs for word combinations alongside snake_case, so the deployment accepts it.

b.text: Incorrect. Whitespace is one of the special characters the docs forbid in the decision id; using it would prevent the result from being accessed by a dependent decision.

c.text: Incorrect. The dot is a special symbol that the docs forbid in the decision id; the result of the decision would then be unreachable from a dependent decision.

explanation: The DMN docs allow any alphanumeric string plus the underscore in the decision id; camelCase and snake_case are recommended for word combinations, while kebab-case, whitespace, and other symbols are not allowed because they break references from dependent decisions.

scenario: Lumen Health, a clinic chain running 6800 triage evaluations per day in Camunda 8.8, deploys multiple DMN files. The platform team needs each Decision Requirements Graph to have a unique technical identifier set exactly where DMN defines it. The platform serves 12 tenants and processes 8400 transactions per day.
question: Where is the unique technical identifier of a DMN Decision Requirements Graph set in the XML?

options.text: In the id attribute on the definitions element, which serves as the unique technical identifier of the DRG at deployment
In the id attribute on a drg element nested inside definitions, which serves as the unique technical identifier at deployment
In the namespace attribute on the definitions element, which serves as the unique technical identifier of the DRG at deployment
In the id attribute on the first decision element, which serves as the unique technical identifier of the DRG at deployment

a.text: Correct. The DRG ID is the technical identifier of the DRG and is set in the id attribute on the definitions element; each DRG should have a unique ID at deployment.

d.text: Incorrect. The id on a decision element identifies that decision, not the enclosing DRG; the DRG ID lives on the definitions element.

b.text: Incorrect. DMN has no nested drg element; the DRG identifier is the id attribute on the root definitions element.

c.text: Incorrect. The namespace attribute declares an XML namespace, not the DRG's technical identifier; that identifier is the id attribute on definitions.

explanation: The DRG ID is the technical identifier of the DRG, set as the id attribute on the definitions element, and must be unique at deployment.

scenario: Nimbus Retailer runs a Camunda 8.8 storefront that serves 12000 customers per hour and picks one advertisement per visitor from a DMN table keyed by age band. The table's hit policy is FIRST, and for a 19 year old visitor several rules in the table are simultaneously satisfied. The operations lead needs to confirm which single output the engine returns into the storefront process for that visitor.
question: When several rules match a single input under the FIRST hit policy, which output does the deployed decision table return?

options.text: The output of the first satisfied rule in document order is returned in the decision result and later matches are ignored
The output of any one satisfied rule is returned in the decision result, provided every match yields the same output value
The output of every satisfied rule is returned in the decision result as a list ordered by the matching rule positions
The output of the last satisfied rule in document order is returned in the decision result and earlier matches are ignored

c.text: Incorrect. Returning a list of all satisfied outputs ordered by rule position is how the docs describe RULE ORDER, not FIRST, which collapses the result to a single output.

b.text: Incorrect. The equal-output guarantee is the documented rule for ANY. FIRST does not require satisfied rules to share an output entry to return cleanly.

a.text: Correct. The First section states that multiple rules can be satisfied but the result contains only the output of the first satisfied rule, illustrated by the 19 year old example where the rule for Cars is used.

d.text: Incorrect. FIRST takes the first satisfied rule in document order, not the last; for the 19 year old visitor that is the Cars rule rather than any later match.

explanation: FIRST allows several rules to be satisfied but reduces the result to a single output, that of the first satisfied rule in document order, which the advertisement example demonstrates by returning the Cars output for a 19 year old visitor.

scenario: Nimbus retailer models a Camunda 8.8 DMN table that totals discount percentages across 18000 orders per day. The modeler picks COLLECT and configures aggregation="SUM" on the decisionTable element to combine matched rules. The reviewer asks how many output columns the decision table may declare under that configuration.
question: How many output columns is the decision table allowed to declare when COLLECT is used with an aggregator on the decisionTable element?

options.text: Two output columns are allowed, with the aggregator applied to the first column and the second column reserved for an aggregated explanation entry during evaluation
The decision table can have only one output, and the aggregator produces a single output entry from the outputs of the satisfied rules during evaluation
An unlimited number of output columns is allowed, and the aggregator is applied independently to every output column during the evaluation of the decision table
Up to five output columns are allowed, and the aggregator is applied independently to each output column during the evaluation of the decision table

d.text: Incorrect. The docs do not allow five output columns under an aggregated COLLECT policy; the info callout explicitly restricts the decision table to a single output when an aggregator is used.

b.text: Correct. The COLLECT info callout states that if the COLLECT hit policy is used with an aggregator, the decision table can only have one output, which the aggregator combines into a single output entry.

c.text: Incorrect. The docs explicitly cap the decision table at one output when COLLECT is paired with an aggregator; an unlimited count contradicts the info callout in the COLLECT section.

a.text: Incorrect. The docs do not document a two-output layout for aggregated COLLECT; the limit is one output, and there is no documented explanation entry produced by the aggregator.

explanation: The COLLECT section includes an info callout stating that if the COLLECT hit policy is used with an aggregator, the decision table can only have one output. The aggregator combines the outputs of all satisfied rules into that single output entry.

scenario: Sable retailer needs to total discount percentages across satisfied rules in a Camunda 8.8 DMN decision table that runs 12000 orders per day. The modeler chooses COLLECT and sets aggregation="SUM" on the decisionTable element so the table emits one combined discount value per order rather than a list.
question: What does the COLLECT hit policy with aggregation="SUM" produce in the decision result for an order whose satisfied rules emit 5%, 10%, and 7%?

options.text: A single output entry containing the maximum value 10% taken across the satisfied rules during the decision evaluation against the order data
A single output entry containing the sum 22% generated by the SUM aggregator across the satisfied rules during the decision evaluation against the order data
A single output entry containing the count 3 of satisfied rules across the satisfied rules during the decision evaluation against the order data
A single output entry containing the list 5%, 10%, and 7% across the satisfied rules during the decision evaluation against the order data

d.text: Incorrect. With an aggregator configured, the COLLECT result is a single output entry produced by the aggregator, not the raw list of values from the satisfied rules in the decision table.

a.text: Incorrect. SUM combines the outputs of the satisfied rules additively rather than picking the maximum value; a maximum aggregator would be a different aggregation choice.

b.text: Correct. The COLLECT section states that with an aggregator the decision result contains a single output entry, and the aggregation=SUM XML example shows that the aggregator sums the satisfied rule outputs into one value.

c.text: Incorrect. COUNT is a different aggregator than SUM; with aggregation=SUM the result is the sum of the satisfied rule output values, not a count of how many rules were satisfied during evaluation.

explanation: The COLLECT section's XML example uses aggregation=SUM on the decisionTable element. When that aggregator is configured, the decision result contains a single output entry generated from all satisfied rule outputs, which for the values 5, 10, and 7 is 22.

scenario: Stellar Retailer authors a Camunda 8.8 DMN table that returns a desiredDish string across 5 menu options for 6800 orders per day. A modeler wants to declare that every output entry converts to a string and asks where exactly the typeRef attribute belongs in the output clause XML and which element carries that attribute in Camunda 8.8.
question: Where does the Camunda 8.8 DMN docs place the typeRef attribute that declares the data type of an output clause?

options.text: Place typeRef on the decisionTable element so that every output clause inherits the same shared data type at evaluation time in Camunda 8.8 DMN
Place typeRef on the output element to declare the data type of the output clause, as documented for Camunda 8.8 DMN decision tables in the modeler
Place typeRef on the inputExpression element so that the input and output clauses share a single type declaration in the Camunda 8.8 DMN file
Place typeRef on a nested outputValues element below the output clause so the engine validates the converted result before assignment in Camunda 8.8

a.text: Incorrect. The decisionTable element does not declare a shared output type; each output clause sets its own typeRef on the output element itself in Camunda 8.8.

d.text: Incorrect. There is no outputValues element used for type declaration in this section; typeRef is an attribute placed directly on the output element.

c.text: Incorrect. The inputExpression element carries the typeRef for an input clause, not for an output clause, which uses its own output element in DMN.

b.text: Correct. The docs specify typeRef as an attribute on the output XML element to declare the data type of the output clause in Camunda 8.8 DMN tables.

explanation: Camunda 8.8 DMN declares the output clause data type by setting the typeRef attribute on the output XML element, and the runtime checks that each evaluated output entry converts to that type before assigning it to the result.

scenario: A retailer pricing team builds a Camunda 8.8 DMN decision table to set discount tiers for 1500 orders per day. A new modeler is reading the deployed XML for the first time and asks which element wraps each individual rule so they can locate every rule in the source file.
question: Which XML element wraps a single rule inside a DMN decision table definition?

options.text: A row element nested inside the decisionTable element, with inputColumn and outputColumn children carrying the rule expressions
A rule element nested inside the decisionTable element, with inputEntry and outputEntry children carrying the rule expressions
A decisionEntry element nested inside the decisionTable element, with inputEntry and outputEntry children carrying the rule expressions
A rule element nested inside the decision element, sitting as a sibling of the decisionTable element rather than as its child

b.text: Correct. The DMN docs describe a rule as a rule XML element placed inside the decisionTable element, and the example shows inputEntry and outputEntry children of that rule element.

c.text: Incorrect. The DMN docs do not define a decisionEntry XML element; rules use a rule element whose children are inputEntry and outputEntry expressions.

a.text: Incorrect. The DMN docs do not use row, inputColumn, or outputColumn element names; rules are rule elements containing inputEntry and outputEntry children.

d.text: Incorrect. The rule element nests inside the decisionTable, which itself nests inside the decision element; rule and decisionTable are not siblings in the XML.

explanation: Each rule in a DMN decision table is represented by a rule XML element placed inside the decisionTable element. Its children are inputEntry elements (the conditions) and outputEntry elements (the conclusions), with the FEEL expression held in a text element inside each entry.

scenario: Crater Bank models a Camunda 8.8 DMN decision that derives the current season from an input date for 4500 transactions per day. Rather than maintaining a decision table, the modeler wants a single FEEL expression such as calendar.getSeason(date) and needs the result to land in a typed output variable named season of type string when the decision is invoked from a BPMN business rule task.
question: Which DMN XML structure represents a decision literal expression for this season decision?

options.text: A decision element containing a variable child with name and typeRef attributes and a literalExpression child whose text node carries the FEEL expression body
A decision element containing a variable child and a contextEntry child whose value carries the FEEL expression body that returns the resulting season string
A decision element containing a decisionTable child with a single rule whose output expression carries the FEEL body that returns the season string output
A decision element containing only a literalExpression child with text and an output attribute that declares the resulting variable name and the variable type

a.text: Correct. The DMN schema example shows a decision literal expression as a decision element with a variable child (carrying name and typeRef attributes) and a literalExpression child whose text element holds the FEEL body, such as calendar.getSeason(date).

c.text: Incorrect. A decisionTable is the table-based decision form, not a literal expression; the literal expression replaces the table with a single FEEL expression carried by the literalExpression element.

d.text: Incorrect. The result variable cannot be declared as an output attribute on literalExpression; the schema requires a separate variable child element of the decision element.

b.text: Incorrect. The DMN literal expression element is literalExpression with a text node, not a contextEntry element; contextEntry belongs to the boxed context expression family.

explanation: A decision literal expression is a decision element with two children: a variable element that declares the result name and typeRef, and a literalExpression element whose text node holds the FEEL expression body that produces the result.

scenario: Helios Bank ships a Camunda 8.8 DMN decision that derives a tier label from an applicant profile for 4500 claims per day. The architect adds a literal expression variable to a downstream decision and wants to follow the DMN naming guidance so dependent decisions can keep referencing the result without rewriting their FEEL expressions later on.
question: Which guidance does the DMN docs give for choosing the variable name of a decision literal expression?

options.text: Use a numeric prefix on the variable name so result lookups remain stable when the decision id is later renamed by another team
Use a kebab-case form of the decision name so dependent decisions can reference it via dotted syntax across the DRG model
Use the FEEL function name from the literal expression as the variable name so the engine can match outputs to expressions
Use the decision id as the variable name so dependent decisions can reference the result by that shared identifier later

c.text: Incorrect. The docs do not tie the variable name to a FEEL function name; the recommended practice is to reuse the decision id as the variable name of the literal expression.

b.text: Incorrect. The kebab-case format is explicitly disallowed for the variable name because the dash is the FEEL subtraction operator; whitespace and other symbols are also forbidden.

a.text: Incorrect. The docs say nothing about numeric prefixes; the recommendation is to use the decision id as the variable name so dependent decisions can reference the result.

d.text: Correct. The docs recommend using the decision id as the variable name, since dependent decisions reference the literal expression result by the variable name rather than by the decision id.

explanation: The DMN docs recommend reusing the decision id as the variable name of a literal expression because dependent decisions reference the result by the variable name, not by the decision id; this keeps downstream FEEL expressions stable.

scenario: Aurora Logistics runs a Camunda 8.8 DMN decision that returns a routing zone string for 5500 shipments per day. The modeler reviews the variable element of the decision literal expression and must decide whether to add a typeRef attribute, given the documented behaviour around type checking of literal expression results at evaluation time.
question: What does the DMN docs say about the typeRef attribute on the variable of a decision literal expression?

options.text: The typeRef attribute is optional but recommended because it adds type safety by checking the result against a supported DMN data type
The typeRef attribute is required only when the decision is invoked from a business rule task and is ignored during standalone DRG tests
The typeRef attribute is required on every literal expression variable so the deployment fails when it is omitted from the decision file
The typeRef attribute is forbidden on literal expression variables and must instead be declared inside the literalExpression text body

a.text: Correct. The docs describe typeRef as not required but recommended; when present, the engine checks that the evaluated FEEL result converts to the declared DMN data type for the variable.

c.text: Incorrect. The docs explicitly state that the type is not required; the deployment succeeds without typeRef, only the runtime type safety check is then skipped.

b.text: Incorrect. The optional but recommended status of typeRef does not depend on the invocation surface; the same rule applies whether or not a business rule task is used.

d.text: Incorrect. The typeRef attribute belongs on the variable element of the decision and cannot be expressed inside the literalExpression text body in the DMN schema.

explanation: The typeRef attribute on the variable of a decision literal expression is optional but recommended because it triggers a runtime check that the FEEL result converts to the declared DMN data type, giving type safety on the expression result.

scenario: Helix Insurance, a regional insurer handling 6500 claims per day, expresses a premium-discount decision as a single FEEL formula rather than a table. The model team needs to know which two parts make up a decision literal expression in DMN 1.3.
question: Which two components make up a DMN decision literal expression?

options.text: A decision table that defines the rule set and a variable label that specifies the human-readable name of the decision result column
A literal expression that defines how the value is generated and a variable that specifies the name and type of the decision result
An input clause that defines the FEEL input and an output clause that specifies the name and type of the decision result via labels
A required decision that supplies the input value and a knowledge source that specifies the authority for the resulting decision value

b.text: Correct. A decision literal expression consists of a literal expression (which defines how the value is generated) plus a variable (which specifies the name and type of the decision result).

c.text: Incorrect. Input and output clauses belong to a decision table, not a decision literal expression.

d.text: Incorrect. Required decisions and knowledge sources are DRG-level concepts, not the parts that compose a literal expression.

a.text: Incorrect. A decision table is an alternative form of decision logic, not a component of a decision literal expression.

explanation: Per the DMN page, a decision literal expression is composed of a literal expression and a variable; together they form the literalExpression element inside a decision.

scenario: Nimbus Bank, a retail bank running 9000 loan checks per day in Camunda 8.8, ships a DMN literal expression decision whose id attribute is set to risk-score-v2. Deployment is accepted but the dependent fraud decision can no longer read the upstream result by id.
question: Why does the kebab-case decision ID risk-score-v2 break access from a dependent decision in DMN?

options.text: The hyphen is parsed as a path separator in FEEL expressions, so the dependent decision can only reference the substring after the last hyphen
The hyphen is interpreted as a wildcard match in FEEL expressions, so the dependent decision resolves the reference to multiple results
The hyphen is reserved as the FEEL string-concatenation operator in expressions, so the dependent decision cannot reference the result by that ID
The hyphen is parsed as the FEEL subtraction operator in expressions, so the dependent decision cannot reference the result by that ID

d.text: Correct. Decision IDs may contain only alphanumerics and underscore; kebab-case is disallowed because the hyphen is the FEEL subtraction operator, so the ID cannot be referenced from a dependent decision.

c.text: Incorrect. String concatenation in FEEL uses a different operator; the hyphen is the subtraction operator and that is why kebab-case decision IDs are disallowed.

a.text: Incorrect. FEEL does not treat the hyphen as a path separator; the prohibition exists because the hyphen is the subtraction operator and would conflict with identifier parsing.

b.text: Incorrect. FEEL has no wildcard semantics on hyphen; the hyphen is the subtraction operator, which is why kebab-case decision IDs are not allowed.

explanation: Decision IDs must be alphanumeric or underscore; kebab-case is rejected because the hyphen is the FEEL minus operator, which breaks references from dependent decisions.

scenario: Maple Health, an insurer adjudicating 7200 claims per day, accidentally sets a DMN decision id attribute to claim status (with a space). Deployment passes, but a downstream eligibility decision that lists this decision as required cannot access its result at runtime in Camunda 8.8.
question: Why can a dependent decision no longer access the result when the source decision ID contains a whitespace character?

options.text: Decision IDs with whitespace are silently stripped at deployment, so the dependent decision resolves to a different ID than the one written in the source
Decision IDs must be alphanumeric or underscore, so an ID with whitespace cannot be referenced from a dependent decision and the result becomes inaccessible
Decision IDs with whitespace are valid only inside decision tables, so the dependent decision must use a decision table to read the upstream literal result
Decision IDs with whitespace are valid only when quoted in FEEL, so the dependent decision must wrap the reference in single quotes to read the result

b.text: Correct. The decision ID may contain only alphanumeric characters and the underscore; if it contains any special character or symbol such as whitespace, the result cannot be accessed in a dependent decision.

a.text: Incorrect. The engine does not silently strip whitespace from decision IDs; an invalid ID simply prevents dependent-decision access to the result.

d.text: Incorrect. There is no FEEL escaping path that rehabilitates a decision ID containing special characters; such IDs cannot be referenced from dependent decisions at all.

c.text: Incorrect. The form of the dependent decision is irrelevant; an invalid decision ID blocks access from any dependent decision, table or literal expression.

explanation: Decision IDs must be alphanumeric or underscore. Any special character or symbol (including whitespace) makes the result inaccessible from a dependent decision.

scenario: Orbit SaaS, a billing platform processing 2800 invoices per hour in Camunda 8.8, models a tax-amount decision as a literal expression. The DMN reviewer asks what role the variable element plays alongside the literal expression in the decision.
question: What does the variable of a DMN decision literal expression specify?

options.text: The name and the type of the decision result, declared on a variable element inside the decision element
The name and the visibility scope of the decision result, declared on a variable element inside the definitions element
The name and the default value of the decision result, declared on a variable element inside the literalExpression element
The name and the FEEL expression of the decision result, declared on a variable element inside the informationRequirement element

a.text: Correct. The variable element of a decision literal expression specifies the name and the type of the decision result and is placed inside the decision XML element.

c.text: Incorrect. The variable carries the name and type, not a default value, and it lives inside the decision element rather than inside literalExpression.

b.text: Incorrect. The variable does not declare a visibility scope on definitions; it specifies the name and type of the decision result inside the decision element.

d.text: Incorrect. The FEEL expression body sits in the literalExpression's text element, not on the variable; the variable only names and types the result.

explanation: The variable element gives the decision result its name and type and is placed inside the decision XML element.

scenario: Lattice Insurance evaluates 4000 claims per day through a Camunda 8.8 DMN model. The team is opening an existing decision requirements graph for the first time in the modeler and wants the human-readable label 'Claim Triage Decisions' to appear at the top of the DRD view and on every Operate decision page.
question: Which DMN XML element and attribute hold the human-readable name of the decision requirements graph?

options.text: The label attribute on the definitions element holds the human-readable label of the entire decision requirements graph shown in the DRD view
The id attribute on the definitions element holds the human-readable label of the entire decision requirements graph shown in the DRD view
The name attribute on the definitions element holds the human-readable label of the entire decision requirements graph shown in the DRD view
The name attribute on the root decision element holds the human-readable label of the entire decision requirements graph shown in the DRD view

c.text: Correct. The docs state that the DRG name describes the DRG and is set as the name attribute on the definitions element, which is exactly the title rendered in the DRD view.

b.text: Incorrect. The id attribute on the definitions element holds the technical identifier of the DRG, not its human-readable label.

d.text: Incorrect. The name attribute on a decision element labels that single decision node in the DRD, not the surrounding DRG itself.

a.text: Incorrect. DMN definitions has no label attribute; the descriptive label of a DRG lives in the standard name attribute on definitions.

explanation: A decision requirements graph carries its descriptive label in the name attribute of the top-level definitions element. The id attribute is the technical identifier, and a per-decision name labels a single node rather than the graph itself.

scenario: A logistics platform has 30 deployments queued for a Camunda 8.8 staging cluster, each containing a separate decision requirements graph for a tenant pricing model. The release engineer is reviewing the DMN files before promotion and wants to confirm where the technical identifier of each DRG lives and what uniqueness rule applies.
question: Which statement correctly describes the technical identifier of a DRG and its uniqueness requirement on deployment?

options.text: The technical identifier sits in the id attribute on the root decision element and must be unique across every DRG deployed to the cluster
The technical identifier sits in the namespace attribute of the definitions element and must be unique among the DRGs deployed to Camunda
The technical identifier sits in the id attribute on the definitions element and each DRG should have a unique value when deployed to Camunda
The technical identifier sits in the id attribute on the decisionTable element and must be unique among every table deployed to the cluster

a.text: Incorrect. The id on a decision element identifies that single decision node inside the DRG, not the DRG itself, so the uniqueness rule does not apply at this level.

c.text: Correct. The docs describe the DRG id as the technical identifier set in the id attribute on the definitions element, and they state that each DRG should have a unique id when deployed to Camunda.

d.text: Incorrect. The id on a decisionTable identifies the table element inside a decision; it is not the DRG identifier and is not the value the deployment uniqueness rule applies to.

b.text: Incorrect. The namespace attribute is an XML namespace URI used for the DMN model, not the technical id, and uniqueness is not enforced on the namespace value at deploy time.

explanation: The DRG id is the technical identifier carried by the id attribute of the top-level definitions element. The documentation explicitly requires this id to be unique per DRG when deployed to Camunda, while ids on decision or decisionTable elements identify lower-level nodes inside the model.

scenario: Atlas Bank scores 5000 loans per week through a Camunda 8.8 DRG in which a 'Beverages' style downstream decision depends on an upstream 'Dish' decision. The team is editing the DMN XML by hand to wire the dependency before redeploying and needs the exact element placement and href format for a required decision.
question: How is a required-decision dependency expressed in the DMN XML of a DRG between a downstream decision and its upstream decision?

options.text: A requiredDecision element is placed under an informationRequirement element with an href starting with hash and the upstream decision id
A requiredInput element is placed under an informationRequirement element with an href starting with hash and the upstream decision id
A requiredDecision element is placed directly under the downstream decision with a name attribute holding the upstream decision id
A requiredDecision element is placed under an authorityRequirement element with an href attribute holding the upstream decision id

c.text: Incorrect. A requiredDecision is not a direct child of the decision element; it lives inside an informationRequirement element, and the reference is the href attribute, not a name.

d.text: Incorrect. An authorityRequirement element wires up knowledge sources as authorities for a decision; data-style dependencies on other decisions use informationRequirement instead.

b.text: Incorrect. A requiredInput element references an inputData node, not another decision; pointing to an upstream decision must use requiredDecision rather than requiredInput.

a.text: Correct. The docs show requiredDecision inside an informationRequirement element with an href that starts with hash followed by the upstream decision id, which matches the documented wiring.

explanation: Decision-to-decision dependencies are modelled with requiredDecision inside informationRequirement, where the href attribute points to the upstream decision id prefixed by a hash. authorityRequirement is reserved for knowledge sources and requiredInput is used for inputData references.

scenario: Polaris Insurance underwrites 12000 policies per month with a Camunda 8.8 DRG that references an internal regulatory handbook as a knowledgeSource attached to a 'Dish' style top-level decision via an authorityRequirement. A reviewer wants to understand how the knowledgeSource participates when the engine evaluates the DRG at runtime.
question: Which statement correctly characterizes a knowledgeSource element in a Camunda 8.8 DRG at evaluation time?

options.text: A knowledgeSource element supplies decision-table inputs to its depending decision and the engine reads it as runtime context during evaluation
A knowledgeSource element triggers an outbound call to an external policy service whenever a depending decision evaluates in the engine
A knowledgeSource element is mandatory for every decision whose decision logic is defined as a decision literal expression in the model
A knowledgeSource element denotes an authority for a decision and is ignored by the engine when the DRG is evaluated at runtime

a.text: Incorrect. A knowledgeSource is documentation, not a data source; the engine never reads it to supply inputs to the depending decision table.

d.text: Correct. The docs state that a knowledgeSource denotes an authority for a decision and that it has no execution semantics and is ignored on evaluation.

b.text: Incorrect. The DMN engine does not invoke external services through a knowledgeSource; the element only models which authority underpins the decision.

c.text: Incorrect. Neither decision tables nor decision literal expressions require a knowledgeSource; the element is always optional in the DRG.

explanation: A knowledgeSource records the human or organisational authority behind a decision, such as a policy document or expert team. The Camunda DMN engine ignores it at evaluation time, so it never affects which inputs are consumed or how the depending decision is computed.

scenario: Pinecrest Bank, a regional lender modeling 3200 loan decisions per day in Camunda 8.8, wants its DMN file's DRG to display the descriptive title Loan Approval DRG in tooling. The architect needs to know exactly where to set this name in the XML.
question: Where is the descriptive name of a DMN Decision Requirements Graph set in the XML?

options.text: As the name attribute on the definitions element, which carries the descriptive name of the DRG itself
As the name attribute on the knowledgeSource element, which carries the descriptive name of the DRG it documents
As the value of a drgName element inside the definitions element, which carries the descriptive name of the DRG
As the name attribute on the first decision element, which carries the descriptive name of the DRG itself

a.text: Correct. The DRG name describes the DRG and is set as the name attribute on the definitions element, which is the DRG itself in the XML.

d.text: Incorrect. The name attribute on a decision element names that specific decision, not the surrounding DRG.

c.text: Incorrect. DMN does not define a drgName element; the DRG name is the name attribute on definitions.

b.text: Incorrect. The knowledgeSource element names a knowledge source, not the surrounding DRG.

explanation: The DRG name is set as the name attribute on the definitions element, which represents the DRG in the XML.

scenario: Cardinal Bank reviews 9000 loans per day on a Camunda 8.8 platform and a modeler is renaming the existing 'RiskScore' decision table to 'Customer Risk Score' so that Operate and Tasklist show the new label. The reviewer wants to confirm where the decision name lives in DMN XML and how it is changed through the Camunda Modeler UI.
question: Where is the decision name stored in DMN XML and how is it edited from the Camunda Modeler UI?

options.text: The decision name lives in the name attribute on the decisionTable element and is edited via the properties panel after selecting the table header cell
The decision name lives in the name attribute on the definitions element and is edited via the properties panel of the DRD root container node
The decision name lives in the name attribute on the decision element and is edited via the properties panel after selecting the decision in the DRD view
The decision name lives in the label attribute on the decision element and is edited inline by renaming the decision node in the DRD canvas

a.text: Incorrect. The decisionTable element holds the table logic, not the user-facing decision name; the name attribute that describes the decision lives one level up on the decision element.

b.text: Incorrect. The name attribute on definitions labels the surrounding DRG, not an individual decision; renaming a decision through that attribute would rename the whole graph.

d.text: Incorrect. The DMN decision element exposes its label through the standard name attribute, not a label attribute, so editing a non-existent attribute would not update the name.

c.text: Correct. The docs state that the decision name is the name attribute on the decision element and can be changed via the properties panel after selecting the Decision in the DRD view.

explanation: The user-facing decision name is the name attribute on the decision element and is the value the modeler displays for that node. From the UI the modeler selects the decision in the Decision Requirements Diagram view and edits the name through the properties panel on the right.

scenario: Keystone Insurance processes 4000 claims per day in a Camunda 8.8 underwriting workflow and links several DMN decisions as required decisions of a parent claim-routing decision. A modeller asks the platform team which identifiers can safely be typed into the decision id attribute through the Properties Panel so that the parent decision can still access each child decision result at runtime.
question: Which decision id format is permitted by the rules described for the DMN decision element in Camunda 8.8?

options.text: Identifiers that begin with a leading dollar sign are permitted, provided the trailing characters stay alphanumeric content
Snake case strings like applicant_credit_score are permitted, made up only of alphanumeric characters and the underscore symbol
Kebab case strings like applicant-credit-score are permitted, even though the dash is one of the forbidden operator symbols
Identifiers that contain a single whitespace character are permitted, provided the surrounding characters stay alphanumeric content

b.text: Correct. The decision table page states the decision id can be any alphanumeric string including the underscore symbol, and recommends camelCase or snake_case for word combinations.

c.text: Incorrect. The same page explicitly forbids the kebab-case format on a decision id because the dash counts as an operator and breaks references from dependent decisions.

d.text: Incorrect. Whitespace is listed as a special character that may not appear in a decision id, and using it prevents dependent decisions from accessing the result.

a.text: Incorrect. A leading dollar sign falls under the forbidden special characters and symbols, so the dependent decision cannot resolve the result through that id.

explanation: The decision id must be alphanumeric with the underscore symbol allowed; camelCase and snake_case are the documented safe formats, while dashes, whitespace, and other symbols are forbidden because they block lookups from dependent decisions.

scenario: Halcyon Bank, a retail bank running 5400 loan-pricing checks per day in Camunda 8.8, wants the descriptive label Loan Pricing to appear on a decision in the DRD. The modeler needs to know exactly where to set this descriptive name in the DMN XML.
question: Where is the descriptive name of a DMN decision table's decision set in the XML?

options.text: As the name attribute on the definitions element, which carries the descriptive name shown for each decision in tooling
As the value of a label element inside the decision element, which carries the descriptive name shown for that decision in tooling
As the name attribute on the decisionTable element, which carries the descriptive name shown for that decision in tooling
As the name attribute on the decision element, which carries the descriptive name shown for that decision in tooling

d.text: Correct. The decision name describes the decision for which the decision table provides logic and is set as the name attribute on the decision element.

c.text: Incorrect. The decisionTable element holds the table body; the descriptive name belongs on the enclosing decision element's name attribute.

b.text: Incorrect. DMN does not use a label element to carry the descriptive name; it is the name attribute on the decision element.

a.text: Incorrect. The name attribute on definitions names the DRG, not the individual decision shown in the DRD.

explanation: The decision name is set as the name attribute on the decision element and is shown for that decision in modeling tools.

scenario: Marigold Insurance, an insurer adjudicating 8800 claims per day in Camunda 8.8, must assign technical identifiers to several DMN decisions whose results feed dependent decisions. The architect needs to honor the documented character rules for the decision ID.
question: Which character rule does a DMN decision ID follow so that its result remains accessible from a dependent decision?

options.text: Alphanumeric characters, whitespace, and the hyphen are allowed; the underscore is disallowed because it conflicts with FEEL identifiers
Alphanumeric characters and the hyphen are allowed; the underscore is disallowed because it conflicts with FEEL identifier rules
Alphanumeric characters and any Unicode symbol are allowed; only the period is disallowed because it conflicts with FEEL path access
Alphanumeric characters and the underscore are allowed; kebab-case is disallowed because the hyphen is a FEEL operator

d.text: Correct. A decision ID can be any alphanumeric string including the underscore; kebab-case is not allowed because the hyphen is a FEEL operator, and special characters block access from dependent decisions.

b.text: Incorrect. The underscore is explicitly allowed; the hyphen is not, because it is parsed as a FEEL operator within identifiers.

a.text: Incorrect. Whitespace is a disallowed special character in decision IDs, and the underscore is explicitly allowed by the DMN reference.

c.text: Incorrect. Decision IDs may not contain arbitrary special characters or symbols; only alphanumerics and the underscore are allowed.

explanation: A DMN decision ID may contain only alphanumeric characters and the underscore; hyphens are excluded because they collide with the FEEL minus operator.

scenario: Lattice Bank deploys a Camunda 8.8 DMN suite that scores 4500 loan applications per day. A second decision table consumes the score by referencing the first decision id, and the modeling team must pick a safe id format so the reference keeps resolving after deployment to the cluster.
question: Which decision id format keeps the result of the scored decision addressable from a dependent decision?

options.text: A space-separated id such as Loan Score that matches the user-facing name shown in the properties panel header
A kebab-case id such as loan-score that matches the BPMN process id style used elsewhere in the Lattice Bank workspace
An alphanumeric camelCase id such as loanScore, optionally with the underscore character separating word segments
A slash-separated id such as lattice/score that namespaces the decision under the workspace folder hierarchy

c.text: Correct. The DMN docs require the decision id to be an alphanumeric string and allow the underscore symbol as the only extra character, and they recommend camelCase or snake_case for multi-word ids.

b.text: Incorrect. The DMN docs explicitly forbid the kebab-case format on the decision id because hyphens are operators, so a hyphenated decision id cannot be referenced from a dependent decision.

a.text: Incorrect. Whitespace is listed as a forbidden special character in the decision id, so a space-separated id breaks evaluation lookups from a dependent decision.

d.text: Incorrect. Slashes are special characters and the DMN docs allow only alphanumerics plus the underscore symbol, so a dependent decision could not resolve the result.

explanation: The decision id must be an alphanumeric string and may include the underscore symbol; camelCase or snake_case is recommended for multi-word names. Kebab-case and other special characters are not permitted because they prevent the result from being accessed by a dependent decision.

scenario: Meridian Bank runs a Camunda 8.8 payments service that evaluates 9000 transactions per hour through a DMN decision table for fee calculation. The table author copied an older table from a colleague and removed the hitPolicy attribute on the decisionTable XML element entirely, intending to fix it later. Operations now needs to know which policy the engine actually applies in production for that deployed table.
question: Which hit policy does the engine apply when the hitPolicy attribute is omitted from the decisionTable XML element on a deployed table?

options.text: FIRST is the implicit default policy and returns the output of the first matching rule in document order
UNIQUE is the implicit default policy and produces a violation whenever more than one rule matches a single input
ANY is the implicit default policy and accepts multiple satisfied rules so long as they produce the same output entries
RULE ORDER is the implicit default policy and returns the outputs of all satisfied rules in declared row order

a.text: Incorrect. FIRST is one of the supported policies in the XML representation table but the docs do not list it as the implicit default when the attribute is omitted.

b.text: Correct. The hit policy page states that if no hit policy is set on the decisionTable XML element, the default hit policy UNIQUE is used.

c.text: Incorrect. ANY appears in the list of supported policies but the page does not name it as the implicit default when the hitPolicy attribute is missing from the decisionTable element.

d.text: Incorrect. RULE ORDER is the example value shown in the XML sample but the docs explicitly call out UNIQUE rather than RULE ORDER as the default when nothing is declared.

explanation: The documentation is explicit: if the hitPolicy attribute is absent on the decisionTable element, the engine treats the table as UNIQUE, which raises a violation whenever more than one rule is satisfied by the same input.

scenario: Lattice Insurance is migrating 250 legacy DMN decisions into a Camunda 8.8 underwriting workflow that handles 6000 policies per day. During code review a developer notices that two different XML elements in the file already carry a hitPolicy attribute, and asks the lead modeller which one the engine actually honours when the deployed table is evaluated.
question: Where in the DMN XML must the hitPolicy attribute be declared so that the engine applies the chosen policy to the deployed table?

options.text: The hitPolicy attribute belongs on the decision element and then applies to every decisionTable that is nested below it
The hitPolicy attribute belongs on the decisionTable element and controls how that one table builds its evaluation result
The hitPolicy attribute belongs on the definitions root element and then applies to every decision in the file at deploy time
The hitPolicy attribute belongs on each rule element and selects how that rule contributes to the overall table result

c.text: Incorrect. The documentation does not place hitPolicy on the definitions root, and a value set there would not control how a specific table chooses its satisfied rules.

a.text: Incorrect. The XML sample shows the policy on the decisionTable element below the decision, not on the decision element itself, so a value on decision would be ignored.

b.text: Correct. The hit policy page states the hit policy is set in the hitPolicy attribute on the decisionTable XML element, as demonstrated by the RULE ORDER sample.

d.text: Incorrect. Individual rule elements do not carry the hit policy in DMN, and the supported policies in the docs are properties of the table as a whole.

explanation: The hit policy is a property of the decision table itself, declared as the hitPolicy attribute on the decisionTable XML element; it determines how many satisfied rules contribute to that table's result.

scenario: Apex Logistics evaluates 7000 shipments per day through a Camunda 8.8 routing process backed by DMN. A new hire is asked to choose a hit policy and has heard that some policies guarantee a single result while others can return several. The architect wants the team to know the precise grouping so they pick a single-result policy for the routing decision and a multi-result policy for an audit decision.
question: Which grouping correctly classifies the supported hit policies by whether they return at most one satisfied rule or possibly several?

options.text: UNIQUE and COLLECT return at most one satisfied rule, while ANY, FIRST, and RULE ORDER can return multiple satisfied rules
UNIQUE, ANY, and FIRST return at most one satisfied rule, while RULE ORDER and COLLECT can return multiple satisfied rules
FIRST and COLLECT return at most one satisfied rule, while UNIQUE, ANY, and RULE ORDER can return multiple satisfied rules
UNIQUE, ANY, and RULE ORDER return at most one satisfied rule, while FIRST and COLLECT can return multiple satisfied rules

c.text: Incorrect. FIRST is documented as a single-rule policy, and COLLECT is documented as a multi-rule policy, so the grouping swaps both sides of the split.

a.text: Incorrect. UNIQUE is single-rule by definition and FIRST returns only the first satisfied rule, so placing them on the multi-rule side contradicts the documented role of each.

d.text: Incorrect. RULE ORDER is explicitly listed alongside COLLECT as a policy that can return multiple satisfied rules, not as a single-rule policy.

b.text: Correct. The role-of-a-hit-policy section states that UNIQUE, ANY, and FIRST always return a maximum of one satisfied rule, while RULE ORDER and COLLECT can return multiple satisfied rules.

explanation: The hit policy page splits the supported policies into two families: UNIQUE, ANY, and FIRST always yield at most one satisfied rule in the result, while RULE ORDER and COLLECT can return several satisfied rules.

scenario: Beacon Healthcare processes 4000 claims per day in a Camunda 8.8 triage workflow. The team chose the ANY hit policy on the triage DMN table because two rules can match the same patient. A late change introduces a second rule whose output differs from the first satisfied rule's output for one specific patient cohort of 250 patients per week, and operations wants to know what the engine will return on those evaluations.
question: What happens at runtime when the ANY hit policy sees two satisfied rules whose output entries are not identical to each other?

options.text: The decision result returns the output entries of the first satisfied rule in document order with no engine error reported
The decision result is treated as a hit-policy violation, and the evaluation fails on that decision instance with an error
The decision result returns the output entries of all satisfied rules as a list ordered by the matching rule positions
The decision result returns the output entries of one satisfied rule and silently drops any conflicting outputs from the table

a.text: Incorrect. Returning the first satisfied rule's output describes the FIRST policy, not ANY, which has no document-order preference between satisfied rules.

b.text: Correct. The Any section states that if multiple rules are satisfied which generate different outputs, the hit policy is violated, so the evaluation fails on that decision instance.

c.text: Incorrect. ANY never returns a list of outputs. The docs say the result contains only the output of one of the satisfied rules under valid conditions, not a list of all of them.

d.text: Incorrect. ANY does not silently drop conflicting outputs. The documented behaviour when outputs differ is a hit-policy violation, not a quiet selection of one entry.

explanation: ANY allows several rules to be satisfied only while their outputs are identical; once two satisfied rules generate different output entries, the hit policy is violated and the evaluation fails on that decision instance.

scenario: Zenith Marketplace operates a Camunda 8.8 advertising service that emits 800000 events per day and uses a DMN table to score advertisements by viewer age. The platform team wants the table to return every satisfied rule's output in a deterministic order so that the priority of the displayed advertisements remains stable across 250 deployments per quarter.
question: How does the RULE ORDER hit policy compose the decision result when several rules of a decision table are simultaneously satisfied?

options.text: It returns one output by selecting the satisfied rule that sits last among the decision table's rule rows at evaluation time
It returns the outputs of all satisfied rules as a list sorted ascending by each rule's output entry value at evaluation time
It returns the outputs of all satisfied rules as a list in the order the rules are declared in the decision table itself
It returns one output by selecting the rule whose declared priority entry carries the highest numeric weight at evaluation time

d.text: Incorrect. RULE ORDER does not consult a priority weight on individual rules; the docs describe ordering purely by the position of the rules in the decision table.

a.text: Incorrect. Picking only the last satisfied rule contradicts the documented behaviour, which is to include every satisfied rule's output, not to collapse the result to one entry.

b.text: Incorrect. RULE ORDER does not sort by output value. The Rule order section orders the result list by the position of the rules in the decision table, not by their output entries.

c.text: Correct. The Rule order section states that multiple rules can be satisfied and the result contains the output of all satisfied rules in the order of the rules in the decision table.

explanation: RULE ORDER returns every satisfied rule's output as a list whose order is fixed by the position of the rules in the decision table, which makes the output sequence usable for stable priority indication.

scenario: Lattice Insurance maintains a Camunda 8.8 DMN decision table that evaluates 12000 claims per week. Several rules may match a single claim for a given insurer customer, and a new developer on the team wants to understand exactly what the COLLECT hit policy returns when no aggregator is configured on the decisionTable XML element.
question: What does the COLLECT hit policy return for a DMN decision table when no aggregator is specified on the decisionTable XML element?

options.text: Returns a list of outputs from all satisfied rules ordered by their position in the decision table so the sequence is stable
Returns a single output produced by summing every satisfied rule's output into one number, regardless of any aggregator setting
Returns the output of only the first satisfied rule and ignores the other rules that also match the inputs for that case
Returns a list of outputs from all satisfied rules in an arbitrary order with no defined ordering between the list entries

b.text: Incorrect. SUM is an optional aggregator on the COLLECT hit policy, but COLLECT without any aggregator does not implicitly sum the outputs; the docs describe the no-aggregator case as returning a list of outputs in arbitrary order.

c.text: Incorrect. Returning only the first matched rule's output is the documented behaviour of the FIRST hit policy, not COLLECT; COLLECT returns the outputs of every satisfied rule rather than just the first one.

d.text: Correct. The DMN docs state that COLLECT returns the output of all satisfied rules as a list in an arbitrary order, and the page explicitly notes that the list produced by this hit policy has no defined ordering.

a.text: Incorrect. Ordering by rule position is the behaviour of the RULE ORDER hit policy; the docs explicitly note that the COLLECT output list has no ordering between its entries.

explanation: The Camunda 8.8 DMN docs define COLLECT without an aggregator as returning a list of all satisfied rule outputs in arbitrary order. The page states the list has no ordering, so consumers cannot rely on any deterministic sequence of entries.

scenario: Aurora Bank operates a Camunda 8.8 DMN decision table that evaluates 4000 loans per month and uses the COLLECT hit policy. A new developer is reviewing the underlying decisionTable XML element to learn which aggregator values can appear in the aggregation attribute so the table can reduce its result to a single output entry.
question: Which set of aggregators does the DMN docs page list as the supported aggregation attribute values for the COLLECT hit policy?

options.text: The aggregators FIRST, LAST, MIN, and MAX are listed as the supported aggregation attribute values for the COLLECT hit policy
The aggregators SUM, MIN, MAX, and CONCAT are listed as the supported aggregation attribute values for the COLLECT hit policy
The aggregators SUM, MIN, MAX, and AVERAGE are listed as the supported aggregation attribute values for the COLLECT hit policy
The aggregators SUM, MIN, MAX, and COUNT are listed as the supported aggregation attribute values for the COLLECT hit policy

c.text: Incorrect. AVERAGE does not appear in the supported aggregators table of the docs page. The fourth aggregator listed alongside SUM, MIN, and MAX is COUNT, which returns the number of satisfied rules rather than an average value.

b.text: Incorrect. CONCAT does not appear in the supported aggregators table on the docs page. The fourth aggregator listed alongside SUM, MIN, and MAX is COUNT, not CONCAT, and there is no string concatenation aggregator defined.

a.text: Incorrect. FIRST and LAST are not listed as COLLECT aggregators on the docs page; the supported set consists of SUM, MIN, MAX, and COUNT, and FIRST is the name of a separate hit policy rather than an aggregator.

d.text: Correct. The DMN docs aggregators table lists exactly four supported aggregation values for the COLLECT hit policy: SUM for summing outputs, MIN for the smallest, MAX for the largest, and COUNT for the number of satisfied rules.

explanation: The DMN docs enumerate exactly four aggregators for the COLLECT hit policy in the supported aggregators table: SUM, MIN, MAX, and COUNT. The chosen aggregator is set as the aggregation attribute on the decisionTable XML element.

scenario: An Aurora insurance team adopts Camunda 8.8 DMN tables for 9000 claims per day. A reviewer deploys a decisionTable XML element with no hitPolicy attribute and wants to understand which policy the engine applies by default across the 5 supported policies.

explanation: The Input label section says an input label is a short description of the input set on the input XML element via the label attribute, and that the label is not required but recommended because it helps to understand the decision.

scenario: Forge Lender runs a Camunda 8.8 DMN table that returns 3 outputs per matched rule across 9500 loans per day in its credit decisioning pipeline. A reviewer asks the modelers to confirm how each output clause is represented in the DMN XML and whether a single decision table is allowed to declare more than one output clause in Camunda 8.8.
question: Where is each output clause defined in the DMN XML and how many output clauses may a Camunda 8.8 decision table declare?

options.text: Each output clause is an output element nested inside the inputExpression element, and the table may declare any number of clauses per rule in Camunda 8.8
Each output clause is an outputClause element nested inside the decision element, and the table is restricted to exactly one output clause per decision
Each output clause is an output element nested directly inside the decisionTable element, and the table may declare one or more output clauses
Each output clause is an output element nested directly inside the definitions root element, and the table may declare at most two output clauses per decision

c.text: Correct. The docs show output elements as direct children of decisionTable and state that a decision table may have one or more output clauses in Camunda 8.8.

b.text: Incorrect. The element name is output, not outputClause, and the schema explicitly allows more than one output clause rather than restricting the table to one.

d.text: Incorrect. Output clauses sit inside the decisionTable element, not directly under the definitions root, and there is no documented two clause limit.

a.text: Incorrect. The output element is nested in decisionTable, not inside an inputExpression, which is part of the input clause structure rather than the output side.

explanation: A Camunda 8.8 DMN decision table represents each output clause as an output element nested directly under decisionTable, and a table may declare one or more output clauses per decision.

scenario: Vertex Insurer models a Camunda 8.8 DMN table whose result is consumed by a calling BPMN process that handles 4200 claims per day. A modeler accidentally adds a whitespace character to the output name, and a reviewer warns that this small typo breaks downstream access to the output value from dependent decisions and from the calling BPMN process.
question: What is the documented consequence of using whitespace or other special characters in a Camunda 8.8 DMN output name?

options.text: The modeler rewrites the output name on deployment by replacing whitespace with underscores so that the calling BPMN process keeps reading the same output value
The output value cannot be accessed in a dependent decision or in a calling BPMN process when the output name contains a special character or symbol
The DMN engine raises an evaluation incident on every matching rule until the special characters are removed from the output name in the modeler and redeployed
The output value is exposed only as a list under the decision id, and any whitespace inside the output name is silently stripped at evaluation time by the engine

c.text: Incorrect. The docs do not describe a runtime incident for this case; instead the value simply becomes unreachable from dependent decisions and from the calling BPMN process.

b.text: Correct. The docs state that an output name containing a special character or symbol cannot be accessed in a dependent decision nor in a calling BPMN process.

a.text: Incorrect. There is no documented automatic rewrite at deployment time; the modeler is expected to use camelCase or snake_case for the output name in Camunda 8.8.

d.text: Incorrect. The list under the decision id is the multi output access pattern, not a workaround that silently strips whitespace from a malformed output name.
