The onboarding form has a single text field whose Key is set to the path user.info.age, and an applicant enters the value 21, expecting the runtime to persist that value within 2 seconds of form submission so a downstream service task can read the applicant age.
question: What variable structure does the runtime produce on submission of that field?

options.text: A nested process variable structure where 21 is stored under user.age while the middle info segment is dropped silently
A single flat process variable literally named user.info.age with the value 21 stored under that exact dotted string
An array process variable named user whose first element places 21 under an info key at index zero of that array list
A nested process variable structure where 21 lives under user, then info, then age inside the resulting submission JSON

b.text: Incorrect. The runtime interprets dots in the Key as a path into nested objects rather than as part of a literal variable name, so the dotted string is not used as a single flat variable identifier.

d.text: Correct. A path key such as user.info.age tells the runtime to build a nested object structure, so a submitted value of 21 lands under user, then info, then age in the resulting JSON.

a.text: Incorrect. The runtime walks every segment of the path key when building the output, so no segment is dropped; all three of user, info, and age appear in the resulting nested structure.

c.text: Incorrect. Path keys produce nested objects rather than arrays; an array output would require a dynamic list with its own path configuration, not a simple path key on a single field.

explanation: A key that contains dots is treated as a path into a nested object. Setting a field with key user.info.age to the value 21 yields a JSON object where user contains info, which in turn contains age set to 21, exactly as described under Simple key vs path key.

docs.title: Forms data binding - simple key vs path key

scenario: Lattice Insurance, a regional insurer, processes 4500 claim intake requests per week through Camunda 8.8 user tasks rendered in Tasklist. The claim intake form has a text field whose Key is set to claimant.email, the same value already held in a process variable, and the team needs that single Key to drive both initial loading and submission within 3 seconds of the task opening.
question: What dual role does the Key attribute play during this user task lifecycle?

options.text: The Key only loads the initial value from the process variable into the field and is entirely skipped on task submission
The Key identifies the field for Tasklist styling rules and has no role in moving data into or out of the process variables
The Key only maps the submitted value back to a process variable and does not influence the initial value rendered in the field
The Key maps a process variable into the field as the initial value and writes the field value back to that variable on submission

a.text: Incorrect. The docs describe the Key as serving a dual purpose, so it also participates in mapping the field data back to the matching process variable on submission, not only on initial load.

c.text: Incorrect. The Key correlates with a process variable on both ends of the task: it serves as the initial value of the field and is updated on submission, not just one direction.

d.text: Correct. The Key serves a dual purpose: it maps process data to the field during the initial loading of the form and maps the field data back to the process variable during form submission.

b.text: Incorrect. The Key is a data-binding identifier between the field and a process variable; it has no role in Tasklist styling or in any presentation concern.

explanation: The Key attribute on a form field has two duties: it pulls the matching process variable into the field as the initial value when the task opens in Tasklist, and it writes the submitted field value back into that same process variable on form submission.

scenario: Borealis Logistics, a freight carrier, processes 12000 shipments per month through Camunda 8.8. A new manifest form embeds a dynamic list with the path items to capture line items, and a developer wants to add a second dynamic list bound to that same items path within 1 hour so two side-by-side views of the array can coexist on the same form schema.
question: Which rule does the Camunda 8.8 forms runtime enforce about path configuration for that second dynamic list?

options.text: The two dynamic lists may share the items path provided their child field keys do not overlap within any single row of the array
A dynamic list cannot share its path with another dynamic list or with any group inside the same form schema configuration
Both dynamic lists may share the same items path as long as each row uses a unique numeric index across the two combined lists
A group may share that items path with a dynamic list without restriction so both forms of structuring can target one array

c.text: Incorrect. Dynamic lists are bound to whole arrays at their path, so two of them at the same path is not allowed even with unique row indices; the runtime does not split the shared array binding by index.

a.text: Incorrect. The non-conflicting child key rule applies to groups sharing a path, not to dynamic lists; a dynamic list owns the array at its path on its own.

d.text: Incorrect. A dynamic list cannot share its path with a group either; only multiple groups may share a path, and only when their child field keys do not conflict.

b.text: Correct. The docs state that because dynamic lists are bound to arrays, they cannot share paths with other dynamic lists or with groups, so the second dynamic list at items is not allowed.

explanation: Path configuration for dynamic lists is more restrictive than for groups. Because a dynamic list binds to the array at its path, it cannot share that path with another dynamic list or with any group, even though multiple groups may share a path when their child field keys remain distinct.

docs.title: Forms data binding - dynamic list path configuration

scenario: Meridian Logistics processes 12000 shipments per month using Camunda 8.8 user tasks. On the consignee review form, a text field has the Key set to consignee, and the team must load the existing process variable into the field at task creation and write any operator edits back to the same variable within 10 seconds of submission.
question: What dual purpose does the Key attribute of a form field serve in Camunda Forms?

options.text: The Key maps data into the field during initial loading of the form and maps the field's data back to the process variable during form submission
The Key translates the field name between FEEL and BPMN names so the runtime can rewrite the variable identifier inside the deployed process model
The Key only maps the field's submitted value back to a process variable; the initial value of the field is loaded from a separate Default value property
The Key only controls which CSS selector identifies the field in the rendered DOM and never influences how the field is populated or submitted

d.text: Incorrect. The Key is not a CSS hook; it correlates with a process variable for both reading the initial value and writing the submitted value.

c.text: Incorrect. The Key handles both directions, not only submission; the same Key is also used to read the matching process variable into the field at load.

a.text: Correct. The Key serves a dual purpose: mapping data to the field during the initial loading of the form and mapping the field's data back to the process variable during form submission.

b.text: Incorrect. The Key does not rewrite variable identifiers inside the BPMN model; it directly correlates the form field to a process variable in both directions.

explanation: The Key attribute of every form field correlates with a process variable: it provides the initial value when the form loads and receives the submitted value when the form is completed.

scenario: Vanta Health, a regional hospital network, admits 2500 records per week through Camunda 8.8 Forms. A number field on the admission form has the Key set to user.info.age, and a nurse enters 42 at the bedside, so the team must know the JSON shape the runtime produces in 3 seconds before downstream FEEL reads it.
question: Which output data structure does the runtime produce on submission of this field?

options.text: A nested object where user contains info, and info contains age set to 42, so the path key materialises as a real nested data structure
An array under user containing one object info that holds the age 42 in its first element, mirroring how dynamic lists serialise their rows
A single variable whose name joins user, info, and age with underscores and holds the value 42 while the runtime collapses dotted keys to a flat name
A single flat variable literally named user.info.age whose value is 42, with no nested object created in the process variables payload

d.text: Incorrect. Path keys are not stored as flat dotted names; the runtime materialises the dotted path into a nested object structure on submission.

b.text: Incorrect. A plain field with a path key produces a nested object, not an array; arrays are the output of dynamic lists, not of a single field.

c.text: Incorrect. The runtime does not rewrite dots into underscores; the documented behaviour is to materialise the path into nested objects.

a.text: Correct. With the Key set to user.info.age and value 42, the documented output is the nested object {"user":{"info":{"age":42}}} on submission.

explanation: Defining the Key as a path like user.info.age tells the runtime to build the matching nested object structure on submission instead of treating the dotted string as a single flat variable name.

scenario: Aurora Retail captures shipping details for 8000 orders per day in Camunda 8.8 Forms. The order form contains a group whose path is customer.address and a child text field whose Key is street, and the operations team needs the resulting variable to land in the right place within 2 seconds of submission so the carrier integration can read it.
question: Which variable path does the runtime produce for the street field on submission?

options.text: The path customer.address.street, formed when the group's path acts as a prefix to the key of each child field within that group at submission
The path customer.address with an embedded street keyed by the field label, where labels override field keys whenever the group has a path
Only a top-level street variable, with the group path influencing layout but not extending the keys of any child field on submission
Two separate variables customer.address and street, with the runtime never concatenating a group path with a child key during submission

a.text: Correct. In a form that uses groups, the group's path acts as a prefix to the key of each child field, so customer.address plus street produces customer.address.street.

c.text: Incorrect. Group paths are documented to extend the keys of child fields, not just to control layout; the child key is prefixed on submission.

b.text: Incorrect. The field label is presentation only; the documented prefix mechanism uses the field's Key, not its label, against the group's path.

d.text: Incorrect. The runtime does concatenate the group path with the child field key, producing a single nested path rather than two separate variables.

explanation: A group's path acts as a prefix to the key of each child field within that group, so a group path of customer.address combined with a child key street resolves to customer.address.street on submission.

scenario: Kestrel Retail, a fashion retailer, processes 9000 orders per day through Camunda 8.8 user tasks where customers pick a preferred language. The Select field exposes 6 language codes such as fr, en-gb, and de from a FEEL expression named languageData, and the form designer wants the shortest valid JSON shorthand for that source because label and value are identical for every entry.
question: Which JSON shorthand is valid for that languageData options array?

options.text: A single string under languageData that lists fr, en-gb, and de separated by commas as the dropdown source for the field
An array of objects under languageData where each item only specifies a label property and omits the value key entirely
An object under languageData whose property keys are the option values and whose property values are the matching labels
An array of plain strings such as fr, en-gb, and de placed under languageData with no label or value keys around each item

d.text: Correct. When the value and label are equal the docs allow a shorthand where the source is an array of bare strings; the runtime then treats each string as both the value and the label for that option.

a.text: Incorrect. The options source must be a JSON array of entries rather than a single comma-separated string; the form runtime does not parse a delimited string into individual options.

c.text: Incorrect. The shorthand reduces each entry, but the overall shape stays as an array; an object map keyed by value is not one of the documented shorthand formats for the options source.

b.text: Incorrect. The object shorthand keeps the value property and omits the label, not the other way around, since the runtime needs a value to submit when the user picks an option.

explanation: When the label and value of every option are identical, the docs allow two shorthand shapes: an array of objects that each carry only a value property, or even simpler, an array of bare strings. Both render the same dropdown and submit the same data on selection.

scenario: Cygnus Bank, a digital bank, serves 40000 customers on Camunda 8.8. A developer is wiring a Radio field whose options come from a FEEL expression, and the team has chosen the shortest shorthand by supplying only a value per entry, with no label, so they must respect the documented constraint on which types the value parameter may take in that no-label case.
question: Which rule governs the supported types for the value parameter when no label is provided for the entry?

options.text: The value parameter must be a string and integer or boolean entries are rejected when no label has been provided for the entry
The value parameter may be any non-null type as long as a string fallback is also supplied at the form schema level configuration
The value parameter must be a string when no label is provided and any numeric or boolean entry is dropped at form load time
The value parameter is restricted to types string, number, or boolean when no label is provided for that option entry

a.text: Incorrect. The docs allow number and boolean for the value parameter when no label is provided; only types beyond string, number, and boolean are excluded in that no-label case.

d.text: Correct. The docs state that when a label is not provided, the value parameter is restricted to the types string, number, and boolean; richer non-null types require an explicit label.

b.text: Incorrect. The any non-null rule for value only applies when a label is provided; without a label the type set is restricted to string, number, and boolean by the docs.

c.text: Incorrect. Number and boolean values are valid for entries without a label; only types outside of string, number, and boolean are excluded by the docs in that no-label case.

explanation: When the label parameter is provided, the value parameter can be any non-null type. When no label is given, the value parameter is restricted to the basic types string, number, and boolean so the runtime can still render a sensible label from the value itself.

scenario: Atlas Telco handles 9000 customer requests per day through Camunda 8.8 Forms. A Select field on the support form must show 12 language options that are already maintained by the back office as a process variable named languageData, so the team avoids redeploying the form whenever the catalogue changes within 30 minutes.
question: Which property of the form field references the process variable that supplies the options array?

options.text: The Output mapping expression configured on the surrounding BPMN element, which forwards the variable into the form at task creation time
The Static options items list, which is populated row by row in the form designer and is the only documented way to feed values into a Select field
The Input values key property in the Dynamic Options configuration group, which names the form variable used as the source for the options
The Default value property of the Select field, which is read at load time and provides every option that will appear in the rendered field

b.text: Incorrect. Static options items hardcode values onto the schema; they do not let the options follow a process variable like languageData without redeploying the form.

d.text: Incorrect. Default value sets an initial single selection for the field; it does not define the catalogue of options that the field offers to the user.

c.text: Correct. The Input values key property within the Dynamic Options configuration group selects which form variable is used as the source for the options array.

a.text: Incorrect. The BPMN output mapping moves data out of a task, not into the form; option binding is done inside the form field's Dynamic Options group.

explanation: Process-data-driven options use the Input values key property within the Dynamic Options group of the form field to name the variable that supplies the options array.

scenario: Helios SaaS hosts 200 tenants per cluster on Camunda 8.8. A Radio field on the tenant settings form populates its region choices from a FEEL expression that runs per task, and the team needs to know the exact shape the expression must return so the field renders correctly within 5 seconds of task creation.
question: What result format must a FEEL expression return to populate the options of a Radio or Select field?

options.text: An array of options formatted as objects with label and value properties, matching the shape used by input-data-driven dynamic options on a Camunda Form
A single string that the form splits on commas to derive labels, with the field treating every entry as both the displayed text and the submitted value
A JSON object whose keys become the labels displayed to the user and whose values become the data submitted with the form on completion
An XML node list whose attributes encode the label and the value separately for each option that the field will show in the rendered form

b.text: Incorrect. The form does not parse a single delimited string into options; the documented format is a JSON array of option objects.

c.text: Incorrect. The documented format is an array, not a keyed object; the form needs explicit label and value properties on each option.

d.text: Incorrect. Camunda Forms consume JSON, not XML; the FEEL result must be a JSON array of options, not an XML node list.

a.text: Correct. FEEL-driven options must return an array of options whose entries carry a label and a value, matching the same JSON shape used for input-driven dynamic options.

explanation: FEEL-driven options must evaluate to an array of options formatted as objects with label and value, the same JSON shape used by the input-data-driven dynamic options example.

scenario: Stellar Broker captures KYC information for 4000 customers per month on Camunda 8.8 Forms. A compliance officer adds three statically configured options to a Radio field that asks for the trading profile, and the team must understand which property is submitted as data versus which is shown to the user within 5 minutes of opening the form.
question: Which property of a statically configured option is submitted as data, and which is displayed to the user?

options.text: Both label and value are submitted together as a tuple, and the runtime displays the first element of the tuple to the user filling the form
The label property is submitted as the option's form data, while the value property is the human-readable text rendered in the radio group
The value property is displayed to the user, and the label property is stored in a separate metadata object alongside the submitted form variables
The value property is submitted as the option's form data, and the label property is displayed to the user filling in the form, on each entry

d.text: Correct. For each statically configured option, the value corresponds to the form-submitted data and the label is what is displayed to the user filling in the form.

b.text: Incorrect. The roles are reversed in this option; the documented rule is that the value is submitted and the label is displayed.

a.text: Incorrect. The runtime submits the value alone, not a tuple of label and value, and the label is purely a presentation concern.

c.text: Incorrect. The label is the displayed text, not metadata, and the value is what gets submitted; the roles in this option are inverted.

explanation: Each statically configured option carries a value, which is submitted as the form data, and a label, which is displayed to the user filling in the form.

scenario: Helios Insurance, a property insurer, processes 5000 claim intake requests per day through Camunda 8.8 user tasks. One task embeds a table component that lists policy items, and the designer chose Expression for the Headers source so a FEEL expression dynamically returns the Name and Surname columns within 2 seconds of the task opening in Tasklist.
question: Which JSON shape must the expression return so the table renders Name and Surname as columns over the policy rows?

options.text: A list of objects, each carrying a label property and a key property, where label drives the header and key picks the data
A nested array where the first inner list holds the Name and Surname labels and the second inner list holds the data keys
A list of two bare strings name and surname which the runtime then uses as both the column label and the row data key
An object whose property keys are the column labels Name and Surname and whose property values are the row data field keys

d.text: Incorrect. The expression must return a list rather than a single top-level object map; an object whose keys are labels is not the format the docs describe for the Headers source.

c.text: Incorrect. The expression returns objects with explicit label and key properties so the two roles can differ, and bare strings do not match the schema the docs require for headers.

a.text: Correct. The docs state that the expression must return a list of objects, each carrying a label and a key property, where the label is shown in the header and the key picks the data for that column.

b.text: Incorrect. The expression returns a flat list of header objects, not a nested array where labels and keys live in two separate inner lists; that nested shape is not documented.

explanation: When the Headers source is set to Expression, the FEEL expression must return a flat list of objects, each providing a label that appears in the column header and a key that names the variable used to populate that column from each row of the table data.

docs.title: Forms table data binding - headers source

scenario: Solara Logistics processes 8200 shipments per week through an operations form in Camunda 8.8 that renders a table of in-flight loads. The Headers source has been set to a static column list with the keys origin and destination, and the team now needs the table to populate runtime rows from a variable fetched by an upstream service task within 3 seconds of form open.
question: What must the Data source property evaluate to so that the table renders one row per shipment with the configured column keys?

options.text: A FEEL expression that returns an object whose keys are origin and destination and whose values are arrays of cell strings read in order
A FEEL expression that returns a flat array of strings alternating origin and destination values for sequential placement into table cells
A plain JSON literal block of column definitions and a body section so the table can read row content directly from the definition
A FEEL expression that returns a list of objects where each object carries an origin property and a destination property used to populate one row

d.text: Correct. The Data source must be a FEEL expression that returns a list of objects, with each object holding a property for every column declared in the Headers source, so the table maps each object to one row.

a.text: Incorrect. The documented shape is a list of objects, not a single object keyed by column with parallel value arrays, so this layout would not align with the row-per-object contract.

c.text: Incorrect. The Data source field accepts only expressions, not a JSON literal block embedded in the property, so the runtime would not parse a definition pasted there.

b.text: Incorrect. A flat alternating array provides no per-row grouping and lacks the named properties the table needs to bind values to the origin and destination columns.

explanation: The Data source property accepts a FEEL expression that must return a list of objects. Each object represents one table row and must carry one property per key declared in the Headers source so the runtime can place values in the right column.

scenario: Junction Wholesaler reviews order summaries for 500 merchants per week through Camunda 8.8 Forms. A table on the supervisor user task must list 6 columns that change per merchant tier, so the team picks the dynamic Headers source approach to avoid redeploying the form every 30 days.
question: Which return shape must the FEEL expression produce when defining table headers dynamically?

options.text: A single object whose property names become the headers and whose property values supply the cell renderers for every row in the table
A list of objects, where each object has a label property and a key property, defining the header text and the variable for the column
An array of plain strings naming each column, with the runtime treating each string as both the header label and the underlying key for the column
A CSV string whose entries are separated by semicolons, with the runtime splitting the string at design time to derive labels and column keys

a.text: Incorrect. The documented dynamic format is an array of header objects, not a single object whose keys double as column headers.

c.text: Incorrect. Plain strings do not give the runtime a label and a key separately; the expression must return objects with label and key properties.

b.text: Correct. The dynamic Headers source expression must return a list of objects where each object has a label property and a key property for that column.

d.text: Incorrect. The expression must return structured JSON, not a delimited string; the runtime does not parse semicolon-separated text into headers.

explanation: When the Headers source uses an expression, the expression must return a list of objects, each carrying a label property for the displayed text and a key property naming the column variable.

scenario: Pinnacle Manufacturer tracks 1500 orders per week through Camunda 8.8 Forms. A table on the supervisor user task lists current orders for the shift, and the team must know which value type the Data source property accepts and what shape its result must take within 5 minutes of opening the designer.
question: What value does the Data source property of a table accept, and what shape must its result take?

options.text: A single process variable name that points to a string already serialised as CSV, with one row per line and one column per comma-separated field
A static JSON literal that is pasted into the property and is reread on each render, with each entry representing one row in the rendered table
An expression whose result is a list of objects where each object has one property per column key declared in the Headers source for the table
A comma-separated list of process variable names that the table iterates over in declaration order, treating each name as one column of the table

b.text: Incorrect. The Data source property accepts only expressions; a pasted static JSON literal is not the documented value type for this property.

a.text: Incorrect. The property does not parse CSV from a variable; it evaluates a FEEL expression whose result is a structured list of objects.

d.text: Incorrect. The property is not a list of variable names; it is a single expression evaluated to a list of row objects.

c.text: Correct. The Data source accepts only expressions, and the expression must return a list of objects, each with a property matching every column key declared in the Headers source.

explanation: The Data source property accepts only expressions, and the expression must return a list of objects, where each object has a property for each column defined in the Headers source.

scenario: Keystone Payroll renders 7000 payslip previews per month inside Camunda 8.8 Forms. A summary template begins with the line Hello {{username}}, you are {{if isAdmin then "an admin" else "a user"}}, and the team must understand what can appear between the double curly braces so 200 admins per day see the badge within 5 seconds.
question: What can be placed inside the double curly braces in Feelers templating syntax for an insert?

options.text: Only a single bare variable name from the form data, with no operators or conditionals allowed inside the double curly braces of an insert
Only a string literal enclosed in double quotes, with all dynamic content moved to surrounding loop or conditional blocks of the Feelers template
Any valid FEEL expression whose evaluated result is then inserted at that position in the rendered template by the Feelers runtime on each form render
Any JavaScript expression that the browser evaluates client-side when the form is rendered, with full access to the window object of the host page

c.text: Correct. To insert a variable use double curly braces, and any valid FEEL expression can be used within these double braces, including conditionals like if-then-else.

a.text: Incorrect. Inserts accept any valid FEEL expression, not only a bare variable name; operators and conditionals work between the braces.

b.text: Incorrect. Inserts evaluate FEEL expressions, not just string literals; dynamic content does not have to be moved out of the braces.

d.text: Incorrect. Feelers evaluates FEEL inside the braces, not JavaScript, and templates do not run with browser window access at render time.

explanation: Feelers inserts use double curly braces and may contain any valid FEEL expression. The runtime evaluates the FEEL inside the braces and substitutes its result into the rendered output.

scenario: Ridge Cooperative issues 200 invoices per month through Camunda 8.8 Forms to its supplier network. Each invoice template loops over 15 records of line items and must show one currency symbol that lives on the surrounding object outside the items array, so the team needs the documented way to reach the symbol from inside the loop within 5 seconds per render.
question: How does a Feelers loop template reach data that lives outside the scope of the current iteration?

options.text: By using the parent accessor inside the loop body to reach data outside the scope of the individual items, such as a currency symbol on the surrounding object
By rebinding the surrounding data into a new local variable before opening the loop, and then referencing only that local variable from inside the loop body
By writing an absolute path from the form schema root inside the loop body, which the runtime resolves against the original data object on every iteration
By closing the loop, reading the outer value with a regular insert, and then opening a second loop that re-iterates the same array with the value passed in

c.text: Incorrect. Feelers does not expose an absolute path from the schema root inside a loop; the documented mechanism is the parent accessor.

a.text: Correct. Inside a loop, the parent accessor is used to reach data outside the scope of the individual items, for example parent.currency next to each item's price.

b.text: Incorrect. Feelers does not require pre-binding outer values; the parent accessor is the documented way to reach them directly from inside the loop.

d.text: Incorrect. Splitting the template into a pre-loop insert and a second loop is unnecessary; the parent accessor works from inside the original loop body.

explanation: Inside a Feelers loop, the parent accessor reaches data that lives outside the iteration scope, so a single template can mix per-item values with values from the surrounding object.

scenario: Iris Utility runs 11000 tasks per month for meter inspections in Camunda 8.8 Forms. The inspection form binds a checkbox to the variable isCompliant, but 1500 legacy records arrive each week carrying the string yes instead of a boolean, and the team must know how the checkbox renders for those records on the first task load.
question: How does the checkbox render an existing value whose datatype is not boolean?

options.text: The form treats any value whose datatype is not boolean as false by default, so the checkbox renders unchecked for the legacy records on the first load
The form auto-converts strings like yes and no into the matching boolean state so the legacy records render as a checked or unchecked box on each load
The form renders the box in a partially checked state until an inspector reviews the legacy value and confirms whether the box should be checked or cleared
The form raises a runtime validation error on load so the inspector is forced to fix the offending field before the task can be opened in Tasklist

d.text: Incorrect. The checkbox does not raise a validation error for a non-boolean value; it falls back to a default rendering instead.

b.text: Incorrect. The checkbox does not heuristically convert string values; any non-boolean input is treated as false by default.

c.text: Incorrect. The checkbox does not support a partially-checked tri-state for non-boolean input; the default rendering is simply false.

a.text: Correct. Checkboxes can be bound to data of the boolean type; any other datatype is treated as false by default, so a string like yes renders as an unchecked box.

explanation: The documented behaviour is that checkboxes are bound to boolean data, and any other datatype is treated as false by default when the form loads.

scenario: Sable Carrier handles 5000 shipments per week through Camunda 8.8 Forms. A customs consent checkbox on the export form must enforce that the operator actively confirms the declaration before submission, even on the 800 last-minute shipments per day that go through the same task within 10 minutes of cutoff.
question: Which checkbox property under Validation enforces that the form only submits when the box has a value?

options.text: The Disabled property turned on, which removes the box from interaction at runtime and forces the operator to use a separate confirmation step instead
The Required validation property, which holds the form submission until the checkbox contains a value and otherwise shows a validation error on the field
The Hide if expression returning false, which keeps the checkbox visible at all times and is documented as the proper way to enforce mandatory consent
The Read only property turned on, which blocks the operator from changing the box and so guarantees that the original value is the one that gets submitted

b.text: Correct. Under Validation, the Required property states that the checkbox must contain a value, and the form will only submit when that condition is fulfilled, otherwise a validation error is displayed.

d.text: Incorrect. Read only prevents the operator from changing the value, but it does not enforce that the box was actively set before submission.

a.text: Incorrect. Disabled is documented for use during development; it does not enforce that the operator has consented at runtime.

c.text: Incorrect. Hide if controls visibility through an expression and does not enforce that the operator has set the box before submitting the form.

explanation: The Required validation property is the documented way to enforce that a checkbox must contain a value before submission, with a validation error shown otherwise.

scenario: Helios SaaS rolls out an internal Camunda 8.8 onboarding form to 200 tenants per cluster. The designer must separate a checkbox that the operator can read but cannot change at runtime from a checkbox the designer turns off only during development for 2 weeks of iteration before the production rollout.
question: Which two checkbox properties separate end-user read-only state from a development-only off state?

options.text: Required handles the end-user case and Hide if handles the development case; Required prevents edits and Hide if removes the box from the form only while designing
Default value enforces the end-user case by locking the initial state, and Columns is used during development to take the field out of the layout grid
Field label covers the end-user case as a non-editable header, while Field description covers the development case by hiding the field via its description text
Read only makes the checkbox read-only so the user can only read its state, while Disabled disables the checkbox and is documented for use during development

a.text: Incorrect. Required enforces submission, not read-only state, and Hide if removes the field at runtime; neither is the documented development-only switch.

d.text: Correct. Read only makes the checkbox read-only at runtime so the user can only read its state, while Disabled is documented as for use during development.

b.text: Incorrect. Default value supplies an initial state and Columns controls layout space; neither is the documented read-only or development toggle.

c.text: Incorrect. Field label and Field description are presentation text, not behavioural switches that lock or disable the checkbox at runtime or design time.

explanation: Read only locks the checkbox so the user can only read its state at runtime, while Disabled is documented as for use during development, giving designers two distinct switches.

scenario: Orion Retail's checkout form on Camunda 8.8 prices a shopping cart for 9500 customers per day, and the modeler adds an Expression field with key cartTotal whose Target value sums the cart line prices via a FEEL expression. The team wants cartTotal to recompute every time a quantity or unit-price changes so the displayed total stays in sync with the cart edits in real time.
question: Which Compute on setting does the modeler select for the cartTotal Expression field to meet that recalculation requirement?

options.text: Compute on set to at form load only, which evaluates the cartTotal FEEL Target value once at the time the checkout form is first rendered for the customer and never again afterwards
Compute on set to whenever a sibling field is focused, which re-evaluates the cartTotal FEEL Target value as soon as the customer tabs into a related row of the cart in the form
Compute on set to whenever the result changes, so the FEEL Target value for cartTotal is re-evaluated each time an input variable to the expression changes during cart editing in the form
Compute on set to only on form submission, which defers the evaluation of the cartTotal FEEL Target value to the moment the customer clicks the submit button to finalise the checkout

c.text: Correct. The Compute on property of the Expression form element offers two documented modes: whenever the result changes, or only on form submission. To keep cartTotal in sync with cart edits, the modeler selects the change-driven mode.

d.text: Incorrect. The on-submission mode delays evaluation until the submit step, so the displayed cartTotal would not update interactively as quantities or unit-prices change in the cart during editing.

b.text: Incorrect. The Compute on property documents two modes (result-change and on-submission); a focus-driven mode tied to sibling fields is not part of the documented options for the field.

a.text: Incorrect. The documented options for Compute on are result-change and on-submission; a one-time at-form-load evaluation is not part of the Expression field's documented behaviour for cartTotal.

explanation: The Expression form element computes intermediary data via a FEEL Target value. Its Compute on property has exactly two documented modes: re-evaluate whenever the result changes, or evaluate only on form submission. The interactive cart total requires the change-driven mode.

scenario: Catalyst Fintech runs a Camunda 8.8 onboarding form where the Number element price-per-share captures bid amounts up to $9999.9999 across 15000 trader sessions per day. The developer binds the field to a string variable bidPrice in the process and configures Decimal digits to 4 while leaving Serialize to string at its default off setting in the panel.
question: What value is written back to bidPrice when a trader enters 250.7525 and submits the Camunda 8.8 form?

options.text: The string value '250.7525' is written back, with the string binding of bidPrice overriding the Number element's documented output type and preserving the entered precision at submit time
The integer value 251 is written back, with the Number element outputting strictly integer data when Serialize to string is off, regardless of the configured Decimal digits in the panel
The numeric value 250.75 is written back, with the Number runtime applying banker's rounding to the configured Decimal digits before bidPrice is updated at form submission time
The numeric value 250.7525 is written back, with the Decimal digits setting driving the precision of the value emitted by the Number form runtime at form submission time

b.text: Correct. The Datatypes paragraph states that the Number element will always output strictly integer data. Decimal digits controls the input precision accepted in the editor, not the output type; the input 250.7525 is therefore written back as the integer 251.

d.text: Incorrect. Decimal digits defines the maximum number of digits after the decimal accepted in the field, but the documented output type for the Number element is strictly integer data when Serialize to string is left off.

a.text: Incorrect. The Number element accepts string-typed bindings that parse to numeric data, but the output is still emitted as strictly integer data per the Datatypes section, not preserved as the original string.

c.text: Incorrect. The documentation does not describe banker's-rounding semantics for the Number element; it specifies that the output is strictly integer data, with the Decimal digits field controlling input precision.

explanation: The Number element accepts numeric data or strings that parse to numeric, and the Decimal digits field caps the precision of the input. According to the Datatypes paragraph, the output written back to the bound variable is always strictly integer data when Serialize to string is off.

scenario: Beacon Healthcare requires a Camunda 8.8 Number field bound to dosageMg on a prescription form to accept only integer multiples of 25 between 25 and 500 across 1200 orders per week. The team is configuring the properties panel of the Number element to enforce that constraint without writing any custom FEEL expressions on the field.
question: Which combination of Camunda 8.8 Number element properties does the team configure to enforce that 25-multiple range?

options.text: The Increment property set to 25, the Minimum validation property set to 25, and the Maximum validation property set to 500, leaving the remaining properties at their defaults on the field
The Required validation property enabled, the Default value property set to 25, and a custom FEEL expression on the field label that enforces the increment-of-25 rule at submit time
The Minimum validation property set to 25, the Maximum validation property set to 500, and the Hide if property set to a FEEL expression enforcing the multiple-of-25 rule at render time
The Decimal digits property set to 0, the Default value property set to 25, and the Increment property set to 25, with no Minimum or Maximum validation entries configured on the field

a.text: Correct. The Configurable properties list Increment as the step between valid field values and the Validation section names Minimum and Maximum as the per-value bounds. Setting Increment to 25, Minimum to 25, and Maximum to 500 captures all three constraints declaratively.

d.text: Incorrect. Decimal digits controls digits after the decimal, not value bounds, and Default value only pre-seeds the field; without Minimum and Maximum the dosage range itself is not enforced at submit time.

c.text: Incorrect. Hide if controls field visibility, not validation, and Minimum and Maximum alone do not enforce the increment-of-25 step; Increment is the documented property for the step between valid values.

b.text: Incorrect. Required controls whether the field can be left empty, the field label has no validation effect, and Default value does not enforce the increment-of-25 rule on the dosage at submit time.

explanation: The Number element enforces step and bounds declaratively: Increment defines the valid step between values, while Validation.Minimum and Validation.Maximum define the inclusive bounds. Together they reject any submitted value outside 25, 50, 75, ..., 500 on the field.

scenario: Orbis Bank onboards 18000 customers per month through a Camunda 8.8 form that uses a Select dropdown bound to the variable accountType. The options source lists checking, savings, and premium, but the upstream service occasionally seeds the task with the legacy string current. The team needs to understand how the rendered Select behaves within 2 seconds of the customer opening the task when the inbound value does not match any of the configured options.
question: What happens when the inbound variable value does not match any option defined in the Select's options source?

options.text: The Select raises a runtime validation error during render that blocks the customer task from being claimed until the value is corrected
The Select coerces the value into a new option on the fly so the original variable still appears as the selected entry for the customer
The Select pre-selects the first option in the options source so the form always renders with a single visible selection for the customer
The Select treats the value as unmatched and ignores it, leaving no option pre-selected when the form renders for the customer

d.text: Correct. The Select correlates the bound data value with the options defined in the options source, and if no option is found the data is simply ignored, so the dropdown renders with nothing selected.

c.text: Incorrect. Auto-selecting the first option would silently change the customer's data, which is not how the Select handles an unmatched value. The Default value property only applies when no input data exists at all and only for static options sources.

a.text: Incorrect. The Select does not block rendering on an unmatched value. Validation rules such as Required check submission, not the inbound value, so the task remains claimable even when no option matches.

b.text: Incorrect. The Select does not synthesize new options at runtime. Options come only from the configured options source, so an unmatched value cannot materialise as a selected entry in the dropdown.

explanation: A Select correlates the bound data value with the options provided by its options source. When no matching option is found, the inbound data is simply ignored and the dropdown renders with no selection, which is why the Default value property exists only for static sources and only when no input data is present.

scenario: Quanta Telco's Camunda 8.8 self-service portal renders a Select dropdown for plan choice on 18000 user tasks per day, with the option list provided by an input-data options source that fetches the catalogue from a connector. The team wants the dropdown to pre-select STANDARD when no input value exists, and the developer asks why the Default value field is greyed out in the Select properties panel.
question: Why is the Default value field greyed out for that Camunda 8.8 Select configuration?

options.text: The Default value field is only available when the Key is bound to a string variable, and the team has bound this Select to a variable typed as any in the panel
The Default value field is only available when the Select is configured with a static options source, and this Select uses an input-data options source instead of a static one
The Default value field is only available when the Select has Searchable disabled, and this configuration has the Searchable toggle enabled in the properties panel
The Default value field is only available when the Select is marked Required, and the team has not enabled the Required validation entry on this Select in the panel

b.text: Correct. The Configurable properties section explicitly states that Default value is only available for static options sources, so input-data and expression-driven options sources disable the field in the properties panel of the Select.

d.text: Incorrect. The availability of the Default value field is not tied to the Required validation toggle; the documented gating is whether the options source is static.

c.text: Incorrect. The Searchable property controls keyboard search behaviour and does not gate the availability of the Default value field in the properties panel.

a.text: Incorrect. The Key binding accepts any data per the Datatypes section; the Default value gating is driven by the options source kind, not by the bound variable type.

explanation: Default value on a Select is only exposed when the options source is static. Input-data or expression-driven options sources hide the Default value field, since the runtime cannot guarantee a stable option list at design time against which to pre-select a value.

scenario: Within Stellar Bank's onboarding form, a Camunda 8.8 Select dropdown is bound to the variable currencyCode and offers seven static options (USD, EUR, GBP, JPY, AUD, CAD, CHF) for a daily volume of 4500 customers per day. The upstream process initialises currencyCode to MXN, which is not one of the configured options, before the form renders for the customer in Tasklist.
question: How does the Camunda 8.8 Select runtime treat the currencyCode value MXN when it renders the form for the customer?

options.text: It auto-adds MXN as a new dynamic option on first render so the customer can keep the original currency without the modeler needing to update the options source manually
It ignores the unmatched data because MXN does not correlate with any option from the configured options source, so the Select renders without any entry pre-selected
It strips the variable from the task scope and rebinds currencyCode to the first option in the source list so the customer always sees a deterministic default selection
It rejects the form render and surfaces a validation error so the customer cannot continue until the upstream variable is corrected to one of the known options at runtime

b.text: Correct. The Datatypes section states that the Select correlates the data value with the appropriate option defined in the options source and that if no option is found the data is simply ignored, leaving the dropdown without a pre-selection.

a.text: Incorrect. The Select does not augment the options source dynamically with unknown values; the unmatched value is silently ignored rather than appearing as a new option in the dropdown.

d.text: Incorrect. The documented behaviour for an unmatched value is to ignore it, not to block form rendering or surface a validation error at render time.

c.text: Incorrect. The runtime does not modify the bound variable or auto-rebind it to another option; it simply ignores the unmatched value when correlating against the options source.

explanation: Select correlates the bound variable with the values exposed by the options source. When no matching option exists, the data is silently ignored and the dropdown renders without a pre-selected entry, leaving the customer free to pick a valid choice.

scenario: Inside Vertex Logistics's Camunda 8.8 dispatch form, a Select bound to driverId offers a list of 250 active drivers and the modeler must guarantee that the form cannot be submitted until the dispatcher has chosen exactly one driver. The flow processes 800 tasks per day and the team is wiring the validation rule on the Select element in the properties panel.
question: Which validation property must the modeler set on the Select to block form submission until a driver is chosen?

options.text: The Minimum length property, set to 1, which enforces a non-empty selection by counting at least one character in the bound driverId variable at submit time
A FEEL expression in the Hide if property, configured to hide the submit button until the driverId variable resolves to a non-null option at form render time in Tasklist
The Pattern validation property, populated with a FEEL regex that matches any non-blank string, which fails submission when the bound driverId value is empty at submit time
The Required validation property, which forces the form to refuse submission until exactly one Select entry has been selected by the dispatcher at runtime in Tasklist

d.text: Correct. The Validation section of the Select component names a single Required property, which states that one select entry must be selected before the form is allowed to submit, exactly matching the dispatcher's requirement.

a.text: Incorrect. Minimum length is a string-field validation property, not part of the Select element's documented validation set; Select only exposes Required for the validation step.

c.text: Incorrect. Pattern validation is not documented for the Select element; the Configurable properties list only Required under Validation for this component in the panel.

b.text: Incorrect. Hide if controls visibility of the Select, not the validity of its value; it does not gate form submission based on whether a driver has been chosen at submit time.

explanation: Select's documented validation set contains exactly one entry: Required, which states that one select entry must be selected. Other validations such as Minimum length or Pattern belong to other elements, not to the Select component in the form element library.

scenario: Helix Insurance, an insurer, reviews 4500 claims per quarter through a Camunda 8.8 form. The adjuster task embeds a Table component that should render the line items prepared by the upstream service, and the team wants to bind the table to a FEEL list of claim rows so the adjuster sees the data within 3 seconds. The forms developer is configuring the Data source property in the properties panel.
question: Which configuration is required for the Data source property of a Table in the Camunda 8.8 form?

options.text: The Data source accepts plain text, a static list literal, or an expression returning the rows that the table will render at runtime
The Data source can be either a static array of rows entered into the properties panel or an expression returning rows at runtime
The Data source can only be an expression, which is evaluated to produce the list of rows that the table will render at runtime
The Data source can be a templating syntax block that resolves at submission time and supplies the list of rows to the table

a.text: Incorrect. The Table's Data source does not accept plain text or static literals. Unlike the Table label, which accepts expressions, plain text, or templating syntax, the Data source is restricted to an expression.

c.text: Correct. The Table documentation states that the Data source can only be an expression, which is evaluated to produce the rows that the table renders. Static literals and templating syntax are not accepted for this property.

b.text: Incorrect. Static lists are accepted for the Headers source, not the Data source. The Data source is restricted to an expression and is evaluated at runtime against the form variables.

d.text: Incorrect. Templating syntax is supported for the Table label, not the Data source. The Data source must be a FEEL expression that returns the list of rows that the table will render.

explanation: Among the Table's configurable properties, the Data source is unique in that it can only be an expression. Headers source can be an expression or a static list, and the Table label can be plain text, an expression, or templating syntax, but rows must come from a FEEL expression evaluated at runtime.

scenario: Borealis Bank publishes a Camunda 8.8 onboarding form viewed by 9000 customers per month. The product team wants the Text component at the top of the form to render a short greeting that mixes a bold heading with a couple of bullet points pulled from a FEEL expression, and they need to confirm how the Text component processes its content before the customer sees the rendered output within 2 seconds.
question: How is the Text component's content processed before being shown to the user in the rendered form?

options.text: The content is processed by a Markdown renderer that supports basic HTML and GitHub-flavored Markdown while filtering harmful HTML
The content is converted to plain text only, stripping every HTML tag and Markdown construct before being displayed in the form
The content is rendered verbatim as raw HTML so any inline tags or scripts inside the expression result appear exactly as written
The content is forwarded to the BPMN engine for evaluation and the rendered string is returned through a job worker before display

c.text: Incorrect. The Text component does not render arbitrary raw HTML. Potentially harmful HTML elements are filtered out to prevent cross-site scripting, so scripts inside the expression result will not be rendered.

b.text: Incorrect. The Text component preserves Markdown and a basic subset of HTML. It does not strip every tag and Markdown construct before display, which is why bold, italics, and lists render correctly.

a.text: Correct. After the expression, plain text, or templating syntax is evaluated, the result is processed by a Markdown renderer that supports basic HTML and GitHub-flavored Markdown while filtering harmful HTML to prevent cross-site scripting.

d.text: Incorrect. The Text component is rendered client-side in the form. It does not call out to the BPMN engine or a job worker to evaluate its content before display.

explanation: The Text component evaluates its Text property as an expression, plain text, or templating syntax, then runs the result through a Markdown renderer. The renderer supports basic HTML and GitHub-flavored Markdown while stripping potentially harmful HTML to prevent cross-site scripting attacks inside Camunda Forms.

scenario: Aster Logistics processes 5500 shipments per week and embeds an instructions banner inside its Camunda 8.8 driver intake form. The product team wants the banner to combine a sub-heading, a styled image, and a structured layout that goes well beyond bold and bullet points, and they expect drivers to read the daily handover rules within 2 minutes of opening the task. The forms developer is deciding between the Text component and the dedicated HTML component.
question: When should the team reach for the dedicated HTML component instead of the Text component in the Camunda 8.8 forms palette?

options.text: When the team wants to render the same bold and italic Markdown that the Text component already supports out of the box natively
When the team needs to embed two clickable buttons inside the banner so the driver can navigate between several pages of the form
When the team needs a presentation use case that relies heavily on HTML markup beyond the small subset of HTML supported by the Text component
When the team needs to bind the banner content to a FEEL expression rather than rely on a static text literal in the properties panel

b.text: Incorrect. Navigation buttons are provided by the dedicated Button element in the forms palette, not by switching from the Text component to the HTML component. The choice between Text and HTML is about presentation richness.

a.text: Incorrect. Bold and italic Markdown are already rendered by the Text component out of the box, so there is no reason to switch components for that styling. The HTML component exists for richer presentation use cases.

d.text: Incorrect. The Text component already accepts an expression, plain text, or templating syntax for its Text property, so binding to a FEEL expression is not a reason to switch to the HTML component.

c.text: Correct. The Text component documentation notes that it supports only a small subset of HTML and cannot be styled, and explicitly directs teams to the dedicated HTML component for presentation use cases that rely heavily on HTML.

explanation: The Text component is intended for lightweight Markdown content with a basic subset of HTML and cannot be styled. When a form needs presentation richness driven primarily by HTML markup, the docs explicitly redirect the developer to the dedicated HTML component instead of the Text component.

scenario: Granite SaaS embeds a Camunda 8.8 form into a self-service onboarding flow handling 7500 tenants per quarter. A developer wires a Text component whose Text property points to a FEEL expression that returns a string containing bold Markdown guidance plus an embedded promotional iframe sourced from a third-party host, gated by a Hide if expression when tenantTier equals free.
question: How does the Camunda 8.8 Text element render that mixed Markdown and iframe content at runtime?

options.text: It evaluates the FEEL expression and renders the resulting string as raw HTML, embedding the iframe verbatim alongside the bold Markdown that the developer authored in the source
It evaluates the FEEL expression, passes the result through the Markdown renderer with basic HTML support, and strips the iframe as a potentially harmful HTML element before rendering
It rejects the form at render time because Text components forbid expressions and only accept literal Markdown strings in the schema for the bound Text property at design time
It evaluates the FEEL expression and renders the result as plain text only, ignoring the bold Markdown markup and stripping the iframe before displaying the result in the rendered form

b.text: Correct. The Text property accepts an expression and the evaluated result is processed by a Markdown renderer that supports basic HTML. To prevent cross-site scripting in Camunda Forms, potentially harmful HTML elements such as iframes are not rendered.

a.text: Incorrect. The renderer does not pass arbitrary HTML through unfiltered; the documentation states that potentially harmful HTML elements are dropped before rendering to prevent cross-site scripting in Camunda Forms.

d.text: Incorrect. Bold Markdown and other basic Markdown plus a subset of HTML are supported; the renderer does not downgrade the content to plain text, only filters out unsafe HTML such as iframes.

c.text: Incorrect. The Text property accepts an expression, plain text, or templating syntax as its source; expressions are explicitly listed as a valid option in the Configurable properties section.

explanation: The Text component evaluates an expression, plain-text, or templating-syntax source, then runs the result through a Markdown renderer that also supports basic HTML. To prevent XSS, harmful elements such as iframes are stripped while safe formatting like bold and lists are kept.

scenario: Junction Marketplace personalises a Camunda 8.8 Tasklist welcome form for 9200 customers per week, where the Text component must greet the user by name, list 3 to 5 hobby tags, and only render the whole block when a boolean variable usingTemplating is true. The developer is choosing the right syntax for the conditional, the variable interpolation, and the iteration inside the Text property.
question: Which template syntax does the Camunda 8.8 Text component require to express that personalised greeting?

options.text: A handlebars-style block wrapping the greeting with {{#if usingTemplating}} and {{/if}}, the user name interpolated as {{user.name}}, and the hobby tags iterated with {{#loop user.hobbies}} {{this}} {{/loop}}

An HTML-only template using <if cond='usingTemplating'> with nested <for each='user.hobbies'> elements, with custom HTML directives providing the templating rather than the Camunda templating language

A Jinja-style template with {% if usingTemplating %}, {{ user.name }}, and {% for hobby in user.hobbies %} loops, treating Jinja blocks as the documented templating engine for Camunda 8.8 Text components
A FEEL expression in the Text field combining if-then-else with for hobby in user.hobbies returning a Markdown string, with FEEL serving as the templating language for the Text component

a.text: Correct. The documented template syntax for the Text component uses {{#if usingTemplating}} ... {{/if}} for conditionals, {{user.name}} for variable expansion, and {{#loop user.hobbies}} ... {{this}} ... {{/loop}} for iteration over a list, exactly as shown in the example.

b.text: Incorrect. HTML directives such as <if> and <for> are not part of the Text component template syntax; the documented template uses handlebars-style double-brace markers, not custom HTML tags for control flow.

d.text: Incorrect. The Text component documents a handlebars-style templating syntax for the personalised content example, distinct from a FEEL expression with for/in comprehensions inside the Text field.

c.text: Incorrect. The documented templating syntax uses double-brace handlebars-style markers, not Jinja-style {% %} blocks; the example explicitly shows {{#if}}, {{#loop}}, {{this}}, and {{/loop}} forms.

explanation: The Text component supports a templating syntax using handlebars-style markers: {{#if cond}} ... {{/if}} for conditionals, {{path.to.variable}} for interpolation, and {{#loop list}} ... {{this}} ... {{/loop}} for iteration. The example in the docs uses precisely this syntax.

scenario: Polar Retailer, an e-commerce retailer that fulfils 6000 orders per week, has just spun up a Camunda 8.8 SaaS project. A new developer is asked to build a returns intake form from scratch in Web Modeler within 2 hours, and the team lead asks her to follow the documented quickstart so the form is consistent with the other intake forms used across the retailer.
question: Which approach to building a brand new form is documented in the Camunda 8.8 quickstart for Web Modeler?

options.text: The developer writes a JSON schema describing each field and uploads the file into Web Modeler so the form renders on the canvas
The developer records a wizard session in Operate and the captured field metadata is exported as a new form into Web Modeler
The developer imports an existing Camunda 7 form definition and the modeler converts it into a Camunda 8 form on the canvas
The developer drags elements from the palette onto the canvas, or alternatively uses the AI Form Generator at the bottom of the palette

d.text: Correct. The quickstart says the developer can start building by dragging elements from the palette onto the canvas, or by using the AI Form Generator at the bottom of the palette. Both are documented entry points to authoring a fresh form.

a.text: Incorrect. The quickstart does not describe uploading a JSON schema to materialise a form. Forms are authored on the canvas by dragging from the palette or by invoking the AI Form Generator.

c.text: Incorrect. There is no Camunda 7 form import flow documented in the quickstart. New forms are built on the canvas inside Web Modeler from scratch or via the AI Form Generator.

b.text: Incorrect. Operate is the runtime monitoring tool and is not used to design forms. The quickstart anchors the authoring workflow in Web Modeler's palette and canvas.

explanation: The Camunda 8.8 quickstart for forms walks the developer through creating a new form in Web Modeler and then authoring it by dragging elements from the palette onto the canvas. The same palette also exposes the AI Form Generator as an alternative starting point for building the form.

scenario: Quanta Insurance, an insurer that issues 6000 policies per quarter, runs a Camunda 8.8 claim intake form in Web Modeler. The forms team edits the layout multiple times per day, and the team lead wants the latest revision available to underwriters within 1 minute of any change. A new joiner asks the team lead how the developer is supposed to persist edits to the form.
question: What does a developer need to do to save changes made to a form in Web Modeler?

options.text: The developer presses Ctrl+S after each change and waits for the save confirmation toast before continuing to edit the form in Web Modeler
The developer does not need to take any extra action because Web Modeler autosaves every change made to the form as it is edited
The developer exports the form as JSON and uploads it back into the project so the cloud storage retains the latest revision of the file
The developer commits the form to a Git branch from inside Web Modeler so the latest revision is preserved alongside the BPMN diagram

a.text: Incorrect. There is no explicit Save action documented for forms in Web Modeler. The docs state that the developer does not need to do anything because every change is autosaved by Web Modeler as it is edited.

b.text: Correct. The quickstart states that to save a form in Camunda 8, the developer does not have to do anything because Web Modeler autosaves every change made. The latest revision is always the one rendered to underwriters at runtime.

d.text: Incorrect. Web Modeler does not require a manual Git commit to persist a form. The autosave behaviour persists every change made to the form as it is edited.

c.text: Incorrect. Exporting and re-uploading the form is not part of the documented save flow. Web Modeler autosaves every change, so the cloud project always reflects the latest revision.

explanation: The Camunda 8.8 quickstart explicitly states that to save a form in Web Modeler the developer does not have to take any action. Web Modeler autosaves every change made to the form, so the latest revision is always available without an explicit save step or external persistence mechanism.

scenario: Nordic Logistics ships 4200 orders per day and is standardising its driver acceptance task across all branches on Camunda 8.8 Forms. A new forms developer is preparing a checklist of the element categories explicitly documented in the quickstart so the team only uses palette items the docs name, and the team lead wants the checklist ready within 1 hour.
question: Which set of element categories does the Camunda 8.8 forms quickstart explicitly document as available in the palette?

options.text: Text fields, numerical values, image carousels, video players, chart widgets, and embedded maps available inside the form palette
Text fields, password fields, file upload widgets, signature pads, audio recorders, and barcode scanners listed inside the form palette
Text fields, numerical values, checkboxes, radio elements, selection menus, text components, and buttons listed inside the form palette
Text fields, numerical values, checkboxes, radio elements, BPMN tasks, and signal events that can be embedded into the form palette

d.text: Incorrect. BPMN tasks and signal events are diagram constructs, not form elements. The quickstart enumerates only form element categories such as text fields, numerical values, checkboxes, radio elements, selection menus, text components, and buttons.

b.text: Incorrect. The quickstart does not list password fields, signature pads, audio recorders, or barcode scanners. Those are not part of the documented set of palette categories for Camunda 8.8 Forms.

c.text: Correct. The quickstart names exactly this set of element categories available in Forms: text fields, numerical values, checkboxes, radio elements, selection menus, text components, and buttons. The element library reference covers each in detail.

a.text: Incorrect. Image carousels, video players, chart widgets, and embedded maps are not documented palette categories. The quickstart's catalogue is centred on form input controls and basic content components.

explanation: The quickstart in the utilizing-forms page enumerates the palette categories available to the form designer: text fields, numerical values, checkboxes, radio elements, selection menus, text components, and buttons. The Camunda Forms reference is where each of these categories is described in detail.

scenario: Helix Telco, a telco operator, runs 5500 instances per week of a service activation diagram on a Camunda 8.3 cluster and links a Camunda 8.8 Forms user task form to the human approval step. The team expects the deployment to keep working for the next 6 months without forcing a cluster upgrade, and they want to know what happens to the linked form when they click Deploy from Web Modeler against the older cluster.
question: What happens to a form linked to a user task when the diagram is deployed to a Camunda cluster with a version lower than 8.4?

options.text: The form is automatically embedded into the user task by the deployment so the older cluster keeps backwards compatibility for that form at runtime
The form is converted into a Camunda 7 form definition and stored alongside the diagram so the older runtime can render the user task to operators
The form is rejected by the deployment endpoint because pre-8.4 clusters cannot read the linked form resource so the diagram fails to deploy
The form is uploaded as a separate resource and the user task fetches it from the Tasklist API the first time that task is claimed by the operator

c.text: Incorrect. Deployment to a pre-8.4 cluster is not rejected for linked forms. The runtime handles the version gap by automatically embedding the form into the user task to keep backwards compatibility.

d.text: Incorrect. The Tasklist API does not perform a separate fetch for linked forms on pre-8.4 clusters. The form is embedded into the user task at deployment time instead.

b.text: Incorrect. There is no automatic conversion to a Camunda 7 form definition. The Camunda 8 form is embedded into the user task to keep the older cluster compatible.

a.text: Correct. When the target cluster is older than 8.4, forms linked to user tasks or none start events are automatically embedded into the user task during deployment so the older runtime can still resolve and render them, preserving backwards compatibility.

explanation: Linked forms are normally deployed alongside the diagram and resolved to their latest version at runtime. When the target cluster version is lower than 8.4, the deployment automatically embeds forms linked to user tasks or none start events into the user task itself to guarantee backwards compatibility with the older runtime.

scenario: Vanta Health, a hospital network processing 4500 intake forms per day, builds a Camunda 8.8 form where patients upload an insurance card image at intake and a follow-up form where 200 clinicians download the discharge summary PDF within 5 minutes of completion.
question: Which pair of form components does the Next steps section recommend for these two distinct file flows?

options.text: Use the Filepicker component on both forms because it handles uploads as well as displaying and downloading documents in a single component for any flow
Use the document preview component on the intake form for uploads and the Filepicker component on the follow-up form for display and download
Use the document preview component on both forms because it handles displaying, downloading, and uploading documents in a single component for any flow
Use the Filepicker component on the intake form for uploads and the document preview component on the follow-up form for display and download

d.text: Correct. The Next steps section directs readers to the Filepicker form component for document upload flows and to the document preview component for displaying and allowing document download from a form.

b.text: Incorrect. The component roles are reversed: the document preview component displays and downloads documents, and the Filepicker component handles uploads, not the other way around.

a.text: Incorrect. The Filepicker is documented for allowing users to upload files; the Next steps section points readers to the document preview component for display and download flows.

c.text: Incorrect. The document preview component is documented for displaying and downloading documents; the Next steps section points readers to the Filepicker component for upload flows.

explanation: The Next steps section of the utilizing forms guide pairs two purpose-built components: the Filepicker form component for allowing users to upload files, and the document preview component for displaying documents and allowing document download from a form.

scenario: Helios Insurance is rolling out 12 new claims-intake forms across 3 regions and the modeling team wants junior developers to author each form within 30 minutes using only Web Modeler. Each developer logs in to a Camunda 8 account, opens the assigned project from the Web Modeler home view, and now needs to bootstrap a brand-new Camunda 8.8 form before dragging any elements onto the canvas.
question: What step does each developer take next to create a new Camunda 8.8 form in Web Modeler?

options.text: They open the File menu in Desktop Modeler from inside Web Modeler and pick New Form, which links the form to the active Desktop Modeler workspace
They invoke the AI Form Generator from the project home view, which generates and saves a blank form before any canvas is opened in the editor
They open any existing BPMN diagram, select a user task, and click the blue link overlay, which scaffolds a new form bound to that user task
They click Create new in the project view and choose Form from the resource list, which opens a blank form canvas ready for elements to be dragged onto it

d.text: Correct. To start building a form, log in to Camunda 8 (or open Desktop Modeler), open any project from the Web Modeler home view, click Create new, and choose Form, which is the documented entry point for a brand-new form.

a.text: Incorrect. The File menu is the Desktop Modeler entry point used as an alternative to Web Modeler, not a sub-menu reached from within the Web Modeler project view, so it does not produce a new form linked to a Desktop workspace.

c.text: Incorrect. The blue link overlay on a user task is the documented way to link an existing form to a BPMN diagram, not the way to scaffold a brand-new form from scratch.

b.text: Incorrect. The AI Form Generator lives at the bottom of the palette, which appears only after a form has already been created, so it cannot bootstrap the form itself from the project home view.

explanation: The Quickstart documents the entry point for a new form as: open a project from the Web Modeler home view, click Create new, and choose Form. The palette (with elements and the AI Form Generator) and form linking from BPMN are post-creation steps, not entry points.

scenario: Within Aurora Bank's loan onboarding flow, developers embed a Camunda 8.8 form that contains a custom submit button into a process averaging 1500 user tasks per day.
