A new developer asks the team lead what happens to that submit button at runtime when the linked form is rendered for a user task in Tasklist.
question: What does the Camunda 8.8 runtime do with a submit button declared inside a Camunda Forms schema linked to a user task?

options.text: It renders the submit button as defined but ignores its click event, leaving task completion to the standard Tasklist complete control
It promotes the custom submit button to the Tasklist toolbar so the user can trigger completion from either the form or the toolbar
It rejects deployment of the linked form because user-task forms must not declare a submit button in their schema before deployment
It hides the submit button at render time so the engine can control the moment the user task is allowed to complete from the Tasklist UI

d.text: Correct. The Quickstart note states that any submit button present in the form schema is hidden when using Camunda Forms, so the platform can control exactly when a user is allowed to complete the task.

a.text: Incorrect. The submit button is not merely silenced behind the scenes; the Quickstart says it is hidden from the rendered form so users only see the Tasklist control for completing the task.

c.text: Incorrect. Forms with a submit button still deploy and run; the documented behaviour is hiding the button at render time, not rejecting the form during deployment.

b.text: Incorrect. Camunda does not surface a per-form submit button in the Tasklist toolbar; task completion is driven by the Tasklist control after the engine signals readiness.

explanation: The Camunda Forms runtime hides any submit button declared in the form schema when the form is linked to a user task. Completion is gated by the engine through the Tasklist UI rather than by a custom button placed inside the form itself.

scenario: Beacon Logistics is migrating from a legacy editor that required manual save, and the Camunda 8.8 forms team is authoring driver-onboarding forms in Web Modeler across 24 engineers. A new engineer asks whether they need to add a 5 minutes save cadence to the project workflow to avoid losing work after each property panel change made on a form element.
question: What does the engineer need to do to persist edits made in the Web Modeler properties panel for each Camunda 8.8 form?

options.text: They do not need to take any action because Web Modeler autosaves every change the engineer makes to the form as it is edited in the project
They click the floppy disk Save button in the top toolbar after every batch of edits to commit the changes to the project storage in Web Modeler
They run a periodic Deploy command, which serves as the save mechanism for forms held inside a Web Modeler project before any explicit publish step
They press a keyboard shortcut after each edit to flush the in-memory form snapshot to the backing Git repository attached to the Web Modeler project

a.text: Correct. The Quickstart explicitly states that to save a form in Camunda 8 the developer does not have to do anything; Web Modeler autosaves every change made in the editor or properties panel, so no manual cadence is needed.

b.text: Incorrect. The Web Modeler form editor relies on autosave; there is no documented manual Save button driving persistence of property panel edits in the Quickstart workflow.

d.text: Incorrect. Forms in a Web Modeler project are persisted by autosave, not by a developer-initiated flush to a Git repository attached to the project.

c.text: Incorrect. Deployment makes a form available to a process at runtime; it is a separate step from persisting the in-progress form edits inside Web Modeler.

explanation: Camunda 8 Web Modeler autosaves every change made to a form, so the documented workflow does not require any explicit save action. Deployment is a separate step for publishing the form to a cluster, not for persisting in-progress edits in the editor.

scenario: Beacon Airlines deploys a Camunda 8.8 booking form that handles 10000 transactions per day. The team uses a shorthand FEEL list with three entries to populate a currency Select field and asks which value types are allowed when no per-entry label is supplied, since invalid entries would block 50 jobs per minute.
question: Which value types are allowed for an option in the shorthand format when no label is provided per entry?

options.text: Any non-null type, including nested objects and arrays, is acceptable for the value when the label is omitted from the shorthand option definition
Without a label, the value of a shorthand option is restricted to the types string, number, and boolean, so the value itself can double as the displayed label
Only enums declared on the surrounding BPMN model are accepted, with the form runtime cross-checking the value against the deployed process definition
Only string is allowed for the value when the label is absent, with the field rendering the value as text and unable to derive a label from any other type

d.text: Incorrect. The documented set is broader than just string; number and boolean are also accepted when no per-entry label is provided in the shorthand form.

b.text: Correct. If a label is not provided, the value parameter is restricted to the types string, number, and boolean, as documented for shorthand options.

a.text: Incorrect. Any non-null type is only allowed when a label is provided; without a label the type set is restricted to string, number, and boolean.

c.text: Incorrect. The form runtime does not consult the BPMN model for permitted values; the constraint comes from the shorthand options shape itself.

explanation: When the shorthand options format is used and no label is supplied per entry, the value parameter is restricted to the types string, number, and boolean.

scenario: Aurora Outdoors sends an order confirmation summary to 4500 customers per month through a Camunda 8.8 templated email task that uses Feelers. The data carries a top-level currency field and an items array, where each item has a name and a price property, and the template must render one line per item showing the item name and the currency-prefixed price.
question: Which Feelers template correctly renders the per-item lines using the documented loop and accessor rules?

options.text: A template with an if block over items that references the name and the currency through the parent accessor on every iteration of the items array
A template with a loop over items that references the item name as this.name and the price as parent.currency joined to price on each rendered line
A template with a loop over items that references the item name as name and the outer currency as parent.currency joined to price on each rendered line
A template with a loop over items that references the currency as this.currency and the name as parent.name on each rendered line of the output

b.text: Incorrect. Inside a loop the object properties are referenced directly by their key, so using this.name introduces an unnecessary accessor that does not match the documented pattern for object items.

c.text: Correct. Inside a loop, object properties are referenced by their key directly, and data outside the item scope, such as the top-level currency, is reached through the parent accessor.

a.text: Incorrect. The if tag conditionally renders a block, but iteration over an array requires the loop tag, so the per-item lines would not repeat with an if construct.

d.text: Incorrect. Within the loop, currency lives in the outer scope and must be reached through parent, while name belongs to the current item and is referenced directly.

explanation: Feelers loops expose object properties of the current item by their key. To reach data outside the item scope, such as the top-level currency, the template uses the parent accessor, so name resolves to the item property and parent.currency reaches the outer field.

scenario: Nimbus Cartography exports 6000 nodes per release into a Camunda 8.8 Feelers template that renders documentation URLs. Each node has an id and a parent property pointing to its tree parent, while the top-level root field holds the shared URL prefix. The template loops over nodes and must include the top-level root in every URL, not the per-node parent value.
question: How must the loop body reference the top-level root so the per-node parent property does not shadow the outer scope accessor?

options.text: The loop body references the top-level value as this.root, treating this as a synonym for the enclosing data when no explicit scope is set in the loop
The loop body references the top-level value as parent.root, treating a single parent accessor as a walk one level up from the current node in the data
The loop body references the top-level value as _parent_.root, using the documented underscore form to bypass the per-node parent property of each item
The loop body references the top-level value as parent.parent.root, chaining two parent accessors to traverse past the node's own parent property safely

b.text: Incorrect. Because the current node already has its own parent property, the bare parent accessor resolves to that property value rather than to the outer scope, breaking the URL.

a.text: Incorrect. The this accessor refers to the current loop item, not to the enclosing data, so this.root would look for a root property on the node and not find one.

c.text: Correct. When the data already uses the parent or this keyword, the documented alternative is to wrap the accessor with underscores as _parent_ or _this_ so the scope accessor wins over the property.

d.text: Incorrect. Chaining parent twice does not address the shadowing problem and is not the documented remedy; the first parent still resolves to the node property.

explanation: When the data already contains keys named parent or this, the bare accessors collide with those properties. The documented alternative form surrounds the accessor with underscores, so _parent_.root reaches the top-level field even when each node carries its own parent value.

docs.title: Forms templating - parent and this accessors

scenario: Forge Government tracks 4000 records per week on Camunda 8.8 Forms. Each node carries a property literally named parent, and the template loops over 25 nodes while also needing to read a root property on the surrounding scope, without the accessor colliding with the node's own parent field within 200 ms per render.
question: Which Feelers syntax avoids the collision when iterated data already uses a property literally named parent?

options.text: Wrapping the loop in an if block and reading the surrounding value through a separate FEEL expression evaluated before the loop opens its iteration body
Renaming the field at the BPMN data input mapping level so the conflicting property is removed from the node objects before the form template is rendered
Using the underscore-wrapped form of the parent accessor inside the loop body, which makes the accessor reach the surrounding scope instead of the node's own parent property
Using parent.parent inside the loop body so the template skips one level above the node and reaches the surrounding scope above the iterated array

b.text: Incorrect. The collision is resolved at the template level by the underscore accessor; rewriting the data through input mappings is not the documented mechanism.

d.text: Incorrect. parent.parent would still resolve the inner parent property of the node first, so the collision is not avoided this way.

c.text: Correct. When the data already uses parent or this as a key, the alternative syntax surrounds the accessor with underscores, so _parent_ reaches the surrounding scope.

a.text: Incorrect. Splitting the template into an if-and-loop pattern does not address the conflict on the parent identifier inside the loop body itself.

explanation: Feelers offers an alternative accessor syntax that surrounds parent or this with underscores, so _parent_ and _this_ reach the surrounding scope or current item without colliding with data fields of the same name.

scenario: Beacon Foods captures a quantity for 60 orders per day inside a Camunda 8.8 number field bound to the key qty. The source variable can arrive either as a JSON number or as a string of digits, and the team needs to know which datatype the field will emit by default so that 3 downstream FEEL expressions can rely on a stable shape.
question: What is the documented output datatype of the number field when no extra serialization property is configured on it?

options.text: The field outputs the value as a string of digits using JavaScript-style parsing rules so downstream FEEL can apply its own numeric coercion later in the flow
The field outputs the value as a decimal number whenever any digits after the decimal are entered, preserving the precision the operator typed into the input
The field outputs the value strictly as integer data, parsing string inputs through JavaScript tryParse before emitting the integer to downstream consumers of the form
The field outputs the value as the original incoming type, mirroring whether the source was a string of digits or a JSON number with no coercion of the data

d.text: Incorrect. The number field does not preserve the source type; it coerces inputs through tryParse and standardises the output, so a string source does not remain a string.

a.text: Incorrect. String-of-digits is not the documented output shape; the field always emits a strict integer rather than passing the raw textual form through to consumers.

c.text: Correct. The documentation states that the number field accepts numeric or parseable string input via JavaScript tryParse and always outputs strictly integer data by default.

b.text: Incorrect. Without extra serialization, the default output is strictly integer data; decimal entries are not preserved in their decimal form by the default emission path.

explanation: By default, a number field accepts numeric input or strings parseable through JavaScript tryParse, but the runtime always emits strictly integer data unless additional serialization is configured to change the output shape.

scenario: Orion Fintech submits 600 records per month from a Camunda 8.8 compliance form. A developer proposes wiring two dynamic lists in the same form to the same path contacts so 50 entries from each list end up combined under one array of objects in the process variables payload.
question: What does the documented data binding behaviour say about two dynamic lists sharing the same path?

options.text: The runtime stores the entries as a nested object whose keys are the dynamic list identifiers and whose values are the individual row arrays
The runtime appends entries from both lists into one array at the shared path, preserving the order in which each list was rendered on the form
Dynamic lists cannot share paths with other dynamic lists or groups, as the data binding contract treats each list as the sole owner of its array root
Tasklist asks the operator at submission time which of the two dynamic lists owns the contacts path for that particular instance and routes accordingly

b.text: Incorrect. The runtime does not merge two dynamic lists onto the same path; sharing is disallowed because each list is bound to an array at its own path.

c.text: Correct. Since dynamic lists are bound to arrays, they cannot share paths with other dynamic lists or groups, and this constraint is part of the data binding contract.

a.text: Incorrect. The runtime does not silently convert two lists into a nested object; the documented constraint is that two dynamic lists cannot share a path at all.

d.text: Incorrect. Path ownership is fixed at design time by the form schema, not negotiated at submission time, and lists cannot share the same path.

explanation: Dynamic lists are bound to arrays and directly own the array under their path, so the documented rule is that they cannot share paths with other dynamic lists or with groups.

scenario: Aurora Insurance issues 1800 policies per week through a Camunda 8.8 renewal form. A checkbox bound to the key autoRenew is wired by mistake to incoming process data where autoRenew carries the string value yes rather than a boolean, and the team needs to predict the checkbox state when the form opens for the operator.
question: What state does the checkbox render when the bound key holds a value that is not a boolean?

options.text: The checkbox renders in the checked state, mapping any non-empty incoming value to a true state on the initial form load for the operator
The checkbox renders the raw string inline next to the label and freezes the toggle until the operator replaces the value with a boolean
The checkbox blocks form load with a validation error and requires the bound key to carry a strict boolean value before the form can mount
The checkbox renders in the unchecked state, treating any datatype other than boolean as a false value by default at the time of form load

a.text: Incorrect. The runtime does not perform a truthy coercion of non-empty values; it treats non-boolean inputs as false rather than mapping them to a checked state.

b.text: Incorrect. The checkbox does not render raw string values beside the label; non-boolean data is silently coerced rather than displayed in place.

c.text: Incorrect. A non-boolean value is not a validation error at load; the form mounts and the checkbox simply renders unchecked using the default behaviour.

d.text: Correct. Checkboxes bind to boolean data, and the documentation states that any other datatype is treated as a false value by default, so the box renders unchecked.

explanation: The checkbox element is bound to boolean data. If the incoming value for the bound key is any other datatype, the runtime treats the value as false by default, so the field renders in the unchecked state without raising a validation error.

scenario: Cobalt Banking computes a service fee inside a Camunda 8.8 payments form for 950 transactions per day using an expression field bound to the key fee. The Target value holds a FEEL expression that aggregates two other form inputs, and the team needs the computed value to refresh live every time any contributing input changes rather than waiting for the operator to submit.
question: Which Compute on configuration must the team pick on the expression field to satisfy the live recalculation requirement?

options.text: The option that evaluates the Target value expression whenever the result changes, so the computed field updates live as the contributing inputs change
The option that evaluates the Target value expression on form load only, so the computed field carries the initial value through to the submission step
The option that evaluates the Target value expression only on form submission, so the computed field updates a single time at the point of submit
The option that evaluates the Deactivate if expression on every keystroke, so the computed field updates after each deactivation check is run

a.text: Correct. The Compute on property documents two values: whenever the result changes or only on form submission. The first matches the requirement to refresh the computed field as contributing inputs change.

c.text: Incorrect. Evaluating only on form submission means the operator sees a stale value during entry, which contradicts the live recalculation requirement described in the scenario.

d.text: Incorrect. Deactivate if is a separate property that gates whether the expression runs at all; it is not a Compute on option and does not drive the evaluation cadence.

b.text: Incorrect. The Compute on property does not offer a form-load-only choice; the documented values are result-change and form-submission, so this configuration is not selectable.

explanation: The expression field exposes a Compute on property with two documented values: evaluate the Target value whenever the result changes, or evaluate only on form submission. The live-update requirement maps to the result-change option.

scenario: Larkfield Realty manages 320 invoices per week through a Camunda 8.8 number field bound to the key invoiceTotal. The team wants the field to accept up to three decimal places, such as 1234.567, and to step up and down by 0.001 each time the operator uses the increment buttons during entry on the form.
question: Which two number field properties must the team configure to allow three decimal places and a 0.001 step size?

options.text: The Maximum property set to three and the Read only property set to 0.001, so the field accepts the decimals and steps by the small configured increment
The Default value property set to 0.001 and the Minimum property set to three, so the field accepts the decimals and steps by the small increment
The Prefix property set to 0.001 and the Suffix property set to three, so the field accepts the decimals and steps by the small configured increment
The Decimal digits property set to three and the Increment property set to 0.001, so the field accepts the decimals and steps by that exact amount

b.text: Incorrect. Default value sets the initial content of the field, and Minimum is a validation lower bound; neither controls the allowed number of decimal places or the step size.

d.text: Correct. Decimal digits defines the maximum digits after the decimal, and Increment defines the increment between valid field values, so three plus 0.001 covers both requirements directly.

c.text: Incorrect. Prefix and Suffix are appearance properties that add visual appendages around the input; they do not configure decimal precision or the increment step.

a.text: Incorrect. Maximum is a validation upper bound and Read only disables editing entirely, neither of which controls how many decimals are accepted or the increment step.

explanation: The Decimal digits property caps how many digits appear after the decimal, and the Increment property sets the step size between valid values, so these two together deliver the three-decimal precision and 0.001 step requested.

scenario: Sable Bank ingests interest rates with 18 digits of precision into a Camunda 8.8 form for a back-office task averaging 600 transactions per day, and the modeler is evaluating the Serialize to string property on the Number element to preserve that precision when writing the bound variable rate at form submission time.
question: What effect does enabling Serialize to string on a Camunda 8.8 Number element have on the value written to the bound variable?

options.text: Enabling Serialize to string forces the value into a 16-digit double-precision float, capping the bound variable at the floating-point precision allowed by the form runtime on submission
Enabling Serialize to string rounds the value to the configured Decimal digits setting before writing the bound variable, dropping any extra precision typed by the user at submit time
Enabling Serialize to string lets the Number element emit the value with unlimited precision digits, configuring the output format used when the runtime writes the bound variable on submission
Enabling Serialize to string converts the value into a localised currency string before writing the bound variable, using the form locale to format the precision shown to the user

c.text: Correct. The Configurable properties section states that Serialize to string configures the output format of the value and enables unlimited precision digits, which is exactly the behaviour the modeler needs to preserve an 18-digit input on the bound variable.

a.text: Incorrect. The documentation describes Serialize to string as unlocking unlimited precision digits, not as constraining the value to a 16-digit double-precision float on output.

b.text: Incorrect. Serialize to string does not couple its behaviour to the Decimal digits setting; its documented effect is to enable unlimited precision digits in the output format of the value.

d.text: Incorrect. The Number element does not perform locale-aware currency formatting; Serialize to string changes the output format to unlock unlimited precision, not to localise the value at the submit step.

explanation: The Serialize to string property on a Number element changes how the runtime emits the bound value, enabling unlimited precision digits in the output. It is the documented mechanism for preserving precision beyond floating-point limits when persisting the variable on submission.

scenario: Ridge Insurance shows a Camunda 8.8 claims-review form to a 4-tier reviewer group spanning 950 weekly reviews, where the Number element approvedAmount must be editable for Tier-1 reviewers but render as read-only for Tier-2 and above. The modeler binds approvedAmount to a process variable and wires the property panel to flip the field's editability dynamically based on the variable reviewerTier.
question: Which Camunda 8.8 Number element property does the modeler set with a FEEL expression to switch the field to read-only for Tier-2 and above?

options.text: The Read only property, which makes the Number field read-only when the bound FEEL expression evaluates to true and can be driven by a reviewerTier comparison at form render time
The Hide if property, which hides the Number field entirely when the bound FEEL expression evaluates to true and removes the value from the rendered form for those reviewers
The Disabled property, which is documented for use during development and accepts an expression to conditionally disable the field for Tier-2 and above reviewers at render time
The Required validation property, which accepts a FEEL expression that toggles a field between editable and read-only states based on the reviewer's tier on the form at render time

a.text: Correct. The Configurable properties section states that Read only makes the Number field read-only and can be dynamically set using an expression, which is exactly what the modeler needs to gate editability by reviewerTier at form render time.

c.text: Incorrect. Disabled is documented for use during development; the dynamic-expression property intended for runtime read-only behaviour is Read only, not Disabled, on the Number element.

b.text: Incorrect. Hide if removes the field from the rendered form entirely; it does not provide a read-only view of the value to Tier-2 and above reviewers in the form.

d.text: Incorrect. Required is a validation property that controls whether the field must contain a value; it has no effect on whether the field is editable for a given reviewer tier on the form.

explanation: The Number element's Read only property documents both a static toggle and a dynamic expression mode for switching the field to read-only at runtime. Disabled is for development, Hide if removes the field, and Required gates emptiness, so none of those substitute for Read only.

scenario: Apex Insurance displays an open-claims table inside a Camunda 8.8 Tasklist form, with the data coming from the variable openClaims (a list of objects) and the page averaging 60 rows per claim handler. The team enables pagination to keep the page at 15 rows per page, and the developer is wiring the Data source field in the Table element's properties panel.
question: What input does the Camunda 8.8 Table element's Data source field accept for the rows of the table?

options.text: A variable name such as openClaims without any expression marker, which the runtime resolves implicitly to the bound variable on the user task scope at render time
Only an expression, so the developer enters a FEEL expression such as =openClaims that resolves at runtime to the list of claim objects rendered as the table rows
Either an expression or a list of static rows authored inline, mirroring the dual option offered by the Headers source field of the same Table element in the panel
A static list of objects pasted into the Data source field, with the column keys derived from the first row's properties at design time in the form editor

b.text: Correct. The Configurable properties section states that the Data source for a Table can only be an expression. The developer must enter a FEEL expression that resolves to the list of row objects at runtime for the table to render rows.

d.text: Incorrect. A static inline list of objects is not accepted by the Data source field; the documentation restricts that field to an expression for resolving the rows of the table.

a.text: Incorrect. The Data source field requires an expression, so a bare variable name without the expression prefix is not a valid configuration for that field.

c.text: Incorrect. Only the Headers source field accepts either an expression or a static list; the Data source field is restricted to an expression for the rows of the table.

explanation: The Configurable properties for Table specify that Data source can only be an expression, whereas Headers source can be either an expression or a list of static headers. This asymmetry is important when wiring the table inputs in the properties panel.

scenario: Within Pinnacle Logistics's Camunda 8.8 dispatch console, a Table form element binds to an openShipments variable holding 3000 rows and the modeler enables Pagination to control the size of each rendered chunk. A junior reviewer asks what constraints the Number of rows per page field imposes and when the field itself is taken into account by the form runtime.
question: What rule does the Camunda 8.8 Table element apply to the Number of rows per page field?

options.text: It is always applied to the Table regardless of the Pagination toggle, and a value of zero results in the runtime falling back to a default chunk size of 10 rows per page
It is only active when Pagination is enabled and the value must be supplied as a FEEL expression that resolves to a positive integer at form render time for each user task
It is used only when Pagination is enabled and the configured value must be greater than zero, defining the size of each page chunked from the Data source rows in the panel
It is used only when Pagination is enabled and accepts any non-negative integer, with a value of zero meaning that all data-source rows are rendered on a single page

c.text: Correct. The Configurable properties section states the Number of rows per page field is used only if pagination is enabled and must be greater than zero, defining the page chunk size for rows produced by the Data source expression.

a.text: Incorrect. The documentation makes the field's effect conditional on the Pagination toggle and does not describe a fallback default chunk size when the value is zero or absent.

d.text: Incorrect. The documented constraint is greater than zero, not non-negative; a value of zero does not trigger a single-page rendering of all rows under the documented rule.

b.text: Incorrect. The Number of rows per page field is documented as a number greater than zero, not as a FEEL-expression-only field; literal integers satisfy the documented constraint.

explanation: The Table element's Number of rows per page field is gated by the Pagination toggle and requires a value greater than zero. It controls how rows from the Data source are split into pages; without pagination enabled, the field has no effect on rendering.

scenario: Catalyst Retail's order-fulfilment process averages 8000 shipments per week through Camunda 8.8, and the modeler wants the existing pick-and-pack BPMN diagram to attach a shipping-label Camunda Form to the 'Confirm shipment' user task. The diagram is already open in Web Modeler and the form has been authored and saved in the same project.
question: What sequence of clicks does the modeler use to link the existing Camunda Form to the Confirm shipment user task?

options.text: They open the form, click a Bind to BPMN button in the form editor toolbar, then select the Confirm shipment user task in a diagram picker dialog to complete the binding
They drag the saved form file from the project panel and drop it onto the Confirm shipment user task on the diagram canvas to create the binding between the two resources
They edit the user task properties panel, paste the form ID under formKey, and redeploy the diagram to bind the saved form to the Confirm shipment task at runtime
They select the Confirm shipment user task, click the blue link overlay on its right side, navigate to the saved form in the menu, then click the blue Link button

d.text: Correct. The Quickstart documents this exact path: select the user task, click the blue link overlay on its right side to open the navigation menu, navigate to the target form, and click the blue Link button to attach the form.

a.text: Incorrect. The Quickstart describes linking from the user task in the diagram, not from a Bind to BPMN action initiated in the form editor toolbar.

b.text: Incorrect. Drag-and-drop from the project panel onto a user task is not the documented mechanism for linking a Camunda Form to a BPMN element in Web Modeler.

c.text: Incorrect. With Web Modeler from Camunda 8.4 onwards, JSON formKey editing is replaced by the link overlay flow, and a manual redeploy is not required to establish the binding.

explanation: The documented form-linking flow is initiated from the user task in the BPMN diagram: select the task, click the blue link overlay on the right, navigate to the form, and click the blue Link button. After linking, the overlay stays visible to indicate the task has a linked form.

scenario: Tessera Healthcare deploys a Camunda 8.8 admissions diagram from Web Modeler that links to a patient-consent Camunda Form and serves 4500 admissions per week. The deployment target is an upgraded Camunda 8.8 cluster, and the modeler asks whether the linked form must be deployed separately before starting the first process instance with the Run button.
question: What happens to the linked Camunda Form when the modeler clicks the blue Deploy button on the diagram?

options.text: The linked form is automatically deployed alongside the diagram in the same Deploy action, removing any need for a separate form deployment step for the cluster
The linked form is embedded as a snapshot inside the user task because this 8.8 cluster inlines all linked forms into the diagram payload before deployment
The linked form remains undeployed and is fetched lazily from the project on the first user-task instance, with no upload to the cluster at deploy time
The linked form must be deployed first from the form editor toolbar, otherwise the diagram deployment will fail validation and stop at the linked user task

a.text: Correct. The Quickstart states that linked forms will automatically be deployed along with the diagram when the Deploy button is clicked, so the modeler only needs to trigger the diagram deployment once for the whole bundle.

d.text: Incorrect. The form is not gated by a separate deploy from the form editor; the diagram Deploy action carries the linked form to the cluster automatically.

b.text: Incorrect. Automatic embedding into the user task only applies when deploying to a Camunda 8 cluster with a version lower than 8.4, not to this upgraded 8.8 target.

c.text: Incorrect. The linked form is uploaded at the time of the diagram deploy, not pulled lazily on demand at the first user-task instance after Run is clicked.

explanation: On clicking Deploy, Web Modeler bundles the diagram with any linked Camunda Forms and deploys them together. The legacy behaviour of inlining the form into the user task only applies when targeting Camunda 8 clusters older than 8.4 for backwards compatibility.

docs.title: Utilizing Forms - Deploy your diagram and start an instance

scenario: Lumen Bank receives 3200 loan application packets per month, each containing scanned IDs that need to be uploaded by the customer through a Camunda 8.8 form and then previewed by a back-office officer later in the same process. The team is evaluating which form components from the Camunda 8.8 form element library to combine across the two forms to satisfy both the upload step and the in-process preview step.
question: Which combination of Camunda 8.8 form components matches the team's upload and preview requirements across the two forms?

options.text: A Filepicker component on the customer upload form, paired with an HTML text component on the officer review form to render embedded base64 thumbnails of the uploads
A Filepicker component on the customer upload form for selecting and uploading the documents, paired with a document preview component on the officer review form for display and download
A single Filepicker component reused on both the customer upload form and the officer review form, with its render mode toggled to preview on the second step of the process
A document preview component on the customer upload form for uploads, paired with a second document preview component on the officer review form for downloads later in the flow

b.text: Correct. The Next steps section documents the Filepicker form component for letting users upload files and the document preview component for displaying and downloading documents within a form, which maps cleanly to the two flows the team needs.

c.text: Incorrect. Filepicker handles file selection and upload, not preview; toggling a mode on a single component is not the documented way to satisfy both flows in different forms of the process.

d.text: Incorrect. The document preview component covers display and download, not file upload; it cannot replace Filepicker on the customer-facing upload form.

a.text: Incorrect. The HTML text component is for rich content rendering, not for previewing or downloading uploaded documents in a process; the dedicated document preview component is what the docs name for that flow.

explanation: The Next steps section names the Filepicker component for upload flows and the document preview component for display and download flows. Combining the Filepicker on the customer upload form with the preview component on the downstream officer review form is the documented composition.

scenario: Vertex Capital records 75 loans per day inside a Camunda 8.8 number field bound to the key principal. The principal amounts can carry more significant digits than a standard JSON number can encode safely, and the team needs unlimited precision digits on the emitted value so the downstream ledger does not lose digits during transport.
question: Which number field property must the team enable to expose unlimited precision digits on the emitted value?

options.text: The Serialize to string property, which configures the output format of the value and enables unlimited precision digits on the emitted result of the bound field
The Decimal digits property, which controls the maximum digits after the decimal that the field will display and emit on form submission to the consumers
The Increment property, which controls the step size between valid field values so finer precision is captured during operator entry into the bound form field
The Maximum property, which caps the value at a documented upper bound so the runtime accepts only inputs that downstream systems can encode safely and without loss

b.text: Incorrect. Decimal digits constrains how many digits appear after the decimal point but does not change the underlying output encoding, so it cannot deliver unlimited precision digits.

c.text: Incorrect. Increment defines the step between valid values during entry and has no effect on the output encoding or the precision of digits emitted on submission.

d.text: Incorrect. Maximum is a validation upper bound that constrains the range of accepted values; it does not enable unlimited precision on the emitted datatype.

a.text: Correct. The Serialize to string property configures the output format of the value, and the documentation states that enabling it allows unlimited precision digits on the emitted result.

explanation: The number field exposes a Serialize to string property that changes the output encoding. When enabled, it allows the field to emit unlimited precision digits, which is the documented mechanism for preserving extra significant figures on the bound value.

scenario: Pinnacle Retail, a marketplace handling 90000 orders per day, calls a tax-rate REST API from Camunda 8.8. When the API returns HTTP 404, the order is a business case that must route to a manual-review user task via a boundary error event; when it returns HTTP 504, the call should fail the job with 2 retries and a 30 second backoff.
question: Which Error Expression correctly distinguishes these two outcomes?

options.text: Throw a ConnectorException with code 404 and a different ConnectorException with code 504 directly from the FEEL Error Expression in the modeler properties panel
Return bpmnError("504", "timeout") for code 504 and ignoreError() for code 404 so the workflow continues with no boundary handling needed
Return bpmnError("404", "not found") for code 404 and jobError("gateway timeout", {}, job.retries - 1, @"PT30S") for code 504 from a single FEEL expression
Use jobError for both codes and rely on the boundary event configuration on the BPMN element to differentiate the 2 outcomes at runtime

c.text: Correct. The bpmnError function triggers a BPMN error event for business outcomes, and jobError fails the job with custom retries and an ISO 8601 retry backoff for transient technical failures.

b.text: Incorrect. The 404 case is the business outcome that must route to manual review, so it requires bpmnError, not ignoreError, which would complete the job silently.

a.text: Incorrect. FEEL Error Expressions cannot throw Java exceptions directly; they return BPMN error or job error contexts via the provided helper functions.

d.text: Incorrect. jobError for both eliminates the boundary-event routing entirely and merges a business outcome with a transient technical failure.

explanation: Error Expression uses bpmnError to raise BPMN errors for business outcomes that boundary events should catch, and jobError to fail the job with optional retries and an ISO 8601 retry backoff for transient failures.

docs.title: How to use connectors - BPMN errors and failing jobs

scenario: Beacon Energy, a utility provider with 400000 customers, configures a Camunda 8.8 REST connector to call a billing API requiring 3 HTTP headers: Authorization (Bearer token), X-Tenant-Id (process variable tenantId), and Accept (literal application/json) for every customer-balance query.
question: Which HTTP Headers field value is the documented format?

options.text: Authorization: Bearer {{secrets.BILLING_TOKEN}}; X-Tenant-Id: tenantId; Accept: application/json as a semicolon-separated plain-text header list
= { headers: [ { name: "Authorization", value: "Bearer {{secrets.BILLING_TOKEN}}" } ] } as a nested context with a headers list inside
[ "Authorization=Bearer {{secrets.BILLING_TOKEN}}", "X-Tenant-Id=tenantId", "Accept=application/json" ] as a FEEL list of key-value strings
= { Authorization: "Bearer {{secrets.BILLING_TOKEN}}", "X-Tenant-Id": tenantId, Accept: "application/json" } as a FEEL context map

d.text: Correct. HTTP Headers in the REST connector accept a FEEL context (map) where keys are header names and values are FEEL expressions or quoted strings including secrets.

a.text: Incorrect. The field expects a FEEL context value, not a semicolon-separated plain-text string, and secrets must be quoted inside FEEL.

c.text: Incorrect. The format is a map, not a list of equal-delimited strings; the connector does not parse that shape into header pairs.

b.text: Incorrect. The connector reads the headers from the field's direct map; there is no nested headers list inside another context object.

explanation: The REST connector Query parameters and HTTP Headers fields are configured using the FEEL context (map) data type. Each entry maps a header name to a FEEL expression or quoted string.

scenario: Helios Logistics calls a weather API from Camunda 8.8 across 5000 requests per hour. The REST connector returns a large JSON document, and the team only needs 3 fields (temperature, windspeed, humidity) mapped into separate process variables that downstream FEEL gateways can compare in 30 seconds or less.
question: Which connector output mapping should the team use for this requirement?

options.text: Use Result Variable named response and write a downstream service task that re-reads the full JSON document and extracts the 3 fields after the connector completes
Use both Result Variable and Result Expression on the same task to write the full response and the 3 named variables, doubling the FEEL evaluation cost per call
Use Result Expression with a FEEL context like = { temp: response.body.main.temp, wind: response.body.wind.speed } to project specific fields into named process variables
Use Result Variable with a comma-separated list of field paths such as response.body.main.temp,response.body.wind.speed so the runtime extracts each one

c.text: Correct. Result Expression is a FEEL context expression that maps fields from the response into multiple named process variables, which is the documented approach for projection.

a.text: Incorrect. Storing the whole response and re-parsing it in a downstream task adds an unnecessary step when Result Expression projects directly during the same task.

d.text: Incorrect. Result Variable accepts a single variable name only; field projection is the job of Result Expression with a FEEL context.

b.text: Incorrect. The two fields are not designed to be combined as a pair on the same task for this use case; Result Expression alone covers the projection requirement.

explanation: Result Variable stores the full response under one variable name. Result Expression is a FEEL context expression that projects selected fields from the response into multiple named process variables.

docs.title: How to use connectors - variable and response mapping

scenario: Meridian Insurance is building a custom outbound connector with the Camunda 8.8 Connector SDK to score 60000 transactions per day against a proprietary fraud API. The Java class annotated @OutboundConnector exposes the name MyFraudConnector with type io.camunda:fraud-connector:1 and must be reachable by the connector runtime as a job worker within 5 seconds of deployment.
question: Which mechanism connects the BPMN task to this Java implementation at runtime?

options.text: The element template declares a hidden property bound to zeebe:taskDefinition with the property type set to the same string io.camunda:fraud-connector:1 so the job type matches
The connector runtime scans the BPMN XML for the literal class name MyFraudConnector and instantiates it directly through Java reflection at deploy time
The element template stores the fully qualified Java class name in a zeebe:taskHeader so the runtime can load it from the classpath when the job activates
The connector runtime registers a single global job type for every custom connector and dispatches by inspecting the input variables on each activated job

a.text: Correct. The element template binds zeebe:taskDefinition.type to the value declared in @OutboundConnector, which is the job type the connector worker fetches.

b.text: Incorrect. The runtime does not scan the BPMN XML for Java class names; the binding is via the job type registered in zeebe:taskDefinition.

c.text: Incorrect. Job headers carry per-task configuration such as resultVariable or errorExpression, not the connector implementation class to load.

d.text: Incorrect. Each custom connector registers its own job type; there is no single global type that dispatches by input shape across all connectors.

explanation: An outbound connector's @OutboundConnector type matches the zeebe:taskDefinition type value declared in the element template. The connector runtime registers a job worker for that exact type to fetch and execute jobs.

docs.title: Connector SDK - outbound connector element template

scenario: Aurora Bank, a fintech processing $4M of card transactions per hour, configures a REST connector in Camunda 8.8 with an Authorization header carrying a bearer token. The team must avoid exposing the API key in the BPMN XML and references a cluster secret named REST_API_KEY from a FEEL header map across 12 connector tasks.
question: Which expression correctly references the secret inside a FEEL header map?

options.text: Use double curly braces inside a quoted string such as = { Authorization: "Bearer {{secrets.REST_API_KEY}}" } so the placeholder is resolved at runtime
Inject the secret value into the BPMN XML at deploy time using a build-time placeholder so the connector receives the literal string at runtime
Reference the secret directly without quotes such as = { Authorization: secrets.REST_API_KEY } so FEEL treats it as a variable lookup
Configure the secret as a process variable on the start event and pass it through input mappings to each connector task on the model

a.text: Correct. Secrets use the {{secrets.NAME}} placeholder and must be wrapped in double quotes when used inside a FEEL expression such as a header map.

c.text: Incorrect. The bare secrets.* syntax is deprecated; the current placeholder is {{secrets.NAME}} wrapped in double quotes inside FEEL.

b.text: Incorrect. Secrets are resolved by the connector runtime from the cluster secret store, not baked into the BPMN XML at deploy time.

d.text: Incorrect. Connector secrets are managed at the cluster level and are not exposed as process variables to other tasks.

explanation: Secrets are referenced with the placeholder {{secrets.NAME}}. Inside FEEL expressions they must be wrapped in double quotes so the connector runtime can replace the placeholder with the actual secret value.

scenario: Stellar Telco, a carrier processing 250000 SIM activations per day, uses a Camunda 8.8 HTTP Webhook connector on a message intermediate catch event. The vendor POSTs {"orderId":"A-123","status":"paid"} and the engine must correlate the message to the running process instance whose orderId process variable equals A-123 within 5 seconds.
question: Which pair of correlation key settings is correct?

options.text: Correlation key (process) = orderId and Correlation key (payload) = request.headers.orderId so the engine reads the value from the HTTP request headers section
Correlation key (process) = request.params.orderId and Correlation key (payload) = orderId so the URL parameter is matched against the process variable name
Correlation key (process) = orderId and Correlation key (payload) = request.body.orderId so both sides resolve to A-123 for correlation
Correlation key (process) = request.body.orderId and Correlation key (payload) = orderId so the variable lookup happens against the incoming HTTP body

c.text: Correct. Correlation key (process) names the process variable that subscriptions use; Correlation key (payload) is a FEEL expression that extracts the same value from the incoming request.

d.text: Incorrect. The two fields are reversed; process side references the process variable, payload side reads from the request object.

a.text: Incorrect. The incoming body field is request.body.orderId; the headers form is only correct when the vendor places the correlation value in an HTTP header.

b.text: Incorrect. URL parameters live under request.params and the process side must be a variable name, not a request expression.

explanation: Correlation key (process) is a FEEL expression referring to the process variable that is used as the subscription key. Correlation key (payload) is a FEEL expression that extracts the matching value from the incoming webhook request.

scenario: Catalyst SaaS, a publisher handling 80000 events per day, integrates Slack with a Camunda 8.8 HTTP Webhook connector. Slack performs a one-time verification challenge by POSTing {"type":"url_verification","challenge":"abc"} and expects the body {"challenge":"abc"} returned with HTTP 200 in under 3 seconds; otherwise normal process correlation must run.
question: Which Verification expression handles the Slack challenge without creating a process instance?

options.text: = if request.body.type = "url_verification" then { challenge: request.body.challenge } else null so the connector returns the raw body and still triggers a process instance for each call
= if request.body.type = "url_verification" then { body: { challenge: request.body.challenge }, statusCode: 200 } else null so the webhook returns the challenge and skips correlation
Set a Response Expression instead of a Verification Expression so the webhook continues normal correlation while also returning the challenge body to Slack
Configure an Activation Condition of = request.body.type != "url_verification" so verification calls are silently dropped without any HTTP response to Slack

b.text: Correct. When the verification expression returns a non-null value, the webhook returns that response immediately and skips activation, HMAC, and correlation.

a.text: Incorrect. Without wrapping in a body object and an explicit statusCode the response format is incomplete, and correlation would still run on the verification call.

d.text: Incorrect. Activation Condition drops the call from process correlation but does not return a custom HTTP response to the caller, which Slack requires.

c.text: Incorrect. Response Expression runs after correlation completes; the verification step must run before correlation is attempted at all.

explanation: The Verification Expression returns a response object (body, statusCode, headers) and short-circuits the entire webhook pipeline so no process instance is created. Returning null falls through to normal processing.

scenario: NovaPay, a fintech receiving 15000 requests per hour on an HTTP webhook from a payment partner on Camunda 8.8, configures an HMAC-protected inbound webhook. The partner sends GET requests to a URL like https://example.com/webhook?id=123456&name=John%20Doe and signs them using HMAC scopes URL and PARAMETERS, expecting the signature data to be derived from the URL plus the query parameters.
question: Which HMAC strategy does the HTTP Webhook connector apply for this GET request with scopes URL and PARAMETERS?

options.text: Body Encoding Strategy, applied as the default whenever the HTTP method is GET, with the signature data computed from an empty request body since GET requests do not carry a body payload

Path-only Encoding Strategy, where only the URL path component is hashed without query parameters because PARAMETERS is treated as an authorization hint rather than as a signature scope by the connector
URL and Body Encoding Strategy, where the URL is concatenated with the raw JSON body even for GET requests, ensuring a uniform signature data shape across all HTTP method types used by the partner

URL and Parameters Encoding Strategy, where the query parameters are sorted alphabetically and concatenated with the URL to build the signature data used to compute the HMAC signature with the secret key and hash algorithm

d.text: Correct. The HTTP Webhook docs state that when HMAC scopes include URL and PARAMETERS and the HTTP method is GET, the URL and Parameters Encoding Strategy is used: query parameters are URL-encoded, sorted alphabetically, concatenated with the URL, and the resulting Signature Data is used with the secret key and hash algorithm.

a.text: Incorrect. Body Encoding Strategy is selected when scopes are only BODY or empty, not when URL and PARAMETERS are set, and GET does not force a Body Encoding default.

c.text: Incorrect. The URL and Body Encoding Strategy specifically falls back to URL and Parameters Encoding when the HTTP method is GET; it does not concatenate URL with body for GET.

b.text: Incorrect. There is no Path-only Encoding Strategy in the docs; PARAMETERS is a first-class HMAC scope that contributes the query string to the signature data.

explanation: The HTTP Webhook connector picks an HMAC strategy from the configured scopes and the HTTP method. Scopes BODY or empty trigger Body Encoding. URL and PARAMETERS on a GET trigger URL and Parameters Encoding, where sorted query parameters are concatenated with the URL. URL and BODY trigger URL and Body Encoding, except on GET which falls back to URL and Parameters.

docs.title: HTTP Webhook connector - Make your HTTP Webhook connector executable

scenario: Apex Retail, an online merchant fulfilling 6000 orders per day in Camunda 8.8, wants to replace an empty BPMN task with an outbound SendGrid Email connector. When a receipt is uploaded for review, the manager must be notified within 5 minutes. A developer opens the process in Modeler and needs to follow the documented quickstart steps to add the connector from the start event.
question: Which modeler workflow attaches a SendGrid Email connector to the start event for this notification?

options.text: Drag a generic Service Task next to the start event, set the task type header to sendgrid-email, then enter the SendGrid API key, the sender and receiver addresses, and the message content
Open the start event's properties panel, set the implementation type to email-send, then enter the SendGrid API key, the sender and receiver addresses, and the message content fields directly
Right-click the BPMN canvas to open the marketplace panel, install the SendGrid Email element template, drop the template onto the start event, and fill in the SendGrid credentials and message

Click the start event to open its context pad, choose Append connector, pick the SendGrid Email connector option, and fill in the API key, sender, receiver, and message content in the properties panel

d.text: Correct. The quickstart documents clicking the start event to open the context pad, selecting Append connector, picking the SendGrid Email connector, naming the new task, and filling out the API key, sender, receiver, and email message content in the properties panel.

b.text: Incorrect. The start event does not expose an email-send implementation type; the documented path is the Append connector action available from the start event's context pad.

a.text: Incorrect. The quickstart attaches connectors via the Append connector context pad action, not by typing a custom job type header onto a plain service task element next to the start event.

c.text: Incorrect. The documented flow uses the start event's context pad to append the connector directly; there is no marketplace install step described in this quickstart guide.

explanation: The quickstart shows four steps: click the start event to open its context pad, choose Append connector, select the SendGrid Email connector option, and fill in the connector's required properties (API key, sender, receiver, and message content) in the properties panel.

scenario: Aurora Bank, a retail bank serving 80000 customers, evaluates Camunda 8.8 out-of-the-box connectors to add 3 third-party integrations to its loan approval process in Web Modeler. A solution architect must explain how a connector is technically structured and where it is configured before the modeling team begins drag-and-drop work.
question: Which statement correctly describes how a Camunda 8.8 out-of-the-box connector is structured and configured?

options.text: A connector is a single Zeebe broker plugin compiled into the cluster jar that declares its modeling fields in a YAML file embedded inside each BPMN diagram at deployment time
A connector consists of business logic implemented as a job worker plus a modeling UI provided by an element template, and is configured through the properties panel in Web Modeler
A connector is a Zeebe gateway interceptor that runs inside the cluster gateway pod and is configured exclusively through environment variables set on that pod at startup time
A connector is a Camunda 7 external task client packaged with each diagram and is configured by editing BPMN XML attributes via a command-line tool prior to deployment

b.text: Correct. The docs state that connectors consist of two parts: the business logic is implemented as a job worker and the user interface during modeling is provided using an element template, and they are configured via the properties panel on the right side of Web Modeler.

a.text: Incorrect. The runtime side of a connector is a job worker, not a broker plugin, and the modeling UI is supplied by an element template, not by YAML embedded inside the BPMN diagram.

d.text: Incorrect. Out-of-the-box connectors are not Camunda 7 external task clients; they are added inside Web Modeler and configured via the properties panel rather than by editing BPMN XML from the command line.

c.text: Incorrect. Connectors run as job workers behind an element template and are configured through the modeler properties panel; they are not gateway interceptors driven only by gateway pod environment variables.

explanation: An out-of-the-box connector is composed of a job worker for the runtime business logic and an element template that drives the modeling UI. The user fills out the required fields in the properties panel on the right side of the Web Modeler screen.

scenario: Lattice Insurance, an insurer processing 12000 customer requests per day, is shipping a Camunda 8.8 custom outbound connector element template. The underwriting team wants the connector response mapped into 4 process variables via a FEEL expression on every invocation, with no extra Java glue code in the connector runtime.
question: Which element template property binding exposes the documented Result Expression on the outbound connector?

options.text: A String property with feel set to optional, binding type zeebe:taskHeader, and key resultVariable on the element template
A Text property with feel set to required, binding type zeebe:taskHeader, and key resultExpression on the element template
A Text property with feel set to required, binding type zeebe:taskDefinition, and property resultExpression on the element template
A Text property with feel set to required, binding type zeebe:input, and name resultExpression on the element template

b.text: Correct. The Result Expression object in the outbound connector element template uses a Text property with feel required and a zeebe:taskHeader binding whose key is resultExpression. The connector runtime reads that custom job header and evaluates the FEEL expression to map the response into process variables.

d.text: Incorrect. The zeebe:input binding creates a connector input variable consumed inside the connector function; it does not produce the result-mapping custom header that the runtime evaluates after the connector returns.

c.text: Incorrect. The zeebe:taskDefinition binding is reserved for the connector type identifier such as io.camunda:template:1; the Result Expression is exposed through a custom job header, not the task definition property.

a.text: Incorrect. The zeebe:taskHeader with key resultVariable stores the whole response under a single variable name; it is not the Result Expression and does not evaluate FEEL to map response fields into process variables.

explanation: Result Expression is defined in the element template as a Text property with feel required bound through zeebe:taskHeader using the key resultExpression. The connector runtime picks up this custom header and translates the FEEL expression into process variables. Result Variable is a separate header (resultVariable) that stores the raw response.

scenario: Northwind Logistics, a 3PL handling 75000 shipments per day, builds a Camunda 8.8 outbound connector that calls a flaky carrier-tracking API. When the API times out, the connector must retry with a 10 second backoff and reduce the remaining retries on every attempt so that the 75000 shipments per day eventually flow through without exhausting the job worker.
question: Who is responsible for decreasing the retries value when throwing ConnectorRetryException from an outbound connector function?

options.text: The connector developer must decrement the retries value passed to the ConnectorRetryExceptionBuilder, and the runtime uses the supplied value as is to override the job
The Zeebe broker subtracts one from the job retries automatically after the connector function rethrows the ConnectorRetryException to the runtime
The OutboundConnectorContext object decreases the retries value as part of the bindVariables call before the execute method returns a result
The connector runtime environment automatically decreases the retries value on each ConnectorRetryException using the job's prior retries count

d.text: Incorrect. The SDK explicitly states the runtime uses the values supplied on ConnectorRetryException as is to override the job. It does not subtract one from the retries it sees on the exception.

c.text: Incorrect. OutboundConnectorContext.bindVariables only handles secret replacement, JSON deserialization, and input validation; it does not manage job retries or mutate the retries field on a ConnectorRetryException.

a.text: Correct. The SDK guide states the developer is responsible for decreasing the retries value on the ConnectorRetryExceptionBuilder and that the connector runtime applies the supplied retries and backoffDuration to the job as is.

b.text: Incorrect. The Zeebe broker does not auto-decrement retries when the connector overrides them via ConnectorRetryException; the runtime forwards the explicit retries value from the exception to the job.

explanation: ConnectorRetryException lets the connector override the job's retries and backoffDuration values, but the runtime applies whatever values the developer supplies as is. The developer must therefore decrement the retries counter passed to the ConnectorRetryExceptionBuilder on each failed attempt so the job eventually stops being retried.

scenario: Helix Marketplace, a retailer running its catalog on Camunda 8.8, packages a custom inbound connector module that subscribes to 12000 webhook events per day from upstream merchants. The team wants the connector class auto-discovered by the runtime without listing it in any element template attribute or annotation parameter across all 30 instances of its Self-Managed cluster.
question: Which file in the inbound connector module exposes the InboundConnectorExecutable implementation to the runtime?

options.text: A connector-runtime.properties file that lists the inbound connector classes under a key named inbound.executable.classes inside the JAR
A resources/META-INF/services/io.camunda.connector.api.inbound.InboundConnectorExecutable file listing the fully qualified executable class name
A META-INF/MANIFEST.MF entry that lists the fully qualified executable class name under a Connector-Class attribute inside the connector JAR
An element-templates/inbound-template-connector.json entry that exposes the fully qualified Java class under a runtimeClass attribute

c.text: Incorrect. The connector SDK does not rely on a Connector-Class manifest attribute. Manifest entries are not part of the documented discovery path for inbound connector executables in the SDK guide.

b.text: Correct. The SDK guide shows the resources/META-INF/services/io.camunda.connector.api.inbound.InboundConnectorExecutable file as the Service Provider Interface entry that makes the executable class discoverable by the connector runtime.

a.text: Incorrect. There is no connector-runtime.properties discovery file in the SDK; the runtime relies on the ServiceLoader SPI under META-INF/services rather than a properties key.

d.text: Incorrect. Element templates describe modeling building blocks and bindings; the runtime does not load the executable Java class from a runtimeClass attribute on the JSON element template.

explanation: Custom inbound connectors are made detectable through the standard Java ServiceLoader SPI. The connector project provides a file under resources/META-INF/services named after the InboundConnectorExecutable interface; the file lists the fully qualified implementation class so the runtime can load it at startup.

scenario: Frontline Bank, a digital bank receiving 600 inbound API calls per hour, builds a Camunda 8.8 inbound connector that subscribes to a message bus. The team wants the activate hook to start the subscription so all 600 calls per hour reach their target processes without blocking the runtime thread that triggered activation across the bank's 5 production instances.
question: What does the SDK require from the activate method implementation in InboundConnectorExecutable?

options.text: The activate implementation must call deactivate at the end of its body to register a graceful shutdown hook before any subscription thread is started by the runtime
The activate implementation must be asynchronous so the runtime thread that invoked it is not blocked while the subscription stays active and forwards inbound events
The activate implementation runs synchronously on the calling thread and returns only after the subscription has delivered its first inbound event to the runtime
The activate implementation must register itself in META-INF/MANIFEST.MF before subscribing so the runtime can locate it during the activation handshake at startup

c.text: Incorrect. The SDK guide explicitly states the activate implementation must be asynchronous; a synchronous activate that waits for the first event would block the runtime thread and prevent further activations.

a.text: Incorrect. The deactivate method is a separate graceful shutdown hook invoked by the runtime later; calling it at the end of activate would stop the subscription immediately after starting it.

d.text: Incorrect. Discovery of the executable class uses the META-INF/services SPI, not the JAR manifest, and discovery is separate from the activate runtime contract documented in the SDK.

b.text: Correct. The SDK guide states the activate method is the trigger to start listening for inbound events and that its implementation has to be asynchronous, so the calling runtime thread is not blocked while the subscription remains active.

explanation: The activate method on InboundConnectorExecutable starts the subscription that listens for inbound events. The SDK requires this implementation to be asynchronous so the runtime thread that triggered activation is not blocked, and the subscription stays alive until deactivate releases its resources.

scenario: Quickstop Retail, a retailer with 4000 inbound API requests per hour, builds a Camunda 8.8 inbound connector that subscribes to a message bus. For each of the 4000 requests per hour the onEvent callback must hand the event payload to the connector runtime so it can correlate against the matching waiting process instances across the retailer's order pipeline.
question: Which InboundConnectorContext method does the documented onEvent callback use to forward an inbound event to the connector runtime?

options.text: The correlateWithResult method on InboundConnectorContext, which the SDK guide invokes from the onEvent callback to forward the inbound event payload to the runtime
The deactivate method on InboundConnectorContext, which the SDK guide invokes from the onEvent callback to forward the inbound event payload to the runtime
The activate method on InboundConnectorContext, which the SDK guide invokes from the onEvent callback to forward the inbound event payload to the runtime
The bindProperties method on InboundConnectorContext, which the SDK guide invokes from the onEvent callback to forward the inbound event payload to the runtime

c.text: Incorrect. activate is a method on InboundConnectorExecutable that the runtime calls once to start the subscription; it is not a forwarding method on InboundConnectorContext invoked per inbound event.

a.text: Correct. The SDK guide shows the onEvent callback calling connectorContext.correlateWithResult(connectorEvent) to forward the inbound event payload to the runtime and obtain a CorrelationResult describing the outcome.

d.text: Incorrect. bindProperties is used inside activate to deserialize the inbound connector configuration into a properties object; it does not forward inbound event payloads from onEvent.

b.text: Incorrect. deactivate is a shutdown hook implemented on InboundConnectorExecutable to release subscription resources; it does not exist as an event-forwarding method on InboundConnectorContext.

explanation: The inbound connector uses correlateWithResult on the InboundConnectorContext from the onEvent callback to push each subscription event to the connector runtime. The returned CorrelationResult exposes the handling strategy so the executable can forward errors upstream or ignore them based on configuration.

scenario: Riverwave Fintech, a fintech handling 25000 API requests per day, writes a JUnit test for its Camunda 8.8 outbound connector. The test must verify that the placeholder secrets.MY_TOKEN on the Authentication.token field is replaced with the literal value 'token value' when bindVariables runs, before any of the 25000 requests per day reach the external API.
question: Which test setup produces the context that the documented secret-replacement test in the SDK uses?

options.text: Use a real ZeebeClient test container with MY_TOKEN stored in the cluster secret store and rely on the deployed connector job worker to replace the secret in the input
Use OutboundConnectorRuntime.test().secret(MY_TOKEN, token value).build() to assemble a runtime harness and call execute on the connector function with the input class
Use OutboundConnectorContextBuilder.create().secret(MY_TOKEN, token value).build() to assemble the context and then invoke context.bindVariables on the MyConnectorType class
Use OutboundConnectorContextBuilder.create().build() and then call context.replaceSecrets with MY_TOKEN and token value before invoking bindVariables on the input class

d.text: Incorrect. The SDK guide does not show a context.replaceSecrets call in the test; secret replacement is triggered automatically by bindVariables on a context that was seeded with the secret through the builder.

b.text: Incorrect. The documented test harness is OutboundConnectorContextBuilder, not an OutboundConnectorRuntime.test entry point; the SDK provides a context builder rather than a full runtime harness for scoped tests.

c.text: Correct. The SDK shows OutboundConnectorContextBuilder.create().secret(MY_TOKEN, token value).build() to construct the test context, after which bindVariables on the input class triggers secret replacement and validation for assertions.

a.text: Incorrect. The SDK explicitly aims to test connectors without Camunda engine internals; spinning up a ZeebeClient container and the deployed worker is not the documented scoped-test approach for secret replacement.

explanation: Secret-replacement tests rely on OutboundConnectorContextBuilder to assemble a test OutboundConnectorContext seeded with secret values via .secret(name, value). Calling bindVariables on the input class then triggers the same replacement and validation routines used at runtime, allowing assertions on the resolved fields.

scenario: Catalyst Bank, a bank running 600 microservices, has a custom outbound connector that calls a downstream pricing API in Camunda 8.8. The author wants the connector to retry on transient HTTP 502 responses with a 10 second backoff and exactly 5 retry attempts, signalled from the connector function itself rather than relying on the job's default behaviour.
question: How does ConnectorRetryException relate to the surrounding job retries and backoffDuration according to the Connector SDK?

options.text: When retries or backoffDuration are not set, the runtime uses the job's existing values; when they are set, they override the job values as is, and the developer is responsible for decreasing the retries count
The runtime automatically decreases the retries field by one on every throw and resets backoffDuration to zero, so the developer only sets the initial limit and never touches the values after that

The exception is purely informational and ignored by the runtime: retries and backoffDuration are always taken from the job's deployment definition, and the developer cannot override either value from the function body
The exception forces an immediate BPMN error event and disables Zeebe job retries entirely, meaning backoffDuration is interpreted as a one-shot delay before throwing the error on the boundary

c.text: Incorrect. The SDK explicitly allows the developer to override retries and backoffDuration through the exception; the runtime does not ignore those values when they are set.

b.text: Incorrect. The runtime does not automatically decrement the retries count or reset the backoff; the developer must decrease the number of retries themselves.

a.text: Correct. The docs state that if retries or backoffDuration are not set on the ConnectorRetryException, the runtime uses the job values, otherwise the values are taken as is to override the job values, and the developer is responsible for decreasing the retries count manually.

d.text: Incorrect. ConnectorRetryException signals a retryable technical error and works with normal Zeebe job retries; it does not force a BPMN error or disable retries, which is what jobError or bpmnError handle instead.

explanation: ConnectorRetryException lets a connector function override the job's retries and backoffDuration. Unset fields fall back to the job's own values, set fields override them as is, and decrementing the retries count is the developer's responsibility in the function body.

docs.title: Connector SDK - Outbound connector runtime logic

scenario: Lumen Bank, a bank deploying 8 outbound connectors per release, wants unit tests for a custom Camunda 8.8 connector. A developer must check that the authentication token attribute correctly resolves a value placeholder like {{secrets.MY_TOKEN}} into the literal string token value, without spinning up a Zeebe cluster or a real job worker, in roughly 60 seconds per test class.
question: Which helper does the Connector SDK provide to test secret replacement for outbound connector functions?

options.text: An OutboundConnectorContextBuilder that creates a test OutboundConnectorContext where the developer registers secrets such as MY_TOKEN, builds the context, and asserts the bound input value matches the resolved string

A CamundaConnectorAssertJ entry point that observes the running job worker thread, captures every secret replacement event, and writes the resolved values into a temporary test log file for later inspection

A SecretsTestRunner annotation that replaces all secret placeholders with a Hashicorp Vault stub during test execution and fails the build whenever a secret value cannot be resolved from the configured stub
A ZeebeTestEngineRule JUnit extension that boots an in-memory Zeebe broker, deploys the connector element template, and asserts that secret placeholders are resolved during real job activation events

d.text: Incorrect. The SDK does not require booting an in-memory Zeebe to test secrets; the testing section abstracts from Camunda 8 internals and uses a context builder instead.

a.text: Correct. The Testing section shows an OutboundConnectorContextBuilder that lets a test register secrets and build an OutboundConnectorContext used to call bindVariables and assert that placeholders are resolved into their secret values.

c.text: Incorrect. There is no SecretsTestRunner annotation or Vault stub documented in the SDK testing section; secret tests work through the context builder.

b.text: Incorrect. The SDK does not provide a CamundaConnectorAssertJ entry point that observes a running job worker thread; tests run directly against the function and a builder-provided context.
