explanation: The Connector SDK provides OutboundConnectorContextBuilder for unit testing outbound connector functions. The builder lets a test register secrets, build an OutboundConnectorContext, and verify that bindVariables resolves placeholders like {{secrets.MY_TOKEN}} into the configured values without involving a real Zeebe cluster.

scenario: Granite Utility, a utility provider with 600000 customers, runs Camunda 8.8 Self-Managed on Kubernetes and packages a custom connector as a JAR. A platform engineer must launch the official Docker connectors runtime image so it picks up the custom JAR, connects to a Zeebe gateway at 10.0.0.42:26500, and skips inbound polling because the same cluster runs no inbound connectors for now.
question: Which command surface does the Connector SDK document for running the Docker runtime image with a custom connector and inbound polling disabled?

options.text: Mount the connector.jar at /opt/app/connector.jar, set ZEEBE_CLIENT_BROKER_GATEWAY-ADDRESS to the gateway URL, and set CAMUNDA_CONNECTOR_POLLING_ENABLED to false in the docker run command
Copy the connector.jar into /var/connectors/classpath, point ZEEBE_GATEWAY_ENDPOINT at the gateway URL, and run the image with a CLI flag --inbound=off to disable inbound polling at startup

Bake the connector.jar into a derived image extending camunda/connectors, set CAMUNDA_OPERATE_CLIENT_URL to the gateway URL, and pass CAMUNDA_CONNECTOR_INBOUND_DISABLED true through a secrets file mounted at runtime
Place the connector.jar in /lib/ext on the host, expose ZEEBE_BROKER_PORT 26500 to the container, and configure the broker itself to set CONNECTORS_POLLING_ENABLED=false via a sidecar configmap

a.text: Correct. The docs show docker run with -v $PWD/connector.jar:/opt/app/connector.jar to add the jar to the classpath, ZEEBE_CLIENT_BROKER_GATEWAY-ADDRESS to point at the Zeebe gateway, and CAMUNDA_CONNECTOR_POLLING_ENABLED=false to disable inbound connectors.

b.text: Incorrect. The documented mount path is /opt/app/connector.jar, the gateway is set through ZEEBE_CLIENT_BROKER_GATEWAY-ADDRESS, and there is no --inbound=off CLI flag in the example.

c.text: Incorrect. CAMUNDA_OPERATE_CLIENT_URL points at Operate (used by inbound connectors), not the Zeebe gateway, and the inbound polling toggle is CAMUNDA_CONNECTOR_POLLING_ENABLED, not CAMUNDA_CONNECTOR_INBOUND_DISABLED.

d.text: Incorrect. The Zeebe broker port and broker configuration are not how the connectors runtime image is wired; the image reads its own environment variables shown in the docker run example.

explanation: The Docker runtime image command mounts the connector jar at /opt/app/connector.jar, configures ZEEBE_CLIENT_BROKER_GATEWAY-ADDRESS for Zeebe, CAMUNDA_OPERATE_CLIENT_URL for inbound connectors, and accepts secrets via env vars or a file. Setting CAMUNDA_CONNECTOR_POLLING_ENABLED=false disables inbound connectors.

scenario: At Verdant Marketplace, a Camunda 8.8 process waits 24 hours after order placement for a courier delivery callback. The team models an intermediate catch event with the HTTP Webhook inbound connector template and must correlate 12000 instances per day against courier payloads keyed by trackingNumber so each callback resumes the right waiting instance.
question: Which two properties on the inbound intermediate catch event template must the team configure so each webhook payload correlates to the right waiting instance?

options.text: Correlation key (process), bound to bpmn:Message#zeebe:subscription#property correlationKey, plus Correlation key (payload), bound to zeebe:property correlationKeyExpression, both with feel: required
HMAC secret key and HMAC header in the endpoint group, both with feel: optional, so the catch event correlates payloads by validating the signature header value against the stored shared secret
Webhook ID, bound to inbound.context, plus Condition, bound to inbound.activationCondition, both with feel: required so the catch event matches on the URL segment and a FEEL filter expression
Variables, bound to inbound.variableMapping, plus the generated UUID hidden field bound to bpmn:Message#property name, both providing implicit correlation through the payload variable map

a.text: Correct. The inbound intermediate catch event template defines Correlation key (process) bound to bpmn:Message#zeebe:subscription#property name correlationKey and Correlation key (payload) bound to zeebe:property correlationKeyExpression; both are feel: required and notEmpty.

c.text: Incorrect. Webhook ID identifies the URL path and Condition gates activation; neither field is documented as performing message correlation between the waiting catch event and the inbound payload.

b.text: Incorrect. HMAC fields authenticate the request signature; the template does not describe them as the FEEL extractors that pick a correlation key value out of the payload.

d.text: Incorrect. Variables maps the payload into process variables and the generated UUID is the message name; neither is the FEEL expression that selects the correlation key value.

explanation: The intermediate catch event template adds two FEEL-required correlation properties: Correlation key (process), bound via bpmn:Message#zeebe:subscription#property name correlationKey, and Correlation key (payload), bound to zeebe:property correlationKeyExpression. Together they correlate inbound webhook payloads with the waiting message subscription on the catch event.

docs.title: Connector templates - Inbound intermediate catch event

scenario: At fintech vendor Bramble Trust, a Camunda 8.8 user task can be cancelled when an external risk system pushes a webhook within 48 hours of activation. The risk system signs every callback with a JWT, and a small team of 4 engineers configures the HTTP Webhook boundary event connector template to validate JWT roles against a well-known JWKs endpoint.
question: Which Authorization type value should the team pick on the boundary event template, and which field then holds the well-known JWKs URL?

options.text: Authorization type APIKEY in the authorization group, with the JWKs endpoint placed in the inbound.auth.apiKeyLocator field as a FEEL expression evaluated on the request payload
Authorization type NONE in the authorization group, with the JWKs endpoint placed in the inbound.verificationExpression field located in the webhookResponse group of the template
Authorization type JWT in the authorization group, with the JWKs endpoint placed in the inbound.auth.jwt.jwkUrl field of the authorization group on the boundary event template
Authorization type BASIC in the authorization group, with the JWKs endpoint placed in the inbound.auth.username field and the shared secret stored in inbound.auth.password

a.text: Incorrect. Selecting APIKEY exposes inbound.auth.apiKey plus inbound.auth.apiKeyLocator; apiKeyLocator is a FEEL expression that extracts the API key from the request, not a JWKs URL holder.

c.text: Correct. The boundary template defines an Authorization type dropdown with NONE, BASIC, APIKEY, and JWT; selecting JWT reveals inbound.auth.jwt.jwkUrl described as the well-known URL of JWKs, alongside permissionsExpression and requiredPermissions.

d.text: Incorrect. Selecting BASIC exposes inbound.auth.username and inbound.auth.password for basic authentication credentials; the template provides no JWKs URL field under BASIC.

b.text: Incorrect. NONE disables authorization and exposes no auth fields; the verification expression is for one-time webhook handshake responses, not for JWT keys.

explanation: On the boundary event webhook template, picking JWT in the Authorization type dropdown reveals JWT-specific properties including inbound.auth.jwt.jwkUrl (the well-known URL of JWKs), inbound.auth.jwt.permissionsExpression, and inbound.auth.jwt.requiredPermissions, all in the authorization group.

scenario: At carrier Polaris Logistics, an engineer configures the HTTP Webhook boundary event connector template on Camunda 8.8 and sets a 30 minutes Message TTL so late courier callbacks expire in the broker before they can interrupt a user task whose SLA window is 2 hours per delivery.
question: Which value format does the boundary event webhook template require in the Message TTL property?

options.text: An ISO-8601 duration string matching the pattern ^(PT.*|)$, written for example as PT30M for a 30 minutes time-to-live in the broker
A FEEL number in seconds matching the regex pattern of the field, written as a plain integer such as 1800 for 30 minutes in the broker
A cron expression in the correlation group matching the pattern of the field so the broker schedules expiry inside the message subscription
A Java Duration literal matching the pattern of the field, written as java.time.Duration.ofMinutes(30) inside the messageTtl property value

b.text: Incorrect. The constraint pattern on messageTtl requires PT-prefixed ISO-8601 durations or an empty string, so a plain numeric seconds value such as 1800 does not match the field pattern.

c.text: Incorrect. messageTtl sits in the correlation group and represents a duration string, not a cron expression scheduled against the message subscription.

a.text: Correct. The boundary template defines messageTtl with constraints pattern ^(PT.*|)$ and the message must be an ISO-8601 duration; the field is feel: optional and the description states Time-to-live for the message in the broker.

d.text: Incorrect. The field holds a String value that matches the ISO-8601 duration pattern; the template does not accept Java Duration literals as the configured value type.

explanation: Message TTL on the boundary event webhook template is a String property in the correlation group with the pattern constraint ^(PT.*|)$, requiring an ISO-8601 duration such as PT30M or an empty value. It controls how long the broker keeps the correlated message available for the boundary event.

scenario: Apex Retail, a retailer attaching 12 boundary webhooks to in-flight order tasks at a peak of 9000 orders per hour on Camunda 8.8, ships a custom boundary event Webhook connector template. A template author needs to confirm which HTTP method choices the Webhook method dropdown lists by default and what value drives the default behaviour when the partner system does not specify a method.
question: Which choices does the inbound boundary event Webhook connector template list for the Webhook method dropdown, and which is the default?

options.text: Choices PUT and POST only, with default value post since HMAC signatures on boundary events are only documented for these two write-style methods in the template
Choices CONNECT, OPTIONS, GET, POST, with default OPTIONS so the connector replies to CORS preflight requests automatically before invoking the boundary correlation logic
Choices Any, GET, POST, PATCH, DELETE, with default value get because the boundary event template assumes idempotent reads and reserves writes for outbound connectors
Choices Any, GET, POST, PUT, DELETE, with default value any so the webhook accepts requests irrespective of the HTTP method used by the caller

b.text: Incorrect. The documented dropdown does not include CONNECT or OPTIONS, and the default value is any, not OPTIONS.

d.text: Correct. The boundary event template defines the Webhook method dropdown with choices Any, GET, POST, PUT, and DELETE, and the default value is any so the webhook accepts requests irrespective of the HTTP method used.

c.text: Incorrect. PATCH is not in the dropdown for the boundary event template, and the default is any rather than get.

a.text: Incorrect. The dropdown lists more than two methods (Any, GET, POST, PUT, DELETE) and HMAC support is not limited to PUT or POST in the template definition.

explanation: The boundary event Webhook connector template defines the Webhook method as a Dropdown bound to inbound.method with choices Any, GET, POST, PUT, and DELETE. The default value any lets the webhook accept requests regardless of HTTP method, leaving filtering to the activation condition.

docs.title: Connector templates - Inbound boundary event connector templates

scenario: Lattice Bank, a bank making 14000 calls per hour to an outbound REST partner from Camunda 8.8 service tasks, ships a custom Conditional REST outbound connector template. A developer must wire the REST Endpoint URL into a task header and the Request Body into a connector input, while letting the body field only appear when the HTTP method is one of patch, post, or delete.
question: Which binding configuration does the Conditional REST outbound connector template apply to the REST Endpoint URL and Request Body fields?

options.text: REST Endpoint URL bound as zeebe:input name url and Request Body bound as zeebe:taskHeader key body, with the body shown unconditionally regardless of which HTTP method is chosen in the dropdown
REST Endpoint URL bound as zeebe:taskHeader key url and Request Body bound as zeebe:taskHeader key body, with no condition on the body field because the same header serves all four HTTP methods
REST Endpoint URL bound as zeebe:taskHeader key url and Request Body bound as zeebe:input name body, with the body shown only when httpMethod is one of patch, post, or delete via a condition
REST Endpoint URL bound as zeebe:taskDefinition property url and Request Body bound as zeebe:taskHeader key body, with the body shown only when httpMethod equals get inside the property condition

a.text: Incorrect. The URL is bound as a zeebe:taskHeader key url, not a zeebe:input; and the body field is conditional on the HTTP method, not shown unconditionally.

d.text: Incorrect. zeebe:taskDefinition holds the task type http, not the URL, and the condition on the body field selects patch, post, or delete, not get.

b.text: Incorrect. The Request Body is bound as a zeebe:input name body, not a zeebe:taskHeader, and it is conditionally shown via a property condition on httpMethod.

c.text: Correct. The template binds REST Endpoint URL as a zeebe:taskHeader with key url and Request Body as a zeebe:input with name body, and the body field is gated by a condition on the httpMethod property using oneOf [patch, post, delete].

explanation: The Conditional REST outbound connector template uses zeebe:taskDefinition to set the task type to http, zeebe:taskHeader bindings for endpoint URL and method, a zeebe:input binding for the request body, and property conditions to show the body and authentication fields only for the appropriate HTTP methods and auth types.

scenario: A Camunda 8.8 fintech team building a notifications webhook for an upstream payments provider returns a custom HTTP 201 status and a JSON body that echoes part of the inbound payload back to the caller. The webhook handles roughly 5000 requests per minute, and the team wants to set the response status, the response body, and one Content-Type header from a single FEEL Response expression on the connector.
question: Which FEEL Response expression sets the body, the status code 201, and a Content-Type header from one expression?

options.text: ={"body": {"result": "ok"}, "status": 201, "headers": {"Content-Type": "application/json"}} as the Response expression returned by the Webhook
={"response": {"body": {"result": "ok"}, "statusCode": 201, "headers": {"Content-Type": "application/json"}}} as the Response expression on the Webhook
={"responseBody": {"result": "ok"}, "statusCode": 201, "responseHeaders": {"Content-Type": "application/json"}} as the Response expression
={"body": {"result": "ok"}, "statusCode": 201, "headers": {"Content-Type": "application/json"}} as the Response expression returned by the Webhook

a.text: Incorrect. The documented status field name is statusCode, not status, so the connector would fall back to the default HTTP 200 status code and ignore the value supplied under status.

b.text: Incorrect. The Response expression reads body, statusCode, and headers directly from the returned object; wrapping them inside a response key prevents the connector from finding any of the three configured fields.

c.text: Incorrect. The documented keys are body and headers, not responseBody and responseHeaders, so renamed keys are ignored and the default 200 status applies to every reply.

d.text: Correct. A FEEL context with body, statusCode, and headers keys is the documented shape for the Response expression; statusCode defaults to 200 when omitted, and headers accepts a context map of name and value pairs.

explanation: The Response expression returns a FEEL context where the body, statusCode, and headers keys control the response payload, HTTP status code, and response headers; key names other than these are ignored, and statusCode defaults to 200 when the key is omitted from the expression.

scenario: Forge Logistics, a carrier processing 35000 shipments per day on Camunda 8.8, attaches an HTTP Webhook intermediate catch event to its dispatch process. The integration team must correlate inbound partner messages to the right process instance using a process variable orderId, while the partner sends a JSON body like {"orderId": "123"} and they want each message buffered for 1 hour if it arrives before the process is ready to consume it.
question: Which correlation configuration does the HTTP Webhook connector documentation prescribe for this intermediate catch event?

options.text: Correlation key (process) set to =request.body.orderId and Correlation key (payload) set to =orderId, with Message TTL configured as 60 (a plain integer in minutes) since the connector parses durations as minutes

Correlation key (process) set to =request.headers.orderId and Correlation key (payload) left blank, with Message ID expression set to =orderId so deduplication and correlation share a single source of truth

Correlation key (process) set to =orderId and Correlation key (payload) set to =request.body.orderId, with Message TTL set to PT1H as an ISO 8601 duration so each message is buffered in Zeebe for 1 hour

Correlation key (process) and Correlation key (payload) set to the same FEEL expression =orderId on both sides so the connector evaluates both expressions against the request body for symmetric correlation across the catch event

a.text: Incorrect. The process side reads from process variables (=orderId), not from the request body, and Message TTL is an ISO 8601 duration like PT1H, not a plain integer count of minutes.

b.text: Incorrect. The payload correlation key is required to extract the value from the incoming message and is not left blank; Message ID expression is for deduplication only, not for the correlation key itself.

c.text: Correct. The HTTP Webhook docs specify Correlation key (process) as a FEEL expression on process variables (=orderId) and Correlation key (payload) as a FEEL expression extracting the value from the incoming message (=request.body.orderId). Message TTL is set as an ISO 8601 duration such as PT1H to buffer messages for one hour in Zeebe.

d.text: Incorrect. The two correlation keys evaluate against different sources: the process key uses process variables and the payload key uses the incoming request; they are not symmetric copies of the same expression.

explanation: The HTTP Webhook Correlation section requires two FEEL expressions: Correlation key (process) defines the subscription correlation key against process variables, while Correlation key (payload) extracts the value from the incoming message. Message TTL accepts ISO 8601 durations such as PT1H to buffer messages in Zeebe before correlation.

scenario: Sentinel Bank, a digital bank with 250000 customers, deploys a Camunda 8.8 SaaS BPMN diagram that starts with an HTTP Webhook connector to receive transaction-approved callbacks from a fraud-screening vendor. After clicking Deploy in Web Modeler, the integration team opens the Webhooks tab in the properties panel to copy the URL the vendor must POST against.
question: Which URL pattern does the activated HTTP Webhook expose on a Camunda 8.8 SaaS cluster?

options.text: The URL follows http(s)://<base URL>/connectors/<webhook ID>, with a fixed connectors segment routed to the inbound runtime
The URL follows http(s)://<base URL>/<process ID>/<webhook ID>, embedding the BPMN process ID before the webhook path segment
The URL follows http(s)://<base URL>/<contextPath>/inbound/<webhook ID>, requiring a contextPath configured in the Helm chart
The URL follows http(s)://<base URL>/inbound/<webhook ID>, where <base URL> contains the region Id and cluster Id from the API tab

d.text: Correct. SaaS webhook URLs match http(s)://<base URL>/inbound/<webhook ID>, and the base URL embeds the region Id and cluster Id available from the API tab of the cluster's client credentials.

c.text: Incorrect. The <contextPath> segment only applies to self-managed installations where a contextPath is set in the Helm chart, and it must be omitted when no contextPath is configured; SaaS uses the simpler /inbound form.

a.text: Incorrect. The activated path segment is /inbound, not /connectors; the webhook runtime exposes inbound endpoints under the fixed /inbound prefix in both SaaS and self-managed deployments.

b.text: Incorrect. The URL does not embed the BPMN process ID; the path after /inbound is the webhook ID that was configured in the properties of the HTTP Webhook connector.

explanation: When the diagram is deployed, the HTTP Webhook becomes publicly available at http(s)://<base URL>/inbound/<webhook ID> in SaaS, where the base URL carries the region Id and cluster Id from the API tab. Self-managed installations add an optional Helm-configured <contextPath> segment between the base URL and /inbound.

scenario: Helix Health, a healthcare provider processing 12,000 orders per day from partner labs, configures a Camunda 8.8 HTTP Webhook connector to accept callbacks from a partner lab. The partner sends the API key in the Authorization header as 'Bearer <token>', and the connector must compare the extracted token against the value stored in the API Key field of the webhook configuration.
question: Which FEEL expression in the API Key locator field extracts the token from a 'Bearer <token>' Authorization header?

options.text: =request.headers.authorization to use the full Authorization header value, leaving the 'Bearer ' prefix in place when the comparison runs
=request.body.authorization to read the API key from the request body's authorization field instead of from the Authorization header
=split(request.headers.authorization, " ")[2] to take the second element of the list produced by splitting the header on the space character
=substring(request.headers.authorization, 7) to return all characters after position 7, hand-skipping the 'Bearer ' prefix length

c.text: Correct. The HTTP Webhook docs show that when the Authorization header carries a Bearer prefix, you remove it by passing the header through the FEEL split() function and selecting the second element of the resulting list with index [2], since FEEL lists are 1-based.

a.text: Incorrect. Using the raw header value passes the literal string 'Bearer <token>' as the API key, which will not match the bare token stored in the API Key field; the prefix must be stripped first.

b.text: Incorrect. The Authorization header arrives under request.headers (lower-cased), not under request.body; reading from the body yields no value for a partner that authenticates via the Authorization header.

d.text: Incorrect. The documented recipe for removing the Bearer prefix uses split() with the FEEL list index [2]; a substring call with a hard-coded offset is outside the recipe shown for this case.

explanation: The API Key locator is a FEEL expression evaluated against the inbound request that yields the value compared to the API Key field. For an Authorization header containing 'Bearer <token>', the documented expression is =split(request.headers.authorization, " ")[2], which uses split() to break the header on the space and returns the token element using the 1-based FEEL list index [2].

scenario: Cobalt Bank, a retail bank handling 3,000 requests per day from onboarding callers, models a Camunda 8.8 process where each request arrives via an HTTP Webhook callback from a KYC vendor. To prevent duplicate process instances when the vendor retries the same callback (the same Message ID arrives twice), the modeler must choose the right BPMN element to host the inbound connector.
question: Which BPMN element should host the inbound connector so retries with the same Message ID never create duplicate process instances?

options.text: A message start event, which supports a Message ID expression that derives a unique value used by Zeebe to guarantee message uniqueness for the create-process flow
A plain start event, which lets the inbound runtime deduplicate based on the upstream HTTP request ID before creating a process instance for the workflow
A boundary event attached to a service task, which absorbs duplicate retries by staying on the same waiting task across the repeated inbound webhook calls
An intermediate catch event, which correlates incoming retries into the same running process instance rather than creating a new one for the duplicate calls

a.text: Correct. The docs call out that, unlike plain start events, message start events support the Message ID expression property, which derives a unique value from the connector output that Zeebe uses to guarantee message uniqueness and avoid duplicate process-instance creation across retries.

b.text: Incorrect. Plain start events do not expose a Message ID expression, so the runtime has no built-in way to deduplicate retries at process creation; the docs steer users to message start events when idempotency matters.

d.text: Incorrect. Intermediate catch events receive messages in an already running process instance, so they cannot govern the question of whether an inbound webhook creates a new process instance for a duplicate callback.

c.text: Incorrect. Boundary events also operate within an already running process instance, so they cannot prevent a new process from being started by a duplicate inbound call landing on a process-starting element.

explanation: Inbound connectors are modeled as catch events: start, message start, intermediate catch, boundary, or receive task. When idempotency matters for process creation, the message start event is the recommended element because it supports the Message ID expression and relies on message publishing, letting Zeebe guarantee uniqueness across duplicate messages.

scenario: Crestline Marketplace, an e-commerce marketplace processing 7,500 requests per day for partner price updates, uses a Camunda 8.8 HTTP Webhook to receive POSTs at /inbound/priceUpdate?source=feedA with header X-Partner-Id: partnerA and body {"sku":"S1","price":12.50}. The Result expression must export the partner ID, the source query parameter, and the SKU as process variables.
question: Which Result expression correctly extracts the partner ID, the source query parameter, and the SKU as process variables?

options.text: ={partner: request.body."x-partner-id", source: request.body.source, sku: request.body.sku} reading every value from the request body context for the inbound call
={partner: request.headers."x-partner-id", source: connectorData.source, sku: request.body.sku} pulling the source from the connectorData object on the request
={partner: request.headers."x-partner-id", source: request.params.source, sku: request.body.sku} reading each value from its matching request context scope
={partner: headers."x-partner-id", source: params.source, sku: body.sku} dropping the request prefix when accessing headers, params, and body in the expression

c.text: Correct. The inbound webhook context exposes request.body, request.headers, and request.params; the docs show headers arrive lower-cased (e.g. myheader), so the Result expression maps the partner from request.headers, the query parameter from request.params, and the SKU from request.body.

a.text: Incorrect. Only the JSON payload appears under request.body, so headers and URL query parameters cannot be read from the body context; they live under request.headers and request.params respectively in the documented context shape.

b.text: Incorrect. connectorData is documented as a special, usually empty object reserved for rare connector-specific cases; URL query parameters are exposed under request.params, not under connectorData.

d.text: Incorrect. The webhook context is rooted at the request object, so dropping the request. prefix leaves the expressions unbound against the inbound webhook payload and the variables would resolve to nothing.

explanation: In the Activation condition, Result expression, and Response body expression you can access the inbound request via the request object: request.body.<property> for parsed JSON fields, request.headers.<lowercased-header> for HTTP headers, and request.params.<name> for URL query parameters. A separate, usually empty connectorData object holds connector-specific data.

scenario: Vector Logistics, a freight broker calling 18000 rate-quote APIs per day, uses an outbound HTTP-JSON connector in Camunda 8.8 to fetch carrier prices. The team wants 12 retries spaced 30 seconds apart per call to absorb upstream throttling, replacing the default of 3 retries executed sequentially without any delay between attempts.
question: Which BPMN-level configuration delivers 12 retries spaced 30 seconds apart for this outbound connector?

options.text: Set retries="12" on the zeebe:taskDefinition element and add a retryDelay task header bound to the ISO 8601 duration value PT30S in the element template
Set retries="12" on the zeebe:taskHeaders element and add a retryBackoff field on the task definition with the ISO 8601 duration value PT30S at deploy time
Set retries="12" in the element template properties and add a retryBackoff process variable that carries the ISO 8601 duration value PT30S at runtime
Set retries="12" on the zeebe:taskDefinition element and add a retryBackoff task header bound to the ISO 8601 duration value PT30S in the element template

d.text: Correct. The retries attribute sits on the zeebe:taskDefinition element, and the connector runtime recognises retryBackoff as a reserved task header with an ISO 8601 duration value; binding retryBackoff to PT30S therefore spaces the 12 retries 30 seconds apart instead of running them immediately.

a.text: Incorrect. The reserved task header documented for spacing retries is retryBackoff, not retryDelay; an unknown header is ignored by the connector runtime, so the retries would still run back-to-back without any delay.

c.text: Incorrect. retryBackoff is a task header on the BPMN element template, not a process variable; the connector runtime reads the value from the task header at job-failure time, not from runtime process variables on the instance.

b.text: Incorrect. The retries attribute belongs to zeebe:taskDefinition (not zeebe:taskHeaders), and retryBackoff is a task header rather than a property on the task definition; the two schema slots have been swapped here.

explanation: Connector execution defaults to 3 immediate retries on failure. To change the count, set the retries attribute on zeebe:taskDefinition in the BPMN XML. To add a delay between attempts, bind a retryBackoff task header (a reserved key handled by the connector runtime) to an ISO 8601 duration such as PT30S; the property can be hidden or made visible by changing its type.

scenario: Riverstack Tools, a developer-tools marketplace serving 8000 developers, configures a Camunda 8.8 HTTP Polling connector against api.github.com to monitor a critical GitHub issue every 10 minutes. The connector targets a single issue endpoint with Bearer token auth, must wake the process only when the issue closes, and should expose the issue URL plus a boolean derived from the 'needs review' label as BPMN process variables.
question: Which Result Expression matches the documented GitHub-issue example for capturing the issue URL and the 'needs review' label?

options.text: ={issueUrl: request.body.html_url, needsReview: list contains(request.body.labels, "needs review")} as the Result Expression configuration
={issueUrl: body.html_url, needsReview: contains(body.labels.name, "needs review")} as the Result Expression on the connector configuration
={issueUrl: body.html_url, needsReview: list contains((body.labels).name, "needs review")} as the Result Expression on the connector
={url: body.html_url, label: "needs review"} as a flat FEEL context that renames the URL field and writes the label name as a constant string

b.text: Incorrect. The FEEL contains function checks substrings on a single string value, so it cannot be applied to the labels array; list contains is the function for testing membership in a list value such as the labels names.

a.text: Incorrect. The HTTP Polling connector exposes the polled payload under the body context, not request.body; the request.* prefix is the convention for the HTTP Webhook connector rather than the polling protocol.

d.text: Incorrect. Writing the label as a constant string ignores the actual labels array on the GitHub issue, so the needsReview process variable would always carry a fixed string rather than a boolean derived from the response.

c.text: Correct. The documented GitHub-issue Result Expression uses list contains on (body.labels).name to test the 'needs review' label and assigns body.html_url to the issueUrl process variable from the polled response body context.

explanation: The HTTP Polling connector reads the polled response from the body context; the GitHub-issue example assigns body.html_url to issueUrl and uses list contains((body.labels).name, "needs review") to compute a boolean for the needsReview process variable on the waiting process instance.

scenario: Aurora Retail, an omnichannel retailer with 9000 active orders, configures a Camunda 8.8 HTTP Polling intermediate catch event to watch a shipment-status REST endpoint every 5 minutes per order. Each running instance holds orderId, the polled response has shape {"orderId":"O42","status":"OK"}, and the catch event should fire only when the polled status equals 'OK'.
question: Which configuration polls every 5 minutes, correlates by orderId, and activates only when status equals 'OK'?

options.text: Interval=PT5M with Correlation key (process)==orderId, Correlation key (payload)==body.orderId, and Activation Condition==(body.status = "OK") on the catch event
Interval=PT5M with Correlation key (process)==body.orderId, Correlation key (payload)==orderId, and Activation Condition==(body.status = "OK") on the catch event
Interval=5M with Correlation key (process)==orderId, Correlation key (payload)==body.orderId, and Activation Condition==(body.status = "OK") on the catch event
Interval=PT5M with Correlation key (process)==orderId, Correlation key (payload)==body.orderId, and Activation Condition==(orderId.status = "OK") on the catch event

a.text: Correct. Interval accepts ISO 8601 durations (PT5M = 5 minutes), Correlation key (process) is the FEEL expression that returns the subscription key from the running instance (=orderId), Correlation key (payload) extracts the same value from the polled response (=body.orderId), and Activation Condition reads body.status against the expected value.

b.text: Incorrect. The two correlation expressions are swapped; the process side must resolve the subscription key from the running instance (=orderId) and the payload side must read it from the polled body (=body.orderId).

d.text: Incorrect. The Activation Condition reads the polled payload, so the field path is body.status; orderId.status would attempt to dereference a string variable as if it were a context, which does not match the polled response shape.

c.text: Incorrect. Interval values must be ISO 8601 durations such as PT5M; a bare 5M is not a valid ISO 8601 duration, and the polling runtime cannot schedule the interval without the leading PT prefix.

explanation: The HTTP Polling connector schedules calls on an ISO 8601 Interval (the docs show PT5M, PT10M, PT1H). On the intermediate catch event you also set Correlation key (process), a FEEL expression resolved against process variables, and Correlation key (payload), a FEEL expression resolved against the polled response using the body.<field> prefix. Activation Condition decides whether the catch event fires.

scenario: Aurora Bank, a retail bank, runs Camunda 8.8 Self-Managed with 12 workers and 4 brokers behind a corporate proxy on port 3128. The platform team must choose between JVM properties and environment variables so the proxy applies at the right scope across the runtime for outbound HTTP traffic.
question: What is the documented scope difference between JVM properties and environment variables for proxy configuration in Self-Managed?

options.text: JVM properties scope the proxy per outbound URL pattern, while environment variables scope the proxy only to inbound webhook traffic received by REST-based connectors at runtime
JVM properties scope the proxy to the whole JVM and any internal HTTP client including the Zeebe client, while environment variables scope it only to the connector and REST-based connectors
JVM properties scope the proxy only to the connector runtime, while environment variables affect the whole JVM and any internal HTTP client including the Zeebe client
JVM properties scope the proxy to a single connector instance per job, while environment variables apply the proxy once per cluster startup across all brokers and workers globally

c.text: Incorrect. The scopes are reversed: JVM properties affect the whole runtime, and environment variables are the option that scopes the proxy to only the connector and REST-based connectors.

b.text: Correct. JVM properties affect the whole JVM and any internal HTTP client (including the Zeebe client), while environment variables narrow the proxy to only the connector and REST-based connectors.

d.text: Incorrect. JVM properties are not per-job or per-instance; they affect the whole runtime. Environment variables also persist continuously, not just at a one-time cluster bootstrap event.

a.text: Incorrect. Proxy configuration applies to outbound HTTP requests issued by the runtime or connector; neither option is keyed by URL pattern or restricted to inbound webhook traffic.

explanation: The REST connector documents two proxy configuration types with different scope. JVM properties affect the whole JVM and any internal HTTP client, including the Zeebe client. Environment variables affect only the connector and REST-based connectors.

docs.title: REST connector - configure a proxy server in Self-Managed

scenario: Kestrel Logistics, a carrier handling 18000 shipments per day, configures a Camunda 8.8 REST outbound connector to call a slow customs API that sometimes takes 90 seconds to respond. The integration team must allow an unlimited read wait time for these responses but keep a fast 5 second connection timeout so the worker fails quickly when the host is unreachable.
question: Which Connection timeout and Read timeout values satisfy these two requirements?

options.text: Set Connection timeout to 5 and Read timeout to 90, matching the worst observed customs API response time so any longer call fails the job immediately at the read phase
Set Connection timeout to 5 and Read timeout to 0, where 0 allows an unlimited wait time for slow responses on the read side and 5 caps the connect phase at 5 seconds
Set Connection timeout to 0 and Read timeout to 5, where 0 disables the connect attempt entirely and 5 caps the read phase at 5 seconds for every customs API call
Set Connection timeout to -1 and Read timeout to -1, where -1 is the documented sentinel for waiting indefinitely on either phase regardless of the default behaviour

c.text: Incorrect. The roles are reversed: setting Connection timeout to 0 means an unlimited wait for the connection, not a disabled connect, and Read timeout 5 caps the read phase at 5 seconds.

d.text: Incorrect. The documented sentinel for an unlimited wait time is 0, not -1; the default for both Connection timeout and Read timeout is 20 seconds when no value is specified.

b.text: Correct. Read timeout 0 allows an unlimited wait for slow responses on the read side, and Connection timeout 5 caps the connection-establishment phase at 5 seconds so unreachable hosts fail fast.

a.text: Incorrect. Read timeout 90 caps the read phase at 90 seconds and would fail any longer call; the requirement is an unlimited read wait, which the docs achieve only by setting the value to 0.

explanation: Connection timeout and Read timeout default to 20 seconds. Setting either to 0 allows an unlimited wait. Connection timeout governs establishing the connection to the server; Read timeout governs reading data from the server once the connection is open.

scenario: Granite Insurance, a self-managed insurer running 24 hours per day, deploys Camunda 8.8 behind a corporate proxy. The platform team wants the proxy to be applied only to the connector runtime so the Zeebe broker and Operate keep using direct connections; the corporate proxy listens on host proxy at port 3128 for both HTTP and HTTPS traffic.
question: Which proxy configuration scopes the proxy to the connector runtime only and not to the rest of the JVM?

options.text: Set CONNECTOR_HTTP_PROXY_HOST=proxy in the Helm chart values for Zeebe so the proxy applies to every Camunda component through the Zeebe broker process
Set CONNECTOR_HTTP_PROXY_HOST=proxy and CONNECTOR_HTTP_PROXY_PORT=3128 as environment variables on the connector deployment so only the connector traffic is proxied
Set -Dhttp.proxyHost=proxy and -Dhttp.proxyPort=3128 as JVM properties on the connector deployment so only the connector traffic is proxied internally
Set http.proxyHost=proxy and CONNECTOR_HTTP_PROXY_PORT=3128, mixing JVM and environment variable names to keep the proxy scoped to the connector runtime

b.text: Correct. The documented environment-variable configuration (CONNECTOR_HTTP_PROXY_HOST, CONNECTOR_HTTP_PROXY_PORT, plus their HTTPS counterparts) affects only the connector and REST-based connectors, which is exactly the scope the platform team needs for the corporate proxy.

c.text: Incorrect. JVM properties such as -Dhttp.proxyHost apply to the whole JVM runtime, so any HTTP client running in that JVM (including the Zeebe client) might be affected, breaking the connector-only scope the team requires.

a.text: Incorrect. CONNECTOR_HTTP_PROXY_HOST is a connector-runtime environment variable, not a Zeebe configuration setting; placing it in Zeebe values does not route Zeebe traffic through the proxy and misplaces the variable.

d.text: Incorrect. A bare http.proxyHost (without the -D prefix on the JVM command line) is not picked up as a JVM property; mixing it with the connector environment variable leaves the proxy host unset for the connector runtime.

explanation: Camunda 8.8 self-managed supports two ways to configure an outbound proxy. JVM properties (http.proxyHost / http.proxyPort) apply to the whole JVM runtime, while CONNECTOR_HTTP_PROXY_HOST / PORT environment variables (and their HTTPS counterparts) scope the proxy to the connector runtime and REST-based connectors only.

scenario: Sapphire Fintech, a fintech serving 30,000 customers in the SMB segment, uses a Camunda 8.8 outbound REST connector to call a partner's payments API protected by OAuth 2.0 client credentials. The team has stored the token endpoint, client ID, and client secret as cluster secrets named OAUTH_TOKEN_ENDPOINT, CLIENT_ID, and CLIENT_SECRET, and the partner requires the credentials to travel inside the form-encoded request body.
question: Which Authentication configuration matches the partner's OAuth 2.0 client credentials requirements?

options.text: Authentication=Bearer token; Bearer token={{secrets.CLIENT_SECRET}}; with the OAuth token endpoint and client ID configured elsewhere via custom HTTP Headers
Authentication=Basic; Username={{secrets.CLIENT_ID}}; Password={{secrets.CLIENT_SECRET}}; with the OAuth token endpoint pasted directly into the URL field of the REST request

Authentication=OAuth 2.0; token endpoint={{secrets.OAUTH_TOKEN_ENDPOINT}}; Client ID={{secrets.CLIENT_ID}}; Client secret={{secrets.CLIENT_SECRET}}; Client authentication=Send client credentials in body
Authentication=OAuth 2.0; token endpoint=secrets.OAUTH_TOKEN_ENDPOINT; Client ID=secrets.CLIENT_ID; Client secret=secrets.CLIENT_SECRET; Client authentication=Send client credentials in body

c.text: Correct. The REST connector OAuth 2.0 setup requires OAuth token endpoint, Client ID, and Client secret referenced via {{secrets.NAME}} placeholders in the Authentication section, plus an explicit Client authentication mode such as 'Send client credentials in body' chosen from the dropdown to match the partner's transport requirement.

b.text: Incorrect. Basic authentication sends a username/password pair on every request and does not perform the OAuth 2.0 client credentials exchange against the token endpoint, so no access token is obtained before calling the API.

d.text: Incorrect. Secrets must be wrapped in the placeholder syntax {{secrets.NAME}}; the bare secrets.* form is the legacy syntax called out in the connectors documentation and is not the reference form used in the OAuth fields.

a.text: Incorrect. Bearer-token authentication uses a static token pasted into the Bearer token field and has no concept of a token endpoint or client ID; it skips the client credentials exchange the partner mandates.

explanation: To configure OAuth 2.0 client credentials on the REST connector, choose OAuth 2.0 in the Authentication section and fill in OAuth token endpoint, Client ID, and Client secret using {{secrets.NAME}} placeholders. Optional Scopes and Audience may also be set, and Client authentication chooses how the credentials travel (for example, 'Send client credentials in body').

scenario: Aspen Health, a healthcare provider exchanging 1200 lab reports per day, calls a partner's intake API via a Camunda 8.8 outbound REST connector. Each call must upload a PDF report held as a Camunda document reference named labReport and also include a metadata JSON object {patient: "P1", facility: "F2"} in the same request. The partner expects multipart/form-data and a connection timeout of at most 5 seconds per call.
question: Which Request configuration uploads the PDF as multipart and meets the partner's documented expectations?

options.text: Content-Type=multipart/form-data; body=a FEEL map binding the file field name to the document reference labReport; Connection timeout=0; the JSON metadata is dropped and the connector waits without any time limit

Content-Type=multipart/form-data; body=a FEEL map containing both the labReport document reference and the metadata JSON map; Connection timeout=5; both fields will travel inside the same multipart request

Content-Type=application/json; body=a FEEL map binding the file field name to the document reference labReport; Connection timeout=5; the file is then sent as a base64-encoded binary attachment inside the JSON body

Content-Type=multipart/form-data; body=a FEEL map binding the file field name to the document reference labReport; Connection timeout=5; the JSON metadata map will be silently dropped from the multipart request

d.text: Correct. With Content-Type set to multipart/form-data, only document or plain-string body fields are sent as multipart parts; any JSON object body field (such as the metadata map) is ignored by the connector, so the PDF document upload succeeds while the JSON stays out, and a 5-second connection timeout satisfies the partner constraint.

b.text: Incorrect. The docs state that for multipart/form-data only documents and plain strings are uploaded; JSON object fields are silently ignored, so the metadata map will not travel alongside the document part in the multipart request.

c.text: Incorrect. With application/json the file is sent base64-encoded inside the JSON body, not as a true multipart binary part, so the partner's multipart/form-data expectation is not satisfied by falling back to JSON.

a.text: Incorrect. A connection timeout of 0 means waiting indefinitely, which contradicts the at-most 5-second requirement; the connection timeout must be set to 5 seconds to honor the partner constraint.

explanation: When the REST connector sends a request with Content-Type=multipart/form-data, only body fields that are document references or plain strings are uploaded as multipart parts; other fields, such as JSON objects, are ignored. With application/json (the default when Content-Type is unset), file references are base64-encoded into the JSON body. Connection timeout defaults to 20 seconds, and 0 means wait indefinitely.

scenario: Vertex Insurance, an insurer evaluating 8000 claims per week, configures a Camunda 8.8 REST outbound connector that returns a JSON body. The team needs two flat process variables, claimNumber and claimTotal, mapped from the response body so downstream exclusive gateways can route claims in under 2 seconds without traversing nested fields.
question: Which Response Mapping field and reserved temporary variable combination produces this mapping per the documentation?

options.text: Use Result Variable with the reserved name response to extract { claimNumber: response.body.number, claimTotal: response.body.total } into multiple process variables on the connector
Use Result Expression with the reserved name result to map result.body.number into claimNumber and result.body.total into claimTotal as two flat process variables for routing
Use Result Variable with the reserved name result.body to write the body directly into the two process variables claimNumber and claimTotal in one mapping step on the connector
Use Result Expression with the reserved name response to extract { claimNumber: response.body.number, claimTotal: response.body.total } into multiple process variables on the connector

a.text: Incorrect. Result Variable declares a single process variable that receives the entire connector response; it does not split a response into multiple flat process variables.

d.text: Correct. Result Expression maps the connector response into multiple process variables via a FEEL map, and inside that expression the reserved name response holds the connector result.

b.text: Incorrect. The reserved temporary name inside Result Expression is response, not result; the docs use result only as a sample user-chosen name in the Result Variable field example.

c.text: Incorrect. Result Variable assigns the whole connector result to one process variable; it cannot accept a path like result.body and split the body into multiple variables in one step.

explanation: Result Variable assigns the entire connector response to one process variable. Result Expression maps the response into multiple process variables via a FEEL map, and within that expression the reserved temporary name response holds the connector result.

docs.title: How to use connectors - variable and response mapping

scenario: Helios Insurance, an insurer processing 12000 requests per day, calls a third-party rating API from a Camunda 8.8 REST outbound connector. The team needs HTTP 404 responses to raise a BPMN error coded 404 and HTTP 500 responses to raise a BPMN error coded 500 so two distinct boundary events route each case to the correct support queue.
question: Which header configuration matches the documented HTTP errors to BPMN errors example for this requirement?

options.text: Set a header named errorExpression with a FEEL expression that returns bpmnError("404", "Got a 404") when error.code = "404", bpmnError("500", "Got a 500") when error.code = "500", else null
Set a header named bpmnErrorCodes containing the values 404,500 as a comma-separated string so the runtime auto-generates BPMN errors with those codes on each HTTP failure that occurs
Set a header named errorMapping with a FEEL list of error codes such as [404, 500] so the connector automatically raises a matching BPMN error with that code for each entry at runtime
Set a header named httpErrors with a JSON object mapping each HTTP status code to a BPMN error code, which the connector interprets at runtime without any FEEL expression being involved

c.text: Incorrect. The documented header name is errorExpression and its value is a FEEL expression, not a list of codes; the connector does not auto-map a code list to BPMN errors.

d.text: Incorrect. The documented mechanism is a header named errorExpression containing a FEEL expression; a static JSON mapping object is not interpreted by the connector at runtime.

a.text: Correct. The HTTP errors to BPMN errors recipe uses a header named errorExpression with a FEEL expression that branches on error.code and returns a bpmnError call for each handled status, else null.

b.text: Incorrect. There is no bpmnErrorCodes header in the documented example; the mechanism is a header named errorExpression containing a FEEL expression that explicitly constructs each bpmnError.

explanation: The HTTP errors to BPMN errors example uses a header named errorExpression. The header value is a FEEL expression that inspects error.code and returns a bpmnError with a code and message for each handled HTTP status, otherwise null so the regular result path continues.

docs.title: How to use connectors - BPMN error examples

scenario: Quantum SaaS, a multi-tenant SaaS platform serving 600 customers, builds a Camunda 8.8 REST URL at runtime by combining the process variable baseUrl with a tenant identifier stored as a cluster secret named TENANT_ID. The URL field must evaluate to https://my.company.domain/<tenant>/accounting when baseUrl resolves to my.company.domain at execution time.
question: Which URL field FEEL expression follows the documented secret-referencing rules?

options.text: "https://" + baseUrl + "/{{secrets.TENANT_ID}}/accounting", a plain-text concatenation without a leading = so the connector receives the rendered URL string
="https://" + baseUrl + "/" + {{secrets.TENANT_ID}} + "/accounting", leaving the placeholder unquoted so FEEL evaluates it directly to the tenant value at runtime
="https://" + baseUrl + "/" + secrets.TENANT_ID + "/accounting", using the secrets.* prefix to read the tenant identifier as if it were a normal FEEL variable
="https://" + baseUrl + "/{{secrets.TENANT_ID}}/accounting", wrapping the secret placeholder in double quotes so the engine passes it through to the connector for resolution

d.text: Correct. The docs show ="https://" + baseUrl + "/{{secrets.TENANT_ID}}/accounting": the secret placeholder is wrapped in double quotes inside the FEEL string, the engine resolves baseUrl at runtime, and the placeholder is passed through unchanged so the connector substitutes the secret at execution time.

c.text: Incorrect. The bare secrets.* form is the legacy syntax called out in a danger admonition; the supported reference is the placeholder {{secrets.NAME}}, which is not a FEEL variable and therefore cannot be read with the secrets.X form.

b.text: Incorrect. Secrets are not variables, so an unquoted {{secrets.TENANT_ID}} does not evaluate inside a FEEL expression; the placeholder must sit inside a double-quoted string so the engine treats it as text and forwards it to the connector.

a.text: Incorrect. The URL field expects a FEEL expression introduced by a leading =; without it, the value is treated as plain text, so the + operator never concatenates baseUrl with the rest of the URL.

explanation: Secrets in connector fields use the placeholder syntax {{secrets.NAME}}, and the bare secrets.* form is the legacy syntax. Secrets are not variables, so the placeholder must be wrapped in double quotes inside a FEEL expression. The engine resolves process variables in the expression and forwards the placeholder to the connector, which substitutes the secret at execution time.

scenario: Aurora Travel, a travel marketplace planning 2,500 orders per month, calls a weather REST API from a Camunda 8.8 outbound connector. The team needs three process variables from the response: cityTemp (current_weather.temperature in Celsius), cityWind (current_weather.windspeed), and cityTempF (the same temperature converted to Fahrenheit) for downstream branching in the itinerary process.
question: Which Result Expression maps the connector response into the three required process variables?

options.text: ={cityTemp: response.body.current_weather.temperature, cityWind: response.body.current_weather.windspeed, cityTempF: response.body.current_weather.temperature * 1.8 + 32}
Set the Result Variable field to response, then add cityTemp, cityWind, and cityTempF as three more Result Variable entries pointing at the same response root object
={cityTemp: body.current_weather.temperature, cityWind: body.current_weather.windspeed, cityTempF: body.current_weather.temperature * 1.8 + 32}, dropping the response root
={cityTemp: result.body.current_weather.temperature, cityWind: result.body.current_weather.windspeed, cityTempF: result.body.current_weather.temperature * 1.8 + 32}

a.text: Correct. The Result Expression FEEL map keys become process variables, and inside the expression the connector's reply is reachable via the reserved response variable, so reading response.body.current_weather.X yields the temperature and wind speed, and the Fahrenheit conversion is done inline with * 1.8 + 32.

d.text: Incorrect. The reserved name in the Result Expression scope is response, not result; using result. would not resolve against the connector's reply and all three target variables would be left unset on the process instance.

c.text: Incorrect. Inside a Result Expression the response root is required to reach the body; bare body.* expressions do not bind to the connector's reply because the expression has no access to other process variables either.

b.text: Incorrect. Result Variable is a single field that names one process variable holding the entire reply; it does not accept multiple comma-separated entries, and Result Expression is the dedicated field for mapping into several variables.

explanation: Connectors expose two response-mapping fields. Result Variable names one process variable that receives the entire reply (status, headers, body). Result Expression is a FEEL map whose keys become process variables; inside it the reserved response variable holds the reply, and you can transform values with FEEL (e.g., temperature * 1.8 + 32 for Fahrenheit).

scenario: Helix Health, a healthcare provider running 700 calls per hour to a partner lab over outbound REST, configures the Error Expression field in a Camunda 8.8 connector's Error Handling section. The team wants to understand which return values from the FEEL expression are accepted so the BPMN error boundary event behaves predictably across thousands of daily connector invocations.
question: Which return value from an Error Expression is documented as valid for the Error Handling section?

options.text: A throw of a ConnectorException directly from the FEEL expression so the connector runtime converts the Java exception into the matching BPMN boundary event
Only a context that contains errorType, errorMessage, retries, and retryBackoff because every Error Expression must produce a job-error structure to the runtime
Null, an empty context, or a context with at least a non-empty errorType and a non-empty code when the errorType value is bpmnError, as documented
Any FEEL value (a string, a number, or a list), as long as the BPMN element has a matching errorCode attribute set on its boundary event element

c.text: Correct. The docs state that the Error Expression must yield a BPMN error object that is null, an empty context, or a context with at least a non-empty errorType and a non-empty code when the errorType is bpmnError; the runtime uses this shape to decide whether to raise a BPMN error.

d.text: Incorrect. Arbitrary FEEL scalars (strings, numbers, lists) are not the documented shape; the expression must yield null, an empty context, or a structured BPMN error object with the required fields.

b.text: Incorrect. errorType, errorMessage, retries, and retryBackoff describe the jobError context (a failing-job shape); the Error Expression accepts either bpmnError or jobError outcomes, so the jobError fields are not always mandatory.

a.text: Incorrect. FEEL Error Expressions cannot throw Java exceptions; the documented helpers are the bpmnError and jobError FEEL functions, which return context values that the connector runtime interprets afterward.

explanation: The Error Expression is a FEEL expression in the Error Handling section that must yield a BPMN error object. Allowed return values are null, an empty context, or a context containing at least a non-empty errorType and a non-empty code (when errorType is bpmnError). The bpmnError and jobError FEEL helpers construct these contexts.

scenario: Beacon Energy, a utility serving 90,000 customers across the region, calls a tariff REST API from a Camunda 8.8 outbound connector. On a transient failure the team wants the connector job to fail with the message 'tariff API down', retry 2 more times with a 30-second backoff between attempts, and they do not need to attach any extra variables to the failure context.
question: Which jobError() call produces a job that retries twice with a 30-second backoff and no extra variables?

options.text: jobError("tariff API down", retries=2, retryBackoff=@"PT30S"), using named parameter syntax so the variables default of {} is kept implicitly in the call
jobError("tariff API down", {}, 2, @"PT30S"), explicitly passing an empty variables context so the retries and retryBackoff positional parameters can be set
jobError("tariff API down", @"PT30S", 2), passing the retry backoff duration first and the retries count second to override the documented defaults
jobError("tariff API down", 2, @"PT30S"), skipping the variables parameter and passing only the retries count and the retryBackoff positional arguments

b.text: Correct. jobError() accepts positional parameters in the order errorMessage, variables, retries, retryBackoff; optional parameters can only be omitted if no later parameter is supplied, so to set retries=2 and retryBackoff=PT30S the empty variables context {} must be passed explicitly.

d.text: Incorrect. With positional arguments, omitting variables makes the runtime interpret the number 2 as the variables parameter, which expects a context (not a number); the empty context must be passed explicitly to reach the retries slot.

a.text: Incorrect. The documented jobError() signature uses positional parameters, not named parameters such as retries= or retryBackoff=; named-argument syntax is not part of this function's calling convention in the docs.

c.text: Incorrect. The documented parameter order after errorMessage is variables, retries, retryBackoff; swapping retries and retryBackoff would treat the duration as the variables value and the number as the retries default, breaking the call shape.

explanation: jobError(errorMessage, variables, retries, retryBackoff) returns a context with errorType="jobError", errorMessage, variables (default {}), retries (default 0), and retryBackoff (default PT0S). Optional parameters can be omitted only when no later parameter is provided, so to specify retries and retryBackoff you must also include an explicit variables context.

scenario: Pinnacle Retail, a retailer processing 50000 catalog lookups per day, calls a product-info REST API from a Camunda 8.8 outbound connector. When the partner returns HTTP 404 the team wants a 'Got a 404' BPMN error, on HTTP 500 a 'Got a 500' BPMN error, and on a body-level status of "failed" a 'FAILED' BPMN error that also forwards the response body to the boundary handler for inspection. The platform serves 12 tenants and processes 8400 transactions per day.
question: Which errorExpression header value covers all three documented HTTP-to-BPMN-error mappings?

options.text: if error.code = "404" then bpmnError("404", "Got a 404") else if error.code = "500" then bpmnError("500", "Got a 500") else if response.body.status = "failed" then bpmnError("FAILED", "Action failed", response.body) else null

if error.code = 404 then bpmnError(404, "Got a 404") else if error.code = 500 then bpmnError(500, "Got a 500") else if response.body.status = "failed" then bpmnError("FAILED", "Action failed") else null
if error.code = "404" then bpmnError("404", "Got a 404") else if error.code = "500" then bpmnError("500", "Got a 500") else if response.body.status = "failed" then jobError("Action failed") else null
bpmnError(error.code, error.message, response.body) as a single FEEL expression that maps every failure to one BPMN error and leaves the boundary event to route 404, 500, and failed cases centrally

a.text: Correct. The docs show this exact chained FEEL expression as the canonical errorExpression header, mapping HTTP 404 and 500 to distinct BPMN errors and a body-level status of "failed" to a FAILED BPMN error that also forwards response.body as the third bpmnError argument for downstream inspection.

b.text: Incorrect. error.code is a string in the connector's error context, so comparing it to the integer 404 never matches; the bpmnError code argument is also documented as a string, and the response body would be dropped without the third argument.

c.text: Incorrect. jobError fails the job and triggers retries; the requirement is to raise a BPMN error so a boundary event can handle the FAILED case, so bpmnError is the documented helper for this third branch as well.

d.text: Incorrect. A single bpmnError for every failure cannot distinguish 404 from 500 or detect a body-level status of "failed"; the chained if/else in the documented recipe is required to route the three cases differently.

explanation: The HTTP-errors-to-BPMN-errors recipe sets a task header named errorExpression to a chained FEEL expression that tests error.code (a string) and response.body fields, returning bpmnError(code, message[, variables]) for matching branches and null otherwise. Passing response.body as the third bpmnError argument forwards the partner's body to the boundary event for handling.

scenario: Helios Logistics, a carrier handling 45000 shipments per day, models a Camunda 8.8 receipt review process in Web Modeler. A developer must replace the initial Notify manager task with the SendGrid Email connector so the manager is notified about each uploaded receipt, using an API key obtained from a SendGrid account.
question: Which sequence does the docs prescribe for adding the SendGrid Email connector to the start event?

options.text: Add a generic service task next to the start event, then use the Change type menu to convert that task into a SendGrid Email connector before filling in the API key and email content

Drag a SendGrid Email shape from the connectors palette directly onto the start event so it overrides the existing task, then enter the API key and recipient in a dialog pop-up that opens automatically
Open the BPMN XML in Web Modeler, edit the start event to set connectorRef to sendgrid, then save and redeploy the diagram so the API key and recipient fields appear in the right-hand panel

Click the start event to reveal the context pad, click Append connector, select the SendGrid Email connector option, then fill in the API key, sender, receiver, and email content in the properties panel

c.text: Incorrect. The documented procedure stays inside the Web Modeler UI and uses the context pad and properties panel; it does not require editing the BPMN XML or setting a connectorRef attribute by hand.

a.text: Incorrect. The docs do not describe adding a generic service task first and then converting it; a connector is appended directly from the context pad of the existing element.

d.text: Correct. The docs describe clicking the start event to show the context pad, choosing Append connector, selecting the SendGrid Email connector option, and filling out the required information (API key, sender, receiver, email content) in the properties panel on the right.

b.text: Incorrect. There is no documented drag-and-drop palette overlay nor an auto-opening pop-up dialog; configuration happens in the right-hand properties panel of the newly appended task.

explanation: To add an out-of-the-box connector in Web Modeler, click the element to reveal its context pad, click Append connector, pick the desired connector (such as SendGrid Email), and complete the required fields in the properties panel on the right of the screen.

docs.title: Configuring an out-of-the-box connector - Add a connector

scenario: Lattice Logistics, a logistics platform processing 4500 shipments per day, builds a custom Camunda 8.8 connector packaged as connector.jar and deploys the official camunda/connectors Docker image to run it as a long-running job worker against its Zeebe cluster.
question: How should the team add their custom connector.jar to the camunda/connectors Docker runtime image at startup?

options.text: Mount the JAR as a volume with -v $PWD/connector.jar:/opt/app/connector.jar so the file lands on the runtime classpath when the camunda/connectors container starts up
Pass the JAR contents as a base64 environment variable through --env-file secrets.txt so the camunda/connectors image decodes and loads the file when it boots
Rebuild the camunda/connectors Docker image with a COPY step that bakes connector.jar into the image and republish a forked tag before running docker run with the new tag
Set CAMUNDA_CONNECTOR_POLLING_ENABLED=false at startup so the runtime fetches connector.jar from the Zeebe gateway over the configured Docker network bridge

a.text: Correct. The docs show docker run mounting $PWD/connector.jar to /opt/app/connector.jar with -v, which adds the connector JAR to the classpath of the prebuilt camunda/connectors image when the container starts.

c.text: Incorrect. The documented approach uses a volume mount, not a forked image rebuild; rebuilding is only described for the runtime image itself, not for loading a connector JAR.

d.text: Incorrect. CAMUNDA_CONNECTOR_POLLING_ENABLED=false is the documented switch for disabling inbound connectors, not a mechanism that transports JAR bytes from Zeebe to the runtime container.

b.text: Incorrect. The --env-file flag is documented for setting secrets as environment variables on the container; it does not ship a JAR payload into the runtime image.

explanation: The camunda/connectors Docker runtime image is started with docker run and a -v volume mount that places connector.jar into /opt/app/connector.jar, which is how the custom connector is added to the classpath of the prepackaged runtime.

scenario: Helios Bank, a digital bank handling 250000 transactions per day on Camunda 8.8, embeds a custom outbound connector inside an existing Java service that already owns a CamundaClient instance, and needs Result Variable, Result Expression, and Error Expression behavior to match the prepackaged connector runtime exactly.
question: Which SDK class should the team wrap MyConnectorFunction with so the custom job worker keeps Result Variable, Result Expression, and Error Expression behavior?

options.text: Register MyConnectorFunction via the io.camunda.connector.api.secret.SecretProvider SPI and let the CamundaClient auto-discover the function as the job worker handler on startup
Pass MyConnectorFunction to OutboundConnectorContext.newWorker(...) directly so the SDK constructs a job worker without going through the ConnectorJobHandler wrapper class
Extend AbstractConnectorContext and pass an instance of that subclass directly to camundaClient.newWorker().handler(...) so the worker reuses the prepackaged context for result mapping
Wrap MyConnectorFunction in a new ConnectorJobHandler with a DefaultValidationProvider and pass that as the handler argument to camundaClient.newWorker().handler(...) when opening the worker

c.text: Incorrect. AbstractConnectorContext is a base class for building a context inside a custom wrapper; it is not itself a JobHandler and the docs do not pass it to newWorker().handler(...).

d.text: Correct. The SDK lists ConnectorJobHandler as the wrapper class that adapts a connector function into a Zeebe job handler and supplies Result Variable, Result Expression, and Error Expression handling, used as new ConnectorJobHandler(new MyConnectorFunction(), new DefaultValidationProvider()).

a.text: Incorrect. SecretProvider is an SPI for supplying secret values to the connector, not for registering a function as the job worker handler with the Camunda client.

b.text: Incorrect. OutboundConnectorContext is a context object the wrapper builds for the function; it does not expose a newWorker(...) builder for the job worker.

explanation: ConnectorJobHandler is the SDK wrapper class that exposes a connector function as a Zeebe job handler. It supplies an OutboundConnectorContext, environment-variable-based secret management via SPI, Result Variable and Result Expression mapping, and Error Expression handling without bespoke code.

scenario: Vertex Insurance, an insurer processing 12000 claims per day, starts a new custom outbound connector project for Camunda 8.8 using Maven. A developer wants to add the Connector SDK dependency manually instead of cloning the GitHub template, so the team can keep its existing repository layout untouched while still implementing OutboundConnectorFunction.
question: Which Maven coordinates does the Connector SDK documentation specify for the core dependency?

options.text: groupId org.camunda.bpm, artifactId camunda-connect-core, version ${version.camunda}, with the connect-plugins module added separately to enable the SDK annotations
groupId io.camunda.zeebe, artifactId zeebe-connector-sdk, version ${version.zeebe}, declared under a runtime scope so it ships with the Zeebe broker at deploy time
groupId io.camunda, artifactId camunda-connector-runtime, version ${version.connectors}, declared as a parent BOM import rather than as a normal dependency entry
groupId io.camunda.connector, artifactId connector-core, version ${version.connectors}, declared as a normal Maven dependency in the project pom.xml file

b.text: Incorrect. The SDK is not published under io.camunda.zeebe and is not a runtime artifact shipped with the broker; the documented coordinates use the connector groupId and artifact connector-core.

c.text: Incorrect. The docs show a regular dependency, not a BOM import, and the artifactId is connector-core rather than camunda-connector-runtime.

a.text: Incorrect. camunda-connect-core belongs to the legacy Camunda 7 Connect framework; the Camunda 8.8 Connector SDK uses io.camunda.connector:connector-core.

d.text: Correct. The Connector SDK setup documentation lists a Maven dependency with groupId io.camunda.connector, artifactId connector-core, and version ${version.connectors}, added directly to the project pom.xml.

explanation: To set up a Connector SDK project manually, add the io.camunda.connector:connector-core dependency at version ${version.connectors} to your Maven pom.xml (or the equivalent Gradle implementation line). The templates on GitHub use the same coordinates.

scenario: Meridian Payments, a fintech routing 9000 transactions per minute, is structuring a new outbound connector project for Camunda 8.8. A senior engineer reviews a pull request and needs to confirm which file makes the OutboundConnectorFunction class discoverable to the connector runtime at startup, so the runtime can auto-load the function without manual wiring.
question: Which file in the outbound connector project outline exposes the function class to the connector runtime?

options.text: The element-templates/template-connector.json file at the project root, which declares zeebe:taskDefinition type so the runtime can resolve the function class by template id alone
The META-INF/services/io.camunda.connector.api.outbound.OutboundConnectorFunction file under src/main/resources, which lists the function class name for the Java ServiceLoader SPI
The pom.xml configuration file at the project root, which declares io.camunda.connector:connector-core as a dependency and registers the function via a Maven plugin during package time
The MyConnectorRequest.java input data object under src/main/java/io/camunda/connector, where an @OutboundConnector annotation tells the runtime which function class implements the worker

c.text: Incorrect. The pom.xml manages dependencies and build configuration but does not expose the function class to the runtime; that role belongs to the ServiceLoader SPI file.

b.text: Correct. The docs require exposing the function class name in the OutboundConnectorFunction SPI file under src/main/resources/META-INF/services so the Java ServiceLoader can discover it for a detectable connector function.

a.text: Incorrect. The element template declares the modeling UI and binds the task definition type but it does not register the runtime function class via the SPI mechanism.

d.text: Incorrect. The request class is an input data object; the @OutboundConnector annotation lives on the function class itself and the SPI services file is what makes it discoverable.

explanation: The outbound connector project outline places the runtime function discovery file at src/main/resources/META-INF/services/io.camunda.connector.api.outbound.OutboundConnectorFunction. That file lists the function class so the Java ServiceLoader SPI can auto-detect it.

docs.title: Connector SDK - Outbound connector project outline

scenario: At Zenith Telco, a telco managing 3000 microservices, a connector author writes an outbound element template for Camunda 8.8. The author needs to map a user-supplied Result Expression into process variables and an Error Expression into BPMN errors using exactly 2 reserved task header keys defined by the SDK.
question: Which binding does the SDK specify for the Result Expression and Error Expression fields in an outbound connector element template?

options.text: Bind Result Expression as a zeebe:output with source resultExpression and Error Expression as a zeebe:output with source errorExpression so the FEEL engine writes them into the audit log
Bind Result Expression as a zeebe:input with name resultExpression and Error Expression as a zeebe:input with name errorExpression, evaluated against process variables at job activation
Bind Result Expression as a zeebe:taskHeader with key resultExpression and Error Expression as a zeebe:taskHeader with key errorExpression so the runtime can read both as custom headers
Bind Result Expression as a zeebe:taskDefinition with property resultExpression and Error Expression as a zeebe:property with name errorExpression scoped to the inbound subtype

c.text: Correct. The SDK shows that Result Expression and Error Expression are bound as zeebe:taskHeader entries with keys resultExpression and errorExpression. The connector runtime picks up these custom headers and translates them into process variables and BPMN errors accordingly.

b.text: Incorrect. zeebe:input bindings produce ordinary connector input variables, not the reserved header keys the runtime uses to drive result mapping and BPMN error handling.

d.text: Incorrect. zeebe:taskDefinition only carries the task type, and zeebe:property scoped to an inbound subtype is used by inbound connector templates, not for outbound result and error mapping.
