a.text: Incorrect. There is no zeebe:output binding for these fields; the SDK explicitly uses zeebe:taskHeader with the resultExpression and errorExpression keys.

explanation: In outbound connector element templates, Result Variable, Result Expression, and Error Expression are exposed as zeebe:taskHeader bindings with the reserved keys resultVariable, resultExpression, and errorExpression. The connector runtime reads these headers and applies result mapping and BPMN error translation.

docs.title: Connector SDK - Outbound connector element template

scenario: At Orion Healthcare, a hospital network running 4 Camunda 8.8 clusters, a junior developer scaffolds a new inbound connector project. A reviewer asks which SPI file under META-INF/services must list the executable class so the connector runtime can auto-detect the inbound function at startup time.
question: Which META-INF services file does the inbound connector project outline use to register the executable class?

options.text: META-INF/services/io.camunda.connector.api.inbound.InboundConnectorContext, where the connector lists the context class that the runtime should inject into each executable instance
META-INF/services/io.camunda.connector.api.inbound.InboundConnectorExecutable, which lists the inbound executable class so the Java ServiceLoader SPI can discover it at startup
META-INF/services/io.camunda.connector.runtime.inbound.SubscriptionStarter, a runtime-only entry point that registers subscriptions before the executable class is loaded by the runtime
META-INF/services/io.camunda.connector.api.outbound.OutboundConnectorFunction, which is the same SPI file used by outbound connectors when their runtime class is exposed

d.text: Incorrect. That file is the SPI entry for outbound connector functions, not for inbound executables; inbound connectors use a separate services file.

b.text: Correct. The inbound connector project outline places the SPI registration at src/main/resources/META-INF/services/io.camunda.connector.api.inbound.InboundConnectorExecutable, listing the executable class for the Java ServiceLoader.

c.text: Incorrect. There is no SubscriptionStarter SPI in the documented outline; the standard mechanism is the InboundConnectorExecutable services file.

a.text: Incorrect. InboundConnectorContext is an interface that the runtime passes into activate(); it is not registered through the services file used for discovery of the executable class.

explanation: The inbound connector project outline relies on Java's ServiceLoader for discovery. The author must add the executable class name to META-INF/services/io.camunda.connector.api.inbound.InboundConnectorExecutable so the connector runtime can find it.

docs.title: Connector SDK - Inbound connector project outline

scenario: Stellar Insurance, an insurer ingesting 50000 events per day from a partner system, builds a custom inbound connector for Camunda 8.8. A developer authors the element template and must bind the runtime type io.camunda:mytestinbound:1 so the connector runtime can match incoming subscriptions to the correct executable class at activation time.
question: Which template property binding does the SDK specify for the inbound runtime type value io.camunda:mytestinbound:1?

options.text: A visible String property labelled Type, bound as a zeebe:taskDefinition with property type so the inbound subscription is routed by Zeebe job type at activation time
A Text property bound as a zeebe:taskHeader with key inbound.type, written into the job custom header so the executable can be discovered by the InboundConnectorExecutable SPI at runtime
A Dropdown property bound as a zeebe:input with name inbound.type, where the value io.camunda:mytestinbound:1 appears as one of the editable choices for users in Web Modeler
A Hidden property with value io.camunda:mytestinbound:1, bound as a zeebe:property with name inbound.type so the runtime resolves the executable class from this connection value

a.text: Incorrect. Inbound connectors are not driven by zeebe:taskDefinition; that binding is used for outbound connectors and service tasks, not for inbound subscriptions.

c.text: Incorrect. The type definition is not a user-editable Dropdown bound to zeebe:input; it is a Hidden property carrying a fixed connection value via zeebe:property.

b.text: Incorrect. The binding type is zeebe:property with name inbound.type, not a zeebe:taskHeader key written to the job; inbound discovery does not use task headers for the type.

d.text: Correct. The inbound element template defines a Hidden property whose value is io.camunda:mytestinbound:1 and whose binding is type zeebe:property with name inbound.type. This is the connection that configures which version of the runtime executable to use.

explanation: An inbound connector element template carries the runtime type as a Hidden property with a fixed value and a zeebe:property binding to the name inbound.type. This identifies the executable class version that should handle the subscription, in the same way the outbound binding via zeebe:taskDefinition selects an outbound function.

docs.title: Connector SDK - Inbound connector element template

scenario: Kestrel Marketplace, a marketplace receiving 7000 messages per minute from an external publisher, implements a custom inbound connector in Camunda 8.8. A developer must wire the executable so that the activate method starts an asynchronous subscription and the deactivate method releases all the resources used by that subscription when the connector instance is shut down.
question: Which pair of responsibilities does the InboundConnectorExecutable interface define for the activate and deactivate methods?

options.text: activate spawns a synchronous polling loop that returns once the first event has been correlated, and deactivate forces a one-time replay of any unhandled events from the subscription buffer
activate triggers asynchronous listening for inbound events using the InboundConnectorContext, and deactivate is a graceful shutdown hook that must release all resources used by the subscription
activate registers the executable class via the OutboundConnectorFunction SPI, and deactivate removes that registration so the same class can be reactivated under a different connector type
activate validates the connector input against the Jakarta Bean Validation API only, and deactivate publishes a final correlation result back to Zeebe before the subscription thread is terminated

b.text: Correct. The docs say the activate method is a trigger function to start listening to inbound events and its implementation has to be asynchronous, while the deactivate method is a graceful shutdown hook that must release all resources used by the subscription.

d.text: Incorrect. Validation can happen via bindProperties but is not the sole job of activate, and deactivate does not publish a final correlation result; it just releases subscription resources.

a.text: Incorrect. activate is asynchronous and does not return after the first event; deactivate is a shutdown hook, not a replay mechanism for unhandled events.

c.text: Incorrect. SPI registration lives in META-INF/services for the InboundConnectorExecutable interface and is not the runtime job of activate or deactivate.

explanation: The InboundConnectorExecutable interface defines activate(context) as an asynchronous trigger that begins listening for inbound events and using the context to correlate them, and deactivate() as a graceful shutdown hook that must release all subscription resources.

docs.title: Connector SDK - Inbound connector runtime logic

scenario: Pinnacle Insurance, an insurer correlating 25000 events per hour to active process instances in Camunda 8.8, runs a custom inbound connector. A reviewer wants to verify how the executable should react when correlateWithResult returns a Failure, because the handling strategy is what decides whether the connector should report the problem upstream or swallow it silently.
question: How does the SDK describe the role of handlingStrategy on the CorrelationResult returned by correlateWithResult?

options.text: handlingStrategy is a callback the executable must invoke at activate time to register custom handlers, after which the runtime stops returning Failure values for the rest of the subscription lifecycle
handlingStrategy is derived by the connector runtime from user configuration and tells the executable whether a Failure should be forwarded to the upstream system or simply ignored
handlingStrategy is a fixed legacy enum that returns the same constant for every failure, with the runtime always logging the error and never forwarding it to the upstream system in any case
handlingStrategy is a Zeebe broker hint that controls how long the correlation is retried inside the engine before the subscription is closed and the executable is marked as failed by the runtime

d.text: Incorrect. handlingStrategy is not a broker retry hint; it is delivered with each CorrelationResult and only tells the executable how to react to a failed correlation.

c.text: Incorrect. The strategy can take more than one value and the runtime explicitly supports forwarding errors upstream, so it is neither a constant nor a deprecated field.

b.text: Correct. The docs state that the handling strategy is derived by the connector runtime based on user configuration and defines whether a Failure should forward the error to the upstream system or ignore it, which is exactly how the example handleResult branches on ForwardErrorToUpstream and Ignore.

a.text: Incorrect. handlingStrategy is a value on the failure result, not a callback registered in activate, and Failure values continue to be possible throughout the subscription lifecycle.

explanation: When correlateWithResult returns a Failure, the CorrelationResult carries a handlingStrategy that the runtime derives from user configuration. The executable inspects that strategy to decide whether to forward the error to the upstream system (ForwardErrorToUpstream) or to ignore it (Ignore).

docs.title: Connector SDK - Inbound connector runtime logic

scenario: Tessera Bank, a bank operating 3 Self-Managed Camunda 8.8 clusters, evaluates how to run a set of custom outbound connector functions in production. The platform team wants to know which runtime options Camunda lists for self-managed deployments and what specific role SaaS plays in the same picture, because the platform engineers do not want to write their own wrapper unless the pre-packaged options do not fit the use case.
question: Which combination correctly describes the runtime environment options for Camunda 8.8 connectors?

options.text: On SaaS each cluster runs a runtime component that knows the OOTB connectors; on Self-Managed the user provides the runtime via the Spring Boot Starter, the Docker runtime image, or a Custom runtime environment

On SaaS clusters run a built-in runtime component; on Self-Managed the user must always deploy the same Docker runtime image, while the Spring Boot Starter is only intended for unit tests and local laptops
On SaaS each cluster runs a built-in runtime; on Self-Managed the only available option is to build a custom runtime environment from scratch, with no packaged runtime artifact shipped by the SDK

On both SaaS and Self-Managed the runtime is part of the Zeebe broker process, so connector functions are invoked by the broker directly and no separate runtime environment is required for either deployment model

c.text: Incorrect. The docs explicitly list a Spring Boot Starter and a Docker runtime image as packaged Self-Managed options alongside the custom runtime route.

d.text: Incorrect. The connector runtime is a separate component from the Zeebe broker; Self-Managed deployments must explicitly provide one of the runtime options.

b.text: Incorrect. The Spring Boot Starter is a first-class production option, not a tests-only artifact, and Self-Managed users are not restricted to the Docker image.

a.text: Correct. The docs say that in Camunda 8 SaaS every cluster runs a runtime component that knows the available out-of-the-box connectors and how to invoke them, and that for Self-Managed there are several options provided by Camunda: the Spring Boot Starter runtime, the Docker runtime image, and a Custom runtime environment.

explanation: Camunda 8.8 SaaS clusters bundle the connector runtime so users do not provision one. For Self-Managed installations the SDK lists three runtime options: the Spring Boot Starter runtime, the Docker runtime image, and a Custom runtime environment for use cases not covered by the first two.

scenario: Junction Bank, a bank running 4 custom connectors across 8000 transactions per day in Camunda 8.8 Self-Managed, builds a custom runtime environment because its serverless platform does not match the pre-packaged options. A developer wires the connector into a Zeebe worker and wants to confirm which class the SDK exposes to wrap a connector function as a job handler with built-in secret management and result mapping.
question: Which SDK class does the Custom runtime environment guide recommend wrapping the connector function with?

options.text: AbstractConnectorContext, subclassed directly as the job handler so the custom runtime inherits the SDK's variable, secret, and result mapping behaviour without any further wrapping required
OutboundConnectorContextBuilder, instantiated for each job and registered as the handler so it can supply secrets and a fresh OutboundConnectorContext to the connector function on every invocation
DefaultValidationProvider, used as the job handler entry point because it validates input data first and then delegates the actual job acknowledgement back to the Zeebe Java client at the end
ConnectorJobHandler, wrapped around the connector function and registered as the handler on a Zeebe worker for the job type defined by the element template

c.text: Incorrect. DefaultValidationProvider is an SDK validation provider passed into the job handler, not the wrapper class itself, and it does not own the job acknowledgement loop.

b.text: Incorrect. OutboundConnectorContextBuilder is a test helper that builds a context for unit tests; it is not the production wrapper used to register a Zeebe worker.

d.text: Correct. The Custom runtime environment guide explains that the SDK provides the ConnectorJobHandler wrapper class to expose a connector function as a job worker, registered on a Zeebe worker for the job type defined by the element template.

a.text: Incorrect. AbstractConnectorContext is meant to be extended when implementing your own context, not as a job handler in itself; the documented wrapper is ConnectorJobHandler.

explanation: The Custom runtime environment guide tells authors to wrap connector functions in ConnectorJobHandler and register the handler on a Zeebe worker for the connector's job type. The wrapper provides an OutboundConnectorContext with secret management, result mapping, and BPMN error handling.

scenario: At fintech vendor Larkspur Pay, 3 developers compare Camunda 8.8 connector documentation and trip over two near-identical names: the Connector templates that ship with OOTB connectors and the connector-template-outbound repository on GitHub used as a project skeleton when building a new custom connector from source.
question: Which categorization correctly distinguishes a connector template from the connector-template-outbound boilerplate repository?

options.text: A connector template is an element template that customizes how a BPMN element is shown and configured, whereas connector-template-outbound supplies boilerplate code and configuration for new custom connectors
A connector template is the OOTB connector implementation in a JAR, whereas the connector-template-outbound repository is the FEEL Error Expression file injected into BPMN tasks at runtime
A connector template is a piece of boilerplate Java code, whereas the connector-template-outbound repository is the JSON configuration loaded into the modeler properties panel for OOTB connectors
A connector template is an Operate dashboard widget, whereas connector-template-outbound is the runtime classpath JAR that the camunda/connectors Docker image loads at container startup

c.text: Incorrect. The docs define a connector template as a specific type of element template (JSON), not Java boilerplate; the connector-template-outbound repository is the boilerplate code source for new custom connectors.

b.text: Incorrect. OOTB connectors and their templates are distinct: the template is a JSON element template that shapes modeler UI, not a JAR implementation, and the boilerplate repo is not a FEEL Error Expression.

a.text: Correct. The docs state that connector templates are a specific type of element template that customizes how a BPMN element is shown and configured, and add a note that the Connector template (connector-template-outbound) is used to supply boilerplate code and configuration when developing a new custom connector.

d.text: Incorrect. Connector templates configure modeler UI for BPMN elements; they are not Operate widgets, and the boilerplate repository is not the runtime classpath JAR.

explanation: The docs explicitly contrast Connector templates (element templates that shape modeler UI and configuration for BPMN elements, also usable with the Connector SDK) with the Connector template repository (connector-template-outbound), which provides boilerplate code and configuration for developing a new custom connector.

scenario: Pinecrest Insurance, an insurer receiving 800 claims per day via a partner webhook, models a Camunda 8.8 BPMN start event with the HTTP Webhook inbound connector template and wants the start event to fire only when the payload field claim.amount is greater than 500 euros, ignoring all other webhook calls.
question: Where in the inbound start event connector template should the team place the FEEL filter so the start event only triggers for qualifying claims?

options.text: In the Variables property bound to inbound.variableMapping, returning null for non-qualifying claims so the start event silently drops the inbound request payload
In the Webhook ID property bound to inbound.context, as a FEEL expression appended to the webhook ID string that forms the public URL path segment
In the HMAC algorithm dropdown bound to inbound.hmacAlgorithm, picking a custom value that rejects payloads where claim.amount stays below the 500 euro threshold
In the Condition property bound to inbound.activationCondition, as a FEEL expression that evaluates the incoming webhook payload before the start event actually triggers

b.text: Incorrect. The Webhook ID property holds the URL path segment and binds as a plain String to inbound.context; the docs do not describe it as a FEEL trigger filter on the payload.

a.text: Incorrect. The Variables property is for mapping the payload into process variables through inbound.variableMapping; the docs do not describe it as gating whether the start event fires.

c.text: Incorrect. The HMAC algorithm dropdown selects sha_1, sha_256, or sha_512 for signature verification; the docs do not describe it as a business filter on payload values.

d.text: Correct. The inbound start event template defines a Condition property in the activation group bound to inbound.activationCondition with feel: required, described as the condition under which the connector triggers; leaving it empty catches all events.

explanation: In the inbound start event template, the activation group includes a Condition field bound to inbound.activationCondition with feel: required. A FEEL expression there decides whether the webhook event triggers the start event; an empty value catches all events.

scenario: At healthcare SaaS Marigold Health, 6 developers fork the outbound REST connector template in Camunda 8.8 before extending it. They notice that some template properties end up as static metadata on the BPMN element while others must reach the job worker as input variables, and want to map the binding types correctly.
question: Which binding type does the outbound REST connector template use for properties that the job worker reads as input variables on the service task?

options.text: zeebe:taskHeader, used for the Authentication Type, Username, Password, and Bearer Token so the worker reads them as headers statically attached to the service task
zeebe:taskDefinition, used for the Request Body, Username, Password, and Bearer Token so the worker resolves them through the task definition type at deployment time
zeebe:property, used for the REST Endpoint URL, REST Method, and Request Body so the worker reads them as message subscription properties at activation time
zeebe:input, used for the Request Body, Authentication Type, Username, Password, and Bearer Token so the worker reads them as input variable mappings on the service task

c.text: Incorrect. zeebe:property is not used by this template at all; URL and Method bind to zeebe:taskHeader and Body binds to zeebe:input, so they do not flow as message subscription properties.

b.text: Incorrect. zeebe:taskDefinition is used only for the hidden value http that sets the task type; it does not carry body or authentication input values to the worker.

a.text: Incorrect. zeebe:taskHeader carries the static REST Endpoint URL and REST Method, not the body or authentication.* fields, which the template binds via zeebe:input.

d.text: Correct. In the outbound REST template, Request Body, Authentication Type, Username, Password, and Bearer Token all bind to zeebe:input with names body, authentication.type, authentication.username, authentication.password, and authentication.token.

explanation: The outbound REST connector template uses three binding types: zeebe:taskDefinition for the hidden http task type, zeebe:taskHeader for the static REST URL and method values, and zeebe:input for runtime variable mappings such as the body and the authentication.* properties read by the job worker.

scenario: Beacon Marketplace, a marketplace onboarding 3 new partner integrations per quarter across 5000 events per day on Camunda 8.8, plans a roadmap of custom connector templates. A solution architect must choose the right template style for: starting a process from an external service callback, continuing a running process from an external event, and triggering an outbound REST API from a service task.
question: Which mapping of use case to connector template type matches what the documentation lists?

options.text: Starting a process maps to inbound start event template, continuing from an external event maps to inbound intermediate catch event template, and triggering an external service maps to outbound connector template
Starting a process and continuing from an external event both map to the same inbound start event template, and triggering an external service uses the legacy ServiceTaskConnector template instead

Starting a process maps to inbound boundary event template, continuing from an external event maps to outbound connector template, and triggering an external service maps to inbound intermediate catch event template

Starting a process maps to outbound connector template, continuing from an external event maps to inbound start event template, and triggering an external service maps to inbound intermediate catch event template

d.text: Incorrect. Starting a BPMN process from an external service is the role of the inbound start event template, not an outbound template; this option swaps the inbound and outbound categories.

a.text: Correct. The docs list exactly these three options: an inbound start event connector template for starting a BPMN process from an external service, an inbound intermediate catch event template for continuing a running process from an external event, and an outbound connector template for triggering an external service.

b.text: Incorrect. Inbound start event and inbound intermediate catch event templates are distinct categories, and there is no legacy ServiceTaskConnector template in the docs.

c.text: Incorrect. The boundary event template is for boundary events, not for starting processes, and outbound connectors trigger external services rather than catching external events on an intermediate catch event.

explanation: Connector templates fall into three documented categories: inbound start event templates for triggering a new process from an external service, inbound intermediate catch event templates for continuing a process from an external event, and outbound connector templates for triggering an external service from a service task.

scenario: Nimbus Insurance, an insurer ingesting 4500 claims per day via a partner webhook, ships an HTTP Webhook element template that starts a new Camunda 8.8 process whenever the partner posts a claim notification. A template author must set the appliesTo so the template only attaches to start events, not to service tasks or intermediate catch events.
question: Which appliesTo value does the inbound start event Webhook connector template documentation use?

options.text: appliesTo of [bpmn:StartEvent], with elementType value bpmn:StartEvent, so Web Modeler offers the template only when the user is configuring a start event element on the canvas
appliesTo of [bpmn:IntermediateCatchEvent] with eventDefinition bpmn:MessageEventDefinition so the template attaches only to message intermediate catches that consume mid-process webhook callbacks
appliesTo of [bpmn:Process], so the template registers on the process root and exposes the webhook URL once per deployed process rather than once per element on the canvas
appliesTo of [bpmn:ServiceTask, bpmn:StartEvent], so the same template can attach to both element types and switch behaviour at runtime based on the inbound.subtype property value

d.text: Incorrect. The inbound start event template restricts appliesTo to start events; it is not shared with service tasks and there is no runtime switch on inbound.subtype to widen the element type.

b.text: Incorrect. That configuration belongs to the inbound intermediate catch event template; the start event template uses bpmn:StartEvent in its appliesTo list.

c.text: Incorrect. Connector templates are bound to BPMN elements like start events, not to the bpmn:Process root; there is no documented appliesTo entry for bpmn:Process.

a.text: Correct. The inbound start event Webhook connector template sets appliesTo to [bpmn:StartEvent] and elementType value to bpmn:StartEvent so that Web Modeler offers it only on start events.

explanation: An inbound start event connector template uses appliesTo: [bpmn:StartEvent] and elementType value bpmn:StartEvent. That is how Web Modeler scopes the template so it only appears as an option when a user is configuring a start event element.

docs.title: Connector templates - Inbound start event connector templates

scenario: Quanta Insurance, an insurer correlating 18000 messages per day to in-flight claims in Camunda 8.8, ships an inbound intermediate catch event Webhook template. A reviewer needs to confirm that the template carries two distinct correlation key fields plus a hidden generated message name, because intermediate catch events require both a subscription correlation key and a payload extraction expression.
question: Which pair of correlation-related bindings does the inbound intermediate catch event connector template define?

options.text: A Correlation key (process) bound as zeebe:property with name correlationKey, and a Correlation key (payload) bound as bpmn:Message#property with name correlationKeyExpression on the same message
A Correlation key (process) bound as bpmn:Message#zeebe:subscription#property with name correlationKey, plus a Correlation key (payload) bound as zeebe:property with name correlationKeyExpression
A Webhook ID bound as bpmn:Message#zeebe:subscription#property with name correlationKey, and a Variables field bound as zeebe:property with name correlationKeyExpression for the payload
A single Correlation key field bound as bpmn:Message#property with name correlationKey, and a Variables field bound as zeebe:taskHeader with key payloadExpression for the payload mapping

b.text: Correct. The template binds Correlation key (process) as type bpmn:Message#zeebe:subscription#property with name correlationKey, and Correlation key (payload) as type zeebe:property with name correlationKeyExpression. Together they define the subscription key and the FEEL expression that extracts it from the incoming payload.

d.text: Incorrect. There are two correlation key fields, not one, and the payload extraction is not bound to a taskHeader key called payloadExpression.

a.text: Incorrect. The process correlation key uses the bpmn:Message subscription binding, not a plain zeebe:property, and the payload extraction is a zeebe:property called correlationKeyExpression, not a bpmn:Message#property.

c.text: Incorrect. The Webhook ID field is a separate zeebe:property called inbound.context; it is not the correlation key binding used for message subscription.

explanation: Intermediate catch event templates need two correlation values. The subscription side uses bpmn:Message#zeebe:subscription#property with name correlationKey, while the payload extraction uses a zeebe:property with name correlationKeyExpression. The template also carries a hidden bpmn:Message#property name with a generated uuid so the message has a unique identifier.

docs.title: Connector templates - Inbound intermediate catch event connector templates

scenario: Sable Bank, a bank processing 26000 loans per month in Camunda 8.8, attaches an HTTP webhook to a user task so a partner-side cancellation event can interrupt the loan in flight. A modeler must choose a boundary event Webhook template that limits the binding to boundary events and uses a message event definition for correlation while still allowing HMAC scopes to be FEEL expressions.
question: Which appliesTo and elementType configuration does the boundary event Webhook connector template specify?

options.text: appliesTo [bpmn:StartEvent, bpmn:BoundaryEvent], with elementType value bpmn:StartEvent and eventDefinition bpmn:SignalEventDefinition for boundary attachment by the modeler

appliesTo [bpmn:BoundaryEvent], with elementType value bpmn:BoundaryEvent and eventDefinition bpmn:MessageEventDefinition, and the inbound.hmacScopes property declared with feel required for FEEL expressions

appliesTo [bpmn:IntermediateCatchEvent], with elementType value bpmn:BoundaryEvent and eventDefinition bpmn:MessageEventDefinition, so the template attaches to either intermediate catches or boundaries

appliesTo [bpmn:UserTask], with elementType value bpmn:BoundaryEvent and eventDefinition bpmn:ErrorEventDefinition, so the boundary handler attaches to a task host rather than the boundary element itself

a.text: Incorrect. The boundary event template scopes appliesTo to [bpmn:BoundaryEvent] only and uses a MessageEventDefinition, not a SignalEventDefinition shared with start events.

c.text: Incorrect. The appliesTo array contains bpmn:BoundaryEvent only; the template does not also attach to intermediate catch events through this same template definition.

b.text: Correct. The boundary event Webhook template defines appliesTo as [bpmn:BoundaryEvent] with elementType value bpmn:BoundaryEvent and eventDefinition bpmn:MessageEventDefinition, and declares inbound.hmacScopes with feel required so HMAC scopes are configured as FEEL expressions.

d.text: Incorrect. The template is scoped to bpmn:BoundaryEvent in appliesTo and the event definition is bpmn:MessageEventDefinition; the host element is the boundary event, not a user task, and the definition is not an ErrorEventDefinition.

explanation: The boundary event Webhook connector template restricts appliesTo to [bpmn:BoundaryEvent] and pairs elementType value bpmn:BoundaryEvent with eventDefinition bpmn:MessageEventDefinition. HMAC scopes are declared as a FEEL property (feel required) so users can supply an expression like =["URL","PARAMETERS"].

docs.title: Connector templates - Inbound boundary event connector templates

scenario: Apex Logistics, a parcel carrier handling 2000000 shipments per year, configures a Camunda 8.8 HTTP Webhook connector with HMAC authentication for an upstream tracking provider. The provider sends GET requests with query parameters such as trackingId and tenantId, and the integration team must specify HMAC Scopes so the signature covers the right portion of every inbound call.
question: Which HMAC Scopes value selects the documented URL and Parameters Encoding Strategy for this GET webhook?

options.text: =["URL","BODY"] as a FEEL list combining the URL scope with the BODY scope on the HMAC Scopes property field
="URL,PARAMETERS" as a plain comma-separated string naming each scope inside the HMAC Scopes property field
=["BODY","PARAMETERS"] as a FEEL list combining the BODY scope with the PARAMETERS scope on the HMAC Scopes property
=["URL","PARAMETERS"] as a FEEL list containing the URL and PARAMETERS scope identifiers in the HMAC Scopes property

d.text: Correct. The URL and Parameters Encoding Strategy is selected when the HMAC Scopes include URL and PARAMETERS and the HTTP method is GET, and the documented Scopes value is the FEEL list =["URL","PARAMETERS"].

c.text: Incorrect. The documented HMAC Scopes are BODY, URL, and PARAMETERS, and a BODY+PARAMETERS combination is not one of the three supported strategies the connector derives from the scopes.

a.text: Incorrect. URL and BODY selects the URL and Body Encoding Strategy, but the docs state that on a GET method that strategy falls back to URL and Parameters, so it is not the canonical value for this GET case.

b.text: Incorrect. The HMAC Scopes property is a FEEL array, not a single comma-separated plain string, so a quoted string value would not be parsed as the two scope identifiers.

explanation: HMAC Scopes is a FEEL array; the example in the docs is =["URL","PARAMETERS"], which selects the URL and Parameters Encoding Strategy whenever the HTTP method is GET. The signature data is then built by combining the URL with the alphabetically sorted query parameters.

scenario: Halton Insurance, a regional insurer routing 45000 claims per month, configures a Camunda 8.8 HTTP Webhook intermediate catch event. The webhook receives payloads shaped {"claimId": "C-882", "status": "OK"}, and the modeler must wire the correlation key so the runtime correlates each inbound message to the waiting process instance via the claimId process variable.
question: Which pair of FEEL expressions correctly fills the Correlation key (process) and Correlation key (payload) fields?

options.text: Correlation key (process) =claimId and Correlation key (payload) =body.claimId so the runtime extracts claimId from the polled response body context
Correlation key (process) =request.body.claimId and Correlation key (payload) =claimId so the runtime maps the inbound body onto the variable name
Correlation key (process) =claimId and Correlation key (payload) =request.body.claimId so the runtime extracts claimId from the inbound request body
Correlation key (process) ="claimId" and Correlation key (payload) ="request.body.claimId" so both fields receive literal quoted string values

b.text: Incorrect. The two fields are reversed; Correlation key (process) names the correlation variable on the waiting instance, and Correlation key (payload) extracts the value from the inbound body.

c.text: Correct. Correlation key (process) is a FEEL expression naming the process correlation variable, and Correlation key (payload) is evaluated in the connector runtime to extract the matching value from the incoming request body.

a.text: Incorrect. The HTTP Webhook connector exposes the inbound payload under request.body, not body; the body context is the convention for the HTTP Polling connector instead.

d.text: Incorrect. Both correlation fields are FEEL expressions, so wrapping the variable name and the path in quotes would correlate against fixed string literals rather than evaluated values.

explanation: Correlation key (process) is a FEEL expression that names the process correlation variable, and Correlation key (payload) is a FEEL expression evaluated in the connector runtime against the incoming request to extract the matching value, prefixed with request.body for the HTTP Webhook connector.

scenario: Spring Bank, a digital bank processing 300000 transactions per hour, integrates a Slack notification webhook with a Camunda 8.8 HTTP Webhook connector. Slack sends a one-time verification challenge as {"type":"url_verification","challenge":"abc123"} that must be echoed back without starting a process instance, while regular events shaped {"type":"event_callback", ...} should correlate to running cases as usual.
question: Which Verification expression returns the documented response for the Slack challenge while letting regular events proceed?

options.text: ={"body": {"challenge": request.body.challenge}} placed in the Response expression field instead of the Verification expression field on the connector
=if request.body.type = "url_verification" then {"body": {"challenge": request.body.challenge}} else null as the Verification expression on the connector
=if request.body.type = "event_callback" then {"body": {"challenge": request.body.challenge}} else null as the Verification expression on the connector
={"body": {"challenge": request.body.challenge}, "statusCode": 200} as the Verification expression so every inbound request echoes the challenge value back

d.text: Incorrect. Returning a non-null value unconditionally would skip activation conditions, HMAC validation, and correlation for every request, so the regular event_callback path would never reach a process instance.

c.text: Incorrect. The guard is inverted; echoing the challenge on event_callback would short-circuit normal correlation for real events and would never satisfy the url_verification handshake.

b.text: Correct. A non-null Verification expression returns the response immediately and skips correlation, so guarding the echo on the url_verification type lets challenge requests reply while event callbacks fall through with null and continue normal processing.

a.text: Incorrect. The Response expression runs after correlation, so a process instance would still be created for the challenge; Verification expression is the field that returns a response before correlation.

explanation: Verification expression returns a custom HTTP response and skips correlation, HMAC validation, and activation conditions when its FEEL result is non-null; returning null lets the rest of the pipeline run, which is the documented pattern for one-time verification challenges from providers like Slack and GitHub.

scenario: Mosaic Insurance, an insurer issuing 4000 policies per week, secures a Camunda 8.8 HTTP Webhook with JWT authorization. The team defines a JWT role property expression that returns a list of role names extracted from the JWT payload before the inbound connector compares it against the Required roles list configured on the webhook for the policy-update process.
question: What is the documented constraint on a JWT role property expression in the HTTP Webhook connector?

options.text: The expression must return a single role string, which the Required roles configuration matches against the first entry of the JWT roles claim
The expression is evaluated only against the JWT payload and must always return an array, with no access to process variables or secrets
The expression is evaluated against the JWT payload merged with process variables, allowing secrets to be substituted before role comparison runs
The expression is evaluated after the activation condition and can read both the request headers and the JWT payload to derive the final role list

b.text: Correct. The HTTP Webhook docs explicitly note that the JWT role property expression is evaluated only against the JWT payload, so process variables and secrets cannot be reached, and the result of the expression should always be an array such as if admin = true then ["admin"] else roles.

c.text: Incorrect. The expression has access only to the JWT payload; process variables and secrets are out of scope, and the docs call this restriction out as a note specifically for the JWT role expression.

a.text: Incorrect. The result must always be an array, and Required roles is compared against the full extracted list, which is why the example resolves to ["admin"] rather than a single role string.

d.text: Incorrect. The JWT role expression runs only over the JWT payload to derive roles, not over request headers; header access and activation-condition timing apply to other webhook fields, not to role extraction.

explanation: The JWT role property expression is a FEEL expression that derives the user's roles from the JWT payload. It is evaluated only against the JWT payload, so it cannot use process variables or secrets, and its result must always be an array because Required roles is matched as a list against the extracted role list.

scenario: Beacon Energy, a utility provider integrating Slack to receive 800 incident alerts per week, subscribes a Camunda 8.8 HTTP Webhook to a Slack Events API endpoint. Slack first sends a one-time verification request with body type 'url_verification' that must be answered by echoing the challenge field, and only genuine events should create new process instances.
question: Which Verification expression answers the Slack url_verification challenge without starting a process instance?

options.text: Use =if request.body.type = "url_verification" then bpmnError("verify", request.body.challenge) else null in the Error expression field of the webhook
Use ={"body": {"challenge": request.body.challenge}} placed in the Activation condition so verification requests skip correlation while still firing the process
Use ={"body": {"challenge": request.body.challenge}, "statusCode": 200} as a constant Response expression that always echoes the challenge field back to Slack
Use =if request.body.type = "url_verification" then {"body": {"challenge": request.body.challenge}, "statusCode": 200} else null in the Verification expression field

d.text: Correct. When the Verification expression returns a non-null value, the webhook responds immediately and skips activation conditions, HMAC validation, and process correlation; the documented Slack url_verification recipe matches this exact shape with body, statusCode, and the else null branch.

a.text: Incorrect. bpmnError belongs to outbound connector error handling, not to the inbound Verification expression; the Verification expression must yield a response object with body/statusCode/headers or null, not a BPMN error structure.

c.text: Incorrect. A constant non-null Verification expression would echo the challenge for every request, including real Slack events, so no event would ever be correlated; the expression must return null for events that should be processed normally.

b.text: Incorrect. The Activation condition decides whether to trigger correlation but cannot craft the HTTP response body or status code; only the Verification expression returns a custom response while bypassing correlation.

explanation: The Verification expression returns a custom HTTP response without triggering process correlation. When it evaluates to a non-null value, the webhook returns the specified body/statusCode/headers immediately and skips activation conditions, HMAC validation, and correlation; when it evaluates to null, normal webhook processing proceeds.

scenario: Pulse Logistics, a logistics provider receiving 5000 shipment-status callbacks per day, fronts a Camunda 8.8 process with an HTTP Webhook connector on a plain start event (no message definition). The integration team needs each webhook response to return the freshly created process instance key to the caller with HTTP status 202 instead of the default 200.
question: Which Response expression returns the new processInstanceKey to the caller with a 202 status code?

options.text: ={"body": {"piKey": request.body.processInstanceKey}, "statusCode": 202} reading the key from the request body that the caller sent into the webhook
={"body": {"piKey": correlation.processInstanceKey}} relying on the default Response status code already being 202 for webhook start events that create instances
={"body": {"piKey": correlation.processInstanceKey}, "statusCode": 202} reading the new instance key from the correlation object on the plain start event
={"body": {"piKey": correlation.messageKey}, "statusCode": 202} reading the messageKey field from the correlation object returned by the start event

c.text: Correct. On a start event without a message, the correlation object exposes processInstanceKey and tenantId, and the Response expression sets the HTTP status with a statusCode key, so {body, statusCode: 202} returns the new instance key and overrides the default 200.

d.text: Incorrect. correlation.messageKey is populated only when the webhook fronts a start event with a message definition; on a plain start event the messageKey is absent and the correct property is processInstanceKey.

a.text: Incorrect. The new process instance key is generated by Zeebe at correlation time, so it is not present in request.body; the caller cannot know it before the webhook responds, so reading it from the request body yields nothing.

b.text: Incorrect. The default HTTP status of the Response expression is 200, not 202; without an explicit statusCode entry the connector returns 200 to the caller regardless of the BPMN element fronted by the webhook.

explanation: In the Response expression you can use the correlation object to access the result of correlation: a start event without a message exposes processInstanceKey and tenantId, while a message start event exposes messageKey and tenantId. The default HTTP status is 200, and you change it by including a statusCode key alongside body in the FEEL response context.

scenario: Mariner Logistics, a fulfillment provider receiving 8000 shipments per day from carrier partners, models a Camunda 8.8 inbound HTTP Webhook to start a new process instance for each parcel-status change. Carriers occasionally redeliver the same event with the same trackingId after a network glitch, and the platform team must prevent duplicate process instances when those reprocessed deliveries arrive.
question: Which inbound connector element type satisfies this idempotency requirement?

options.text: An intermediate catch event with a Correlation key extracted from trackingId so duplicate deliveries correlate into the existing running instance
A boundary event attached to a manual receive task that catches duplicate trackingId messages on the parent process instance for new shipments
A plain start event with a Result expression that copies trackingId into a process variable so that downstream tasks deduplicate the parcel themselves
A message start event with a Message ID expression derived from the connector output so retried deliveries share a single Message ID for uniqueness

d.text: Correct. The docs call out message start events when idempotency on process creation matters because they publish a message whose Message ID expression Zeebe uses to guarantee message uniqueness, so retried deliveries do not create duplicate process instances.

c.text: Incorrect. Plain start events are triggered directly by process instance creation and do not expose a Message ID expression, so each redelivered request would still produce a fresh process instance regardless of the result mapping.

b.text: Incorrect. Boundary events receive messages in an already running process instance attached to a task, so they cannot be used to start a new process and therefore do not address the duplication risk at creation time.

a.text: Incorrect. Intermediate catch events receive messages in an already running process instance, so they cannot start a new process and do not solve duplicate creation for new shipment events arriving fresh.

explanation: Inbound connectors can be modeled as start, message start, intermediate catch, boundary, or receive elements. When idempotency on process creation is required, the docs recommend message start events because they publish messages whose Message ID expression Zeebe uses to enforce uniqueness across reprocessed deliveries.

docs.title: Use inbound connectors - Creating the connector event

scenario: A Camunda 8.8 healthcare provider receives 12000 records per day at an inbound HTTP Webhook from a lab-results gateway. Payloads are shaped {"id": 1, "status": "OK"} and the webhook should only start a process when status equals "OK". The id field from each accepted payload must be exposed as the BPMN process variable myDocumentId, matching the example in the inbound connector docs.
question: Which combination of Activation condition and Result expression matches the documented HTTP webhook example?

options.text: Activation condition =(request.body.status = "OK") and Result expression ={myDocumentId: request.body.id} so the FEEL evaluates against the incoming request
Activation condition =(request.body.status = "OK") and Result variable myDocumentId only, without a Result expression, so the entire request body is bound to the variable
Activation condition =(status = "OK") and Result expression ={myDocumentId: id} so the FEEL evaluates against the process variables of the running instance
Activation condition =request.body.status and Result expression ={myDocumentId: request.body} so the status string itself is read as the boolean trigger value

c.text: Incorrect. The Activation condition cannot reference process variables; it only sees the inbound request payload, so referencing bare status without the request.body prefix would not evaluate the incoming field at all.

a.text: Correct. The documented Activation condition evaluates a FEEL boolean against the inbound payload via request.body.status, and the Result expression is a FEEL context that maps request.body.id onto the new process variable myDocumentId.

b.text: Incorrect. Result variable and Result expression are separate fields, and setting only Result variable binds the whole body, not the documented {myDocumentId: request.body.id} mapping shown in the webhook example.

d.text: Incorrect. The Activation condition expects a boolean FEEL expression, so returning the status string by itself is not equivalent to the documented equality comparison that gates trigger execution.

explanation: The inbound HTTP webhook example shows that Activation condition is a FEEL boolean evaluated against the inbound request and may not reference process variables, while Result expression is a FEEL context that maps request.body.* fields onto BPMN process variables of the new instance.

docs.title: Use inbound connectors - Example HTTP webhook

scenario: Lattice Insurance, an insurer settling 4500 claims per month, models a Camunda 8.8 claim process with an intermediate catch event that uses the HTTP Webhook connector. Each running instance holds the claim's external reference in a process variable named claimRef, and the partner's settlement callback arrives with body {"claim":{"ref":"X9"}} at the configured webhook ID claim-settled.
question: Which Correlation key configuration matches each settlement callback to its waiting process instance?

options.text: Correlation key (process) = =request.body.claim.ref and Correlation key (payload) = =claimRef, swapping the two FEEL expressions so the payload side reads the variable
Correlation key (process) = =claimRef with Activation condition = =(request.body.claim.ref = claimRef) and no separate Correlation key (payload) expression configured
Correlation key (process) = =claimRef and Correlation key (payload) = =request.body.claim.ref, with both FEEL expressions yielding the same external claim reference value
Correlation key (process) = =claimRef and Correlation key (payload) = =claim.ref, omitting the request.body prefix when reading from the inbound webhook payload

c.text: Correct. The process correlation key is a FEEL expression that resolves the subscription key from the running instance (=claimRef), and the payload correlation key extracts the same value from the inbound body via FEEL using the request.body prefix (=request.body.claim.ref), as the docs show with =requestIdValue and =request.body.request.id.

a.text: Incorrect. The two correlation expressions are swapped; the process side must evaluate against process variables and the payload side must evaluate against the inbound request, not the other way around, or the correlation will not find the waiting instance.

d.text: Incorrect. Inbound payload references must use the request.body prefix to reach the parsed request body; a bare claim.ref expression does not resolve against the incoming webhook payload context.

b.text: Incorrect. The Activation condition only decides whether the webhook fires, and the docs note it cannot access process variables; intermediate catch events still need both Correlation key (process) and Correlation key (payload) to route the message to its instance.

explanation: For an HTTP Webhook on an intermediate catch event, Correlation key (process) is a FEEL expression returning the subscription key from process variables, and Correlation key (payload) is a FEEL expression that extracts the matching value from the incoming request body using the request.body prefix. Both expressions must resolve to the same value for the runtime to deliver the message.

scenario: Northwind Retail, an online retailer with 60,000 SKUs across 12 warehouses, watches stock levels of a popular Bluetooth-earbuds product (ID 12345) every hour with a Camunda 8.8 HTTP Polling intermediate catch event. The team wants the catch event to fire only when the polled stock level drops below 10 units so a replenishment process can react quickly.
question: Which Interval and Activation Condition match the documented stock-monitoring polling example?

options.text: Interval=PT1H and Activation Condition==(body.stockLevel <= 10), polling once an hour and firing at or below 10 units with a non-strict comparison
Interval=PT60M and Activation Condition==(body.stock < 10), reading a body.stock field once per hour as the trigger for the replenishment process
Interval=PT1H and Activation Condition==(body.stockLevel < 10), polling the stock endpoint every hour and firing only when stockLevel is below 10 units
Interval=P1H and Activation Condition==(body.stockLevel < 10), polling every hour using the ISO 8601 period-only format P1H without the time designator

c.text: Correct. The product-stock scenario in the docs configures Interval=PT1H to poll the inventory endpoint every hour, and uses Activation Condition==(body.stockLevel < 10) so the intermediate catch event only fires once stock drops below 10 units for the monitored product.

d.text: Incorrect. ISO 8601 durations begin with PT for hour/minute/second components; P1H without the T designator denotes a period and is not the documented Interval format used by the HTTP Polling connector examples.

b.text: Incorrect. The example response from the docs exposes stock as body.stockLevel, so reading body.stock dereferences a field the polled response does not include, and the Activation Condition would never fire.

a.text: Incorrect. The documented Activation Condition uses a strict less-than (<), which fires only below 10; a non-strict comparison (<=) would also trigger at exactly 10 units, contradicting the example threshold.

explanation: The HTTP Polling docs include a stock-monitoring example with Interval=PT1H (every hour) and Activation Condition==(body.stockLevel < 10). Interval values use ISO 8601 duration syntax (PT prefix for time-of-day components like PT10M or PT1H), and Activation Condition is a FEEL expression evaluated against the polled body to decide when the catch event fires.

scenario: Halcyon Bank, a self-managed bank with 1,200 engineers on staff, configures Camunda 8.8 to route outbound REST traffic through a corporate proxy via JVM properties so the whole JVM runtime is proxied. The platform team must keep Camunda's internal traffic direct so the Zeebe broker and Keycloak login flows are not blocked by the proxy on the way out of the cluster.
question: Which value for the http.nonProxyHosts JVM property keeps internal Camunda traffic from being routed through the proxy?

options.text: http.nonProxyHosts must contain CONNECTOR_HTTP_NON_PROXY_HOSTS=camunda-platform-zeebe to mirror the connector environment variable inside the JVM property value
http.nonProxyHosts must contain camunda-zeebe,camunda-keycloak using a comma-separated list of literal internal hostnames as the bypass entries
http.nonProxyHosts must contain *.camunda.local so every Camunda component matches a single wildcard pattern under the local domain for the bypass
http.nonProxyHosts must contain camunda-platform-zeebe|camunda-platform-keycloak so the JVM bypasses the proxy for traffic to those internal hostnames

d.text: Correct. The docs note explicitly that, when using JVM properties for the proxy, http.nonProxyHosts must contain camunda-platform-zeebe|camunda-platform-keycloak (pipe separator) so the JVM bypasses the proxy when Camunda components talk to Zeebe and Keycloak internally.

b.text: Incorrect. The non-proxy hosts list uses a pipe (|) as the pattern separator (not commas), and the documented hostnames are the longer camunda-platform-zeebe and camunda-platform-keycloak rather than shorter aliases.

c.text: Incorrect. A *.camunda.local wildcard does not match the documented internal service names; the docs require the exact patterns camunda-platform-zeebe and camunda-platform-keycloak to appear in the bypass list.

a.text: Incorrect. The JVM property value is the bypass-host list itself, not the name of the connector environment variable; CONNECTOR_HTTP_NON_PROXY_HOSTS is configured separately and does not belong inside the JVM property value.

explanation: When configuring a self-managed proxy via JVM properties, http.nonProxyHosts takes a pipe-separated list of host patterns that should bypass the proxy. The docs add an explicit requirement: this list must contain camunda-platform-zeebe|camunda-platform-keycloak so Camunda's internal traffic to Zeebe and Keycloak still flows directly, regardless of the proxy.

scenario: Lyra Insurance, an insurer pricing 8,000 transactions per day for fresh quotes, calls a partner's risk API via a Camunda 8.8 outbound REST connector. When the partner returns HTTP 400 with body.temperature = 36, Lyra wants to raise a BPMN error called 'Too hot' that carries the partner's response body to a manual-review user task downstream so the reviewer can inspect the message, temperature, and booleanField values.
question: Which Error Expression raises the 'Too hot' BPMN error and forwards the partner's response body to the boundary event?

options.text: =if error.code = 400 and error.body.temperature = 36 then bpmnError("Too hot", error.body.message, error.body) else null, reading the body directly from the error context root
=if matches(error.code, "400") and error.variables.response.body.temp = 36 then bpmnError("Too hot", error.variables.response.body.message, error.variables.response.body) else null
=if matches(error.code, "400") and response.body.temp = 36 then bpmnError("Too hot", response.body.message, response.body) else null, using the response variable instead of error.variables
=if matches(error.code, "400") and error.variables.response.body.temp = 36 then bpmnError("Too hot", error.variables.response.body.message) else null, dropping the third bpmnError argument

b.text: Correct. On a connector failure the Operate error variable exposes the HTTP code as a string and the partner response under error.variables.response.body; matches(error.code, "400") tests the code, and the third bpmnError argument forwards the full response body so the boundary event can read message, temperature, and booleanField downstream.

a.text: Incorrect. The error context is structured as error.code and error.variables.response.body per the documentation; there is no error.body shortcut, so neither the temperature nor the message fields resolve under that path.

d.text: Incorrect. Omitting the third argument means no variables travel with the BPMN error, so the boundary event cannot access message, temperature, or booleanField for the reviewer's manual-review task.

c.text: Incorrect. On a failure the partner reply sits under error.variables.response, not under a top-level response variable; the bare response.body path would not resolve in the failure Error Expression scope.

explanation: When a REST connector call fails, Operate's error variable contains code (a string), variables.response.headers and variables.response.body, message, and type. The Error Expression can match on error.code and reach into error.variables.response.body, and the third argument to bpmnError attaches extra variables (such as the response body) to the BPMN error so a boundary handler can read them.

scenario: Nimbus Payments, a fintech processing 75000 transactions per hour, configures a Camunda 8.8 REST outbound connector to call a partner gateway. A developer must reference the secret PARTNER_API_KEY in the Authorization header of a FEEL context map without exposing the value anywhere in the BPMN model or in process variables.
question: Which Authorization header value uses the documented secrets syntax correctly inside the FEEL expression?

options.text: Set Authorization to ${secrets.PARTNER_API_KEY} inside the FEEL map so the dollar-brace template is expanded by the connector at execution time as a configured secret reference
Set Authorization to "{{secrets.PARTNER_API_KEY}}" wrapped in double quotes inside the FEEL map so the placeholder is passed through and resolved by the connector at execution time
Set Authorization to secrets.PARTNER_API_KEY directly inside the FEEL map without quotes, treating the secret like a regular process variable that the engine resolves at runtime
Set Authorization to {{ secrets.PARTNER_API_KEY }} wrapped in backticks inside the FEEL map so the engine treats it as a template literal rather than a plain string value

c.text: Incorrect. Secrets are not variables, and the bare secrets.* form is the deprecated syntax; the documented placeholder is {{secrets.NAME}} and it must be wrapped in double quotes inside a FEEL expression.

d.text: Incorrect. FEEL has no backtick template-literal syntax; the documented form is {{secrets.NAME}} inside a regular double-quoted string within the FEEL expression.

a.text: Incorrect. The documented placeholder is {{secrets.NAME}}, not the dollar-brace form; the connector replaces the {{...}} placeholder at execution time, not a shell-style template.

b.text: Correct. The placeholder {{secrets.PARTNER_API_KEY}} is wrapped in double quotes inside the FEEL expression; the engine passes the placeholder through to the connector, which then resolves the secret on execution.

explanation: Secrets are referenced with the {{secrets.NAME}} placeholder. Because secrets are not variables, the placeholder must be wrapped in double quotes when used in a FEEL expression. The engine passes the placeholder through and the connector resolves it at execution time.

docs.title: How to use connectors - using secrets

scenario: Atlas Bank, a retail bank handling 250000 transactions per day, configures a Camunda 8.8 REST outbound connector to call a fraud-screening service. The integration team needs to write an Error Expression that fails the job with one fewer retry on transient technical exceptions and raises a BPMN error when the response body status equals failed.
question: Which set of temporary variables does the Error Expression provide for building this FEEL expression per the documentation?

options.text: The connector result in response, the deployment in deployment with a retries field, the tenant in tenant, and any unhandled exception in throwable with a stackTrace field for diagnosis
The connector result in response, the job in job with a retries field, any Result Variable or Result Expression outputs, and the technical exception in error with a message and an optional code
The connector input in request, the process variables in variables, the activity in activity with a retries field, and the technical exception in fault with a description field for diagnosis
The HTTP body in body, the worker in worker with a retries field, the BPMN element in element, and the technical exception in exception with a message field and a mandatory code field

d.text: Incorrect. There is no documented body, worker, element, or exception temporary variable; the technical exception is exposed as error with a message and an optional code, and code is never required.

c.text: Incorrect. The expression cannot access connector input or process variables directly; to ensure process isolation the documented names are response, job, the result variables, and error.

a.text: Incorrect. There is no deployment, tenant, or throwable temporary variable in the Error Expression; the documented exception object is error with a message and an optional code.

b.text: Correct. The Error Expression exposes response (connector result), job with a retries field, any Result Variable or Result Expression outputs, and error (technical exception with message and an optional code).

explanation: Inside the Error Expression a FEEL expression can access response (the connector result), job (with field retries), any Result Variable or Result Expression outputs, and error (a technical exception with message and an optional code that is only present when the connector supplied one).

docs.title: How to use connectors - error expression

scenario: A Camunda 8.8 retailer team configures an HTTP Polling connector to watch a third-party inventory endpoint for 1500 records across its catalog without specifying the optional Interval field. The lead architect wants to confirm the default cadence at which the connector will poll the configured URL before the team picks a custom Interval value for production.
question: What is the documented default polling Interval when the Interval field is left blank on the HTTP Polling connector?

options.text: PT30S as an ISO 8601 duration of 30 seconds between polls of the configured URL endpoint
PT5M as an ISO 8601 duration of 5 minutes between polls of the configured URL endpoint
PT1M as an ISO 8601 duration of 1 minute between polls of the configured URL endpoint
PT50S as an ISO 8601 duration of 50 seconds between polls of the configured URL endpoint

b.text: Incorrect. PT5M is not the documented default for the Interval field; the HTTP Polling connector defaults to 50 seconds, not 5 minutes, so a blank Interval would poll considerably more often than this value.

a.text: Incorrect. PT30S is not the documented default; the HTTP Polling connector defaults to 50 seconds, so leaving the Interval field unset polls less often than every 30 seconds against the URL.

d.text: Correct. The HTTP Polling connector docs state the default Interval is 50 seconds, expressed as the ISO 8601 duration PT50S, applied automatically when the optional Interval field is left unset by the modeler.

c.text: Incorrect. PT1M is not the documented default; the HTTP Polling connector defaults to 50 seconds, which is 10 seconds shorter than a one-minute polling cadence on the configured URL.

explanation: The Interval field on the HTTP Polling connector is optional and accepts ISO 8601 durations; when no value is supplied, the connector polls the configured URL every 50 seconds (PT50S) as the documented default cadence.

scenario: Aurora Marketplace migrates a TypeScript service that consumes 18000 jobs per day from Camunda 8.7 to Camunda 8.8. The team must replace the legacy SDK class name used to construct a client and update calls to the unified surface introduced in 8.8 across the entire codebase.
question: Which client class do TypeScript developers instantiate in the Camunda 8.8 SDK?

options.text: CamundaClient is the unified TypeScript SDK class in Camunda 8.8 that replaces the legacy ZeebeClient used in earlier minor versions
OperateClient is the unified TypeScript SDK class in Camunda 8.8 and it covers Zeebe, Operate, and Tasklist APIs through a single surface
TasklistClient is the unified TypeScript SDK class in Camunda 8.8 and developers configure Zeebe endpoints on it via runtime options
ZeebeClient is the canonical class in Camunda 8.8 and the SDK keeps the original name for the entire 8.x release series

a.text: Correct. Camunda 8.8 introduced CamundaClient as the unified TypeScript SDK class; ZeebeClient is the legacy name being phased out.

d.text: Incorrect. The class name changed in 8.8 to align with the unified API; ZeebeClient is the legacy form.

b.text: Incorrect. OperateClient is a single-component client, not the unified entry class.

c.text: Incorrect. TasklistClient is a single-component client, not the unified entry class in 8.8.

explanation: Camunda 8.8 introduced CamundaClient in the TypeScript SDK as the unified client class, replacing the legacy ZeebeClient.

scenario: Oasis Retail computes shipping fees for 8500 orders per day in Camunda 8.8. A FEEL expression must round the variable shipFeeEur up to 2 decimal places to produce a customer-facing total. The team must pick the standard FEEL function and its documented argument order for rounding.
question: Which FEEL function rounds a number up to 2 decimal places in Camunda 8?

options.text: Use ceiling(shipFeeEur * 100) / 100 to round shipFeeEur up to 2 decimal places using FEEL's ceiling and integer arithmetic helpers in combination
Use shipFeeEur.toFixed(2) which is a FEEL number method that returns the value rounded to the requested number of decimal places as a number value
Use round up(shipFeeEur, 2) to round shipFeeEur to 2 decimal places, always away from zero for positive values per the FEEL language reference
Use decimal(shipFeeEur, 2) to round shipFeeEur to 2 decimal places using FEEL's banker rounding mode as documented in the language reference

d.text: Incorrect. decimal(n, scale) rounds half-even (banker's rounding), not always up.

c.text: Correct. FEEL provides round up(n, scale) which rounds away from zero to the configured scale; this is the documented up-rounding function.

b.text: Incorrect. FEEL is not method-chained; toFixed is a JavaScript convention, not FEEL.

a.text: Incorrect. While arithmetically valid, the documented FEEL idiom for up-rounding to a scale is round up(n, scale).

explanation: FEEL number rounding: decimal(n, scale) for half-even, round up(n, scale) for away-from-zero, round down(n, scale) for toward-zero.

scenario: Echo Bank runs 25000 jobs per day in Camunda 8.8 against process instances with very large variable payloads. The team wants the job worker activation to fetch only orderId and amountCents instead of every variable on the process scope, to keep activation responses small for downstream workers.
question: Which Camunda 8 job worker configuration limits the variables returned at activation?

options.text: Configure a process-level variable allow list on application.properties; the broker reads the allow list at deployment time on every activation
Configure fetchVariables on the worker (or @JobWorker attribute fetchVariables) with the explicit list of names; the broker returns only those variables
Configure a maxVariableBytes limit on the worker; the broker truncates the variable payload at the byte limit on every activation per call
Configure an input mapping on the service task with an exhaustive list of every variable to exclude; the broker reads the inversion at activation

b.text: Correct. Camunda 8 job activation supports a fetchVariables list (or @JobWorker(fetchVariables = {...}) in the Spring SDK); only the listed variables are returned.

d.text: Incorrect. Input mappings rename or compute variables on the task, they do not control the activation fetch scope on the worker.

c.text: Incorrect. The broker does not truncate by byte count; it returns the explicit fetchVariables list as configured.

a.text: Incorrect. Allow-listing variables is not a process-level config; the documented mechanism is fetchVariables on the worker activation.

explanation: Job activation accepts a fetchVariables list; only the listed variables are returned in the activation response for the worker.

scenario: Umbra Bank reduces job activation latency for 32000 jobs per day in Camunda 8.8. The team configures the job worker to receive jobs as they become available on the broker rather than waiting for a long-poll round trip between worker and broker on every batch cycle.
question: Which Camunda 8 job worker feature streams activated jobs to the worker without long-poll round trips?

options.text: Enable SSE activation on the worker; the broker pushes activated jobs over a Server-Sent Events HTTP channel on every job event in the broker
Enable job streaming on the worker (also called streaming activation); the broker pushes activated jobs over an open gRPC stream to the worker
Enable kafka activation on the worker; the broker publishes activated jobs to a per-type Kafka topic that the worker subscribes to on connection
Enable websocket activation on the worker; the broker upgrades the gRPC connection and pushes activated jobs over a websocket channel on each tick

b.text: Correct. Camunda 8 supports job streaming: the broker pushes activated jobs to the worker over an open gRPC stream, eliminating long-poll latency.

d.text: Incorrect. There is no websocket upgrade in the Camunda 8 broker protocol; streaming runs over gRPC.

c.text: Incorrect. Kafka is not used as a job activation transport; gRPC streaming is the documented mechanism.

a.text: Incorrect. SSE is not the documented job streaming transport; gRPC streaming is.

explanation: Camunda 8 job streaming uses gRPC server-streaming so the broker pushes activated jobs to the worker without long-poll round trips.

scenario: Vanta Marketplace hosts 4 tenants and 9000 transactions per day in Camunda 8.8. The team's job worker uses the Java client and must restrict the activations to a single tenant id at a time, so workers running for one tenant do not pick up jobs belonging to a different tenant id.
question: Which Camunda 8 client mechanism scopes a job worker activation to one tenant?

options.text: Configure a tenant custom header on every service task in the BPMN; the broker matches the worker's tenant header on activation by string equality
Configure the worker to authenticate with a per-tenant client id; the broker associates the worker session with the tenant from the OAuth token claim
Configure a tenant prefix on the job type (e.g., t1__credit-check); the broker isolates activations by parsing the prefix on every activation call
Configure tenantIds on the activate-jobs command (or @JobWorker attribute tenantIds); the broker returns jobs only for the configured tenant ids

d.text: Correct. Camunda 8 client activate-jobs commands accept tenantIds (and the Spring SDK exposes the attribute on @JobWorker); the broker filters jobs by tenant.

c.text: Incorrect. Tenant isolation is a first-class field on the activation command, not a string prefix on job type.

b.text: Incorrect. While Identity scopes access, the activation filter is the explicit tenantIds command attribute.

a.text: Incorrect. Custom headers are static per-element data on the BPMN; tenant filtering is on the activation request.

explanation: Camunda 8 multi-tenancy: the activate-jobs command (and SDK worker config) accepts tenantIds; the broker returns only jobs for those tenants.

scenario: Delta Bank's Spring SDK job worker handles 9000 jobs per day in Camunda 8.8 for the type charge-payment. The method returns a map of variables, and the team wants the framework to call complete-job automatically on a successful return rather than requiring an explicit completion call.
question: Which @JobWorker attribute controls whether the SDK auto-completes the job on a successful method return?

options.text: Return null from the method; the SDK interprets a null return as a signal to call complete-job with no variables on the activated job
Set autoActivate = true on the @JobWorker annotation; the SDK calls activate-job in advance and complete-job on every method return path
Set fetchVariables = {} on the @JobWorker annotation; the SDK reads the empty set as an opt-in for auto-completion of jobs of the type
Set autoComplete = true on the @JobWorker annotation; the SDK calls complete-job with the returned map on successful return

d.text: Correct. The @JobWorker annotation exposes autoComplete; when true, the SDK invokes complete-job with the returned variables on success.

b.text: Incorrect. There is no autoActivate attribute; activation is always handled by the SDK runtime.

a.text: Incorrect. Returning null does not implicitly switch on auto-completion; the explicit attribute controls it.

c.text: Incorrect. fetchVariables is a filter for which variables the worker receives, not an auto-completion flag.

explanation: @JobWorker(autoComplete = true) makes the Spring SDK call complete-job with the returned variables on a successful method return.

scenario: Beacon Insurance writes a Spring Boot job worker that processes 22000 jobs per day in Camunda 8.8 for the type credit-check. The team wants the framework to scan for worker methods on Spring beans and subscribe them to the broker without manual fluent calls in main().
question: Which Camunda 8 Spring SDK annotation declares a method as a job worker subscriber?

options.text: Annotate the method with @JobWorker(type = "credit-check"); the Spring SDK auto-subscribes to the type at application start
Annotate the bean class with @WorkerType("credit-check"); the Spring SDK auto-subscribes the bean's only public method at start
Annotate the method with @ZeebeWorker(type = "credit-check"); the Spring SDK auto-subscribes at start and is the only auto-registration mechanism
Annotate the method with @Subscribe("credit-check"); the Spring SDK auto-subscribes the method and resolves variable bindings from the method parameters

a.text: Correct. The Camunda 8 Spring SDK uses @JobWorker with a type attribute on a bean method to register a subscriber at start time.

c.text: Incorrect. @ZeebeWorker is the legacy name; the current Spring SDK annotation is @JobWorker.

b.text: Incorrect. Subscription is per-method via @JobWorker, not at the class level via @WorkerType.

d.text: Incorrect. There is no @Subscribe annotation in the Camunda 8 Spring SDK; the documented annotation is @JobWorker.

explanation: The Camunda 8 Spring SDK uses @JobWorker(type = ...) on bean methods; the framework auto-subscribes them at application start.

scenario: Sable Marketplace runs 7000 orders per day in Camunda 8.8. A FEEL output mapping must transform the list of contexts lineItems into a list of strings by formatting each element's id and priceEur fields (e.g., "SKU-42: 9.99") for use as a downstream display list on the parent scope.
question: Which FEEL expression maps every element of lineItems through a formatting function in Camunda 8?

options.text: map(lineItems, li -> li.id + ": " + string(li.priceEur)) calls a top-level FEEL map helper with the list and a lambda transformer as positional arguments
