for li in lineItems return li.id + ": " + string(li.priceEur) iterates the list with a for-comprehension that returns a string per element
lineItems.map(li -> li.id + ": " + string(li.priceEur)) uses a FEEL map method with arrow lambda syntax that the language adopted in 8.8
[li.id + ": " + string(li.priceEur) | li in lineItems] uses FEEL list comprehension brackets with the pipe-separator syntax over the source list

b.text: Correct. FEEL's iteration is the for-comprehension: for <var> in <list> return <expr>; it produces a list with one element per iteration.

c.text: Incorrect. FEEL is not method-chained; there is no .map method on lists.

a.text: Incorrect. FEEL does not include a top-level map() higher-order helper.

d.text: Incorrect. FEEL does not use the bracket-pipe list comprehension syntax; for-return is the documented form.

explanation: FEEL's iteration is the for-comprehension: for <var> in <list> return <expr>; it produces a list with one element per iteration.

scenario: Quanta Insurance computes claim deadlines for 7500 claims per day in Camunda 8.8. A FEEL output mapping must compute a deadline that is 14 days after the variable filedAt (a date-time value), as a date-time value, for use on a downstream intermediate timer event of the deadline.
question: Which FEEL expression returns filedAt plus 14 days as a date-time in Camunda 8?

options.text: filedAt + 14 returns a date-time 14 days after filedAt because FEEL coerces numbers to day durations during date-time arithmetic for compatibility
filedAt + duration("P14D") returns a date-time 14 days after filedAt using FEEL date-time and duration arithmetic
add days(filedAt, 14) returns a date-time 14 days after filedAt using a FEEL built-in helper for adding day counts to date-time values
filedAt.plusDays(14) returns a date-time 14 days after filedAt using FEEL date-time method-chaining over the Java time package's plusDays helper

b.text: Correct. FEEL date-time arithmetic uses date-time + duration(ISO 8601) with explicit duration values like duration("P14D").

a.text: Incorrect. FEEL does not coerce numbers to durations; you must use an explicit duration value.

d.text: Incorrect. FEEL is not method-chained; plusDays is a Java API not a FEEL idiom.

c.text: Incorrect. FEEL has no add days helper; the documented form is date-time + duration.

explanation: FEEL date-time arithmetic uses date-time + duration(ISO 8601); duration("P14D") is fourteen days.

scenario: Wren Logistics implements a job worker in a language without an official Camunda SDK and handles 6000 jobs per day in Camunda 8.8. The team must complete an activated job via the REST API by posting the resulting variables as JSON keyed by the job key returned at activation time.
question: Which Camunda 8 REST API endpoint completes a job by its key?

options.text: POST /v2/jobs/{jobKey}/completion with a JSON body containing a variables object on the payload root; the broker marks the job complete
POST /zeebe/grpc with a base64-encoded CompleteJob protobuf body; the broker accepts gRPC over REST as a transport bridge for HTTP-only clients
POST /api/v1/jobs/complete with a JSON body containing the job key and a variables object as the payload root for the broker to match
PUT /v2/jobs/{jobKey} with a JSON body containing a status field set to COMPLETED and a variables object as the payload root

a.text: Correct. The Camunda 8 REST API completes a job at POST /v2/jobs/{jobKey}/completion with a JSON variables payload.

d.text: Incorrect. There is no PUT /v2/jobs/{jobKey} status form; completion is a dedicated sub-resource.

c.text: Incorrect. The v1 path is not the documented Camunda 8 surface; /v2/jobs/{jobKey}/completion is the canonical endpoint.

b.text: Incorrect. The REST API is native HTTP/JSON, not a gRPC tunnel.

explanation: Camunda 8 REST API: POST /v2/jobs/{jobKey}/completion with a JSON variables payload completes a job.

scenario: Yarrow Insurance builds a custom Tasklist UI for 18 agents and 4000 user tasks per day in Camunda 8.8. The team needs to list the open user tasks assigned to the calling user, assign or claim a task, and complete a task with the form's variables payload via the platform's task-oriented API.
question: Which Camunda 8 API surface exposes user task list, claim, and complete operations?

options.text: The Operate API exposes user task list, claim, and complete operations; Tasklist is an aggregator of the same Operate API endpoints for human task workflows
The Zeebe gRPC API exposes user task assign and complete operations directly; user tasks are a special job type and the Tasklist API is a thin proxy to it
The Tasklist API exposes endpoints to search, assign, claim, and complete user tasks; the broker exposes only job-level operations in the Zeebe API
The Identity API exposes user task list and claim operations; user task assignment is treated as a role grant rather than a process engine operation

c.text: Correct. The Tasklist API exposes the human-task surface (search, assign, claim, complete); Zeebe handles only the underlying job lifecycle.

b.text: Incorrect. Zeebe gRPC handles jobs, not user task-level assign/claim operations directly.

a.text: Incorrect. Operate is operational data and admin; user task list/claim/complete operations live in the Tasklist API.

d.text: Incorrect. Identity manages users, roles, and tokens, not user task lifecycle.

explanation: The Tasklist API exposes user task search, assign, claim, and complete operations as the documented surface for human task UIs.

scenario: Lumen Logistics consumes 30000 jobs per day in Camunda 8.8 from a custom job worker written in Go. The team uses the native Zeebe protocol directly for the lowest activation latency and must understand which transport the broker exposes for high-throughput worker subscriptions.
question: Which protocol does the Zeebe broker natively expose for job worker activation in Camunda 8?

options.text: Zeebe exposes a gRPC API; clients open a streaming activation channel and receive jobs as messages on the channel for the lowest activation latency
Zeebe exposes an AMQP API; clients subscribe to a per-type queue on the broker and receive jobs as AMQP messages with delivery acknowledgements per call
Zeebe exposes a GraphQL API; clients open a subscription on the activation query and receive jobs as subscription events on the same channel for low latency
Zeebe exposes a SOAP API; clients post activation requests as XML envelopes and receive job activations as XML responses on every poll cycle

a.text: Correct. Zeebe natively exposes a gRPC API; job workers use it for activation streaming and command requests.

d.text: Incorrect. Zeebe does not expose a SOAP API; gRPC is the native protocol.

c.text: Incorrect. Zeebe does not expose a GraphQL API; gRPC is the native protocol.

b.text: Incorrect. Zeebe does not expose an AMQP API; gRPC is the native protocol.

explanation: Zeebe natively exposes a gRPC API; job workers use it for low-latency activation streaming and command requests.

scenario: Aurora Telco polls an external REST endpoint to drive a Camunda 8.8 process for 1500 events per day. The integration must run an inbound connector that periodically calls the configured URL and publishes a Zeebe message when the response shape matches a configured condition expression.
question: Which Camunda 8 inbound connector pattern handles periodic polling of a remote endpoint?

options.text: Use the REST inbound polling connector; the runtime schedules the configured URL fetch and publishes a message on a matching condition expression
Use a custom outbound connector wrapped in a timer event loop; the modeler combines the timer and outbound connector to simulate periodic inbound polling
Use the Kafka inbound connector with a poll mode flag; the runtime reuses the Kafka subscriber as a REST poller when poll mode is enabled on the connector
Use the Webhook inbound connector with a scheduler header; the runtime self-invokes the webhook URL on a cron schedule and publishes a message on each fire

a.text: Correct. The REST inbound polling connector schedules the configured URL fetch and publishes a Zeebe message on a matching condition expression.

d.text: Incorrect. The Webhook connector is event-driven HTTP, not polling; polling is a separate inbound type.

c.text: Incorrect. Kafka inbound is a Kafka subscriber and does not double as a REST poller.

b.text: Incorrect. There is a dedicated inbound polling connector; combining timer plus outbound is unnecessary.

explanation: Camunda 8 provides an inbound REST polling connector; the runtime schedules the URL fetch and publishes a Zeebe message on a matching condition expression.

scenario: Granite Marketplace exposes a webhook to 6000 events per day in Camunda 8.8. The team wants an inbound connector to receive HTTP POST callbacks at a configured URL, correlate each call to a waiting process instance by orderId, and complete the message subscription without writing custom HTTP listener code.
question: Which Camunda 8 inbound connector mechanism handles synchronous HTTP webhook callbacks?

options.text: The Webhook inbound connector listens on a Kafka topic; the runtime publishes a message correlated by the configured key expression on each new record
The Webhook inbound connector exposes a URL endpoint; the runtime publishes a message correlated by the configured key expression on each HTTP POST
The Webhook inbound connector polls the configured URL every 30 seconds; the runtime publishes a message correlated by the configured key expression on changes
The Webhook inbound connector subscribes to a JMS queue; the runtime publishes a message correlated by the configured key expression on each delivered message

b.text: Correct. The Camunda 8 Webhook inbound connector exposes an HTTP endpoint; on each POST it publishes a Zeebe message correlated by the configured key expression.

c.text: Incorrect. Webhook is event-driven HTTP, not polling; the polling style is a different inbound type (e.g., REST polling).

a.text: Incorrect. Kafka inbound is a separate connector; Webhook specifically denotes HTTP.

d.text: Incorrect. JMS is not the Webhook connector; queue-based subscriptions are different connector types.

explanation: The Webhook inbound connector exposes an HTTP endpoint; on each POST the runtime publishes a Zeebe message correlated by the configured key expression.

scenario: Forge Insurance writes a custom Camunda 8.8 outbound connector to invoke a private REST API for 7000 transactions per day. The connector receives configuration via the element template and reads variables from the job; the team must implement the contract the connector runtime expects on each invocation.
question: Which Java interface or annotation does a Camunda 8 outbound connector implementation provide?

options.text: Annotate a Spring bean with @ConnectorFunction(type = "my-connector"); the runtime scans for the annotation and invokes the bean as the connector
Extend BaseConnector and override invoke(Map<String,Object>); the runtime instantiates the class and calls invoke for each activated job
Implement JobHandler with the handle(JobClient, ActivatedJob) method; the connector runtime treats every outbound connector as a job worker class
Implement OutboundConnectorFunction with the execute(OutboundConnectorContext) method; the runtime calls execute on each activated job for that type

d.text: Correct. Outbound connectors in Camunda 8 implement OutboundConnectorFunction and define execute(OutboundConnectorContext) which the runtime calls per activated job.

c.text: Incorrect. JobHandler is the lower-level job worker interface; outbound connectors implement OutboundConnectorFunction instead.

a.text: Incorrect. The Connector SDK uses OutboundConnectorFunction discovery (e.g., service loader or annotations on the function class), not @ConnectorFunction on Spring beans.

b.text: Incorrect. There is no BaseConnector class with invoke(Map); the supported contract is OutboundConnectorFunction.execute.

explanation: Outbound connectors implement OutboundConnectorFunction; the runtime calls execute(OutboundConnectorContext) on each activated job for that connector type.

scenario: Tessera Insurance configures an HTTP REST outbound connector for 5500 transactions per day in Camunda 8.8. The team must not embed an API key literal in the BPMN file or element template fields; instead the connector runtime must resolve the key from a managed secret at execution time.
question: Which Camunda 8 syntax references a managed secret inside a connector property?

options.text: Set the property value to ENV(MY_API_KEY); the connector runtime resolves the value from the broker process environment variables at every invocation
Set the property value to {{secrets.MY_API_KEY}}; the connector runtime resolves the placeholder against the configured secret store at invocation
Set the property value to vault://MY_API_KEY; the connector runtime resolves the value from the HashiCorp Vault integration bundled with the broker
Set the property value to ${MY_API_KEY}; the connector runtime resolves it from broker process environment variables on every invocation

b.text: Correct. Camunda 8 connectors reference managed secrets via the {{secrets.NAME}} placeholder; the runtime resolves the value from the secret store at invocation.

a.text: Incorrect. ENV(NAME) is not the documented placeholder syntax; secrets are managed via the secret store.

d.text: Incorrect. Dollar-brace syntax references variables not secrets; secret references use the {{secrets.NAME}} placeholder.

c.text: Incorrect. There is no vault:// scheme in the connector configuration; the documented syntax is {{secrets.NAME}}.

explanation: Connector secrets are referenced via the {{secrets.NAME}} placeholder; the runtime resolves the value from the secret store at invocation time.

scenario: Harbor Bank packages a custom Camunda 8.8 outbound connector used by 12 modelers for 6000 transactions per day. The team wants the connector to appear in Web Modeler's element template chooser with named input fields, dropdown options, and per-field validation so modelers configure it without editing raw XML.
question: Which Camunda 8 artefact defines the UI for a connector in Web Modeler?

options.text: A pom.xml dependency declares the connector; Web Modeler resolves transitive dependencies and generates a UI from reflection on the connector class
A web modeler extension JAR is uploaded; the runtime scans the JAR for connector classes and generates a UI from the field annotations on the class
An element template JSON file is registered in Web Modeler; the file defines properties, dropdowns, defaults, and bindings for the connector's BPMN element
A README.md inside the connector repository documents the input fields; Web Modeler reads the headings to generate a form input UI for the connector

c.text: Correct. Element templates are JSON files registered with Web Modeler; they declare the connector's element kind, input properties, validation, and BPMN bindings.

b.text: Incorrect. Web Modeler does not introspect Java JARs to build property panels; the documented mechanism is element template JSON.

d.text: Incorrect. Markdown documentation is not parsed by Web Modeler to render UI controls.

a.text: Incorrect. Maven coordinates do not drive the modeler UI; element templates are the documented mechanism.

explanation: Element templates are JSON files registered with Web Modeler; they declare the connector's BPMN element kind, input properties, validation, and bindings.

scenario: Pinnacle Logistics aggregates 13000 records per day in Camunda 8.8. A FEEL output mapping must compute the sum of the priceEur field across every element of the list lineItems on the process scope and write the result as the totalEur variable for downstream evaluation.
question: Which FEEL expression sums priceEur across the list of contexts in Camunda 8?

options.text: Use sum(for li in lineItems return li.priceEur) which iterates with a FEEL for-comprehension producing a list of numbers to sum
Use lineItems.reduce(0, sum) which is a FEEL higher-order operation that reduces every list of contexts using the named binary operator on the values
Use sum(lineItems.priceEur) which projects priceEur from every element of lineItems into a list of numbers and then sums them
Both expressions are valid in Camunda 8 FEEL: dot-projection auto-maps over lists, and the for-comprehension is the explicit form of the same projection

c.text: Incorrect. The expression is valid (dot-projection over a list), but option c is the better answer because both forms work in Camunda 8 FEEL.

a.text: Incorrect. The expression is valid (for-comprehension), but option c is the better answer because both forms work in Camunda 8 FEEL.

d.text: Correct. FEEL supports both list-projection (lineItems.priceEur) and for-comprehension (for li in lineItems return li.priceEur); each produces a list of numbers sum() can total.

b.text: Incorrect. FEEL is not method-chained; there is no reduce method on lists. Sum is a top-level function over a list.

explanation: FEEL supports list-projection over contexts (lineItems.priceEur) and explicit for-comprehensions; both produce a list of numbers usable by sum().

scenario: Nimbus Bank normalises customer ids for 19000 transactions per day in Camunda 8.8. A FEEL expression must uppercase the variable customerId and then return only the first 8 characters of the result for use as a routing key in a downstream business rule task on the parent scope.
question: Which FEEL string functions produce this uppercased 8-character routing key?

options.text: Use substring(upper case(customerId), 1, 8) which uppercases the string first and then takes the first 8 characters using a 1-indexed start position
Use upper case(substring(customerId, 0, 8)) which takes 8 characters from a 0-indexed start position and then uppercases the resulting string
Use customerId.toUpperCase().substring(0, 8) which chains FEEL string methods and uses a 0-indexed start position similar to most other languages
Use upper(left(customerId, 8)) which calls FEEL's left(text, length) helper and then applies the upper(text) function to the resulting string

a.text: Correct. FEEL string functions are upper case(string) and substring(string, start, length) with 1-indexed start positions.

c.text: Incorrect. FEEL is not method-chained; functions take the value as a positional argument and start positions are 1-indexed.

b.text: Incorrect. FEEL substring is 1-indexed, not 0-indexed; the start position 0 is out of range.

d.text: Incorrect. FEEL does not provide upper or left helpers; the documented names are upper case and substring.

explanation: FEEL string functions: upper case(string) and substring(string, start, length) with 1-indexed start positions.

scenario: Catalyst Logistics starts 14000 transactions per day in Camunda 8.8 from a Java service. The team uses the official Java client and must start a process by its bpmnProcessId, send a variables map, and use the latest deployed version of the definition without explicitly looking up version numbers.
question: Which Java client fluent call starts a process instance with the latest version in Camunda 8?

options.text: client.deploy("order").startInstance(map).send() deploys-and-starts in one call and is the documented Java client fluent path in 8.8
client.newCreateInstanceCommand().processId("order").variables(map).send() resolves the latest version by default and is the documented fluent path
client.newCreateInstanceCommand().bpmnProcessId("order").latestVersion().variables(map).send() resolves the latest version on the broker and returns a future
client.startProcess("order", map) is a static helper that resolves the latest version internally and is the documented Java client surface

c.text: Correct. The Camunda 8 Java client uses a fluent builder: newCreateInstanceCommand().bpmnProcessId(...).latestVersion().variables(...).send().

b.text: Incorrect. The fluent step is bpmnProcessId not processId, and latestVersion() must be called explicitly.

a.text: Incorrect. There is no deploy-and-start fluent call in the Java client; deploy and start are separate commands.

d.text: Incorrect. The Java client uses a fluent builder; there is no static client.startProcess helper of that signature.

explanation: The Java client starts an instance via newCreateInstanceCommand().bpmnProcessId(...).latestVersion().variables(...).send().

scenario: Xander Bank exports historical process analytics for 22000 transactions per day in Camunda 8.8 to a downstream BI platform. The team must fetch the JSON definition of a saved Optimize report so the BI platform can render the same chart configuration without rebuilding the query externally.
question: Which Camunda 8 Optimize API capability exports a saved report definition for external use?

options.text: Optimize exposes a CSV-only export; clients can read the rendered report data but the original definition is only available through the UI export dialog
Optimize embeds a Kibana endpoint; clients query the embedded path to retrieve the report definition as a Kibana saved object on each export
Optimize exposes a Zeebe stream of report results; clients subscribe to the stream and receive the saved report definition once per export interval over gRPC
Optimize exposes a Public API to read a saved report by id; the response contains the report definition for external rendering or programmatic import

d.text: Correct. Camunda 8 Optimize exposes a Public API that retrieves saved report definitions by id; the response is JSON for external consumption.

c.text: Incorrect. Optimize results are not streamed over Zeebe gRPC; the Public API is the documented external surface.

b.text: Incorrect. There is no embedded Kibana endpoint exposed by Optimize; the Public API is the documented surface.

a.text: Incorrect. The Public API also exposes the report definition as JSON, not only the rendered CSV data.

explanation: Optimize's Public API retrieves saved report definitions and data; clients integrate by calling the API with OAuth-issued tokens.

scenario: Beacon Marketplace publishes 11000 messages per day in Camunda 8.8 from an external scheduler. The team must publish a message with a name, a correlationKey value, a variables payload, and a timeToLive so the broker correlates the message to a unique waiting instance over the cluster's REST API surface.
question: Which Camunda 8 REST API endpoint publishes a single message for correlation?

options.text: POST /zeebe/messages/{name} with a JSON body containing correlationKey and variables; the URL path carries the message name as a path parameter for routing
POST /v2/messages/broadcast with a JSON body containing name and variables; the broker fans the broadcast out to every waiting subscription for the message name
POST /v2/messages/publication with a JSON body containing name, correlationKey, variables, and timeToLive as fields on the request root
POST /v2/signals/publication with a JSON body containing the message name as the signal name; messages are routed as signals on the published name string

c.text: Correct. POST /v2/messages/publication accepts name, correlationKey, variables, and timeToLive in the request body for message correlation.

b.text: Incorrect. There is no broadcast endpoint for messages; messages are point-to-point and use the publication endpoint.

d.text: Incorrect. Messages and signals are distinct; signals do not carry correlation keys.

a.text: Incorrect. The documented endpoint carries name in the JSON body, not the URL path, on the /v2/messages/publication route.

explanation: Camunda 8 REST API publishes messages at POST /v2/messages/publication with name, correlationKey, variables, and timeToLive in the body.

scenario: Junction Telco's batch job calls the Camunda 8.8 REST API for 4000 transactions per day on Camunda SaaS. The team must authenticate the service-to-service calls without an interactive user, using a long-lived machine identity issued by the platform's identity provider.
question: Which OAuth grant authenticates the Camunda 8 REST API for machine-to-machine calls?

options.text: The resource owner password grant; the service stores a username and password and exchanges them for a token on every batch call to the API
The authorization code grant; the service redirects to the Identity authorization endpoint and exchanges a one-time code for a token on each call
The client credentials grant; the service exchanges a client id and client secret for an access token from the Identity token endpoint
The implicit grant; the service sends a request to the Identity authorize endpoint and receives a token in the URL fragment on the redirect

c.text: Correct. Camunda 8 machine-to-machine API calls use the OAuth client credentials grant; the service exchanges client id and secret for an access token.

b.text: Incorrect. Authorization code is for interactive users with browsers, not for service-to-service calls.

a.text: Incorrect. The password grant is deprecated and not the documented mechanism for Camunda 8 machine calls.

d.text: Incorrect. The implicit grant is deprecated and not the documented mechanism for Camunda 8 machine calls.

explanation: Camunda 8 machine-to-machine API access uses the OAuth client credentials grant; clients are issued a client id and secret to obtain tokens.

scenario: Iris Insurance starts 11000 transactions per day in Camunda 8.8 from a curl integration. The team prefers the HTTP REST API to start a process instance by bpmnProcessId with a JSON variables payload over installing a language SDK in a constrained environment.
question: Which Camunda 8 REST API endpoint creates a new process instance?

options.text: POST /zeebe/grpc with a base64-encoded gRPC payload; the REST API tunnels gRPC requests as a fallback for HTTP-only environments without protocol negotiation
PUT /process/{bpmnProcessId}/start with no body; the broker uses the most recent set of process variables from the previous instance of the definition
POST /v2/process-instances with a JSON body containing processDefinitionKey or bpmnProcessId and a variables object as the payload root
POST /api/v1/instances with a JSON body containing the bpmnProcessId only; variables are supplied via subsequent PUT /api/v1/instances/{id}/variables

c.text: Correct. Camunda 8's REST API uses POST /v2/process-instances with processDefinitionKey or bpmnProcessId and a variables object to start an instance.

d.text: Incorrect. /api/v1/instances is not the documented Camunda 8 path; /v2/process-instances is the canonical surface.

b.text: Incorrect. There is no implicit reuse of prior variables and no PUT-style start endpoint; the documented endpoint is POST /v2/process-instances.

a.text: Incorrect. The Camunda 8 REST API is native HTTP/JSON, not a gRPC tunnel; gRPC is a separate Zeebe protocol.

explanation: Camunda 8 REST API: POST /v2/process-instances starts an instance with processDefinitionKey or bpmnProcessId plus a variables object.

scenario: Meridian Insurance integrates a Robotic Process Automation tool into a Camunda 8.8 process to handle 3000 transactions per day. The team must invoke an existing RPA bot from a BPMN service task and pass the bot's result back into process variables for the downstream business rule task to evaluate.
question: Which Camunda 8 integration pattern invokes an external RPA bot from a BPMN service task?

options.text: Configure the RPA bot URL as a custom header on the service task; the broker invokes the bot via HTTP GET on each activated job and reads JSON result as variables
Run the RPA worker bridge subscribed to a configured job type; the bridge translates Zeebe jobs to RPA bot invocations and returns the bot result as variables
Embed the RPA bot as a Java class on the broker classpath; the broker invokes the class on each activated job and returns the bot result as variables
Deploy the RPA bot as a connector secret; the runtime reads the secret on activation and invokes the bot from inside the broker's connector runtime worker

b.text: Correct. Camunda 8's RPA integration runs an RPA worker bridge that subscribes to a job type and forwards activations to the RPA tool, returning the result as job complete variables.

c.text: Incorrect. The broker does not embed third-party Java classes; integration is via a separate worker process.

a.text: Incorrect. The broker does not invoke arbitrary URLs via custom headers; the RPA bridge worker is the documented mechanism.

d.text: Incorrect. Connector secrets are static reference values, not executable RPA bots; the documented mechanism is the RPA worker bridge.

explanation: Camunda 8 invokes RPA bots via a dedicated RPA worker bridge that subscribes to a job type and forwards activations to the RPA tool, returning the bot's result as job complete variables.

scenario: Apex Insurance deploys 7 BPMN files and 3 DMN files at application start for 5000 events per day in Camunda 8.8. The team uses the Spring SDK and wants the framework to auto-deploy classpath resources from the resources/bpmn directory on each application start without writing deploy code.
question: Which Camunda 8 Spring SDK annotation triggers classpath resource auto-deployment at startup?

options.text: Annotate every BPMN file with a Camunda extension element named deploy=auto; the Spring SDK reads the extension and auto-deploys the file at start
Annotate a configuration class with @Deployment(resources = "classpath*:bpmn/*.bpmn"); the Spring SDK auto-deploys matched resources at start
Annotate the main class with @EnableZeebeAutoDeploy; the Spring SDK scans the entire classpath for .bpmn files and deploys every match at start
Annotate the main class with @ZeebeDeployment; the Spring SDK scans the default resources/bpmn directory and deploys matched .bpmn files on every restart

b.text: Correct. The Camunda 8 Spring SDK exposes @Deployment(resources = ...) on a configuration class to declare classpath resources for auto-deployment at start.

c.text: Incorrect. There is no @EnableZeebeAutoDeploy annotation in the Camunda 8 Spring SDK.

d.text: Incorrect. The annotation is @Deployment with an explicit resources attribute; @ZeebeDeployment is the legacy name.

a.text: Incorrect. There is no deploy=auto BPMN extension element; deployment is configured on the Spring side.

explanation: The Camunda 8 Spring SDK uses @Deployment(resources = "classpath*:bpmn/*.bpmn") on a configuration bean to auto-deploy at start.

scenario: Keystone Bank automates BPMN deployments for 5 microservices and 8000 events per day on Camunda 8.8. The team must trigger deployment of a BPMN file managed in Web Modeler to a target cluster via a CI/CD pipeline without copying the file to a local repository.
question: Which Web Modeler integration deploys a managed BPMN file from a CI/CD pipeline?

options.text: Call the Optimize API import endpoint with the file id; Optimize forwards the file to the target cluster's Zeebe broker as part of the same call
Schedule a Web Modeler webhook that fires on every save; the webhook deploys the file to the target cluster on every modeler save by every developer
Call the Web Modeler API endpoint to deploy the file by id to the target cluster; the API authenticates via OAuth client credentials
Call the Zeebe gRPC endpoint with the file URL from Web Modeler; the broker fetches the file from Web Modeler at deploy time over the same gRPC channel

c.text: Correct. The Web Modeler API exposes a deploy endpoint that pushes a managed file by id to the target cluster, authenticated via OAuth client credentials.

b.text: Incorrect. Auto-deploying on every save is not the documented CI/CD pattern; the API endpoint is invoked explicitly from the pipeline.

d.text: Incorrect. The broker does not fetch files from Web Modeler; the Web Modeler API performs the deploy itself.

a.text: Incorrect. Optimize is an analytics component, not a deployment intermediary for BPMN files.

explanation: The Web Modeler API exposes a deploy endpoint that pushes a managed file to the target cluster; CI/CD pipelines call it via OAuth client credentials.

scenario: Catalyst Bank reads nested JSON for 21000 transactions per day in Camunda 8.8. A FEEL output mapping must read the field address.city from the variable customer (a context). When customer is null, the expression must return the string "UNKNOWN" instead of failing the evaluation with a null error.
question: Which FEEL expression safely reads a nested context field with a default when the parent is null?

options.text: Use customer?.address?.city default "UNKNOWN" which is FEEL's optional-chaining operator that returns the default when any intermediate value is null
Use coalesce(customer.address.city, "UNKNOWN") which is a FEEL built-in helper that returns the second argument when the first evaluates to null
Use try (customer.address.city) catch "UNKNOWN" which is the FEEL exception-style fallback for null path traversal failures on context values
Use if customer != null then customer.address.city else "UNKNOWN" as a FEEL conditional that branches on the null check before path access

d.text: Correct. FEEL supports conditional expressions; the idiomatic null-safe form is an explicit if-then-else check before the path access.

a.text: Incorrect. FEEL has no optional-chaining operator (?.); explicit conditionals or path-navigation are required.

b.text: Incorrect. FEEL has no built-in coalesce function with that signature; the conditional form is documented.

c.text: Incorrect. FEEL has no try/catch syntax; the conditional form is documented.

explanation: FEEL has no optional-chaining or coalesce helper; the idiomatic null-safe pattern is an explicit if-then-else branch before path access.

scenario: Ridge Bank classifies 15000 transactions per day in Camunda 8.8 with FEEL. A FEEL output mapping must return the string "HIGH" when the variable amountCents exceeds 1000000 and otherwise return the string "LOW" as a single value used by a downstream decision table.
question: Which FEEL expression implements this conditional in Camunda 8 FEEL?

options.text: switch(amountCents > 1000000, "HIGH", "LOW") uses the FEEL built-in switch helper that takes a boolean and two branch values as positional arguments
amountCents > 1000000 ? "HIGH" : "LOW" uses the ternary conditional operator supported in Camunda 8 FEEL on every expression position
case when amountCents > 1000000 then "HIGH" else "LOW" end uses the FEEL case keyword form with explicit when and end markers in a block
if amountCents > 1000000 then "HIGH" else "LOW" uses the FEEL conditional expression keyword form with the documented syntax

d.text: Correct. FEEL conditional expressions use the if-then-else keyword form.

b.text: Incorrect. FEEL does not include the C-style ternary operator.

c.text: Incorrect. FEEL does not include a case-when-then-end statement form.

a.text: Incorrect. FEEL has no built-in switch helper of that signature.

explanation: FEEL conditional expressions are written as if <cond> then <a> else <b> using keywords.

scenario: Zenith Bank monitors 18000 transactions per day in Camunda 8.8 with a custom dashboard external to Operate. The team must fetch active process instances filtered by a process definition key and a state filter (active vs incidents) via the platform's read-side API designed for operational queries.
question: Which Camunda 8 API surface returns the list of active process instances by state?

options.text: The Operate API exposes search endpoints for process instances filtered by state, key, definition, and tenant; it is the documented read-side surface
The Zeebe gRPC API exposes a list-instances query that returns active process instances; Operate is a thin read cache of the same Zeebe gRPC query results
The Tasklist API exposes a list-instances endpoint that returns active process instances filtered by state; the same endpoint is used for human task queries
The Identity API exposes a list-instances endpoint; the platform treats every active process instance as a session resource owned by the starting user

a.text: Correct. The Operate API is the read-side surface for operational queries: process instances by state, definition, key, and tenant.

b.text: Incorrect. Zeebe gRPC is command-oriented (create instance, complete job); it is not the read-side query surface.

c.text: Incorrect. Tasklist exposes user task queries, not the operational process instance search.

d.text: Incorrect. Identity manages users and roles, not process instance queries.

explanation: The Operate API is the read-side surface for operational queries: process instances by state, definition, key, and tenant.

scenario: NovaPay, a fintech merchant onboarding 3200 customers per week, configures the Camunda 8 TypeScript SDK against a Camunda 8.8 SaaS cluster. The team must pick the recommended configuration style so that ZEEBE_REST_ADDRESS, ZEEBE_GRPC_ADDRESS, ZEEBE_CLIENT_ID, ZEEBE_CLIENT_SECRET, and CAMUNDA_OAUTH_URL can be rotated and promoted across environments without rebuilding the application.
question: Which configuration style does the SDK documentation recommend for supplying the cluster credentials?

options.text: Read each credential from a YAML file shipped with the SDK so that ZEEBE_REST_ADDRESS, ZEEBE_CLIENT_ID, and CAMUNDA_OAUTH_URL load automatically on first call
Pass each credential as a positional constructor argument so that ZEEBE_REST_ADDRESS, ZEEBE_CLIENT_ID, and CAMUNDA_OAUTH_URL are fixed at build time for the runtime
Use the zero-configuration Camunda8 constructor and embed all values as JSON literals inside the source file checked into the merchant repository on every release
Use the zero-configuration Camunda8 constructor and supply all values via environment variables such as ZEEBE_REST_ADDRESS, ZEEBE_CLIENT_ID, and CAMUNDA_OAUTH_URL on the host

d.text: Correct. The docs recommend the zero-configuration constructor with all values supplied via environment variables, which makes secret rotation, secret management, and environment promotion safer and simpler.

c.text: Incorrect. Setting values explicitly in code is explicitly called out as not recommended; embedding secrets in source defeats the rotation and promotion benefits.

b.text: Incorrect. The SDK reads configuration from environment variables, not positional constructor arguments; fixed build-time values block credential rotation across environments.

a.text: Incorrect. The documented options are explicit constructor arguments or environment variables; the SDK does not load credentials from a bundled YAML file.

explanation: The TypeScript SDK documents two configuration options and recommends the zero-configuration constructor with all values supplied via environment variables such as ZEEBE_REST_ADDRESS, ZEEBE_CLIENT_ID, ZEEBE_CLIENT_SECRET, and CAMUNDA_OAUTH_URL.

scenario: NovaPay processes 4500 payment jobs per hour in Camunda 8.8. When the downstream card network is unreachable for 30 seconds, the worker must fail the job so it is retried only after a configurable delay rather than immediately, giving the external system time to recover before the 3 remaining retries are exhausted and an incident is raised.
question: Which fail-job parameter delays the next retry of a Camunda 8.8 job for a configurable duration?

options.text: The retry back off on the fail job command delays the next retry by the specified amount of time so the external system has a chance to recover before remaining retries are exhausted
The remaining retries on the fail job command delays the next retry by the specified amount of time so the external system has a chance to recover before remaining retries are exhausted
The variables payload on the fail job command delays the next retry by the specified amount of time so the external system has a chance to recover before remaining retries are exhausted
The activation Timeout on the fail job command delays the next retry by the specified amount of time so the external system has a chance to recover before remaining retries are exhausted

a.text: Correct. Camunda 8.8 documents that fail job commands may specify a retry back off which delays the retry by the specified duration; without it the job is retried immediately, which is fine when the external system is healthy but harmful when it is down.

b.text: Incorrect. Remaining retries controls whether a retry occurs at all and triggers an incident at zero or negative values, but it does not delay the next retry; that delay is controlled by retry back off.

d.text: Incorrect. The activation Timeout is set when the job is requested and bounds how long the job is assigned to a worker before reassignment; it does not delay retries after a fail job command.

c.text: Incorrect. Variables on a fail job command are only created in the local scope of the task and provide additional context for incidents; they do not delay the next retry of the failed job.

explanation: On Camunda 8.8 a fail job command may include a retry back off that delays the next retry by the specified duration. Without it the job is retried immediately, which can exhaust remaining retries and raise an incident when the external system is temporarily unavailable.

scenario: NovaPay Bank evaluates the Camunda 8.8 Orchestration Cluster REST API to drive 4500 instances per day from an external payments portal. The architecture board asks the integration team to confirm a documented key capability of the API so the upcoming portal release does not depend on undocumented or legacy engine surfaces.
question: Which capability is listed as a key feature of the Camunda 8.8 Orchestration Cluster REST API?

options.text: Bundled Camunda 7 process engine endpoints under engine-rest so existing Camunda 7 clients keep working unchanged against the orchestration cluster
Full process lifecycle management that lets clients deploy, start, and monitor BPMN processes through a single secured REST surface on the orchestration cluster
Direct SQL access to the Elasticsearch indices that back Operate and Tasklist so analytics tools can query process data through the orchestration cluster
Native gRPC streaming of job activations to external workers using the legacy Zeebe gateway protocol exposed on the orchestration cluster's internal port

b.text: Correct. The documented key capabilities of the Orchestration Cluster REST API begin with full process lifecycle management, covering deploy, start, and monitor for BPMN processes through one secured REST surface, alongside user task operations, variable management, incident resolution, and advanced search.

d.text: Incorrect. The Orchestration Cluster REST API is a REST API; gRPC streaming over the legacy Zeebe protocol is not described as one of its key features in the overview.

c.text: Incorrect. Direct SQL access to Elasticsearch indices is not part of the documented key features; the API exposes advanced search and filtering through REST endpoints, not raw index access.

a.text: Incorrect. The Orchestration Cluster REST API is a Camunda 8 surface and does not bundle Camunda 7 engine-rest endpoints; key features are scoped to Camunda 8 process lifecycle management.

explanation: The Camunda 8.8 Orchestration Cluster REST API overview lists full process lifecycle management, user task operations, variable management, incident resolution, and advanced search as key features, all secured with authentication and fine-grained resource authorization.

scenario: Borealis Bank's payments service makes 9000 requests per hour to the Zeebe gateway in Camunda 8.8 from a custom Go client. The team implements OAuth client credentials against the authorization server and must avoid hitting the token endpoint on every call to stay well below the 24 hours token lifetime returned by the OAuth response when integrating the new client.
question: Which token-handling strategy aligns with the OAuth procedure documented for a custom Camunda 8 client?

options.text: Cache the client_id and client_secret pair as the bearer credential on the Authorization header and skip the token endpoint entirely once the credentials have been verified one time
Request a fresh access token on every Zeebe API call so the authorization server stays the single source of truth for each outbound request that the payments service makes per hour
Cache the access token for its 86400 second lifetime returned in expires_in and request a new access token only when the cached one is close to expiring at the end of its window
Cache the access token for 60 seconds and refresh it on every minute boundary regardless of the expires_in value that the authorization server returns alongside the access token body

c.text: Correct. The docs state the token is valid for 86400 seconds (24 hours) as returned in expires_in, and they advise considering a mechanism to cache the token for that duration before requesting a new one from the authorization server.

b.text: Incorrect. Requesting a token on every API call ignores the documented advice to cache the access token for its lifetime and creates avoidable load on the OAuth authorization server.

a.text: Incorrect. The bearer credential placed on Zeebe API calls is the access_token returned by the OAuth endpoint, not the raw client_id and client_secret pair, which are used only at the token endpoint.

d.text: Incorrect. A fixed 60-second refresh interval ignores the documented expires_in value of 86400 seconds and triggers many unnecessary calls to the authorization server every day.

explanation: A custom Camunda 8 client obtains an access token via a POST to the authorization server with client_credentials. The response carries expires_in set to 86400 seconds (24 hours); the docs recommend caching the token for that lifetime instead of requesting a fresh one on every call.

scenario: Halcyon Logistics writes a custom Go client to send 4000 requests per day to a Camunda 8.8 Zeebe cluster. The team has installed grpcurl, obtained an OAuth access token, and now needs the contract artifact so the language stack can encode and decode messages for the gateway endpoint.
question: Which artifact must a custom gRPC client obtain from the Zeebe repository to invoke the Camunda 8.8 gateway?

options.text: Download a WSDL contract from the Zeebe repository and load it at runtime to discover the available gRPC service methods of Zeebe
Download the gateway.proto buffer file from the Zeebe repository and import it when invoking gRPC methods on the gateway endpoint
Download an OpenAPI specification from the Zeebe repository and feed it into a code generator to produce gRPC stubs for the gateway
Download a precompiled gRPC stub library from the Zeebe repository and link it into the application without any proto buffer file

b.text: Correct. The documented procedure has the client fetch gateway.proto from the camunda/zeebe repository and pass it as the proto file to the gRPC invocation against the Zeebe gateway.

c.text: Incorrect. The build-your-own-client steps reference a gRPC proto buffer file, not an OpenAPI specification, as the contract for the Zeebe gateway calls.

d.text: Incorrect. The docs explicitly require the gateway.proto file as input to the gRPC tooling; no precompiled stub library is provided in place of the proto.

a.text: Incorrect. The Zeebe gateway exposes gRPC services described by gateway.proto, not by a WSDL contract; WSDL is a SOAP service description format.

explanation: The build-your-own-client guide instructs developers to download gateway.proto from the camunda/zeebe repository and pass it to grpcurl (or any gRPC library) to invoke methods such as gateway_protocol.Gateway/Topology on the Zeebe endpoint.

scenario: Aurora Bank builds a custom HTTP client against Camunda 8.8 SaaS that issues 4500 requests per day to the Zeebe gateway. The team requests an OAuth bearer token via a POST to the Auth URL with client_id, client_secret, audience zeebe.camunda.io, and grant_type client_credentials, and they must decide for how long the issued token may be cached before a new one is fetched.
question: How long is the OAuth access token valid in the documented authentication flow?

options.text: The access token is valid for 86400 seconds and clients may cache the bearer token for up to 24 hours before fetching a new one from the Auth URL
The access token is valid for 3600 seconds and clients must fetch a fresh bearer token from the Auth URL roughly every hour during a working day
The access token is valid for 600 seconds and clients must fetch a fresh bearer token from the Auth URL roughly every ten minutes during long sessions
The access token is valid for 7200 seconds and clients may cache the bearer token for up to two hours before fetching a new one from the Auth URL

b.text: Incorrect. The documented response shows expires_in 86400, not 3600, so a one-hour cache lifetime understates the documented validity by a factor of 24.

a.text: Correct. The documented token response carries expires_in 86400 seconds, which is 24 hours; the docs advise caching the token for that duration before requesting a new one.

c.text: Incorrect. The documented response shows expires_in 86400, so a ten-minute lifetime is far shorter than the actual 24-hour validity returned by the Auth URL.

d.text: Incorrect. The documented response shows expires_in 86400, which is 24 hours, not two hours, so a two-hour cache window under-uses the issued token.

explanation: The OAuth response from the Auth URL contains expires_in 86400, meaning the bearer token is valid for 86400 seconds or 24 hours. Custom clients should cache the token for that duration before re-requesting one.

scenario: Tessera Logistics wires a custom Go client to Camunda 8.8 SaaS that drives 12000 jobs per day over gRPC. Before writing code the team verifies connectivity with grpcurl, importing the gateway.proto file fetched from the zeebe repository, exporting the OAuth ACCESS_TOKEN, and pointing the call at the cluster id host so that the response prints the cluster topology JSON.
question: Which gRPC method does the documented grpcurl call invoke against the SaaS cluster id host on port 443?

options.text: The grpcurl call invokes zeebe.Gateway/Topology on the cluster id host on port 9090 with a bearer ACCESS_TOKEN supplied in the Authorization header for verification
The grpcurl call invokes gateway_protocol.Gateway/Topology on the cluster id host on port 443 with a bearer ACCESS_TOKEN supplied in the Authorization header for verification
The grpcurl call invokes gateway_protocol.Zeebe/Topology on the cluster id host on port 26500 with a bearer ACCESS_TOKEN supplied in the Authorization header for verification
The grpcurl call invokes gateway_protocol.Gateway/Status on the cluster id host on port 443 with a bearer ACCESS_TOKEN supplied in the Authorization header for verification

d.text: Incorrect. The documented method is Topology, not Status; the proto file exposes gateway_protocol.Gateway/Topology as the call used to retrieve cluster information.

c.text: Incorrect. The fully qualified service is gateway_protocol.Gateway/Topology and the SaaS endpoint uses port 443, not the internal broker port 26500.

b.text: Correct. The documented invocation runs gateway_protocol.Gateway/Topology against $CLUSTER_ID.zeebe.camunda.io:443 with the bearer ACCESS_TOKEN header, returning the topology JSON.

a.text: Incorrect. The proto package is gateway_protocol with service Gateway, and the SaaS endpoint listens on port 443, not on a custom port 9090.

explanation: The documented gRPC verification step invokes gateway_protocol.Gateway/Topology on $CLUSTER_ID.zeebe.camunda.io:443 with an Authorization bearer header, using the proto file imported from /tmp/gateway.proto.

scenario: Lattice Insurance models a claims process and deploys it from a Node.js script with the Camunda 8 TypeScript SDK against Camunda 8.8, expecting 7500 instances per day. The developer must structure the deploy code correctly so that the network call resolves before the script exits, because the SDK documents that methods which operate over the network return promises rather than blocking.
question: Why does the quickstart wrap the deploy call inside an async main function in the documented example?

options.text: Network operations are asynchronous and SDK methods that operate over the network return promises, so callers must await them inside an async function such as main to obtain the response
Network operations are synchronous in the SDK but TypeScript requires every top-level program entry point to be declared as async main even when no promise is returned by the call
Network operations expose RxJS observables and the async main wrapper exists to subscribe to the deploy observable so that the script does not exit before the first emission
Network operations stream events via callbacks and the async main wrapper exists to register an event listener on the camunda instance before the first deploy call is made

a.text: Correct. The docs state that network operations are asynchronous and that methods over the network return promises, so the quickstart wraps the main function as async to await deployResourcesFromFiles.

b.text: Incorrect. Network operations are not synchronous in the SDK; the async wrapper is required because the deploy call returns a promise that must be awaited for the response payload.

d.text: Incorrect. The deploy step does not register a callback subscription; deployResourcesFromFiles returns a promise that resolves to the deploy response payload.

c.text: Incorrect. The TypeScript SDK does not expose deploy operations as RxJS observables; deployResourcesFromFiles returns a standard promise resolved with the deploy response.

explanation: Network operations in the TypeScript SDK are asynchronous and methods over the network return promises, so the quickstart wraps the deploy logic in an async main function and awaits camunda.deployResourcesFromFiles.

scenario: Forge Bank operates a Camunda 8.8 service that processes 9500 jobs per day from a Node.js worker. The team wires camunda.createJobWorker with jobType service-task, workerName test-worker, maxParallelJobs 20, pollIntervalMs 1000, pollTimeoutMs 50000, and jobTimeoutMs 5000, and must ensure each invocation of jobHandler returns the correct completion shape required by the SDK type system.
question: What must the jobHandler function return so that the TypeScript SDK type system is satisfied?

options.text: The jobHandler must return a Promise that resolves to a variables object, after which the SDK calls newCompleteCommand on the broker automatically using the resolved variables
The jobHandler must return a job completion call such as job.complete, job.fail, job.error, or job.ignore, which the type system enforces so every path responds to Zeebe
The jobHandler must return the original ActivatedJob object so that the polling loop can correlate the completion command with the original job key on the next tick
The jobHandler must return undefined and call the broker via the global jobClient instance; createJobWorker delegates completion to the surrounding worker loop after the handler exits

a.text: Incorrect. The SDK does not call newCompleteCommand on the worker's behalf based on the handler return; the handler itself must return one of the documented completion calls.

b.text: Correct. The docs state that the handler must return a job completion function such as fail, complete, error, or ignore, and the type system enforces that every code path responds to Zeebe.

d.text: Incorrect. Returning undefined does not signal a completion to Zeebe; the type system requires the handler to return job.complete, job.fail, job.error, or job.ignore.

c.text: Incorrect. Returning the ActivatedJob does not close the job on the broker; the handler must return a job completion call such as job.complete with optional variables.

explanation: The jobHandler passed to createJobWorker must return a job completion call (job.complete, job.fail, job.error, or job.ignore). The SDK type system enforces this so every code path responds to Zeebe after picking up a job.

scenario: Meridian Insurance runs a Camunda 8.8 claims process with a follow-up user task that is currently produced 2400 instances per day. A Node.js program built with the TypeScript SDK must continuously discover new CREATED user tasks, claim them as assignee jwulf, and then mark them complete with userTaskStatus 'Got done', using the documented polling subscription idiom on the camunda client.
question: Which SDK call sets up the polling subscription that the quickstart uses to find CREATED user tasks?

options.text: Call camunda.streamUserTasks with a filter for state CREATED and pass a predicate handler that calls assignUserTask and completeUserTask for each new userTaskKey discovered
Call camunda.watchUserTasks with filter state CREATED and consistency.intervalMs 1000, where the watcher fires callbacks for each new userTaskKey returned by the polling cycle
Call camunda.searchUserTasks with filter state CREATED and consistency.waitUpToMs Infinity, where the predicate becomes a polling subscription that handles each new userTaskKey
Call camunda.subscribeUserTasks with a filter for state CREATED and pass a predicate handler that calls assignUserTask and completeUserTask for each new userTaskKey discovered

d.text: Incorrect. The SDK does not expose subscribeUserTasks for this flow; the documented pattern uses searchUserTasks with consistency.waitUpToMs Infinity to turn the predicate into a polling subscription.

a.text: Incorrect. There is no streamUserTasks call in the quickstart; the documented call is searchUserTasks together with a consistency block that includes waitUpToMs Infinity.

c.text: Correct. The quickstart sets up camunda.searchUserTasks with filter state CREATED and a consistency object whose waitUpToMs is Infinity, so the predicate becomes a polling subscription invoked on each cycle.

b.text: Incorrect. The SDK does not document a watchUserTasks API; the documented user task polling uses searchUserTasks with consistency.waitUpToMs Infinity and a predicate function.

explanation: The quickstart uses camunda.searchUserTasks with filter state CREATED and a consistency block whose waitUpToMs is Infinity and pollIntervalMs is 1000, so the predicate function becomes a polling subscription that claims and completes each new user task.

scenario: Atlas Marketplace launches a Camunda 8.8 checkout process for 18000 orders per day in which the calling Node.js script must receive the final value of serviceTaskOutcome and userTaskStatus directly. The team uses the TypeScript SDK and wants the call that blocks the awaiting code until the short-running process instance finishes and returns the final variable map, rather than returning immediately with the process instance key.
question: Which SDK call returns once the short-running process instance has completed and exposes the final variables?

options.text: Call camunda.createProcessInstanceWithResult with the processDefinitionId and variables, passing awaitCompletion true so the promise resolves on completion with the final variable values
Call camunda.startProcess with the processDefinitionId and variables, passing waitForResult true so the promise resolves on completion with the final variable values for the run
Call camunda.createProcessInstance with the processDefinitionId and variables and then poll getProcessInstance until state COMPLETED is observed to retrieve the final variable values
Call camunda.createProcessInstance with the processDefinitionId and variables, passing awaitCompletion true so the promise resolves on completion with the final variable values

a.text: Correct. The quickstart uses camunda.createProcessInstanceWithResult with awaitCompletion true, which awaits process completion and resolves with the processInstanceKey and the final variable values.

d.text: Incorrect. createProcessInstance returns as soon as the instance is created and exposes the process instance ID; awaiting completion with final variables is the role of createProcessInstanceWithResult.

b.text: Incorrect. The documented API is createProcessInstance and createProcessInstanceWithResult; there is no startProcess with a waitForResult flag in the quickstart.

c.text: Incorrect. Polling getProcessInstance reads historical state from the Zeebe exporter which is eventually consistent; the direct way is createProcessInstanceWithResult with awaitCompletion true.

explanation: For short-running processes the quickstart calls camunda.createProcessInstanceWithResult with awaitCompletion true. The promise resolves once the instance completes and includes processInstanceKey and the final variable map.

scenario: Aurora Marketplace plans a Camunda 8.8 integration that handles 6000 requests per day from a customer-facing single page app. Engineers want to import the Camunda 8 TypeScript SDK directly into the browser bundle, but they must first confirm the runtime the SDK actually supports before committing to the architecture.
question: Which runtime does the Camunda 8.8 TypeScript SDK require, and how should browser applications be served?

options.text: Deno is the supported runtime for the SDK and a separate browser bundle is shipped from the same package for single page apps in 8.8
Bun is the supported runtime for the SDK and a small shim allows the same package to also run inside an evergreen web browser tab
Any modern JavaScript runtime including web browsers can host the SDK because it ships as an isomorphic build starting in 8.8
Node.js is the supported runtime for the SDK and browser applications must use the @camunda8/orchestration-cluster-api package instead

a.text: Incorrect. The prerequisites table names Node.js as the SDK runtime, not Deno, and no browser bundle is shipped from the same package; browsers must use a different package.

d.text: Correct. The prerequisites state the SDK runs in Node.js and cannot run in a web browser due to technical limitations; browser apps must use @camunda8/orchestration-cluster-api.

b.text: Incorrect. The SDK targets Node.js according to the prerequisites; Bun is not listed and no in-browser shim is provided for the camunda-8-js-sdk package.

c.text: Incorrect. The SDK explicitly cannot run in a web browser due to technical limitations; browser applications must adopt @camunda8/orchestration-cluster-api.

explanation: The TypeScript SDK prerequisites require Node.js and call out that the package cannot run in a web browser; browser applications use the @camunda8/orchestration-cluster-api client instead.

scenario: Helvetia Bank runs a Camunda 8.8 SaaS cluster handling 25000 requests per day from a Node.js orchestrator. The platform team wants the SDK configuration to live entirely outside source control so rotation, secret management, and environment promotion stay safe; they need to know which approach the documentation recommends.
question: Which configuration approach does the Camunda 8.8 TypeScript SDK documentation recommend?

options.text: Store the cluster credentials in a local SQLite file beside the binary so the SDK reads them at startup with no external dependency
Define a YAML manifest at the workspace root that the SDK auto-loads at startup so values stay versioned alongside the application code
Pass all connection values as explicit arguments to the Camunda8 constructor so the source code is the single source of truth for endpoints
Use the zero-configuration constructor and supply ZEEBE_REST_ADDRESS and OAuth values via environment variables for safer rotation and promotion

c.text: Incorrect. The docs explicitly caution that passing values to the Camunda8 constructor in code is not recommended; environment variables are preferred for safer rotation and promotion.

b.text: Incorrect. The SDK does not document a YAML manifest auto-load mechanism; configuration is supplied via explicit constructor arguments or environment variables.

d.text: Correct. The recommended approach is the zero-configuration constructor with values such as ZEEBE_REST_ADDRESS and the OAuth client supplied through environment variables.

a.text: Incorrect. The SDK does not document a SQLite credential store; configuration is supplied via the Camunda8 constructor or environment variables.

explanation: The Configure the connection section recommends the zero-configuration constructor with environment variables such as ZEEBE_REST_ADDRESS, ZEEBE_GRPC_ADDRESS, ZEEBE_CLIENT_ID, ZEEBE_CLIENT_SECRET, and CAMUNDA_OAUTH_URL, and cautions against passing them explicitly in code.

scenario: Aurora Insurer migrates a Node.js orchestrator that starts 9000 instances per day on Camunda 8.8. Engineers want to deploy a BPMN file from disk inside an async main function and log the deployed process definition identifier returned by the gateway before any process instances are created.
question: Which TypeScript SDK call deploys a BPMN file from disk and returns the deployed process metadata in Camunda 8.8?

options.text: Invoke camunda.deployResourcesFromFiles with an array of file paths and await the returned promise to receive the deployed process metadata
Invoke camunda.uploadBpmn with the file path so the SDK reads the resource from disk and submits it synchronously to the Zeebe gateway
Invoke camunda.publishProcess with the bpmnProcessId so the SDK locates and uploads any matching file from a configured resources directory
Invoke camunda.deployFromGit with a repository URL so the SDK fetches the BPMN file from the configured git remote on application startup

b.text: Incorrect. There is no uploadBpmn method documented for the SDK; the deployment entry point used in the guide is deployResourcesFromFiles, which takes an array of file paths.

c.text: Incorrect. There is no publishProcess method that resolves files by bpmnProcessId; the documented call passes explicit file paths to deployResourcesFromFiles.

d.text: Incorrect. The SDK does not document a deployFromGit method; the deploy step in the walkthrough reads the BPMN file from the local filesystem via deployResourcesFromFiles.

a.text: Correct. The walkthrough deploys process.bpmn by awaiting camunda.deployResourcesFromFiles with the file path; the response exposes processes[0].processDefinitionId for logging.

explanation: Deploying a process model in the TypeScript SDK uses camunda.deployResourcesFromFiles, which is awaited inside an async function and returns a deployResponse exposing processes[0].processDefinitionId.

scenario: Helix Healthcare orchestrates a triage workflow that creates 1200 instances per day on Camunda 8.8. A Node.js worker needs to continuously claim and complete CREATED user tasks via the Tasklist API client, polling once every 1000 milliseconds rather than running a one-shot search of the queue.
question: Which configuration turns camunda.searchUserTasks into a continuous user task subscription in the Camunda 8.8 TypeScript SDK?

options.text: Pass a webhookUrl alongside the filter so the Tasklist gateway pushes every newly created user task to the SDK over an HTTP callback channel
Set the option streaming to true on the search request so the gateway opens a server-sent events channel and pushes user tasks to the client
Set the consistency option pollIntervalMs to zero so the SDK polls the Tasklist API as fast as the network allows without blocking the loop
Set the consistency option waitUpToMs to Infinity so the SDK keeps polling and treats the predicate function as a long-running subscription

c.text: Incorrect. Setting pollIntervalMs to zero is not the documented subscription switch; the SDK turns searchUserTasks into a subscription when waitUpToMs is set to Infinity.

d.text: Correct. The user task poller comment states that setting waitUpToMs to Infinity converts the predicate into a polling subscription that continues to run until cancelled.

a.text: Incorrect. The user task example uses polling via searchUserTasks; there is no webhookUrl option documented for Tasklist callbacks in the SDK example.

b.text: Incorrect. The SDK example uses polling with the consistency options pollIntervalMs and waitUpToMs, not a server-sent events streaming flag on the search request.

explanation: The programmatic user task worker uses camunda.searchUserTasks with consistency.waitUpToMs set to Infinity and a predicate function that claims and completes each task; this configuration is what turns the search into a continuous subscription.

scenario: Meridian Retail runs a short-lived order intake process on Camunda 8.8 that handles 7200 orders per day. The Node.js caller starts an instance and must receive the final variable values such as userTaskStatus in the response, rather than only the process instance key, so a confirmation can be returned to the customer.
question: Which TypeScript SDK call starts a short-lived process instance and returns the final variable values once it completes?

options.text: Invoke camunda.startAndAwait with the processDefinitionId so the SDK starts the instance and returns the variables only after the process completes
Invoke camunda.runProcessSync with the processDefinitionId so the SDK starts the instance and synchronously blocks the thread until the result returns
Invoke camunda.createProcessInstance with the processDefinitionId so the SDK creates the instance and returns the final variables once the process ends
Invoke camunda.createProcessInstanceWithResult with awaitCompletion set to true so the SDK returns once the process completes with final variables

c.text: Incorrect. createProcessInstance returns as soon as the instance is created with only the process instance ID; it does not wait for completion or return final variable values.

a.text: Incorrect. There is no startAndAwait method on the SDK; the documented short-running call is createProcessInstanceWithResult with awaitCompletion set to true.

d.text: Correct. The Create a process instance section uses createProcessInstanceWithResult with awaitCompletion set to true, which returns once the process completes and exposes result.variables.

b.text: Incorrect. There is no runProcessSync method on the SDK; the documented approach for short-running processes is createProcessInstanceWithResult with awaitCompletion set to true.

explanation: For short-running processes the SDK uses createProcessInstanceWithResult with awaitCompletion set to true; the returned object exposes the processInstanceKey and the final result.variables once the process ends.

scenario: Vertex Logistics, a carrier handling 22000 shipments per day, evaluates Camunda 8 TypeScript clients. One service still targets a Camunda 8.7 broker, another streams jobs via gRPC, and a third is a brand new Camunda 8.8 microservice that uses only the Orchestration Cluster API. The architect must decide for which of these the @camunda8/sdk package is the documented choice.
question: Which use case does the SDK page list as a reason to use the @camunda8/sdk package?

options.text: Writing a brand new Camunda 8.8 service from scratch that only consumes the Orchestration Cluster API and never targets a Camunda 8.7 broker or gRPC job streaming endpoint
Building a brand new web browser SPA that talks to Camunda 8.8 only via the Orchestration Cluster REST API and that has no need for gRPC job streaming on the client side
Migrating an existing application to the 8.8 Orchestration Cluster API, where the page lists migration of an existing app among the reasons to pick @camunda8/sdk
Standing up a brand new dashboard that runs entirely in the browser and reads Operate data via the Orchestration Cluster API, with no server-side Node.js worker at all

b.text: Incorrect. The SDK runs in Node.js and cannot run in a web browser, so a browser SPA is not a documented use case for @camunda8/sdk.

c.text: Correct. The docs list migrating an existing application to the 8.8 Orchestration Cluster API among the reasons to choose @camunda8/sdk, alongside gRPC job streaming and targets of 8.7 or earlier.

a.text: Incorrect. The docs steer brand new 8.8+ projects without gRPC or v1 API needs toward the Orchestration Cluster API TypeScript client instead of @camunda8/sdk.

d.text: Incorrect. The @camunda8/sdk package does not run in a browser due to technical limitations; a browser dashboard is not a documented use case for it.

explanation: The page lists three reasons to pick @camunda8/sdk: needing the gRPC API for job streaming, targeting a server on 8.7 or earlier, or migrating an existing application to the 8.8 Orchestration Cluster API. New 8.8+ projects without those needs are pointed at the Orchestration Cluster API TypeScript client.

scenario: Pinnacle SaaS, a publisher running 14 microservices on Camunda 8.8, plans a browser-based portal for 3500 users per day plus a Node.js batch worker. The architect must decide whether the same @camunda8/sdk dependency can run inside the browser bundle or whether a different documented package is required, because the official prerequisites describe a strict runtime constraint for the TypeScript SDK.
question: Which runtime constraint does the Prerequisites table place on the @camunda8/sdk TypeScript SDK?

options.text: The SDK can run in both Node.js and modern web browsers once polyfills for fetch and stream APIs are bundled by the build pipeline of the publisher portal
The SDK runs in Node.js and cannot run in a web browser due to technical limitations, so a browser portal must use the @camunda8/orchestration-cluster-api package instead
The SDK runs only in Deno today and is mirrored to Node.js via a community polyfill, so the publisher portal can choose either runtime for the worker and the browser tier
The SDK runs in any JavaScript runtime that supports ES2020 features, so the publisher can reuse it inside the browser bundle as long as ESM imports are configured properly

a.text: Incorrect. The prerequisite explicitly forbids browser use due to technical limitations; polyfills do not lift this constraint per the docs.

d.text: Incorrect. The page does not list browser support as conditional on ESM or ES2020; it states the SDK cannot run in a web browser at all.

b.text: Correct. The Prerequisites table states the SDK runs in Node.js and cannot run in a web browser due to technical limitations, and points browser projects at @camunda8/orchestration-cluster-api.

c.text: Incorrect. The page lists Node.js as the runtime; Deno is not the documented target for @camunda8/sdk and there is no described community Node.js polyfill.

explanation: The Prerequisites table for the TypeScript SDK lists Node.js as the runtime and notes that the SDK cannot run in a web browser due to technical limitations. Browser applications must use @camunda8/orchestration-cluster-api instead.

scenario: Aurora Marketplace evaluates Camunda 8.8 client options for 9000 jobs per day across a mixed Java and TypeScript estate. The platform team must choose only the officially supported Camunda clients for the marketplace stack, distinguishing them from the community-maintained client libraries hosted in the Camunda Community Hub which are not covered by Camunda commercial services to enterprise customers.
question: Which client offering is officially supported by Camunda for the Camunda 8.8 platform according to the community clients page?

options.text: The .NET custom Tasklist client and the Java Operate client are both officially supported Camunda 8.8 clients maintained by Camunda as part of the commercial product offering
The Quarkus Zeebe client and the Python Zeebe gRPC client are both officially supported Camunda 8.8 clients distributed through the Camunda Community Hub for enterprise teams
The TypeScript SDK and the Camunda Spring Boot Starter are both officially supported Camunda 8.8 clients maintained by Camunda as part of the commercial product offering
The Ballerina Zeebe client and the Go Zeebe client are both officially supported Camunda 8.8 clients distributed through the Camunda Community Hub for enterprise teams

d.text: Incorrect. The Ballerina and Go Zeebe clients are listed on the community clients page as community-maintained libraries in the Camunda Community Hub; Camunda does not back them through commercial services.

b.text: Incorrect. The Quarkus and Python Zeebe gRPC clients are listed on the community clients page as community-maintained libraries in the Camunda Community Hub and are not part of the commercial product.

c.text: Correct. The community clients page explicitly notes that Camunda now officially supports the TypeScript SDK and the Camunda Spring Boot Starter alongside the core Camunda-maintained clients.

a.text: Incorrect. The .NET Tasklist client and the Java Operate client are listed on the community clients page as community-maintained libraries in the Camunda Community Hub and are not part of the commercial product.

explanation: Camunda officially supports the TypeScript SDK and the Camunda Spring Boot Starter as called out on the community clients page; the other libraries on that page live in the Camunda Community Hub and are maintained by the community rather than backed by Camunda commercial services.

scenario: Sextant Bank runs a Camunda 8.8 Spring Boot service with 12 workers spread across 5 clusters. The platform team must externalize the camunda.client.worker.defaults.max-jobs-active property as an environment variable across every deployment so that operators can override the value without rebuilding the application, following Spring Boot relaxed binding conventions for environment variables.
question: Which environment variable name corresponds to the Camunda 8.8 property camunda.client.worker.defaults.max-jobs-active under Spring Boot relaxed binding?

options.text: CAMUNDA_CLIENT_WORKER_DEFAULTS_MAXJOBSACTIVE is the environment variable name for the property camunda.client.worker.defaults.max-jobs-active under Spring Boot relaxed binding conventions
CAMUNDA.CLIENT.WORKER.DEFAULTS.MAX-JOBS-ACTIVE is the environment variable name that maps to the property when Spring Boot preserves dots and dashes verbatim in the variable name itself
camunda_client_worker_defaults_maxJobsActive is the environment variable name that maps to the property when Spring Boot keeps original case and only replaces dots with underscores
CAMUNDA_CLIENT_WORKER_DEFAULTS_MAX_JOBS_ACTIVE is the environment variable name that maps to the property when each dash in the property is converted into an underscore character

a.text: Correct. Spring Boot relaxed binding converts the configuration property to uppercase, removes the dashes, and replaces every dot delimiter with an underscore, yielding CAMUNDA_CLIENT_WORKER_DEFAULTS_MAXJOBSACTIVE.

b.text: Incorrect. Environment variable names cannot contain dots or dashes; the documented Spring Boot conversion removes the dashes and replaces dot delimiters with underscores in the resulting name.

d.text: Incorrect. The documented conversion removes the dashes from the property segment rather than converting each one to an underscore; max-jobs-active becomes the joined token MAXJOBSACTIVE.

c.text: Incorrect. The documented conversion uppercases the whole name and removes the dashes; Spring Boot does not preserve the original camelCase or lowercase form for environment variables.

explanation: Configuration properties are mapped to environment variables by uppercasing the name, removing any dashes, and replacing dot delimiters with underscores; camunda.client.worker.defaults.max-jobs-active therefore becomes CAMUNDA_CLIENT_WORKER_DEFAULTS_MAXJOBSACTIVE in the environment.
