scenario: Sable Bank deploys a Camunda 8.8 Spring Boot service that must point at the Camunda SaaS offering with templated URLs across 3 clusters and 75 workers. The team needs to activate a single Camunda client mode whose preset populates the SaaS connection defaults so that only the cluster identifiers and OAuth credentials must be supplied; only one mode may be used at a time per Spring Boot application.
question: Which Camunda 8.8 Spring Boot Starter property value activates the SaaS mode preset so the SaaS URL defaults are applied?

options.text: Set camunda.client.cloud-mode to true so the Spring Boot Starter loads the SaaS preset URLs and only the cluster identifier and OAuth credentials must be supplied
Set camunda.client.mode to saas so the Spring Boot Starter loads the SaaS preset URLs and only the cluster identifier and OAuth credentials must be supplied at runtime
Set camunda.client.mode to self-managed so the Spring Boot Starter loads the SaaS preset URLs and only the cluster identifier and OAuth credentials must be supplied
Set camunda.client.profile to saas so the Spring Boot Starter loads the SaaS preset URLs and only the cluster identifier and OAuth credentials must be supplied

a.text: Incorrect. The documented activation property is camunda.client.mode taking a string value, not a boolean cloud-mode flag; setting mode to saas loads the SaaS preset defaults for connection URLs.

b.text: Correct. The SaaS mode is activated by setting camunda.client.mode to saas, which loads the SaaS preset URLs so only the auth credentials and cluster identifier and region must be supplied.

c.text: Incorrect. The self-managed value activates the Self-Managed mode whose preset URLs target localhost addresses by default rather than the SaaS templated URLs for cloud clusters.

d.text: Incorrect. The documented activation property is camunda.client.mode and not camunda.client.profile; only one mode may be used at a time and its value selects the preset bundle.

explanation: The Spring Boot Starter exposes mode presets via the camunda.client.mode property; setting it to saas loads the SaaS connection defaults, while self-managed loads localhost defaults; only one mode may be active per application, and any explicit property overrides the mode defaults.

scenario: Crestline Logistics runs a Camunda 8.8 Spring Boot client against a Self-Managed Zeebe Gateway sized for 11 brokers and 30000 jobs per day. The platform team must ensure the client uses gRPC for every gateway call instead of the default REST preference, with camunda.client.grpc-address set to http://gateway.crestline.local:26500 in the application.yaml file.
question: Which configuration change forces the Camunda 8.8 Spring Boot client to use gRPC instead of REST for gateway calls by default?

options.text: Omit the camunda.client.rest-address property entirely on the Camunda client so it falls back to gRPC for every gateway call once the REST address fails to resolve at runtime
Set camunda.client.prefer-rest-over-grpc to false on the Camunda client so the default REST preference is inverted and every call to the gateway flows through the gRPC channel
Set camunda.client.grpc-only to true on the Camunda client so REST is fully disabled and every call to the gateway flows through the gRPC channel by default at runtime
Set camunda.client.protocol to grpc on the Camunda client so REST is fully disabled and every call to the gateway flows through the gRPC channel by default at runtime

d.text: Incorrect. There is no camunda.client.protocol toggle in the documented connectivity properties; the documented switch between REST and gRPC is the prefer-rest-over-grpc boolean property.

c.text: Incorrect. There is no grpc-only flag in the documented connectivity properties; the documented switch between REST and gRPC is the prefer-rest-over-grpc boolean property that defaults to true.

a.text: Incorrect. The documented switch is the explicit prefer-rest-over-grpc property; omitting rest-address does not flip the client preference and leaves the default REST behavior unchanged at runtime.

b.text: Correct. By default the Camunda client prefers REST over gRPC whenever possible; setting camunda.client.prefer-rest-over-grpc to false inverts that preference so calls flow through the gRPC channel.

explanation: The Camunda 8 client defaults to REST over gRPC whenever possible; setting camunda.client.prefer-rest-over-grpc to false inverts the preference so the client uses gRPC for gateway calls, alongside the grpc-address property pointing at the gateway gRPC endpoint.

scenario: Lattice Healthcare runs a Camunda 8.8 Self-Managed cluster with 6 brokers and an external OIDC identity provider serving 250 users. The platform team needs the Spring Boot service to select OIDC client-credentials authentication explicitly rather than relying on implicit detection so that operators can reason about credential flow during quarterly security reviews of the deployment.
question: Which property value explicitly selects OIDC authentication on the Camunda 8.8 Spring Boot Starter client according to the configuration page?

options.text: Set camunda.client.auth.method to basic on the Spring Boot Starter so the client authenticates using a username and password pair on every gateway call as documented
Set camunda.client.auth.method to oidc on the Spring Boot Starter so the client authenticates via OpenID Connect using a client ID and client secret pair as documented
Set camunda.client.auth.method to none on the Spring Boot Starter so the client skips authentication entirely and connects to the gateway with anonymous calls as documented
Set camunda.client.auth.method to client-credentials on the Spring Boot Starter so the client authenticates via OpenID Connect using a client ID and client secret pair as documented

a.text: Incorrect. The value basic selects Basic authentication with a username and password pair against a cluster configured for Basic, rather than OIDC client-credentials flow against an identity provider.

c.text: Incorrect. The value none selects no authentication at all and leaves every gateway call anonymous; OIDC is selected with the value oidc on the auth.method property instead.

b.text: Correct. The documented value for explicit OIDC selection is oidc; setting client-id or client-secret would also imply OIDC via detection, but explicit activation uses camunda.client.auth.method oidc.

d.text: Incorrect. The documented enum value for the auth.method property is oidc; client-credentials is not the accepted value even though OIDC itself uses a client-credentials grant under the hood.

explanation: The Spring Boot Starter selects OIDC authentication explicitly when camunda.client.auth.method is set to the value oidc; setting either camunda.client.auth.client-id or camunda.client.auth.client-secret also implies OIDC via implicit detection of the configured properties.

scenario: Beacon Retail processes 22000 orders per day on Camunda 8.8 with 12 workers behind the Spring Boot Starter. The platform team must constrain the foo worker to fetch only variable1 and variable2 from the broker, defining the override in application.yaml so that the configuration takes precedence over annotation-based detection across every deployment for the retail order pipeline.
question: Which Camunda 8.8 Spring Boot Starter configuration causes the foo job worker to fetch only variable1 and variable2 on each job activation?

options.text: Define camunda.client.worker.override.foo.force-fetch-all-variables with value true so the foo worker activates jobs with only variable1 and variable2 loaded from broker state
Define camunda.client.worker.override.foo.fetch-variables-as-list with variable1 and variable2 so the foo worker activates jobs with only those two variables loaded from broker state
Define camunda.client.worker.defaults.fetch-variables with the entries variable1 and variable2 so the foo worker activates jobs with only those two variables loaded from broker state
Define camunda.client.worker.override.foo.fetch-variables with the entries variable1 and variable2 so the foo worker activates jobs with only those two variables loaded from broker state

d.text: Correct. The documented per-worker constraint is camunda.client.worker.override.WORKER_TYPE.fetch-variables expressed as a YAML list; this properties-defined form overrides all other detection strategies for that worker type.

c.text: Incorrect. The defaults section applies to every worker rather than only the foo worker; per-worker constraints live under the override section keyed by the specific worker type such as foo.

a.text: Incorrect. Setting force-fetch-all-variables to true forces every process variable to be fetched, which is the opposite of constraining the list to variable1 and variable2 for the foo worker.

b.text: Incorrect. The documented property name is fetch-variables and it accepts a YAML list of variable names directly; there is no fetch-variables-as-list variant in the worker override configuration.

explanation: Per-worker variable filtering is expressed as camunda.client.worker.override.WORKER_TYPE.fetch-variables with a YAML list of variable names; this properties-defined form overrides all other detection strategies, including annotation-based fetchVariables on the JobWorker annotation.

subtopic: Spring Boot Starter job worker parameters
scenario: Helios Insurance writes a Camunda 8.8 Spring Boot job worker that handles 7500 claims per day across 90 workers in the cluster. The handler signature uses an ActivatedJob parameter so the developer can retrieve variables programmatically via getVariablesAsMap inside the method, and the team needs to understand how this choice influences automatic variable fetching by the Spring Boot Starter at runtime.
question: How does declaring an ActivatedJob parameter on a Camunda 8.8 JobWorker method affect implicit variable fetching for that worker?

options.text: It enables implicit detection only when fetchAllVariables is also set to true on the JobWorker annotation so the worker receives every process variable along with the job activation by default
It prevents implicit variable fetching detection so only explicit variable fetching such as fetchVariables on the annotation or property overrides is effective for that worker at runtime
It still triggers implicit detection so all process variables are sent to the worker by default and getVariablesAsMap simply reads from the prefetched local cache held by the handler at runtime
It enables implicit detection scoped to the variables referenced in the handler body so the runtime statically analyses the method bytecode to choose which variables to send on activation

c.text: Incorrect. Using an ActivatedJob parameter disables the implicit variable fetching detection; variables are not auto-prefetched into a cache and must be retrieved programmatically from the job at runtime.

a.text: Incorrect. The fetchAllVariables attribute forces every variable to be fetched but does not re-enable implicit detection on the ActivatedJob signature; explicit fetching remains the only effective path.

d.text: Incorrect. The Spring Boot Starter does not perform bytecode analysis of the handler body; ActivatedJob simply prevents implicit detection in favor of explicit fetching strategies on the worker.

b.text: Correct. The documentation states that using ActivatedJob as a parameter prevents implicit variable fetching detection; only explicit fetching such as fetchVariables on the annotation or property overrides is effective.

explanation: Declaring an ActivatedJob parameter prevents implicit variable fetching detection in the Spring Boot Starter; only explicit fetching mechanisms such as fetchVariables on the JobWorker annotation, the @Variable annotation, @VariablesAsType, or property overrides will load variables for the worker at activation time.

scenario: Lattice Insurance runs a Camunda 8.8 Spring Boot service that processes 12000 jobs per day for claims intake. A reviewer notices that the @JobWorker(type = "foo") handler method finishes its synchronous work and returns a Map of process variables, yet the developers never call client.newCompleteCommand() anywhere in the codebase. The reviewer wants to confirm why the job is still completed every time the handler returns normally on Camunda 8.8.
question: How does the Camunda Spring Boot Starter complete a @JobWorker handler that never calls newCompleteCommand?

options.text: The autoComplete attribute defaults to true on every @JobWorker, so the Spring integration completes the job after the handler method returns synchronously
The autoComplete attribute defaults to true only for void handlers, so the Spring integration completes typed return values through a separate annotation parameter
The autoComplete attribute defaults to false on every @JobWorker, so the Spring integration completes the job only after a separate retry timer fires
The autoComplete attribute defaults to true only when the broker runs in cluster mode, so the Spring integration completes the job after a broker acknowledgement arrives

a.text: Correct. The configuration docs state that autoComplete defaults to true for any @JobWorker; the Spring integration triggers completion right after the synchronous handler returns, and the return value is sent as process variables.

c.text: Incorrect. The docs state the opposite default; autoComplete is true out of the box, which is exactly why the handler does not need to call newCompleteCommand.

b.text: Incorrect. Auto-completion applies to every @JobWorker regardless of return type; supported returns include Map, String, InputStream, and any Object that can be serialised to JSON.

d.text: Incorrect. The auto-complete default is unconditional on the worker side and does not depend on broker topology; the Spring integration completes the job whenever the handler returns normally.

explanation: In the Camunda Spring Boot Starter, the @JobWorker autoComplete attribute defaults to true, so the integration completes the job right after the synchronous handler returns and forwards any returned Map, String, InputStream, or Object as process variables.

scenario: Helios Bank runs a Camunda 8.8 Spring Boot worker that settles 8000 payments per day. When the downstream settlement endpoint times out, the team wants the job to fail in a controlled way that decrements the remaining retries and delays the next activation by 10 seconds, without raising a BPMN error event in the process model. The team uses the helpers exposed by the Spring Boot Starter to throw the right typed error from inside the @JobWorker method.
question: Which Spring Boot Starter helper fails the job in a controlled way with custom retries and a retryBackoff?

options.text: Throw a plain RuntimeException with retries and retryBackoff fields; the Spring integration converts the exception into a job failure with that retry policy
Throw CamundaError.fail with errorMessage and a Duration only; the helper returns a generic failure that decrements retries by one and applies that retryBackoff
Throw CamundaError.jobError with errorMessage, variables, retries, and retryBackoff; the helper returns a JobError that fails the job with that retry policy
Throw CamundaError.bpmnError with an error code and message; the helper returns a BpmnError that drives the matching BPMN error event handler

d.text: Incorrect. CamundaError.bpmnError raises a BPMN error event for business problems; it does not control retries or retryBackoff, so it is not the controlled-failure helper the docs describe.

c.text: Correct. The docs describe CamundaError.jobError as the static helper that returns a JobError taking errorMessage, variables, retries, and retryBackoff so the job fails in a controlled way.

a.text: Incorrect. A plain RuntimeException does not carry retries or retryBackoff parameters; the documented mechanism is the JobError returned by the CamundaError.jobError helper.

b.text: Incorrect. The docs do not describe a CamundaError.fail helper; the controlled-failure helper is CamundaError.jobError, which takes five parameters including variables.

explanation: The Camunda Spring Boot Starter exposes CamundaError.jobError as the static helper that returns a JobError; throwing it from a @JobWorker method fails the job with the supplied errorMessage, optional variables, retries, and retryBackoff Duration.

scenario: Vanta Logistics is migrating a shipment service to Camunda 8.8 over 4 weeks. The legacy Spring Boot application defines 6 workers including one whose @JobWorker(type = "foo") will move to a new application. During the transition the operations team wants to leave the legacy application running with its Camunda client active, but disable only the foo worker through configuration so the new application can pick it up without code changes.
question: Which application.yaml override disables only the foo worker while leaving the other workers and the client running?

options.text: Set camunda.client.worker.override.foo.enabled to false; the configuration keeps the Camunda client available and disables only the foo worker through the documented override key
Set camunda.client.worker.foo.enabled to false; the configuration keeps the Camunda client available and disables only the foo worker through a per-name top-level key
Set camunda.client.worker.foo.annotation.enabled to false; the configuration keeps the Camunda client available and forces every other worker to inherit its annotation default
Set camunda.client.worker.defaults.enabled to false; the configuration keeps the Camunda client available but stops every job worker registered in the application from running

d.text: Incorrect. The defaults.enabled key disables every worker in the application, which is the opposite of the scoped per-worker override the team needs for the foo worker during migration.

b.text: Incorrect. The documented structure nests per-worker settings under override, not directly under worker; a top-level worker.foo key is not the configuration path described in the docs.

a.text: Correct. The docs show that per-worker settings live under camunda.client.worker.override.<name>; setting override.foo.enabled to false disables only the foo worker and leaves the other workers and the client running.

c.text: Incorrect. There is no annotation child key under the per-worker configuration; the documented override path is camunda.client.worker.override.<name>.enabled.

explanation: The Camunda Spring Boot Starter lets you override individual @JobWorker settings under camunda.client.worker.override.<name>; setting override.foo.enabled to false disables only the foo worker, while defaults.enabled controls every worker at once.

scenario: Stellar Bank deploys a Camunda Spring Boot Starter worker on Camunda 8.8 that polls 1800 jobs per hour. The platform team must externalize the property camunda.client.worker.defaults.max-jobs-active via an environment variable instead of a YAML file, so the same container image runs across staging and production without rebuilding, and they need to apply Spring Boot relaxed-binding rules.
question: Which environment variable name maps to the property camunda.client.worker.defaults.max-jobs-active under Spring Boot relaxed binding?

options.text: Set camunda.client.worker.defaults.max-jobs-active on the container so Spring Boot resolves the property and overrides the YAML value at startup time for the running worker
Set CAMUNDA_CLIENT_WORKER_DEFAULTS_MAX_JOBS_ACTIVE on the container so Spring Boot resolves the property and overrides the YAML value at startup time for the running worker
Set CAMUNDA_CLIENT_WORKER_DEFAULTS_MAXJOBSACTIVE on the container so Spring Boot resolves the property and overrides the YAML value at startup time for the running worker
Set CAMUNDA-CLIENT-WORKER-DEFAULTS-MAX-JOBS-ACTIVE on the container so Spring Boot resolves the property and overrides the YAML value at startup time for the running worker

d.text: Incorrect. The docs state that you convert the property to uppercase and replace delimiters '.' with underscore '_'; dash-separated environment variable names are not the documented relaxed-binding form.

c.text: Correct. The docs give exactly this example: camunda.client.worker.defaults.max-jobs-active is represented by the environment variable CAMUNDA_CLIENT_WORKER_DEFAULTS_MAXJOBSACTIVE.

a.text: Incorrect. Lower-case dotted property names are the YAML form, not the documented relaxed-binding environment variable form for the Spring Boot Starter.

b.text: Incorrect. The relaxed-binding rule removes any dashes inside a key segment; only the dot delimiters become underscores, so max-jobs-active becomes MAXJOBSACTIVE, not MAX_JOBS_ACTIVE.

explanation: Spring Boot relaxed binding for the Camunda Spring Boot Starter uppercases the property, removes any dashes, and replaces dot delimiters with underscores. The page gives the example mapping camunda.client.worker.defaults.max-jobs-active to CAMUNDA_CLIENT_WORKER_DEFAULTS_MAXJOBSACTIVE.

scenario: Nimbus Insurance integrates a Camunda Spring Boot Starter worker with a self-managed Camunda 8.8 cluster that authenticates via OpenID Connect. The cluster expects 5200 jobs per day and the team configures camunda.client.auth.client-id and camunda.client.auth.client-secret without setting camunda.client.auth.method explicitly. A reviewer asks which authentication method will actually be applied in this configuration.
question: Which authentication method does the starter activate when only client-id and client-secret are configured?

options.text: The starter activates the none preset because camunda.client.auth.method was not set explicitly, so the worker connects without sending any credentials to the self-managed cluster
The starter activates the oidc preset because the oidc method is implied when either camunda.client.auth.client-id or camunda.client.auth.client-secret is set in the application properties
The starter activates the basic preset because client-id and client-secret are accepted as username and password aliases when camunda.client.auth.method is omitted from configuration
The starter refuses to start because camunda.client.auth.method is required whenever any auth property is set, so the worker fails fast at context initialization with an error

a.text: Incorrect. The none preset is only the default when no other property implies an authentication method; setting client-id or client-secret implies oidc.

c.text: Incorrect. Basic auth is implied by camunda.client.auth.username or camunda.client.auth.password, not by client-id or client-secret.

d.text: Incorrect. The starter detects the auth method automatically when camunda.client.auth.method is omitted; it does not require the method to be set explicitly.

b.text: Correct. The docs state that the oidc method will be implied if you set either camunda.client.auth.client-id or camunda.client.auth.client-secret, even without setting method explicitly.

explanation: When camunda.client.auth.method is omitted, the starter detects the method from other properties. Setting camunda.client.auth.client-id or camunda.client.auth.client-secret implies the oidc method, while setting username or password implies basic, and otherwise the none preset is used.

scenario: Junction Telco runs a Camunda Spring Boot Starter worker on Camunda 8.8 that handles 3800 jobs per day. A developer adds a method annotated @JobWorker without setting the type attribute, and another method annotated @JobWorker(type = "foo"). A reviewer asks which job type each method binds to so that operators can map them to BPMN service-task definitions in the process model.
question: Which job type does each method bind to under the documented @JobWorker rules?

options.text: The annotation @JobWorker(type = "foo") binds to job type foo and the annotation @JobWorker without type attribute fails fast at startup with a missing-type configuration error
The annotation @JobWorker(type = "foo") binds to job type foo and the annotation @JobWorker without type attribute binds to the method name foo via the documented fallback rule
The annotation @JobWorker(type = "foo") binds to job type foo and the annotation @JobWorker without type attribute binds to a synthetic UUID generated by the starter for that method
The annotation @JobWorker(type = "foo") binds to job type foo and the annotation @JobWorker without type attribute binds to the simple class name of the enclosing Spring bean by default

b.text: Correct. The docs state that with @JobWorker(type = "foo") the worker handles jobs of type foo, and when type is omitted the method name is used by default, so @JobWorker on a method named foo also handles type foo.

d.text: Incorrect. The fallback when type is omitted is the method name, not the simple class name of the bean; class-name binding is not the documented fallback rule.

c.text: Incorrect. The starter does not generate a synthetic UUID job type; it falls back to the method name when type is omitted from the annotation.

a.text: Incorrect. The starter does not fail at startup when type is omitted; it uses the method name as the job type per the documented default rule.

explanation: The starter binds @JobWorker(type = "foo") to job type foo. When the type attribute is omitted, the method name is used by default as the job type, so a method named foo annotated @JobWorker also handles jobs of type foo.

scenario: Echo Bank runs a Camunda Spring Boot Starter worker on Camunda 8.8 that activates 5400 jobs per hour. A developer sets @JobWorker(type = "foo", fetchVariables = {"v1"}) on the handler and at the same time configures camunda.client.worker.override.foo.fetch-variables with v2 and v3 in application.yaml. A reviewer wants to know which variables the worker will actually fetch when it activates a job.
question: Which variables does the worker fetch when both the @JobWorker annotation and the override properties list fetch-variables?

options.text: The worker merges the values from the annotation and the properties, so it fetches v1, v2, and v3 from every activated job in the worker thread pool during normal operation
The worker honours only the properties-defined fetch-variables list, so it fetches v2 and v3 because the properties form overrides all other detection strategies for that worker
The worker activates jobs without any variables because the conflict between the annotation and the properties disables the variable fetching feature for the affected worker handler
The worker honours only the @JobWorker(type, fetchVariables) annotation values, so it fetches v1 because the annotation takes precedence over the overrides in application.yaml

a.text: Incorrect. The docs note that the properties-defined fetch-variables list does not merge with the annotation; it overrides all other detection strategies for that worker.

d.text: Incorrect. The annotation does not win over the property override; the properties-defined fetch-variables list overrides all other detection strategies including the annotation.

b.text: Correct. The docs explicitly caution that using the properties-defined way of fetching variables will override all other detection strategies, so the override.foo.fetch-variables list wins over the annotation.

c.text: Incorrect. There is no documented behaviour that disables variable fetching when the annotation and properties disagree; the properties simply override the annotation list.

explanation: The variable-fetching section warns that the properties-defined fetch-variables list overrides all other detection strategies for that worker. With override.foo.fetch-variables set, that list wins over fetchVariables on the @JobWorker annotation.

scenario: Wren Insurance models a Camunda 8.8 claims process and writes a Camunda Spring Boot Starter worker that handles 4100 jobs per day. The developer wants the handler signature to materialize process variables as a strongly typed MyProcessVariables object so the worker can mutate fields and return the object to push changes back to Zeebe, and a reviewer checks that the documented annotation is used.
question: Which @JobWorker parameter annotation materializes process variables as a strongly typed object for the handler?

options.text: Use the @CustomHeaders annotation on a MyProcessVariables parameter so the starter binds the class fields to the activated job variables and returns the updated object to Zeebe automatically
Use the @VariablesAsType annotation on a MyProcessVariables parameter so the starter binds the class fields to the activated job variables and returns the updated object to Zeebe automatically
Use the @Variable annotation on a MyProcessVariables parameter so the starter binds the class fields to the activated job variables and returns the updated object to Zeebe automatically
Use the @VariableMap annotation on a MyProcessVariables parameter so the starter binds the class fields to the activated job variables and returns the updated object to Zeebe automatically

a.text: Incorrect. @CustomHeaders binds a Map<String, String> of job custom headers and has no effect on the variable fetching behaviour for the worker.

c.text: Incorrect. @Variable binds a single process variable to a method parameter by name; it does not materialize an entire typed object from the variable map.

d.text: Incorrect. The starter does not document a @VariableMap annotation; the documented bulk-binding annotation for typed variable objects is @VariablesAsType.

b.text: Correct. @VariablesAsType maps the activated process variables into a custom class such as MyProcessVariables, comparable to getVariablesAsType, and Jackson's @JsonProperty annotation is respected for field mapping.

explanation: The starter exposes the @VariablesAsType annotation to map activated process variables into a custom class. The names of the fields of the type join the list of variables to fetch, and returning the object pushes any changes back to Zeebe on auto-completion.

scenario: Catalyst Bank runs a Camunda Spring Boot Starter worker on Camunda 8.8 that handles 8200 jobs per day. A developer wants to complete each job from a reactive whenComplete callback after a downstream HTTP call resolves, so the handler calls client.newCompleteCommand(job.getKey()).send() itself, and a reviewer wants the configuration that prevents the starter from also completing the same job.
question: Which @JobWorker setting prevents the starter from auto-completing a job that the handler completes programmatically?

options.text: Set autoComplete to false on @JobWorker so that the starter does not call complete itself, avoiding the race condition between programmatic completion and Spring integration completion of the same job

Set autoComplete to true on @JobWorker so that the starter waits for the handler method to return before sending complete, which is the documented setting for programmatic completion of jobs in reactive callbacks
Set enabled to false on @JobWorker so that the starter does not run its own completion path when the handler calls newCompleteCommand on the JobClient from a reactive whenComplete callback
Set fetchAllVariables to false on @JobWorker so that the starter skips its own completion path when the handler runs reactive callbacks that finish after the handler method returns to the worker pool

a.text: Correct. The docs state that when completing jobs programmatically the developer must specify autoComplete = false, otherwise there is a race condition between the programmatic job completion and the Spring integration job completion, leading to unpredictable results.

b.text: Incorrect. autoComplete = true is the default and triggers Spring integration to complete the job right after the handler returns, which is exactly the race condition with programmatic completion.

d.text: Incorrect. fetchAllVariables controls variable fetching, not completion behaviour; auto-completion is controlled by the autoComplete attribute on @JobWorker.

c.text: Incorrect. Setting enabled = false disables the worker entirely so it would not poll jobs; the documented setting to allow programmatic completion is autoComplete = false.

explanation: When the handler completes jobs programmatically via newCompleteCommand, the docs require autoComplete = false on @JobWorker. Otherwise a race condition arises between the programmatic completion and the Spring integration auto-completion that runs right after the handler returns.

scenario: Sable Insurance configures a Camunda Spring Boot Starter worker on Camunda 8.8 that emits 3700 jobs per hour. The team uses the default auto-completing handlers and wants to return updated process variables from each handler method so that downstream BPMN elements receive the new values without calling the JobClient directly, and a reviewer audits which return types the starter accepts.
question: Which return type does the documented auto-completing @JobWorker handler accept for process variables?

options.text: Return a CompletionStage<Void> that resolves once the downstream service replies, after which the starter reads variables back from the original ActivatedJob payload to forward to the broker on completion
Return a JobClient.CompleteCommand that the starter executes on the developer's behalf, picking the variable map from the command builder before it issues the complete request to the broker

Return a Map<String, Object> containing the process variables, which the docs list as one of the accepted return shapes alongside a JSON String, an InputStream of JSON, or any Object serialized to JSON

Return a List<Object> of positional variable values that the starter zips with a configured names list under worker.override.foo.variable-names to produce the variable map for the broker on completion

a.text: Incorrect. The starter does not read variables back from the ActivatedJob after the handler resolves; the handler itself returns the variable map that the starter sends with complete.

b.text: Incorrect. The handler does not return a CompleteCommand builder; the documented return shapes are Map, JSON String, JSON InputStream, or Object serialized to JSON.

c.text: Correct. The docs list the accepted auto-complete return types as Map<String, Object>, String containing a valid JSON object, InputStream streaming a valid JSON object, and Object that is serialized to a JSON object.

d.text: Incorrect. The starter does not consume positional value lists zipped with a names property; it expects a keyed structure such as a Map or a JSON object payload to derive the variable map.

explanation: Under autoComplete the @JobWorker handler may return a Map<String, Object>, a String containing a valid JSON object, an InputStream streaming a valid JSON object, or any Object that the starter serializes to a JSON object. The starter forwards the resulting variables on completion.

scenario: Harbor Insurance operates a Camunda Spring Boot Starter worker on Camunda 8.8 that handles 4600 jobs per day. The handler must fail the job in a controlled way with a custom error message, three retries, and a 10 seconds retryBackoff, and the team must choose between throwing a BpmnError to trigger a BPMN boundary error and throwing a JobError to drive the broker fail-job command.
question: Which CamundaError factory method does the page document for failing a job in a controlled way with retries and retryBackoff?

options.text: Call CamundaError.systemError with the message, retries, and retryBackoff and rethrow it so the broker stops the worker and unblocks the process token at the boundary catch event
Call CamundaError.jobError with errorMessage, variables, retries, retryBackoff, and cause and rethrow it so the SDK calls the Fail Job API with the documented parameters
Call CamundaError.retryError with the message and retryBackoff and rethrow it so the broker reschedules the job in the worker queue after the configured backoff duration elapses
Call CamundaError.bpmnError with the BPMN error code and the explanation message and rethrow it so the broker translates the throw into a Fail Job API call with the configured retry settings

d.text: Incorrect. CamundaError.bpmnError throws a BpmnError to raise a BPMN error event, which is a business-level signal and is distinct from failing the job with retries and retryBackoff.

a.text: Incorrect. There is no CamundaError.systemError factory documented; the controlled-fail path uses CamundaError.jobError with errorMessage, variables, retries, retryBackoff, and cause.

c.text: Incorrect. CamundaError.retryError is not part of the documented API; the documented call is CamundaError.jobError, which takes retries and retryBackoff as parameters.

b.text: Correct. The docs document CamundaError.jobError with five parameters errorMessage, variables, retries (default job.getRetries() minus one), retryBackoff (default PT0S), and cause, and note the SDK then calls the Fail Job API.

explanation: To fail a job in a controlled way the handler throws CamundaError.jobError(errorMessage, variables, retries, retryBackoff, cause). The SDK then calls the Fail Job API; retries defaults to job.getRetries() minus one and retryBackoff defaults to PT0S.

scenario: Ridge Bank rolls out a Camunda Spring Boot Starter worker on Camunda 8.8 that polls 5400 jobs per hour with default settings, but profiling shows the broker can stream jobs faster than the worker can poll. The architect plans to enable job streaming on the foo worker via configuration and asks which property toggle the page documents for that specific behaviour change on a single worker.
question: Which property toggle enables job streaming on the foo worker in the documented configuration?

options.text: Set camunda.client.worker.override.foo.stream-jobs to enabled so that the broker uses long-lived gRPC streams to deliver activated jobs to the foo worker in real time
Set camunda.client.worker.override.foo.max-jobs-active to 64 so that the foo worker pre-fetches more jobs in advance, effectively achieving the same throughput as job streaming on the broker
Set camunda.client.worker.override.foo.stream-enabled to true so that job streaming is activated for the foo worker, which is otherwise disabled by default on every job worker
Set camunda.client.worker.override.foo.streaming-mode to push so that the broker pushes activated jobs at the worker as soon as they appear in the queue for that job type

d.text: Incorrect. The documented property is stream-enabled, not streaming-mode; the page does not define a push value for a streaming-mode property.

c.text: Correct. The docs state that job streaming is disabled by default and is enabled per worker via camunda.client.worker.override.foo.stream-enabled set to true, or via @JobWorker(streamEnabled = true) on the handler annotation.

a.text: Incorrect. The property name is stream-enabled with a boolean value true, not stream-jobs with a string value enabled.

b.text: Incorrect. max-jobs-active controls how many jobs are polled and the thread pool size; it does not enable the gRPC job streaming feature on the broker side.

explanation: Job streaming is disabled by default. To enable it on the foo worker via configuration the docs use camunda.client.worker.override.foo.stream-enabled set to true; the same effect is achieved via @JobWorker(streamEnabled = true) on the handler.

scenario: Aurora Bank runs a Camunda 8.8 Spring Boot service that owns 14 BPMN files for retail customer onboarding and must deploy them automatically every time the application starts so that 9000 orders per day continue to flow without a manual upload step against the Orchestration Cluster.
question: Which Camunda Spring Boot Starter mechanism deploys process models on application start-up?

options.text: Place the BPMN files inside src/main/resources/META-INF/processes and rely on the starter to auto-scan that folder on application start-up without any annotation or configuration property
Set the property camunda.client.deployment.auto-scan=true so the starter inspects the entire classpath and deploys every .bpmn file it encounters on application start-up
Annotate a registered Spring bean with @Deployment and pass resource paths such as classpath:demoProcess.bpmn so the starter loads them via the Spring resource loader on application start-up
Implement the ApplicationRunner interface and manually call CamundaClient newDeployResourceCommand inside the run method for every BPMN file the application needs at start-up

c.text: Correct. The Camunda Spring Boot Starter ships a @Deployment annotation that internally uses the Spring resource loader, supports lists of resources and wildcard patterns such as classpath*:/bpmn/**/*.bpmn, and lets you set tenantId on the annotation.

a.text: Incorrect. The starter does not auto-scan META-INF/processes for BPMN files; deployment on start-up is driven by the @Deployment annotation on a registered Spring bean.

b.text: Incorrect. There is no camunda.client.deployment.auto-scan property; the deployment toggle is camunda.client.deployment.enabled and the resource list comes from the @Deployment annotation.

d.text: Incorrect. Calling newDeployResourceCommand manually from an ApplicationRunner is possible but is not the documented start-up mechanism the starter provides; the documented mechanism is the @Deployment annotation.

explanation: The Camunda Spring Boot Starter documents @Deployment as the way to deploy process models on application start-up. The annotation accepts one resource, a list of resources, or wildcard patterns through the Spring resource loader, supports a tenantId property, and falls back to camunda.client.tenant-id when none is provided.

scenario: Kestrel Logistics operates a Camunda 8.8 Spring Boot service that runs 7 job workers processing 12000 shipments per day for a regional carrier. The operations team wants to chart how many jobs are activated, completed, failed, and finished with a BPMN error per job type, using the metrics surface the starter already exposes through Spring Actuator.
question: Which metric does the Camunda Spring Boot Starter expose out of the box to track job worker activity per job type?

options.text: zeebe.broker.partitions reports the number of partitions and lag per partition leader, tagged by job type, and is the documented job worker activity metric of the starter
camunda.job.invocations counts invocations of job workers tagged by the job type, with actions activated, completed, failed, and bpmn-error recorded for each job type
spring.boot.actuator.jobs counts jobs processed by every Spring bean and is filtered by job type via the management.endpoints.web.exposure.include property of the starter
camunda.client.requests counts every gRPC and REST request the Camunda client sends, tagged by request type, and is the documented way to chart worker activity per BPMN job type

d.text: Incorrect. The starter does not expose a camunda.client.requests metric for worker activity; the documented metric is camunda.job.invocations, tagged by the job type.

a.text: Incorrect. zeebe.broker.partitions is not a starter-side metric for charting worker activity; the documented metric is camunda.job.invocations on the worker side.

b.text: Correct. The starter publishes camunda.job.invocations tagged by job type, and records the actions activated, completed, failed, and bpmn-error so each job type can be charted by outcome via Spring Actuator.

c.text: Incorrect. There is no spring.boot.actuator.jobs metric; the management.endpoints.web.exposure.include property only controls which Actuator endpoints are served.

explanation: The Camunda Spring Boot Starter exposes camunda.job.invocations through Spring Actuator. The metric is tagged with the job type and records four actions: activated when a job is picked up, completed on success, failed on exception, and bpmn-error when the worker reports a BPMN error.

scenario: Pinnacle Retailer is replicating the Flowing Retail Kafka pattern on Camunda 8.8 to process 22000 orders per day. The architects want to mirror the exact technology stack from the documented example so that an incoming Kafka topic record triggers a new process instance, and so an outgoing service task publishes a new Kafka record. They need to confirm which Java framework combination is shown in the Apache Kafka section of the best-practice docs.
question: Which Java technology stack does the Flowing Retail Kafka example use to subscribe to a topic and start process instances?

options.text: Use Spring Kafka with a manual KafkaConsumer poll loop alongside the Zeebe Java client; the loop reads each record from the topic and starts a new process instance for every consumed message
Use Spring Integration Kafka channel adapters alongside the Camunda Connector runtime; the adapter routes records to an inbound connector that starts a new process instance for every record
Use Spring Cloud Stream Kafka Streams binder alongside the Zeebe Node.js client; the binder consumes the topic and a stateless processor function starts a new process instance for every record
Use Java with Spring Boot and Spring Cloud Streams as shown in the Flowing Retail example; the listener subscribes to the Kafka topic and starts a new process instance for every consumed record

a.text: Incorrect. The Apache Kafka section does not show Spring Kafka with a manual KafkaConsumer loop; the documented example uses Spring Cloud Streams on top of Spring Boot to subscribe to the topic.

c.text: Incorrect. The example is a Java stack on Spring Boot, not a Node.js client; Spring Cloud Streams is correct but the rest of the stack does not match the documented Flowing Retail example.

b.text: Incorrect. The Apache Kafka section does not route through Spring Integration channel adapters or the Connector runtime; it uses Spring Cloud Streams in a plain Java Spring Boot application.

d.text: Correct. The Apache Kafka section names Java, Spring Boot, and Spring Cloud Streams as the Flowing Retail example stack; a listener consumes the topic and starts a new process instance for every record.

explanation: In the Technology examples section, the Apache Kafka pattern uses Java with Spring Boot and Spring Cloud Streams as demonstrated in the Flowing Retail example; a Kafka listener subscribes to the topic and starts a new Camunda process instance for every incoming record.

docs.title: Connecting the workflow engine with your world

scenario: Vertex Telco is building a Camunda 8.8 microservice that handles 6500 customers per day on a self-managed cluster. A senior engineer embeds the Zeebe client library into the microservice and wants to confirm the two fundamental things the application can do with that client, before sketching the BPMN model and worker glue code.
question: Which two things can an application do with the embedded Zeebe client library according to the best-practices guide?

options.text: Query the Zeebe internal database directly using SQL for reporting and patch process instance state in place when a corrective release ships across regulated environments
Replicate process variables to a secondary broker for warm standby and elect a partition leader from the client when the current leader misses 3 heartbeats in a row
Actively call Zeebe to start process instances, correlate messages, or deploy process definitions, and subscribe to tasks created in the workflow engine in the context of BPMN service tasks
Embed the workflow engine in-process for unit testing and stream BPMN diagram changes to a connected Modeler instance from inside the client without involving any broker

c.text: Correct. The best-practices page lists exactly two responsibilities: actively call Zeebe (start instances, correlate messages, deploy process definitions) and subscribe to tasks created for BPMN service tasks.

a.text: Incorrect. The client does not query a Zeebe internal database with SQL or patch instance state in place; it talks to the workflow engine over the documented APIs.

d.text: Incorrect. Embedded engine and Modeler streaming are not capabilities of the client library described on this page; Zeebe is a remote system.

b.text: Incorrect. Replication and leader election are responsibilities of the broker, not of an application using the client library.

explanation: The best-practices page calls out two fundamental things the application can do with the Zeebe client library: it can actively call Zeebe to start process instances, correlate messages, or deploy process definitions, and it can subscribe to tasks created by BPMN service tasks so that worker glue code is invoked when a job is created.

docs.title: Connecting the workflow engine with your world

scenario: Orion Marketplace runs a Camunda 8.8 Spring Boot service for a retailer that activates 15000 jobs per day for service tasks of type serviceA. A platform engineer wants the shortest snippet that opens a subscription for that job type and runs the worker glue code without the team writing any explicit polling loop or worker registration boilerplate.
question: Which Camunda Spring Boot Starter feature opens the subscription for the job worker automatically?

options.text: Annotating a bean method with @JobWorker(type = "serviceA") lets the Spring integration start the job worker and open the subscription for that job type automatically in the background
Annotating the application class with @EnableZeebeWorker and listing the job type in application.yaml causes the starter to long-poll for that job type without further code from the worker bean
Subclassing AbstractJobHandler and registering the bean with the Spring context causes the starter to open a subscription against the broker for the configured job type at application start-up
Registering a Spring @Scheduled method with the job type as a parameter causes the starter to translate the schedule into a long-poll subscription against the broker for that job type

c.text: Incorrect. There is no documented AbstractJobHandler base class that the starter scans for subscriptions; the documented mechanism is the @JobWorker annotation on a bean method.

b.text: Incorrect. The documented mechanism is the @JobWorker annotation on a bean method, not an @EnableZeebeWorker plus YAML job-type list.

a.text: Correct. The Camunda Spring Boot Starter starts the job worker and opens the subscription automatically when a bean method is annotated with @JobWorker(type = ...); the snippet on the page shows the type set to serviceA.

d.text: Incorrect. @Scheduled drives time-based execution and is not used to translate into Zeebe long-poll subscriptions; @JobWorker is the documented integration.

explanation: With the Camunda Spring Boot Starter a developer only annotates a bean method with @JobWorker(type = "serviceA"). The starter takes care of starting the worker, opening the subscription, and long-polling the broker for jobs of that type, while the method body holds the business logic.

docs.title: Connecting the workflow engine with your world

scenario: Beacon SaaS is designing an onboarding microservice on Camunda 8.8 that will handle 7200 customers per day. The team is debating whether each service task should live in its own application or whether the BPMN model, the REST endpoint that starts instances, and the workers should all sit inside one codebase together as a single process solution.
question: Which packaging guidance does the best-practices guide give for the glue code of a typical Camunda 8 process solution?

options.text: Concentrate every worker in a shared platform team monolith and forbid product teams from owning workers, REST endpoints, or BPMN models inside their own microservice codebases entirely

Bundle the BPMN process definition, the REST endpoint that starts the instance, and the workers that implement the service tasks together in one process solution; split a worker into its own application only when a specific exception calls for it
Always isolate each worker in its own application and let the BPMN process definition live in a separate repository so the four pieces never share a deployment artifact across the platform
Treat every BPMN service task as its own runtime service deployed with a sidecar container that hosts the matching worker glue code so the workers never share JVM resources across activities

b.text: Correct. The guide says a typical application bundles multiple pieces of glue code: the BPMN process definition that auto-deploys at start-up, a REST endpoint that starts the instance, and the workers for each service task; standalone workers are an exception.

c.text: Incorrect. The page recommends bundling the workers with the BPMN model and entry-point glue code; isolation per worker is the exception, not the rule.

d.text: Incorrect. There is no recommendation to deploy each service task as a separate service with its own sidecar worker; that contradicts the documented bundling guidance.

a.text: Incorrect. The guide encourages product teams to own the process solution together, not to centralise every worker in a platform monolith.

explanation: The onboarding example shows a single microservice with a REST endpoint to start instances, an auto-deployed BPMN process definition, and the job workers for each service task. The best-practices page recommends bundling all workers inside one process solution and only splitting a worker into its own application when a specific situation justifies it.

docs.title: Connecting the workflow engine with your world

scenario: Apex Fintech runs a Camunda 8.8 platform that handles 22000 transactions per day for a fintech provider. The team is weighing two implementation options for a brand-new payments service task that has very specific business logic and may evolve every 6 weeks: writing custom glue code with the Java client, or installing a community connector with generic configuration.
question: Which general rule of thumb does the best-practices guide give for choosing between custom glue code and an existing connector?

options.text: Always prefer an existing connector because connectors are easier to operate and test than glue code, and only fall back to custom glue code when no community or marketplace connector covers the integration scenario at hand

Prefer connectors for every BPMN service task that calls more than 3 systems and custom glue code for any task that only calls a single system, irrespective of customization, testing, or operational concerns

Prefer custom glue code whenever there is no good reason to go with an existing connector; choose a connector for complex integrations with little customization or scenarios where no custom glue code is needed at all

Always prefer custom glue code for inbound integrations and existing connectors for outbound integrations, regardless of testing, customization needs, or operational complexity of the connector runtime

a.text: Incorrect. The recommendation reverses this: glue code is the default; connectors are chosen only when there is a clear reason.

d.text: Incorrect. The inbound/outbound split is not the rule of thumb on the page; the decision is driven by customization, testing, and operational trade-offs.

c.text: Correct. The Recommendation section states the general rule: prefer custom glue code unless there is a good reason for a connector, such as complex integrations with little customization (RPA bridge) or scenarios where no custom code is needed (AWS Lambda).

b.text: Incorrect. The page does not use a system-count rule; the decision is based on customization needs, testability, and operational complexity.

explanation: The Recommendation section says to prefer custom glue code by default. Connectors are a good fit for complex integrations with little customization (such as the RPA bridge), for scenarios where no custom glue is needed (such as AWS Lambda), or for reusable generic adapters. Downsides include the connector's limits, extra operational runtime, and harder testing.

docs.title: Connecting the workflow engine with your world

scenario: Forge Bank runs Camunda 8.8 workers that handle 9000 jobs per day for a retail bank. After a noisy release the on-call team needs to remember which three completion calls a Camunda 8 worker can send back to the workflow engine when its glue code finishes a service task, so they can wire the right code path for each outcome.
question: Which three calls can a Camunda 8 worker send back to the workflow engine after finishing a service task?

options.text: PublishJobResult to write the worker outcome to a Kafka topic; FailJob with negative retries; ThrowError with a Java stack trace serialized as the BPMN error payload to the engine

PauseJob to put the instance on hold while a human reviews the case; ResumeJob to continue processing once the review is done; CancelJob to terminate the entire process instance with a single completion call
CompleteJob to advance the process; FailJob which retries while retries are positive and raises an incident when retries are zero or below; ThrowError to report a BPMN error handled on the BPMN level

AcknowledgeJob to confirm the receipt of the job; ExtendJob to ask the engine for more retry attempts; RejectJob to mark the service task as permanently failed without further intervention from any operator

d.text: Incorrect. There is no AcknowledgeJob, ExtendJob, or RejectJob call documented; the three calls are CompleteJob, FailJob, and ThrowError.

b.text: Incorrect. There is no PauseJob, ResumeJob, or CancelJob call from the worker; the documented three are CompleteJob, FailJob, and ThrowError.

a.text: Incorrect. There is no PublishJobResult call, and ThrowError carries a BPMN errorCode rather than a Java stack trace.

c.text: Correct. The page lists exactly three completion options for the worker: CompleteJob to advance the instance, FailJob (retries decremented; incident raised when remaining retries reach zero or below), and ThrowError to raise a BPMN error handled on the BPMN level.

explanation: Whenever a worker finishes a service task, it sends one of CompleteJob, FailJob, or ThrowError back to Zeebe. FailJob is retried while retries are positive and raises an incident when retries are zero or below. ThrowError reports a BPMN error so the process model can react to it.

scenario: Sable Bank runs Camunda 8.8 workers that retrieve money for 7500 transactions per day inside a regional bank. A developer authors a retrieveMoney worker with the taskDefinition retries attribute set to 5 in BPMN, and now must implement the catch block correctly so the engine keeps retrying until the configured retry budget is exhausted.
question: Which call must the worker make on a caught exception so that Zeebe leverages the built-in retry mechanism?

options.text: Call newThrowErrorCommand with errorCode RETRY so the BPMN error mechanism reschedules the service task automatically and increments the remaining retries counter on the job for the next attempt

Call newCompleteCommand with a process variable retryRequested set to true so the workflow engine schedules the next attempt against the original taskDefinition retries budget configured in the BPMN model
Call newUpdateJobRetriesCommand with retries set back to the original BPMN value of 5 every time the catch block runs so the engine never raises an incident on this particular service task

Call newFailCommand with retries set to job.getRetries() minus one and a meaningful errorMessage; once the remaining retries reach zero, Zeebe raises an incident that Operate surfaces to a human operator

d.text: Correct. The retry snippet calls newFailCommand, decrements retries with job.getRetries() minus one, and provides a meaningful errorMessage; once remaining retries reach zero, Zeebe raises an incident that Operate exposes to a human operator.

b.text: Incorrect. CompleteCommand advances the instance; it does not trigger a retry, and there is no documented retryRequested variable convention.

a.text: Incorrect. ThrowError reports a BPMN error to the process model; it is not the retry mechanism and does not increment the retry counter.

c.text: Incorrect. Restoring retries to 5 inside the catch block would defeat the retry budget; the documented snippet decrements retries instead.

explanation: The retries attribute on the taskDefinition sets the initial retry budget. Inside the catch block, the worker uses newFailCommand, decrements retries by one with job.getRetries() minus one, and writes a meaningful errorMessage. When the remaining retries reach zero, an incident is raised and surfaced in Operate for human resolution.

scenario: Harbor Insurance ships a Camunda 8.8 claims process for a regional insurer that runs 2800 instances per day. The lead developer is reviewing the model and notices that an item-unavailable BPMN error is thrown by a service task but no boundary error event catches it anywhere in the process; the operations team asks what happens to the running instance.
question: Which behavior does the BPMN spec define for an unhandled BPMN error inside a Camunda 8 process instance?

options.text: An unhandled BPMN error raises a technical incident in Operate so a human operator can decide whether to retry the activity, terminate the instance, or move the token to the BPMN error end event manually

An unhandled BPMN error terminates the process instance silently rather than raising an incident, so a parent scope should always catch the error with a boundary event when the deviation must be handled inside the model
An unhandled BPMN error is converted into a FailJob with retries set to zero, which raises an incident on the originating service task instead of terminating the process instance prematurely

An unhandled BPMN error pauses the instance for 24 hours waiting for a matching subscription to appear via a deployment, and only terminates the instance after that grace period has elapsed without a handler

b.text: Correct. The page explicitly states that according to the BPMN spec a BPMN error is either handled in the process or terminates the process instance, and it does not lead to an incident being raised.

a.text: Incorrect. An unhandled BPMN error does not raise an incident; the docs state this explicitly.

c.text: Incorrect. An unhandled BPMN error is not converted into a FailJob; the page says it terminates the instance instead.

d.text: Incorrect. There is no documented 24-hour grace period for BPMN errors; an unhandled error terminates the instance.

explanation: Per the BPMN spec, a BPMN error is either handled inside the process model or it terminates the process instance. The Camunda docs explicitly call out that an unhandled BPMN error does not raise an incident, which is why a boundary error event on a parent scope is usually the safety net when the deviation must be handled in the process.

scenario: Vanta Bank runs a Camunda 8.8 onboarding process at a regional bank that handles 4500 customers per day. The CRM-add-customer step can succeed while the billing-add-customer step later fails inside the same business transaction; the CRM API does not support delete and an architect needs the documented BPMN technique that undoes the CRM creation in this case.
question: Which BPMN mechanism does the best-practices guide recommend to implement the Saga pattern for the add-customer step?

options.text: Link the add-customer task with a compensation handler that deactivates the customer in the CRM, and trigger compensation from the error path; all completed activities in scope will run their compensating activities

Replace the BPMN model with a long-running scheduled batch job that runs every 6 hours and queries the CRM and billing systems for divergences before issuing corrective REST calls to either side as needed

Reissue the original add-customer command from a job worker that listens to a Kafka topic and let downstream consumers cancel the original CRM creation when they observe a duplicate event in the partition log

Wrap the add-customer task in an event subprocess with a timer start event so the engine repeatedly reissues the call until the billing step finally succeeds and the inconsistency window between systems closes by itself

d.text: Incorrect. The Saga pattern undoes side effects rather than retrying them; the page uses BPMN compensation events, not a timer-driven retry loop.

c.text: Incorrect. Kafka cancellation logic is not the documented Saga technique; the docs use BPMN compensation events.

a.text: Correct. The Saga pattern section says Camunda supports Saga through BPMN compensation events, which link a task with its undo task; when compensation is triggered on the error path, completed activities run their compensating activities, e.g. deactivating the customer in the CRM.

b.text: Incorrect. A batch reconciliation job is one of the strategies under resolve, but it is not the documented Saga implementation; the page uses BPMN compensation events.

explanation: The Saga pattern and BPMN compensation section describes the documented pattern. A BPMN compensation event links the add-customer task with its compensating activity. When an error is caught further in the business transaction, the process triggers compensation; the engine runs the compensating activities of all completed tasks in scope, for example deactivating the customer in the CRM when the API does not support deletion.

scenario: Nimbus Logistics runs Camunda 8.8 workers that process 8000 shipment jobs per day for a carrier integration. A worker finishes a REST call to confirm a shipment without any failure and must notify the workflow engine that the service task succeeded so the process instance moves on to the next activity in the model.
question: Which Zeebe gateway API does the worker invoke to report that the shipment service task completed successfully in Camunda 8.8?

options.text: The worker invokes FailJob with the remaining retries set to a positive number so the workflow engine re-dispatches the job to another competing consumer and the process instance advances to the next activity
The worker invokes ResolveIncident on the job so the workflow engine clears any pending incident on the service task and the process instance moves past it to the next activity automatically

The worker invokes ThrowError with a BPMN error code so the workflow engine routes the process instance through a matching boundary error event and continues execution along the success path of the model
The worker invokes CompleteJob so the workflow engine marks the service task as finished and the process instance moves on to the next activity defined in the BPMN model

a.text: Incorrect. FailJob signals a failure to the workflow engine; with positive remaining retries the job is retried by another worker, but the service task is not marked complete and the process instance does not advance.

c.text: Incorrect. ThrowError raises a BPMN error that is handled on the model level via a matching error event; it does not signal a successful completion of the service task.

b.text: Incorrect. There is no ResolveIncident reply from a worker on a happy-path completion; incidents are raised when the retry count reaches zero and are resolved by an operator in Operate, not by the worker on success.

d.text: Correct. CompleteJob is the call the worker sends back to the workflow engine to report that the service task went well, after which the process instance can move on to the next activity in the BPMN model.

explanation: When a worker has finished its work successfully, it calls CompleteJob so the workflow engine marks the service task as done and the process instance advances; FailJob signals a failure with retry semantics, and ThrowError reports a BPMN error to be handled on the model level.

scenario: Apex Bank processes 12000 jobs per day on Camunda 8.8 for the retrieveMoney service task against a flaky downstream payments service. The team configured the service task with zeebeTaskDefinition retries set to 5 so transient downstream failures do not raise incidents immediately, and the worker must drive the built-in retry mechanism correctly on every caught exception.
question: How does the Camunda 8.8 worker correctly drive the built-in retry mechanism when it catches an exception while handling a retrieveMoney job?

options.text: The worker calls newFailCommand on the job with retries set to job.getRetries() plus one so the workflow engine keeps the retry count steady across attempts and never raises an incident for this task type at runtime

The worker calls newCompleteCommand on the job with a meaningful error message attached so the workflow engine treats the attempt as a failed retry and re-dispatches the job to another competing consumer in the pool

The worker calls newFailCommand on the job with retries set to job.getRetries() minus one and provides a meaningful error message so the workflow engine decrements the count and raises an incident once the count reaches zero

The worker calls newThrowErrorCommand on the job with a BPMN error code so the workflow engine reduces the retry count automatically and triggers a matching boundary error event after the final retry attempt

c.text: Correct. The documented Java pattern catches the exception, then calls newFailCommand on the job with retries set to job.getRetries() minus one and a meaningful error message so the workflow engine decrements the count and raises an incident in Operate once the remaining retries reach zero.

b.text: Incorrect. CompleteJob signals success and clears the job from the queue; it does not decrement the retry counter and it does not trigger another attempt.

d.text: Incorrect. ThrowError raises a BPMN error for handling on the process model, but it does not interact with the technical retry counter that drives the FailJob retry behavior.

a.text: Incorrect. The retry counter must be decremented by the worker, not incremented; raising it would create an unbounded retry loop and bypass the incident mechanism entirely.

explanation: To leverage the built-in retry mechanism, the Camunda 8.8 worker catches the exception and calls newFailCommand on the job with retries set to job.getRetries() minus one plus a meaningful error message. When the remaining retries reach zero, an incident is raised in Operate for human intervention.

scenario: Tessera Insurance integrates a Camunda 8.8 onboarding process with a CRM system and a billing system for 7000 customers per day. The team wants 'all or nothing' semantics across both downstream systems and asks which Camunda 8.8 best-practice construct represents that consistency boundary in the process model.
question: Which Camunda 8.8 best-practice construct provides 'all or nothing' semantics across the CRM and billing services in this onboarding process?

options.text: A business transaction marks a section of the process for which 'all or nothing' semantics apply from a business perspective, even though intermediate inconsistent states between CRM and billing may be observed during execution

A single technical ACID transaction spans the workflow engine, the CRM database, and the billing database so the three commit together at the gateway after both service tasks complete in the process instance

The CRM system and the billing system share one local ACID transaction managed by the workflow engine and intermediate inconsistent states between them cannot occur during the onboarding orchestration at runtime

The workflow engine elevates its own local transaction to a two-phase commit coordinator at runtime so the CRM system and the billing system join one global ACID transaction across the cluster for every onboarding

b.text: Incorrect. ACID transactions cannot be applied across distributed systems; the CRM, the billing system, and the workflow engine each run their own local transactions and there is no joined technical transaction across them.

d.text: Incorrect. The workflow engine does not act as a global two-phase commit coordinator; the guide states there cannot be a joined technical transaction across the engine, the CRM, and the billing system.

c.text: Incorrect. The CRM and the billing system run separate ACID transactions; intermediate inconsistent states (such as a new customer being present in CRM but not yet in billing) can and do occur in between steps.

a.text: Correct. A business transaction marks a section of the process for which 'all or nothing' semantics apply from a business perspective; intermediate inconsistent states between distributed systems may still be observed in between the participating steps.

explanation: Camunda 8.8 cannot stretch a technical ACID transaction across distributed services; the engine, CRM, and billing each commit their own local transactions. The best-practice answer is a business transaction: a process section with 'all or nothing' semantics at the business level, accepting intermediate inconsistencies in between steps.

scenario: Nimbus fintech is rebuilding a payments service on Spring Boot for Camunda 8.8 that consumes 12000 jobs per day. The team must pick the official integration library and align with the documented migration path away from the legacy SDK before any later upgrade.
question: Which integration library is the official Spring Boot path for Camunda 8.8?

options.text: The Spring Zeebe SDK remains the supported Spring Boot integration for Camunda 8.8 and is scheduled to keep that role across the entire 8.x release series
The Camunda REST connector is the official Spring Boot integration for Camunda 8.8 and replaces the Spring Zeebe SDK as part of the 8.8 release line
The Camunda Java Client is the official Spring Boot integration for Camunda 8.8 and developers add it directly without any Spring Boot starter dependency
The Camunda Spring Boot Starter is the official Camunda 8.8 integration and replaces the Spring Zeebe SDK as of version 8.8 with REST as the default protocol

a.text: Incorrect. The Spring Zeebe SDK is being phased out and will be removed in version 8.10; teams must migrate to the Camunda Spring Boot Starter before upgrading past 8.8.

d.text: Correct. The Camunda Spring Boot Starter is the official Camunda 8.8 integration; it replaces the Spring Zeebe SDK as of 8.8, uses the new Camunda Java Client under the hood, and defaults to REST with gRPC configurable.

c.text: Incorrect. The Camunda Java Client powers the starter under the hood but is not the Spring Boot integration itself; Spring Boot projects add the Camunda Spring Boot Starter.

b.text: Incorrect. There is no REST connector that replaces the SDK; the official replacement for the Spring Zeebe SDK as of 8.8 is the Camunda Spring Boot Starter.

explanation: As of Camunda 8.8 the official Spring Boot integration is the Camunda Spring Boot Starter, which replaces the Spring Zeebe SDK. It uses the Camunda Java Client under the hood and defaults to REST with gRPC available as a configurable protocol. The legacy Spring Zeebe SDK will be removed in version 8.10, so teams must migrate before then.

docs.title: Camunda Spring Boot Starter getting started

scenario: Helios SaaS runs a tenant provisioning service on Camunda 8.8 that handles 25000 requests per day. The platform team is upgrading the underlying framework to Spring Boot 4.0 to align with a wider company initiative and must pick the supported Camunda Spring Boot Starter artifact for that target.
question: Which Camunda Spring Boot Starter artifact provides Spring Boot 4.0 support on Camunda 8.8?

options.text: Use camunda-spring-boot-4-starter from version 8.8.9 which is the dedicated artifact that bundles Spring Boot 4.0.x on JDK 17 or higher
Use camunda-spring-boot-3-starter from version 8.8.15 which is the dedicated artifact added for Spring Boot 4.0 support across all 8.8 patches
Use camunda-spring-boot-starter on version 8.8.x which bundles Spring Boot 3.5.x and already covers Spring Boot 4.0 on the same JDK 17 baseline
Use camunda-spring-boot-4-starter on version 8.8.x which is the dedicated artifact that bundles Spring Boot 3.5.x on JDK 17 or higher

c.text: Incorrect. The default camunda-spring-boot-starter on 8.8.x stays on Spring Boot 3.5.x for all 8.8 patch releases; Spring Boot 4.0 support is provided by a separate artifact.

b.text: Incorrect. camunda-spring-boot-3-starter is an alias for the default starter that keeps Spring Boot 3 when upgrading to 8.9 or newer; it is not the Spring Boot 4.0 artifact.

a.text: Correct. Starting with the Camunda 8.8.9 patch release a dedicated starter artifact, camunda-spring-boot-4-starter, was added that bundles Spring Boot 4.0.x and requires JDK 17 or higher.

d.text: Incorrect. camunda-spring-boot-4-starter bundles Spring Boot 4.0.x, not 3.5.x, and is available from the 8.8.9 patch onward as a drop-in replacement.

explanation: On Camunda 8.8 the Spring Boot 4.0 path is camunda-spring-boot-4-starter, introduced with the 8.8.9 patch release and bundling Spring Boot 4.0.x on JDK 17 or higher. The default camunda-spring-boot-starter remains on Spring Boot 3.5.x across all 8.8 patches.

docs.title: Camunda Spring Boot Starter version compatibility

scenario: Zenith Bank runs a Spring Boot fleet processing 9500 transactions per day on Camunda 8.8.14. The architecture team plans to upgrade to Spring Boot 4.0 in 6 months and must select the dedicated Camunda Spring Boot Starter artifact that ships Spring Boot 4.0 support while remaining on the 8.8 release line.
question: Which Camunda 8.8 Starter artifact does the team add to obtain Spring Boot 4.0 support on the 8.8 release line?

options.text: Use the camunda-spring-boot-starter artifact at version 8.8.x, which is upgraded in-place to Spring Boot 4.0 starting with the 8.8.9 patch release and continues to run on JDK 11 or higher
Use the camunda-spring-boot-3-starter artifact at version 8.8.x, which is the only artifact that bundles Spring Boot 4.0 on the 8.8 line and remains the default for 8.9 and later releases
Use the camunda-spring-zeebe-4-starter artifact at version 8.8.x, which is the legacy artifact from the Spring Zeebe SDK era relabelled to provide Spring Boot 4.0 support on Camunda 8.8
Use the camunda-spring-boot-4-starter artifact from the 8.8.9 patch release, which is a drop-in replacement for the default Starter that bundles Spring Boot 4.0.x and requires JDK 17 or newer

a.text: Incorrect. The default camunda-spring-boot-starter stays on Spring Boot 3.5.x for all 8.8 patch releases, and the JDK requirement across the 8.8 table is JDK 17 or higher rather than JDK 11.

b.text: Incorrect. camunda-spring-boot-3-starter is an alias for the default Starter on Spring Boot 3.5.x; the artifact that ships Spring Boot 4.0 on the 8.8 line is camunda-spring-boot-4-starter.

c.text: Incorrect. There is no camunda-spring-zeebe-4-starter artifact; the documented Spring Boot 4.0 artifact is camunda-spring-boot-4-starter, available from the 8.8.9 patch release and requiring JDK 17 or higher.

d.text: Correct. The version-compatibility table introduces camunda-spring-boot-4-starter from the 8.8.9 patch release as a drop-in replacement for the default Starter, bundled with Spring Boot 4.0.x and requiring JDK 17 or newer.

explanation: The version-compatibility table on the 8.8 line lists three artifacts: camunda-spring-boot-starter on Spring Boot 3.5.x, camunda-spring-boot-3-starter from 8.8.15 as its alias, and camunda-spring-boot-4-starter from 8.8.9 onwards bundled with Spring Boot 4.0.x. All three artifacts require JDK 17 or higher.

docs.title: Camunda Spring Boot Starter version compatibility

scenario: Granite Bank runs a self-managed Camunda 8.8 cluster behind an HTTP basic auth proxy and handles 4200 jobs per hour.
