A new Spring Boot worker must be wired with the Camunda Spring Boot Starter so it can authenticate against the self-managed gRPC and REST endpoints using a fixed service-account username and password issued by the platform team for that environment.
question: Which application.yaml entries enable HTTP basic authentication for the Camunda Spring Boot Starter against a self-managed Camunda 8.8 cluster?

options.text: Set camunda.client.mode to self-managed with camunda.client.auth.method set to oidc and supply client-id and client-secret as the basic credentials, along with grpc-address and rest-address for the cluster
Set camunda.client.mode to saas with camunda.client.auth.method set to basic and supply username and password under camunda.client.auth, along with grpc-address and rest-address for the cluster
Set camunda.client.mode to self-managed with camunda.client.auth.method set to none and supply a base64 token in camunda.client.auth.basic-token, along with grpc-address and rest-address values

Set camunda.client.mode to self-managed with camunda.client.auth.method set to basic and supply username and password under camunda.client.auth, along with grpc-address and rest-address for the cluster

d.text: Correct. The basic-authentication block configures camunda.client.mode as self-managed with auth.method set to basic, supplies username and password under camunda.client.auth, and points grpc-address and rest-address at the self-managed cluster as absolute URIs.

b.text: Incorrect. SaaS mode in the docs uses OIDC client credentials with client-id, client-secret, and token-url; it does not document a username and password configuration.

c.text: Incorrect. There is no basic-token field for the Starter; basic authentication is configured with method set to basic plus literal username and password values under camunda.client.auth.

a.text: Incorrect. Auth method oidc is documented for OIDC client credentials and adds client-id, client-secret, token-url, audience, and scope; HTTP basic auth uses method basic with username and password.

explanation: The basic-authentication tab in the Camunda Spring Boot Starter getting-started guide sets camunda.client.mode to self-managed, camunda.client.auth.method to basic, and supplies username and password under camunda.client.auth alongside absolute gRPC and REST addresses for the self-managed cluster.

docs.title: Get started with the Camunda Spring Boot Starter

scenario: Catalyst Marketplace ships a Camunda 8.8 order approval process that handles 18000 orders per day. Before standardising the variable strategy across teams, the architects review how Camunda 8 stores process variable values, where variables are declared, and how BPMN data objects relate to process variables in the running engine.
question: Which statement matches the Camunda 8.8 data-handling guidance for process variables and BPMN data objects?

options.text: Process variables are declared in element templates, values are stored as binary protobuf payloads on each token, and BPMN data objects are persisted as the single source of truth for the process instance

Process variables are not declared in the process model, values are stored as JSON in Camunda 8, and BPMN data objects are not treated as process variables and are recommended only as occasional documentation

Process variables are declared as Java fields on a Process Data Accessor at deployment time, values are stored as Avro records on the broker, and BPMN data objects shadow variables for gateway decisions

Process variables must be declared inside the BPMN process model with typed names, values are stored as XML in the engine database, and BPMN data objects are treated as the primary process variable container

d.text: Incorrect. Variables are not declared in the model and values are stored as JSON, not XML; BPMN data objects are explicitly not treated as process variables by Camunda 8.

a.text: Incorrect. The docs state values are stored as JSON, variables are not declared in the model, and BPMN data objects are recommended only for documentation rather than as the persisted source of truth.

c.text: Incorrect. A Process Data Accessor is an optional helper class used in client code for type-safe access; it does not declare variables on the engine, which stores values as JSON.

b.text: Correct. The Understanding-data-handling section states that variables are not declared in the process model, that values are stored as JSON in Camunda 8, and that BPMN data objects are not treated as process variables and should only be used occasionally for documentation.

explanation: Camunda 8 stores process variable values as JSON, does not require variables to be declared in the process model (allowing for flexibility), and treats BPMN data objects only as documentation rather than as process variable containers used by the engine at runtime.

scenario: Aurora Marketplace runs a Camunda 8.8 Java job worker with maxJobsActive set to 10 to process 6000 orders per day. The platform team wants to predict when the worker triggers its next poll for additional jobs while it is already actively handling activated jobs in the in flight buffer.
question: When does a Camunda 8.8 Java job worker poll for additional jobs while it is handling activated jobs from a previous batch?

options.text: The worker polls for additional jobs only after every activated job from the previous batch finishes so the broker at Aurora Marketplace sees only one in flight activation at a time
The worker polls for additional jobs after every single handled job and ignores the maxJobsActive threshold so the local buffer at Aurora Marketplace always stays completely full of jobs
The worker polls for additional jobs the first time the count of unhandled jobs drops below 30 percent of maxJobsActive rounded up while it is handling activated jobs from the current batch
The worker polls for additional jobs only at fixed pollInterval ticks regardless of how many jobs remain unhandled in the local buffer that the Aurora Marketplace client maintains

b.text: Incorrect. The worker does not poll after every single handled job; it polls when unhandled jobs first drop below 30 percent of maxJobsActive rounded up.

a.text: Incorrect. The worker does not wait for the entire previous batch to finish; it polls earlier, the first time unhandled jobs fall below 30 percent of maxJobsActive.

d.text: Incorrect. The pollInterval only governs polls when the previous poll activated no jobs or the buffer is fully drained; an active worker polls at the 30 percent threshold.

c.text: Correct. Every time a job is handled the worker checks whether unhandled jobs have dropped below 30 percent of maxJobsActive rounded up; the first time that happens it polls for more jobs.

explanation: While a Camunda 8.8 Java job worker is handling activated jobs, it checks after each completion whether the count of unhandled jobs has dropped below 30 percent of maxJobsActive rounded up, and the first time it has, it polls for more jobs.

scenario: Aurora Bank tracks job polling rates on a Camunda 8.8 Java worker that activates 6500 jobs per day. The platform engineering team wants the ActivateJobsCommand polling count exported to Prometheus today without waiting for a built in counter, and prefers an approach that does not change the broker configuration.
question: Which Camunda 8.8 documented workaround lets Aurora Bank track the ActivateJobsCommand polling count from a Java job worker?

options.text: Register a gRPC ClientInterceptor or an Apache HttpClient AsyncExecChainHandler on the Camunda client builder so the interceptor counts every ActivateJobsCommand invocation issued by the Aurora Bank Java worker on its client channel

Enable a camunda.client.metrics.activateJobs property on the JobWorkerBuilder so the Java client publishes a built in polling counter directly to the configured Micrometer MeterRegistry at Aurora Bank without any interceptors

Subclass the BackoffSupplier and increment a Micrometer Counter inside its method so the Aurora Bank Java worker treats every retry delay calculation as a single ActivateJobsCommand polling event for the metric

Set zeebe.broker.metrics.activateJobs to true in the broker configuration so Zeebe exports the polling rate of every connected Java worker through the orchestration cluster monitoring endpoint used by Aurora Bank

a.text: Correct. The documented workaround uses a gRPC ClientInterceptor or Apache HttpClient AsyncExecChainHandler attached to the Camunda client builder to track client calls, including the ActivateJobsCommand invocation issued each time the worker polls for jobs.

b.text: Incorrect. The Java client does not expose a built in activate jobs polling counter on the JobWorkerBuilder; the documented option is the gRPC ClientInterceptor or HttpClient chain handler workaround.

d.text: Incorrect. The broker does not expose a per client polling rate metric via a zeebe.broker.metrics.activateJobs flag; tracking is done on the client side through an interceptor or chain handler.

c.text: Incorrect. The BackoffSupplier only computes retry delays after failed polls and does not run on every poll, so it cannot be used as a proxy for the ActivateJobsCommand polling count.

explanation: Camunda 8.8 deliberately limits the built in JobWorkerMetrics to jobs activated and jobs handled. To track additional metrics like the ActivateJobsCommand polling count, the docs recommend attaching a gRPC ClientInterceptor or an Apache HttpClient AsyncExecChainHandler to the client builder.

scenario: Helios Logistics processes 24000 jobs per hour through a Java worker pool in Camunda 8.8 and the platform team wants to remove the polling round trip to cut activation latency for shipment dispatch. The architect must change exactly one builder option so the worker opens a long-living gRPC stream to the gateway and has activated jobs pushed downstream without an extra poll request.
question: Which builder configuration enables job streaming on a Java job worker in Camunda 8.8?

options.text: Call streamEnabled(true) on the JobWorkerBuilderStep3 so the worker opens a long-living stream to a gateway and activated jobs are pushed without an extra poll round trip
Call maxJobsActive(0) on the JobWorkerBuilderStep3 so the broker pushes every newly created job directly into the worker without buffering inside the gateway during activation
Call streamTimeout(Duration.ZERO) on the JobWorkerBuilderStep3 so the worker keeps a permanent stream open and activated jobs are pushed without any polling round trip at all
Call pollInterval(Duration.ZERO) on the JobWorkerBuilderStep3 so the worker polls the gateway continuously and activated jobs arrive without any waiting period between requests

a.text: Correct. Camunda 8.8 documents enabling job streaming as toggling streamEnabled(true) on JobWorkerBuilderStep3; this opens a long-living stream between the worker and a gateway through which activated jobs are pushed, removing the poll round trip.

d.text: Incorrect. The Java job worker builder does not have a pollInterval setting that enables streaming; the documented switch is streamEnabled(true), and zero-duration polling would only intensify polling.

b.text: Incorrect. The maxJobsActive parameter bounds how many jobs a worker handles concurrently for backpressure and does not enable streaming; the documented streaming flag is streamEnabled(true) on the builder.

c.text: Incorrect. streamTimeout sets when an underlying long-living stream is refreshed for load balancing, not whether streaming is enabled; the documented switch to enable streaming is streamEnabled(true).

explanation: Camunda 8.8 documents that enabling job streaming consists of toggling a single flag, streamEnabled(true), on the JobWorkerBuilderStep3. The worker then opens a long-living gRPC stream to a gateway through which activated jobs are pushed, eliminating the activation poll round trip.

scenario: Lattice Insurance operates Java job workers for 12 tenants across 3 environments in Camunda 8.8 and processes 8000 claims per day. The platform team wants every newly opened worker on a shared client to default to the same set of tenant identifiers without repeating the tenantIds builder call in 40 worker classes, and the client is already authorized for all 12 tenants.
question: Which environment variable configures default tenant ids for Camunda 8.8 Java job workers?

options.text: ZEEBE_DEFAULT_JOB_WORKER_TENANT_IDS sets the default tenant identifiers for every job worker opened by a Camunda 8.8 Java client, across all tenants the client is authorized for
CAMUNDA_DEFAULT_JOB_WORKER_TENANT_IDS sets the default tenant identifiers for every job worker opened by a Camunda 8.8 Java client, across all tenants the client is authorized for
CAMUNDA_CLIENT_WORKER_TENANT_IDS sets the default tenant identifiers for every job worker opened by a Camunda 8.8 Java client, across all tenants the client is authorized for
CAMUNDA_TENANT_DEFAULT_JOB_WORKER_IDS sets the default tenant identifiers for every job worker opened by a Camunda 8.8 Java client, across all tenants the client is authorized for

c.text: Incorrect. CAMUNDA_CLIENT_WORKER_TENANT_IDS is not the documented environment variable; the Java client reads default worker tenants from CAMUNDA_DEFAULT_JOB_WORKER_TENANT_IDS, with the equivalent system property camunda.client.worker.tenantIds.

b.text: Correct. Camunda 8.8 documents CAMUNDA_DEFAULT_JOB_WORKER_TENANT_IDS as the environment variable that sets default tenants for every worker opened on the client; the equivalent system property is camunda.client.worker.tenantIds, and the client must be authorized for all listed tenants.

a.text: Incorrect. The Camunda 8.8 unified client documents the CAMUNDA_ prefix rather than the legacy ZEEBE_ prefix for default tenants; the documented variable is CAMUNDA_DEFAULT_JOB_WORKER_TENANT_IDS.

d.text: Incorrect. The token order CAMUNDA_TENANT_DEFAULT_JOB_WORKER_IDS does not match the documented Camunda 8.8 variable; the correct form is CAMUNDA_DEFAULT_JOB_WORKER_TENANT_IDS.

explanation: Default tenants for Java job workers in Camunda 8.8 are configured via the environment variable CAMUNDA_DEFAULT_JOB_WORKER_TENANT_IDS or the system property camunda.client.worker.tenantIds. The client must be authorized for every tenant in the list, otherwise the worker will not pick up any jobs.

scenario: A payments fintech runs a Camunda 8.8 Java job worker that processes 5400 jobs per day with maxJobsActive set to 10 and a pollInterval of 100 ms. The lead engineer needs to predict when the worker fetches more jobs from the cluster while jobs from the previous activation batch are still being handled in memory by the worker's executor.
question: When does a Camunda 8.8 Java job worker poll the cluster for more jobs after it has already activated a batch under maxJobsActive set to 10?

options.text: Once half of the originally activated jobs have completed (which is 5 of the original 10), the worker triggers an additional poll for more jobs from the cluster regardless of the configured pollInterval

Once the configured pollInterval has elapsed since the previous batch was activated, the worker unconditionally fetches up to maxJobsActive additional jobs from the cluster even while jobs are still queued
Once the count of unhandled buffered jobs drops below 30 percent of maxJobsActive rounded up (which is 3 of the original 10), the worker triggers an additional poll for more jobs from the cluster
Once any single job in the batch has been completed, the worker immediately polls for one replacement job and refills the local buffer back up to maxJobsActive on every individual completion event

c.text: Correct. After each job is handled the worker checks whether the number of unhandled jobs has dropped below 30 percent of maxJobsActive rounded up; the first time the threshold is reached the worker polls again for more jobs from the cluster.

a.text: Incorrect. The documented threshold is 30 percent of maxJobsActive rounded up, not half of the activated batch; in the example with maxJobsActive 10 the threshold is 3.

b.text: Incorrect. pollInterval governs polling when the previous poll activated zero jobs; refills during active processing are driven by the 30 percent buffered-job threshold instead.

d.text: Incorrect. The worker does not poll after every single completion; it waits until unhandled buffered jobs fall below the 30 percent threshold of maxJobsActive before polling again.

explanation: The How-job-workers-work section explains that on open the worker waits pollInterval milliseconds then polls for maxJobsActive jobs. While handling jobs it re-polls only when unhandled buffered jobs drop below 30 percent of maxJobsActive rounded up. If a poll yields no jobs it waits pollInterval ms and tries again; on error responses a backoff is applied.

docs.title: How Java client job workers work

scenario: An airline running Camunda 8.8 handles 7500 jobs per hour through a fleet of Java job workers. During a backpressure event the cluster returns GRPC_STATUS_RESOURCE_EXHAUSTED on activation requests, and the platform team needs to confirm how the workers react out of the box and which builder method customises that reaction for their workload.
question: How does a Camunda 8.8 Java job worker react to GRPC_STATUS_RESOURCE_EXHAUSTED on activation, and which builder method overrides that reaction?

options.text: The worker drops the activation attempt and raises a local exception immediately to the caller; the reaction is overridden by calling .errorHandler(new MyErrorHandler()) on the JobWorkerBuilder before opening the worker

The worker reduces maxJobsActive by one each time the error is observed; the recovery slope is overridden by calling .activeJobsStep(1) on the JobWorkerBuilder so the worker can ramp back up to its original capacity

The worker tears down the active session and reconnects with new TLS credentials; the reconnect behaviour is overridden by calling .reconnectStrategy(strategy) on the JobWorkerBuilder before opening the worker for jobs

The worker waits for a backoff delay before polling again, by default an exponential strategy from BackoffSupplier; the strategy is replaced by calling .backoffSupplier(supplier) on the JobWorkerBuilder before opening the worker

a.text: Incorrect. The worker does not drop the job and raise an exception; it waits for a backoff delay and polls again, and the documented builder method is .backoffSupplier.

d.text: Correct. On a polling error such as GRPC_STATUS_RESOURCE_EXHAUSTED the worker applies a backoff before the next poll, by default an exponential strategy from BackoffSupplier.newBackoffBuilder(), and the strategy is replaced through .backoffSupplier(supplier) on the JobWorkerBuilder.

b.text: Incorrect. The Java client does not automatically shrink maxJobsActive on backpressure; backoff between polls is configured via .backoffSupplier on the JobWorkerBuilder.

c.text: Incorrect. There is no .reconnectStrategy method on the worker builder; backoff after polling errors is configured via .backoffSupplier on the JobWorkerBuilder.

explanation: The Backoff-configuration section describes that on a polling error the worker waits a delay computed by a BackoffSupplier before polling again. The default is an exponential strategy from BackoffSupplier.newBackoffBuilder(), and a custom strategy is installed via .backoffSupplier(...) on JobWorkerBuilder. This is especially useful for GRPC_STATUS_RESOURCE_EXHAUSTED responses caused by Zeebe backpressure.

docs.title: Java client job worker backoff configuration

scenario: A telco running Camunda 8.8 with 3200 jobs per hour wants to know how many jobs each of its Java workers has activated and handled at any moment. The platform team adds the standard JobWorkerMetrics callback through the Java client without enabling anything else in the worker builder beyond the metrics implementation itself.
question: What does the Camunda 8.8 Java client track in JobWorkerMetrics by default before any custom implementation is registered on the worker?

options.text: The Java client tracks polling counts and gRPC round-trip latency by default and exposes them through the JobWorkerMetrics interface to OpenTelemetry exporters at application start-up automatically

The Java client tracks both the number of jobs activated and the number of jobs handled and exposes them automatically through a built-in Micrometer Prometheus registry without any further configuration on the worker

The Java client tracks the buffered job count by default and surfaces it through the JobWorkerMetrics interface so that backpressure decisions can be made without further configuration in the worker builder
The Java client tracks nothing by default; JobWorkerMetrics is a callback interface and the caller must register an implementation on the worker builder before any counter is incremented at runtime

b.text: Incorrect. The docs note that by default job workers do not track any metrics; built-in Micrometer support is optional and must be wired up explicitly by the caller through the worker builder.

a.text: Incorrect. The default behaviour is no tracking, and the available counters are jobs-activated and jobs-handled rather than polling counts or round-trip latency.

d.text: Correct. The Metrics section states that by default job workers track no metrics; JobWorkerMetrics is a callback interface and the caller must register an implementation on the JobWorkerBuilder for the counters to be updated at runtime.

c.text: Incorrect. The two callbacks exposed are jobActivated(int) and jobHandled(int); the buffered-job count is derived by subtracting the two counters in user code rather than tracked directly.

explanation: The Metrics section describes JobWorkerMetrics as a callback interface with two methods, jobActivated(int) and jobHandled(int). By default the worker does not track any metrics. The caller registers an implementation (for example the optional built-in Micrometer one) via .metrics(...) on the worker builder for the counters to be updated.

scenario: A SaaS provider running Camunda 8.8 with 21000 jobs per day wires Micrometer into its Java workers using the optional bundled JobWorkerMetrics implementation. The observability team must update its Prometheus dashboards to read the new metric names introduced for the Java client and to phase out the legacy zeebe-prefixed counters before upgrading.
question: Which two new metric names does the Camunda 8.8 Java client's Micrometer-backed JobWorkerMetrics implementation expose?

options.text: camunda.worker.jobs.activated.count and camunda.worker.jobs.handled.count, which sit under a camunda.worker namespace shared with the Spring Boot Starter and replace the older Zeebe names
zeebe.client.worker.job.activated and zeebe.client.worker.job.handled, which remain the recommended names through the entire 8.x release line and require no dashboard changes
io.camunda.client.worker.jobActivated and io.camunda.client.worker.jobHandled, which mirror the JobWorkerMetrics method names in a dotted notation for monitoring backends
camunda.client.worker.job.activated and camunda.client.worker.job.handled, which replace the legacy zeebe.client.worker.job names scheduled for removal in version 8.10 of Camunda

b.text: Incorrect. The zeebe.client.worker.job.* counters are the legacy names and the docs schedule their removal at version 8.10; teams should switch to the camunda.client.worker.job names.

a.text: Incorrect. The published metric names sit under camunda.client.worker.job, not camunda.worker.jobs, and use plain activated/handled suffixes rather than .count.

c.text: Incorrect. The published names use camunda.client.worker.job.activated and camunda.client.worker.job.handled; there are no io.camunda dotted-method variants in the metrics table.

d.text: Correct. The Available-metrics table lists camunda.client.worker.job.activated and camunda.client.worker.job.handled as the new metric names, replacing the legacy zeebe.client.worker.job.* counters scheduled for removal in version 8.10.

explanation: The Available-metrics section publishes two new Micrometer metric names: camunda.client.worker.job.activated counts jobs activated and camunda.client.worker.job.handled counts jobs handled. They replace the legacy zeebe.client.worker.job.activated and zeebe.client.worker.job.handled counters, which the docs schedule for removal in version 8.10.

docs.title: Java client job worker available metrics

scenario: A subscriber telco running Camunda 8.8 with 60000 jobs per day wants to instrument both how often workers poll the cluster and how busy the scheduled executor is. The lead engineer reviews the Workarounds-for-additional-metrics section for two specific extension points the Java client exposes for these counters beyond the standard JobWorkerMetrics callbacks.
question: Which two Java client extension points does the Workarounds-for-additional-metrics section recommend for tracking polling counts and executor utilisation?

options.text: Wrap the client in a JobWorkerProxy bean exposed as a Micrometer MeterBinder, which counts ActivateJobsCommand calls and binds the internal executor through reflective hooks at application start-up

Register a gRPC ClientInterceptor (or an Apache HttpClient AsyncExecChainHandler) on the client builder to count polling calls, and pass an instrumented ScheduledExecutorService through builder.jobWorkerExecutor for executor metrics
Register a Tasklist webhook for poll telemetry and configure the Spring Boot Starter's actuator endpoint to expose the underlying executor pool through Micrometer at /actuator/jobs for the worker
Override JobWorkerBuilder.pollListener() and JobWorkerBuilder.executorListener() on each worker to register Micrometer callbacks for poll counts and executor utilisation respectively at runtime

d.text: Incorrect. There is no pollListener or executorListener method on the worker builder; polling is tracked through a gRPC ClientInterceptor and executor metrics are tracked through builder.jobWorkerExecutor instead.

b.text: Correct. The Workarounds section shows a gRPC ClientInterceptor (or an Apache HttpClient AsyncExecChainHandler) registered on the client builder to track ActivateJobsCommand calls, and an instrumented ScheduledExecutorService passed to builder.jobWorkerExecutor for executor metrics.

c.text: Incorrect. Tasklist webhooks and Spring Boot actuator endpoints are not the documented hooks for additional metrics; the Java client uses gRPC interceptors and a custom executor instead.

a.text: Incorrect. The Java client does not ship a JobWorkerProxy bean or reflective executor binder; metrics workarounds use a gRPC interceptor on the client builder and a custom executor through jobWorkerExecutor.

explanation: The Workarounds-for-additional-metrics section lists two extension points. Job-poll counts are tracked by registering a gRPC ClientInterceptor (or an Apache HttpClient AsyncExecChainHandler) on the client builder, while executor utilisation is tracked by passing an ExecutorServiceMetrics.monitor wrapped ScheduledExecutorService to builder.jobWorkerExecutor.

docs.title: Java client job worker additional metrics

scenario: A marketplace running Camunda 8.8 with 8000 jobs per hour wants to reduce activation latency on a critical Java worker. The lead engineer enables job streaming and must confirm how the stream interacts with maxJobsActive and the default stream timeout so the worker memory footprint stays bounded and the underlying stream refreshes on a known cadence.
question: Which statement matches the Camunda 8.8 Java client documentation for job streaming on a worker builder?

options.text: Job streaming is enabled by calling .streamEnabled(true) on the worker builder; once enabled the worker disables polling entirely and the streamTimeout defaults to 24 hours of refresh on the long-living stream

Job streaming is enabled by calling .openStream() on the JobWorker after .open(); once enabled the maxJobsActive setting is ignored in favour of internal gRPC flow-control windows on the underlying transport

Job streaming is enabled by calling .pushMode(true) on the worker builder; once enabled the worker accepts an unbounded number of jobs from the gateway until the application heap is exhausted at runtime

Job streaming is enabled by calling .streamEnabled(true) on the worker builder; the worker still buffers only up to maxJobsActive jobs at once and the streamTimeout defaults to one hour of refresh on the stream

c.text: Incorrect. The flag is .streamEnabled(true), and the worker still bounds buffered jobs by maxJobsActive rather than accepting an unbounded stream of jobs from the gateway.

a.text: Incorrect. Streaming does not disable polling outright (polling still occurs for backfilling), and the documented default streamTimeout is one hour rather than 24 hours.

d.text: Correct. The Job-streaming section explains that .streamEnabled(true) opens a long-living gRPC stream; the worker still buffers at most maxJobsActive jobs at once, and the default streamTimeout is one hour, configurable through .streamTimeout(...).

b.text: Incorrect. Streaming is configured before .open() through .streamEnabled(true); maxJobsActive continues to soft-bound the number of in-flight jobs even when streaming is enabled on the worker.

explanation: The Job-streaming section explains that calling .streamEnabled(true) on the worker builder opens a long-living gRPC stream over which jobs are pushed. The worker still buffers at most maxJobsActive jobs (soft-bounding memory), polling is reduced rather than removed, and the streamTimeout defaults to one hour and can be changed via .streamTimeout(...).

docs.title: Java client job worker job streaming

scenario: Vertex Marketplace runs a Camunda 8.8 multi-tenant cluster with 12 tenants registered for its merchant onboarding domain. The platform team must open a Java worker that picks up jobs for three specific tenants and must accept that if authorisation is missing for any one of those tenants the worker stops picking up jobs entirely instead of just those tenants.
question: How does a Camunda 8.8 Java job worker handle multi-tenancy when configured for several tenant ids on the builder?

options.text: The worker fans out jobs across the supplied tenants by round-robin polling and silently skips any tenant for which the client is not authorised while continuing to serve the authorised tenants from the supplied set

The worker accepts a primary tenant id and falls back to a default tenant when authorisation is missing for the primary tenant, ensuring that at least the default tenant continues to be processed at runtime

The worker is configured with a single tenant id per call to .open(), and rotating tenants requires opening a separate worker per tenant; default tenants configured on the client are ignored entirely by every worker

The worker is configured with one or more tenant ids on the builder (or inherits default tenants from the client) and refuses to pick up any jobs if the client is not authorised for every supplied tenant

b.text: Incorrect. There is no documented fallback to a default tenant on missing authorisation; the worker stops working on jobs entirely when the client is not authorised for every supplied tenant.

c.text: Incorrect. The builder exposes both .tenantId(...) and .tenantIds(...) for multiple tenants, and default tenants configured via CAMUNDA_DEFAULT_JOB_WORKER_TENANT_IDS or camunda.client.worker.tenantIds apply to every worker the client opens.

a.text: Incorrect. The Multi-tenancy section states that the client must be authorised for all supplied tenants; if it is not, the worker will not work on any jobs rather than only skipping the unauthorised tenants.

d.text: Correct. The Multi-tenancy section says workers are configured with one or more tenant ids on the builder (or inherit default tenants from CAMUNDA_DEFAULT_JOB_WORKER_TENANT_IDS or camunda.client.worker.tenantIds), and that the worker will not work on any jobs unless the client is authorised for every configured tenant.

explanation: Multi-tenancy on the Java job worker is configured through .tenantId(...) or .tenantIds(...) on the worker builder, or through default tenants set on the client via the environment variable CAMUNDA_DEFAULT_JOB_WORKER_TENANT_IDS or the property camunda.client.worker.tenantIds. The client must be authorised for every configured tenant; otherwise the worker will not work on any jobs.

scenario: Nimbus Bank runs a Camunda 8.8 order process that produces 3000 jobs per hour for the fraud-check worker pool. The platform team wants to copy reference data such as trace-id and team labels from the process instance onto each created job so workers can read them without fetching variables. The architect must choose the documented job property that carries these immutable labels at job creation time.
question: Which job property carries immutable labels copied from the process instance at job creation in Camunda 8.8?

options.text: The Type property of a job carries immutable labels copied from the process instance at job creation and is used to attach reference data such as trace-id or team to the activated job
The Tags property of a job carries immutable labels copied from the process instance at job creation and is used to attach reference data such as trace-id or team to the activated job
The Variables property of a job carries immutable labels copied from the process instance at job creation and is used to attach reference data such as trace-id or team to the activated job
The Custom headers property of a job carries immutable labels copied from the process instance at job creation and is used to attach reference data such as trace-id or team to the activated job

a.text: Incorrect. The Type property describes the work item defined in each task and is referenced by workers to request jobs they can perform; it does not carry immutable labels copied from the process instance.

d.text: Incorrect. Custom headers carry additional static metadata defined in the process model to configure reusable workers, such as a Slack channel; they are not immutable labels copied from the process instance.

b.text: Correct. Camunda 8.8 documents Tags as immutable labels copied from the process instance at job creation, ideal for metadata such as reference, team, or trace-id that workers can read on every activated job.

c.text: Incorrect. Variables hold contextual business data of the process instance that the worker needs to do its work, while immutable labels copied at job creation are exposed through Tags.

explanation: On a Camunda 8.8 job, Tags are immutable labels copied from the process instance at job creation. Type identifies the work item, Custom headers carry static per-task metadata defined in the model, and Variables carry the mutable contextual business data of the process instance.

scenario: Helios Logistics scales 6 workers for the ship-parcel job type in a Camunda 8.8 cluster that processes 9000 shipments per day. The platform engineer wants to stop the workers from sending empty activation requests every 100 ms when there is no work, and instead keep one request open until at least one job of the type arrives at the gateway.
question: Which Zeebe activation parameter keeps a job request open until at least one job becomes available?

options.text: Setting the request-timeout parameter on the activation request keeps the request open until at least one job of the requested type becomes available for activation in the cluster
Setting the Timeout parameter on the activation request keeps the request open until at least one job of the requested type becomes available for activation in the cluster
Setting the Worker parameter on the activation request keeps the request open until at least one job of the requested type becomes available for activation in the cluster
Setting the MaxJobsToActivate parameter on the activation request keeps the request open until at least one job of the requested type becomes available for activation in the cluster

c.text: Incorrect. The Worker parameter is the identifier of the worker used for auditing purposes and does not control long polling on activation requests in Camunda 8.8.

b.text: Incorrect. Timeout is the duration a job is assigned to the worker before Zeebe may reassign it; it does not control whether the activation request waits for jobs.

d.text: Incorrect. MaxJobsToActivate caps how many jobs a single activation request may return; it does not keep the request open while no jobs are available.

a.text: Correct. Camunda 8.8 documents long polling for ActivateJobs as the request-timeout parameter, which keeps the activation request open until at least one job becomes available.

explanation: Long polling in Zeebe is enabled via the request-timeout parameter on the ActivateJobs RPC. With long polling the activation request stays open while no jobs are available and completes when at least one job becomes available, reducing resource use compared to repeated empty polls.

scenario: Tessera Insurance runs Camunda 8.8 workers that activate claim validation jobs with a 5 minutes initial timeout, but some claims take 90 minutes when external policy lookups are slow. The team wants to extend the timeout of an already activated job once the worker realises the work will take longer, and the architect must explain how UpdateJobTimeout interprets the duration it receives.
question: How does the Camunda 8.8 UpdateJobTimeout gRPC command interpret the duration it receives?

options.text: The duration is the new timeout measured from the current time, so the deadline is the current time plus the supplied duration, which lets the worker extend or shorten the assignment
The duration replaces the remaining retries counter on the job so the deadline shifts forward by the supplied duration, which lets the worker extend the assignment for very long jobs
The duration is the new timeout measured from the job activation time, so the deadline is the activation time plus the supplied duration, regardless of how long the worker has been running
The duration is added to the previous timeout so the new deadline is the previous deadline plus the supplied duration, which lets the worker extend the assignment incrementally for long jobs

d.text: Incorrect. Camunda 8.8 does not add the supplied duration to the previous timeout; the documentation states that UpdateJobTimeout sets the new timeout from the current time, not as an increment to the existing deadline.

a.text: Correct. Camunda 8.8 documents that UpdateJobTimeout takes a duration which becomes the new timeout measured from the current time, so the deadline is current time plus the supplied duration; this also lets workers shorten the assignment when needed.

c.text: Incorrect. UpdateJobTimeout is measured from the current time rather than from the original activation time, so the new deadline depends on when the update command is processed by the broker.

b.text: Incorrect. UpdateJobTimeout does not touch the retries counter on the job; it sets the new timeout duration measured from the current time, leaving retries unchanged.

explanation: Camunda 8.8 documents UpdateJobTimeout as setting the new timeout from the current time rather than adding to the previous one. Workers can therefore both extend and shorten the timeout, and should apply a buffer before the deadline because the update itself takes some processing time.

scenario: Aurora Marketplace deploys 24 Camunda 8.8 job workers behind 3 gateways serving 18000 jobs per hour for the shipment-prepare type. The platform engineer wants gateway aggregation of streams to actually coalesce these workers into one aggregated stream per broker so load is evenly distributed across gateways and the network footprint stays small.
question: Which set of attributes must match across worker streams for Camunda 8.8 gateways to treat them as logically equivalent?

options.text: Two streams are logically equivalent when they share the same job type, the same worker name, the same job activation timeout, and the same fetch variables list declared on the worker builder
Two streams are logically equivalent when they share the same job type, the same worker name, the same poll interval, and the same fetch variables list declared on the worker builder
Two streams are logically equivalent when they share the same job type, the same gateway endpoint, the same activation timeout, and the same fetch variables list declared on the worker builder
Two streams are logically equivalent when they share the same job type, the same tenant id, the same activation timeout, and the same maxJobsActive value declared on the worker builder

d.text: Incorrect. Camunda 8.8 documents tenant id and maxJobsActive as separate concerns from stream equivalence; logical equivalence is determined by job type, worker name, activation timeout, and fetch variables.

b.text: Incorrect. Poll interval is not part of the documented stream equivalence criteria for Camunda 8.8 gateways; the documented attributes are job type, worker name, job activation timeout, and fetch variables.

a.text: Correct. Camunda 8.8 documents two streams as logically equivalent when they target the same job type, share the same worker name, the same job activation timeout, and the same fetch variables; gateways aggregate such streams across brokers.

c.text: Incorrect. Gateway endpoint is a client connection detail and is not part of logical equivalence; the documented attributes are job type, worker name, job activation timeout, and fetch variables.

explanation: Camunda 8.8 gateways aggregate logically equivalent streams and register each aggregated stream to every broker. Two streams are logically equivalent when they target the same job type, share the same worker name, the same job activation timeout, and the same fetch variables.

scenario: Lumen Banking runs a Camunda 8.8 cluster that streams 12000 jobs per hour to a fleet of payment workers. The SRE team suspects the broker is the throughput bottleneck and asks which job stream metric exposes the rate at which a single broker pushes jobs out across all aggregated streams, so they can plot it against gateway-side push rates and confirm where the queue is building up.
question: Which job stream metric derives the rate at which a Camunda 8.8 broker pushes jobs out to all streams?

options.text: The metric zeebe_gateway_job_stream_push_total derives the rate at which a gateway pushes jobs out to clients and helps compare gateway throughput with broker push counts
The metric zeebe_broker_open_job_stream_count reports the count of job streams registered on the broker and should equal the sum of all gateway aggregated streams
The metric zeebe_broker_jobs_pushed_count_total derives the rate at which a broker pushes jobs out to all streams and helps identify when the broker is the throughput bottleneck
The metric zeebe_gateway_job_stream_servers reports the number of known broker stream servers per gateway and should equal the total brokers in the cluster

a.text: Incorrect. zeebe_gateway_job_stream_push_total is a gateway-side metric for the rate of pushes from a gateway to its connected clients, not the broker-side push rate to all streams.

b.text: Incorrect. zeebe_broker_open_job_stream_count is a count of registered streams on the broker, not a push-rate metric, so it cannot show whether the broker itself is the throughput bottleneck.

c.text: Correct. zeebe_broker_jobs_pushed_count_total lets operators derive the rate at which a broker pushes jobs out to all streams, which is the metric used to detect a broker-side throughput bottleneck.

d.text: Incorrect. zeebe_gateway_job_stream_servers reports how many broker stream servers a gateway sees and is used to detect clustering issues, not to derive broker push rates.

explanation: The job streams troubleshooting metrics on Camunda 8.8 separate broker push counters from gateway push counters. zeebe_broker_jobs_pushed_count_total exposes the per-broker push rate to all streams and is the right metric to check when looking for a broker-side throughput bottleneck.

scenario: Forge Bank operates a Camunda 8.8 order processing system that creates 15000 jobs per day across BPMN service tasks of type process-payment, fetch-items, and ship-parcel. The lead architect explains to a new engineer which job properties travel from the engine to the worker and which of them is immutable after the job is created from a BPMN element.
question: Which property travels from the Camunda 8.8 engine to a job worker as immutable labels copied from the process instance at job creation?

options.text: Custom headers are immutable labels copied from the running process instance at job creation; once on the job they cannot be changed, and a worker reads them for routing or filtering decisions

The job key is an immutable label copied from the running process instance at job creation; once on the job it cannot be changed, and a worker reads it for routing or filtering decisions during execution
Tags are immutable labels copied from the process instance at job creation; once on the job they cannot be changed, and a worker reads them for routing or filtering decisions during job execution

Variables are immutable labels copied from the running process instance at job creation; once on the job they cannot be changed, and a worker reads them for routing or filtering decisions during execution

c.text: Correct. The job-properties list states that tags are immutable labels copied from the process instance at job creation; once on the job, tag values cannot change and they support metadata such as reference:1234, team:accounting, and trace-id:3004.

a.text: Incorrect. Custom headers are static metadata defined on each task in the process model rather than labels copied from the running process instance at job creation; tags fill that role.

b.text: Incorrect. The job key is a unique identifier used to complete or fail the job, not metadata labels copied from the process instance at job creation by the engine.

d.text: Incorrect. Variables hold contextual business data of the process instance and are mutable as the process progresses; the immutable labels copied at job creation are tags.

explanation: The job-workers concept page lists five job properties: type, custom headers, key, variables, and tags. Tags are introduced as immutable labels copied from the process instance at the moment the job is created; the docs give examples such as reference:1234, team:accounting, and trace-id:3004 and link to process-instance-creation tags for additional context.

scenario: Helios Logistics runs a Camunda 8.8 shipment routing process that creates 6000 jobs per hour across several BPMN service tasks. The lead engineer must configure the job activation request from each Java worker so that workers identify themselves, cap the batch size returned by the engine, and request only a focused subset of process variables rather than the full instance variable map.
question: Which four parameters does the Camunda 8.8 job-workers documentation list for a job activation request from a worker?

options.text: Worker name, retries, batch size, and a list of pinned BPMN task ids that constrain which service task instances of the requested job type the worker may take on during activation from the engine
Job key, custom headers, tags, and a maxQueueDepth value that defines how many activated jobs may remain unhandled before further activations are throttled by the gateway on subsequent requests
Worker name, timeout, maxJobsToActivate, and fetchVariables that optionally narrows the activated jobs to a specific list of required process variable names rather than every variable on the instance
Tenant id, audience, lease duration, and a long-polling toggle that keeps the activation request open until at least maxJobsToActivate jobs have been collected by the worker through the gateway

a.text: Incorrect. The activation request parameters listed in the docs are worker, timeout, maxJobsToActivate, and fetchVariables; retries and pinned BPMN ids are not part of that list.

c.text: Correct. The Requesting-jobs section lists worker (identifier used for auditing), timeout (how long the job is assigned to the worker), maxJobsToActivate (batch cap), and fetchVariables (an optional list of required variable names; if empty, all instance variables are returned).

b.text: Incorrect. Job key, custom headers, and tags are properties of activated jobs themselves, not parameters of the activation request that the worker sends to the gateway.

d.text: Incorrect. The activation request does not take audience or lease duration; long polling is governed by the separate request-timeout parameter on the gRPC ActivateJobs RPC.

explanation: The Requesting-jobs section lists four request parameters: worker (identifier used for auditing), timeout (how long the job is assigned to the worker before it can be reassigned), maxJobsToActivate (maximum number of jobs returned), and fetchVariables (a list of required variable names; if empty, all process instance variables are returned).

scenario: Iris Marketplace runs a Camunda 8.8 catalogue update process that creates 9500 jobs per day across several BPMN service tasks. The platform team must encode failure handling on its Java workers so the engine raises an incident when remaining retries hit zero and so workers can optionally wait before the next retry through a documented retry back off field on the fail-job command.
question: Which value in the Camunda 8.8 fail-job command causes the engine to raise an incident instead of immediately reassigning the job to another worker?

options.text: Setting maxJobsToActivate to zero on the original activation request causes Zeebe to raise an incident on the failure path, and the retry back off otherwise governs how soon the job is retried by the next worker
Setting remaining retries to zero or a negative value when failing the job causes Zeebe to raise an incident, and the job is not retried until the incident is resolved by a user or by an API call

Setting the job timeout to zero when failing the job causes Zeebe to raise an incident, and the retry back off is then interpreted as the suspension duration of the raised incident before the job is retried

Setting the failure reason to INCIDENT in the fail-job payload causes Zeebe to raise an incident on the active task instead of reassigning the job, and the remaining retries field is then ignored by the engine

d.text: Incorrect. The fail-job command takes a remaining-retries count rather than a textual reason value; incident creation is driven by the retries value, not by an INCIDENT reason flag.

a.text: Incorrect. maxJobsToActivate is part of the activation request, not the fail-job command; it does not control whether failing a job raises an incident.

b.text: Correct. The Completing-or-failing-jobs section states that if remaining retries is zero or negative the job is not retried and an incident is raised; once the incident is resolved the job becomes retriable again.

c.text: Incorrect. Incident creation is driven by the remaining-retries value supplied with the fail-job command, not by the job timeout; the timeout governs how long an activation remains assigned to a worker.

explanation: When a job worker sends a fail-job command, it supplies a remaining-retries count. If that count is greater than zero the job is retried and reassigned, optionally after a retry back off delay. If the count is zero or negative the engine raises an incident and the job is not retried until the incident is resolved.

scenario: Atlas Logistics runs a Camunda 8.8 customs-clearance service whose jobs typically last between 5 and 240 minutes across different lanes. Each worker activates a job with a default timeout of 10 minutes and must extend or shorten the timeout dynamically while processing 1800 shipments per day so jobs are not reassigned prematurely or held longer than necessary.
question: How does the Camunda 8.8 UpdateJobTimeout gRPC command interpret the duration value it receives for an already activated job?

options.text: The duration is the new total timeout measured from the moment the command is processed; the value can shorten or extend the timeout and replaces the previously configured timeout on the activated job

The duration is subtracted from the remaining time-to-timeout so that workers can speed up reassignment; only positive durations are accepted and a negative value is rejected by the broker for the activated job

The duration is added to the originally requested activation timeout and the engine keeps a running sum; sending the command three times with PT5M extends the timeout by 15 minutes in total on the active job

The duration replaces the originally requested activation timeout and starts counting from the original activation moment, so short durations may already be expired when the command is applied to the active job

a.text: Correct. The Timeout-update section states that the duration becomes the new total timeout from the current moment, so the same command can both extend and shorten the timeout; the docs add that workers should leave a buffer to avoid races with the broker-side timeout.

c.text: Incorrect. The command does not extend by a delta added to a running sum; it replaces the timeout with a new duration measured from when the broker processes the command on the active job.

b.text: Incorrect. The command does not interpret the duration as a shrink delta; both shorter and longer total timeouts are supported and the duration is measured from the current moment of processing on the broker.

d.text: Incorrect. The duration is measured from when the command is processed, not from the original activation moment; a short duration becomes a near-term timeout from the time of update.

explanation: UpdateJobTimeout takes a duration that becomes the new total time-to-timeout measured from the moment the broker processes the command. The same command can therefore extend or shorten the active timeout, so workers can start with a standard initial value and adjust later based on actual job progress, leaving a buffer to avoid expiry during the update.

scenario: Forge Logistics runs a Java job worker fleet processing 12000 jobs per hour in a Camunda 8.8 self-managed cluster. The platform team notices the workers are falling behind and wants to use client-side metrics, not just broker metrics, to estimate the queue depth and decide when to scale out the worker deployment.
question: Which metric calculation estimates the rate of queued jobs on a Camunda 8.8 job worker from the client side?

options.text: Divide zeebe_broker_jobs_push_fail_try_count_total by zeebe_gateway_job_stream_servers to estimate the rate of queued jobs, and scale workers if the result approaches maxJobsActive
Subtract camunda.client.worker.job.handled from camunda.client.worker.job.activated to estimate the rate of queued jobs, and scale workers if the result approaches maxJobsActive
Subtract camunda.client.worker.job.activated from camunda.client.worker.job.handled to estimate the rate of queued jobs, and scale workers if the result approaches maxJobsActive
Read zeebe_gateway_job_stream_push_total on a single gateway to estimate the rate of queued jobs, and scale workers if the result approaches maxJobsActive

b.text: Correct. The job-workers docs recommend estimating queued jobs as activated minus handled (camunda.client.worker.job.activated - camunda.client.worker.job.handled); a value consistently close to maxJobsActive indicates the worker deployment needs to scale.

c.text: Incorrect. The direction is reversed; handled minus activated does not represent queue depth, since handled cannot exceed activated for a healthy worker.

a.text: Incorrect. zeebe_broker_jobs_push_fail_try_count_total counts failed push attempts on the server side and zeebe_gateway_job_stream_servers counts known brokers; their ratio is not the queue-depth estimator the docs describe.

d.text: Incorrect. zeebe_gateway_job_stream_push_total measures the push rate from a gateway, not the client-side queue depth, and reading a single gateway misses the per-worker view.

explanation: On the client side, queue depth is estimated by subtracting the handled rate from the activated rate using camunda.client.worker.job.activated and camunda.client.worker.job.handled. When this difference stays close to maxJobsActive, the docs advise scaling the worker deployment.

scenario: Beacon Retail runs a Camunda 8.8 self-managed cluster handling 4500 order-fulfillment jobs per hour. A worker for the type packPallet is registered but no jobs of that type are being activated. The on-call SRE wants to inspect the registered job streams from the broker and gateway monitoring port (localhost:9600) before restarting any pods.
question: Which actuator endpoint exposes the registered job streams on a Camunda 8.8 broker or gateway?

options.text: /actuator/jobstreams returns the current view of registered job streams, with client referring to streams opened on the gateway and remote referring to aggregated gateway streams on each broker
/actuator/health/jobstreams returns the current view of registered job streams, with both client and remote sections listed only on the broker actuator port
/actuator/streams returns the current view of registered job streams, with client referring to streams opened on the gateway and remote referring to aggregated gateway streams on each broker
/actuator/jobs returns the current view of registered job streams, with client referring to streams opened on the broker and remote referring to aggregated broker streams on each gateway

a.text: Correct. Each broker and gateway exposes /actuator/jobstreams on the monitoring port. The response separates client (streams opened on the gateway) from remote (aggregated gateway streams as opened on each broker), which is exactly what the SRE needs to trace a missing stream.

d.text: Incorrect. /actuator/jobs is not the documented endpoint; the docs specify /actuator/jobstreams as the actuator for job stream inspection.

c.text: Incorrect. /actuator/streams is not the documented endpoint; the docs name /actuator/jobstreams as the actuator that returns registered job streams.

b.text: Incorrect. The actuator is /actuator/jobstreams (not nested under /health), and it is exposed on both broker and gateway monitoring ports, not only on the broker.

explanation: Both brokers and gateways expose /actuator/jobstreams on the monitoring port. The payload distinguishes client streams (opened on the gateway) from remote (aggregated streams seen on each broker), which lets operators trace where a registered stream is missing before restarting anything.

scenario: Stellar Retailer ships a custom outbound connector that handles 4500 orders per day on local developer laptops running Camunda 8.8 Run. The platform team needs the connector runtime auto-loaded by Camunda 8 Run so that BPMN processes referencing the new template can execute jobs without the developer manually wiring a separate runtime container next to the broker.
question: How does a developer provision the custom outbound connector runtime in a local Camunda 8.8 Run installation?

options.text: Build the connector with mvn clean verify package, then copy the resulting jar with all dependencies into the custom_connectors directory in the root of Camunda 8 Run and start the stack with start.sh --docker
Place the connector jar in the connector-secrets folder of Camunda 8 Run and restart the broker process so the runtime picks up the new template definitions at the next startup cycle
Deploy the connector jar through the Orchestration Cluster REST API by sending a POST request to /v2/deployments with the jar attached as a resources multipart form field on the running cluster
Publish the connector jar to the Camunda Console connector marketplace and bind it to the local cluster from the cluster details page so Camunda 8 Run loads it on next boot

a.text: Correct. The documented flow builds the connector jar with mvn clean verify package, places the resulting jar with all dependencies into the custom_connectors directory at the root of Camunda 8 Run, and then starts the stack with start.sh --docker so the runtime loads it.

c.text: Incorrect. /v2/deployments accepts BPMN, forms, and DMN resources for the cluster, not connector runtime jars; connector runtimes are provisioned by placing the jar in the custom_connectors directory.

b.text: Incorrect. There is no connector-secrets folder used to host connector runtimes; the documented location for a custom connector jar in Camunda 8 Run is the custom_connectors directory.

d.text: Incorrect. Camunda 8 Run does not pull custom connector jars from a Console marketplace; the jar must be copied into the local custom_connectors directory of the Camunda 8 Run installation.

explanation: To add a custom connector runtime to Camunda 8 Run, build the connector jar that bundles all dependencies, copy it into the custom_connectors directory at the root of the Camunda 8 Run installation, and start the stack with start.sh --docker. The connector is then ready to execute jobs whenever a process references it.

scenario: Vanta Insurance plans a 3 months migration window from /v2 to a future /v3 of the Orchestration Cluster REST API on Camunda 8.8. The architecture team needs to understand how Camunda's SemVer policy versions the API and whether different version numbers of the same endpoint can run side by side during the migration window.
question: How does Camunda 8.8 version endpoints in the Orchestration Cluster REST API?

options.text: Camunda treats adding new endpoints or attributes to existing responses as a breaking change, which forces the API version to bump alongside any purely additive schema change
Camunda versions the API only at major product releases such as 9.0.0, so /v2 paths remain frozen through every 8.x minor release even when breaking changes are introduced
Camunda versions the entire API as a whole, so a breaking change in any endpoint bumps the API version (for example v2 to v3), and both versions may coexist during the migration window
Camunda versions each endpoint independently, so an individual path like /user-tasks/search can advance to v3 while every other path on the API stays pinned at v2 for callers

c.text: Correct. The Versioning section states Camunda versions the entire API rather than individual endpoints; a breaking change anywhere bumps the API version, and during migration multiple API versions (for example v2 and v3 of /user-tasks/search) may coexist in the same release.

d.text: Incorrect. Individual endpoints are not versioned in isolation; the entire API is versioned together when a breaking change occurs.

b.text: Incorrect. The API version is determined by the API version number and the product version together, and is not frozen until a major product release; SemVer drives the bump.

a.text: Incorrect. The docs explicitly note that adding new endpoints or attributes to existing responses is not considered a breaking change.

explanation: Camunda versions the Orchestration Cluster REST API as a whole using SemVer, with the API version number (v2) combined with the product version (for example 8.8.0). Any breaking change bumps the API version, and during migration windows multiple API versions of the same endpoint may coexist. Purely additive changes are not breaking.

scenario: Stellar Bank drives 9000 instances per hour through the Camunda 8.8 Orchestration Cluster REST API from a Spring Boot integration. Under peak load the gateway begins rejecting calls. The reliability team needs to know which documented HTTP status code signals backpressure so the retry library can apply an exponential backoff specifically for that code.
question: Which HTTP status does the Camunda 8.8 Orchestration Cluster REST API return when the broker signals backpressure?

options.text: 503 Service Unavailable when the system signals backpressure to protect server compute resources, with the recommendation that clients retry the request after a backoff period
412 Precondition Failed when the system signals backpressure to protect server compute resources, with the recommendation that clients retry the request after a backoff period
409 Conflict when the system signals backpressure to protect server compute resources, with the recommendation that clients retry the request after a backoff period
429 Too Many Requests when the system signals backpressure to protect server compute resources, with the recommendation that clients retry the request after a backoff period

a.text: Correct. The HTTP status codes table maps 503 to backpressure: the service is currently unavailable because the system signals backpressure to prevent server compute resources from being exhausted, and clients are recommended to retry after a backoff period.

d.text: Incorrect. 429 Too Many Requests is not the documented backpressure code in this table; the overview reserves 503 for the backpressure case.

c.text: Incorrect. 409 Conflict is used when a request modifies a resource that is not in the correct state, not for backpressure signalling.

b.text: Incorrect. 412 Precondition Failed asks the client to check cluster status, but is not the code used to signal broker backpressure under load.

explanation: When the broker signals backpressure the Orchestration Cluster REST API returns 503 Service Unavailable. The status table documents this case as a temporary unavailability caused by load shedding, and instructs clients to retry the request after a backoff period rather than treating it as a permanent failure.

scenario: Kestrel Retail finishes the Camunda 8.8 Orchestration Cluster REST API tutorial with 4 developers and now wants to add a job worker to handle 12000 jobs per day from the order-fulfillment process. The team checks the overview's What's next? section to choose the path Camunda recommends for building a job worker that complements the REST API.
question: Which next step does the Camunda 8.8 Orchestration Cluster REST API overview recommend for building a job worker?

options.text: Build a job worker using only the legacy zeebe-client-node-js package, which the overview's What's next? section links as the recommended follow-up after the REST API guide
Build a job worker by polling /v2/jobs from a hand-rolled HTTP loop, which the overview's What's next? section links as the recommended follow-up after the REST API guide
Build a job worker on top of the gRPC StreamActivatedJobs primitive directly, which the overview's What's next? section links as the recommended follow-up after the REST API guide
Build a job worker using the Camunda Spring Boot Starter (Spring SDK), which the overview's What's next? section links as the recommended follow-up after the REST API guide

d.text: Correct. The What's next? section links Build a job worker using the Spring SDK (the Camunda Spring Boot Starter) as the recommended follow-up step after the REST API overview.

a.text: Incorrect. The overview's What's next? list does not point to a legacy Node.js client for building job workers; it points to the Spring SDK.

b.text: Incorrect. Hand-rolled HTTP polling against /v2/jobs is not the documented next step; the overview directs readers to the Spring SDK guide.

c.text: Incorrect. The overview does not send readers to the gRPC StreamActivatedJobs primitive as the next step; the recommended path is the Spring SDK.

explanation: The What's next? section of the Orchestration Cluster REST API overview points readers who want to add a worker to the Camunda Spring Boot Starter (Spring SDK) guide. Other links cover process testing and migrating from v1 component REST APIs, but the worker recommendation is the Spring SDK.

scenario: Helios Fintech drives 9000 requests per minute against the Camunda 8.8 Orchestration Cluster REST API from a payments microservice. During load tests the client receives sporadic responses that the platform team must classify so the resilience library applies a retry-after-backoff strategy only when the cluster is signalling backpressure to protect its compute resources.
question: Which HTTP status code from the Camunda 8.8 Orchestration Cluster REST API signals backpressure and should be retried after a backoff?

options.text: Status code 412 Precondition failed signals that the client should check the cluster status before sending more requests of the same kind to the orchestration endpoints
Status code 401 Unauthorized signals that the client is not authenticated and should retry the request after attaching a modified authorization header to the call
Status code 503 Service unavailable signals that the cluster is engaging backpressure and clients retry the request after a backoff period to ease load
Status code 409 Conflict signals that the request tries to modify a resource that is not in the correct state to accept the change on the orchestration endpoints

a.text: Incorrect. 412 Precondition failed prompts the client to check the cluster status, not to back off and retry; backpressure is reported with a different status code on the Orchestration Cluster REST API.

d.text: Incorrect. 409 Conflict reports that the target resource is not in the correct state for the requested change; it does not indicate backpressure or recommend a backoff and retry strategy.

c.text: Correct. The overview documents 503 Service unavailable as the response used when the system signals backpressure to protect its compute resources, and explicitly recommends clients retry such requests after a backoff period.

b.text: Incorrect. 401 Unauthorized is an authentication failure that asks the client to retry with a modified authorization header; it is not the backpressure signal on the Orchestration Cluster REST API.

explanation: Among common HTTP status codes returned by the Camunda 8.8 Orchestration Cluster REST API, 503 Service unavailable is the one tied to the broker backpressure mechanism that protects compute resources from exhaustion, and clients are recommended to retry such requests after a backoff period.

scenario: Aurora Bank queries the Camunda 8.8 Operate API to feed 4 dashboards that cover 9000 instances per day. The team writes its first POST /v1/process-instances/search request and needs to confirm the four documented top-level components of an Operate query request body, so pagination and sorting work exactly as the API expects across releases.
question: Which components make up a query request body for the Camunda 8.8 Operate API search endpoint?

options.text: A query consists of conditions (object fields to match), count (max items to return), rank (field plus ASC or DESC), and cursor for pagination using a base64 cursor returned by previous results
A query consists of where (object fields to match), limit (max items to return), orderBy (field plus ASC or DESC), and offset for pagination using a numeric row index from previous results
A query consists of filter (object fields to match), size (max items to return), sort (field plus ASC or DESC), and searchAfter for pagination using sortValues from previous results
A query consists of must (object fields to match), top (max items to return), order (field plus ASC or DESC), and pageToken for pagination using an opaque token returned by previous results

c.text: Correct. The Query section defines the request as filter (which fields to match), size (max items), sort (an array of {field, order} with order ASC or DESC), and searchAfter for pagination, where you copy the sortValues from the previous results into searchAfter to fetch the next page.

b.text: Incorrect. The docs use filter/size/sort/searchAfter, not SQL-style where/limit/orderBy/offset, and Operate paginates with search-after rather than numeric offsets.

d.text: Incorrect. The keys are filter/size/sort/searchAfter; there is no must/top/order/pageToken envelope in the documented query body.

a.text: Incorrect. There is no conditions/count/rank/cursor schema for Operate queries; the documented schema is filter/size/sort/searchAfter.

explanation: Operate API query bodies have four documented components: filter (which fields the items must match), size (maximum items to return, default 10), sort (array of {field, order} with order ASC or DESC, default sorts ascending by key), and searchAfter, which takes the sortValues array from the previous results to page forward.

scenario: Iris Healthcare operates 7 Self-Managed Camunda 8.8 environments and wants developers to explore the Operate API interactively against a local cluster without configuring an external tool. The platform team must direct them to the Swagger UI URL that a running Self-Managed Operate instance serves on the default port so the 240 users can sanity-check requests.
question: Which URL serves the Operate API Swagger UI on a Self-Managed Camunda 8.8 Operate instance using default settings?

options.text: The Swagger UI is served at http://localhost:9600/actuator/swagger on a Self-Managed Operate instance using the broker monitoring port for the Operate API endpoints
The Swagger UI is served at https://operate.camunda.io/swagger-ui.html on a Self-Managed Operate instance proxied through the public Camunda 8 SaaS gateway for the Operate API
The Swagger UI is served at http://localhost:8080/swagger-ui.html on a Self-Managed Operate instance running with default ports for direct exploration of the Operate API endpoints
The Swagger UI is served at http://localhost:8080/api-docs/index.html on a Self-Managed Operate instance through the API Explorer bridge bundled with Operate for the Operate API

c.text: Correct. The Operate API overview documents that a running Self-Managed Operate instance exposes the Swagger UI at http://localhost:8080/swagger-ui.html on the default port, alongside the SaaS form under https://${REGION}.operate.camunda.io/${CLUSTER_ID}/swagger-ui.html.

a.text: Incorrect. Port 9600 hosts broker monitoring and actuator endpoints, not the Operate Swagger UI; the documented Self-Managed Operate path is /swagger-ui.html on port 8080.

b.text: Incorrect. https://operate.camunda.io/swagger-ui.html is not the documented Self-Managed endpoint; Self-Managed Operate serves the Swagger UI at http://localhost:8080/swagger-ui.html on the local instance.

d.text: Incorrect. There is no /api-docs/index.html path documented for the Operate Swagger UI; the documented Self-Managed location is /swagger-ui.html on port 8080 of the running Operate instance.

explanation: The Operate API overview lists the Swagger UI served inside a running Operate instance at /swagger-ui.html. For Self-Managed installations the documented URL is http://localhost:8080/swagger-ui.html, while SaaS uses https://${REGION}.operate.camunda.io/${CLUSTER_ID}/swagger-ui.html.

scenario: Helios Bank runs a fintech back office on Camunda 8.8 and queries the Operate API for 7000 instances per hour. A developer is building a paginated dashboard and must construct a query request body using the four documented top-level fields that scope matches, cap the returned page size, sort items, and resume pagination across pages.
question: Which four top-level fields make up the Operate API query request body in Camunda 8.8?

options.text: match, count, order, and cursor scope matches, cap the returned page size, order items, and resume pagination on the next call to the Operate API
where, top, sortBy, and continuationToken scope matches, cap the returned page size, order items, and resume pagination on the next call to the Operate API
filter, limit, orderBy, and offset scope matches, cap the returned page size, order items, and resume pagination on the next call to the Operate API
filter, size, sort, and searchAfter scope matches, cap the returned page size, order items, and resume pagination on the next call to the Operate API

d.text: Correct. The Operate API query body consists of filter, size, sort, and searchAfter; sort takes field and order ASC or DESC entries, and searchAfter accepts the previous response's sortValues array to fetch the next page.

c.text: Incorrect. The documented field names are size and searchAfter, not limit and offset; offset and orderBy are not part of the Operate API query body schema.

b.text: Incorrect. The keys where, top, sortBy, and continuationToken do not appear in the Operate API query schema; the documented names are filter, size, sort, and searchAfter.

a.text: Incorrect. The Operate API uses filter, size, sort, and searchAfter; the names match, count, order, and cursor are not part of the documented query body schema.

explanation: The Operate API query request body has four documented parts: filter to match object fields, size for the number of items to return, sort with a field name plus order ASC or DESC, and searchAfter that takes the identifier from which the next search should start.

scenario: An insurance platform on Camunda 8.8 runs a Spring Boot worker fleet that issues 800 requests per minute to a self-managed broker presenting a certificate signed by an internal Certificate Authority. The platform team needs the worker to trust that root CA without modifying the JVM default trust store on every host in the fleet.
question: Which configuration role does the camunda.client.ca-certificate-path property play in the Camunda 8.8 Spring Boot starter?

options.text: It points to a directory of CA bundles merged into the default JVM trust store when the client verifies the broker server certificate
It points to the client private key file presented to the broker during a mutual TLS handshake when the client authenticates to the gateway
It points to a root Certificate Authority certificate used instead of the certificate in the default trust store when verifying the broker
It points to a PKCS12 keystore containing both the client certificate and key used during a mutual TLS handshake with the Camunda broker gateway

d.text: Incorrect. The property takes a string path to a single root CA certificate used by the client to verify the broker; it is not a PKCS12 keystore holding the client side of a mutual TLS pair.

b.text: Incorrect. ca-certificate-path is for trusting the broker certificate via a root CA; it does not configure the client private key presented during mutual TLS authentication.

a.text: Incorrect. The property replaces the default trust certificate rather than merging additional CA bundles into the JVM default store, and its value is a single file path.

c.text: Correct. camunda.client.ca-certificate-path is a string property whose value is the path to a root CA certificate used in place of the certificate in the default trust store.

explanation: camunda.client.ca-certificate-path lets the Camunda 8.8 Spring Boot starter trust a broker whose certificate is signed by a private root CA by pointing at a CA certificate file used instead of the certificate in the default trust store. The default value is null.

scenario: Nimbus Healthcare runs a Spring Boot client on Camunda 8.8 against a self-managed cluster authenticated via OIDC. The platform team must set the resource value that the access token returned by the authorization server is required to be valid for, so the broker accepts 4500 jobs per hour from the worker fleet without rejecting the token.
question: Which Spring Boot property names the resource that the OIDC access token must be valid for in a Camunda 8.8 client configuration?
