options.text: Set camunda.client.auth.scope to the resource identifier the access token must be valid for when authenticating the Camunda client against the OIDC provider
Set camunda.client.auth.client-id to the resource identifier the access token must be valid for when authenticating the Camunda client against the OIDC provider
Set camunda.client.auth.audience to the resource identifier the access token must be valid for when authenticating the Camunda client against the OIDC provider
Set camunda.client.auth.resource to the resource identifier the access token must be valid for when authenticating the Camunda client against the OIDC provider

c.text: Correct. camunda.client.auth.audience names the resource the access token must be valid for; a default is set by camunda.client.mode saas combined with camunda.client.auth.method oidc.

a.text: Incorrect. Scope describes which permissions an access token carries; the resource the token must be valid for is configured via camunda.client.auth.audience in the Spring Boot starter.

d.text: Incorrect. The documented key is named audience, not resource; the Camunda 8.8 Spring Boot starter does not expose a camunda.client.auth.resource key under client auth.

b.text: Incorrect. camunda.client.auth.client-id identifies the client when requesting a token from the authorization server; the target resource is named via camunda.client.auth.audience.

explanation: camunda.client.auth.audience holds the resource value that the OIDC access token must be valid for. A default is applied when camunda.client.mode is saas and camunda.client.auth.method is oidc. The client-id property identifies the requesting client, not the protected resource.

scenario: Keystone Insurance runs a Spring Boot fleet on Camunda 8.8 that processes 5000 jobs per hour and authenticates to an OIDC provider via a signed JWT client assertion instead of a client secret. The keystore on disk holds several keys, so operations needs to know which property selects a specific signing key by its alias for the client assertion.
question: Which property selects a specific signing key from the keystore by its alias for the OIDC client assertion in Camunda 8.8?

options.text: Set camunda.client.auth.client-assertion.signing-key to the alias of the key that signs the client assertion JWT presented to the OIDC provider during access token requests
Set camunda.client.auth.client-assertion.alias to the alias of the key that signs the client assertion JWT presented to the OIDC provider during access token requests
Set camunda.client.auth.client-assertion.keystore-key-alias to the alias of the key that signs the client assertion JWT presented to the OIDC provider during access token requests
Set camunda.client.auth.client-assertion.keystore-key-password to the alias of the key that signs the client assertion JWT presented to the OIDC provider during access token requests

b.text: Incorrect. The documented key for selecting the signing key alias is keystore-key-alias under camunda.client.auth.client-assertion; no shorter alias property is exposed at this level.

a.text: Incorrect. There is no signing-key key under camunda.client.auth.client-assertion; the documented property for choosing the signing key by alias is keystore-key-alias.

c.text: Correct. camunda.client.auth.client-assertion.keystore-key-alias selects the keystore alias of the key that signs the client assertion; if not set, the first alias from the keystore is used.

scenario: Delta SaaS upgrades a Camunda 8.8 Spring Boot service from a legacy 8.4 codebase that still configures zeebe.client.connection-mode for its 7800 jobs per day. The team must migrate the YAML key to the documented 8.8 replacement listed in the deprecated zeebe.client table.
question: Which property does the 8.8 reference list as the replacement for the legacy zeebe.client.connection-mode key?

options.text: The 8.8 reference replaces zeebe.client.connection-mode with camunda.client.mode under the unified Spring Boot starter properties table for 8.8 services.
The 8.8 reference replaces zeebe.client.connection-mode with camunda.client.connection.mode under the unified Spring Boot starter properties table for 8.8 services.
The 8.8 reference replaces zeebe.client.connection-mode with camunda.client.cloud.mode under the unified Spring Boot starter properties table for 8.8 services.
The 8.8 reference replaces zeebe.client.connection-mode with camunda.client.auth.mode under the unified Spring Boot starter properties table for 8.8 services.

a.text: Correct. The zeebe.client table maps connection-mode to camunda.client.mode with the hint that client modes are now available under the unified prefix.

c.text: Incorrect. There is no camunda.client.cloud.mode entry; the client mode selector lives directly under camunda.client in the 8.8 reference.

d.text: Incorrect. camunda.client.auth.method is a separate enum for authentication; the connection-mode replacement is camunda.client.mode in 8.8.

b.text: Incorrect. There is no camunda.client.connection subsection; the documented replacement key is camunda.client.mode in the unified reference.

explanation: zeebe.client.connection-mode migrates to camunda.client.mode in 8.8. The hint column adds that client modes are now available under the unified camunda.client prefix.

docs.title: Camunda Spring Boot starter properties reference

scenario: Keystone Logistics runs Camunda 8.8 with 12 Spring Boot services that issue 18000 calls per hour and still defines zeebe.client.broker.rest-address. The platform team must adopt the documented 8.8 replacement key listed in the deprecated zeebe.client.broker section before the next release.
question: Which property does the 8.8 reference list as the replacement for the legacy zeebe.client.broker.rest-address key?

options.text: The 8.8 reference replaces zeebe.client.broker.rest-address with camunda.client.cloud.rest-address at the unified camunda.client level of the Spring Boot starter.
The 8.8 reference replaces zeebe.client.broker.rest-address with camunda.client.broker.rest-address at the unified camunda.client level of the Spring Boot starter.
The 8.8 reference replaces zeebe.client.broker.rest-address with camunda.client.rest-address at the unified camunda.client level of the Spring Boot starter.
The 8.8 reference replaces zeebe.client.broker.rest-address with camunda.client.grpc-address at the unified camunda.client level of the Spring Boot starter.

c.text: Correct. The zeebe.client.broker table maps rest-address to camunda.client.rest-address as the documented 8.8 replacement key under the unified prefix.

a.text: Incorrect. The camunda.client.cloud section does not list a rest-address entry; rest-address sits directly under camunda.client in 8.8.

b.text: Incorrect. The unified replacement drops the broker segment, so camunda.client.broker.rest-address is not in the 8.8 reference.

d.text: Incorrect. The gRPC endpoint maps to camunda.client.grpc-address; the REST address replacement is camunda.client.rest-address.

explanation: zeebe.client.broker.rest-address migrates to camunda.client.rest-address in the 8.8 reference. The unified property exposes one REST gateway endpoint for all client interaction with Camunda.

docs.title: Camunda Spring Boot starter properties reference

scenario: Aurora Bank runs 12 Spring Boot job workers that authenticate against Camunda 8 SaaS. The team upgrades to the Camunda 8.8 Spring Boot starter and finds that several deprecated zeebe.client.cloud.* properties must be renamed to keep the existing OAuth client credentials working for 24000 jobs per day.
question: Which mapping correctly reflects the documented replacement for the deprecated zeebe.client.cloud.client-id property?

options.text: Rename zeebe.client.cloud.client-id to camunda.client.identity.client-id under the identity component group so the OAuth client is shared with Operate and Tasklist by default
Rename zeebe.client.cloud.client-id to camunda.client.auth.client-id, which the Spring Boot starter properties reference page documents as the replacement on the cloud table
Rename zeebe.client.cloud.client-id to camunda.client.security.client-id under the security namespace alongside the certificate and override authority properties
Rename zeebe.client.cloud.client-id to camunda.client.cloud.client-id, keeping the cloud namespace because Spring Boot binds the legacy property name through configuration aliases

b.text: Correct. The properties reference page lists camunda.client.auth.client-id (env CAMUNDA_CLIENT_AUTH_CLIENTID) as the replacement for the deprecated zeebe.client.cloud.client-id property in the zeebe.client.cloud table.

d.text: Incorrect. The replacement moves the property out of the cloud namespace into camunda.client.auth; the cloud namespace keeps only properties like cluster-id and region.

a.text: Incorrect. There is no camunda.client.identity.client-id property in the reference; OAuth client configuration moved under camunda.client.auth, not under an identity namespace.

c.text: Incorrect. The security namespace covers certificate path and override authority, not client credentials; client-id moves to camunda.client.auth.client-id.

explanation: On the Spring Boot starter properties reference, the deprecated zeebe.client.cloud table maps the legacy zeebe.client.cloud.client-id to camunda.client.auth.client-id. The cloud namespace retains only cluster-id, region, and domain replacements; OAuth credentials live under camunda.client.auth.

docs.title: Camunda Spring Boot starter properties reference

scenario: An insurer's platform team migrates 7 Spring Boot services from the Camunda 8.7 starter to 8.8 and prepares a configuration cutover for 18000 instances per day. They must remove old zeebe.client.cloud properties and confirm exactly which entry from the table no longer has a direct replacement in the new camunda.client namespace.
question: Which deprecated property from the zeebe.client.cloud table has no direct replacement and uses a Hint that the Zeebe client URL is now configured as an HTTP/HTTPS URL?

options.text: The legacy property zeebe.client.cloud.auth-url has no replacement in the table and the Hint states the Zeebe client URL is now configured as an HTTP/HTTPS URL instead of a separate auth-url property
The legacy property zeebe.client.cloud.base-url has no replacement in the table and the Hint states the Zeebe client URL is now configured as an HTTP/HTTPS URL instead of a separate base-url property
The legacy property zeebe.client.cloud.port has no replacement in the table and the Hint states the Zeebe client URL is now configured as an HTTP/HTTPS URL instead of a separate port property
The legacy property zeebe.client.cloud.region has no replacement in the table and the Hint states the Zeebe client URL is now configured as an HTTP/HTTPS URL instead of a separate region property

c.text: Correct. In the zeebe.client.cloud table, the zeebe.client.cloud.port row has an empty Replacement column and a Hint that states the Zeebe client URL is now configured as an HTTP/HTTPS URL, so the port is part of the URL rather than a separate property.

b.text: Incorrect. zeebe.client.cloud.base-url has a documented replacement of camunda.client.cloud.domain, so it does not match the no-replacement Hint about the HTTP/HTTPS URL.

a.text: Incorrect. zeebe.client.cloud.auth-url maps to camunda.client.auth.token-url in the table, so it is not the row without a replacement.

d.text: Incorrect. zeebe.client.cloud.region has the documented replacement camunda.client.cloud.region in the table, so it is not the entry without a direct replacement.

explanation: In the zeebe.client.cloud deprecation table, only the zeebe.client.cloud.port row has an empty Replacement; its Hint explains the Zeebe client URL is now configured as an HTTP/HTTPS URL so the port is embedded in the URL. Other rows map to camunda.client.* equivalents.

docs.title: Camunda Spring Boot starter properties reference

scenario: Lattice Logistics runs a Spring Boot job worker that polls Zeebe for 9000 jobs per day. After upgrading to the Camunda 8.8 starter, the operations engineer needs to migrate the deprecated zeebe.client.job.poll-interval and zeebe.client.job.timeout properties so the worker keeps the same long polling timeout of 30 seconds.
question: Which renaming pair matches the documented replacements for the deprecated zeebe.client.job properties?

options.text: Use camunda.client.job.poll-interval as the replacement for zeebe.client.job.poll-interval, and camunda.client.job.timeout as the replacement for zeebe.client.job.timeout under a job namespace
Use camunda.client.message.poll-interval as the replacement for zeebe.client.job.poll-interval, and camunda.client.message.time-to-live as the replacement for zeebe.client.job.timeout
Use camunda.client.worker.defaults.poll-interval as the replacement for zeebe.client.job.poll-interval, and camunda.client.worker.defaults.timeout as the replacement for zeebe.client.job.timeout

Use camunda.client.worker.poll-interval as the replacement for zeebe.client.job.poll-interval, and camunda.client.worker.timeout as the replacement for zeebe.client.job.timeout without a defaults level

c.text: Correct. The zeebe.client.job table lists camunda.client.worker.defaults.poll-interval (env CAMUNDA_CLIENT_WORKER_DEFAULTS_POLLINTERVAL) as the replacement for zeebe.client.job.poll-interval, and camunda.client.worker.defaults.timeout as the replacement for zeebe.client.job.timeout.

a.text: Incorrect. The replacement namespace is camunda.client.worker.defaults, not camunda.client.job; there is no camunda.client.job group in the table.

d.text: Incorrect. The documented properties live under camunda.client.worker.defaults, not directly under camunda.client.worker; the defaults segment is part of the property path.

b.text: Incorrect. The zeebe.client.message group maps to camunda.client.max-message-size and camunda.client.message-time-to-live; job worker properties move under camunda.client.worker.defaults instead.

explanation: The zeebe.client.job deprecation table maps poll-interval and timeout to camunda.client.worker.defaults.poll-interval and camunda.client.worker.defaults.timeout. The new namespace is the worker defaults section under the unified camunda.client configuration.

docs.title: Camunda Spring Boot starter properties reference

scenario: A fintech platform publishes 12000 messages per day from a Spring Boot service to Camunda 8 message catch events. While moving to the 8.8 starter, the team must migrate the deprecated zeebe.client.message properties and keep both the max message size of 4 MB and a time-to-live of 1 hour aligned with the documented replacements.
question: Which renaming pair matches the documented replacements for the deprecated zeebe.client.message properties?

options.text: Use camunda.client.message.max-message-size as the replacement for zeebe.client.message.max-message-size, and camunda.client.message.time-to-live as the replacement for zeebe.client.message.time-to-live under a message group

Use camunda.client.worker.defaults.max-message-size as the replacement for zeebe.client.message.max-message-size, and camunda.client.worker.defaults.message-time-to-live for zeebe.client.message.time-to-live
Use camunda.client.cloud.max-message-size as the replacement for zeebe.client.message.max-message-size, and camunda.client.cloud.message-time-to-live for zeebe.client.message.time-to-live
Use camunda.client.max-message-size as the replacement for zeebe.client.message.max-message-size, and camunda.client.message-time-to-live as the replacement for zeebe.client.message.time-to-live

d.text: Correct. The zeebe.client.message deprecation table lists camunda.client.max-message-size (env CAMUNDA_CLIENT_MAXMESSAGESIZE) as the replacement for zeebe.client.message.max-message-size, and camunda.client.message-time-to-live as the replacement for zeebe.client.message.time-to-live.

a.text: Incorrect. The replacements live directly under camunda.client (as max-message-size and message-time-to-live), not under a camunda.client.message subgroup.

b.text: Incorrect. Worker defaults cover properties like name, type, poll-interval, and timeout; max message size and message TTL move under camunda.client, not under camunda.client.worker.defaults.

c.text: Incorrect. The cloud namespace covers cluster-id, region, and domain; message size and TTL do not belong to camunda.client.cloud in the deprecation tables.

explanation: The zeebe.client.message deprecation table maps max-message-size to camunda.client.max-message-size and time-to-live to camunda.client.message-time-to-live. Both replacements sit directly under the camunda.client root rather than within a message subgroup.

docs.title: Camunda Spring Boot starter properties reference

scenario: Aurora Bank models a customer onboarding process in Camunda 8.8 that handles 8000 customers per month. The BPMN file has only one starting point and the process is launched via a single upstream API channel for every new applicant. The team must pick the start event symbol that fits a clearly standard entry point with one trigger source.
question: Which BPMN start event symbol fits a process definition that has only one clearly standard starting point?

options.text: Use a signal start event; it starts several process instances at once whenever a broadcast signal arrives at the workflow engine.
Use a timer start event; it automatically starts process instances on a time controlled schedule rather than from a single upstream API call.
Use a message start event; it differentiates several start events whenever a process has more than one distinct trigger source per definition.
Use a none start event; it indicates the typical starting point and only one such start event can exist in a single process definition.

d.text: Correct. The best practice table marks the none start event as the choice when you have only one start event or one which is clearly standard. The page also notes that only one such start event can exist in a process definition, which matches the single upstream API channel described for the onboarding flow.

c.text: Incorrect. The message start event is the choice when you have to differentiate several start events in one process. With only one clearly standard entry point on the BPMN file, the table points to the none start event instead.

b.text: Incorrect. The timer start event is reserved for automatically starting instances time controlled, not for a single API triggered onboarding. The standard entry point in the scenario is the none start event according to the table.

a.text: Incorrect. The signal start event is reserved for the rare case of starting several process instances at once via a broadcast. A single upstream API call to one standard entry point matches the none start event row of the table.

explanation: The routing events best practice lists the none start event as the choice when there is only one clearly standard starting point and notes that only one such event can exist in a process definition.

scenario: Catalyst Bank runs a Camunda 8.8 order-to-cash process that handles 750 orders per day and waits at an intermediate message event for payment confirmation. The team chose not to model a user task because the accountant prefers a custom search screen listing orders awaiting payment. When the accountant clicks Paid, the right waiting instance must move on.
question: Which Camunda 8 mechanism routes the accountant Paid click to the correct waiting order process instance?

options.text: Display the matching waiting order in Camunda Tasklist; the accountant claims the inbox item and Tasklist completes the awaiting message event on the affected instance.
Publish a signal from the custom screen; the workflow engine broadcasts the signal to every waiting order instance and resumes each of them at the same time.
Configure an interrupting boundary timer on the waiting activity; the workflow engine resumes the right order automatically once the accountant logs into the search screen.
Build a custom search screen for the accountant; on Paid click the application calls the Zeebe API to correlate a message to the affected waiting order process instance.

a.text: Incorrect. The best practice notes that these scenarios are not directly supported by Camunda Tasklist. A Tasklist inbox does not carry the Paid click; the page recommends a custom screen plus an API call from the application to correlate the user message instead.

d.text: Correct. The best practice describes a custom search screen for the accountant that observes waiting orders. When the accountant hits Paid, custom code uses the Zeebe API to correlate the user generated message to the affected waiting process instance, which fits the Catalyst Bank flow.

c.text: Incorrect. A boundary timer fires on time elapsing, not on a user click. The page documents a custom screen plus an API correlation call from the application, not a time controlled boundary on the activity, to deliver the accountant trigger.

b.text: Incorrect. The page calls out correlating the user message to the affected instance, not broadcasting to all waiting orders. A signal would resume every waiting instance and would not target the single order that just received its payment from the accountant.

explanation: When a human user replaces a task with an active trigger, the best practice pairs a custom search screen with API code that correlates the user generated message to the affected waiting process instance; Camunda Tasklist does not directly support this pattern.

scenario: A retailer's order process running on Camunda 8.8 handles 4200 orders per day and must wait for payment-received signals while still supporting boundary cancellation events. A modeler must select the intermediate event types that are documented as supported for execution and avoid the one event type that is not yet supported.
question: Which intermediate event from the routing-events table is documented as not yet supported for execution in Camunda 8?

options.text: The timer intermediate event is documented as not yet supported in Camunda 8 in the intermediate-events table; the table recommends using a conditional intermediate event with a polling worker instead
The receive task is documented as not yet supported in Camunda 8 in the intermediate-events table; the table recommends using a message intermediate event with a boundary timer instead
The signal intermediate event is documented as not yet supported in Camunda 8 in the intermediate-events table; the table recommends using a message event to route to all waiting instances instead

The conditional intermediate event is documented as not yet supported in Camunda 8 in the routing-events intermediate-events table, while message, receive task, timer, and signal events are all marked supported

d.text: Correct. The intermediate-events table on the routing-events-to-processes page lists message event, receive task, timer event, and signal event with a check mark for Supported for Execution. The conditional event row states Not yet supported in Camunda 8.

c.text: Incorrect. The signal intermediate event is shown with a check mark for execution support and is documented for routing an incoming signal to all process instances waiting for it.

b.text: Incorrect. The receive task is shown with a check mark for execution support and is documented as an alternative to message events for leveraging boundary events such as timeouts.

a.text: Incorrect. The timer intermediate event is shown with a check mark for execution support and is documented for making a process instance wait for a certain point in time.

explanation: In the intermediate-events table, message events, receive tasks, timer events, and signal events are all supported for execution in Camunda 8. Only the conditional intermediate event is documented as Not yet supported in Camunda 8.

scenario: A bank's accounts team checks new payments on a Camunda 8.8 order process handling 8000 orders per day. The accountant actively monitors the bank account rather than waiting for a Tasklist entry, and the modeler needs to decide how to involve the accountant for the order-paid signal.
question: Which design matches the documented guidance for handling messages sent by a user when the user actively monitors an external system?

options.text: Model a conditional intermediate event watching a paid process variable and let the accountant change the variable directly through Operate, relying entirely on the Operate UI without any custom screen or correlation code

Model a signal intermediate event waiting for an order-paid signal, with Tasklist broadcasting the signal to every waiting order instance when the accountant marks payments as received in a Tasklist view

Model a standard Camunda user task for the accountant in Tasklist, then let the Tasklist completion automatically publish the order-paid message to all waiting process instances at once with no extra code

Model an intermediate message catch event waiting for an order-paid message, build a custom search screen outside Tasklist, and call the message-correlation API from custom code when the accountant hits Paid

d.text: Correct. The Handling messages sent by a user section says the process should wait for a message rather than model a user task because the user is not actively triggered. The accountant uses a custom search screen built outside Tasklist to find orders waiting for payment, and on Paid, custom API code must correlate the user's message to the affected process instances.

c.text: Incorrect. The section explicitly states that a Camunda user task is not appropriate here because the user does not want to observe a task in Tasklist; instead the process waits for a user-generated message.

b.text: Incorrect. The documented design uses a message intermediate event with manual correlation, not a signal intermediate event broadcast from Tasklist.

a.text: Incorrect. The page does not recommend a conditional intermediate event combined with Operate variable edits; it describes a message catch event correlated by custom code from a custom screen.

explanation: When the human user wants to actively trigger an event from an external view rather than work a Tasklist entry, the process should use an intermediate message catch event. A custom search screen lets the user find waiting instances, and custom API code must correlate the user's message to the affected process instances.

scenario: Helix Healthcare reviews 1800 integration calls per week in a Camunda 8.8 order process. The architect must record the documented default BPMN element to model both synchronous and asynchronous request/response interactions before the team starts modeling the next release of the patient onboarding flow.
question: Which BPMN element does Camunda recommend as the default for synchronous and asynchronous request/response calls?

options.text: The send task is the documented default for both synchronous and asynchronous request/response calls because its envelope symbol makes integrations explicit on the diagram
The service task is the documented default for both synchronous and asynchronous request/response calls because it removes visual clutter from the diagram for stakeholders
A send task followed by a receive task is the documented default for both synchronous and asynchronous request/response calls in the integration patterns guidance
A message intermediate throw event paired with a receive task is the documented default for both synchronous and asynchronous request/response calls in Camunda 8.8

b.text: Correct. The Service task vs. send/receive task combo section recommends using the service task as the default option for synchronous and asynchronous request/response calls because it removes visual clutter for stakeholders.

a.text: Incorrect. Send tasks are recommended for fire-and-forget asynchronous messages, not as the default for request/response calls.

c.text: Incorrect. The send+receive task combo is recommended only when responses take longer (more than seconds) or when a self-controlled correlation ID is required.

d.text: Incorrect. The integration patterns chapter compares service tasks and send/receive tasks; it does not promote a throw+receive event pair as the default.

explanation: Camunda 8 documents the service task as the default for both synchronous and asynchronous request/response interactions because it keeps the diagram free of visual clutter while still letting the engine wait for the response.

scenario: Lattice Bank uses Camunda 8.8 to wait on 900 jobs per hour inside a service task that holds open for asynchronous responses. To complete the open service task when the response message arrives, the integration glue must call the Zeebe API with the right identifier. The team needs to document which identifier the third-party service must echo back.
question: Which identifier must the third-party service return so the worker can complete the held-open service task?

options.text: The BPMN process definition id, which the worker passes to the Zeebe API in order to complete the open service task once the asynchronous response is received and parsed
The Zeebe-internal job instance key, which is the workflow-engine identifier passed to the worker and required by the API call that completes the open service task on receipt
The correlation key configured on the receive task, which is the value Zeebe uses to match the inbound response message to the open service task at completion
The element id of the service task in the BPMN XML, which the worker submits to the Zeebe API so the open service task can be completed once the response is parsed

b.text: Correct. The docs state you can keep a service task open and complete it later, but completing it requires the Zeebe-internal job instance key, which the third party can echo back as part of the response.

a.text: Incorrect. The process definition id identifies the deployed process model, not a specific job; the engine cannot complete an open service task with it.

c.text: Incorrect. Correlation keys apply to message catch elements such as receive tasks, not to completing a service task job; that path uses the job key.

d.text: Incorrect. The BPMN element id is metadata about the model and is not the identifier the Zeebe API uses to complete an outstanding job.

explanation: To complete an open service task held for an asynchronous response, the integration must call the Zeebe API with the job instance key. The key is an internal engine identifier that can either be passed to the third party for echo back or looked up via a correlation table.

scenario: Forge Bank integrates a credit bureau in a Camunda 8.8 loan process for 2600 applications per day. The bureau response arrives between 30 seconds and 4 hours later, and compliance requires the team to use a self-generated UUID as the correlation key for every request so they can audit retries and reuse the key after process restarts.
question: Which BPMN modelling option does the integration patterns guidance recommend for this long-running, self-controlled correlation case?

options.text: A message intermediate throw event followed by a service task that polls the bureau using the workflow engine job key as the correlation identifier between request and reply messages
A single service task that waits on the bureau reply for hours and completes through the Zeebe job key passed to the bureau as a piece of opaque payload metadata in every call
A send task on its own that publishes the request to the bureau and treats the reply as fire and forget without any matching receive element later in the diagram for the bureau answer
A send task that issues the request, followed by a receive task that consumes the bureau reply using a self-generated UUID as the correlation key under the team is full control

d.text: Correct. The integration patterns table tells teams to use send + receive tasks when the response will take more than a few seconds or when the correlation ID must be self-controlled, which matches the credit bureau scenario.

b.text: Incorrect. The service-task option is documented for replies within milliseconds and relies on the Zeebe job key; it is not recommended for multi-hour responses or self-controlled correlation IDs.

c.text: Incorrect. Fire and forget through a lone send task does not let the process consume the bureau reply, so it cannot satisfy the auditable request/response requirement.

a.text: Incorrect. The guidance compares service tasks and send/receive task combos for request/response, not a throw event plus polling service task.

explanation: Send + receive task combos are recommended when responses take longer than a few seconds or when teams need a self-controlled correlation ID. The team owns the UUID and can replay it after restarts, but must model timeouts and retries explicitly because they are not built in.

scenario: Apex Marketplace models a Camunda 8.8 order flow that waits up to PT2H for a fraud-check reply. The fraud team must be able to cancel the wait if the customer cancels their order during that 2 hours, so the modeller has to choose between a receive task and a receive intermediate event for the wait step, knowing both share execution semantics.
question: Which capability is available on a receive task that is not available on a receive intermediate event?

options.text: The ability to participate in message correlation against a buffered message in Zeebe, which the receive intermediate event variant cannot do in Camunda 8.8 environments
The ability to be invoked by the Zeebe message API in the first place; receive intermediate events cannot be triggered by published messages in Camunda 8.8 deployments at all today
The ability to attach boundary events such as a cancellation message catch, which let the modeller cancel the wait when the customer cancels their order during the fraud check
The ability to run with identical execution semantics to a send/receive task pair, which is reserved for tasks only and not available on receive events under Camunda 8.8

c.text: Correct. The Tasks vs. events section states that only tasks can carry boundary events; this is the one functional difference and it is what enables the cancel-during-wait pattern shown.

a.text: Incorrect. Message correlation and buffering work for both receive tasks and receive intermediate events; this is not what distinguishes them.

d.text: Incorrect. The docs explicitly state the execution semantics of send and receive events are identical to send and receive tasks.

b.text: Incorrect. Receive events can absolutely be triggered by message correlation in Zeebe; they just cannot carry boundary events.

explanation: Send/receive events and send/receive tasks share execution semantics in Camunda 8. The single documented difference is that only tasks can carry boundary events, which lets you model cancellation of the wait without restructuring the diagram.

scenario: Zenith Retailer runs a Camunda 8.8 fulfilment flow that processes 1200 orders per day and must react to a customer cancellation message at any point across a six-step section of the process. The architect wants one model where the happy path stays clean and a single cancellation handler can fire whenever the cancellation message arrives during those steps.
question: Which response-handling pattern does the integration patterns guidance recommend for this case?

options.text: An event-based gateway placed before each step, which waits for either the normal continuation message or the cancellation message at every stage of the fulfilment flow on its own
A receive task with two boundary message events on the happy path, which catches the cancellation message attached to the active step of the fulfilment flow at the time of arrival
A payload field on the receive task followed by an XOR gateway, which routes the flow toward the cancellation path whenever the payload field is set during the fulfilment flow
An event subprocess on the parent scope, which gets activated whenever the cancellation message is received while the process is still active anywhere in that section of the flow

d.text: Correct. The docs introduce event subprocesses as the option to use when you need bigger scopes that can react to events arriving any time, calling out cancellation handling across a chunk of the process as the canonical use case.

b.text: Incorrect. Boundary events are limited (the guidance suggests at most two), and they attach to a single task, not to a multi-step chunk of the process.

a.text: Incorrect. Event-based gateways are documented as something to try to avoid because they are hard for non-BPMN audiences to read, and they sit on the happy path rather than covering a scope.

c.text: Incorrect. The payload + XOR gateway pattern works well for a single response choice, but it hides the happy path and does not cover a multi-step scope that can be cancelled anytime.

explanation: Event subprocesses are recommended when you need a larger scope that can react to events arriving at any time, such as a cancellation that may interrupt several activities. They keep the happy path clean while still handling deviations as messages arrive.

scenario: Granite Insurer prepares a Camunda 8.8 review checklist for 12 BPMN models, each handling around 5 messages per integration. The architect must record which of the four response-handling patterns the integration guidance explicitly recommends to avoid, so that reviewers can flag it during a 2 hours walkthrough scheduled next week.
question: Which of the four response-handling patterns does the integration guidance recommend reviewers try to avoid?

options.text: The payload field plus XOR gateway pattern, which the guidance recommends teams try to avoid in every model that handles more than one type of response message reply
The receive task with boundary message events pattern, which the guidance recommends teams try to avoid even though it keeps the happy path of the flow easy to see on the diagram
The event subprocess pattern, which the guidance recommends teams try to avoid because once the flow moves into the subprocess it cannot easily return to the typical flow
The event-based gateway pattern, which the guidance recommends teams try to avoid because it is hard to understand for non-BPMN professionals and it also hides the happy path of the flow

d.text: Correct. The Handling different response messages table assigns the recommendation "Try to avoid" only to the event-based gateway, calling out that it is hard to understand for non-BPMN audiences and hides the happy path.

b.text: Incorrect. The receive-task-with-boundary-events row recommends using it when message types should be visible on the diagram, capped at two boundary events; it is not the one to avoid.

a.text: Incorrect. The payload + XOR gateway row is recommended for many response types or when the response can be treated as a result, not avoided.

c.text: Incorrect. Event subprocesses are recommended for bigger scopes that can react to events at any time; the docs flag a known limitation but do not place them in the avoid bucket.

explanation: In the Handling different response messages table, only the event-based gateway carries the "Try to avoid" recommendation because it is hard to understand for non-BPMN audiences and obscures the happy path. The other three patterns are recommended in their respective contexts.

scenario: Beacon Bank integrates a document storage service in Camunda 8.8 that cannot push callbacks because of firewall constraints, so the adapter polls every 60 seconds for 800 documents per day. The architect needs the main business process to stay clean of these technical details and only invoke the polling adapter as a single step in the main diagram.
question: Which BPMN construct does the integration guidance recommend for hiding the polling adapter from the main process?

options.text: A call activity in the main process that invokes a separate adapter process model holding the polling and persistent waiting logic of the document storage integration in its own diagram
A boundary timer event attached to the user task in the main process that fires every 60 seconds to poll the document storage service from inside the main business process model
A multi-instance subprocess on the main process that performs the polling inline so the document storage integration logic stays visible in the main process diagram for reviewers
An event-based gateway in the main process that polls the document storage service in line and routes the flow based on the response message received from the storage service

a.text: Correct. The Hiding technical complexity behind call activities section recommends moving complicated service-integration technicalities into a separate process model and invoking it via a call activity from the main business process.

c.text: Incorrect. A multi-instance subprocess keeps the polling logic inline and therefore does not hide the technical detail from the main business process.

d.text: Incorrect. The chapter does not recommend an in-line event-based gateway for polling; in fact it recommends teams try to avoid event-based gateways in general.

b.text: Incorrect. A boundary timer event keeps the polling on the main diagram and is not the documented pattern for isolating adapter technicalities.

explanation: When service-integration details such as periodic polling and persistent waiting get complicated, the integration patterns chapter recommends extracting them into their own process model and invoking it from the main business process via a call activity so the main diagram stays clean.

scenario: Vanta Telco integrates a Camunda 8.8 billing process with three downstream services and routes 25000 requests per day across HTTP endpoints and AMQP queues. The architects must classify the integration pattern when the BPMN process model calls a downstream service over HTTP and blocks until the response returns to the caller in the same call.
question: Which communication pattern matches a process that calls a downstream service over HTTP and blocks until the response arrives?

options.text: Asynchronous request/response; the sender pushes the request through a message broker and waits for the response message right after the original send returns from the broker.
Event-driven publish/subscribe; the sender emits an event into a topic and never expects a logically correlated response from the consumers that subscribed to the original event.
Asynchronous messages or events; the response is treated as a separate message that may arrive hours after the original request, not on the same blocking call to the service.
Synchronous request/response; the sender uses a synchronous protocol such as HTTP and blocks for the result on the same call that was issued to the downstream service endpoint.

c.text: Incorrect. The page describes asynchronous messages or events for cases where the peer service takes a long time and the response arrives much later as a separate message; HTTP blocking calls instead match the synchronous request/response row of the integration table.

a.text: Incorrect. Asynchronous request/response uses a message broker such as AMQP or JMS for the request and waits for a separate response message. The Vanta call over HTTP that blocks for the result instead lands in the synchronous request/response row.

d.text: Correct. The page describes synchronous request/response as using a synchronous protocol like HTTP and blocking for the result on the same call. That is exactly the Vanta Telco call pattern over HTTP described in the integration scenario for the billing process.

b.text: Incorrect. The page does not list a separate publish/subscribe row in its three pattern table; it groups event-driven traffic into asynchronous messages or events. Blocking HTTP request/response calls do not fit that asynchronous classification at all.

explanation: The service integration patterns page lists three options: synchronous request/response over a synchronous protocol like HTTP, asynchronous request/response over message brokers such as AMQP, and asynchronous messages or events for long delayed or event-driven responses.

scenario: Lumen Brokerage runs a Camunda 8.8 quoting process with 9000 requests per day where downstream pricing returns responses in 200 ms after the request. The team keeps a single service task open during the round trip and must complete that service task from a job worker once the asynchronous pricing response message arrives back from the service.
question: Which Zeebe identifier does the worker need in order to complete a service task that was held open for an asynchronous response?

options.text: The BPMN process ID from the diagram; the worker passes it to the pricing service so the workflow engine can match the open service task by its element identifier on the BPMN file.
The job instance key from Zeebe; the worker passes it to the pricing service or keeps it in a lookup table so it can complete the open service task once the response arrives back.
The element instance key of the activity; the worker passes it to the pricing service so the engine can resolve the open service task without relying on the internal job identifier scheme.
The process instance key from Zeebe; the worker passes it to the pricing service so the gateway can locate and complete the open service task as soon as the response arrives at the gateway.

b.text: Correct. The page states that to complete the held open service task you need the job instance key from Zeebe and recommends either passing it through to the third party service or building a lookup table that maps your own correlation data to the right job key for completion.

d.text: Incorrect. The page reserves the job instance key, not the process instance key, as the value needed to complete the open service task. The process instance key identifies the running process flow rather than the individual job awaiting completion in the engine.

a.text: Incorrect. The BPMN process ID identifies the deployed process definition on the diagram, not a single open service task instance in the engine. The page specifies the internal job instance key as the value required to complete the held open service task.

c.text: Incorrect. The page names the job instance key as the value the worker must hold to complete the open service task, not the element instance key of the activity. The element instance key is a different internal identifier in the workflow engine model.

explanation: To complete a service task held open for an asynchronous response, the worker needs the Zeebe job instance key; the page recommends either passing this internal ID to the third party service or maintaining a lookup table that maps user controlled correlation data to the job key.

scenario: Lumen Insurance models a Camunda 8.8 claims process that must react if no reply arrives within 4 hours for 8000 claims per day, and the modelers debate whether to use send and receive tasks or the equivalent throwing and catching message events for the request/response interaction.
question: Which capability is available only on send and receive tasks but not on send and receive events in Camunda 8.8 BPMN?

options.text: Send and receive events are the only elements that support the message buffering mechanism built into Zeebe in 8.8
Send and receive events are the only elements that support timeout handling for unfulfilled message correlations in 8.8
Boundary events can be attached to send and receive events but not to send and receive tasks in the Camunda 8.8 modeler
Boundary events can be attached to send and receive tasks but not to send and receive events in the Camunda 8.8 modeler

c.text: Incorrect. The guide is explicit that only tasks accept boundary events, so attaching a boundary timer or boundary message to a send or receive event is not part of the modeling capability in Camunda 8.8.

b.text: Incorrect. Send and receive events and send and receive tasks share identical execution semantics, so neither category has exclusive ownership of timeout handling for unfulfilled correlations in 8.8.

d.text: Correct. Only tasks can carry boundary events, which is the small difference noted in the tasks vs events comparison, making it easy to model cancelling a wait for a message after some timeout.

a.text: Incorrect. Message buffering is a property of the Zeebe message concept used by send and receive elements in general, so it is not a feature unique to events versus tasks in 8.8.

explanation: Send and receive tasks and send and receive events have identical execution semantics, but only tasks may have boundary events attached, which is why tasks are typically preferred when a cancel-on-timeout pattern is required.

scenario: Atlas Bank integrates a Camunda 8.8 loan process that calls a legacy document storage service for 4500 customers per day. Firewall limits prevent the storage system from issuing real callbacks, so the integration developer must implement persistent polling every 60 seconds without polluting the visible business diagram with technical detail.
question: Which Camunda 8.8 modeling technique does the guide recommend to hide complex polling technicalities from the main business process?

options.text: Model the polling and waiting logic in a dedicated technical adapter process and invoke it from the main process via a call activity
Model the polling and waiting logic as a sequence of boundary timer events on the main process service task to drive the retry behavior
Model the polling and waiting logic as a multi-instance subprocess directly inside the main business process to keep technical state local
Model the polling and waiting logic as repeated send and receive task pairs visible on the top level of the main business process

a.text: Correct. The guide recommends placing the technical polling and persistent waiting in a separate adapter process and invoking it from the main process via a call activity, so the main diagram stays focused on business logic.

c.text: Incorrect. A multi-instance subprocess inside the main process exposes the polling mechanics to business readers, which contradicts the goal of hiding technical complexity behind a call activity.

b.text: Incorrect. Boundary timers on a main-process service task still surface the retry mechanics in the business diagram; the guide instead extracts the polling into a separate adapter process.

d.text: Incorrect. Repeating send and receive task pairs on the top level adds visual clutter to the business diagram, which is exactly the situation the call activity pattern is meant to prevent in the guide.

explanation: When one integration becomes technically complex, the guide recommends extracting the technicalities into a dedicated adapter process and invoking it via a call activity, so the main business process stays clean and readable.

scenario: Pinnacle Healthcare integrates a custom task application with Camunda 8.8 for 220 clinicians who process 4500 user tasks per week. The lead developer must record exactly what the Tasklist REST API offers their application as documented in the Tasklist API REST overview, before scoping the next sprint of the task application work.
question: Which set of capabilities does the Tasklist REST API document for human-centered task applications?

options.text: Query user tasks, assign users to those tasks, and complete the tasks, which are the three capabilities the Tasklist REST API overview documents for human-centered task applications today
Query user tasks, complete user tasks, and start a process instance, which the Tasklist REST API overview documents as the three capabilities offered to task applications using it today
Query user tasks, complete user tasks, and deploy a new BPMN process, which the Tasklist REST API overview documents as the three capabilities offered to task applications using it today
Query user tasks, complete user tasks, and modify a running process instance, which the Tasklist REST API overview lists as the three capabilities offered to task applications today

a.text: Correct. The Tasklist API overview describes the API as designed for task applications and lists three capabilities: query user tasks, assign users to these tasks, and complete these tasks.

c.text: Incorrect. Deploying a BPMN process is not part of the Tasklist API capability list; it belongs to other Camunda 8 APIs.

b.text: Incorrect. Starting process instances is not in the documented Tasklist API capability list; assigning users to tasks is the third capability instead.

d.text: Incorrect. Modifying running process instances is not in the documented Tasklist API capability list; the third documented capability is assigning users to tasks.

explanation: The Tasklist REST API is scoped to human-centered task work. The overview explicitly lists three capabilities for task applications: query user tasks, assign users to those tasks, and complete the tasks. Authentication is required before any of these operations.

scenario: Catalyst Retailer runs Camunda 8.8 on Camunda SaaS with 75 users on one cluster. A developer must open the Swagger UI for the Tasklist REST API at the exact URL pattern the docs publish for SaaS, using the region id and cluster id revealed under Connection information for client credentials inside the API tab of the cluster.
question: Which Swagger UI URL pattern does the Tasklist API overview document for SaaS clusters?

options.text: https://${REGION}.zeebe.camunda.io:443/${CLUSTER_ID}/swagger-ui/index.html, which the overview lists as the Swagger UI URL pattern for SaaS clusters using the region id and cluster id values
http://localhost:8080/swagger-ui/index.html, which the overview lists as the Swagger UI URL pattern for SaaS clusters using the region id and cluster id values from the API tab
https://${REGION}.tasklist.camunda.io:443/${CLUSTER_ID}/swagger-ui/index.html, which the overview lists as the Swagger UI URL pattern for SaaS clusters using the region id and cluster id values
https://${CLUSTER_ID}.tasklist.camunda.io:443/${REGION}/swagger-ui/index.html, which the overview lists as the Swagger UI URL pattern for SaaS clusters using the region id and cluster id values

c.text: Correct. The API Explorer section documents the SaaS Swagger UI URL as https://${REGION}.tasklist.camunda.io:443/${CLUSTER_ID}/swagger-ui/index.html, with region id and cluster id sourced from Connection information.

a.text: Incorrect. The tasklist subdomain is required; the documented SaaS host is tasklist.camunda.io and not zeebe.camunda.io.

d.text: Incorrect. The position of region id and cluster id is reversed; the region id is the subdomain and the cluster id sits in the path.

b.text: Incorrect. http://localhost:8080/swagger-ui/index.html is the Self-Managed URL pattern, not the SaaS one.

explanation: For SaaS, the Swagger UI is exposed at https://${REGION}.tasklist.camunda.io:443/${CLUSTER_ID}/swagger-ui/index.html. Region id and cluster id come from Connection information for the cluster client credentials under the API tab.

scenario: Vertex SaaS migrates a Camunda 8.8 onboarding process used by 250 tenants to the new Camunda Process Test introduced in 8.8. The team must align every test class on a specific unit test framework version and choose the exact @Test annotation import so that the @CamundaSpringProcessTest engine starts correctly during the build pipeline.
question: Which JUnit version and @Test annotation import are required when writing Camunda Process Test cases in Camunda 8.8?

options.text: Use JUnit 5 with the org.junit.jupiter.api.Test annotation; this combination is mandatory for every test class running Camunda Process Test in 8.8
Use JUnit 5 with the org.testng.annotations.Test annotation; Camunda Process Test in 8.8 standardizes on TestNG-compatible imports at runtime
Use JUnit 4 with the org.junit.Test annotation; the Camunda Process Test runtime in 8.8 also bridges legacy tests to the JUnit Jupiter engine automatically
Use JUnit 5 with the org.junit.Test annotation; the JUnit Jupiter runtime in 8.8 maps the legacy import to a Jupiter Test by default at startup time

c.text: Incorrect. The guide is explicit that Camunda Process Test was introduced with 8.8 and you must use JUnit 5; JUnit 4 is not the supported unit test framework for these tests in 8.8.

d.text: Incorrect. The @Test annotation that you import must be org.junit.jupiter.api.Test; the legacy org.junit.Test import from JUnit 4 is not accepted by Camunda Process Test in 8.8.

a.text: Correct. The guide states that you must use JUnit 5 in every test class and that the @Test annotation you import must be org.junit.jupiter.api.Test for Camunda Process Test.

b.text: Incorrect. Camunda Process Test relies on JUnit 5; TestNG annotations are not part of the documented setup for the @CamundaSpringProcessTest engine in 8.8 at all.

explanation: Camunda Process Test was introduced with Camunda 8.8 and requires JUnit 5 in every test class. The @Test annotation must be imported from org.junit.jupiter.api.Test, not from JUnit 4 or another test framework.

scenario: Orion Insurance writes a Camunda 8.8 happy-path test for a claims process that handles 2400 claims per day. The test drives the process from wait state to wait state, completes the human approval, and the team debates which assertions to include so the test remains stable as the process model evolves over the next 12 months.
question: Which assertion rule of thumb does the Camunda 8.8 testing guide give for driving a process from wait state to wait state?

options.text: Always assert that the expected external effects took place and carefully choose which internal process state aspects are important enough to assert
Assert every single element identifier in hasCompletedElementsInOrder for the happy path so any future model change is immediately flagged by the assertion
Assert only the final isCompleted state of the process instance and skip verification of external business service calls during the happy-path test
Assert the exact internal variable values at every wait state and ignore external business service invocations, treating internal state as the only truth

b.text: Incorrect. The guide warns against overspecifying tests; asserting every element identifier breaks the test on benign model changes and is not the documented rule of thumb for happy-path tests.

c.text: Incorrect. Skipping verification of external business service calls contradicts the rule of thumb that external effects of the process should always be asserted in a process test for stability.

d.text: Incorrect. The guide says external effects should always be asserted; internal variable state should only be asserted when it is important enough to warn about future model changes carefully.

a.text: Correct. The guide states the rule of thumb: always assert that the expected external effects of the process took place, and carefully choose which internal process state aspects deserve assertions.

explanation: The testing guide warns against overspecifying tests. Its rule of thumb is to always assert the external effects of the process, such as business services being called, while choosing carefully which internal state aspects are important enough to assert.

scenario: Nimbus Insurance runs 4200 invoices per day through an approval process in Camunda 8.8. The team writes a process test for the timeout path where 5 days elapse on the approval user task and the process auto-approves. The test must advance the engine clock without touching real wall-clock time.
question: Which process test API call advances the engine clock by 5 days inside the test method?

options.text: Call processTestContext.increaseTime(Duration.ofDays(5)) to advance the engine clock before asserting the auto-approval completion path on the instance
Call client.newClockResetCommand().offset(Duration.ofDays(5)).send().join() to move the engine clock before asserting the auto-approval completion path
Call processTestContext.advanceClock(Duration.ofDays(5)) to move the engine clock forward before asserting the auto-approval completion path on the instance
Call Thread.sleep(Duration.ofDays(5).toMillis()) to let the engine timer fire naturally before asserting the auto-approval completion path on the instance

a.text: Correct. The documented timeout test uses processTestContext.increaseTime(Duration.ofDays(5)) to advance the engine clock so the approval timer fires inside the unit test.

c.text: Incorrect. The documented helper is named increaseTime, not advanceClock; the snippet shows processTestContext.increaseTime(Duration.ofDays(5)).

d.text: Incorrect. Thread.sleep would block the test for five days of real time; the docs use processTestContext.increaseTime to move the engine clock.

b.text: Incorrect. The source uses processTestContext.increaseTime(Duration.ofDays(5)); there is no clock-reset command shown for advancing time in the test.

explanation: In Camunda 8.8 process tests, processTestContext.increaseTime advances the engine clock so timer boundary events fire inside a unit test, as shown in the documented testApprovalTimeout example using Duration.ofDays(5).

scenario: Delta Insurance writes a Camunda 8.8 process test for an invoice approval flow with 3 worker classes including an ArchiveInvoiceWorker that calls a legacy ArchiveService. The team wants the test to cover the BPMN model and its glue code without contacting the real archive system on every CI run.
question: Which scope should the Delta team include in the process test for the invoice approval flow?

options.text: Cover the BPMN model only, exercising gateway FEEL expressions but excluding all worker classes and client API code; glue code belongs to a separate worker-unit scope

Cover the BPMN model plus glue code such as worker classes, FEEL expressions, and any client API that maps data before calling the Camunda Client, while mocking workflow engine-independent business services
Cover the BPMN model and every backend system the workers call, including the real archive system, so the process test detects regressions across the full production landscape on every run
Cover the workers and FEEL expressions in isolation, without deploying the BPMN model, and rely on integration tests to verify that the model and the glue code work together

b.text: Correct. The Test scope and mocking section says the process test should cover the executable BPMN definition plus glue code that logically belongs to it: workers, FEEL expressions for gateways and IO mappings, and any client API doing data mapping before the Camunda Client call. Engine-independent business services are mocked.

a.text: Incorrect. The best practice puts workers, FEEL, and the client API inside the process test scope; only engine-independent business services like ArchiveService are mocked out.

c.text: Incorrect. The process test deliberately mocks engine-independent business services such as the real ArchiveService to stay fast and isolated; full backend coverage belongs to integration tests.

d.text: Incorrect. The process test must include the BPMN model itself; isolating workers and FEEL alone does not validate the data flowing through the process.

explanation: A Camunda 8.8 process test covers the executable BPMN definition together with the glue code that belongs to it, including workers, FEEL expressions, and client-side mapping APIs, while mocking workflow engine-independent business services.

scenario: Ironwood Bank ships an updated Camunda 8.8 invoice approval process every 2 weeks with about 250 production invoices in flight at any time. A senior tester is deciding how big the single happy-path test method should be and how it should relate to dedicated tests for the rejection, timer, and archive-error chunks.
question: Which structuring rule from the Camunda 8.8 best practices best fits this test layout?

options.text: Author the happy-path coverage exclusively as an integration test against a near-production cluster and keep process tests only for deviations such as the timer and the archive error
Author one small focused happy-path test method per service task, and rely on a single combined test method for all deviation paths so the suite stays compact and easy to maintain over time

Author one large happy-path test method that drives the whole process end to end with a consistent data flow, then add separate dedicated test methods for the rejected, timer-approved, and archive-error chunks of the same process
Author one parameterized test method per process release that exercises every path in a single run, so each release contributes exactly one test method to the suite as it grows over time

c.text: Correct. The Testing your process in chunks section recommends fully testing the happy path in one big test method to give one consistent data flow, then adding dedicated test methods for the other important chunks such as rejection, timer-driven approval, and archive error.

b.text: Incorrect. Splitting the happy path per service task contradicts the recommended one consistent data flow in one big method.

a.text: Incorrect. The happy path is part of the process-test layer with the same chunk-based strategy; integration tests are an additional verification on top.

d.text: Incorrect. The best practice does not describe a one-method-per-release parameterized approach; it organizes tests by paths through the model.

explanation: Camunda 8.8 testing best practice keeps the happy path in one big end-to-end test method, then adds dedicated test methods for each important chunk such as rejection, timer-driven approval, and archive error.

scenario: Maple Logistics implements job workers for 3 different service tasks in a Camunda 8.8 order fulfillment process that handles 1200 orders per day. A junior backend engineer asks how to structure the worker code at the application level before writing the first task handler.
question: What does the Camunda 8.8 best practice tell Maple Logistics to do when organizing job workers for these service tasks?

options.text: Write all glue code in one application that contains separate classes or functions for the different task types, think about idempotency, read or write as little data as possible from or to the process
Write one application per service task, configure a dedicated thread pool for each application, and write all process variables back on every job completion so downstream tasks see the full state
Write all glue code in one application and one shared class for every task type, ignore idempotency considerations because Zeebe guarantees exactly-once execution of every job in Camunda 8
Write one dedicated application per service task with its own deployment pipeline, share a single class across task types inside each application, and read every available process variable for safety

a.text: Correct. The Writing good workers page tells you to write all glue code in one application with separate classes or functions per task type, to think about idempotency, and to read or write as little data as possible from or to the process.

d.text: Incorrect. The best practice keeps all workers in one application, uses separate classes per task type rather than a shared one, and recommends reading as little data as possible.

c.text: Incorrect. Idempotency is explicitly something workers must think about; Zeebe does not provide exactly-once worker execution, so the best practice lists idempotency as a rule.

b.text: Incorrect. The best practice tells you not to configure thread pools yourself and to minimize the data written back on completion, the opposite of one app per task plus full-variable writeback.

explanation: Camunda 8.8 best practice puts all worker glue code in one application with separate classes per task type, requires workers to be designed for idempotency, and keeps reads and writes from process variables as small as possible.

scenario: Nimbus Retail's order fulfillment process on Camunda 8.8 calls 3 internal REST services for payment, inventory, and shipping. The architect is choosing between deploying 3 separate worker applications versus a single process solution and wants a recommendation grounded in the best practice.
question: Which application layout does the Camunda 8.8 best practice recommend for these three workers?

options.text: Bundle the workers into one application but exclude the BPMN model; the process solution pattern is documented for monoliths and not for microservice teams owning a Camunda 8 process
Bundle the BPMN model and all three workers as a single process solution application, so the whole thing is easy to version, test, integrate, and deploy and may even deploy the BPMN model at startup
Deploy the BPMN model on its own and pair each worker with the team that owns the downstream system; collocating workers with the model couples release cadences to the BPMN file
Deploy each of the three workers as its own application with an independent release cycle, and keep the BPMN model in a fourth deployment artifact owned by a separate team for governance reasons

b.text: Correct. The Organizing glue code and workers in process solutions section says to implement all glue code in one application, the process solution, which may also include the BPMN process model deployed at startup, producing a self-contained app that is easy to version, test, integrate, and deploy.

d.text: Incorrect. The best practice puts all workers in one application instead of one app per task, and ties the BPMN model to that same process solution rather than to a separate team.

c.text: Incorrect. Co-locating the model and the workers as a process solution is the recommended pattern; the best practice does not warn against coupling worker and model releases this way.

a.text: Incorrect. The process solution pattern explicitly may include the BPMN model alongside the workers and is the recommended layout, not limited to monoliths.

explanation: The process solution pattern in Camunda 8.8 packages the BPMN model and its workers as a single self-contained application that is easy to version, test, integrate, and deploy.

scenario: Onyx Bank tunes a Camunda 8.8 job worker that runs across 5 service tasks where each process instance carries about 30 process variables of varying size. The performance lead wants the worker to use the FetchVariables parameter consistently to reduce broker and network load.
question: How does the Camunda 8.8 best practice describe the FetchVariables parameter for job activation?

options.text: Use FetchVariables to encrypt sensitive variables in transit between the broker and the worker; by default the activated job carries the variable names but never the variable values until a fetch confirmation arrives

Use FetchVariables to declare which variables Zeebe should drop from the process scope before activation; by default the activated job carries no variables and must reload them from the broker explicitly

Use FetchVariables to mark variables that may be cached locally in the worker; by default the activated job carries only the bpmnProcessId and the task type and any variable read still requires a separate REST round trip

Use FetchVariables to declare only the variables your job handler needs; by default the activated job carries all variables visible within the scope, which keeps transferred data minimal and helps performance

d.text: Correct. The Data minimization in workers section says by default, when the FetchVariables parameter is omitted, the job payload contains all variables visible within the scope, and recommends using FetchVariables to only fetch the variables the job handler needs to keep data transferred minimal.

b.text: Incorrect. The default is all visible variables, not none, and FetchVariables narrows what is fetched rather than dropping variables from the process scope.

c.text: Incorrect. FetchVariables controls which variables are included in the activation payload, not a local caching hint, and the default does include the visible variables.

a.text: Incorrect. FetchVariables is about data minimization, not encryption, and it does not relate to a separate fetch confirmation step.

explanation: In Camunda 8.8 the FetchVariables parameter lets a worker fetch only the variables its handler needs; if omitted, the job activation payload includes all variables visible within the scope, which is heavier on bandwidth and broker resources.

scenario: Polaris Marketplace must scale a Camunda 8.8 worker that calls an external REST API taking about 100 ms per call, with a target throughput of 100 jobs per second. The platform lead is choosing between sequential processing, a thread pool, and reactive non-blocking code.
question: How do the Camunda 8.8 best practices guide Polaris on scaling this worker beyond sequential execution?

options.text: Keep the worker sequential and request one job per long-poll; the Camunda 8 documentation states that going beyond sequential execution requires running multiple worker JVMs on dedicated host machines
Disable long polling, request one job per remote call, and process each job on a dedicated newly spawned thread; this gives the best parallelism for blocking REST clients against the Camunda 8 broker

Prefer reactive non-blocking code so the runtime can optimize thread utilization itself; with 100 ms REST latency a sequential worker is capped near 10 jobs per second and configuring thread pools manually is discouraged

Configure a large thread pool tuned to the broker partition count and run blocking REST calls; reactive programming is only recommended for read-only workers and not for workers that mutate downstream systems

c.text: Correct. The Scaling workers section notes a sequential worker with 100 ms REST latency tops out near 10 jobs per second, recommends reactive non-blocking code so the runtime optimizes thread utilization, and says configuring thread pools yourself is generally not recommended.

d.text: Incorrect. The best practice does not steer you toward manual thread-pool tuning by partition count, and reactive programming is recommended generally for parallel work, not limited to read-only workers.

a.text: Incorrect. The page explicitly describes batching up to 50 jobs per request and reactive code as ways to scale beyond sequential execution, not running more JVMs as the prescribed path.

b.text: Incorrect. The page recommends long polling and batched job activation, not disabling long polling and spawning a thread per job.

explanation: Camunda 8.8 best practice points to reactive non-blocking code for high-throughput workers because the runtime manages thread utilization, sequential workers are bandwidth-bound by per-call latency, and manual thread-pool configuration is generally discouraged.

scenario: Quartz Bank notices that its Camunda 8.8 job workers see an extra 2 seconds of activation delay on a large cluster during quiet periods, even with long polling enabled across 8 worker instances. A senior engineer wants the official recommendation for cutting this delay.
question: Which Camunda 8.8 worker performance best practice does the page recommend to reduce job activation latency?

options.text: Switch workers to short polling and disable batched activation; fetching one job per request reduces the per-job activation overhead and is the recommended Camunda 8.8 performance pattern

Increase the broker partition count to one partition per active worker; job activation delay is caused by partitions being assigned to multiple workers and is independent of the polling mechanism used

Enable job streaming so workers no longer have to balance aggressive polling of every partition against minimizing cluster impact, which on large clusters can otherwise add a noticeable delay in the order of seconds

Disable long polling on all workers and shorten the poll interval to 10 ms; long polling forces every worker to wait for the full server-side timeout before receiving newly activated jobs from the broker

c.text: Correct. The Performance best-practices section recommends enabling job streaming to reduce latency to a maximum, since with long polling workers must periodically poll every partition and balance aggression against cluster load, which on large clusters can add a noticeable delay in the order of seconds.

d.text: Incorrect. The best practice keeps long polling as a baseline and adds job streaming on top of it; tightening short-polling intervals is not the documented fix.

b.text: Incorrect. Job activation delay on large clusters is described as a polling-pattern problem solved by streaming, not a partition-to-worker mapping problem.

a.text: Incorrect. The best practice does not recommend disabling batched activation or moving to short polling for performance.

explanation: Camunda 8.8 best practice recommends enabling job streaming on workers to cut job activation latency, since long polling on large clusters forces a tradeoff between polling aggression and cluster load that can add seconds of delay.

scenario: Rivendell Bank standardizes on the Java client across 4 backend teams to implement Camunda 8.8 job workers. A new developer asks which official client the Writing good workers page points to as the example for writing worker code in Java.
question: Which official client does the Camunda 8.8 Writing good workers page name for Java worker examples?

options.text: The official Camunda Java Client published under github.com/camunda/camunda-platform-get-started/tree/master/java, used as the example for writing job worker code in Java in the Writing good workers best practice

A community-maintained Java client published under github.com/camunda-community-hub; the official Camunda 8.8 Java client only ships as part of the Camunda Spring Boot Starter and not as a standalone library

The Camunda 7 External Task Client for Java published under github.com/camunda/camunda-external-task-client-java, used as the worker library in the Writing good workers best practice page for Camunda 8.8

A generated Java client built from the Zeebe gRPC proto files; the Writing good workers page steers Java developers to regenerate the client themselves rather than depend on a published Camunda Java Client artifact

a.text: Correct. The Java section of Writing good workers links to the Java Client at github.com/camunda/camunda-platform-get-started/tree/master/java and uses it as the example for writing worker code in Java.

c.text: Incorrect. The Camunda 7 External Task Client is a Camunda 7 artifact and is not the client linked from the Camunda 8.8 Writing good workers page.

b.text: Incorrect. The page points to the official camunda/camunda-platform-get-started Java repository, not a community fork, and the Java client is available standalone.

d.text: Incorrect. The best practice points developers at the published official Java client, not at regenerating one from gRPC proto files.

explanation: The Camunda 8.8 Writing good workers best practice names the official Java Client at github.com/camunda/camunda-platform-get-started/tree/master/java as the example library for Java worker code.

scenario: Stargate Healthcare evaluates Camunda 8.8 client choices for a new worker codebase that 12 developers will own. The architect is checking which language client is positioned in Writing good workers as a worked example for Java teams building job workers, alongside roughly 800 jobs per day.
