question: Which client does the Writing good workers page position as the example for Java teams writing Camunda 8.8 workers?

options.text: A vendor-neutral Kafka-based client is positioned as the example for Java workers; direct gRPC clients to Zeebe are described as legacy patterns to phase out during the migration to 8.8
The Camunda Java Client linked from the get-started repository is positioned as the example for Java teams writing worker code against Camunda 8.8 in the Writing good workers best practice page
The Camunda CLI is positioned as the example for Java workers; the Writing good workers page recommends shell-script-driven invocations of the CLI for simple Java worker implementations in 8.8
The Camunda Spring Boot Starter is positioned as the only recommended way to write workers in Java, and the standalone Camunda Java Client is shown only as a counter-example to avoid in new projects

b.text: Correct. The Java section of Writing good workers points Java teams at the official Camunda Java Client in the camunda-platform-get-started Java directory and uses it to show how worker code looks in Java for Camunda 8.8.

d.text: Incorrect. The Spring Boot Starter is one option, but the Writing good workers page directly points to the standalone Java Client for its example without treating it as a counter-example.

a.text: Incorrect. The page does not suggest a Kafka-based client or describe direct gRPC clients as legacy in 8.8.

c.text: Incorrect. The CLI is not positioned as the example for Java workers in Writing good workers.

explanation: For Java workers, the Camunda 8.8 Writing good workers page uses the official Camunda Java Client from the camunda-platform-get-started repository as the example implementation.

scenario: Tidepool Marketplace runs a Camunda 8.8 platform supporting 7 internal teams, processing about 5000 jobs per day. Three of the teams plan to ship Java job workers and want to align with the Camunda 8.8 Writing good workers guidance instead of inventing their own gRPC clients on top of Zeebe.
question: Which Camunda 8.8 path should the Java teams at Tidepool follow per the Writing good workers best practice?

options.text: Adopt a community-maintained Kotlin client and run it inside a coroutine runtime; the Writing good workers page describes Kotlin as the recommended JVM language for new Camunda 8.8 worker implementations

Write a custom thin Java wrapper around the Zeebe gRPC API and skip the official client; the best practice tells teams to avoid depending on external client libraries that may lag a few weeks behind a Zeebe release

Reuse the legacy Camunda 7 Java engine embedded in their applications and treat Camunda 8 only as a broker; the Writing good workers page positions the Camunda 7 client as the supported entry point on the JVM

Adopt the official Camunda Java Client from the camunda-platform-get-started Java repository as the basis for their job workers, which is the implementation the Writing good workers best practice uses as the Java example

d.text: Correct. The Java section of Writing good workers links to the official Camunda Java Client in github.com/camunda/camunda-platform-get-started/tree/master/java as the implementation used in the Java worker example for Camunda 8.8.

b.text: Incorrect. The best practice steers Java teams to the official client; it does not tell teams to write their own gRPC wrapper to avoid the client library.

c.text: Incorrect. The Camunda 7 Java engine is not the path recommended for Camunda 8.8 workers, and Writing good workers does not position it as the JVM entry point.

a.text: Incorrect. The best practice does not name Kotlin as the recommended JVM language or point to a community Kotlin client.

explanation: For Java teams the Camunda 8.8 Writing good workers page tells you to adopt the official Camunda Java Client from the camunda-platform-get-started Java repository as the basis for job workers.

scenario: Orion Retailer runs an order fulfillment process on Camunda 8.8 that calls 3 synchronous REST services for payment, inventory, and shipping. The platform team handles 22000 orders per day and is deciding whether to ship one process solution that owns all 3 workers and the BPMN model or to split each worker into its own deployable application from day one.
question: What does the Camunda 8.8 best-practices guide recommend as the default packaging for the glue code workers of a single process?

options.text: Deploy each job worker as its own standalone microservice from day one, keeping the BPMN model in a separate central modeling repository owned by a platform team
Run all workers inside the Zeebe broker as in-process handlers and keep the BPMN model in a separate deployment pipeline owned by the operations team
Bundle all glue code workers and the BPMN model into one process solution application that is versioned, tested, and deployed together as a single self-contained unit
Embed each worker directly inside the calling system itself, so payment runs in the payment service and shipping runs in the shipping service with no shared application

c.text: Correct. The guide recommends implementing all glue code in one process solution application that also deploys the BPMN model, producing a self-contained unit that is easy to version, test, integrate, and deploy.

a.text: Incorrect. Splitting every worker into its own service is described only as an exception for scaling, throttling, or polyglot needs, not the default packaging.

d.text: Incorrect. Embedding workers in the called systems is not the recommended pattern; the default is a self-contained process solution holding the glue code.

b.text: Incorrect. Job workers run as external clients, not inside the Zeebe broker; the recommended default is one process solution application owning the workers and model.

explanation: The Camunda 8.8 writing-good-workers guide recommends a single process solution application that contains all glue code workers and deploys the BPMN model, with carved-out workers used only when a task needs independent scaling, throttling, or a different language.

scenario: Meridian Bank runs a loan origination process on Camunda 8.8 with 40 process variables per instance and 6500 jobs per day. A specific job handler only reads 2 of those variables. The platform team must minimize broker load and network bandwidth on job activation while keeping the rest of the variables untouched on the instance.
question: Which job worker configuration does the best-practices guide recommend to minimize data transferred on job activation?

options.text: Set the FetchVariables parameter on the worker to the specific variable names the handler needs, so only those variables are read and transferred from the broker
Activate every variable in the scope but compress the payload on the broker side using gzip so the network bandwidth stays low even for large instances
Disable variable activation entirely on the worker and read each required variable through a separate REST call against the Operate variables API at job start
Keep the default activation behaviour so every variable visible in the scope is fetched, then ignore the unused ones inside the worker handler code at runtime

d.text: Incorrect. By default the job payload contains all variables in scope; the guide explicitly recommends configuring FetchVariables rather than relying on the default.

a.text: Correct. The guide recommends using the FetchVariables parameter and only fetching the variables the handler needs to keep transferred data minimal and improve performance.

c.text: Incorrect. The recommended mechanism is the FetchVariables parameter on the job worker, not an out-of-band call to the Operate variables API.

b.text: Incorrect. The guide focuses on minimizing what data is read on activation via FetchVariables, not on compressing the full payload of every variable in scope.

explanation: Camunda 8.8 job workers can declare the FetchVariables parameter to receive only the variables a handler actually needs on activation, saving broker resources and network bandwidth compared to fetching the full set of in-scope variables.

scenario: Stellar Fintech runs a payment authorization process on Camunda 8.8 that performs 20 REST calls per job, each averaging 100 ms. The team observes a ceiling of 10 jobs per second on a single worker thread and needs to lift throughput to roughly 100 jobs per second across the worker pool while keeping a small CPU footprint.
question: Which scaling technique does the writing-good-workers guide cite as raising blocking REST throughput from about 10 to 100 jobs per second?

options.text: Move from a thread pool of 10 threads to a thread pool of 1000 threads to overlap thousands of REST calls regardless of the I/O latency or available CPU cores on the host
Use a thread pool of 10 threads so blocked I/O threads overlap waiting time and roughly 10 times as many REST calls run in parallel against the same worker process
Disable long polling on the worker and request a single job at a time so the broker pushes work faster than the worker can synchronously process REST calls in sequence
Pin one worker thread per CPU core and process all jobs strictly sequentially on that core to avoid context switching and maximize raw single-thread REST call throughput

c.text: Incorrect. The guide promotes batching and long polling for fetching jobs; reducing batch size or disabling long polling does not lift the blocking REST throughput ceiling.

d.text: Incorrect. Strictly sequential per-core processing is the worst case for scalability; the guide raises throughput by using a thread pool of blocked I/O threads.

b.text: Correct. The guide uses a thread pool of 10 threads with 100 ms REST latency to raise throughput from 10 to 100 jobs per second because blocked I/O threads do not consume CPU cores.

a.text: Incorrect. The guide notes that sizing thread pools requires careful understanding; it recommends reactive programming over arbitrarily huge pools for further scaling.

explanation: Camunda 8.8 worker guidance shows that, given 100 ms blocking REST calls, sizing a thread pool to 10 threads overlaps I/O waits and lifts throughput from about 10 to about 100 jobs per second; reactive programming is recommended for further scaling.

scenario: Vertex Logistics runs a parcel routing process on Camunda 8.8 across a large Zeebe cluster that handles 45000 jobs per day. The platform team sees activation delays in the order of seconds during off-peak hours and needs to cut job activation latency to a minimum without aggressively raising poll frequency against every partition.
question: Which Camunda 8.8 worker-side feature does the performance guide recommend to reduce job activation latency to a maximum?

options.text: Enable job streaming on the worker so jobs are pushed from the partitions as they become available and the poll-induced activation delay no longer dominates worker latency
Subscribe the worker directly to the Operate jobs WebSocket so the workflow engine pushes activations through Operate instead of through the worker long-polling Zeebe gateway path
Raise the worker long-poll interval against every Zeebe partition to a much higher rate so jobs are picked up faster even in idle clusters during low-traffic off-peak windows
Replace the worker with a Camunda Connector worker so the connector runtime takes over the activation polling loop entirely and bypasses the partition-level long polling completely

a.text: Correct. The performance best-practices guide recommends enabling job streaming so workers do not periodically poll every partition; this reduces activation delay that can otherwise reach the order of seconds in large clusters.

c.text: Incorrect. The guide warns that polling more aggressively raises cluster load even when no jobs are available; job streaming is the recommended remedy for activation delay.

b.text: Incorrect. Operate does not expose a jobs WebSocket for activation; the guide recommends Zeebe job streaming directly between workers and partitions.

d.text: Incorrect. Connectors run on top of the same job activation mechanism; the guide recommends enabling job streaming itself to cut activation latency for any worker.

explanation: Camunda 8.8 recommends enabling job streaming to minimize activation latency: it removes the periodic per-partition polling that, in large clusters, can introduce delays in the order of seconds before a worker sees a newly available job.

scenario: Catalyst Bank operates a Camunda 8.8 Java worker that performs a blocking RestTemplate call for a loan check on 8000 jobs per day. The platform team measures strictly sequential job processing on the default Java client configuration and needs to enable a fixed thread pool of 5 worker threads to overlap the blocking REST calls.
question: Which Java client builder call sets the worker execution thread pool size to 5 threads in Camunda 8.8?

options.text: Call .numJobWorkerExecutionThreads(5) on the ZeebeClient builder and build the client; the default thread pool size is one, so this widens parallelism to 5 threads for blocking workers
Call .workerThreads(5) on the ZeebeClient builder and build the client; the default thread pool size is one, so this widens parallelism to 5 threads for blocking workers
Call .executor(Executors.newFixedThreadPool(5)) on the ZeebeClient builder and build the client; the default thread pool size is one, so this widens parallelism to 5 threads
Call .jobWorkerPoolSize(5) on the ZeebeClient builder and build the client; the default thread pool size is one, so this widens parallelism to 5 threads for blocking workers

a.text: Correct. The documented Java snippet uses ZeebeClient.newClientBuilder().numJobWorkerExecutionThreads(5).build() to override the default single-thread pool and allow blocking workers to run in parallel.

d.text: Incorrect. There is no jobWorkerPoolSize method in the documented Java client builder; the documented name is numJobWorkerExecutionThreads.

c.text: Incorrect. The documented Java client builder does not take an external Executor for worker threads; it exposes numJobWorkerExecutionThreads to size the pool.

b.text: Incorrect. The documented builder method is numJobWorkerExecutionThreads, not workerThreads; the example in the guide uses that exact name.

explanation: The Camunda 8.8 Java client defaults to a single worker execution thread; to scale blocking workers, call numJobWorkerExecutionThreads(n) on the ZeebeClient builder, as shown in the writing-good-workers documentation example with 5 threads.

scenario: Helios Bank consumes 8000 jobs per day from a Camunda 8.8 cluster with a Java worker built on the official Java client. The team has not touched the executor configuration and is surprised that REST calls run sequentially even when the broker activated 32 jobs in a single poll, instead of running them concurrently across multiple threads.
question: What is the default job execution thread pool size on a Java client worker in Camunda 8.8?

options.text: The Java client defaults to a job worker thread pool size of 1, so blocking REST calls run sequentially until numJobWorkerExecutionThreads is raised on the builder
The Java client defaults to a job worker thread pool size of 5, so blocking REST calls run with up to five jobs in parallel out of the box
The Java client defaults to a job worker thread pool size equal to the JVM CPU count, scaling automatically with the host hardware sizing at startup
The Java client defaults to a job worker thread pool size of 32, matching the maxJobsActive poll batch returned by the broker on each activation

b.text: Incorrect. The Java client does not default to 5 worker threads. The page documents a default thread pool size of one; the value 5 only appears as an example argument to numJobWorkerExecutionThreads in the builder snippet.

a.text: Correct. The page states the default thread pool size of a Java client worker is one. Blocking handlers therefore run jobs sequentially until numJobWorkerExecutionThreads is increased on the ZeebeClient builder.

d.text: Incorrect. The 32 jobs visible in the logs are the activated batch returned by one poll, not the executor thread count. The documented default thread pool size remains one.

c.text: Incorrect. The Java client default is not derived from CPU count. The documentation pins it to a fixed value of one, regardless of host hardware sizing.

explanation: Camunda 8.8's Java client starts a worker with a documented default job execution thread pool size of one. Teams that need parallelism for blocking handlers must raise it explicitly with newClientBuilder().numJobWorkerExecutionThreads(N) on the ZeebeClient builder.

scenario: Nimbus SaaS runs 12000 jobs per day on Camunda 8.8 using the Camunda Node.js client to call a downstream REST API with Axios. The team is evaluating whether they must tune a worker thread pool size like the Java client to scale handler concurrency under load, or whether the Node.js runtime already takes care of parallelism on its own.
question: Which statement matches how the Camunda 8 Node.js client achieves parallelism in worker handlers?

options.text: The Node.js client relies on the Node.js event loop for parallelism, so no thread pool is defined and reactive handlers scale without extra tuning by the developer
The Node.js client spins up one operating system thread per activated job and tears it down after job.complete returns to the broker over the gRPC connection
The Node.js client uses a fixed pool of 5 worker threads inside the SDK, so handlers run with up to five concurrent jobs once Axios calls return successfully
The Node.js client requires a numJobWorkerExecutionThreads setting on the createWorker call to raise concurrency above one handler invocation at a time

d.text: Incorrect. numJobWorkerExecutionThreads is a Java client builder option for the JVM-based SDK. The Node.js createWorker call has no thread pool size to set; parallelism comes from the event loop.

c.text: Incorrect. The Node.js SDK does not define a fixed worker thread pool of 5. The documented model uses the Node.js event loop for parallelism, and the SDK itself is built around reactive programming.

a.text: Correct. The page states the Node.js client uses the event loop for parallelism, that no specific thread pool is defined or necessary, and that the SDK uses reactive programming internally for its workers.

b.text: Incorrect. The Node.js runtime does not spawn one OS thread per job. Concurrent handler invocations are scheduled cooperatively on the single event loop provided by Node.js.

explanation: The Camunda 8 Node.js client takes advantage of the Node.js event loop and the reactive style of JavaScript. There is no worker thread pool to configure, and the page's recommendation for Node.js is to always use reactive code.

scenario: HelixHealth, a hospital network deploying 24 microservices on Camunda 8.8, ships a Node.js TypeScript service that must upload a process.bpmn file at startup. The team wants the SDK call that reads a local file path and returns the deployed processDefinitionId so the worker can log it after deployment, and they wrap the call in an async main function because network operations return promises.
question: Which TypeScript SDK call deploys the local process.bpmn file in the documented quickstart?

options.text: Call camunda.newDeployResourceCommand().addResourceFile('process.bpmn').send() and read processes[0].processDefinitionId from the resolved deploy response payload for logging
Call camunda.deployResourcesFromFiles([path.join(process.cwd(), 'process.bpmn')]) and read processes[0].processDefinitionId from the resolved deploy response payload for logging
Call camunda.deployProcess({ file: path.join(process.cwd(), 'process.bpmn') }) and read processes[0].processDefinitionId from the resolved deploy response payload for logging
Call camunda.uploadResourceFile(path.join(process.cwd(), 'process.bpmn')) and read processes[0].processDefinitionId from the resolved upload response payload for logging

d.text: Incorrect. There is no uploadResourceFile method; the documented deployment call is deployResourcesFromFiles, which accepts an array of file paths and returns the deploy response.

c.text: Incorrect. The documented call is deployResourcesFromFiles taking an array of paths; deployProcess with a file option is not the documented method shape.

a.text: Incorrect. The fluent newDeployResourceCommand builder is the Java client style; the TypeScript SDK exposes deployResourcesFromFiles directly on the Camunda8 instance.

b.text: Correct. The quickstart awaits camunda.deployResourcesFromFiles with an array of absolute paths, then logs deployResponse.processes[0].processDefinitionId after the promise resolves.

explanation: The documented deployment step calls camunda.deployResourcesFromFiles([path.join(process.cwd(), 'process.bpmn')]) inside an async main function, then reads deployResponse.processes[0].processDefinitionId.

scenario: Quanta Healthcare runs Camunda 8.8 workers that process 6400 claims per day for a regional hospital network. After a worker crash during a billing run, an architect must explain why a single createCustomer call could be executed more than once and what design property of the worker code prevents duplicate side effects from corrupting the downstream system.
question: Which delivery guarantee does the docs page describe for Zeebe job handlers, and what design property must the worker code provide?

options.text: Job handlers run under an at-least-once strategy because a worker that crashes after doing the work cannot complete the job; the engine reassigns the job after a timeout, so worker glue code must be idempotent

Job handlers run under a best-effort strategy that allows them to be skipped after a fixed number of broker outages, so worker glue code only needs to be retried manually from Operate by a human operator on call duty

Job handlers run under an at-most-once strategy because the engine deletes a job from the queue the moment a worker activates it, so worker glue code may safely skip idempotency checks against the downstream service provider system

Job handlers are invoked under an exactly-once guarantee inside the broker transaction, so worker glue code does not need to be idempotent because Zeebe deduplicates duplicate completions transparently for the application developer

d.text: Incorrect. The docs explicitly say Zeebe uses at-least-once, not exactly-once, and that worker code must be designed for duplicate invocations.

a.text: Correct. The page states Zeebe uses the at-least-once strategy: if a worker that polled a job crashes before completing, the engine re-assigns the job after the configured timeout, so worker glue code must be idempotent.

c.text: Incorrect. Zeebe is not at-most-once; the page explicitly calls out at-least-once and warns that handlers may run more than once.

b.text: Incorrect. The delivery strategy is at-least-once and is not described as best-effort or operator-driven.

explanation: Zeebe uses an at-least-once strategy for job handlers. A worker that crashes after performing the side effect but before completing the job will see the same job reassigned after the configured timeout, so the same handler may run multiple times. Worker code must therefore be idempotent through natural idempotency, business identifiers, or a custom idempotency key.

scenario: Pinnacle Bank orchestrates an onboarding process on Camunda 8.8 that updates a CRM and a billing system for 8800 customers per day in a regional bank. A new architect asks whether one ACID transaction can span the workflow engine, CRM, and billing system so the customer either exists in both downstream systems or in neither one after each onboarding case finishes.
question: Which statement does the best-practices guide make about technical transactions across the workflow engine, CRM, and billing systems?

options.text: Camunda 8 exposes a distributed XA coordinator that joins the broker, CRM, and billing systems into one ACID transaction so a process scope can roll back the entire transaction as a single atomic unit on any failure across the systems

The Zeebe broker exposes a Saga-as-a-service flag in the cluster configuration that automatically wraps every BPMN scope in a distributed transaction across CRM and billing systems without the team having to model compensation handlers manually

Process variables persist atomically through a two-phase commit between the broker and downstream services, so a service task either commits in both the broker and the downstream service or rolls back in both during the call

Each system has a local ACID transaction and the workflow engine itself runs transactional too, but no joined technical transaction is possible across distributed systems; consistency on the business level is enforced by a business transaction in the model

a.text: Incorrect. There is no distributed XA coordinator in Camunda 8; the page explicitly says ACID transactions cannot be applied across distributed systems.

d.text: Correct. The page states that each external system has its own local ACID transaction, that the workflow engine itself runs transactional, but that no joined technical transaction exists across systems; business-level consistency is modeled as a business transaction.

b.text: Incorrect. There is no Saga-as-a-service flag; the Saga pattern is implemented in the BPMN model using compensation events.

c.text: Incorrect. There is no two-phase commit between the broker and downstream services; ACID cannot span the distributed systems involved here.

explanation: The Technical vs business transactions section is explicit: in distributed integrations, each external system has its own local ACID transaction and the workflow engine runs transactional too, but no single joined technical transaction can span them. Consistency must therefore be modeled at the business level as a business transaction in BPMN, which is what makes inconsistencies between CRM and billing temporarily possible.

scenario: Iris SaaS orchestrates onboarding for 6700 customers per day on a Camunda 8.8 platform that updates a CRM and a billing system in sequence inside the same business transaction. The product team observed a marketing campaign sent to a customer who was active in the CRM but never reached the billing system, and is asking what design property describes this window.
question: Which property does the best-practices guide say teams must accept and actively resolve in distributed integrations like this one?

options.text: Snapshot isolation across distributed transactions, enforced by the Zeebe gateway taking a consistent snapshot of CRM and billing systems at each service task boundary so no inconsistency window can ever be observed

Read-your-writes consistency for human users only, enforced by Operate caching the latest customer state across all upstream and downstream systems so any deviations are invisible to the campaign-management application layer

Strong consistency across all participating systems, enforced by a single Zeebe partition leader that holds a global lock until both the CRM and billing systems have acknowledged the write within a configured timeout window

Eventual consistency, which acknowledges that temporary inconsistencies are possible between systems and that the team must take measures to drive the state back to a consistent one, for example by deactivating the customer in the CRM

d.text: Correct. The Eventual consistency section names this property: temporary inconsistencies are possible and the team must take measures to get back to a consistent state eventually, for example by deactivating the customer in the CRM when billing fails.

c.text: Incorrect. The page does not claim strong consistency across systems; on the contrary, it accepts that temporary inconsistencies are possible.

b.text: Incorrect. Operate does not provide cross-system read-your-writes guarantees; the page describes eventual consistency, not a caching mechanism.

a.text: Incorrect. There is no snapshot-isolation guarantee provided by the Zeebe gateway across downstream systems on this page.

explanation: The Eventual consistency section explains that in distributed integrations, the intermediate state where the CRM has a customer but the billing system does not is possible. Teams must accept this window and design measures, such as deactivating the customer in the CRM when billing fails, to drive the systems back to a consistent state eventually.

scenario: Stellar Telco starts a fresh Camunda 8.8 service that will handle 5400 requests per day for a regional telco operator. A new Spring Boot project must be set up from scratch following the getting-started steps so that process variables can be bound to method parameter names without annotation values, before any application.yaml mode is configured for the orchestration cluster.
question: Which two Get started steps does the Camunda Spring Boot Starter page recommend before configuring the Orchestration Cluster connection?

options.text: Step 1 adds the io.camunda:camunda-spring-boot-starter dependency at 8.8.x to the build; Step 2 enables the Java compiler -parameters flag so process variables can be bound to method parameter names without setting an annotation value

Step 1 generates a new Spring Boot 3 project from the Camunda Initializr; Step 2 enables the @EnableCamundaClient annotation on the main class so the starter auto-wires the Camunda Java Client beans into the Spring context

Step 1 adds the io.camunda:camunda-spring-zeebe-sdk dependency at 8.8.x; Step 2 configures the maven-compiler-plugin source level to 21 so process variables can be bound to method parameter names without further compiler flags

Step 1 adds the io.camunda:zeebe-client-java dependency at 8.8.x; Step 2 sets the spring.profiles.active property to camunda so the starter scans the classpath for @JobWorker beans during application start-up

a.text: Correct. The Get started section starts with Step 1: add the io.camunda:camunda-spring-boot-starter dependency at 8.8.x. Step 2 enables the Java compiler -parameters flag so process variables can be bound to method parameter names without annotation values.

b.text: Incorrect. There is no Camunda Initializr or @EnableCamundaClient step on the page; the documented dependency is io.camunda:camunda-spring-boot-starter and the optional flag is the -parameters compiler argument.

d.text: Incorrect. The page does not point at the io.camunda:zeebe-client-java artifact for the starter path, and there is no documented spring.profiles.active=camunda toggle for worker scanning.

c.text: Incorrect. The Spring Zeebe SDK is being replaced and is not the documented dependency; the documented artifact is io.camunda:camunda-spring-boot-starter.

explanation: The Get started section lists Step 1: add the io.camunda:camunda-spring-boot-starter Maven dependency at version 8.8.x. Step 2 is optional and enables the Java compiler -parameters flag (in Maven, via the compilerArgs of maven-compiler-plugin), so process variables can be bound to method parameter names without specifying annotation values. Steps 3a and 3b then configure the cluster connection.

docs.title: Camunda Spring Boot Starter getting started

scenario: NovaPay is a fintech bank running a loan origination process on Camunda 8.8 with 4500 loan applications per day. The core banking system already stores the customer and loan record data as the leading system, and the development team must decide what to keep inside Zeebe as process variables for each instance.
question: Which approach matches the Camunda 8.8 best practice for process variables when leading systems already hold the business data?

options.text: Hold a denormalized snapshot of every related record from the core banking system so downstream service tasks throughout the loan process can avoid making any further external lookups
Hold a full copy of every customer and loan record returned by the core banking system so gateway decisions inside the process can run without ever contacting the leading system at runtime
Hold references such as numeric loan IDs that point to records kept inside the leading core banking system, so the process variable map stays lean across the 4500 daily loan applications at NovaPay
Hold every JSON payload returned by every external call to the core banking system so Operate can later replay the full message body for any audit request on any past instance

c.text: Correct. The handling data in processes best practice tells teams to store references only, such as IDs, when leading systems already hold the business relevant data, keeping the process variable map lean and avoiding the variable size limitation.

b.text: Incorrect. The best practice advises against excessive use of process variables and explicitly recommends holding references when the leading system already stores the business data, not full record copies.

d.text: Incorrect. The page advises storing only the parts of an external payload that are relevant to the process, not the entire message body, which also increases coupling to the service interface.

a.text: Incorrect. Storing a denormalized snapshot of every related record duplicates data already held by the leading system, which the best practice explicitly discourages in favor of references.

explanation: The Camunda 8.8 handling data in processes best practice recommends storing as few process variables as possible. When a leading system already holds the business relevant data, store references such as IDs to that data and avoid copying the full payload into Zeebe.

scenario: HelixHealth is a healthcare provider running a patient referral process on Camunda 8.8 that handles 7200 referrals per day. The Java development team wants to remove copy pasted process variable name strings from across 14 microservices, and also needs type safe reads of variables inside its job workers with optional custom serialization.
question: Which Camunda 8.8 pattern centralizes process variable names and adds type safe deserialization inside a Java job worker?

options.text: Define a single global enum shared across every process definition and rely on the Zeebe broker to enforce variable type checks on every activate jobs request issued by HelixHealth Java workers
Define worker properties in a Spring configuration file and let the Camunda 8 broker inject typed variable holders into every JobHandler invocation at HelixHealth by matching variable names
Define BPMN level data objects in the modeler and let the Java client automatically generate Process Data Accessor classes from each deployed process definition at HelixHealth startup time
Define string constants in a Java interface per process definition and use a Process Data Accessor object that the Java client deserializes from process variables JSON for type safe variable reads

a.text: Incorrect. The best practice recommends an interface of string constants per process definition for variable names; there is no global enum that Zeebe enforces type checks against on activate jobs requests.

d.text: Correct. The handling data page recommends collecting variable names as constants in a per process Java interface and using a Process Data Accessor class; the Java client deserializes the process variables JSON into that object for type safe access.

b.text: Incorrect. The broker does not inject typed variable holders into JobHandler invocations based on Spring configuration; type safe access is achieved by the client deserializing variables into a Process Data Accessor.

c.text: Incorrect. Data Accessor classes are written by the developer as plain Java objects; the Java client does not auto generate them from BPMN data objects at deployment or at startup.

explanation: Camunda 8.8 recommends collecting process variable names as string constants in a Java interface per process and using a Process Data Accessor object. The Java client deserializes process variables JSON into that object, giving type safe access while ignoring unknown variables.

scenario: Iris Retailer runs a Camunda Spring Boot Starter worker on Camunda 8.8 that processes 2900 orders per hour. The developer needs to read BPMN service-task custom headers such as routingKey from the handler without enlarging the fetched variable list, so they pick the documented parameter annotation that exposes the headers as a Map and leaves variable fetching behaviour unchanged.
question: What does the @CustomHeaders annotation provide on a @JobWorker handler parameter?

options.text: It binds a JobClient instance positioned at the activated job, and the docs note that using it as a parameter prevents the implicit variable fetching detection on the handler method
It binds a Map<String, String> of custom headers defined on the activated job, and the docs note it does not have any effect on the variable fetching behaviour for the worker
It binds a Map<String, Object> of process variables active on the calling token, and it adds those variable names to the joint list of variables that the worker fetches
It binds an ActivatedJob holder for the current job, and the docs note that using it as a parameter prevents the implicit variable fetching detection on the handler method

c.text: Incorrect. @CustomHeaders binds custom headers as a Map<String, String>, not process variables, and the docs explicitly state it does not affect variable fetching.

b.text: Correct. The docs state @CustomHeaders is used on a Map<String, String> parameter to retrieve custom headers for a job and that this will not have any effect on the variable fetching behaviour of the worker.

a.text: Incorrect. JobClient is a separate parameter type from the native JobHandler interface; @CustomHeaders is the annotation for a Map of custom headers.

d.text: Incorrect. ActivatedJob is a separate parameter type and is the one that prevents implicit variable fetching detection; @CustomHeaders has no such effect.

explanation: The @CustomHeaders annotation binds a Map<String, String> parameter to the custom headers of the activated job. The docs state that using @CustomHeaders has no effect on the variable fetching behaviour of the worker.

scenario: Lattice Banking is designing a Camunda 8.8 onboarding microservice that handles 4500 customers per day applying for new accounts. The application must expose a REST endpoint that starts a process instance, deploy the BPMN definition at startup, and host the workers that subscribe to the two service tasks calling a remote REST API.
question: What artifacts does the onboarding microservice typically bundle together as one process solution in Camunda 8.8?

options.text: The application bundles the REST start endpoint, the BPMN definition that is auto-deployed at application startup, and the job workers subscribing to the two service-task types in one codebase
The application bundles the BPMN definition and an embedded Tasklist UI, while job workers are skipped because Camunda 8.8 connectors automatically resolve every service task call at runtime
The application bundles only the BPMN definition while the REST start endpoint and the job workers for the service tasks live in two separate dedicated applications managed by other teams
The application bundles the REST start endpoint and an embedded workflow engine broker, the BPMN is uploaded by hand through Web Modeler, and the workers run as a third independent application

a.text: Correct. The best-practice example bundles the REST endpoint that starts a process instance, the BPMN definition auto-deployed during application startup, and the glue code subscribing to the two service tasks into a single application; a job worker is started automatically as part of that application.

c.text: Incorrect. The example bundles workers within the same process solution that hosts the BPMN and the REST endpoint; splitting each piece into its own application is documented as an exception, not the default.

d.text: Incorrect. The workflow engine runs as a separate component in Camunda 8.8 and the application does not embed a broker; the BPMN is auto-deployed at application startup rather than uploaded by hand.

b.text: Incorrect. The workers must subscribe to the service-task types and process the resulting jobs; connectors are an optional alternative addressed elsewhere, not an automatic substitute for worker subscriptions.

explanation: The Camunda 8.8 best-practice process solution for the onboarding microservice bundles the REST endpoint that starts the process, the BPMN definition (auto-deployed during application startup), and the job workers subscribing to each service-task type into one application written in any supported language.

docs.title: Connecting the workflow engine with your world

scenario: Granite Retailer is rolling out a Camunda 8.8 purchase process that handles 3400 orders per day in a high-volume retailer. The purchase activity is modeled as a single BPMN service task rather than a subprocess, and the worker glue code must report a GOOD_UNAVAILABLE outcome up to the process so a boundary handler in the parent scope reacts to it.
question: Which Zeebe API call mimics a BPMN error end event from inside the worker glue code so the process reacts as if the activity had ended with an error end event?

options.text: Call newCompleteCommand with a process variable named bpmnError set to the string GOOD_UNAVAILABLE so the engine resolves the variable into the corresponding error end event of the surrounding subprocess at runtime
Call newFailCommand with retries set to zero and let the resulting incident in Operate be auto-resolved by a generic listener that translates the failure into a BPMN error in the surrounding scope

Call newCreateInstanceCommand to start a side process named handleError with a correlationKey equal to the bpmnProcessId of the parent instance so a separate process model deals with the unavailability
Call newThrowErrorCommand with the errorCode GOOD_UNAVAILABLE; the consequences for the process are the same as if an explicit error end event had been reached inside the activity

a.text: Incorrect. CompleteCommand advances the activity normally; it does not trigger an error end event, regardless of process variables.

b.text: Incorrect. FailCommand with zero retries raises an incident; it does not propagate as a BPMN error to the surrounding scope.

c.text: Incorrect. Starting a separate process is not the documented technique; the page uses ThrowError on the current job.

d.text: Correct. The page shows newThrowErrorCommand with errorCode GOOD_UNAVAILABLE; the engine reacts as if an explicit error end event had been reached, so a boundary error event on the activity catches the error.

explanation: Inside the worker, newThrowErrorCommand with an errorCode such as GOOD_UNAVAILABLE produces the same behavior as an explicit BPMN error end event inside the activity. The process model can then react with a boundary error event or a matching error catch in a parent scope, instead of treating the situation as a technical incident.

scenario: Pinnacle retailer is adding a new Spring Boot worker that consumes 3200 jobs per hour on Camunda 8.8. The team wants to define handler methods whose parameter names like customerId and orderId map directly to process variables without annotating each parameter explicitly with its variable name.
question: Which optional Java compiler configuration lets parameter names map to process variables without annotation values?

options.text: Enable the -source 17 flag on the Java compiler which records method parameter names in the class file when targeting JDK 17 or higher only
Enable the -g flag on the Java compiler which preserves all debugging information and exposes parameter names to the runtime reflection layer
Enable the --release 17 flag on the Java compiler which records parameter names alongside the bytecode for Spring Boot worker method binding
Enable the -parameters flag on the Java compiler which records method parameter names in the class file for the starter to bind variables

b.text: Incorrect. The -g flag emits debug information about variables and line numbers but does not retain method parameter names for runtime reflection by the Camunda Spring Boot Starter.

c.text: Incorrect. --release sets the Java language and API level for compilation; it does not control whether parameter names are recorded in the class file for reflection.

d.text: Correct. Step 2 of Getting Started instructs developers to enable the Java compiler flag -parameters so parameter names are recorded in the class file and used as variable names without annotation values.

a.text: Incorrect. -source sets the source language level for compilation; recording parameter names in the class file is controlled by the -parameters compiler flag.

explanation: The Camunda 8.8 Spring Boot Starter binds method parameter names to process variables only when the Java compiler is run with -parameters so names are kept in the class file. Step 2 of the Getting Started guide shows enabling it via the Maven compiler plugin or the equivalent Gradle option.

docs.title: Camunda Spring Boot Starter get started

scenario: A retailer running Camunda 8.8 with 6500 orders per day implements a Java job worker for the BPMN service task labelled send-email. The lead engineer studies the quick-start example in the Java client guide before adding the worker to the existing application and verifying that the worker can complete activated jobs through the documented API.
question: Which Camunda 8.8 Java job worker invocation matches the quick-start example for opening a worker on a specific job type?

options.text: Open the worker via client.newJobWorker().setType(jobType).setListener(new EmailJobHandler()).start(), then mark the job complete by calling jobClient.acknowledge(job.getKey()) inside the handler
Open the worker via client.newWorker().jobType(jobType).onJob(new EmailJobHandler()).run(), then inside the handler call jobClient.success(job.getKey()).join() to confirm completion to the cluster
Open the worker via client.subscribe(jobType, new EmailJobHandler()), then inside the handler invoke jobClient.publish(new CompleteJobEvent(job.getKey())) followed by jobClient.flush() to confirm

Open the worker via client.newWorker().jobType(jobType).handler(new EmailJobHandler()).open(), then inside the handler call client.newCompleteCommand(job.getKey()).variables(Map.of(...)).send().join()

a.text: Incorrect. The documented fluent builder uses jobType(...) and handler(...) and the open call returns a JobWorker AutoCloseable; there is no setType, setListener, or acknowledge method in the Java client API.

c.text: Incorrect. The Java client exposes an explicit JobWorker builder rather than a publish/subscribe API, and completing a job uses newCompleteCommand(job.getKey()) on the JobClient.

b.text: Incorrect. The fluent builder method is handler(...) followed by open(), not onJob(...) and run(); completion uses newCompleteCommand rather than a success method.

d.text: Correct. The quick-start example opens the worker with client.newWorker().jobType(jobType).handler(new EmailJobHandler()).open(), and inside the handler the job is completed via client.newCompleteCommand(job.getKey()).variables(...).send().join().

explanation: The quick-start example in the Java client job-worker guide opens a JobWorker via client.newWorker().jobType(jobType).handler(handler).open() returned as an AutoCloseable. Inside the handler, jobs are completed through client.newCompleteCommand(job.getKey()).variables(...).send().join(), and failures are reported via newFailCommand.

docs.title: Java client job worker quick start

scenario: Forge Manufacturing operates Camunda 8.8 with 18 job workers handling 8000 jobs per hour. The team has set camunda.client.worker.defaults.fetch-variables to a list of 4 audit fields but also wants every worker to receive the entire process payload, so it is consulting the documented relationship to force-fetch-all-variables.
question: What does camunda.client.worker.defaults.force-fetch-all-variables do relative to fetch-variables in the 8.8 reference?

options.text: The reference documents force-fetch-all-variables as a boolean that augments fetch-variables with the audit subset declared on each handler annotation.
The reference documents force-fetch-all-variables as a duration that throttles fetch-variables to a polling interval across all 8.8 workers globally.
The reference documents force-fetch-all-variables as a boolean that overrides fetch-variables to pull every variable on job activation across all 8.8 workers.
The reference documents force-fetch-all-variables as a boolean that toggles auto-complete behavior whenever fetch-variables is non-empty on a worker.

c.text: Correct. The 8.8 reference lists force-fetch-all-variables as a boolean and notes it overrides fetch-variables so every variable is pulled on job activation.

a.text: Incorrect. The flag does not selectively augment the list; the reference says it overrides fetch-variables and fetches all variables instead.

b.text: Incorrect. The property is a boolean, not a duration; poll-interval is a separate property under the same defaults section.

d.text: Incorrect. The flag is unrelated to auto-complete; auto-complete is a separate boolean under camunda.client.worker.defaults in 8.8.

explanation: The camunda.client.worker.defaults.force-fetch-all-variables property is a boolean that, when true, overrides fetch-variables so the worker fetches every process variable on job activation.

docs.title: Camunda Spring Boot starter properties reference

scenario: Wren Retailer runs Camunda 8.8 with 8 Spring Boot services handling 6500 orders per hour and still sets camunda.client.zeebe.defaults.timeout in its YAML. The platform team must adopt the documented 8.8 replacement key before retiring the legacy entry.
question: Which property does the 8.8 reference list as the replacement for the legacy camunda.client.zeebe.defaults.timeout key?

options.text: The 8.8 reference replaces camunda.client.zeebe.defaults.timeout with camunda.client.worker.defaults.timeout under the unified worker namespace.
The 8.8 reference replaces camunda.client.zeebe.defaults.timeout with camunda.client.worker.override.timeout under the unified worker namespace.
The 8.8 reference replaces camunda.client.zeebe.defaults.timeout with camunda.client.timeout under the unified worker namespace.
The 8.8 reference replaces camunda.client.zeebe.defaults.timeout with camunda.client.defaults.timeout under the unified worker namespace.

a.text: Correct. The camunda.client.zeebe.defaults section maps timeout to camunda.client.worker.defaults.timeout as the documented 8.8 replacement key.

b.text: Incorrect. Override entries live under the job-type key; the defaults timeout migrates to camunda.client.worker.defaults.timeout instead.

c.text: Incorrect. There is no flat camunda.client.timeout entry; the replacement keeps the worker.defaults path for the job timeout.

d.text: Incorrect. The camunda.client.defaults prefix is not in the 8.8 reference; the documented home is camunda.client.worker.defaults.

explanation: camunda.client.zeebe.defaults.timeout migrates to camunda.client.worker.defaults.timeout in 8.8. The property controls how long an activated job is exclusively assigned to the worker.

docs.title: Camunda Spring Boot starter properties reference

scenario: Umbra Banking runs a Camunda 8.8 Spring Boot service with 6 workers serving 11000 transactions per hour and inherits a YAML file that still uses common.url to point at the cluster. The team must migrate to the documented 8.8 replacement listed under the deprecated common section.
question: Which property does the 8.8 reference list as the replacement for the legacy common.url key?

options.text: The 8.8 reference replaces common.url with camunda.client.rest-address as the unified REST endpoint for all interaction with Camunda in the starter.
The 8.8 reference replaces common.url with camunda.client.auth.token-url as the unified REST endpoint for all interaction with Camunda in the starter.
The 8.8 reference replaces common.url with camunda.client.cloud.domain as the unified REST endpoint for all interaction with Camunda in the starter.
The 8.8 reference replaces common.url with camunda.client.grpc-address as the unified REST endpoint for all interaction with Camunda in the starter.

a.text: Correct. The common table maps common.url to camunda.client.rest-address; the hint column adds that the REST address is the unified endpoint for all Camunda interaction.

d.text: Incorrect. gRPC traffic uses camunda.client.grpc-address; common.url migrates to the REST address, not the gRPC address.

b.text: Incorrect. The auth.token-url replacement is documented for common.auth-url; common.url itself maps to camunda.client.rest-address.

c.text: Incorrect. The cloud.domain key holds the SaaS domain suffix; the documented replacement for common.url is the unified REST address.

explanation: common.url migrates to camunda.client.rest-address in 8.8. The hint in the common section calls out that the REST address is the unified endpoint for all interaction with Camunda.

docs.title: Camunda Spring Boot starter properties reference

scenario: Apex Health migrates a Spring Boot job worker to the Camunda 8.8 starter for 6000 jobs per day across an HTTPS Self-Managed Orchestration Cluster. The team finds 3 deprecated zeebe.client.security properties in their application.yaml and needs to know how each one maps to the replacement camunda.client.* properties or hints.
question: Which migration matches what the zeebe.client.security deprecation table documents for these three properties?

options.text: Move cert-path to camunda.client.auth.ca-certificate-path, move override-authority to camunda.client.auth.override-authority, and convert plaintext to camunda.client.auth.plaintext for OAuth token endpoints

Move cert-path to camunda.client.security.ca-certificate-path, move override-authority to camunda.client.security.override-authority, and keep plaintext as camunda.client.security.plaintext for explicit control

Move cert-path to camunda.client.cloud.ca-certificate-path, move override-authority to camunda.client.cloud.override-authority, and drop plaintext because Camunda 8 SaaS always uses HTTPS endpoints by default
Move cert-path to camunda.client.ca-certificate-path, move override-authority to camunda.client.override-authority, and drop plaintext because it is now determined by the URL protocol (HTTP or HTTPS)

d.text: Correct. The zeebe.client.security table lists camunda.client.ca-certificate-path as the replacement for cert-path and camunda.client.override-authority as the replacement for override-authority. The plaintext row has no replacement and the Hint states plaintext is now determined by the URL protocol (HTTP or HTTPS).

b.text: Incorrect. The replacement properties live directly under camunda.client, not under a camunda.client.security subgroup, and plaintext has no replacement at all in the new namespace.

a.text: Incorrect. camunda.client.auth holds OAuth-related properties such as token-url, client-id, and client-secret; certificate path and override authority do not live under camunda.client.auth.

c.text: Incorrect. The TLS properties move to camunda.client.ca-certificate-path and camunda.client.override-authority directly, not under camunda.client.cloud.

explanation: The zeebe.client.security deprecation table renames cert-path to camunda.client.ca-certificate-path and override-authority to camunda.client.override-authority. The plaintext property has no direct replacement and the Hint clarifies that plaintext is determined by the URL protocol (HTTP or HTTPS).

docs.title: Camunda Spring Boot starter properties reference

scenario: A retailer runs 4 Spring Boot job workers processing 21000 jobs per day across 8 execution threads. The team upgrades to the Camunda 8.8 starter and must migrate the deprecated zeebe.client.worker properties for default name, default type, max jobs active, and worker threads to the documented replacements.
question: Which renaming set matches the documented zeebe.client.worker replacements?

options.text: Rename default-name to camunda.client.worker.name, default-type to camunda.client.worker.type, max-jobs-active to camunda.client.worker.max-jobs-active, and threads to camunda.client.worker.threads

Rename default-name to camunda.client.worker.defaults.name, default-type to camunda.client.worker.defaults.type, max-jobs-active to camunda.client.worker.defaults.max-jobs-active, and threads to camunda.client.worker.defaults.threads

Rename default-name to camunda.client.job.default-name, default-type to camunda.client.job.default-type, max-jobs-active to camunda.client.job.max-jobs-active, and threads to camunda.client.execution-threads

Rename default-name to camunda.client.worker.defaults.name, default-type to camunda.client.worker.defaults.type, max-jobs-active to camunda.client.worker.defaults.max-jobs-active, and threads to camunda.client.execution-threads

d.text: Correct. The zeebe.client.worker deprecation table maps default-name to camunda.client.worker.defaults.name, default-type to camunda.client.worker.defaults.type, max-jobs-active to camunda.client.worker.defaults.max-jobs-active, and threads to camunda.client.execution-threads (env CAMUNDA_CLIENT_EXECUTIONTHREADS).

b.text: Incorrect. The threads replacement is camunda.client.execution-threads at the root camunda.client level, not camunda.client.worker.defaults.threads.

a.text: Incorrect. The new path includes the defaults segment between worker and the leaf property name (for example, camunda.client.worker.defaults.name), not directly under camunda.client.worker.

c.text: Incorrect. There is no camunda.client.job group; worker defaults sit under camunda.client.worker.defaults rather than under a job namespace.

explanation: The zeebe.client.worker deprecation table places worker defaults under camunda.client.worker.defaults.* (name, type, max-jobs-active) and moves the worker threads count to camunda.client.execution-threads at the root of the camunda.client namespace.

docs.title: Camunda Spring Boot starter properties reference

scenario: Umbra Bank ships a small Camunda 8.8 worker in Node.js for a back-office task that runs about 80 jobs per day. A new contributor asks which JavaScript SDK the Writing good workers page recommends for the example Node.js worker that uses an HTTP library such as Axios.
question: Which Node.js SDK does the Camunda 8.8 Writing good workers page name as the basis for its Node.js example?

options.text: A community Node.js wrapper around the Zeebe gRPC API is the example library, with HTTP calls performed exclusively through the Node.js fetch API since Axios is described as removed in 8.8 in Camunda 8.8
The Camunda 7 External Task Client for Node.js is the example library, used together with Express to expose worker endpoints to the broker via webhooks in Camunda 8.8

The Camunda 8 JavaScript SDK published under github.com/camunda/camunda-8-js-sdk is the example library, and any HTTP library such as Axios may be used alongside it for outbound REST calls from the worker code
There is no Node.js example in Writing good workers; the page redirects Node developers to call the Camunda 8 REST APIs directly from their own HTTP stack without any SDK at all

c.text: Correct. The Node.js client section of Writing good workers links to the camunda-8-js-sdk repository as the basis for the Node.js worker example, and notes that Axios is the assumed HTTP library while any other HTTP library is also fine.

a.text: Incorrect. The page points to the official camunda-8-js-sdk, not a community gRPC wrapper, and does not describe Axios as deprecated.

b.text: Incorrect. The page references the Camunda 8 JavaScript SDK and not a Camunda 7 external-task client, and Express + webhooks is not the pattern shown.

d.text: Incorrect. A Node.js example is present and is based on the camunda-8-js-sdk.

explanation: The Node.js example in Camunda 8.8 Writing good workers is built on the official camunda-8-js-sdk and may use any HTTP library, with Axios used as the illustrative choice in the snippet.

scenario: Zenith Bank, a wholesaler lender that runs 6800 loans per week on Camunda 8.8, queries the historical record of a finished checkout instance from a Node.js TypeScript script. The team observes that even when the instance just completed, the response state field sometimes reports ACTIVE rather than COMPLETED, and they must explain that behaviour to a reviewer before publishing the worker.
question: Why does camunda.getProcessInstance sometimes return state ACTIVE for an instance that just completed in the SDK quickstart?

options.text: The getProcessInstance call returns cached responses for 30 seconds inside the Camunda8 SDK client, so the state field reflects the prior state until the cache expires for that key
The getProcessInstance call reads from the broker write path which always reports the previous state for 10 seconds before the new state is broadcast to attached SDK consumers
The getProcessInstance call reads historical data from the Zeebe exporter which lags behind the actual system state and is eventually consistent, so the state may still read ACTIVE
The getProcessInstance call refuses to return COMPLETED instances unless awaitCompletion was true on the original create call, so the lookup downgrades the state to ACTIVE in the response

b.text: Incorrect. The lag is not a fixed 10-second broker hold; the docs explain it as eventual consistency of historical data read from the Zeebe exporter behind the API.

a.text: Incorrect. The SDK does not document a 30-second client-side response cache for getProcessInstance; the lag comes from the exporter that feeds the historical API.

d.text: Incorrect. The API does not downgrade the state based on the original awaitCompletion flag; the state may read ACTIVE because the exporter has not yet caught up to the broker.

c.text: Correct. The docs note that getProcessInstance reads historical data from the Zeebe exporter, which is eventually consistent and lags behind the actual state, so a just-finished instance can still appear ACTIVE.

explanation: camunda.getProcessInstance reads from the historical store fed by the Zeebe exporter. This data is eventually consistent and lags behind the live broker state, so the response state field may still show ACTIVE for an instance that has actually completed.

scenario: Northwind Insurance processes 4500 claims per day on Camunda 8.8 and needs to fetch the historical process instance state after createProcessInstance returns the process instance key. The TypeScript service must query Operate via the Camunda 8 SDK and accept eventual consistency from the Zeebe exporter when the state is read back through the SDK call inside the main function.
question: Which Camunda 8.8 SDK call retrieves the historical process instance state for a given process instance key?

options.text: Call camunda.queryProcessInstance passing the id field and a consistency strong option so the SDK blocks until the Zeebe exporter has fully caught up to the latest broker state
Call camunda.getProcessInstance passing processInstanceKey and a consistency option with waitUpToMs 5000 so the SDK returns historical data sourced from the Zeebe exporter pipeline
Call camunda.getOperateProcess passing processInstanceKey and a waitMs 5000 option so the SDK returns the live broker state proxied through the Operate gateway endpoint
Call camunda.getProcessByKey passing the result.processInstanceKey value so the SDK returns the live process instance state from the Zeebe broker without any exporter lag at all

d.text: Incorrect. The documented SDK method is camunda.getProcessInstance taking a processInstanceKey, not a getProcessByKey method; the data is sourced from the Zeebe exporter and is eventually consistent.

b.text: Correct. The Camunda 8 SDK exposes camunda.getProcessInstance taking processInstanceKey with an optional consistency.waitUpToMs setting such as 5000; the response comes from the Zeebe exporter and is eventually consistent.

a.text: Incorrect. The documented call is camunda.getProcessInstance, and the consistency option is waitUpToMs measured in milliseconds; there is no strong consistency mode that blocks for full exporter catch-up.

c.text: Incorrect. There is no camunda.getOperateProcess method on the SDK; historical retrieval uses camunda.getProcessInstance, which reads from the Operate API backed by the Zeebe exporter.

explanation: The Camunda 8 SDK retrieves historical process instance data via camunda.getProcessInstance using the processInstanceKey, with an optional consistency option such as waitUpToMs 5000; the response comes from the Zeebe exporter and is eventually consistent, so the state may appear as ACTIVE before showing COMPLETED.

scenario: Beacon, a fintech subscriber to Camunda 8.8 SaaS processing 5400 transactions per day, scaffolds a new Node.js TypeScript project for the Orchestration Cluster API. A senior engineer must follow the documented Get started steps so that the project has TypeScript configured and the SDK installed as a runtime dependency before the team writes the first worker module.
question: Which npm command does the Get started guide list as the step that installs the SDK as a runtime dependency?

options.text: Run npm i camunda-platform-8 in the project directory after npm init and npm install -D typescript, which adds the legacy meta-package as a runtime dependency in package.json
Run npm i -D @camunda8/sdk in the project directory after npm init and npm install -D typescript, which adds the SDK as a development-only dependency in package.json
Run npm i @camunda8/sdk in the project directory after npm init and npm install -D typescript, which adds the SDK as a runtime dependency in package.json
Run npm i zeebe-node in the project directory after npm init and npm install -D typescript, which adds the historical Zeebe-only package as a runtime dependency in package.json

c.text: Correct. The Get started steps say to install the SDK as a dependency with npm i @camunda8/sdk, after creating the project with npm init -y and adding TypeScript via npm install -D typescript.

b.text: Incorrect. The docs install @camunda8/sdk as a regular runtime dependency with npm i, not as a dev dependency; only the typescript tooling itself is installed with -D.

a.text: Incorrect. The published quickstart package is @camunda8/sdk; camunda-platform-8 is not the package listed in the Get started guide.

d.text: Incorrect. The Get started guide installs @camunda8/sdk, the unified Camunda 8 SDK for Node.js; zeebe-node is not the package referenced in the quickstart.

explanation: The Get started guide creates a Node.js TypeScript project with npm init -y, adds TypeScript with npm install -D typescript and npx tsc --init, then installs the SDK as a runtime dependency with npm i @camunda8/sdk.

scenario: Kestrel Telco evaluates a Ruby Zeebe client and a Go Zeebe client from the Camunda Community Hub to integrate Camunda 8.8 with 14 microservices handling 9200 events per day. The procurement team needs to understand the official support stance before signing the rollout, because Camunda commercial support depends on whether the chosen library is a community extension or an officially supported client.
question: Which statement matches the documented support stance for Camunda Community Hub clients in the page intro?

options.text: Camunda Community Hub extensions ship under Camunda commercial support contracts, so the Ruby Zeebe client and Go Zeebe client are covered alongside the official TypeScript SDK in production
Camunda Community Hub extensions are jointly maintained by Camunda and partner vendors, so the Ruby Zeebe client and Go Zeebe client receive enterprise SLA response times from Camunda directly

Camunda Community Hub extensions are listed on the page only as historical references and have been removed from active distribution, so the Ruby Zeebe client and Go Zeebe client must be self-built from forks

Camunda Community Hub extensions are maintained by the community and are not part of the commercial Camunda product, so Camunda does not support them as part of its commercial services to enterprise customers

a.text: Incorrect. The page intro explicitly states that community extensions are not part of the commercial Camunda product, so they fall outside Camunda commercial support contracts.

b.text: Incorrect. The page describes the libraries as community-maintained, not jointly maintained under enterprise SLAs from Camunda for partner vendors.

c.text: Incorrect. The page still lists active community-maintained libraries for Zeebe, Tasklist, Operate and other categories; they have not been removed from distribution.

d.text: Correct. The page intro states that community extensions are maintained by the community, are not part of the commercial Camunda product, and Camunda does not support them as part of its commercial services to enterprise customers.

explanation: The community clients page intro states that Camunda Community Hub extensions are maintained by the community and are not part of the commercial Camunda product, and that Camunda does not support them as part of its commercial services. Officially supported clients are the TypeScript SDK and the Camunda Spring Boot Starter.

scenario: Beacon Healthcare keeps every BPMN model for its Camunda 8.8 Spring Boot service under src/main/resources/bpmn in a nested folder tree, and the service handles 4500 records per day. The team wants a single @Deployment annotation that deploys every BPMN file under that tree at application start-up, without listing each file by name and without changing the tenant from the configured default.
question: Which @Deployment value deploys every BPMN file under the bpmn folder tree at application start-up?

options.text: Apply @Deployment(resources = "classpath:bpmn/*.bpmn", tenantId = "myTenant"); the Spring resource loader deploys every top-level bpmn file to the named tenant at start-up
Apply @Deployment(resources = {"classpath:demoProcess.bpmn","classpath:demoProcess2.bpmn"}); the Spring resource loader deploys the two listed files at start-up
Apply @Deployment(resources = "classpath:demoProcess.bpmn"); the Spring resource loader deploys one named file from the application classpath at start-up
Apply @Deployment(resources = "classpath*:/bpmn/**/*.bpmn"); the Spring resource loader deploys every BPMN file matching that wildcard pattern at start-up

c.text: Incorrect. A single classpath:demoProcess.bpmn value deploys only that one named file; it does not traverse the nested bpmn folder tree the team wants to deploy.

b.text: Incorrect. The array form deploys only the listed files; it does not pick up additional BPMN files added under the bpmn folder tree later.

a.text: Incorrect. The single-asterisk classpath form only matches files in one folder, and changing tenantId routes the deployment to a non-default tenant the team wanted to leave alone.

d.text: Correct. The docs show classpath*:/bpmn/**/*.bpmn as the wildcard pattern; the Spring resource loader walks the nested folder tree at start-up and deploys every matching BPMN file.

explanation: The @Deployment annotation uses the Spring resource loader, so a wildcard like classpath*:/bpmn/**/*.bpmn deploys every BPMN file under the folder tree at application start-up, while single-file and small array forms only deploy the explicitly named resources.

scenario: Tessera Insurance runs a Camunda 8.8 Spring Boot service that warms 250 records into a lookup cache the moment the Camunda client becomes available. The team wants to register the warm-up logic as a regular Spring listener that reacts to the lifecycle event published by the Camunda Spring Boot Starter once the underlying Camunda client has been created, instead of polling for readiness from its own startup code.
question: Which Spring event signals that the Camunda Spring Boot Starter has created the Camunda client?

options.text: Annotate a method with @EventListener accepting CamundaClientClosingEvent; Spring invokes the method once the Camunda Spring Boot Starter is about to close the Camunda client gracefully
Annotate a method with @EventListener accepting CamundaClientReadyEvent; Spring invokes the method once the Camunda Spring Boot Starter has finished registering all @JobWorker subscriptions
Annotate a method with @EventListener accepting CamundaClientStartedEvent; Spring invokes the method once the Camunda Spring Boot Starter has opened the first job subscription on the broker
Annotate a method with @EventListener accepting CamundaClientCreatedEvent; Spring invokes the method once the Camunda Spring Boot Starter has created the Camunda client instance

d.text: Correct. The docs list CamundaClientCreatedEvent as the event published when the Camunda client is created; a method annotated with @EventListener receiving that type runs once at that lifecycle moment.

b.text: Incorrect. CamundaClientReadyEvent is not one of the lifecycle events the Spring Boot Starter publishes; the creation hook is CamundaClientCreatedEvent.

c.text: Incorrect. CamundaClientStartedEvent is not one of the events documented for the Starter; the creation hook is named CamundaClientCreatedEvent.

a.text: Incorrect. CamundaClientClosingEvent fires when the client is closing, not when it has been created, so it does not signal the start of the lifecycle the team needs.

explanation: The Camunda Spring Boot Starter publishes lifecycle events including CamundaClientCreatedEvent and CamundaClientClosingEvent; an @EventListener method that accepts CamundaClientCreatedEvent is invoked the moment the Camunda client has been created.

scenario: Quanta Insurance maintains a Camunda Spring Boot Starter worker that talks to Camunda 8.8 SaaS in production for 4200 policies per day and to a local Self-Managed cluster for integration tests. A reviewer asks how the camunda.client.mode property is supposed to be used across the two environments, because the docs describe meaningful defaults aligned with the distribution's default connection details for each mode.
question: Which statement about camunda.client.mode is consistent with the Modes section?

options.text: Only one mode may be used at a time, and each mode applies meaningful defaults aligned with the distribution's default connection details for that specific Camunda 8 setup
Modes only configure logging verbosity and do not influence connection defaults, so applications must always set grpc-address and rest-address explicitly even when a mode is selected
Modes may be combined freely so that saas and self-managed can both be activated at the same time when the worker connects to two clusters from the same Spring Boot process
Modes are computed automatically by the starter from the runtime DNS suffix of the cluster, so applications cannot select saas or self-managed via the camunda.client.mode property

a.text: Correct. The Modes section states that each mode is made for a Camunda 8 setup, that only one mode may be used at a time, and that each mode applies meaningful defaults aligned with the distribution's default connection details.

c.text: Incorrect. The Modes section explicitly states that only one mode may be used at a time, so saas and self-managed cannot both be active in the same Spring Boot process.

b.text: Incorrect. Modes set connection defaults such as URLs aligned with the distribution; they do not just configure logging verbosity.

d.text: Incorrect. The mode is selected explicitly by setting camunda.client.mode to saas or self-managed; the starter does not derive it from the cluster DNS suffix automatically.

explanation: The Modes section explains that camunda.client.mode supports saas and self-managed presets, each tailored to a Camunda 8 setup with meaningful defaults aligned with the distribution's default connection details. Only one mode may be used at a time, and any other set property can override the defaults.

scenario: Orion Bank operates a Camunda Spring Boot Starter worker on Camunda 8.8 that drives 7600 transactions per hour through the Zeebe gateway. The team currently lets the client choose its transport but performance tests show that the gRPC channel reduces tail latency for their workload. A reviewer wants to confirm which property flips the default behaviour from REST to gRPC.
question: Which property change forces the Camunda client to use gRPC instead of REST as the default transport?

options.text: Set camunda.client.use-grpc-by-default to true so that the Camunda client uses gRPC to communicate with the Camunda APIs by default for every operation in the worker process
Set camunda.client.prefer-rest-over-grpc to true so that the Camunda client uses gRPC to communicate with the Camunda APIs by default for every operation in the worker process
Set camunda.client.prefer-rest-over-grpc to false so that the Camunda client uses gRPC to communicate with the Camunda APIs by default for every operation in the worker process
Set camunda.client.transport to grpc so that the Camunda client uses gRPC to communicate with the Camunda APIs by default for every operation in the worker process

b.text: Incorrect. The property name is prefer-rest-over-grpc; setting it to true keeps REST as the default, since true means preferring REST.

d.text: Incorrect. The documented property is camunda.client.prefer-rest-over-grpc, not camunda.client.transport; there is no transport property listed in the connectivity section.

c.text: Correct. The docs state that by default the client uses REST whenever possible, and that to use gRPC by default you configure camunda.client.prefer-rest-over-grpc to false.

a.text: Incorrect. There is no use-grpc-by-default property in the connectivity section; the documented flag is camunda.client.prefer-rest-over-grpc.

explanation: The connectivity section documents camunda.client.prefer-rest-over-grpc. By default the client uses REST whenever possible; setting prefer-rest-over-grpc to false makes the Camunda client use gRPC by default to communicate with the Camunda APIs.

scenario: Granite Bank runs a Camunda Spring Boot Starter worker on Camunda 8.8 that drives 6300 transactions per hour and must authenticate to a Keycloak identity provider using a client X.509 certificate instead of a client_credentials secret. The platform team wants an SSL context that applies only to the identity provider call, so they configure the documented camunda.client.auth.* keystore properties on the worker process.
question: Which property set configures an exclusive SSL context for the identity provider call in the starter?

options.text: Configure camunda.client.auth.keystore-path, keystore-password, keystore-key-password, truststore-path, and truststore-password to use an exclusive SSL context for the identity provider call
Configure javax.net.ssl.keyStore, javax.net.ssl.keyStorePassword, javax.net.ssl.trustStore, and javax.net.ssl.trustStorePassword under camunda.client.auth to scope the SSL context
Configure camunda.client.ssl.client-keystore, client-keystore-password, client-truststore, and client-truststore-password to use an exclusive SSL context for the identity provider call
Configure spring.ssl.bundle.jks.identity.keystore-path, keystore-password, keystore-key-password, truststore-path, and truststore-password to scope the SSL context to the identity provider call
