b.text: Incorrect. javax.net.ssl.* is the global JVM SSL context; the docs note it is applied as a fallback, while camunda.client.auth.* properties scope the SSL context exclusively to the identity provider call.

a.text: Correct. The docs list keystore-path, keystore-password, keystore-key-password, truststore-path, and truststore-password under camunda.client.auth as the exclusive identity-provider SSL context properties, taking precedence over javax.net.ssl.* and spring.ssl.*.

c.text: Incorrect. The starter property names are camunda.client.auth.keystore-path and similar, not camunda.client.ssl.client-keystore; the camunda.client.ssl prefix is not the documented one.

d.text: Incorrect. spring.ssl.* is a generic Spring Boot SSL context the starter falls back to; the exclusive identity-provider context is configured under camunda.client.auth.* properties.

explanation: The docs list five properties under camunda.client.auth for an exclusive identity-provider SSL context: keystore-path, keystore-password, keystore-key-password, truststore-path, and truststore-password. When set, these take precedence over javax.net.ssl.* and spring.ssl.* configurations for the identity provider call.

scenario: Tessera Insurance runs a Camunda 8.8 Spring Boot service that processes 21000 claims per day for a regional insurer. A platform engineer must run a warm-up routine that primes 4 in-memory caches and pre-fetches reference data the moment the Camunda client finishes connecting, before any job worker starts polling for service tasks.
question: Which Camunda Spring Boot Starter integration lets the team react when the Camunda client has just been created?

options.text: Implement HealthIndicator and inspect the camunda.client.connected gauge so the warm-up routine triggers when the gauge transitions from zero to one during the application start sequence
Annotate a bean method with @JobWorker(type = "startup") so the orchestration cluster pushes a synthetic job to the worker the moment the Camunda client finishes its handshake
Override the run method of CommandLineRunner and call CamundaClient newTopologyRequest in a loop until it returns, then trigger the warm-up routine from inside the runner method
Register a Spring @EventListener method that accepts CamundaClientCreatedEvent so the starter invokes the warm-up routine right after the Camunda client has been created

a.text: Incorrect. The starter does not expose a camunda.client.connected gauge as the lifecycle entry point; the documented hooks are Spring events such as CamundaClientCreatedEvent and CamundaClientClosingEvent.

d.text: Correct. The Camunda Spring Boot Starter integrates with Spring events and publishes CamundaClientCreatedEvent and CamundaClientClosingEvent; a Spring @EventListener consuming CamundaClientCreatedEvent fires exactly when the Camunda client has been created.

b.text: Incorrect. @JobWorker beans subscribe to BPMN service task jobs; there is no synthetic startup job pushed by the orchestration cluster when the Camunda client is created.

c.text: Incorrect. Polling newTopologyRequest from a CommandLineRunner is not the documented lifecycle hook; the starter exposes Spring lifecycle events for that purpose.

explanation: The Spring Boot Starter publishes Camunda client lifecycle events such as CamundaClientCreatedEvent and CamundaClientClosingEvent, and also offers the CamundaClientLifecycleAware interface with onStart and onStop callbacks. A Spring @EventListener for CamundaClientCreatedEvent is the documented way to run code as soon as the client is ready.

scenario: Sable Bank handles 9000 messages per day from a payments gateway and wants to correlate each inbound payment confirmation to the right waiting process instance on Camunda 8.8. The integration team already uses the Zeebe Java client to start instances from the channel adapter, and now needs the matching client call to publish each correlated payment message with a deduplication id and a correlation key drawn from the gateway payload.
question: Which Zeebe Java client call correlates an inbound message to a waiting process instance on Camunda 8.8?

options.text: Call zeebeClient.newBroadcastSignalCommand() with signalName, correlationKey, and variables; the call broadcasts a signal so every running process instance receives the same payload at once
Call zeebeClient.newCorrelateMessageCommand() with messageName, processInstanceKey, and variables; the call correlates the message to one specific running instance by its instance key
Call zeebeClient.newPublishMessageCommand() with messageName, messageId, correlationKey, and variables; the call publishes the message so the broker correlates it to the waiting process instance
Call zeebeClient.newCreateInstanceCommand() with bpmnProcessId, latestVersion, and variables; the call starts a new process instance for the supplied process definition every time it runs

d.text: Incorrect. newCreateInstanceCommand starts a fresh process instance and does not correlate any incoming message; the documented correlation call is newPublishMessageCommand.

c.text: Correct. The docs show newPublishMessageCommand taking messageName, messageId, correlationKey, and variables, and the broker correlates the published message to the waiting process instance.

b.text: Incorrect. There is no newCorrelateMessageCommand on the Zeebe Java client in the docs; the correlation entry point is the publish-message command shown in the example.

a.text: Incorrect. The Calling Zeebe section does not show a broadcast-signal command for message correlation; messages flow through newPublishMessageCommand with a correlationKey instead.

explanation: The Calling Zeebe section shows newPublishMessageCommand as the Zeebe Java client entry point for correlation; it takes messageName, messageId, correlationKey, and variables, and the broker correlates the published message to the waiting process instance.

docs.title: Connecting the workflow engine with your world

scenario: Wren Logistics opens a Camunda 8.8 Zeebe Java client subscription for the jobType serviceA to drive 7500 shipments per day through its dispatch handler. The platform team reviews the glue code and wants to verify that the fluent builder matches the documented snippet exactly, including the order of jobType, handler, and timeout, and the call that actually opens the subscription rather than only registering it.
question: Which fluent Zeebe Java client builder opens a serviceA job subscription with a 10 seconds timeout on Camunda 8.8?

options.text: Chain zeebeClient.newJobSubscription().type("serviceA").lockOwner("w1").lockTime(Duration.ofSeconds(10)).handler(new ExampleJobHandler()).open() to open the subscription
Chain zeebeClient.newPollCommand().jobType("serviceA").handler(new ExampleJobHandler()).timeout(Duration.ofSeconds(10)).send() to poll jobs from the broker once
Chain zeebeClient.newWorker().type("serviceA").handler(new ExampleJobHandler()).lockTime(Duration.ofSeconds(10)).register() to register the worker on the broker
Chain zeebeClient.newWorker().jobType("serviceA").handler(new ExampleJobHandler()).timeout(Duration.ofSeconds(10)).open() to open the subscription for the worker

a.text: Incorrect. The Zeebe Java client builder is newWorker with jobType and timeout, not newJobSubscription with lockOwner and lockTime; that shape belongs to legacy non-Zeebe clients.

c.text: Incorrect. The documented terminal call is open(), and the builder uses jobType plus timeout instead of type plus lockTime, so register() does not match the example.

d.text: Correct. The docs show newWorker().jobType("serviceA").handler(...).timeout(Duration.ofSeconds(10)).open() as the exact fluent chain that opens the long-polling subscription for the worker.

b.text: Incorrect. There is no newPollCommand entry point in the Zeebe Java client snippet; long polling is set up by newWorker, and the terminal call is open() rather than send().

explanation: The Subscribing to tasks section uses zeebeClient.newWorker().jobType("serviceA").handler(...).timeout(Duration.ofSeconds(10)).open(), which long-polls the broker for serviceA jobs; the open() terminal call is what actually starts the subscription.

docs.title: Connecting the workflow engine with your world

scenario: Aurora Marketplace evaluates two connectors for a Camunda 8.8 integration handling 22000 events per day. The architects must compare the HTTP connector, used for outbound REST calls on service tasks, with the Kafka connector, used to bridge messages between the marketplace event bus and Zeebe, and decide which directionality each one offers.
question: Which statement correctly characterizes the directionality of the HTTP connector versus the Kafka connector in Camunda 8.8?

options.text: The HTTP connector is bidirectional with an inbound listener and an outbound worker, while the Kafka connector is one-way and only forwards Kafka records to Zeebe without a job worker on service tasks

Both the HTTP connector and the Kafka connector are bidirectional, each containing a listener that forwards external events into Zeebe and a job worker that emits external messages when service tasks execute
The HTTP connector and the Kafka connector are both one-way connectors that only contain a job worker reacting to service-task execution and they do not subscribe to any external events at all

The HTTP connector is one-way and contains a job worker that processes service tasks doing HTTP calls, while the Kafka connector is bidirectional with a Kafka listener forwarding records to Zeebe plus a worker creating records on service-task execution

b.text: Incorrect. The HTTP connector is documented as a one-way connector containing only a job worker for HTTP calls; only the Kafka connector is bidirectional with both an inbound listener and an outbound worker.

d.text: Correct. The HTTP connector is a one-way connector with a job worker that processes service tasks doing HTTP calls, while the Kafka connector is bidirectional, combining a Kafka listener that forwards records to Zeebe with a job worker that creates Kafka records when a service task is executed.

a.text: Incorrect. The roles are inverted relative to the documentation: the HTTP connector is one-way and the Kafka connector is bidirectional.

c.text: Incorrect. The Kafka connector contains a Kafka listener that forwards records into Zeebe, which makes it bidirectional rather than a pure outbound worker.

explanation: Per the Camunda 8.8 best-practices guide, the HTTP connector is one-way and consists of a job worker that handles service tasks doing HTTP calls, while the Kafka connector is bidirectional, combining a Kafka listener that forwards records into Zeebe with a job worker that emits Kafka records when a service task is executed.

docs.title: Connecting the workflow engine with your world

scenario: Helios Insurance maintains 35 Camunda 8.8 processes that integrate with downstream services across 6000 claims per day. The platform team must decide a default policy for when developers reach for a custom job-worker glue code versus when they extract or adopt a dedicated connector, given the operational cost and limited extensibility of connectors.
question: What is the general recommendation in Camunda 8.8 for choosing between custom glue code and an existing connector?

options.text: Prefer custom glue code for outbound calls and use connectors exclusively for inbound integrations, regardless of how complex the integration is or how reusable the connector would be across processes
Always write custom glue code and never adopt a connector for any service-task integration, even when orchestrating a serverless function across 40 different process definitions in the cluster
Always prefer an existing connector over custom glue code; connectors centralize integration logic and remove the operational overhead from every team running a Camunda 8.8 cluster in production

Prefer custom glue code by default and only adopt a connector when there is a good reason, such as a complex integration requiring little customization or a scenario where no custom glue code is actually needed

c.text: Incorrect. The guide advises preferring custom glue code unless there is a good reason to adopt a connector; connectors are not the default and they come with operational and testing trade-offs.

b.text: Incorrect. The guide endorses connectors for specific scenarios such as orchestrating serverless functions with the AWS Lambda connector, so refusing every connector is not the documented policy.

d.text: Correct. The rule of thumb is to prefer custom glue code unless there is a good reason to go with an existing connector, such as a complex integration with little customization, a scenario where no custom glue code is needed, or a reusable generic adapter.

a.text: Incorrect. The recommendation is not framed by inbound versus outbound direction; it is framed by whether the integration justifies the limits and operational cost of running a connector.

explanation: As a rule of thumb in Camunda 8.8, prefer custom glue code and only adopt a connector when there is a good reason, such as a complex integration that needs little customization, a scenario where no custom glue code is required, or a reusable generic adapter that can be operated centrally.

docs.title: Connecting the workflow engine with your world

scenario: Zenith Bank is sketching the architecture for a Camunda 8.8 process solution that will orchestrate 18000 transactions per day across a payments microservice. An architect must decide how the application will reach the workflow engine before any BPMN is drawn, and wants to align on the two basic options the docs describe for connecting an application to Zeebe.
question: Which pair of options does the best-practices guide describe for connecting an application to Zeebe in Camunda 8?

options.text: Run the workflow engine inside the application as a library or run it as a sidecar in the same pod and let both modes share the same in-memory event bus for communication
Write programming glue code using the client library for the chosen language, or use an existing connector that just needs configuration to integrate with the target system
Use BPMN listeners that the broker pushes directly into the application classpath, or rely on a SOAP gateway the broker exposes for legacy systems that cannot consume gRPC
Use a JDBC-style driver shipped with the broker for in-process calls or attach to the embedded engine running inside the application JVM and bypass any remote protocol layer

d.text: Incorrect. Zeebe is always a remote system for the application and is reached via gRPC or REST; there is no in-process JDBC-style driver or embedded engine in Camunda 8.

a.text: Incorrect. The workflow engine is not embedded into the application in Camunda 8; the docs describe Zeebe as a remote system reached over remote protocols.

c.text: Incorrect. The docs do not describe broker-pushed listeners or a SOAP gateway as connection options; Zeebe is reached over gRPC or REST.

b.text: Correct. The connecting-the-workflow-engine page describes two basic options: write programming code that leverages a client library for the chosen language, or use an existing connector that only needs configuration.

explanation: Zeebe is a remote system for the application and is reached via remote protocols such as gRPC or REST, similarly to how a database is reached via JDBC. The best-practices page lists exactly two options to connect an application to Zeebe: writing programming glue code with the client library, or using an existing connector that only needs configuration.

docs.title: Connecting the workflow engine with your world

scenario: Lattice Insurance runs a Camunda 8.8 Java service that ingests 4200 policies per day from a regional insurer. A developer using the Zeebe Java client must start a new process instance for every incoming policy and later correlate an asynchronous broker message back to the waiting instance using the documented client API calls.
question: Which Zeebe Java client commands does the best-practices guide use to start a new process instance and correlate a message to an existing instance?

options.text: Call zeebeClient newCreateInstanceCommand with bpmnProcessId, latestVersion, and variables to start an instance, and zeebeClient newPublishMessageCommand with messageName, messageId, correlationKey, and variables to correlate a message
Call zeebeClient newWorker with jobType to subscribe a worker that internally creates a process instance, and zeebeClient newThrowErrorCommand to deliver a message to a waiting BPMN instance
Call zeebeClient newCorrelateMessageCommand with correlationKey to start a process instance, and zeebeClient newJobCompleteCommand to forward the inbound message to the targeted process instance
Call zeebeClient newDeployResourceCommand to start a process instance and zeebeClient newSetVariablesCommand to inject a payload into the instance that should receive the broker message

d.text: Incorrect. newDeployResourceCommand deploys a process definition rather than starting an instance, and newSetVariablesCommand updates variables on an existing instance instead of correlating a message.

a.text: Correct. The Java client snippet uses newCreateInstanceCommand with bpmnProcessId, latestVersion, and variables to start a process instance, and newPublishMessageCommand with messageName, messageId, correlationKey, and variables to correlate a message to a waiting instance.

b.text: Incorrect. newWorker registers a job worker subscription rather than starting an instance, and newThrowErrorCommand reports a BPMN error from inside a job, not a message correlation.

c.text: Incorrect. There is no newCorrelateMessageCommand or newJobCompleteCommand on the documented Java client surface; the documented call is newPublishMessageCommand.

explanation: The Calling Zeebe snippet uses two Java client commands. newCreateInstanceCommand starts a process instance by bpmnProcessId, latestVersion, and variables; newPublishMessageCommand correlates a message by messageName, a deduplication messageId, the correlationKey from the inbound message, and any variables that should be merged into the receiving instance.

docs.title: Connecting the workflow engine with your world

scenario: Helios Airline is planning a Camunda 8.8 booking platform that will move 8500 messages per day between Zeebe and three downstream systems for a regional carrier. The lead architect wants to align the team on the three integration technologies the best-practices guide highlights as most common when teams write custom glue code.
question: Which technology examples does the best-practices guide highlight as the most common integration targets for custom glue code?

options.text: Apache Camel, Mulesoft, and IBM MQ are the three integration technologies highlighted with code samples in the technology examples section of the best-practices guide

REST endpoints, messaging such as AMQP and RabbitMQ, and Apache Kafka are the three integration technologies highlighted with code samples in the technology examples section of the best-practices guide
GraphQL, MQTT, and Apache Pulsar are the three integration technologies highlighted with code samples in the technology examples section of the best-practices guide
WebSockets, gRPC streaming, and Apache Cassandra are the three integration technologies highlighted with code samples in the technology examples section of the best-practices guide

c.text: Incorrect. GraphQL, MQTT, and Apache Pulsar are not the examples on the page; the documented examples are REST, messaging (AMQP/RabbitMQ), and Kafka.

a.text: Incorrect. Apache Camel, Mulesoft, and IBM MQ are not the examples on the page; the documented examples are REST, messaging, and Kafka.

d.text: Incorrect. WebSockets, gRPC streaming, and Cassandra are not the examples on the page; the documented examples are REST, messaging, and Kafka.

b.text: Correct. The technology examples section calls out REST endpoints, messaging (often AMQP via RabbitMQ), and Apache Kafka topics, and links to the Ticket Booking and Flowing Retail examples for each.

explanation: The technology examples section explicitly says most projects ask for REST, messaging, or Kafka. It links to a Java/Spring Boot REST endpoint that starts process instances, a RabbitMQ/AMQP example that correlates inbound messages with running instances, and a Kafka example using Spring Cloud Streams to subscribe to topics and emit records from service tasks.

docs.title: Connecting the workflow engine with your world

scenario: Nimbus Utility runs a Camunda 8.8 platform that orchestrates 11000 events per day across 5 microservices. An architect must explain to a steering group what a connector is in Camunda 8, how it differs from custom glue code that lives inside each microservice, and what its deployment shape looks like before approving a procurement decision.
question: Which definition of a Camunda 8 connector matches the best-practices guide?

options.text: A connector is a BPMN extension element evaluated inside the broker that replaces job workers entirely and removes the need for any external runtime for inbound or outbound communication with target systems

A connector is a dedicated application that implements a uni- or bidirectional connection translating in one or both directions of communication, configured rather than coded, and offered through Camunda or the Marketplace
A connector is a YAML mapping file inside the broker configuration directory that describes which Spring Boot beans should be wired to which BPMN service task type when the broker boots up
A connector is a community-maintained library bundled into the Camunda client jar, run inside the application JVM, and only used for outbound calls such as REST or Kafka publishing scenarios

a.text: Incorrect. Connectors are not BPMN extension elements evaluated inside the broker; they are external applications that translate communication.

b.text: Correct. The Connectors section defines a connector as a typically dedicated application that implements a uni- or bidirectional translation with a target system, is configured rather than coded, and is offered both by Camunda and via the Marketplace.

d.text: Incorrect. Connectors are not client-jar libraries running inside the application JVM; they are separate applications and can be bidirectional, not only outbound.

c.text: Incorrect. Connectors are not YAML wirings inside the broker; they are dedicated runtime applications described in the connectors section of the best-practices page.

explanation: The Connectors section describes a connector as a dedicated application that translates communication in one or both directions, replacing the need to write glue code with configuration. Examples on the page include the HTTP connector (one-way job worker) and the Kafka connector (bidirectional with a listener and a job worker).

docs.title: Connecting the workflow engine with your world

scenario: Catalyst Insurance ships 13000 invoices per day from a Camunda 8.8 service for a regional insurer. The send-invoice automation succeeds for 92% of cases but fails for the remaining cases, and the business demands that an unsent invoice is automatically routed to a human user task instead of staying as an unresolved technical incident in Operate forever.
question: Which BPMN modeling technique does the best-practices guide recommend so that the process reacts to an unsent invoice instead of leaving it as a technical incident?

options.text: Replace the send-invoice service task with a script task that wraps the call in a try/catch and writes the unsent flag into a process variable that a downstream user task reads at runtime
Wrap the send-invoice service task in an embedded subprocess with a timer boundary event so the process retries the activity every 2 hours until the invoice service eventually becomes available again

Configure a high taskDefinition retries number on the send-invoice service task and rely on the resulting incident in Operate to alert a human operator to take care of the invoice manually outside the BPMN model
Attach a BPMN error event to the send-invoice task and route its outgoing flow to a user task assigned to a human user who is now in charge of taking care of delivering the invoice manually

c.text: Incorrect. Incidents are technical interventions in Operate; the page recommends modeling a BPMN error event so the process itself reacts to the deviation.

a.text: Incorrect. The recommended pattern is a BPMN error event with a user task on its outgoing flow, not a script-task plus variable plus downstream user task.

d.text: Correct. The page recommends a BPMN error event on the send-invoice activity so the process can route to a user task assigned to a human user who is now in charge of taking care of delivering the invoice.

b.text: Incorrect. A timer boundary event is not the documented pattern for this deviation; the page describes a BPMN error event with a user task fallback.

explanation: The Using BPMN error events section uses exactly this example: when the invoice cannot be sent automatically, a BPMN error event leaves the activity and routes to a user task assigned to a human user, who is now in charge of taking care of delivering the invoice. This makes the deviation visible and resolvable inside the process model rather than as a technical incident in Operate.

scenario: Junction Lender models a Camunda 8.8 onboarding process for 5200 loans per day at a regional lender. The check-creditworthiness service task returns the customer's credit rating most of the time, but sometimes the ID it received does not match any known person and no rating can be produced; the team needs to model these two situations cleanly inside one BPMN diagram.
question: Which rule of thumb does the best-practices guide give for modeling expected results versus situations where the result cannot be reached?

options.text: Replace the exclusive gateway with a script task that reads the result variable and uses sequence flow conditions only for the unreachable-result case, while expected results continue along the default flow of the diagram

Model every deviation, including expected business outcomes and unreachable results, with the same boundary error event so the BPMN diagram has a single uniform fallback path that downstream readers can follow easily

Treat both expected results and unreachable results as message events emitted by the service task and let a downstream event-based gateway choose which downstream path the instance takes when each message arrives

Deal with expected results of activities by means of gateways, and model exceptional errors that hinder reaching the expected result as boundary error events on the activity that produced the deviation in the model

b.text: Incorrect. The page distinguishes expected results from exceptional errors; it does not collapse them into one boundary error event.

d.text: Correct. The rule of thumb states: expected results of activities (creditworthy yes/no) are handled with gateways, while exceptional errors that hinder reaching the expected result (ID not associated with any known person) are modeled with boundary error events.

c.text: Incorrect. Message events and an event-based gateway are not the documented pattern here; the pattern uses XOR gateways for expected results and boundary error events for exceptions.

a.text: Incorrect. The page keeps the exclusive gateway for the expected result; the exceptional path is modeled as a boundary error event, not via sequence flow conditions.

explanation: The Distinguishing between exceptions and results section gives the rule of thumb: an exclusive gateway handles expected results such as creditworthy yes or no, while a boundary error event handles the case where the result cannot be obtained, such as an unknown customer ID. Both can lead to the same downstream consequence, but they remain visually and semantically distinct in the BPMN model.

scenario: Meridian Bank ships 4800 invoices per day from a Camunda 8.8 service for a regional bank. The invoice service is down and the team debates whether a missing rating from a downstream scoring service is technical or business; the lead architect wants to refocus the discussion on how the process should react rather than where the error originates inside the systems.
question: Which framing does the best-practices guide recommend when the team labels errors as business versus technical?

options.text: Classify every error strictly as either business or technical at design time and reject any service task that cannot be unambiguously categorised against that taxonomy during process review meetings

Restrict business reactions to BPMN error events and forbid retries on any service task that emits a business error code, so the process model documents every business decision exhaustively without exception

Treat every observable error as technical, hide it from the BPMN diagram, and only escalate to the business when an operations engineer in Operate decides that the incident has a measurable revenue impact at runtime

Talk about business reactions, which are modeled in the process, and technical reactions, which are handled generically through retries and incidents; even a technical problem can qualify for a business reaction in the model

d.text: Correct. The page recommends talking about business reactions (modeled in the process) and technical reactions (retries or incidents), and explicitly says that even a technical problem can qualify for a business reaction.

b.text: Incorrect. The page does not restrict business reactions to BPMN error events; the framing is about the reaction, not the BPMN construct.

a.text: Incorrect. The page actively warns against long discussions about strict business-versus-technical classification of the source error.

c.text: Incorrect. The recommendation is the opposite: business reactions should appear in the model rather than only as Operate incidents.

explanation: The Business vs technical errors section reframes the conversation: instead of debating the source of an error, focus on the reaction. Business reactions live in the BPMN model (manual invoice send, default rating). Technical reactions stay generic (retries, incidents). The example shows that an invoice-service outage can lead to either a business or a technical reaction.

scenario: Lumen Insurer runs an onboarding process on Camunda 8.8 for 3300 policies per day at a regional insurer. After a CRM-billing inconsistency hit the marketing list, a process owner asks the architect to list the three basic business strategies the best-practices guide names for dealing with such inconsistencies before deciding which one to apply to the campaign-list case.
question: Which three basic strategies for handling a consistency problem does the best-practices guide list?

options.text: Detect, Notify, and Archive, where Detect runs a scheduled audit, Notify pages an on-call engineer through Operate, and Archive freezes the affected records in a read-only data store for later forensic analysis

Retry, Compensate, and Escalate, where Retry replays the failed call automatically, Compensate undoes the upstream side effect, and Escalate routes the case to a senior business owner for manual reconciliation against the records

Lock, Quarantine, and Replay, where Lock holds the downstream entity, Quarantine isolates a corrupted record, and Replay reissues the original event from an audit log of broker-side commands kept in storage

Ignore the inconsistency when business impact is low; apologize when the effects only become visible later; resolve it directly through means such as reconciliation jobs or BPMN-modeled Saga compensation in the process

b.text: Incorrect. The page lists three strategies: ignore, apologize, and resolve; retry and escalate are not among the three.

d.text: Correct. The Business strategies section names exactly three: ignore the inconsistency, apologize for it, or resolve it directly, for example through reconciliation jobs or the BPMN Saga pattern with compensation events.

c.text: Incorrect. Lock, Quarantine, and Replay are not the three strategies on this page; the three are ignore, apologize, and resolve.

a.text: Incorrect. Detect, Notify, and Archive are not the three documented strategies; the three are ignore, apologize, and resolve.

explanation: The Business strategies to handle inconsistency section lists three options. Ignore the inconsistency when its business impact is low. Apologize for it once the effects come to light. Resolve it directly through reconciliation jobs or by modeling a Saga in BPMN with compensation events. The right choice is a business decision driven by the cost/value ratio.

scenario: A Helios Insurance modeling team handles 9000 invoice events per day in Camunda 8.8 and debates whether a downstream invoice-service outage should be classified as a 'business error' or a 'technical error' before deciding what to draw in the BPMN. The team risks long discussions about classification and wants to align on the documented framing.
question: What does the Camunda 8.8 best-practices guide recommend as the primary distinction when deciding how to handle a problem in a process model?

options.text: Treat every downstream technical outage as a business error so it always becomes a modeled BPMN branch and the operations team can decide later if a retry would have been the better generic technical response at runtime

Classify the problem strictly as a business error or as a technical error based on the originating source, then map technical errors to retries and business errors to boundary events without further analysis of the desired reaction

Treat every problem as a technical error first and only escalate to a modeled BPMN branch after the workflow engine has exhausted all configured retries on the offending service task across the cluster runtime

Distinguish between business reactions, which are modeled in the process, and technical reactions, which are handled generically with retries or incidents, focusing on the reaction to the problem rather than its source

b.text: Incorrect. The guide explicitly cautions that classifying by source leads to confusing discussions about whether a problem is technical or not; the relevant distinction is how you react, not where the error came from.

a.text: Incorrect. Many technical outages should fall back to generic retries or incidents rather than always becoming a modeled BPMN branch; the choice depends on the desired business reaction.

d.text: Correct. The recommendation is to talk about business reactions, which are modeled in the process, and technical reactions, which are handled generically using retries or incidents, and to focus on the reaction rather than the source of the error.

c.text: Incorrect. The decision between a modeled reaction and a generic retry is driven by the desired business reaction to the problem, not by an unconditional 'retries first, model later' policy on every problem.

explanation: The Camunda 8.8 best-practices guide reframes the discussion: instead of arguing whether an error is business or technical by source, it recommends distinguishing business reactions (modeled explicitly in the process, like sending the invoice manually) from technical reactions (handled generically via retries or incidents).

scenario: Lattice Insurance models a customer onboarding business transaction in Camunda 8.8 that registers 8000 policies per day across a CRM and a billing system. When a downstream step fails after the CRM has already created the customer record, the team needs the process to undo the prior successful step instead of leaving stale data behind.
question: Which Camunda 8.8 mechanism implements the Saga pattern for long-running business transactions?

options.text: BPMN signal end events broadcast a global rollback signal that automatically reverts every external system call made during the completed business transaction
BPMN error boundary events on each service task roll back database changes through the orchestration cluster transaction coordinator within the broker
BPMN message boundary events publish rollback messages to every prior task and require the broker to acknowledge each compensating step as transactional
BPMN compensation events link each completed task with its undo task and trigger compensating activities when the process raises a compensation throw event

d.text: Correct. Camunda supports the Saga pattern through BPMN compensation events, which link tasks with their undo tasks; on a compensation throw, all compensating activities of previously completed tasks are executed, such as deactivating the customer in the CRM.

b.text: Incorrect. Error boundary events catch failures during execution but do not invoke registered undo activities for already completed tasks; the Saga pattern relies on BPMN compensation, not transactional rollback.

c.text: Incorrect. Message boundary events handle inter-process correlation and do not provide a mechanism to undo previously completed tasks; Camunda's Saga support is built on compensation events.

a.text: Incorrect. Signal events broadcast notifications but do not run the per-task undo activities that the Saga pattern requires; the documented mechanism is BPMN compensation linked to each task.

explanation: The Saga pattern undoes completed work instead of rolling it back as a single distributed transaction. Camunda 8.8 supports Saga through BPMN compensation events that link each task to its undo task; throwing a compensation runs the compensating activities of all successfully completed tasks, for example deactivating a customer that the CRM cannot delete.

scenario: Orion fintech is bootstrapping a new Spring Boot 3 service that will process 7500 transactions per day on Camunda 8.8. A developer is adding the very first Maven dependency to pom.xml and must use the correct artifact identifier for the official Camunda 8.8 Spring Boot integration.
question: Which Maven coordinates add the Camunda Spring Boot Starter for Camunda 8.8?

options.text: groupId io.zeebe, artifactId spring-zeebe-starter, version 8.8.x which keeps the Spring Zeebe SDK identifier on the Camunda 8.8 release line
groupId io.camunda, artifactId camunda-spring-boot-starter, version 8.8.x which is the official starter dependency for Camunda 8.8 projects
groupId org.camunda.bpm, artifactId camunda-spring-boot-starter, version 8.8.x which reuses the Camunda 7 groupId for the Camunda 8 starter
groupId io.camunda, artifactId camunda-spring-zeebe-starter, version 8.8.x which renames the legacy SDK starter under the io.camunda groupId

a.text: Incorrect. The Spring Zeebe SDK used io.zeebe coordinates and is being phased out; on Camunda 8.8 the documented dependency is camunda-spring-boot-starter under io.camunda.

d.text: Incorrect. There is no camunda-spring-zeebe-starter artifact; the documented dependency on Camunda 8.8 is camunda-spring-boot-starter under the io.camunda groupId.

c.text: Incorrect. org.camunda.bpm is the Camunda 7 Maven groupId; the Camunda 8 starter is published under io.camunda, not under the Camunda 7 namespace.

b.text: Correct. Step 1 of the Getting Started guide adds the dependency with groupId io.camunda, artifactId camunda-spring-boot-starter, and version 8.8.x for Camunda 8.8 Spring Boot projects.

explanation: Step 1 of the Camunda Spring Boot Starter getting started guide adds io.camunda:camunda-spring-boot-starter:8.8.x as the Maven dependency. The io.zeebe coordinates belong to the legacy Spring Zeebe SDK and org.camunda.bpm belongs to Camunda 7.

docs.title: Camunda Spring Boot Starter get started

scenario: Wren Bank runs 4 Java microservices on Camunda 8.7 that handle 6300 transactions per day for a regional bank. The platform team is planning the jump to Camunda 8.8 and must align on which Spring integration is now the official Camunda 8 entry point for Spring Boot projects, and which legacy artifact must be migrated away from before 8.10 ships.
question: Which Spring integration is the official Camunda 8 entry point as of version 8.8, and what is the migration deadline?

options.text: The Camunda Connect SDK is the official entry point in 8.8 and replaces both the Spring Zeebe SDK and the Camunda Java Client; Spring Boot projects should migrate away from those two artifacts before the 8.9 release

Camunda 8.8 introduces a generic Camunda gRPC starter for any JVM framework that wraps the Camunda Java Client; the Spring Zeebe SDK is renamed to Camunda Spring Zeebe SDK and is supported indefinitely

Spring Zeebe SDK remains the official integration in Camunda 8.8 and only the artifact id changes; teams can keep their existing imports and configuration untouched for the entire 8.x release series without any migration work

The Camunda Spring Boot Starter replaces the Spring Zeebe SDK as of version 8.8, uses the new Camunda Java Client with REST as the default protocol, and teams should migrate before the 8.10 release removes the SDK

c.text: Incorrect. The Spring Zeebe SDK is being phased out and will be removed in 8.10; the official entry point in 8.8 is the Camunda Spring Boot Starter.

a.text: Incorrect. The Camunda Connect SDK is not the documented entry point; the page names the Camunda Spring Boot Starter and the 8.10 removal of the SDK.

b.text: Incorrect. There is no generic Camunda gRPC starter and the Spring Zeebe SDK is not supported indefinitely; the page calls out the 8.10 removal.

d.text: Correct. The page states that the Camunda Spring Boot Starter replaces the Spring Zeebe SDK as of version 8.8, uses the new Camunda Java Client, has REST as the default protocol (gRPC configurable), and the Spring Zeebe SDK will be removed in version 8.10.

explanation: Camunda 8.8 promotes the Camunda Spring Boot Starter to the official Spring Boot integration. It uses the new Camunda Java Client, defaults to REST while letting teams keep gRPC, follows semantic versioning for non-alpha features, and replaces the Spring Zeebe SDK. The SDK will be removed in version 8.10, so teams should migrate before upgrading.

docs.title: Camunda Spring Boot Starter getting started

scenario: Yarrow Insurer is upgrading a Camunda 8.8 platform that handles 7100 policies per day at a regional insurer. The team standardised on Spring Boot 4.0 last quarter, and the lead developer must pick the correct Maven artifact and the minimum Camunda 8.8 patch release that ships a dedicated starter for Spring Boot 4.0 instead of the default Spring Boot 3.5 baseline.
question: Which Maven artifact and minimum Camunda 8.8 patch release should the team pick for Spring Boot 4.0 compatibility?

options.text: Use the camunda-spring-zeebe-sdk artifact from version 8.8.0 and add the spring-boot-starter-parent BOM at version 4.0.0; this is the documented combination for Spring Boot 4.0 in 8.8

Use the camunda-spring-boot-4-starter artifact starting from Camunda Spring Boot Starter version 8.8.9, which is the dedicated artifact that adds Spring Boot 4.0 support as a drop-in replacement for the default starter

Use the default camunda-spring-boot-starter artifact from version 8.8.0 with the property camunda.spring.boot.version set to 4.0 in application.yaml; the starter then switches its bundled Spring Boot to 4.0
Use the camunda-spring-boot-3-starter artifact from version 8.8.15 with a Spring Boot 4.0 override in the parent pom; this artifact is the dedicated path to running Spring Boot 4.0 on Camunda 8.8

b.text: Correct. The version compatibility table documents camunda-spring-boot-4-starter at version >=8.8.9 as the dedicated artifact for Spring Boot 4.0 and recommends it as a drop-in replacement for the default starter.

c.text: Incorrect. There is no camunda.spring.boot.version property that switches the bundled Spring Boot; a dedicated artifact is provided instead.

d.text: Incorrect. camunda-spring-boot-3-starter is an alias for the Spring Boot 3 path; the Spring Boot 4.0 path uses camunda-spring-boot-4-starter.

a.text: Incorrect. The Spring Zeebe SDK is being phased out; the Spring Boot 4.0 path uses camunda-spring-boot-4-starter at version >=8.8.9.

explanation: The version compatibility table on the getting-started page lists three artifacts. The default camunda-spring-boot-starter at 8.8.x bundles Spring Boot 3.5.x. The dedicated camunda-spring-boot-4-starter from 8.8.9 onward bundles Spring Boot 4.0.x and is a drop-in replacement. From 8.8.15 a camunda-spring-boot-3-starter alias is available to stay on Spring Boot 3 across newer Camunda releases.

docs.title: Camunda Spring Boot Starter getting started

scenario: Vertex bank orchestrates 4500 transactions per day across Spring Boot microservices on Camunda 8.8. The architecture review needs to confirm which transport protocol the Camunda Spring Boot Starter uses by default before approving the orchestration cluster network policies.
question: Which protocol does the Camunda Spring Boot Starter use by default in Camunda 8.8?

options.text: REST is the default protocol of the Camunda Spring Boot Starter in 8.8 and gRPC is available as a configurable alternative for the same client surface
AMQP is the default protocol of the Camunda Spring Boot Starter in 8.8 and REST is offered for Tasklist endpoints only across Spring Boot services
GraphQL is the default protocol of the Camunda Spring Boot Starter in 8.8 and REST is offered only for orchestration cluster admin endpoints
gRPC is the default protocol of the Camunda Spring Boot Starter in 8.8 and REST is offered only as an experimental alpha for the same client surface

a.text: Correct. The migration note states that the Camunda Spring Boot Starter uses REST as the default protocol in 8.8 with gRPC as a configurable alternative on the same Camunda Java Client surface.

d.text: Incorrect. The defaults are reversed: REST is the default protocol in 8.8 and gRPC is the configurable alternative, not an alpha.

b.text: Incorrect. The starter integrates the Camunda 8 gRPC and REST APIs; AMQP is not a transport protocol offered by the Camunda Spring Boot Starter.

c.text: Incorrect. The starter does not use GraphQL; it integrates the Camunda 8 gRPC and REST APIs with REST as the default protocol from 8.8 onward.

explanation: The Camunda Spring Boot Starter in 8.8 wraps the new Camunda Java Client and defaults to REST, with gRPC available as a configurable protocol. It replaces the Spring Zeebe SDK as of 8.8 and is part of the Camunda 8 public API that follows Semantic Versioning.

scenario: Quanta logistics runs 9200 shipments per day on Camunda 8.8.15 with Spring Boot 3.5 and plans to upgrade to Camunda 8.9 next quarter. Two carrier integrations are not yet certified for Spring Boot 4.0, so the team must pick a Camunda Spring Boot Starter artifact that lets the project stay on Spring Boot 3 across the upgrade.
question: Which Camunda Spring Boot Starter artifact lets the team stay on Spring Boot 3 when upgrading beyond Camunda 8.8?

options.text: Use camunda-spring-boot-starter from 8.8.x onward which keeps Spring Boot 3.5.x as the bundled version even after upgrading to Camunda 8.9 releases
Use spring-zeebe-starter from the 8.8.15 patch which is the renamed alias dedicated to teams that want to keep Spring Boot 3 across the 8.x line
Use camunda-spring-boot-3-starter available from the 8.8.15 patch which is an alias that keeps Spring Boot 3 when upgrading to Camunda 8.9 or newer
Use camunda-spring-boot-4-starter from the 8.8.9 patch which bundles Spring Boot 3.5.x as a long-term alias while you remain on a JDK 17 baseline

a.text: Incorrect. In Camunda 8.9 or newer the default camunda-spring-boot-starter will be based on Spring Boot 4.0; staying on Spring Boot 3 across the upgrade requires the dedicated alias artifact.

c.text: Correct. From the 8.8.15 patch a camunda-spring-boot-3-starter module is available as an alias for camunda-spring-boot-starter in 8.8; it lets teams stay on Spring Boot 3 when upgrading to Camunda 8.9 or newer.

d.text: Incorrect. camunda-spring-boot-4-starter bundles Spring Boot 4.0.x and is the Spring Boot 4 path, not an alias that keeps Spring Boot 3 on later Camunda releases.

b.text: Incorrect. spring-zeebe-starter is the legacy Spring Zeebe SDK identifier and will be removed in 8.10; the documented Spring Boot 3 alias is camunda-spring-boot-3-starter.

explanation: Camunda 8.8.15 introduced camunda-spring-boot-3-starter as an alias for the default starter. The default camunda-spring-boot-starter switches to Spring Boot 4.0 in Camunda 8.9, so teams that want to stay on Spring Boot 3 should pin the camunda-spring-boot-3-starter alias when upgrading.

docs.title: Camunda Spring Boot Starter version compatibility

scenario: Aurora Bank runs a Java Spring Boot service that orchestrates 7500 transactions per hour against a Camunda 8.8 SaaS cluster. The platform engineer configures the Camunda Spring Boot Starter through application.yaml and must select the correct mode and authentication block so that the service obtains an OIDC token at start-up and reaches the gRPC and REST addresses provided by the SaaS cluster.
question: Which application.yaml mode and auth combination connects the Camunda Spring Boot Starter to a Camunda 8.8 SaaS cluster?

options.text: Set camunda.client.mode to self-managed with auth.method basic and provide username and password under camunda.client.auth along with grpc-address and rest-address pointing at the SaaS endpoints
Set camunda.client.mode to saas and provide camunda.client.auth with client-id, client-secret, and token-url; populate grpc-address and rest-address as absolute URIs pointing at the SaaS endpoints
Set camunda.client.mode to self-managed with auth.method none and supply grpc-address and rest-address pointing at the SaaS endpoints without any further authentication block configured
Set camunda.client.mode to saas with auth.method oidc and supply audience, scope, client-id, and client-secret in camunda.client.auth along with grpc-address and rest-address

b.text: Correct. The SaaS block of the docs uses camunda.client.mode set to saas with a camunda.client.auth block containing client-id, client-secret, and token-url; both grpc-address and rest-address must be absolute URIs of the form scheme://host(:port).

a.text: Incorrect. Basic authentication is documented under self-managed mode for clusters protected by HTTP basic auth; SaaS clusters expect OIDC client credentials rather than username and password.

c.text: Incorrect. The no-authentication block targets self-managed clusters running without auth; SaaS always requires OIDC client credentials issued by the Camunda Console for the Orchestration Cluster.

d.text: Incorrect. The saas block in the docs lists only client-id, client-secret, and token-url under camunda.client.auth; audience and scope are documented under the self-managed OIDC block, not saas mode.

explanation: For SaaS, the Camunda Spring Boot Starter is configured with camunda.client.mode: saas and a camunda.client.auth block containing client-id, client-secret, and token-url. The gRPC and REST addresses must be absolute URIs. Basic authentication and the no-auth method are documented under self-managed mode only.

docs.title: Get started with the Camunda Java Client

scenario: Helios Insurance ships a Camunda 8.8 Spring Boot service that processes 12000 claims per day and must deploy its BPMN process model packaged on the classpath each time the application starts. The lead developer annotates the main Spring Boot application class so that the Starter automatically deploys the bundled BPMN resource to the Orchestration Cluster on every application start-up.
question: Which annotation does the Camunda Spring Boot Starter use to deploy a classpath BPMN resource on application start-up?

options.text: Annotate the main application class with @CamundaDeploy(resources = "classpath:demoProcess.bpmn") so that the Starter deploys the named BPMN resource to the Orchestration Cluster on start-up
Annotate the main application class with @Deployment(resources = "classpath:demoProcess.bpmn") so that the Starter deploys the named BPMN resource to the Orchestration Cluster on start-up
Annotate the main application class with @ProcessDeployment(resources = "classpath:demoProcess.bpmn") so that the Starter deploys the named BPMN resource to the Orchestration Cluster on start-up
Annotate the main application class with @ZeebeDeployment(resources = "classpath:demoProcess.bpmn") so that the Starter deploys the named BPMN resource to the Orchestration Cluster on start-up

a.text: Incorrect. There is no @CamundaDeploy annotation in the Camunda Spring Boot Starter; the documented annotation for start-up deployment of BPMN resources is @Deployment alongside @SpringBootApplication.

b.text: Correct. The Deploy-process-models section shows @Deployment(resources = "classpath:demoProcess.bpmn") placed beside @SpringBootApplication so the Camunda Spring Boot Starter deploys the named BPMN resource on application start-up.

d.text: Incorrect. @ZeebeDeployment is associated with the legacy Spring Zeebe SDK; the Camunda Spring Boot Starter that replaces it uses the @Deployment annotation in Camunda 8.8.

c.text: Incorrect. @ProcessDeployment is not part of the Spring Boot Starter API; the documented annotation is @Deployment, paired with @SpringBootApplication on the main application class.

explanation: The Camunda Spring Boot Starter exposes the @Deployment annotation. Placing @Deployment(resources = "classpath:demoProcess.bpmn") on a class annotated with @SpringBootApplication causes the Starter to deploy the listed BPMN resources to the Orchestration Cluster on application start-up.

docs.title: Get started with the Camunda Java Client

scenario: Sable Bank operates 18 microservices on Spring Boot that previously embedded the Spring Zeebe SDK. With Camunda 8.8 released, the architecture team must understand the supported replacement Starter, the default transport protocol it uses, and when the legacy Spring Zeebe SDK leaves the public API before scheduling the migration work across all 18 microservices.
question: Which statement describes the Camunda Spring Boot Starter introduced as the official integration in Camunda 8.8?

options.text: The Camunda Spring Boot Starter coexists with the Spring Zeebe SDK throughout 8.x, uses the Camunda Java Client only when explicitly enabled, and keeps gRPC as the default transport for REST endpoints
The Camunda Spring Boot Starter replaces the Spring Zeebe SDK as of 8.8, uses the new Camunda Java Client, defaults to REST with gRPC configurable, and removes the Spring Zeebe SDK in version 8.10
The Camunda Spring Boot Starter replaces the Spring Zeebe SDK as of 8.8, defaults to the gRPC protocol with REST as a configurable alternative, and retires the Spring Zeebe SDK at version 8.9
The Camunda Spring Boot Starter wraps the legacy Spring Zeebe SDK as a thin facade, defaults to the gRPC protocol, and keeps the Spring Zeebe SDK supported through the entire 8.x release series

d.text: Incorrect. The Starter is not a facade over Spring Zeebe SDK; it uses the new Camunda Java Client under the hood, defaults to REST with gRPC configurable, and Spring Zeebe SDK is scheduled to be removed in version 8.10.

c.text: Incorrect. The default protocol is REST and gRPC is configurable; the docs schedule removal of Spring Zeebe SDK in version 8.10, not 8.9.

b.text: Correct. The migration note states that the Camunda Spring Boot Starter replaces the Spring Zeebe SDK as of 8.8, uses the new Camunda Java Client, defaults to REST with gRPC configurable, and the Spring Zeebe SDK will be removed in version 8.10.

a.text: Incorrect. The Starter uses the Camunda Java Client by default rather than on demand, defaults to REST instead of gRPC, and Spring Zeebe SDK is on a removal path at version 8.10.

explanation: The intro to the Camunda Spring Boot Starter states that it replaces the Spring Zeebe SDK as of Camunda 8.8, uses the new Camunda Java Client under the hood, defaults to REST while keeping gRPC configurable, and that the Spring Zeebe SDK will be removed in version 8.10. Teams should migrate before upgrading to 8.10 to avoid breaking changes.

docs.title: Get started with the Camunda Spring Boot Starter

scenario: A fintech provider standardising on Camunda 8.8 across 14 microservices needs to add the Camunda Spring Boot Starter Maven dependency and decide whether to enable the optional Java compiler flag that lets process variables bind by parameter name without explicit annotation values, before rolling out the configuration template to every team.
question: Which Maven coordinates and which optional Java compiler flag does the Camunda Spring Boot Starter getting-started guide specify?

options.text: Add the dependency io.camunda:camunda-spring-boot-starter at version 8.8.x and enable the Java compiler argument -g:vars so process variables are exposed to handlers by parameter name automatically

Add the dependency io.camunda:camunda-spring-zeebe-starter at version 8.8.x and enable the Java compiler argument -Adebug so handlers can bind process variables by parameter name without annotation values

Add the dependency io.camunda:camunda-spring-boot-starter at version 8.8.x and optionally enable the Java compiler argument -parameters so handlers can bind process variables by parameter name without annotation values

Add the dependency org.camunda:camunda-spring-boot-starter at version 8.8.x and optionally enable the Java compiler argument -parameters so handlers can bind process variables by parameter name without annotation values

b.text: Incorrect. The published artifactId is camunda-spring-boot-starter under the io.camunda group; the optional parameter-binding flag documented in Step 2 is -parameters rather than -Adebug.

c.text: Correct. Step 1 of the guide publishes io.camunda:camunda-spring-boot-starter at version 8.8.x. Step 2 introduces the optional Java compiler flag -parameters so handlers can reference process variables by parameter name without supplying annotation values.

d.text: Incorrect. The groupId is io.camunda, not org.camunda; otherwise the artifactId and the documented optional -parameters compiler flag are correct.

a.text: Incorrect. The compiler argument documented for parameter-name binding is -parameters; there is no -g:vars argument suggested in the getting-started guide.

explanation: Step 1 of the Camunda Spring Boot Starter getting-started guide publishes the Maven dependency io.camunda:camunda-spring-boot-starter at version 8.8.x. Step 2 explains that enabling the Java compiler flag -parameters is optional but lets handlers reference process variables through parameter names without specifying annotation values.

docs.title: Get started with the Camunda Spring Boot Starter

scenario: A logistics carrier running Camunda 8.8 on Spring Boot needs every redeploy of its 6 microservices to push the latest BPMN diagrams to the Orchestration Cluster so that 3500 shipments per day continue to route through the most current process version without manual deploy steps from the operations team.
question: Which statement describes the @Deployment annotation in the Camunda Spring Boot Starter getting-started guide?

options.text: The @Deployment annotation runs on application start-up alongside @SpringBootApplication and accepts a resources attribute pointing at a classpath BPMN file such as classpath:demoProcess.bpmn for deployment
The @Deployment annotation runs at every BPMN service task and accepts a single resources attribute that names the classpath BPMN file to redeploy to the engine before the service task is executed

The @Deployment annotation runs at application shutdown to undeploy resources from the Orchestration Cluster and accepts a single resources attribute listing classpath BPMN files that the engine should remove
The @Deployment annotation runs on application start-up alongside @EnableScheduling and accepts a path attribute that points at an external file system folder containing the BPMN diagrams to deploy

c.text: Incorrect. The annotation deploys resources on application start-up rather than undeploying them at shutdown; its resources attribute lists BPMN files to deploy to the Orchestration Cluster.

b.text: Incorrect. @Deployment fires once during Spring Boot start-up, not on every service task; service tasks do not redeploy BPMN files during normal process execution.

a.text: Correct. The getting-started guide places @Deployment(resources = "classpath:demoProcess.bpmn") on a class annotated with @SpringBootApplication, deploying the listed classpath BPMN resource to the Orchestration Cluster on start-up.

d.text: Incorrect. The annotation is paired with @SpringBootApplication, not @EnableScheduling, and its documented attribute is resources expecting classpath references rather than external folder paths.

explanation: The Deploy-process-models section shows the @Deployment annotation placed beside @SpringBootApplication on the main application class. Its resources attribute accepts classpath references such as classpath:demoProcess.bpmn, and the Starter deploys those resources to the Orchestration Cluster on application start-up.

docs.title: Deploy process models with the Camunda Spring Boot Starter

scenario: Wren Insurance integrates a Camunda 8.8 approval flow with a leading policy management system that already stores 22000 policies per month. The architect must decide what to persist as process variables versus what to leave in the leading system so that variable storage in the engine stays lean and each process instance only references the policy by its primary identifier.
question: Which approach matches the Camunda 8.8 data-handling guidance when a leading system already stores the business data?

options.text: Store a fresh snapshot of the policy on the start event of every instance and refresh that snapshot at the end of each service task so the local view stays close to the leading system at runtime
Store only references such as the primary identifier in a process variable like policyId, and continue to read the full policy record from the leading system whenever a work item or gateway needs it

Store both the full policy payload and a reference identifier on the process instance so that gateways and service tasks always have a complete local copy independent of the leading system at any time
Store every field of the policy as separate scalar process variables on every instance to enable rich queries inside the Camunda engine without contacting the leading system at runtime for any field

b.text: Correct. When a leading system already stores the business data, the docs recommend keeping only references such as an identifier (for example policyId) as a process variable and reading the full record from the leading system on demand from tasks and gateways.

c.text: Incorrect. The docs explicitly recommend against storing full payloads when a leading system is the source of truth; only references should be kept in process variables to keep the variable map lean.

d.text: Incorrect. Spreading every field as a separate variable runs counter to the rule of thumb that process variables should be kept as few as possible and within the documented variable size limitation.

a.text: Incorrect. Refreshing a full snapshot at each service task adds excessive variable churn; the guidance is to store references and consult the leading system on demand instead of caching the full payload.

explanation: The Storing-just-the-relevant-data section recommends holding only references (typically IDs) as process variables when a leading system stores the business objects, treating that leading system as the source of truth and keeping the variable map as lean as possible.

scenario: Beacon Bank runs 11 microservices on Camunda 8.8 that share a quote-approval process and reference process variables by string. The team wants to remove the copy-and-paste of variable name literals across services that handle 4500 transactions per day and add type-safe access with optional serialization logic for complex business objects.
question: Which combination of techniques does the data-handling guidance recommend to avoid duplicated variable name strings and to add type-safe access?

options.text: Collect variable names as constants in a dedicated interface and add a Process Data Accessor or Process Variable Accessor class that encapsulates casting and serialization logic for those variables

Define every variable as a static field on a generated handler class produced at process deployment and require all service task handlers to extend that base class for type-safe process variable access
Define the variable names as XML attributes on the BPMN diagram and rely on Camunda Modeler to validate every string reference against the diagram at deployment time across all client services
Define a JSON schema document next to the BPMN file, register it as a process header in Operate, and read variables through that header instead of through the Camunda Java client API at runtime

c.text: Incorrect. The docs do not propose BPMN-attribute declarations or Modeler-time validation for variable names; constants in code combined with accessor classes are the documented pattern.

a.text: Correct. The guidance is to collect variable names in a constants holder (for example a Java interface such as TwitterDemoProcessConstants) and to add a Process Data Accessor or Process Variable Accessor class that centralises casting and serialization for those variables.

d.text: Incorrect. There is no JSON-schema-via-Operate-header pattern in the guidance; constants plus accessor classes remain the documented recommendation for type-safe variable access.

b.text: Incorrect. The Starter does not generate handler base classes at deployment; the documented pattern uses a constants interface and a hand-written Data Accessor class for type-safe access.

explanation: The Using-constants-and-data-accessors section recommends two layers. First, collect variable names as constants (for example in a Java interface) so the code base no longer copy-pastes string literals. Second, add a Data Accessor class that centralises casting and serialization, available as either a Process Data Accessor for one process definition or a Process Variable Accessor for a single reusable variable.

scenario: Atlas Insurance models a claim-handling process in Camunda 8.8 that processes 25000 claims per day, where each claim carries deeply nested customer, vehicle, and document data. The architecture team must decide how to persist this complex data without bloating process variables in the engine while still allowing rich SQL queries and custom data migration strategies.
question: Which pattern does the data-handling best-practice page recommend for complex business data alongside a Camunda 8.8 process?

options.text: Inline the entire nested object graph into a single process variable named context so every service task and gateway has immediate JSON access to all 25000 claim fields without using any external storage

Introduce custom entities in a relational database keyed by an artificial UUID, store only that UUID as a process variable, and use a Data Accessor class to read or write the entity from the database when needed

Spread each nested field of the complex object across hundreds of typed process variables on the instance so that gateways can reference any leaf directly through scalar FEEL expressions on the engine
Attach the complex object as a BPMN data object on the start event and rely on Camunda to persist that data object as the canonical store for the instance instead of using regular process variables

a.text: Incorrect. Inlining the full object graph violates the rule of thumb to keep variable maps lean and runs against the variable size limitation documented for process instances.

c.text: Incorrect. Spreading every leaf into a separate variable also bloats the variable map; the documented pattern is to externalise the complex data and keep only a reference inside the process instance.

b.text: Correct. The Complex-data-as-entities section recommends storing complex data as custom entities in a relational database, keeping only the entity's primary key (typically a UUID) as a process variable, and reading the entity through a Data Accessor class.

d.text: Incorrect. Camunda 8 does not treat BPMN data objects as process variables; the docs recommend using data objects only occasionally for documentation, not as a canonical engine-side store.

explanation: The Complex-data-as-entities section describes externalised process context. Complex business data is held in custom relational-database entities, only the entity's primary key (typically a UUID) is stored as a process variable, and a Data Accessor class reads or writes the entity. This pattern enables rich SQL queries and custom data migration strategies between process versions.

scenario: Beacon marketplace runs a social post approval process on Camunda 8.8 that handles 1800 messages per day. A worker completes the Review task by passing a structured payload via the Tasklist API, and the team needs to confirm how Camunda 8.8 stores the resulting variable values internally before integrating with the audit pipeline.
question: How does Camunda 8.8 store process variable values internally?

options.text: Variable values are stored as typed Java objects in the broker memory model and serialized only when an external client requests them via the API
Variable values are stored as plain key value strings and complex objects must be flattened by the worker before completing a job through the client API
Variable values are stored as JSON in Camunda 8 which is a key difference from Camunda 7 where supported value types and serialization differ
Variable values are stored as XML fragments within the BPMN element of the running activity and the engine validates them against an XSD on completion

a.text: Incorrect. The page states that in Camunda 8 values are stored as JSON; the broker does not keep variables as typed Java objects deferred to API serialization.

d.text: Incorrect. There is no XML or XSD storage path for variables; Camunda 8 stores process variable values as JSON inside the engine.

b.text: Incorrect. Variables are stored as JSON and the engine accepts structured objects directly, for example a Map of String to Object when completing a task.

c.text: Correct. The handling data page states that in Camunda 8 values are stored as JSON and explicitly cautions that Camunda 7 handles variables differently with a different set of supported value types.

explanation: Camunda 8.8 stores all process variable values as JSON, which the handling data page calls out as a key difference from Camunda 7 where variables are not handled as JSON and the set of supported value types differs. Workers can pass structured payloads such as a Map when completing tasks.

scenario: Aurora Logistics writes its first Camunda 8.8 Java job worker to process 9000 shipments per day across 12 microservices. A developer follows the Quick start example in the Java client docs and must pick the correct builder chain that opens a worker and hands each activated job to a JobHandler implementation.
question: Which Camunda 8.8 Java client builder chain opens a job worker that delivers activated jobs to a JobHandler?

options.text: Invoke client.deployJobHandler(type, handler).build() and the Camunda runtime then registers the handler in Operate so that activated jobs are pushed directly to it without any further builder chain

Invoke client.subscribe().topic(type).handler(handler).open() and then await activated jobs delivered through a Tasklist topic subscription endpoint that the broker maintains for the Java client instance

Invoke client.newJobWorker().jobType(type).handler(handler).start() and then call worker.poll() in a loop to manually fetch each batch of activated jobs from the broker on every iteration of the worker
Invoke client.newWorker().jobType(type).handler(handler).open() and the returned JobWorker handles polling automatically and submits each activated job to the provided JobHandler implementation

c.text: Incorrect. The Quick start example uses .open() not .start(), and the JobWorker polls automatically; developers do not manually call a poll method on the returned worker instance.

b.text: Incorrect. The Java client has no client.subscribe().topic() API; that shape comes from Camunda 7 topic subscriptions, not the Camunda 8.8 job worker.

d.text: Correct. The Quick start shows client.newWorker().jobType(jobType).handler(new EmailJobHandler()).open(); the returned JobWorker handles polling and delivers each ActivatedJob to the JobHandler#handle method.

a.text: Incorrect. There is no deployJobHandler API and Operate does not push jobs to handlers; workers are opened from the Java client using the newWorker builder chain.

explanation: The Camunda 8.8 Java client Quick start opens a job worker via client.newWorker().jobType(type).handler(handler).open(). The returned JobWorker manages polling and submits each ActivatedJob to the JobHandler#handle implementation provided by the developer.

scenario: Tessera Insurance recovers from periodic GRPC_STATUS_RESOURCE_EXHAUSTED responses on a Camunda 8.8 Java job worker that handles 5500 claims per day. Engineers want to override the default exponential backoff strategy that the worker applies between failed activate jobs polls so the cluster has more time to recover.
question: Which Camunda 8.8 Java client API call replaces the default backoff strategy applied after a failed job activation poll?

options.text: Call .pollInterval(Duration) on the JobWorkerBuilder so the Java client substitutes the configured poll interval for the exponential backoff after any failed activate jobs request response from the broker
Call .backoffSupplier(BackoffSupplier) on the JobWorkerBuilder so the Java client uses the supplied retry delay instead of the default exponential backoff after any failed activate jobs poll response
Call .resourceExhaustedHandler(Handler) on the gateway configuration so Zeebe replaces its broker backpressure mechanism whenever this Java client sends activate jobs requests through the gateway
Call .retryPolicy(RetryPolicy) on the CamundaClientBuilder so the broker rejects activate jobs commands according to a custom global retry budget shared across every Java worker on the Camunda client

a.text: Incorrect. The pollInterval governs spacing between polls when no jobs were activated, not the retry delay after a failed poll; the backoff strategy is configured separately.

b.text: Correct. The retry delay after a failed poll is provided by a BackoffSupplier and is replaced via the .backoffSupplier() method on the JobWorkerBuilder; by default the Java client uses an exponential backoff implementation.

d.text: Incorrect. There is no .retryPolicy(RetryPolicy) API on CamundaClientBuilder for this purpose; the worker backoff is configured per worker through the BackoffSupplier on the JobWorkerBuilder.

c.text: Incorrect. Zeebe broker backpressure is a server side mechanism configured separately from the client; clients adjust their own retry delay through the BackoffSupplier on each JobWorkerBuilder.

explanation: The Camunda 8.8 Java client provides the retry delay between failed activate jobs polls through a BackoffSupplier and the JobWorkerBuilder exposes a .backoffSupplier() method to replace the default exponential backoff implementation used by the job worker.

scenario: Zenith Bank monitors a Camunda 8.8 Java job worker that processes 8400 transactions per day. The platform team has the built in JobWorkerMetrics counters wired into Prometheus and wants to derive the live count of jobs that have been activated by the worker but have not yet been handled by the JobHandler.
question: How can Zenith Bank derive the count of buffered jobs inside its Camunda 8.8 Java worker from the built in JobWorkerMetrics counters?

options.text: Subtract the configured pollInterval from the latest backoff delay reported by the BackoffSupplier so the Zenith Bank team estimates the buffered job count from the timing of the next scheduled poll

Read the gauge exposed by the Zeebe gateway named zeebe.client.jobs.buffered that the broker increments for every activate jobs response sent to a connected Java client worker registered at Zenith Bank

Read the unhandledJobs counter exposed automatically by the JobHandler interface that the Java client increments before invoking the handler and decrements upon return from JobHandler#handle at Zenith Bank

Subtract the count of jobs handled from the count of jobs activated reported by JobWorkerMetrics so the Zenith Bank platform team derives the count of queued or buffered jobs not yet handled by the worker

b.text: Incorrect. There is no zeebe.client.jobs.buffered gauge exposed by the gateway; the built in metrics are the activated and handled counters on JobWorkerMetrics on the client side.

c.text: Incorrect. JobHandler does not expose an unhandledJobs counter; the documented approach is to derive buffered jobs by subtracting handled from activated counts reported by JobWorkerMetrics.

d.text: Correct. JobWorkerMetrics exposes the count of jobs activated and the count of jobs handled; subtracting handled from activated yields the count of queued or buffered jobs that the worker has not yet handled.

a.text: Incorrect. The BackoffSupplier delay relates to retry timing after failed polls and has no relationship to the count of buffered jobs inside the worker.

explanation: JobWorkerMetrics in the Camunda 8.8 Java client tracks two counters, jobs activated and jobs handled. Subtracting handled from activated gives the count of queued or buffered jobs that the worker has yet to hand to its JobHandler.

scenario: Helios Fintech wires a JobWorkerMetrics implementation into a Camunda 8.8 Java job worker that processes 4800 transactions per day. The developer uses the built in Micrometer adapter with a Prometheus MeterRegistry and needs the correct builder call to attach the metrics instance to the specific worker being opened.
question: Which builder call attaches a JobWorkerMetrics implementation to a Camunda 8.8 Java job worker on the Java client?

options.text: Register the JobWorkerMetrics instance with the gateway through client.registerMetrics(metrics) so the broker tags activate jobs responses sent to this Java client at Helios Fintech with the metrics instance

Wrap the JobHandler with a MetricsJobHandler decorator and the Java client at Helios Fintech automatically discovers the JobWorkerMetrics implementation through a JobHandler annotation on the wrapped handler

Pass the JobWorkerMetrics instance to the JobWorkerBuilder via .metrics(metrics) on client.newJobWorker so the worker at Helios Fintech reports its activated and handled job counts to the supplied metrics instance

Pass the JobWorkerMetrics instance to CamundaClientBuilder using .metrics(metrics) so the metrics instance applies globally to every job worker opened by the configured Camunda client at Helios Fintech

d.text: Incorrect. JobWorkerMetrics is attached per worker on the JobWorkerBuilder via .metrics(metrics); the CamundaClientBuilder has no global metrics method that applies to every worker.
