Operate writes the error to the Zeebe audit log and the operator must run zbctl to fetch the message for each failing instance
Operate opens an incident detail dialog where the operator must click Show stack trace to reveal the inline error message

a.text: Incorrect. The docs describe an inline expansion of the failed row inside the Processes view as the way to read the error message; emailing the operations team is not the documented surface for operation failures.

d.text: Incorrect. The docs describe an inline row expansion in the process instances list that directly shows the error message, not a separate dialog requiring a Show stack trace click.

c.text: Incorrect. Operate does not require zbctl to surface operation failures; the docs describe the failure being shown in line by expanding the red-marked row inside the Processes view.

b.text: Correct. Per docs, if an operation fails the related process instance row is marked red and the operator can expand the row to reveal the error message related to the selected operation.

explanation: When an operation fails on a specific process instance, Operate marks that row red in the Processes view and the operator can expand the row to read the error message related to the selected operation inline, without leaving the view.

scenario: Crest Marketplace runs Camunda 8.8 SaaS for 22000 orders per day. A downstream payment API outage caused 3000 orders to raise incidents at the payment service task because of a stale token variable. The operations team wants to fix the variable on each affected order and let the engine continue execution from the failed task without redeploying the BPMN model.
question: Which Operate capability lets the marketplace resolve the incident and continue execution on each affected order?

options.text: Operate exposes a Camunda 7 style external job lock API so the operator can lock and complete each stuck job from the marketplace tooling
Operate exposes an action to resolve incidents and update process variables so an operator can fix each affected instance and continue
Operate exposes a Replay from cluster feature that rolls back each affected instance to the prior service task and replays it from there
Operate exposes a Zeebe job worker that automatically clears incidents once the broker reports the failed task as healthy again

d.text: Incorrect. Operate does not run an internal job worker that auto-clears incidents; the docs describe operator-driven incident resolution where the variable is updated and the task is then retried.

b.text: Correct. Per docs, Operate lets operators resolve incidents and update process variables, which is the flow needed for the marketplace to fix the stale token on each affected order and continue execution.

c.text: Incorrect. Operate offers modification and batch move actions for active instances, but there is no Replay from cluster feature in the documented Operate feature set.

a.text: Incorrect. Camunda 8 does not use the Camunda 7 style external job lock API; in Operate, incidents are resolved by updating variables and retrying the task, not by locking jobs.

explanation: Among the Operate capabilities listed in the docs is carrying out key operations such as resolving incidents and updating process variables, which is exactly the path needed to fix the stale token on each affected payment task and continue execution.

scenario: Beacon Telco runs Camunda 8.8 SaaS for 9000 instances of a subscriber onboarding process. After a model defect was discovered, 400 instances are stuck at the wrong service task, and the operations team needs to relocate all of them to a different element inside the same process model in one action without redeploying or losing their variable state.
question: Which Operate feature lets operators move many active process instances to a different element in one action?

options.text: The Resume from incident operation moves a batch of active process instances forward to a different element of the same model
The Cancel and recreate operation moves a batch of active process instances forward to a different element of the same model
The Delete and replay operation moves a batch of active process instances forward to a different element of the same model
The Batch move modification moves a batch of active process instances forward to a different element of the same model

a.text: Incorrect. Resume from incident is not a documented Operate operation; incidents are resolved by updating variables and retrying the task, not by jumping a batch of instances to a different element.

b.text: Incorrect. Cancelling and recreating would drop history and start fresh instances; the documented batch move action keeps the existing instances and relocates their tokens in place.

d.text: Correct. Per docs, Operate offers a batch move action that relocates a set of active process instances to a different element of the same process so execution can continue from there.

c.text: Incorrect. Operate can delete finished instances, but Delete and replay is not a documented action for relocating active instances to a different element of the same process model.

explanation: The Operate docs list batch move as one of its key capabilities on active process instances, where many instances are moved to a different element of the same process so execution can continue from the new element.

scenario: Nimbus Marketplace runs Camunda 8.8 SaaS for 24000 orders per day. After a deploy regression left 1500 order instances stuck on the same wrong service task, the operator needs to retry or cancel many of these process instances at once without scripting against the Zeebe API.
question: Which Operate capability lets the operator retry or cancel many process instances at once?

options.text: Optimize emits a scheduled report that, when subscribed to, retries or cancels the matched process instances on the next cron tick from the cluster
Selections in Operate let the operator pick a set of process instances and apply a retry or cancel operation to all of them at once on the cluster
Tasklist exposes a bulk select on user tasks that propagates the retry or cancel decision back to each parent process instance in the cluster
Selections in Web Modeler share a tagged set of orders with the operator, who then deploys a patched BPMN file to apply the retry or cancel action

d.text: Incorrect. Web Modeler authors BPMN files and does not provide selection-based runtime operations on process instances; retry and cancel at scale are surfaced by Operate selections.

c.text: Incorrect. Tasklist focuses on user task work and does not provide a bulk retry or cancel action on parent process instances; that capability lives in Operate.

b.text: Correct. The Operate introduction lists retrying or canceling many process instances at once via selections in Operate as one of the key operational features available to the operator.

a.text: Incorrect. Optimize is read-only analytics; it does not retry or cancel process instances. Bulk retry and cancel actions are performed in Operate via selections on the processes list.

explanation: The Operate introduction lists, as a key operation, the ability to retry or cancel many process instances at once via the selections feature on the processes list, alongside resolving incidents and modifying or batch-moving instances.

scenario: Quanta Healthcare runs Camunda 8.8 SaaS with 4500 instances per day across its admissions flow. A typo in a FEEL expression left 220 patient instances stuck in an incident state with a stale variable value, and the operator needs Operate to both clear the incident and correct the underlying variable on each affected instance.
question: Which Operate capability lets the operator clear the incidents and correct the stale variable values on the 220 affected instances?

options.text: Run process instance migration to a newer process version where the FEEL typo is fixed and the variable is mapped on the source side of the migration plan
Use Operate to resolve the incident and update the process variable on each affected instance, then let the engine reprocess the failed job on the instance
Apply a batch move modification to each stuck instance, which transparently retries the FEEL expression with the corrected variable on every affected instance
Cancel each stuck instance from Operate and let an external client re-create new process instances with the corrected variable passed in at start time as a payload

c.text: Incorrect. Batch move modification moves tokens between flow nodes; it does not resolve incidents or update variable values, which are surfaced as separate operations in the Operate introduction.

a.text: Incorrect. Migration switches a stuck instance to a new process version but is not the operation listed for correcting a stale variable; that is the resolve incidents and update variables capability.

b.text: Correct. The Operate introduction lists resolving incidents and updating process variables together as a key operation that fixes a stuck instance in place without canceling it or starting a new instance.

d.text: Incorrect. Cancel-and-recreate loses each instance's state and history and is not the targeted Operate operation for fixing stale variables on stuck instances; that capability is resolve incident plus update variables.

explanation: Per the Operate introduction, one of Operate's key operations is to carry out resolving incidents and updating process variables on affected instances, which is the targeted fix when a stuck instance has a bad variable that needs to be corrected.

scenario: Lumen SaaS runs Camunda 8.8 with 30000 transactions per day across its provider tenants. After 6 months in production, the cluster carries millions of finished process instances and several unused deployed resources, and the platform engineer wants Operate to free up storage in a controlled way.
question: Which Operate capability removes finished process instances and unused deployed resources to free storage?

options.text: Operate offers explicit actions to delete finished process instances and to delete deployed resources such as process definitions from the cluster
Operate triggers Zeebe to roll over the data partition to a fresh log segment, which detaches the prior finished process instances data from the cluster
Operate emits a Console-side prompt that asks an administrator to archive the cluster snapshot to a cold storage bucket on a fixed monthly cadence
Operate ships a TTL setting on the cluster that automatically purges finished process instances on a daily Elasticsearch rotation in the background

a.text: Correct. The Operate introduction lists, among its key operations, deleting finished process instances and deleting deployed resources, both surfaced as explicit operator actions in the Operate UI.

b.text: Incorrect. Partition log rollover is a broker concern and is not the operator-facing storage cleanup path; the Operate introduction names explicit delete actions on finished instances and resources.

d.text: Incorrect. There is no Operate-managed TTL purge of finished instances; the Operate introduction lists explicit delete actions for finished process instances and deployed resources instead.

c.text: Incorrect. Cluster snapshot archiving belongs to Console-level operations, not to the Operate user actions listed in the introduction, which call out delete finished instances and delete resources.

explanation: The Operate introduction explicitly lists deleting finished instances and deleting deployed resources as Operate operations the user can carry out from the UI to free storage and prune the cluster state.

scenario: Stellar Insurance runs Camunda 8.8 with 5000 claims per day. While preparing a batch move of 200 claim instances, an underwriter requests a 2 hours pause to verify the target task. The operator wants to drop the in-progress selection in Operate without leaving the list view and without affecting any earlier operations.
question: Which actions allow the operator to discard the currently selected process instances while staying in batch modification mode?

options.text: Refresh the browser page; any in-progress batch selection in Operate is preserved server-side and is reset to empty on the next reload of the view.
Click the Exit button at the bottom of the list view, which both leaves batch mode and silently reapplies any prior modifications that were pending in the queue.
Click Apply Modification once to commit the current selection, then use the browser back button to revert the batch back to an empty selection state.
Click the Discard option in the process instance toolbar, or clear the current selection to drop all currently selected instances from the batch.

d.text: Correct. The docs say that in batch modification mode the operator can discard all currently selected instances either by clicking the Discard option in the process instance toolbar or by clearing the current selection.

c.text: Incorrect. Clicking Apply Modification commits the batch move; it does not provide an undo-by-back-button workflow, and the docs describe Discard or clearing the selection as the way to drop selections.

a.text: Incorrect. The docs do not describe in-progress selections as server-side preserved state, and refreshing the page is not the documented way to discard selections in batch mode.

b.text: Incorrect. The Exit button at the bottom of the list view leaves batch modification mode rather than reapplying pending modifications; it is not the documented discard mechanism for selections.

explanation: Inside batch modification mode the operator can discard all currently selected instances either by clicking the Discard option in the process instance toolbar or by clearing the current selection; the Exit button at the bottom of the list view leaves the modification mode entirely.

scenario: Meridian Bank operates Camunda 8.8 with 22000 customers and 18000 payments per day. After a faulty exclusive gateway pushed 1800 payment instances down a misconfigured branch, the operations lead has a 2 hours window to launch a batch move from the wrong service task to the corrected one, using Operate only and without scripting any zbctl calls.
question: Which sequence of steps correctly starts batch modification mode for those 1800 affected instances in Operate?

options.text: Use the Operations panel filter, click Discard to clear stale entries, then click Move to load a fresh batch selection containing every active instance on that flow node.
Open the Operations panel, choose a target flow node, click Apply Modification, then begin selecting the affected source instances inside the confirmation modal that opens.
Click Move from the process diagram panel before selecting any instances so that every active instance on the source flow node is auto-batched into a single grouped move.
Select the source flow node, choose the affected instances in the list view, click Move when the toolbar appears, then click Continue on the information modal to enter batch mode.

b.text: Incorrect. The docs entry to batch mode does not start in the Operations panel; the operator must first select a source flow node and individual instances, click Move, then Continue on the information modal.

d.text: Correct. The docs describe exactly this sequence: select the flow node containing the instances to move, select the instances in the list view so the Move action appears in the toolbar, click Move, then click Continue on the information modal to enter batch modification mode.

c.text: Incorrect. Clicking Move before selecting instances does not auto-batch every active instance; the Move action becomes available only after instances are selected and the toolbar appears.

a.text: Incorrect. Discard is used inside batch mode to drop currently selected instances, not as part of the entry sequence, and the docs do not describe loading a batch from the Operations panel filter.

explanation: To enter batch modification mode the operator selects the source flow node, picks the relevant process instances in the list view so the Move action appears in the toolbar, clicks Move, then clicks Continue on the information modal that confirms the switch to batch move mode.

scenario: Kestrel Telco runs Camunda 8.8 with 17000 customers and identifies 600 broken provisioning instances per day stuck on a misrouted service task. After entering batch modification mode, the on-call operator must finish moving those 600 instances to a retry-eligible service task inside a 24 hours operational window.
question: Which two actions complete the batch move once the operator is already inside batch modification mode?

options.text: Choose a target flow node, then click Move once more in the process instance toolbar to confirm and commit the configured batch move operation.
Select the target flow node, then click Apply Modification below the process instance list view to commit the batch move to all selected instances.
Reselect the source flow node, then click Continue once more on the information modal to schedule the batch move as a delayed background job.
Pick the source flow node again, then click the Exit button below the list view to flush every chosen instance through to the desired target flow node.

d.text: Incorrect. The Exit button leaves batch modification mode rather than applying the move; the docs require selecting the target flow node and then clicking Apply Modification to commit.

a.text: Incorrect. Clicking Move again does not commit the batch; once the target flow node is selected, the documented commit action is Apply Modification below the list view.

b.text: Correct. The docs say to select the target flow node to move the selected process instances to, then click Apply Modification below the process instance list view to start the batch move.

c.text: Incorrect. Continue on the information modal is part of entering batch mode, not committing it; the commit step is Apply Modification on the selected target flow node.

explanation: Once inside batch modification mode the operator selects the target flow node where the chosen process instances should be moved, then clicks Apply Modification below the process instance list view to commit the batch move.

scenario: Catalyst Marketplace runs Camunda 8.8 with 9500 orders per day. After launching a batch move of 700 stuck order instances onto a corrected payment service task, the on-call operator must track progress and confirm exactly how many of the 700 process instances have been modified before signing off the maintenance window.
question: Where does the operator monitor batch modification progress and check how many process instances have already been modified?

options.text: On a dedicated Migrations dashboard linked from the cluster home page, which polls the broker for batch progress counters and refreshes every minute.
In the BPMN viewer overlay, which animates each moved instance until the batch operation finishes and the live counter rolls back automatically to zero.
In the operations panel on the instances list view, by clicking the Instances link in the entry to see how many process instances have been modified.
In the Decisions tab, where each modified instance lists per-element timing data alongside a batch operation status counter that ticks down to zero.

a.text: Incorrect. There is no separate Migrations dashboard for batch modification monitoring; the docs direct operators to the operations panel on the instances list view after the batch starts.

d.text: Incorrect. The Decisions tab is not where batch modification progress is tracked; the documented monitoring surface is the operations panel on the process instance list view.

b.text: Incorrect. The BPMN viewer does not animate per-instance batch progress; monitoring is done through the operations panel entry on the instances list view.

c.text: Correct. The docs state that after the batch modification is started the user is directed to the instances list view, where progress can be tracked in the operations panel and the Instances link on the entry shows how many process instances have been modified.

explanation: After starting a batch modification, Operate directs the user to the instances list view, where progress is tracked through the operations panel; clicking the Instances link on the corresponding operations entry reveals how many process instances have been modified so far.

scenario: Pinnacle Marketplace runs Camunda 8.8 SaaS with 18000 orders per day. After a deploy regression, the operator wants to batch-move active process instances stuck on the wrong service task to the correct one and is reviewing the documented order of steps for starting a batch modification in Operate.
question: What is the first step the operator takes in Operate to start a batch move modification?

options.text: Open the cluster settings page and toggle the Batch Modification feature flag, then return to the Processes view to start picking the target instances
Open the API client console, mint a token with the batch-write scope, and only then enter the Processes view to select target process instances for batch
Open the operations panel from the top navigation and click New Batch to seed an empty selection of process instances for editing in the cluster
Open the process diagram of the affected process and select the flow node that contains the process instances intended to be moved by the operation

c.text: Incorrect. The batch flow does not start from the operations panel with a New Batch action; per Operate docs, the operator first picks the source flow node on the process diagram for the affected process.

d.text: Correct. Per the batch modification documentation, the first step is to open the process you want to apply the modification to and select the flow node containing the process instances intended to be moved.

a.text: Incorrect. There is no feature flag toggle in cluster settings for batch modification; the flow is started by selecting the source flow node on the process diagram in Operate.

b.text: Incorrect. The operator does not need to mint a batch-write API token in the console first; the batch modification flow starts from the process diagram by selecting the source flow node.

explanation: The Operate batch modification guide begins with selecting, on the process you want to apply the modification to, the flow node that contains the process instances you intend to move; this is the first step of the documented batch flow.

scenario: Catalyst Bank runs Camunda 8.8 SaaS with 7200 loans per day. After selecting 35 instances stuck on the wrong service task and clicking Move, an operator is shown an information modal explaining that the UI is switching to batch move mode, and now wants to know what visual cue confirms the mode change after Continue.
question: What visual cue indicates that batch modification mode has been entered after the operator clicks Continue?

options.text: A green checkmark appears next to each selected process instance row, and the Move button is dimmed to indicate that batch modification mode is now active
A red banner with a stop icon appears at the top of the Processes view and the operations panel collapses until the modifications are applied to the cluster
A blue border appears around the view, including a blue banner at the top and two buttons at the bottom for applying modifications or exiting the batch mode
An orange dashed border appears around the process instance toolbar, and the operations panel is hidden for the remainder of the batch move session in Operate

b.text: Incorrect. The batch modification guide describes a blue border, a blue banner, and apply or exit buttons; a red stop banner is not the visual cue Operate uses to indicate batch modification mode.

a.text: Incorrect. There is no per-row green checkmark or dimmed Move button signal for batch mode; Operate uses a blue border, a blue banner, and apply or exit buttons in the view.

d.text: Incorrect. The documented visual cue is a blue border on the view, plus a blue banner at the top and apply or exit buttons at the bottom; an orange toolbar border is not part of the design.

c.text: Correct. The batch modification guide states the UI changes to indicate batch modification mode via a blue border, a blue banner at the top, and two buttons at the bottom for applying or exiting modifications.

explanation: Operate signals that batch modification mode is active by drawing a blue border around the view, with a blue banner at the top and two buttons at the bottom: one to apply the modifications and one to exit the batch mode.

scenario: Helios Bank runs Camunda 8.8 SaaS for 13000 transactions per day. After clicking Apply on a batch move spanning 500 process instances, the platform engineer is taken to the instances list view and wants to track how many of those 500 instances have been modified so far without scripting against the engine API.
question: How can the platform engineer track how many of the 500 instances have been modified after the batch starts?

options.text: Open the Tasklist view and filter user tasks by the batch operation id, where each modified instance briefly raises a tracking user task for the operation
Open the Console cluster page and read the partition-level event throughput metric, which equals the count of modified process instances for the operation
Open the operations panel on the instances list view and click the Instances link in the batch operation entry to see how many instances have been modified
Open the Optimize report library and run the Batch Modifications report, which lists the latest modification operations in real time during the cluster operation

c.text: Correct. The batch modification guide says that after the operation is started, the operator can click the Instances link in the operations panel entry to check how many process instances have been modified so far.

b.text: Incorrect. Cluster throughput metrics are unrelated to per-operation batch progress; the documented progress signal is the Instances link in the operations panel for the batch entry.

a.text: Incorrect. Tasklist does not raise per-instance tracking user tasks for batch operations; the documented place to check batch progress is the operations panel on the instances list.

d.text: Incorrect. Optimize is an analytics product and is not the documented place for live batch progress; Operate exposes batch progress via the Instances link in the operations panel entry.

explanation: Once the batch modification has started, Operate redirects the operator to the instances list view and exposes batch progress in the operations panel; clicking the Instances link in the batch entry shows how many process instances have been modified.

scenario: Kestrel SaaS runs Camunda 8.8 with 4200 instances per day across its tenant onboarding flow. The platform engineer is curating the runbook for Operate batch move modifications and needs to recall which BPMN element types cannot be the source of a move modification in the current Operate release.
question: Which BPMN element types cannot be the source of a batch move modification in Operate?

options.text: Start events, boundary events, and events attached to event-based gateways cannot be the source of a batch move modification in the current Operate release
Parallel gateways, exclusive gateways, and inclusive gateways cannot be the source of a batch move modification in the current Operate release on the Camunda 8 cluster
Intermediate throw events, end events, and call activities cannot be the source of a batch move modification in the current Operate release on the Camunda 8 cluster
Service tasks, user tasks, and receive tasks cannot be the source of a batch move modification in the current Operate release on the Camunda 8 cluster

a.text: Correct. The batch modification guide lists start events, boundary events, and events attached to event-based gateways among the element types that cannot be the source of a move modification in Operate.

d.text: Incorrect. Service tasks, user tasks, and receive tasks are common sources of batch move modifications; they are not listed in the non-supported elements section of the batch modification guide.

b.text: Incorrect. Gateways are not in the list of types that cannot be moved; the documented restrictions cover start events, boundary events, and events attached to event-based gateways.

c.text: Incorrect. Intermediate throw events, end events, and call activities are not listed as element types that cannot be the source of a move modification in the batch modification guide.

explanation: The batch modification guide is explicit that move modifications are not possible for start events, boundary events, and events attached to event-based gateways, as well as for a subprocess itself and elements with multiple running scopes.

scenario: Zephyr Logistics runs Camunda 8.8 SaaS with 2200 instances on shipmentProcess version 5 and wants to migrate them to version 6. The operator has finished mapping every source flow node to a target flow node, clicked Next to review the preview, and pressed Confirm to enter the final confirmation dialog.
question: What additional input does Operate require in the final confirmation dialog before the migration operation starts?

options.text: The operator selects the target process version one more time from a dropdown in the dialog and then clicks Confirm to execute the planned flow node mapping.
The operator types the word MIGRATE into the text box and clicks Confirm again, which is the gate that actually starts the migration operation on the selected instances.
The operator pastes the list of broker-generated process instance keys into a text box in the dialog to authorize the specific instances participating in the plan.
The operator enters the bpmnProcessId of the target process into a text box to acknowledge the destination and then clicks Confirm to begin the migration operation.

a.text: Incorrect. The target version is selected earlier from the dropdown next to the Target box, not again in the final confirmation dialog; the dialog asks for the literal word MIGRATE instead.

c.text: Incorrect. Operate does not require the operator to paste process instance keys; the final guard is typing the literal word MIGRATE into a text box before clicking Confirm again.

b.text: Correct. After clicking Confirm to review, the operator must type the literal word MIGRATE into the provided text box and click Confirm again, which is the documented action that starts the migration operation.

d.text: Incorrect. The bpmnProcessId of the target was picked via the Target box dropdown; the final safeguard step is typing the literal word MIGRATE into the text box and confirming.

explanation: Operate displays a confirmation step that requires the operator to type the literal word MIGRATE into a text box and click Confirm again before the migration operation actually starts. This deliberate gate guards against accidental migrations of running production instances.

scenario: Lattice Insurance runs Camunda 8.8 with 12000 claim instances spread across claimProcess versions 1, 2, and 3 in production. The operations team wants to migrate the running version 1 instances to version 3 in one batch and is about to start the workflow in Operate.
question: Where in Operate does the operator pick the source process and version before clicking Migrate?

options.text: The operator selects the source process from the deployments table in the Operate cluster overview and chooses the version from inside the deployment detail dialog.
The operator picks the source process and the source version from the filters panel on the process instances page before selecting instances and clicking Migrate.
The operator opens the Target box on the migration view and selects the source version from its dropdown before later choosing the target version for the running instances.
The operator types the bpmnProcessId of the source process into the browser URL bar, and Operate auto-loads the matching version of the process for the migration workflow.

c.text: Incorrect. The Target box is reserved for selecting the destination process and version inside the migration view; the source process and version are scoped earlier from the filters panel.

a.text: Incorrect. The processes overview lists deployments for inspection, but the migration workflow itself begins by filtering the process instances list with the filters panel.

d.text: Incorrect. There is no URL-based source selection in the migration workflow; Operate scopes the source process and version through the filters panel on the instances page.

b.text: Correct. Selecting a specific process and version from the filters panel scopes the instances list to that source version, after which the operator selects instances from the list and clicks Migrate to enter the migration view.

explanation: The first step in the Operate migration workflow is to select the source process and version from the filters panel on the process instances page. That filter scopes which running instances are visible for selection before the operator clicks Migrate to enter the migration view.

scenario: Helios Bank runs Camunda 8.8 with 600 paymentProcess instances per hour. The operator has selected version 1 instances and entered version 2 as the target; the bottom panel of the migration view lists every service task from the source process, including Check payment, awaiting a target binding for each row.
question: Which control does the operator use in the bottom panel to set the target flow node for each source flow node?

options.text: A target flow node dropdown next to each source flow node row in the bottom panel selects the target flow node for that source element of the migration.
A drag-and-drop arrow from each source flow node row onto a node in the target process diagram creates the flow node mapping used by the migration plan.
An XML edit applied to the target process model declares the source-to-target flow node correspondence that Operate then reads into the migration plan.
A FEEL expression entered next to each source flow node row computes the target flow node id for that row during execution of the migration operation.

b.text: Incorrect. Mapping is established through per-row dropdowns in the bottom panel rather than drag-and-drop on the diagrams; the diagrams are read-only visualizations of source and target processes.

a.text: Correct. For each source flow node row in the bottom panel, the operator selects a target flow node from a dropdown, and that selection forms the mapping that the migration plan applies to every running instance.

d.text: Incorrect. FEEL is not used to express migration mappings; each source flow node is bound to a target through the dropdown control on its row in the bottom panel.

c.text: Incorrect. The target process model is not edited from Operate; flow node mappings are chosen interactively through the dropdown next to each source row in the bottom panel.

explanation: In the migration view's bottom panel, every source flow node row has a dropdown for selecting its target flow node. The operator uses these dropdowns to bind each source element to a target element, and the resulting mapping is what Operate applies to every running instance during the migration operation.

scenario: Vanta Retailer runs Camunda 8.8 SaaS with 9000 instances of orderProcess version 1 per day. The operator has filtered the process instances list to version 1 and ticked three running instances that need to move to version 2 of the same process.
question: Which action moves the operator from the process instances list into the Operate migration view?

options.text: The operator clicks Edit on each selected process instance row in turn, then chooses Move flow node from the per-row menu to bring up the migration view in Operate.
The operator opens the operations menu on the cluster header and selects Batch migrate, which loads the source-to-target diagrams and the flow node mapping panel for the selection.
The operator clicks the Migrate button after selecting the source instances, which opens the migration view showing the source diagram, the target diagram, and the flow node mapping panel.
The operator clicks the modify icon on the process instance header, then picks Migrate from the available modification operations list to load the migration view for the cohort.

c.text: Correct. After selecting one or more running instances from the filtered process instances list, the Migrate button enters the migration view that shows the source diagram on the top left, the target diagram on the top right, and the flow node mapping panel at the bottom.

d.text: Incorrect. The modify icon on the instance header opens single-instance modification mode, not the migration view; migration is entered through the Migrate button on the process instances list.

a.text: Incorrect. There is no per-row Edit action that opens the migration view; migration is started for the selected cohort at once through the Migrate button on the instances list.

b.text: Incorrect. The documented entry point is the Migrate button on the process instances list after selecting source instances, not a Batch migrate option in the cluster header menu.

explanation: After filtering the process instances list to a single source process version and selecting the desired running instances, clicking Migrate enters the migration view. That view contains the source process diagram on the top left, the target process diagram on the top right, and the flow node mapping panel at the bottom.

scenario: Junction Retail runs Camunda 8.8 SaaS with 14000 orders per day. After selecting 180 active instances of orderProcess from the process instances list, the operator wants to enter the migration view to switch them from version 1 to version 2 of the orderProcess BPMN file in Operate.
question: Which action takes the operator into the migration view after selecting source instances?

options.text: Click the operations panel entry for orderProcess, then select Migrate from the contextual menu attached to that operation in the Processes view of Operate
Click Move in the process instance toolbar, then choose Migrate as the modification subtype from the appearing batch confirmation dialog for the operation
Click Migrate to enter the migration view from the selected source instances of the chosen process version in the Processes view of Operate
Click Apply Modification on the current selection, then accept the Migrate option from the resulting confirmation summary modal in the Processes view

b.text: Incorrect. Move is the entry to batch modification, not migration; the migration view is entered through the Migrate button on the Processes view after selecting source instances of a version.

c.text: Correct. The migration guide states that, after selecting the source instances on the chosen process version, the operator clicks Migrate to enter the migration view in Operate.

d.text: Incorrect. Apply Modification is part of the batch modification commit flow, not the migration flow; the migration view is entered by clicking the Migrate button after selecting instances.

a.text: Incorrect. The operations panel hosts existing operations and their progress, not the entry to the migration view; migration is entered via the Migrate button on the Processes view.

explanation: Per the migration guide, after selecting a process and version in the filters panel and choosing the instances to migrate, the operator clicks Migrate to enter the migration view where the target process and mappings are configured.

scenario: Harbor SaaS runs Camunda 8.8 with 3800 instances per day across its merchant onboarding flow. After mapping every source flow node to a target on the orderProcess migration, the operator wants to recall which button shows the preview of how flow nodes are mapped and how many process instances will migrate.
question: Which button shows the preview of mappings and the expected migrated instance count in the migration view?

options.text: The Apply button in the bottom panel, which both shows the preview and commits the migration once the operator types MIGRATE into the confirmation text box
The Confirm button in the summary modal, which displays the mapping preview before the operator types MIGRATE to start the migration operation across the cluster
The Next button in the migration view, which produces a preview of the flow node mappings and the count of expected migrated instances for the operation in Operate
The Migrate button in the process instance toolbar, which previews the planned mappings while the migration view is still being edited by the operator in Operate

a.text: Incorrect. There is no Apply button in the migration view that both previews and commits; the preview step is the Next button, and the commit step is Confirm plus typing MIGRATE.

c.text: Correct. The migration guide says the operator clicks Next to get a preview of the migration plan, including how flow nodes are mapped and how many process instances are expected to be migrated.

b.text: Incorrect. Confirm in the summary modal is the commit step that finishes the flow after typing MIGRATE; the preview step that shows mappings and counts is the Next button in the migration view.

d.text: Incorrect. The Migrate button in the toolbar enters the migration view; it does not preview mappings. The preview is produced by Next inside the migration view itself.

explanation: Per the migration guide, the operator clicks Next in the migration view to see a preview of how flow nodes are mapped and how many process instances will be migrated, before moving on to the final Confirm step.

scenario: Apex Healthcare runs Camunda 8.8 SaaS with 7400 instances per day across its patient onboarding flow. A platform engineer is reviewing which BPMN elements the Operate migration view will let the operator map from source to target on the cluster, and is checking the documented constraint that governs this capability.
question: Which constraint governs which BPMN elements can be mapped in the Operate migration view?

options.text: Only elements deployed within the same tenant as the source process version can be mapped from source to target in the migration view of Operate across the cluster
Only elements decorated with the camunda:migratable extension can be mapped from source to target in the migration view of Operate on the affected cluster setup
Only elements that share the same BPMN id between source and target versions can be mapped from source to target in the migration view of Operate across the cluster
Only elements with migration supported by Zeebe can be mapped from source to target in the Operate migration view, per the documented supported elements list

b.text: Incorrect. There is no camunda:migratable extension attribute; the documented constraint is that only elements with migration supported by Zeebe can be mapped in the migration view.

c.text: Incorrect. Element BPMN ids do not have to match across versions; the documented gate is whether the element has migration supported by Zeebe, not id equality between source and target.

d.text: Correct. The migration guide says it is currently only possible to map elements with migration supported by Zeebe, and links out to the list of supported BPMN elements that can be mapped.

a.text: Incorrect. The constraint is not tenant scoping; it is element type support. Mapping is gated by whether the BPMN element has migration support in Zeebe, not by tenant boundaries.

explanation: Per the migration guide, mapping in the migration view is currently only possible for BPMN elements with migration supported by Zeebe, and the supported elements list is referenced from the migration concepts documentation.

scenario: Aurora Bank runs Camunda 8.8 with 750 active loanProcess instances. A routing issue earlier today sent 320 instances down a Manual review branch when the gateway should have steered them onto Auto approve. Each instance is otherwise healthy, and the operations team wants a single operation rather than 320 separate modifications.
question: Which Operate feature is the documented remedy when many process instances share the same routing mistake?

options.text: The operator cancels the 320 instances from the list and starts fresh instances on the correct branch, carrying the original payload variables forward by hand.
A new deployment of the loanProcess model with a corrected gateway is published, and a follow-up migration moves the 320 instances onto the new process version.
Modification mode is applied to each of the 320 instances individually, processing them one by one through the modification editor on the instance header.
Process instance batch modification selects the 320 instances at once and moves them all to the correct flow node in a single operation against the cluster.

c.text: Incorrect. Modification mode operates on one instance at a time and is intended for the single-instance case; for many instances stuck in the same branch the documented operation is batch modification.

b.text: Incorrect. The mistake is a runtime routing issue rather than a model defect, so redeployment plus migration is not the documented remedy; batch modification relocates the existing instances directly.

d.text: Correct. Process instance batch modification is the documented Operate operation for selecting multiple process instances and moving them all to the correct flow node in a single operation when they share the same routing mistake.

a.text: Incorrect. Cancelling discards in-flight execution and history; batch modification preserves the 320 running instances and only relocates their active tokens to the correct flow node.

explanation: Operate's process instance batch modification is the documented path when many process instances share the same routing mistake. Rather than modifying each instance separately, the operator selects multiple instances and moves them all to the correct flow node in a single operation.

scenario: Meridian Insurance runs Camunda 8.8 and an operator opens a single stuck claimProcess instance in Operate. They need to enter modification mode so they can move the token from Review claim back to Receive documents, which would otherwise block the claim for 2 hours until a timer fires.
question: How does the operator enter modification mode from the process instance view in Operate?

options.text: The operator clicks the running token on the process diagram, then picks Modify from the context menu to switch the page into modification mode for the instance.
The operator clicks the modify icon on the process instance header, which switches the page into modification mode with a blue banner on top and apply/discard buttons at the bottom.
The operator opens the instance variables panel, edits a system flag to enable modify, and the page automatically reloads into modification mode for the open instance.
The operator opens the operations dropdown in the cluster header and picks Modify instance, which loads the modification editor for the open process instance.

d.text: Incorrect. Modification mode is entered from the instance header, not from a cluster-level operations dropdown; the entry point is the modify icon on the process instance header itself.

a.text: Incorrect. Clicking a token on the diagram does not expose a Modify context menu; modification mode is entered through the modify icon located on the process instance header.

c.text: Incorrect. There is no system variable that toggles modification mode for the page; the documented path is clicking the modify icon on the process instance header to switch the UI.

b.text: Correct. Clicking the modify icon on the process instance header switches Operate into modification mode, which the UI signals with a blue banner at the top and two buttons at the bottom for applying or discarding pending modifications.

explanation: Modification mode is entered by clicking the modify icon on the process instance header. Once active, Operate shows a blue banner at the top of the page and exposes two buttons at the bottom for applying or discarding pending modifications before they commit to the running process instance.

scenario: An Aurora Bank operations team reviews a stuck process instance in Camunda 8.8 Operate where 12 running tokens are waiting on a single user task after a downstream payments-gateway outage of 4 hours. The team wants to cancel every running token on that one flow node at once rather than picking each token individually from the instance history panel.
question: How does an operator cancel all running tokens on a single flow node from Operate's modification mode?

options.text: Select the flow node in the diagram and choose Add from the dropdown so all running tokens on that node are scheduled for cancellation as a pending modification
Select the flow node in the diagram and click Cancel instance from the dropdown so all running tokens on that node are scheduled for cancellation as a pending modification
Open each token row in the instance history panel and use the Cancel instance dropdown on every selected row before applying the modifications to the running instance
Switch to the variables panel and remove the local scope of the flow node so the engine cancels all running tokens nested under that node on the next refresh

b.text: Correct. Operate's modification mode lets an operator select a flow node in the diagram and click Cancel instance from the dropdown, which schedules cancellation of every running token on that node as a single pending modification visible in the instance history.

c.text: Incorrect. The dropdown action on the diagram flow node already cancels all running tokens on that node at once, so iterating row-by-row through the instance history panel is not the workflow that produces this bulk cancellation.

a.text: Incorrect. Add from the flow node dropdown creates a new token on the selected node; it does not cancel any running tokens. The matching action for cancelling running tokens on a node is Cancel instance from the dropdown.

d.text: Incorrect. Deleting variables from a scope does not cancel any token. Cancellation of all running tokens on a flow node is queued only by an explicit Cancel instance modification chosen from that flow node's dropdown.

explanation: To cancel all running tokens on a flow node in Operate, the operator selects the flow node in the diagram and clicks Cancel instance from the dropdown. The pending modification is then reflected in the instance history before being applied.

scenario: An Operate operator at Helios Insurance is repairing a Camunda 8.8 process instance where a missed event left a service task without a running token for 2 hours, blocking 350 claims downstream. The insurer's runbook calls for injecting a fresh token onto that service task without cancelling anything else on the instance.
question: Which dropdown action on a selected flow node adds a new token to that node as a pending modification?

options.text: Cancel instance from the flow node dropdown places a new token on the selected flow node and records it as a pending modification in the instance history panel
Edit variable from the flow node dropdown places a new token on the selected flow node and records it as a pending modification in the instance history panel
Add from the flow node dropdown places a new token on the selected flow node and records it as a pending modification visible in the instance history panel
Move instance from the flow node dropdown places a new token on the selected flow node and records it as a pending modification in the instance history panel

a.text: Incorrect. Cancel instance removes the running tokens from a flow node; it does not add a new token. The dropdown action documented for adding a new token to a chosen flow node is Add.

c.text: Correct. Selecting the flow node and clicking Add from the dropdown creates a new token on that node, and the resulting pending modification is reflected in the instance history panel before the operator applies the changes.

d.text: Incorrect. Move instance combines a cancel on the source node with an add on a target node; it is not the single dropdown action for adding one new token on a chosen flow node.

b.text: Incorrect. Edit variable changes the value of an existing variable in a scope; it does not create any tokens on a flow node and is not the dropdown action that performs an Add token modification.

explanation: Operate's Add modification creates a new token on a chosen flow node. The operator selects the target flow node and clicks Add from the dropdown, and the new token appears as a pending modification in the instance history panel.

scenario: An operator at Lattice Insurance is repairing a Camunda 8.8 process instance handling 800 claims per day, after a pending Add modification created a brand new subprocess scope on the instance. The operator needs to seed that fresh scope with starter variables in the next 15 minutes before applying all modifications, so downstream service tasks have the required inputs when execution resumes.
question: Which sequence in Operate adds variables to the newly generated scope as pending modifications?

options.text: Click Add Variable in the diagram toolbar, type the variable name and value, and the variable is queued as a pending modification on the parent process instance root scope of the instance
Open the variables panel on the root process scope, type the variable name and value, and the variable is queued as a pending modification on every nested running scope of the instance
Select the new scope in the instance history, click Add Variable in the variables panel, type the Name and Value, and on blur the variable becomes a pending modification on the new scope
Select the new scope in the instance history, click Edit Variable in the variables panel, type the name and value, and the variable is queued as a pending modification on that new scope

a.text: Incorrect. The Add Variable action lives in the variables panel of the selected scope, not in a diagram toolbar. It targets the chosen new scope rather than the root scope of the process instance.

b.text: Incorrect. Variables are added to a specific selected scope from the variables panel of that scope. Opening the root scope does not seed every nested scope at once; each scope is targeted by its own Add Variable action.

d.text: Incorrect. Edit Variable changes the value of an existing variable. The action documented for seeding a freshly generated scope with a new Name and Value is Add Variable in the variables panel.

c.text: Correct. With modification mode active the operator selects the new scope from the instance history, clicks Add Variable in the variables panel, fills Name and Value, and on blur with valid values the variable is queued as a pending modification on that scope.

explanation: To add variables to a newly generated scope, the operator selects the scope in the instance history, clicks Add Variable in the variables panel, fills the Name and Value fields, and on blur with valid values the variable is added to the pending modifications.

scenario: Pinnacle Marketplace runs Camunda 8.8 and an operator is repairing a process instance that handles 1500 orders per hour, where a pricing variable on the root scope was set to the wrong currency 2 hours ago. The retailer wants to correct the existing variable value on that root scope without cancelling any tokens, creating new scopes, or restarting the process instance.
question: Which Operate workflow queues an Edit Variable modification on the existing scope without creating any new scope?

options.text: Select the existing scope in the instance history, change the value in the variables panel, blur out of the field, and the Edit Variable modification is queued on that existing scope
Click Add Variable in the variables panel on the existing scope, type a new name for the corrected currency, and the value change is queued as a pending Edit Variable on that root scope
Open the diagram, right-click the flow node that owns the variable, choose Edit Variable from its dropdown, and the corrected value is queued as a pending Edit Variable on that scope
Switch to the modification summary modal, choose the JSON Diff Viewer for the variable, type the new value inline, and the Edit Variable modification is queued on the existing root scope

b.text: Incorrect. Add Variable creates a brand new variable on the scope. Correcting an existing value uses Edit Variable, which is driven by changing the value in the variables panel of the selected scope, not by Add Variable.

a.text: Correct. The docs state that during modification mode, selecting an existing scope in the instance history, changing the value in the variables panel, and blurring out with a valid value queues an Edit Variable pending modification on that scope.

c.text: Incorrect. Edit Variable is not driven from a flow node dropdown on the diagram. The value change happens in the variables panel of the selected existing scope in the instance history.

d.text: Incorrect. The summary modal only displays pending modifications and offers a JSON Diff Viewer for review. The edit itself is performed earlier in the variables panel by changing the value and blurring out of the field.

explanation: Editing an existing variable in modification mode is done from the variables panel of the selected scope. The operator picks the existing scope in the instance history, changes the value, and on blur with a valid value the Edit Variable modification is queued as pending.

scenario: An Aurora Bank operator at a retail bank queues several modifications across three scopes on a single Camunda 8.8 process instance over 30 minutes. Before committing, the operator wants to review every pending change in one place, including a JSON diff for any added or edited variable on the root scope, and selectively delete individual modifications without leaving modification mode.
question: Where in Operate does the operator review every pending modification and a JSON diff for any edited variable before applying?

options.text: The summary modal opened from Apply Modifications in the footer lists every pending modification and offers a JSON or Diff Viewer for any added or edited variable before applying changes
The diagram overlay opened from Apply Modifications in the footer lists every pending modification and offers a JSON or Diff Viewer for any added or edited variable directly on the diagram
The instance history panel renders every pending modification inline with a JSON diff for each edited variable next to the affected scope row in the instance history tree on the page
The variables panel switches to a diff view when modification mode is active, listing every pending modification alongside a JSON diff for each edited variable across every running scope

c.text: Incorrect. Pending modifications are not rendered as inline rows inside the instance history tree. They are gathered in a dedicated summary modal that the operator opens from the Apply Modifications button in the footer.

d.text: Incorrect. The variables panel shows variables on the selected scope only. It does not list every pending modification across scopes and does not provide the JSON or Diff Viewer that the summary modal exposes.

a.text: Correct. Clicking Apply Modifications in the footer opens a modal that lists every pending modification, supports deleting individual entries with a delete icon, and offers a JSON or Diff Viewer for any added or edited variable before the operator applies changes.

b.text: Incorrect. The review surface is a modal opened from the Apply Modifications button, not a diagram overlay. The diagram stays in the background while the modal collects review and per-entry delete actions.

explanation: Operate exposes a summary modal via the Apply Modifications button in the footer. The modal lists every pending modification, supports per-entry delete, offers a JSON or Diff Viewer for added or edited variables, and lets the operator apply or cancel without leaving modification mode.

scenario: Lattice Insurance runs Camunda 8.8 on a claims subprocess and processes 12000 claims per day at the insurer. An on-call operator opens Operate after a 2 hour outage and wants to use process instance modification to add a token at a boundary event attached to a service task, expecting Operate to accept the operation just like any other flow node in the diagram.
question: Which non-supported modification rule applies to the boundary event the operator picked in Operate?

options.text: Move tokens from a subprocess itself is allowed, while Add token to start events and to every boundary event always succeeds for any running process instance shown in Operate's view
Add token and Move tokens to modifications are blocked on start events, boundary events, and events attached to event-based gateways inside the Operate process instance modification dialog
Add token modifications are blocked only on events attached to event-based gateways, while start events and boundary events both accept Add token and Move tokens to operations in Operate
Add token modifications are blocked only on start events, while boundary events and events on event-based gateways accept both Add token and Move tokens to operations in Operate

b.text: Correct. The Operate documentation lists start events, boundary events, and events attached to event-based gateways as flow node types that do not support Add token or Move tokens to modifications, so the operator's boundary event target is blocked by design.

d.text: Incorrect. The non-supported list is broader than start events alone; it also includes boundary events and events attached to event-based gateways for both Add token and Move tokens to operations in Operate.

a.text: Incorrect. Move tokens from a subprocess itself is explicitly listed as not possible, and Add token on start events and boundary events is also blocked rather than universally allowed in Operate.

c.text: Incorrect. The non-supported list covers start events, boundary events, and events attached to event-based gateways together; boundary events are not an exception that accepts Add token in Operate.

explanation: Operate's process instance modification page documents that Add token and Move tokens to operations cannot target start events, boundary events, or events attached to event-based gateways, and that Move tokens from is not possible for a subprocess itself.

scenario: Lattice Bank runs Camunda 8.8 with 3500 active payment instances in Operate. One instance is stuck mid-flow after a misrouted decision and the operator needs to advance that single instance past the stuck activity, allowing its execution to continue while the other 3499 instances keep running untouched.
question: How does process instance modification in Operate let the operator handle the single stuck instance?

options.text: Process instance modification lets the operator continue execution by skipping or repeating activities on the one stuck instance, without altering any of the other running payment instances
Operate cannot advance a stuck instance, so the operator must cancel it and create a brand new process instance from scratch to keep processing the underlying payment work
Process instance batch modification is the only available path, so the operator selects every running payment instance and moves all of them to the same next activity in one operation
Operate signals the broker to automatically push the stuck token forward to the next healthy flow node, with no operator action required on the affected instance

a.text: Correct. The docs describe process instance modification as the mechanism to allow execution to continue on a stuck instance, where the operator can skip or repeat activities for that single instance without affecting any others.

c.text: Incorrect. Batch modification is intended for the separate case where many instances entered the wrong branch and need to move together; using it here would alter healthy payment instances unintentionally.

d.text: Incorrect. The docs describe an operator-driven modification mode in Operate; the broker does not silently advance stuck tokens to a healthy flow node on its own.

b.text: Incorrect. Operate offers modification mode precisely so that a stuck instance can continue execution by skipping or repeating activities, avoiding the need to cancel and recreate the instance.

explanation: Process instance modification in Operate is the operator action that lets a single stuck instance continue execution by skipping or repeating activities. Batch modification is a separate operation for cases where many instances entered the wrong branch and need to be moved together.

scenario: Apex Logistics runs Camunda 8.8 with 800 instances in Operate. For one stuck instance the operator wants to start a new token at the Pack Shipment flow node so that activity will execute, without removing any tokens that are already running elsewhere in the same instance.
question: Which modification-mode action adds a new token to a flow node on a single process instance?

options.text: In modification mode the operator selects the target flow node in the diagram and clicks Add from its dropdown, after which the new token appears as a pending modification in the instance history
The operator deploys a new version of the BPMN model with the target flow node marked as a start point, so the engine picks up an extra token for that flow node on existing instances
In modification mode the operator selects the target flow node and chooses Spawn worker, which immediately starts a token there and bypasses the pending modification summary entirely
The operator opens the process instance variables panel and sets a hidden activityId variable to the target flow node, prompting the broker to seed a token there on the next poll

a.text: Correct. The docs describe adding a new token by selecting the flow node and clicking Add from its dropdown in modification mode; the new token is then visible as a pending modification reflected in the instance history.

c.text: Incorrect. There is no Spawn worker action in the modification flow, and added tokens are always staged as pending modifications first rather than starting immediately.

d.text: Incorrect. Tokens are not introduced by setting hidden variables; the documented mechanism is the explicit Add action on a selected flow node inside modification mode.

b.text: Incorrect. Adding a token to a single running instance is an Operate modification action; redeploying the BPMN model does not retroactively seed tokens onto existing process instances.

explanation: To add a new token, the operator enters modification mode, selects the target flow node, and clicks Add from its dropdown. The new token is then staged as a pending modification reflected in the instance history before being applied.

scenario: Helios Health runs Camunda 8.8 with 1500 instances. For one instance the operator wants to move every running token from the Triage flow node to the Specialist Review flow node, capturing the relocation as a single pending change in the instance history before applying it to the running data.
question: Which workflow performs the documented Move instance modification on a single process instance in Operate?

options.text: The operator drags the running tokens from the source flow node onto the target flow node in the diagram, and Operate applies the move directly without showing any pending modification
The operator opens the variables panel, sets a moveTargetActivityId variable to the target flow node id, then refreshes the instance view so the broker reschedules the tokens to the target
The operator selects the source flow node, picks Move instance from its dropdown, then selects the target flow node, and the relocation appears as a pending modification in the instance history
The operator selects the source flow node and clicks Move instance, and the relocation completes immediately for all instances on that flow node across the cluster without any further input

c.text: Correct. The docs describe the move modification as selecting the source flow node, clicking Move instance, then selecting the target flow node; the pending move is then reflected in the instance history before being applied.

a.text: Incorrect. Operate does not move tokens through drag-and-drop on the diagram, and modifications are always staged as pending entries before being applied to the instance.

b.text: Incorrect. There is no moveTargetActivityId variable that drives a relocation; the move is configured through the flow node dropdown in modification mode, not through a hidden variable name.

d.text: Incorrect. The move modification operates on the single open instance and requires selecting a target flow node; it does not fan out across every instance running on that flow node cluster-wide.

explanation: The Move instance modification is performed by selecting the source flow node, choosing Move instance from the dropdown, then selecting the target flow node. The relocation is then staged as a pending modification reflected in the instance history.

scenario: During modification mode on a Vanguard Airlines Camunda 8.8 instance covering 220 booking changes, an operator has selected a freshly generated scope in the instance history. The operator now needs to recall the exact two input fields the Add Variable form on the variables panel requires before the entry can be staged.
question: Which two fields does the Add Variable form on a new scope require the operator to fill out?

options.text: The Add Variable form requires the operator to fill out a Variable Key field and a Variable Type field, where the type selects between string, number, or boolean before staging the entry
The Add Variable form requires the operator to fill out a Scope Id field for the target scope and a Value field, with the variable name being derived from the scope id selection above
The Add Variable form requires the operator to fill out a Name field for the variable identifier and a Value field for the variable value before staging the entry as a pending modification
The Add Variable form requires the operator to fill out a Path field that references a parent variable and a Value field that overrides the parent value on the selected scope

c.text: Correct. The docs say that on the new scope the operator clicks Add Variable in the variables panel and fills the Name and Value fields for the variable to add before the entry is staged as a pending modification.

a.text: Incorrect. The documented form takes Name and Value; there is no Variable Key plus Variable Type pair with an explicit string, number, or boolean selector in the Add Variable form.

b.text: Incorrect. The form does not collect a Scope Id field; the scope is fixed by which scope the operator selected in the instance history before opening the Add Variable form.

d.text: Incorrect. The Add Variable form takes Name and Value rather than a Path plus Value pair that overrides a parent variable on the selected scope.

explanation: The Add Variable form for a new scope takes a Name field and a Value field. Once the operator blurs out of the last field with valid values, the entry is staged as a pending modification on the selected scope.

scenario: Beacon Retailer runs Camunda 8.8 with 1800 instances. An operator opens one of these instances in modification mode and wants to change the discountRate variable, which is already set on an existing scope in the instance history, before applying any other pending modifications staged on the instance.
question: Which sequence edits an existing variable on an existing scope through modification mode in Operate?

options.text: The operator opens the variables panel, deletes the existing variable entry, and creates a brand new entry with the same name;  modification mode does not expose any edit-in-place flow for existing variables

The operator double-clicks the variable in the diagram view and types the new value directly on the canvas, and Operate writes the change to the running instance without any pending modification entry

The operator selects the existing scope in the instance history, edits the variable value in the variables panel, and blurs the field so that an Edit Variable entry is added to the pending modifications
The operator selects the parent process instance instead of the inner scope, edits the variable there, and modification mode propagates the new value down into the matching existing scope on apply

c.text: Correct. The docs say the operator selects the existing scope in the instance history, edits the variable value in the variables panel, and blurs the field; once the value is valid an Edit Variable modification joins the pending modifications.

a.text: Incorrect. Modification mode does expose an edit-in-place flow for variables on existing scopes; deleting and recreating is not the documented path for changing an existing variable value.

b.text: Incorrect. Variables are edited from the variables panel, not by double-clicking on the BPMN diagram canvas, and edits are staged as pending modifications rather than written directly to the instance.

d.text: Incorrect. The edit must be done on the specific scope that holds the variable; editing the parent process instance variable does not propagate down into a different inner scope on apply.

explanation: To edit an existing variable, the operator selects the holding scope in the instance history and edits the value in the variables panel. Blurring out with a valid value stages an Edit Variable modification in the pending list.

scenario: During a Camunda 8.8 rollout at Granite Insurance covering 4800 instances, an operator has just clicked Apply inside the summary modal on one instance. The operations team wants to understand exactly what feedback Operate gives when the modification operation is created successfully and how quickly the changes show up.
question: What does Operate show when the modification operation is created successfully from the summary modal?

options.text: Operate shows a success notification and the staged changes are then reflected on the instance in a short time once the modification operation has been created successfully from the summary modal
Operate requires the operator to manually run a Sync Now action on the cluster console before any of the staged changes become visible on the affected single process instance in the view
Operate redirects the operator out of the instance view to a dedicated Operations history page, where a completion bar moves from 0 percent to 100 percent before the changes appear on the instance
Operate refuses to apply the changes until every other operator currently viewing the same instance has explicitly approved the pending modification set from their own session of Operate

a.text: Correct. The docs say that on a successful apply from the summary modal the operator sees a success notification and changes are reflected on the instance in a short time, without further manual steps from the operator.

c.text: Incorrect. Operate stays in the instance view and shows a success notification on a successful apply; there is no documented redirect to an Operations history page with a percentage completion bar.

b.text: Incorrect. Changes are reflected in a short time after a successful apply; there is no documented Sync Now action on the cluster console required to surface the staged changes on the instance.

d.text: Incorrect. The documented apply flow does not require explicit approval from other operators viewing the instance before the modification operation can be created and reflected on the instance.

explanation: After Apply, if the modification operation is created successfully, Operate displays a success notification and the staged changes are reflected on the instance in a short time. No additional manual sync or cross-operator approval is required.

scenario: A Cascadia Health operator runs Camunda 8.8 Operate and tries to cancel all running tokens within a single flow node instance nested in a multi-instance body that currently carries 1300 tokens. The operator needs to plan how many tokens will be affected by a single cancel or move action in this version.
question: How many tokens at most can be affected by a single cancel or move operation on this multi-instance scope in this version?

options.text: A single cancel or move operation affects every running child token in the multi-instance body in one step;  the documented limit applies only to plain non multi-instance flow nodes in the instance
A single cancel or move operation affects at most 50 tokens at a time, so the operator must trigger 26 separate operations against the same flow node instance to cover all 1300 running tokens here
A single cancel or move operation affects at most 5000 tokens, so the entire 1300 token multi-instance body can always be processed in one cancel or one move action without any further repetition

A single cancel or move operation affects at most 500 tokens within the flow node instance nested in the multi-instance body, so the operation must be repeated until all child tokens have been handled

d.text: Correct. The docs state that when cancelling or moving all tokens within a single flow node instance nested in a multi-instance body, a maximum of 500 tokens can be affected; the modification can be repeated on the same flow node instance until all tokens have been handled.

a.text: Incorrect. The documented limit specifically applies when cancelling or moving all tokens within a single flow node instance nested in a multi-instance body, so a single operation does not always cover every running child token in one step.

b.text: Incorrect. The documented per-operation cap on a multi-instance scope is 500 tokens, not 50, so the number of repeated operations needed to cover 1300 tokens is not 26.

c.text: Incorrect. The documented per-operation cap is 500 tokens for this case, not 5000; the 1300 token multi-instance body cannot be processed in a single cancel or move action under this limit.

explanation: For a cancel or move that targets all tokens within a single flow node instance nested in a multi-instance body, a maximum of 500 tokens can be affected per operation. The modification must be repeated on the same flow node instance until every token has been handled.

scenario: Sable Logistics runs Camunda 8.8 with a multi-instance body that has 2200 active child tokens on one flow node instance. Working around the documented 500 token per operation limit, the operator wants to know the supported alternatives to handle individual element instances rather than repeating the bulk modification many times.
question: Which alternatives are documented for handling individual element instances around the 500 token per operation cap on this multi-instance scope?

options.text: The operator may temporarily disable Operate write protection from the Console settings to bypass the cap, after which a single modify-all-tokens action affects every running child token on the body in one step

The operator may use single-instance modification by selecting the relevant instances in the instance history, or use the Orchestration Cluster API endpoint where a custom filter can be specified for affected instances
