The operator must scale the broker partition count to lift the cap;  the 500 token limit comes from a per-partition setting on the cluster rather than from a documented modification rule on the body itself

The operator must split the multi-instance body into two BPMN tasks and redeploy the process definition;  the docs state that modification mode does not support multi-instance bodies of any kind at all

b.text: Correct. The docs say that to modify individual element instances instead, the operator can use single-instance modification by selecting the relevant instances in the instance history, or use the Orchestration Cluster API to specify a custom filter on the affected instances.

c.text: Incorrect. The 500 token cap is a per-operation modification limit documented for this version, not a partition setting on the cluster; scaling broker partitions is not listed as a workaround.

d.text: Incorrect. The docs do support modification on multi-instance bodies up to 500 tokens per operation, so splitting the body into two BPMN tasks is not the documented alternative for individual element instances.

a.text: Incorrect. There is no documented Operate write-protection toggle in Console that lifts the 500 token per operation cap on this kind of multi-instance modification.

explanation: Around the 500 token per operation limit, the docs let the operator either pick relevant instances in the instance history via single-instance modification, or call the Orchestration Cluster API with a custom filter to target individual element instances.

scenario: Granite Bank runs Camunda 8.8 and a junior operator is reviewing the modification mode limits before working on a multi-instance body that holds 700 running child tokens on a single flow node instance. The operator needs the exact action types that the 500 token per operation cap applies to in this version of Operate.
question: Which modification action types does the 500 token per operation limit on a multi-instance body apply to?

options.text: The 500 token cap applies only to edit variable actions on a multi-instance body;  each edit fans out to a child token and the limit bounds how many child copies can change at once

The 500 token cap applies to every modification action in Operate including add token, edit variable, and add variable, so each kind of modification is capped at the same 500 token boundary per operation
The 500 token cap applies when cancelling or moving all tokens within a single flow node instance nested in a multi-instance body, which are the documented actions covered by the per-operation limit
The 500 token cap applies only to add token actions;  the documented limit was introduced to prevent operators from seeding too many extra tokens on the same multi-instance body at once

c.text: Correct. The docs say the 500 token per operation cap applies when cancelling or moving all tokens within a single flow node instance nested in a multi-instance body; those are the action types named under the maximum token modification limit.

b.text: Incorrect. The documented cap names cancelling or moving all tokens on a multi-instance scope; it does not state that every modification action including add token, edit variable, and add variable is capped at the same number.

d.text: Incorrect. The cap names cancelling or moving all tokens specifically, not add token actions, so the limit is not framed as a control on seeding additional tokens on the body.

a.text: Incorrect. The cap names cancelling or moving all tokens on a multi-instance flow node instance, not edit variable actions on a multi-instance body, so the scope of the limit is different.

explanation: The 500 token per operation limit is documented for cancelling or moving all tokens within a single flow node instance nested in a multi-instance body. Other modification action types are not listed under the same per-operation cap.

scenario: Junction Retail runs Camunda 8.8 on the order-process model with 9000 orders per day at the retailer. An operator created a fresh instance with zbctl and set orderValue to the string "99" instead of an integer, so the XOR gateway condition orderValue>=100 raised an incident in Operate within 3 minutes of activation on the broker.
question: What did the operator do in Operate to clear the incident and let the XOR gateway evaluate correctly on that instance?

options.text: Delete the orderValue variable on the failed instance and create a fresh process instance with the orderValue variable correctly typed as an integer
Replay the instance from the start event with the orderValue variable configured correctly, clearing the incident without deleting the instance
Edit the orderValue variable in Operate by removing the surrounding quotation marks so the value is stored as an integer, then initiate a retry of the process instance from the top right of the page
Add a new variable named orderValueInt holding the integer 99 alongside the existing orderValue string variable while preserving both on the instance

b.text: Incorrect. The documented resolution path is to edit the variable in place and retry the instance rather than replaying it; Operate exposes an edit icon and a retry action on the process instance page for exactly this case.

c.text: Correct. The Operate guide instructs the operator to click the edit icon next to orderValue, remove the quotation marks so the value becomes an integer, save the change, and then initiate a retry of the process instance to clear the incident.

a.text: Incorrect. Operate allows editing the existing variable on the failed instance; there is no requirement to delete the variable or create a fresh instance to resolve the type mismatch on the XOR gateway.

d.text: Incorrect. Operate supports editing the existing orderValue variable; adding a parallel variable would not satisfy the XOR gateway condition orderValue>=100, which reads the same variable name on the instance.

explanation: Operate documents that a string-typed orderValue can be edited in place by removing the quotation marks so the value becomes an integer, saved with the checkmark icon, and the process instance can then be retried from the process instance page to clear the incident.

scenario: Pinnacle Marketplace runs Camunda 8.8 on the order-process model with 5000 orders per day for the retailer. The on-call operator must recall which variable the XOR gateway evaluation reads on every newly created process instance so the marketplace's routing path through the order-process diagram stays correct on the broker.
question: Which variable does the order-process model require on every new process instance for the XOR gateway evaluation to happen properly?

options.text: The customerId variable is required on every new process instance so the XOR gateway evaluation can route the order through the higher-value or lower-value path shown for the retailer in Operate
The orderId variable is required on every new process instance so the XOR gateway evaluation can compare the order identifier against the 100 threshold defined on the outgoing sequence flow
The paymentStatus variable is required on every new process instance so the XOR gateway evaluation can split traffic between the insured shipping and uninsured shipping paths in the broker
The orderValue variable is required on every new process instance so the XOR gateway evaluation can route the instance along the correct outgoing sequence flow inside the order-process model

b.text: Incorrect. The documentation names orderValue, not orderId, as the variable the XOR gateway evaluates; orderId is used in the example payload to identify the order but does not drive the gateway condition on the model.

a.text: Incorrect. customerId does not appear in the documented order-process example; the XOR gateway condition reads orderValue to decide between the higher-value and lower-value routing paths in the model.

d.text: Correct. The documentation states that every process instance created for the order-process model requires an orderValue so the XOR gateway evaluation will happen properly and route the instance along the right path.

c.text: Incorrect. paymentStatus is not the variable the order-process XOR gateway reads; the documented condition uses orderValue to choose between the outgoing sequence flows after Initiate Payment.

explanation: The Operate guide for resolving incidents states that every process instance created for the order-process model requires an orderValue variable so the XOR gateway evaluation can route the instance along the correct outgoing sequence flow.

scenario: Aurora Logistics ships 4000 orders per day in Camunda 8.8 on the order-process model. After repeated XOR gateway incidents during a 2 hour window at the logistics provider, the operations team must recall the exact data type their order-process model requires for orderValue so the gateway condition stops failing on new instances.
question: Which data type must orderValue carry so the XOR gateway condition orderValue>=100 evaluates without raising an incident?

options.text: The orderValue variable must be a boolean flag so the XOR gateway condition orderValue>=100 can route the instance along the higher-value sequence flow for every order processed by the provider
The orderValue variable must be an integer so the XOR gateway condition orderValue>=100 can compare numeric values and route the order instance along the correct outgoing sequence flow on the model
The orderValue variable must be a string formatted as an ISO 4217 currency literal so the XOR gateway condition orderValue>=100 can compare it against the 100 threshold defined on the gateway model
The orderValue variable must be a JSON object containing a nested amount field so the XOR gateway condition orderValue>=100 can read the numeric amount of the order from the payload on the broker

d.text: Incorrect. The documented model requires orderValue to be an integer; the example incident message Cannot compare values of different types: STRING and INTEGER confirms the gateway compares orderValue itself as an integer, not a nested object field.

c.text: Incorrect. A string value is exactly what causes the documented incident; the engine cannot compare a STRING against an INTEGER, so orderValue must be an integer for the gateway condition orderValue>=100 to evaluate.

a.text: Incorrect. A boolean cannot satisfy a numeric comparison like orderValue>=100; the documented model requires an integer orderValue so the XOR gateway can evaluate the threshold and route the instance.

b.text: Correct. The Operate guide shows that storing orderValue as a string raises an incident because the engine cannot compare STRING and INTEGER, so the operator removes the quotation marks to make orderValue an integer for the gateway.

explanation: The order-process XOR gateway condition orderValue>=100 requires orderValue to be an integer so the engine can compare numeric values; a string value raises an incident with the message Cannot compare values of different types: STRING and INTEGER.

scenario: Helios Banking runs Camunda 8.8 on the order-process model with 7000 instances per day at the bank. After creating a new instance with zbctl, the operator must advance it to the XOR gateway by completing the Initiate Payment service task using community-supported zbctl on a Linux host inside the bank's tooling environment.
question: Which zbctl command creates the job worker that completes the Initiate Payment task and advances the instance toward the XOR gateway?

options.text: The command ./bin/zbctl --insecure create worker initiate-payment --handler cat starts a worker that subscribes to the Initiate Payment job type and completes activated jobs so the instance advances to the XOR gateway

The command ./bin/zbctl --insecure complete job initiate-payment --variables empty marks the Initiate Payment task as completed from the command line and advances the instance to the XOR gateway on the broker for the bank

The command ./bin/zbctl --insecure publish message initiate-payment --correlationKey 1234 advances the Initiate Payment task and routes the instance toward the XOR gateway through message correlation inside the bank's cluster

The command ./bin/zbctl --insecure activate task initiate-payment --workerName cat activates the Initiate Payment task with a one-shot worker and advances the instance toward the XOR gateway on the broker for the bank

a.text: Correct. The Advance an instance to an XOR gateway section shows the operator running ./bin/zbctl --insecure create worker initiate-payment --handler cat to start a worker that completes Initiate Payment jobs and advances the instance to the XOR gateway.

b.text: Incorrect. The documented step uses zbctl create worker with a handler, not a complete job command; the worker subscribes to the job type and completes activated jobs as they arrive on the broker.

c.text: Incorrect. Initiate Payment is a service task completed by a worker; the publish message command is used later for the Payment Received intermediate message catch event, not for the Initiate Payment task.

d.text: Incorrect. zbctl uses create worker with a handler to subscribe to a job type rather than a one-shot activate task command, and the documented handler value on Linux is cat with the initiate-payment job type.

explanation: The Advance an instance to an XOR gateway section instructs the operator to create a job worker for the Initiate Payment task with ./bin/zbctl --insecure create worker initiate-payment --handler cat so activated jobs are completed and the instance moves toward the XOR gateway.

scenario: Meridian Brokerage runs Camunda 8.8 on the order-process model with 8000 orders per day at the brokerage. After the operator starts an instance with orderId 1234 and completes Initiate Payment within 4 minutes, the team must choose the right correlationKey on the published payment-received message so it correlates with that exact instance on the broker.
question: Which correlationKey value on the published payment-received message correlates with the running instance carrying orderId 1234 in this case?

options.text: The correlationKey value must be set to the bpmnProcessId order-process on the published payment-received message
The correlationKey value must be set to the broker-generated process instance key on the order-process instance
The correlationKey value must be set to 1234, the orderId variable set on the running order-process instance on the broker
The correlationKey value must be set to the deployment id of the order-process model on the published payment-received message

b.text: Incorrect. The documented command uses --correlationKey=1234, which matches the orderId variable on the instance rather than the broker-generated process instance key; correlation happens on the configured correlation key, not the engine instance key.

a.text: Incorrect. bpmnProcessId names the process definition and is shared across every instance, so it cannot pick a single running instance; correlation uses the correlationKey value that matches the catch event's correlation key expression.

d.text: Incorrect. Deployment ids identify a deployment batch and cannot pick a single running instance; correlation of the payment-received message uses the correlationKey value tied to the instance's correlation key expression.

c.text: Correct. The documented step runs ./bin/zbctl --insecure publish message payment-received --correlationKey=1234, where 1234 is the orderId set on the instance, so the message correlates with that specific running process instance.

explanation: The Operate guide publishes a payment-received message with --correlationKey=1234, where 1234 is the orderId set on the instance when it was created; this correlation key value targets the specific running instance so it advances past the Payment Received catch event.

scenario: The Camunda 8.8 getting-started guide walks Aurora Retail through running the order-process.bpmn model for 600 orders. Every order-process instance is created with an orderValue variable that drives the XOR gateway, but on one run the orderValue arrives as a JSON string instead of an integer and the instance triggers an incident.
question: Why does Operate raise an incident on this order-process instance when orderValue is supplied as a string?

options.text: The XOR gateway accepts the string value and treats it as zero, so the instance always takes the no-insurance branch and an incident is only raised when no branch matches the comparison rule

The engine silently converts the orderValue string to an integer and routes the instance through the default flow, so an incident is raised only if the converted number falls below the gateway threshold
The engine rejects the create-instance call outright when orderValue is a string, so no process instance is ever created and no XOR gateway evaluation takes place at all for that order

The XOR gateway condition orderValue greater than or equal to 100 cannot compare values of different types, so the engine raises an incident reporting a comparison between STRING and INTEGER on the instance

d.text: Correct. The docs show the incident message Expected to evaluate condition orderValue greater than or equal to 100 successfully, but failed because cannot compare values of different types, STRING and INTEGER, raised at the XOR gateway evaluation on the order-process instance.

b.text: Incorrect. The engine does not silently coerce orderValue from string to integer; the docs show the incident comes from a type mismatch at the XOR gateway, not from a converted value falling below a threshold.

c.text: Incorrect. The docs show the instance is created with the string-typed orderValue and an incident appears later when the XOR gateway tries to evaluate; the create-instance call itself is not rejected.

a.text: Incorrect. The docs show the XOR gateway raises an incident because of a type mismatch rather than treating the string as zero and routing the instance through a no-insurance default branch.

explanation: The XOR gateway in order-process.bpmn evaluates orderValue greater than or equal to 100, which the engine cannot evaluate when orderValue is a STRING and the threshold is an INTEGER. The mismatch raises an incident on the instance that must be resolved before progression.

scenario: A Tessera Bank operator runs Camunda 8.8 and creates an order-process.bpmn instance with orderValue set as a string of value 99 for one of 250 orders. The operator now wants to use Operate to repair the incident in place without cancelling and recreating the underlying process instance for the order.
question: Which Operate workflow resolves this incident by repairing the offending variable in place?

options.text: The operator opens the Optimize dashboard, marks the incident as ignored on the affected instance, and the engine then bypasses the XOR gateway and continues execution at the next flow node downstream
The operator deletes the orderValue variable through the variables panel and lets the engine restart the gateway evaluation with no orderValue at all;  a missing variable is treated as zero on retry

The operator cancels the running instance from the instance view and creates a new instance with a corrected payload;  the documented incident-resolution path does not allow editing variables in place
The operator clicks the edit icon next to orderValue, removes the quotation marks so the value becomes an integer, clicks the checkmark to save, and then initiates a retry of the process instance

d.text: Correct. The docs say the operator clicks the edit icon next to the variable, removes the quotation marks so orderValue becomes an integer, clicks the checkmark to save, and then initiates a retry of the process instance to clear the incident.

b.text: Incorrect. The documented resolution edits the existing orderValue variable to a valid integer rather than deleting it; a missing variable is not described as being treated as zero on retry.

c.text: Incorrect. The docs explicitly describe editing the variable in place from Operate and then retrying the instance, so cancelling and recreating is not the documented incident-resolution path.

a.text: Incorrect. Resolution happens in Operate by editing the offending variable and retrying the instance, not by marking an incident as ignored in Optimize; Optimize does not bypass XOR gateway evaluation.

explanation: In Operate the operator clicks the edit icon next to orderValue, removes the quotation marks so the value is parsed as an integer, clicks the checkmark to save the change, and then initiates a retry of the process instance to resolve the incident.

scenario: A Wren Bank operator follows the Camunda 8.8 getting-started guide on a development cluster sized for 5 instances. The operator has just created an order-process instance and now needs to push it to the XOR gateway, which requires completing the Initiate Payment job and clearing the Payment Received intermediate message catch event.
question: Which two zbctl actions does the guide use to advance the instance to the XOR gateway?

options.text: The guide deploys an Operate signal worker for Initiate Payment using zbctl deploy worker and broadcasts a payment-received signal with zbctl broadcast signal correlated against every running instance
The guide creates a worker for the Initiate Payment job using zbctl create worker initiate-payment and publishes a payment-received message with zbctl publish message correlated by the orderId key

The guide uses zbctl complete instance to mark Initiate Payment as done and zbctl correlate event to trigger Payment Received, which the engine then automatically propagates to the XOR gateway condition

The guide uses zbctl create timer to fire an Initiate Payment timer event and zbctl create job to seed a synthetic Payment Received job, which together drive the instance toward the gateway evaluation

b.text: Correct. The guide first runs zbctl create worker initiate-payment to complete the Initiate Payment task, then runs zbctl publish message payment-received with a correlationKey matching the orderId so the intermediate message catch event correlates with the instance.

a.text: Incorrect. The guide uses zbctl publish message rather than zbctl broadcast signal, and the documented advancement step targets one correlated instance rather than broadcasting to every running instance at once.

d.text: Incorrect. The guide drives the instance forward with a worker and a published message, not with zbctl create timer or zbctl create job seeding a synthetic Payment Received job on the instance.

c.text: Incorrect. The advancement commands are zbctl create worker initiate-payment and zbctl publish message payment-received, not zbctl complete instance or zbctl correlate event named in this option.

explanation: To advance an order-process instance to the XOR gateway, the guide creates a worker for Initiate Payment using zbctl create worker initiate-payment, then publishes a payment-received message with zbctl publish message and a correlationKey matching the orderId.

scenario: During a Camunda 8.8 demo at Stellar Insurance covering 12 instances, the operator must recall the exact zbctl publish message command, including its correlationKey value, that the guide uses to drive an order-process instance past the Payment Received intermediate message catch event before the XOR gateway.
question: Which zbctl publish message invocation does the guide use, and what correlationKey value does it pass?

options.text: The guide runs zbctl publish event payment-received with correlationKey order-process, which correlates against the bpmnProcessId of the order-process model rather than against an instance variable
The guide runs zbctl publish message order-confirmed with correlationKey orderValue, which correlates against the orderValue variable that drives the XOR gateway evaluation on the order-process model

The guide runs zbctl publish message payment-received with correlationKey orderInstanceKey, which the engine resolves to the broker-generated process instance key before correlating against the catch event

The guide runs zbctl publish message payment-received with correlationKey 1234, which correlates against the orderId 1234 that was set in the variables payload when the order-process instance was created

d.text: Correct. The guide invokes zbctl publish message payment-received with correlationKey 1234 to correlate against the orderId 1234 set at instance creation time and clear the Payment Received intermediate message catch event before the XOR gateway.

b.text: Incorrect. The guide publishes a payment-received message rather than an order-confirmed message, and the correlationKey is a literal 1234 value rather than the orderValue variable that drives the XOR gateway.

a.text: Incorrect. The guide uses zbctl publish message rather than zbctl publish event, and the correlationKey is the orderId 1234 set on the instance, not the bpmnProcessId of the order-process model.

c.text: Incorrect. The correlationKey value used by the guide is the literal orderId 1234, not a special orderInstanceKey value resolved to the broker-generated process instance key on the instance.

explanation: The guide uses zbctl publish message payment-received with correlationKey 1234. That value matches the orderId 1234 set on the order-process instance at creation time and clears the Payment Received intermediate message catch event.

scenario: A Quanta Logistics operator follows the Camunda 8.8 getting-started guide on a Linux box for 8 instances. After running zbctl to create an order-process instance the operator wants to complete the Initiate Payment service task with a community zbctl worker rather than wiring up a custom Java handler from scratch for the demo.
question: Which Linux zbctl invocation does the guide use to complete the Initiate Payment service task on the instance?

options.text: The guide runs ./bin/zbctl --insecure assign task initiate-payment to a Tasklist user, who then completes the Initiate Payment service task interactively from the Tasklist UI in the browser
The guide runs ./bin/zbctl --insecure complete job initiate-payment, which directly marks any pending Initiate Payment job as completed without starting a long-running zbctl worker process on the box

The guide runs ./bin/zbctl --insecure deploy worker initiate-payment.bpmn, which expects a worker BPMN model on disk that the engine compiles into an Initiate Payment completion routine on the instance

The guide runs the Linux invocation ./bin/zbctl --insecure create worker initiate-payment --handler cat, which starts a community zbctl worker that completes Initiate Payment jobs using cat as the job handler

d.text: Correct. The guide shows the Linux invocation ./bin/zbctl --insecure create worker initiate-payment --handler cat, which starts a community zbctl worker that completes the Initiate Payment service task using cat as the handler.

b.text: Incorrect. The guide drives the Initiate Payment task with a zbctl worker rather than a zbctl complete job command, so the documented flow is not a direct one-shot completion of the pending job.

a.text: Incorrect. The guide does not assign the task to a Tasklist user; the Initiate Payment service task is completed by a zbctl worker process that uses a shell command as its handler.

c.text: Incorrect. There is no documented zbctl deploy worker command for an initiate-payment.bpmn model; the guide starts a worker process directly with create worker --handler cat on Linux.

explanation: On Linux the guide runs ./bin/zbctl --insecure create worker initiate-payment --handler cat to start a community zbctl worker that completes the Initiate Payment service task. The shell cat command serves as the job handler in the demo.

scenario: A Zenith Bank developer follows the Camunda 8.8 getting-started guide on a Windows laptop while exploring 4 instances. The developer needs the equivalent of the Linux worker handler that the guide demonstrates for completing the Initiate Payment service task on the order-process instance using a community zbctl binary.
question: Which Windows zbctl invocation does the guide use to complete the Initiate Payment service task on the instance?

options.text: The guide runs powershell.exe -File complete-payment.ps1 to call into Camunda Operate over HTTPS, which then marks the Initiate Payment service task as done on the order-process instance
The guide runs the Windows invocation .\bin\zbctl.exe --insecure create worker initiate-payment --handler findstr .*, which starts a community zbctl worker using findstr as its job handler on Windows
The guide runs .\bin\zbctl.exe --secure create worker initiate-payment --handler cat from a WSL bash shell;  the documented Windows flow simply reuses the Linux cat handler unchanged

The guide runs .\bin\zbctl.exe --insecure run worker initiate-payment without any --handler value;  on Windows the worker defaults to a no-op handler that completes Initiate Payment jobs automatically

b.text: Correct. The Windows tab in the guide shows .\bin\zbctl.exe --insecure create worker initiate-payment --handler findstr .*, which starts a community zbctl worker using findstr as the Windows equivalent of the Linux cat handler for Initiate Payment.

a.text: Incorrect. The guide drives Initiate Payment with a community zbctl worker rather than a PowerShell script that calls into Operate over HTTPS to mark the service task as done.

c.text: Incorrect. The Windows tab uses zbctl.exe with the findstr handler rather than reusing the Linux cat handler from a WSL bash shell with the --secure flag.

d.text: Incorrect. The Windows command keeps an explicit --handler findstr .* value rather than a no-op default, and the worker action is create worker rather than run worker on the binary.

explanation: On Windows the guide starts the Initiate Payment worker with .\bin\zbctl.exe --insecure create worker initiate-payment --handler findstr .*. The findstr .* shell command is the Windows equivalent of the cat handler used on Linux and macOS in the guide.

scenario: At Nimbus Marketplace a developer follows the Camunda 8.8 getting-started guide on macOS to drive 15 instances forward, each carrying orderId 1234 and a 99 orderValue. The developer must complete both the Initiate Payment service task and the Payment Received intermediate message catch event end to end on a single instance.
question: Which two zbctl.darwin commands does the macOS tab of the guide use to advance the instance to the XOR gateway?

options.text: The macOS tab uses ./bin/zbctl --insecure create worker initiate-payment --handler cat unchanged and skips the publish message step;  on macOS the Payment Received event fires automatically on worker completion
The macOS tab uses brew services start zeebe-worker initiate-payment and brew services start zeebe-message payment-received, which together drive the instance up to the XOR gateway evaluation step

The macOS tab uses ./bin/zbctl.darwin --insecure create worker initiate-payment --handler cat and ./bin/zbctl.darwin --insecure publish message payment-received --correlationKey 1234 to drive the instance forward

The macOS tab uses ./bin/zbctl.darwin --insecure submit job initiate-payment and ./bin/zbctl.darwin --insecure send signal payment-received --target-instance 1234 against the broker on the same machine

c.text: Correct. The macOS tab of the guide runs ./bin/zbctl.darwin --insecure create worker initiate-payment --handler cat to complete the Initiate Payment job and then ./bin/zbctl.darwin --insecure publish message payment-received --correlationKey 1234 to advance the instance.

d.text: Incorrect. The macOS flow uses create worker and publish message commands rather than submit job and send signal commands, and the correlationKey is supplied with --correlationKey 1234 rather than a --target-instance flag.

b.text: Incorrect. The guide drives the macOS demo through community zbctl.darwin commands, not through brew services start commands that manage a separate zeebe-worker or zeebe-message service on the host.

a.text: Incorrect. The publish message step is required on macOS as well, and the macOS tab uses the zbctl.darwin binary rather than reusing the Linux zbctl binary or relying on an automatic Payment Received event.

explanation: On macOS the guide first completes Initiate Payment with ./bin/zbctl.darwin --insecure create worker initiate-payment --handler cat. It then runs ./bin/zbctl.darwin --insecure publish message payment-received --correlationKey 1234 to clear the Payment Received intermediate message catch event before the XOR gateway.

scenario: Cascade Payments runs a Camunda 8.8 order-flow tutorial for 4 onboarding engineers. After deploying the model, the team needs to advance an instance past the Payment Received intermediate message catch event so it reaches the downstream XOR gateway. The instance carries 1 outstanding correlationKey value of 1234 that must match the catch event.
question: Which zbctl command advances the instance past the Payment Received intermediate message catch event in this tutorial?

options.text: Run zbctl publish message "payment-received" with the correlationKey flag set to 1234 so the broker correlates the message to the waiting instance
Run zbctl create instance for the payment-received process id with variables set to a correlationKey of 1234 so a new instance is started
Run zbctl complete job payment-received with the correlationKey flag set to 1234 so the broker closes the open catch event job
Run zbctl create worker payment-received with a cat handler so the broker pushes the catch event to the worker for completion

a.text: Correct. The tutorial advances the instance past the Payment Received intermediate message catch event by publishing a message named payment-received with correlationKey 1234, which the broker correlates to the waiting instance.

b.text: Incorrect. Creating a new instance does not unblock the existing one; intermediate message catch events are advanced by publishing a correlated message, not by starting another instance.

d.text: Incorrect. Intermediate message catch events do not produce jobs for workers; they wait for a published message correlated by name and correlationKey, so a worker subscription does not unblock them.

c.text: Incorrect. There is no zbctl complete-job command that correlates messages by key; message catch events are advanced by publishing a message, not by completing a job from the CLI.

explanation: Intermediate message catch events in Camunda 8 wait for a message published with a matching name and correlationKey. The Operate tutorial uses zbctl publish message "payment-received" --correlationKey=1234 to unblock the waiting instance so it can progress to the XOR gateway.

scenario: An Operate trainer at Lattice Insurance walks 6 platform engineers through the official incident-resolution flow in Camunda 8.8 against a sample process with 120 instances stuck for 2 hours. The trainer wants to explain the supported variable-edit gestures and the two locations from which a retry can be initiated on a single process instance page.
question: Which combination of variable gesture and retry locations does the Operate incident-resolution flow actually support?

options.text: Edit or delete a variable from the instance view, then initiate a retry only from the top right corner of the page using the retry icon
Edit or add a variable from the instance view, then initiate a retry from the top right corner of the page or from below the Operations heading
Add a variable from the instance view, then initiate a retry from the incidents list view by selecting the affected instance row
Edit a variable from the instance view, then initiate a retry from the Modeler diagram by clicking the affected element in the BPMN canvas

b.text: Correct. The flow lets operators edit an existing variable and also add a missing variable from the instance view, then start a retry from either the top right corner of the instance page or from below the Operations heading.

a.text: Incorrect. The docs describe editing and adding variables, not deleting them, and they list two retry locations on the instance page rather than only the top right corner.

c.text: Incorrect. The retry buttons described live on the instance page itself; editing variables and starting a retry from the incidents list view is not the gesture covered in this flow.

d.text: Incorrect. Operate does not initiate retries from the Modeler canvas; retries are triggered from the process instance page in Operate, not from a BPMN editor.

explanation: When resolving an incident, Operate supports editing an existing variable or adding a missing one from the process instance view, and the instance page exposes two retry entry points: one in the top right corner and one in the Operations panel.

scenario: At Forge Retail, an on-call engineer inspects a Camunda 8.8 order process with 3500 instances stuck on the same condition expression. Operate shows: Expected to evaluate condition 'orderValue>=100' successfully, but failed because: Cannot compare values of different types: STRING and INTEGER. The engineer wants to clear the incident on one instance before scripting a batch.
question: Which sequence of actions on the instance page resolves this incident according to the Operate tutorial?

options.text: Click the edit icon next to orderValue, wrap the value in additional quotation marks, click save, then call the retry endpoint from the Tasklist application for the same instance
Cancel the affected instance from the instance page, then create a new instance with orderValue set as an integer using zbctl so the condition can evaluate successfully
Open Modeler, change the gateway condition to compare strings instead of integers, redeploy the process, and the affected instances retry automatically once the new version is live
Click the edit icon next to orderValue, remove the quotation marks from the value, click the checkmark icon to save, then initiate a retry from the top right or the Operations area

d.text: Correct. The tutorial fixes the type-mismatch incident by editing orderValue to remove the quotation marks (making it an integer), saving with the checkmark, and then starting a retry from the top right or the Operations area of the instance page.

a.text: Incorrect. Adding more quotation marks keeps the value as a string, which is what caused the type-mismatch in the first place; the fix is to remove the quotes so the value is an integer.

c.text: Incorrect. Redeploying a model creates a new version, and existing instances continue on their original version; the tutorial resolves the incident by editing the variable on the running instance and retrying.

b.text: Incorrect. Cancelling and recreating is not the documented fix; Operate supports editing the variable on the existing instance and triggering a retry, preserving the original instance and its history.

explanation: The example incident is a type mismatch where orderValue is a STRING but the condition expects INTEGER. The documented resolution is to edit orderValue, remove the quotation marks so it becomes an integer, save with the checkmark, then retry the instance from the top right or the Operations area.

scenario: An Aurora Logistics operator opens an incident in Camunda 8.8 Operate after 750 instances stalled at a gateway. Operate displays: Expected to evaluate condition 'orderValue>=100' successfully, but failed because: Cannot compare values of different types: STRING and INTEGER. The operator must explain what the message tells them about the failure before editing anything.
question: What does this Operate incident message tell the operator about the cause of the failure?

options.text: The gateway condition orderValue>=100 evaluated against a missing variable, so the broker raised an incident asking the operator to add orderValue before the instance can continue
The gateway condition orderValue>=100 was evaluated successfully and returned false, so the instance took a path with no outgoing sequence flow and stopped at the gateway
The gateway condition orderValue>=100 timed out after the broker's default condition evaluation budget, so the broker rolled back the transition and raised an incident
The gateway condition orderValue>=100 could not be evaluated because the orderValue variable is a STRING while the literal 100 is an INTEGER, producing a type-mismatch error

d.text: Correct. The message states that evaluation failed because the values being compared have different types: STRING and INTEGER. The fix is to make orderValue an integer so the >= comparison can succeed.

b.text: Incorrect. The message explicitly says evaluation failed, not that it returned false. A successful evaluation that just chose another path would not raise this type-mismatch incident at all.

c.text: Incorrect. The message describes a type-comparison failure, not a timeout; it points to the operand types STRING and INTEGER as the root cause, not an evaluation budget.

a.text: Incorrect. The message reports a type mismatch between STRING and INTEGER, meaning orderValue exists but holds a string; if it were missing, the error would be about an undefined variable instead.

explanation: Operate surfaces incident messages directly from the engine. This one explains that the condition orderValue>=100 could not be compared because the operands have different types (STRING vs INTEGER), so the operator must change orderValue from a string to an integer before retrying.

scenario: A junior operator at Beacon Bank has just fixed the orderValue variable on a stalled Camunda 8.8 instance. The instance is 1 of 40 incidents the team needs to clear this morning. The operator now needs to actually re-run the failed step so the instance can progress to the next activity in the model.
question: What action on the instance page actually moves the process past the resolved incident?

options.text: Wait for the broker's automatic retry interval to elapse so the engine re-evaluates the failed gateway condition with the new variable value
Save the edited variable; Operate detects the change and resumes the instance automatically without any retry button being pressed by the operator
Initiate a retry of the process instance using the retry control in the top right corner of the page or the one below the Operations heading
Cancel the instance and start a fresh one with the corrected variable values from the process definition list view in Operate

c.text: Correct. After editing the variable, the operator must initiate a retry. The instance page exposes the retry control in the top right corner and again below the Operations heading; either one triggers the re-evaluation.

b.text: Incorrect. Saving the variable change does not automatically resume the instance; the operator must explicitly initiate a retry to re-evaluate the failed step.

d.text: Incorrect. The documented flow keeps the original instance and retries it after editing the variable, rather than cancelling and recreating, which would lose context and instance history.

a.text: Incorrect. Resolving an incident in Operate requires an explicit operator-initiated retry; there is no automatic broker retry that picks up edited variables after a condition-evaluation incident.

explanation: Editing a variable resolves the data problem, but the engine still needs to be told to re-run the failed step. Operate exposes two retry controls on the process instance page: one in the top right corner and one below the Operations heading; either one initiates the retry.

scenario: An operator at Harbor Insurance opens a Camunda 8.8 instance with 1 stuck gateway and needs to change the orderValue variable so it is an integer instead of a string. The operator has located the variable in the instance view and now needs to perform the right gestures to change the value and persist it before retrying.
question: Which icons does the operator click on the instance view to change orderValue and save the new value?

options.text: Click the variable name to open a side panel, type the new orderValue value, then click an Apply button at the bottom of the side panel to save
Right-click the orderValue variable to open a context menu, choose Edit value, then press Enter on the keyboard to save the new value to the instance
Click the retry icon next to the orderValue variable, type the new value into the prompt that appears, then click the same retry icon a second time to save
Click the edit icon next to the orderValue variable, change the value to remove the quotation marks, then click the checkmark icon to save the change

d.text: Correct. The tutorial steps are: click the edit icon next to the variable you want to edit, change the value (here, removing the quotation marks so it is an integer), then click the checkmark icon to save the change.

a.text: Incorrect. Operate's documented gesture uses the edit icon on the variable row and a checkmark icon to save, not a side panel with an Apply button below the variable list.

b.text: Incorrect. The Operate incident tutorial documents an edit icon and a checkmark icon for saving; it does not describe a right-click context menu for editing variables on the instance view.

c.text: Incorrect. The retry control is for re-running the instance after editing, not for editing variables; variable changes use the edit icon and are saved with the checkmark icon.

explanation: To edit a variable on the instance view, the operator clicks the edit icon next to the variable, changes the value (for the example incident, removing the quotation marks so orderValue is an integer), then clicks the checkmark icon to save before triggering a retry.

scenario: A developer at Ember Couriers runs the Camunda 8.8 Operate tutorial on a Windows 10 laptop with 1 cluster connected via zbctl.exe. The tutorial finishes with a Ship Without Insurance service task and the developer needs the platform-specific worker command so the open job can be completed in under 5 minutes without changing operating systems.
question: Which zbctl invocation completes the Ship Without Insurance service task on Windows according to the tutorial?

options.text: Use zbctl.darwin with create worker ship-without-insurance and the cat handler arg, which is the cross-platform handler shipped with zbctl on every operating system platform
Use zbctl.exe with create worker ship-without-insurance and the findstr handler arg so the worker handles the open job on Windows using the documented Windows handler binary
Use zbctl.exe with create worker ship-without-insurance and the cat handler arg; cat is provided as a built-in fallback when running zbctl on the Windows binary distribution
Use zbctl.exe with publish message ship-without-insurance and a correlationKey flag so the broker treats the open service task as a message correlation target field

b.text: Correct. The Windows command in the tutorial is zbctl.exe create worker ship-without-insurance with the handler argument set to findstr matching any line, which acts as the Windows equivalent of the cat handler used on Linux and macOS.

a.text: Incorrect. zbctl.darwin is the macOS binary, not the Windows binary, and cat is not available by default on Windows; the tutorial uses findstr .* as the Windows handler.

c.text: Incorrect. The Windows command uses findstr .* rather than cat, because cat is not a standard Windows command; zbctl does not provide a built-in cat fallback.

d.text: Incorrect. Ship Without Insurance is a service task and is completed via a worker job, not via a published message; message correlation does not complete service tasks.

explanation: The tutorial provides per-OS commands: Linux uses ./bin/zbctl with the cat handler, macOS uses ./bin/zbctl.darwin with the cat handler, and Windows uses ./bin/zbctl.exe with a findstr handler matching any line. The handler binary differs because cat is not a standard Windows command.

scenario: Lattice Insurance runs Camunda 8.8 with 12000 claims per day. After a downstream microservice was down for 4 hours and then restored, an operator finds many process instances stuck with the same incident in Operate and wants to act on them as one group from the process instance list view on the cluster.
question: What does a selection represent in Operate for this batch workflow?

options.text: A saved query that filters the process instance list view by error message text so the operator can search the same failing instances again later on the cluster
A scheduled job that scans the cluster every minute for instances matching a saved filter and replays them automatically without the operator having to confirm anything
A set of process instances chosen from the list view on which the operator can carry out a batch retry or a batch cancellation operation in Operate
A draft deployment that bundles affected process definitions for redeployment so the microservice fix is applied to running instances through the broker on the cluster

a.text: Incorrect. A saved filter or query is separate from a selection; a selection is the concrete set of instances chosen for a batch operation, not the query that found them.

c.text: Correct. Operate defines a selection as a set of process instances on which a batch retry or a batch cancellation can be carried out, picked by checking the box next to each instance in the list view.

b.text: Incorrect. Operate does not run automated selections on a schedule; the operator chooses the instances and confirms the batch operation explicitly from the list view.

d.text: Incorrect. Selections do not deploy process definitions; they group already-running instances so a batch retry or cancellation can be applied to them in one operation.

explanation: Selections in Operate group existing process instances from the list view so the operator can apply a batch retry or batch cancellation to all of them at once after a shared root cause has been resolved.

scenario: Aurora Bank runs Camunda 8.8 with 35000 payments per day. After a payment provider outage of 90 minutes is resolved, an operator needs to retry about 800 stuck process instances from the Operate list view in one batch rather than opening each instance and clicking retry one at a time on the cluster.
question: Which two steps in Operate create a selection and apply a batch operation to those stuck instances?

options.text: Drag the affected process instances into the Operations panel, then drop them onto the retry icon so the engine processes the whole group at once on the broker side
Click the incident banner on a single instance, then choose Apply to all similar instances from the dropdown so the engine fans the operation out across the partitions
Check the box next to each process instance you want to include in the selection, then choose the operation you want to apply from the controls on the list view
Open the Operations panel, type a FEEL filter that matches the stuck instances, then click apply so the broker retries every matching instance on the cluster in order

d.text: Incorrect. The Operations panel reports the status of submitted operations; it is not the place where selections are typed as FEEL filters and applied.

b.text: Incorrect. Operate does not offer an Apply to all similar instances action on the incident banner; the supported flow is to build a selection from the list view.

c.text: Correct. The documented two-step flow is to check the box next to each instance that should be part of the selection and then choose the operation (retry or cancel) to apply.

a.text: Incorrect. Operate has no drag-and-drop target on the Operations panel; selections are built by check boxes on the list view and acted on through the operation controls.

explanation: Operate documents two simple steps for batch handling: check the box next to each process instance to include and then select the operation to apply. After confirmation, the Operations panel reports progress.

scenario: Stellar Retailer runs Camunda 8.8 with 22000 orders per day. After confirming a batch retry on 600 stuck process instances in Operate, an operator needs visibility into how the broker is progressing through the requested operations on the cluster so the team can decide when to escalate or re-run any leftover instances.
question: Where in Operate can the operator monitor the current status of the confirmed batch operation?

options.text: The incidents dashboard on the home page lists each instance the batch touched and refreshes the row colour as the broker processes each request across the cluster nodes
The process instance detail page shows a progress bar at the top right that fills as the broker works through the batch operation on each instance in the selection
The Operations panel in Operate displays the current status of all operations, including the batch that was just confirmed on the selected process instances by the operator
The cluster health view in Console reports per-partition counters for completed retries so the operator can confirm the batch finished on the broker side of the platform

a.text: Incorrect. The incidents dashboard summarizes incidents; tracking submitted batch operations and their progress is the role of the Operations panel after confirmation.

d.text: Incorrect. Console reports cluster health, but Operate itself shows the status of submitted operations through the Operations panel attached to the instance views.

b.text: Incorrect. The process instance detail page surfaces incidents and variables for a single instance; it does not aggregate progress for a multi-instance batch operation.

c.text: Correct. After confirmation, the Operations panel displays the current status of all operations, which includes any batch retry or cancellation the operator has just applied to a selection of process instances.

explanation: Operate exposes an Operations panel that reports the current status of all operations after confirmation. This is the surface where progress of a batch retry or cancellation submitted from a selection is observed.

scenario: At Quanta Logistics, a downstream payments microservice was down for 2 hours and 1800 Camunda 8.8 order-fulfillment instances now share an identical incident. The payments service is healthy again, so an operator needs to clear the stuck instances in one gesture rather than clicking each row individually in Operate.
question: What Operate feature lets the operator retry these 1800 instances together in a single gesture?

options.text: Open Tasklist, filter by the affected process definition, and click the bulk retry button at the top of the Tasklist queue to retry all matching instances
Run a SQL UPDATE statement against the broker database to mark the affected instances as resolved, then the engine retries them on the next scheduler tick
Create a selection by checking the boxes next to the affected process instances, then choose the retry operation to apply it to every instance in the selection at once
Click retry on the topmost incident in the list; Operate then walks the rest of the list automatically and retries each instance one by one in order

c.text: Correct. Operate supports a selection: the operator checks the boxes next to the process instances to include, picks the retry operation, confirms it, and the operation runs against every instance in the selection at once.

b.text: Incorrect. Camunda 8 instance state lives in the broker, not in a database operators are expected to mutate; Operate exposes selections and batch operations as the supported way to retry many instances.

d.text: Incorrect. Operate does not auto-walk an incident list after a single click; bulk retry requires building a selection by checking the boxes next to each instance the operator wants to include.

a.text: Incorrect. Tasklist is for user task work, not for retrying broker-level incidents; batch retries on process instances are performed from Operate using selections and operations.

explanation: Operate's selection feature is built for exactly this scenario. The operator checks the boxes next to the affected process instances to form a selection, picks the retry operation, confirms it, and the engine then retries every instance in the selection.

scenario: An Operate trainer at Halcyon Insurance teaches 10 platform engineers the official terminology used in Camunda 8.8 documentation. The trainer wants the team to use the exact term for the grouping mechanism that lets operators apply a batch retry or batch cancellation to many process instances at once on a typical incident affecting 200 instances at the same time.
question: What does the Operate documentation call the grouping of process instances used for batch retries and cancellations?

options.text: A worklist, which is a Tasklist construct shared between Operate and Tasklist for bulk progressing process instances that share the same task type
A batch, which is a server-side queue of process instances scheduled for retry by the broker once the next polling interval elapses on the cluster
A collection, which is a saved query that re-evaluates against the broker each time the operator opens it and triggers retries on every matching instance
A selection, which is a set of process instances on which an operator can carry out a batch retry or a batch cancellation operation together

d.text: Correct. Operate calls this a selection: a set of process instances on which an operator can carry out a batch retry or a batch cancellation. The user creates the selection by checking the boxes next to the instances to include.

b.text: Incorrect. Operate uses the word selection for the operator-built set; it does not describe a server-side polling queue as the abstraction for batch operations on process instances.

c.text: Incorrect. The docs refer to a selection that the operator builds by checking boxes, not to a saved query that re-evaluates automatically against the broker.

a.text: Incorrect. Worklist is not the Operate term for this concept; selections live in Operate and are not shared as a Tasklist abstraction for task-level work.

explanation: In Operate, a selection is the official term for a set of process instances an operator builds by checking boxes in the instance list. Once the selection is in place, the operator picks an operation (such as batch retry or batch cancellation) and applies it to every instance in the selection.

scenario: An operator at Northwind Retail confirms a batch retry across 2400 stalled Camunda 8.8 order instances after a microservice outage. The team lead wants visibility into how many of those instances have actually been processed by the broker over the next 30 minutes without polling each instance row individually in the instance list view.
question: Where in Operate can the team lead see the live progress of the confirmed batch retry across the 2400 instances?

options.text: Open the Operations panel in Operate, which lists the current status of all operations including the just-confirmed batch retry across the selection of instances
Open the broker exporter dashboard in Elasticsearch, which is the only place where the per-instance status of an in-flight batch retry operation is exposed live
Refresh the process instance list view repeatedly; Operate does not surface a dedicated status view for batch operations on selections of instances
Open the Tasklist queue and filter by the affected process definition; the queue depth shows how many instances remain to be retried by the broker over time

a.text: Correct. After confirming a batch retry on a selection, Operate shows the Operations panel which lists the current status of all operations, so the team lead can see progress without inspecting each instance row.

b.text: Incorrect. Operate already exposes batch operation status in the Operations panel; operators are not expected to read Elasticsearch dashboards to track in-flight retries.

d.text: Incorrect. Tasklist surfaces user tasks, not the progress of broker-level batch operations; that visibility comes from Operate's Operations panel.

c.text: Incorrect. The Operations panel is the documented status view for confirmed batch operations; operators do not have to refresh the instance list view to track them.

explanation: After an operator confirms a batch operation on a selection, Operate surfaces the Operations panel that lists the current status of all operations. This is the supported way to watch progress of a batch retry or batch cancellation as it runs against the selection.

scenario: An on-call engineer at Riverstone Bank inspects 950 Camunda 8.8 loan-application instances stuck on an incident caused by a misconfigured downstream API. The API has been decommissioned and the affected instances cannot be salvaged; the team needs to clear them from the active list without retrying them so the next 24 hours of work are unblocked.
question: Which selection-and-operation sequence in Operate is appropriate for clearing these unsalvageable instances?

options.text: Apply a batch retry operation across the entire process definition without building a selection; Operate scopes the retry only to instances currently in an incident state
Delete the deployed process definition from the deployments view; Operate then cascades a cancellation to every running instance of that definition automatically
Build a selection of the affected process instances by checking the boxes, then apply a batch cancellation operation; confirm and monitor progress in the Operations panel
Build a selection of the affected process instances by checking the boxes, then apply a batch retry operation; the broker will then prune any instance whose downstream API has been removed

c.text: Correct. When stuck instances cannot be salvaged, the documented pattern is to check the boxes to build a selection, pick the batch cancellation operation, confirm it, and follow progress in the Operations panel.

d.text: Incorrect. A batch retry tries to resume execution; it does not prune unsalvageable instances. To remove them, the operator applies a batch cancellation operation on the selection instead.

b.text: Incorrect. Operate does not document a cascading cancellation triggered by deleting a process definition; cancellations are applied through selections and the cancel operation on the instance list view.

a.text: Incorrect. Operations are applied to a selection the operator builds by checking the boxes; there is no documented option to run a retry across an entire definition while implicitly scoping it to incidents.

explanation: Operate models bulk work as selections plus operations. To remove unsalvageable instances, the operator builds a selection of the affected instances, picks the batch cancellation operation, confirms it, and watches the Operations panel for progress.

scenario: Aurora Logistics runs Camunda 8.8 SaaS where dispatchers receive about 900 user tasks per day in Tasklist. Some tasks are pre-assigned by the BPMN model to specific groups, while others land in a shared queue until a dispatcher decides to claim one and start working on it.
question: Which two assignment patterns does Tasklist support for user tasks in Camunda 8?

options.text: User tasks can be auto-assigned to users or groups directly by the BPMN process, or left unassigned so a Tasklist user can self-assign and later unassign before completion
User tasks must always be auto-assigned in the BPMN process to a single user; the self-assignment flow in Tasklist is reserved for service tasks and message catch events
User tasks must always be self-assigned by a Tasklist user; auto-assignment to users or groups via the BPMN process is available only through an external identity sync
User tasks must be assigned by a Tasklist administrator after the instance starts; auto-assignment by the BPMN process and self-assignment by end users are both unavailable

a.text: Correct. Tasklist supports both BPMN-driven assignment to users or groups and self-assignment from the queue; the assignee may also unassign the task before completion.

c.text: Incorrect. Auto-assignment to users or groups is configured directly on the BPMN user task element; no external identity sync layer is needed for the standard Tasklist flow.

b.text: Incorrect. Self-assignment from Tasklist applies to user tasks specifically; service tasks are completed by job workers and do not appear in the Tasklist queue at all.

d.text: Incorrect. Tasklist users themselves can claim and complete tasks; administrator-only assignment is not the model described for the standard Tasklist user task flow.

explanation: Tasklist user tasks can be automatically assigned to users or groups in the BPMN process or self-assigned from the queue; once assigned, the user can complete the task or unassign it.

scenario: Vertex Bank runs a loan-review workflow in Camunda 8.8 with 2500 user tasks per day in Tasklist. A reviewer self-assigns an urgent task from the shared queue, then realises a colleague with regional expertise should handle it instead, and wants to release the task without forcing completion.
question: Which Tasklist action lets the reviewer release the self-assigned task back to the queue?

options.text: The reviewer must ask a Tasklist administrator to remove her assignment; end users themselves cannot release a user task that has already been claimed from the queue
The reviewer can unassign the task at any time before completion, which returns it to the queue so another Tasklist user can pick it up and complete it later
The reviewer must complete the task once she has self-assigned it; Tasklist does not expose an unassign action after a user task has been claimed from the queue
The reviewer can transfer the task to a named colleague via a direct reassignment action, which overwrites the original BPMN assignment with the new Tasklist user reference

b.text: Correct. The Tasklist docs state that once a task is assigned to a user, the user can unassign it if they do not intend to work on it, returning the task to the queue.

c.text: Incorrect. Tasklist explicitly allows the assignee to unassign a claimed task; completion is not the only exit path for an assigned user task in the Camunda 8 docs.

a.text: Incorrect. End users can release their own assigned tasks directly in Tasklist; no administrator intervention is documented for the standard unassign action on a user task.

d.text: Incorrect. Tasklist does not document a direct user-to-user transfer action; the assignee unassigns the task and another user then claims it from the shared queue instead.

explanation: When a user task in Tasklist is assigned, the assignee can either complete it or unassign it. Unassigning returns the task to the queue so another user can pick it up and finish the work.

scenario: At Helios Healthcare, clinicians work through 1500 tasks per day in Tasklist on Camunda 8.8. A new clinician opens the Tasks page for the first time and wants to understand which screen region holds the queue and filters and which region shows the selected task contents.
question: How is the Tasks page laid out in Tasklist for Camunda 8?

options.text: The top of the page shows the queue of tasks as a horizontal strip, and the bottom area shows the details and forms for the currently selected task in the queue
The left side of the page shows task filters and the queue of tasks, and the right side shows the details of the currently selected task in the queue
The left side shows only tasks assigned to the current user, and the right side shows a separate read-only feed of unassigned tasks still waiting in the shared queue
The left side shows the BPMN process diagram for navigation, and the right side shows the queue of tasks plus the details of the currently selected task in the queue

b.text: Correct. The Tasks page is split into a left pane that holds task filters and the queue of available tasks, and a right pane that shows the selected task details.

d.text: Incorrect. The Tasks page does not embed the BPMN diagram in its main layout; the diagram is reached from the Process tab inside an opened task detail view instead.

a.text: Incorrect. The Tasks page uses a side-by-side queue and detail layout, not a top strip with a bottom pane; that arrangement does not match the documented page structure.

c.text: Incorrect. The Tasks page does not split into separate assigned and unassigned feeds; the filters in the left pane control which subset of tasks the single queue shows.

explanation: The Tasks page in Tasklist shows task filters and the queue on the left side and the details of the currently selected task on the right side, forming a side-by-side layout.

scenario: Lattice Bank routes 400 tasks per day for KYC reviews to Tasklist in Camunda 8.8. An analyst opens a review task whose BPMN user task element does not embed a Camunda Form, and she still needs to see the task data so she can verify the applicant before clicking complete the task.
question: What does the Tasklist task detail page show when the selected user task has no attached form?

options.text: The detail page displays a generic completion button only and hides task data, requiring the analyst to query the process variables through the Camunda REST API instead
The detail page displays the task variables in place of a form, so the analyst can read the task data and then complete the task from the same screen
The detail page shows an empty pane with an error icon and blocks task completion until a Camunda Form is later linked to the user task in the deployed BPMN model
The detail page redirects the analyst to Operate to view the variables; the Tasklist detail pane is rendered only when a Camunda Form is attached to the user task

b.text: Correct. The docs state that if the task does not have a form, the detail page displays the task variables, so the analyst can review the data and complete the task in Tasklist.

a.text: Incorrect. Tasklist does not hide task data when no form is attached; the variables are rendered in place so users can review and complete the task without external tooling.

d.text: Incorrect. Tasklist renders the task variables itself when no form is attached; no documented redirect to Operate replaces the detail pane for variable review in this flow.

c.text: Incorrect. A missing form does not block task completion; Tasklist falls back to a variable view that the assignee can use to complete the task from the detail page directly.

explanation: On the Tasklist task detail page, a task that includes a Camunda Form shows the form for completion. If the task has no form, Tasklist instead displays the task variables in the same pane.

scenario: Meridian Insurance rolls out Camunda 8.8 Tasklist to 350 users who handle 6000 tasks per day across claims triage and underwriting. The product owner is preparing onboarding videos and wants a one-line summary of the top-level pages an end user sees in Tasklist, so the materials match the navigation that ships in the Camunda 8.8 release.
question: Which two main pages does Tasklist present to an end user in Camunda 8.8?

options.text: The Inbox page lists tasks claimed by the user, and the Outbox page lists tasks the user has completed during the latest reporting period
The Dashboard page summarizes assigned tasks, and the Search page lets the user run free-text queries across the historical task archive
The Operations page lists running process instances, and the Tasks page lets the user create ad-hoc tasks for colleagues across the cluster
The Tasks page lists tasks available to the user or group, and the Processes page lets the user start new processes from deployed definitions

d.text: Correct. The Tasklist user guide names two main pages: a Tasks page to manage tasks and a Processes page to start processes. End users navigate between exactly these two top-level views.

a.text: Incorrect. Tasklist does not expose Inbox and Outbox pages. The two documented main pages are the Tasks page (manage tasks) and the Processes page (start processes).

c.text: Incorrect. Browsing running process instances is an Operate concern. Tasklist's two main pages per the user guide are the Tasks page and the Processes page.

b.text: Incorrect. Tasklist has no Dashboard or Search page at the top level. The user guide documents exactly two main pages: Tasks and Processes.

explanation: Tasklist's user guide lists two main top-level pages: the Tasks page, used to manage tasks available to the user or group, and the Processes page, used to start new processes from deployed definitions.

scenario: Vertex Bank rolls out Camunda 8.8 Tasklist and an operations manager triages 4500 tasks per day across loan origination and KYC reviews. She wants to know which preview attributes Tasklist lists on each task tile in the queue, so the design team can decide which process variables to surface as a context description for human reviewers.
question: Which set of fields does the Tasklist queue preview show on each task tile in Camunda 8.8?

options.text: Task name, candidate group, task version, last update date, due date, follow up date, process instance key, and tenant id per tile
Task name, process name, optional context description, assignee, priority, creation date, due date, and follow up date on every task tile
Task name, process name, assignee, claimed date, completion date, business key, and SLA breach indicator displayed on every visible task tile
Task name, bpmnProcessId, taskDefinitionId, assignee, last modified date, due date, claim count, and cluster identifier per tile

c.text: Incorrect. The documented tile fields are task name, process name, optional context description, assignee, priority, creation date, due date, and follow up date. Claimed date, completion date, business key, and SLA indicators are not part of the queue preview.

b.text: Correct. The Tasks queue section lists exactly task name, the name of the process the task belongs to, an optional context description, assignee, priority, creation date, due date, and follow up date as the queue tile preview fields.

a.text: Incorrect. Candidate group, task version, last update date, process instance key, and tenant id are not documented queue tile fields. The tile shows priority alongside the creation, due, and follow up dates.

d.text: Incorrect. bpmnProcessId, taskDefinitionId, last modified date, claim count, and cluster identifier are not part of the queue tile. The documented preview uses task name, process name, context description, assignee, priority, and three date fields.

explanation: The Tasklist tasks queue tile preview shows the task name, the name of the process the task belongs to, an optionally configured task context description, the assignee, the priority, the creation date, the due date, and the follow up date.

scenario: Atlas Marketplace operates a Camunda 8.8 Tasklist deployment serving 7000 tasks per day. A product manager asks what an agent sees on the right-hand detail panel after picking a task from the queue, particularly for user tasks where the BPMN modeler did not attach a Camunda Form to the element before deploying the process model.
question: What does the Tasklist task detail view display when the selected task has no attached form in Camunda 8.8?

options.text: The detail view renders a generic completion form with one text input per BPMN attribute so the agent can capture freeform notes before completing
The detail view shows an empty placeholder and the task cannot be completed from Tasklist until a Camunda Form is attached and the process is redeployed
The detail view displays the task variables so the agent can review their values and complete the task without filling out a Camunda Form
The detail view redirects to Operate's process instance page and the agent completes the task by editing variables in the audit panel there

a.text: Incorrect. Tasklist does not auto-generate a generic completion form from BPMN attributes. When the task has no attached form, the detail view falls back to displaying the existing task variables.

d.text: Incorrect. Tasklist does not redirect the agent to Operate. When the task has no form, the Tasklist detail panel itself displays the task variables and the agent completes the task in place.

c.text: Correct. The Tasklist user guide states that if a task does not have a form attached, the task details view displays the task variables so the agent can review them and complete the task.

b.text: Incorrect. Tasks without an attached form remain completable from Tasklist. The detail view simply shows the task variables instead of a Camunda Form, and the agent can still complete the task.

explanation: In the Tasklist detail view, a task with an attached Camunda Form shows the form to fill out and submit. If no form is attached, the panel instead displays the task variables, and the agent completes the task from that variables view.

scenario: Beacon Logistics dispatches 1200 tasks per day through Camunda 8.8 Tasklist. A new agent sees three date columns on each task tile and is unsure whether the due date or the follow up date marks the deadline for finishing the task, versus the latest moment at which she should start working on it, so her team lead asks for a precise mapping.
question: Which statement correctly distinguishes the follow up date and the due date on a Tasklist task in Camunda 8.8?

options.text: The follow up date marks the latest moment the assignee should start work, while the due date marks the deadline by which the task should be finished
The follow up date marks when an automatic escalation message fires, while the due date marks when the broker times out the task with an incident
The follow up date is a reminder timestamp set by the assignee, while the due date is a server clock that ticks down once the task has been claimed
The follow up date is the deadline for finishing the task, while the due date marks the latest moment the assignee should start working on the task

b.text: Incorrect. Neither field triggers automatic escalation messages or broker incidents in Tasklist. The user guide says the follow up date defines when work should start and the due date defines the finish deadline.

d.text: Incorrect. The two roles are reversed in this option. The follow up date defines when work should start and the due date defines the deadline by which the task should be finished.

c.text: Incorrect. The follow up date and due date are process data attached to the user task, not assignee-set reminders or claim-driven server timers. The mapping is start versus finish.

a.text: Correct. The Ordering section states that the follow up date defines the latest time you should start working on a task and that the due date provides a deadline for when the task should be finished.

explanation: Tasklist distinguishes the follow up date (the latest moment the assignee should start work on a task) from the due date (the deadline by which the task should be finished). Both dates are available as ordering keys alongside creation date and priority.

scenario: Sable Bank runs 250000 instances per week of its loan-origination process through Camunda 8.8 and is evaluating Optimize for executive dashboards. The platform team is concerned that running analytical queries on the live runtime would slow down the brokers, so they need to know how Optimize obtains and persists its data.
question: How does Optimize collect and persist process data for its dashboards in Camunda 8?
