options.text: Optimize embeds the runtime view from Operate inside its dashboards and keeps no storage layer of its own, so each dashboard query reads directly from the runtime store
Optimize queries the Zeebe brokers directly through their gRPC API, then caches the results in a local file store on disk for analytical querying from each dashboard
Optimize makes REST API calls to the Camunda server to collect new historical data and stores that data in its own Elasticsearch database for analytical querying
Optimize subscribes to the runtime broker log over a Kafka topic and stores the consumed events in a PostgreSQL database that powers all of its dashboards and reports

c.text: Correct. The docs state Optimize makes REST API calls to the Camunda server to collect new historical data and stores it in its Elasticsearch database for offline analysis.

b.text: Incorrect. Optimize ingests via REST API calls and persists into Elasticsearch; the documented path is not direct gRPC traffic to the brokers or a local file-based cache layer.

d.text: Incorrect. The Optimize ingestion path documented for Camunda 8 is REST-based collection into Elasticsearch; no Kafka topic feeding a PostgreSQL store is described here.

a.text: Incorrect. Optimize maintains its own Elasticsearch store of collected historical data so analysts can query reports and dashboards independently without affecting the runtime.

explanation: Optimize calls the Camunda server through its REST API to collect new historical data and persists it in an Elasticsearch database, allowing reports to be queried without touching the runtime.

scenario: At Junction Retail, 40 business analysts review weekly process performance across 18000 orders per day in Camunda 8.8 Enterprise. The COO asks the platform team which Camunda 8 component is positioned for non-technical stakeholders who want collaborative reports, shared dashboards, and bottleneck analysis.
question: Which Camunda 8 component does the docs position for these business stakeholders?

options.text: Operate, which is positioned for viewing and analyzing individual process instances at the runtime level rather than for executive dashboards and shared performance reports
Tasklist, which is positioned for reviewing and completing user tasks in a queue rather than for executive dashboards and shared process performance reports across instances
Modeler, which is positioned for designing BPMN, DMN, and Form diagrams rather than for executive dashboards or shared process performance reports across many instances
Optimize, which is geared toward business stakeholders and offers business intelligence tooling for Camunda enterprise customers to share reports and analyze bottlenecks

d.text: Correct. The Optimize overview states it is geared toward business stakeholders and offers business intelligence tooling for Camunda enterprise customers to share process intelligence.

a.text: Incorrect. Operate is positioned for viewing and analyzing process instances at the runtime layer; it is not the business intelligence component the docs position for collaborative dashboards.

b.text: Incorrect. Tasklist is positioned for reviewing and completing user tasks; it is not the component the docs position for business intelligence reporting and bottleneck analysis.

c.text: Incorrect. Modeler is positioned for designing BPMN, DMN, and Form diagrams; it is not the component the docs position for shared business intelligence dashboards and reports.

explanation: Optimize is positioned in the Camunda 8 docs as the business intelligence component for enterprise customers, geared toward business stakeholders who collaborate on reports and analyze bottlenecks.

scenario: Tessera SaaS runs 50000 instances per month of its onboarding process on Camunda 8.8 and is mapping each Camunda 8 component to one of the three pillars the docs call out: Design, Automate, and Improve. The platform architect needs to confirm which pillar Optimize specifically supports across the suite.
question: Which of the three Camunda 8 pillars does Optimize specifically support according to the Optimize overview?

options.text: Optimize supports the Improve pillar, leveraging process data captured during execution to analyze bottlenecks and identify areas for ongoing process improvement at the org
Optimize supports the Design pillar, providing BPMN authoring tools that let business analysts model new processes before they reach the Camunda 8 runtime for execution
Optimize supports the Automate pillar, executing service tasks and external job workers on behalf of the Camunda 8 runtime to drive process instances forward end to end
Optimize supports all three pillars equally, replacing Modeler for Design, Zeebe for Automate, and the historical reporting layer for Improve inside a single combined product

a.text: Correct. The Optimize page lists Design, Automate, and Improve as the three pillars and positions Optimize as the Improve component leveraging process data for improvement.

b.text: Incorrect. Design is supported by Modeler, where users author BPMN, DMN, and Form diagrams; Optimize is not described as a process design tool anywhere in the Camunda 8 docs.

c.text: Incorrect. Automate is the runtime layer carried by Zeebe and connectors; Optimize is an analytical layer that reads historical data and does not execute process instances itself.

d.text: Incorrect. Each of the three pillars is supported by distinct Camunda 8 components; Optimize is described specifically as the Improve component, not a replacement for the other two.

explanation: Camunda 8 covers three pillars: Design, Automate, and Improve. Modeler covers Design and Zeebe covers Automate, while Optimize is positioned as the Improve component using historical process data.

scenario: At Catalyst Bank, 200 analysts run ad-hoc reports against Optimize built on Camunda 8.8 while 5000 instances per hour execute on the cluster. The reliability engineer needs to confirm that running Optimize reports during business hours will not degrade runtime throughput on the brokers.
question: Which property of the Optimize ingestion model lets analysts run reports without affecting the Camunda runtime?

options.text: Optimize streams every event in real time from the Zeebe broker partitions directly into each dashboard, so analytical queries do hit the runtime store but are throttled by a quota
Optimize requires analysts to schedule queries during a nightly window when the runtime is paused, so reports cannot run concurrently with live process execution against the cluster
Optimize disables runtime queries while a dashboard is rendering and re-enables them once the report completes, so runtime impact is bounded by short query windows during the day
Optimize stores collected historical data in its own Elasticsearch database and serves dashboards from that store, so analytical queries do not load the Camunda runtime broker layer

d.text: Correct. The docs state Optimize collects historical data into its own Elasticsearch database, enabling users to analyze reports and dashboards independently without affecting runtime.

a.text: Incorrect. The documented ingestion path is REST API collection of historical data with dashboards served from Elasticsearch; the runtime store is not queried for each dashboard render.

c.text: Incorrect. There is no documented mechanism that pauses runtime queries while a dashboard renders; isolation comes from the separate Elasticsearch analytical store, not query throttling.

b.text: Incorrect. The Camunda runtime does not need to be paused for Optimize reporting; isolation is achieved by storing collected data in Elasticsearch and serving reports from that store.

explanation: Because Optimize ingests data via REST API into its own Elasticsearch database, analysts can query reports and dashboards from that analytical store without putting load on the runtime brokers.

scenario: Stellar Bank runs 60000 instances per month on Camunda 8.8 and the platform lead is summarising the product suite for a steering committee. He wants to state, in one slide, which Camunda 8 component covers each of the three top-level pillars of process automation that the Optimize overview page calls out as the framing for the platform.
question: What are the three key aspects of process automation that Camunda 8.8 is built to handle, per the Optimize overview?

options.text: The three pillars are Design, Automate, and Improve; Modeler covers Design, the engine plus Tasklist and Operate cover Automate, and Optimize covers Improve
The three pillars are Plan, Deploy, and Observe; Modeler plans, the orchestration cluster deploys, and Optimize plus Operate jointly cover the Observe layer
The three pillars are Model, Run, and Monitor; Modeler covers Model, Zeebe and Tasklist cover Run, and Operate covers Monitor as the BI surface in the suite
The three pillars are Design, Run, and Audit; Modeler covers Design, the engine and Tasklist cover Run, and Operate is the audit and BI reporting surface

a.text: Correct. The Optimize overview names Design, Automate, and Improve as the three pillars. Modeler covers Design, the engine plus Tasklist and Operate cover Automate, and Optimize is positioned as the Improve component.

c.text: Incorrect. The documented pillars are Design, Automate, and Improve, not Model, Run, and Monitor. Operate is not the BI surface; Optimize is the BI component for business stakeholders.

d.text: Incorrect. The pillars are Design, Automate, and Improve, not Design, Run, and Audit. Operate views and analyzes runtime process instances; it is not the BI or audit reporting layer.

b.text: Incorrect. The pillars are Design, Automate, and Improve, not Plan, Deploy, and Observe. Optimize alone, not Optimize plus Operate, is positioned against the Improve pillar in the overview.

explanation: The Optimize overview frames Camunda 8 around three pillars: Design (the Modeler), Automate (the engine together with Tasklist and Operate), and Improve. Optimize is the component aligned to the Improve pillar, leveraging historical process data for BI-style reporting.

scenario: Kestrel Insurance plans to enable Optimize alongside Camunda 8.8 to analyze 45000 instances per day from a claims workflow. The architecture review board asks how Optimize gets process data out of the engine without affecting runtime, and where it persists the historical records it shows on its reports and dashboards for the business team.
question: How does Optimize collect process data from the Camunda 8.8 server and where does it store that data?

options.text: Optimize subscribes to a Kafka exporter on the broker and persists the records into a relational database that powers the analytical reports and dashboards
Optimize attaches as a Zeebe partition replica and the broker streams events into a built-in column store running inside each Optimize cluster node
Optimize makes REST API calls to the Camunda server, collects new historical data, and stores the data in its Elasticsearch database for the reports and dashboards
Optimize reads directly from the broker's RocksDB state files on disk and writes the snapshots into a managed MongoDB cluster that backs the dashboards

a.text: Incorrect. Optimize does not consume a Kafka exporter or use a relational database. The overview states it makes REST API calls to the Camunda server and stores collected data in an Elasticsearch database.

c.text: Correct. The overview states Optimize makes REST API calls to the Camunda server, collects new historical data, and stores it in its Elasticsearch database so users can analyze reports and dashboards independently of runtime.

d.text: Incorrect. Optimize does not read RocksDB state files on disk or use MongoDB. It pulls data from the Camunda server via REST API calls and stores it in Elasticsearch as documented.

b.text: Incorrect. Optimize is not a Zeebe partition replica and does not run an embedded column store. It calls REST APIs against the Camunda server and persists historical data in Elasticsearch.

explanation: Optimize collects historical process data by making REST API calls to the Camunda server as instances run, and stores that data in its own Elasticsearch database. This lets business users analyze reports and dashboards independently, without affecting the runtime cluster.

scenario: Catalyst Healthcare adopts Camunda 8.8 across a patient referral workflow with 30000 instances per week. The chief operating officer asks the platform team which Camunda 8 component is positioned for business stakeholders and what they can do with it, since clinical leads need bottleneck and improvement reports without using developer-oriented or operator tooling.
question: Which audience does Optimize target in Camunda 8.8 and what capability does the overview attribute to it?

options.text: Optimize targets end users completing tasks and gives them a personal dashboard listing open user tasks ranked by SLA breach probability and follow up date proximity
Optimize targets platform operators and gives them low-level cluster health metrics so they can rebalance Zeebe partitions during peak load windows on the cluster
Optimize targets business stakeholders with BI tooling so they can collaboratively share reports, analyze bottlenecks, and examine areas in business processes for improvement
Optimize targets process developers and gives them debugging tools, including breakpoints on BPMN elements and step through execution of running process tokens

b.text: Incorrect. Cluster health metrics and partition rebalancing are operator concerns addressed by other components and tooling. The overview positions Optimize for business stakeholders, not platform operators.

d.text: Incorrect. Optimize is not a developer debugger. It does not provide breakpoints on BPMN or step through execution. The overview frames it as a BI tool aimed at business stakeholders.

c.text: Correct. The overview states Optimize is geared toward business stakeholders and offers business intelligence tooling so they can collaboratively access reports, share process intelligence, and analyze bottlenecks.

a.text: Incorrect. Personal task dashboards for end users live in Tasklist. Optimize targets business stakeholders with BI reports for analyzing bottlenecks and identifying improvement areas.

explanation: The Optimize overview positions Optimize as business intelligence tooling for Camunda enterprise customers, geared toward business stakeholders. They use it to collaboratively access reports, share process intelligence, analyze bottlenecks, and examine process areas for improvement.

scenario: Zenith Telco operates 180000 instances per quarter on Camunda 8.8 across a number porting workflow. The product manager evaluating Optimize for the business team must articulate to a procurement committee what Optimize provides beyond traditional business intelligence tools, so the committee can compare it against the BI stack they already license for the analytics group.
question: What does the Optimize overview state that Optimize offers beyond traditional business intelligence tooling in Camunda 8.8?

options.text: Optimize ships canned dashboards aligned to ITIL incident management metrics so service desks can replace their existing BI vendor on the cluster on day one of rollout
Optimize embeds a low code automation layer that lets stakeholders modify BPMN diagrams from a report and redeploy them to the connected cluster from the same screen
Optimize ingests CRM and ERP telemetry alongside Camunda data, joining external rows so reports cover end to end revenue beyond what the engine itself observes per run
Optimize guides users toward continuous process improvement by understanding their goals and helping them rapidly identify constraints within an individual's or organization's system

a.text: Incorrect. The overview does not promise ITIL-aligned dashboards or vendor replacement. The documented differentiator is goal-driven continuous improvement guidance and rapid identification of constraints in the system.

b.text: Incorrect. Optimize does not edit BPMN diagrams or redeploy them. Modeling and deployment remain responsibilities of Modeler and the deployment APIs, not the Optimize BI surface.

c.text: Incorrect. Optimize collects historical Camunda data via REST and stores it in Elasticsearch. The overview does not describe joining CRM or ERP telemetry into the reports it produces.

d.text: Correct. The overview states Optimize goes beyond traditional BI tools by guiding users toward continuous process improvement, understanding their goals, and facilitating rapid identification of constraints within the system.

explanation: The Optimize overview frames the differentiator as continuous process improvement guidance: Optimize understands user goals and helps rapidly identify constraints within an individual's or organization's system, going beyond the static reporting capabilities of traditional BI tools.

scenario: A Meridian SaaS operator runs Camunda 8.8 on a tenant with 600 instances. During modification mode on one instance a new scope was just generated, and the operator wants to confirm at exactly which step Operate records an added variable as part of the pending modifications list shown for the instance.
question: At what point does Operate record an added variable on a new scope as a pending modification?

options.text: Operate records the added variable as a pending modification once the operator blurs out of the last edited field with valid Name and Value entries, not the moment of typing inside the variable form
Operate records the added variable as soon as the operator types the first character of the Name field, so the pending modifications list updates live with every keystroke that follows
Operate records the added variable only after the operator clicks Apply Modifications;  variables are buffered locally and never appear in the pending modifications view of the instance
Operate records the added variable only when the operator manually refreshes the instance page;  pending modification updates are loaded on the next page render of the instance

a.text: Correct. The docs say that once the operator blurs out of the last edited field, assuming Name and Value have valid values, the new variable is added to the pending modifications shown for the instance.

b.text: Incorrect. The pending modifications list does not update on each keystroke; the entry joins the list only after the field is blurred with valid Name and Value values.

c.text: Incorrect. Pending modifications do appear in the summary before Apply, so the variable shows up as soon as the field is blurred with a valid entry rather than only at apply time.

d.text: Incorrect. The variable is reflected in the pending modifications when the field is blurred, not on a manual page refresh of the process instance view.

explanation: For added variables on a new scope, the entry becomes a pending modification when the operator blurs out of the last edited field with valid Name and Value values. The change is then visible in the pending modifications list before being applied.

scenario: Cobalt Bank runs Camunda 8.8 Operate and is reviewing the modification-mode contract during a 90 minute compliance walk-through. The operators must understand the exact precondition that turns a typed value into a staged Edit Variable modification before the change is visible in the pending modifications list of the instance.
question: Under which precondition does Operate add an edited variable on an existing scope to the pending modifications list?

options.text: Operate adds the edit to the pending modifications once the operator blurs out of the last edited variable field and the new value is valid for the existing variable on the selected scope
Operate adds the edit to the pending modifications the instant the operator presses the Tab key, regardless of whether the typed value is valid for the type of the underlying variable
Operate adds the edit to the pending modifications only after the operator explicitly clicks a Stage Edit button;  blurring the field alone leaves the change as a local draft on the page
Operate adds the edit to the pending modifications only when the operator runs a verification step from the footer that validates the value against the BPMN data schema bound to the variable

a.text: Correct. The docs say that once the operator blurs out of the field, assuming the new value is valid, the Edit Variable modification is added to the pending modifications shown on the instance.

b.text: Incorrect. The precondition is value validity, not the Tab key alone; an invalid typed value is not staged as a pending Edit Variable modification just because focus moved off the field.

c.text: Incorrect. There is no Stage Edit button in the documented flow; the field blur plus a valid value is what records the change in the pending modifications.

d.text: Incorrect. The docs do not describe a footer-driven validation against a BPMN data schema as the precondition for staging an Edit Variable modification.

explanation: An Edit Variable modification on an existing scope is recorded in the pending modifications once the operator blurs out of the edited field and the typed value is valid. The change is then visible in the pending list before being applied.

scenario: Catalyst Bank runs Camunda 8.8 on the order-process model with 6000 orders per day across the bank's payment platform. After the operator creates an instance with orderId 1234 and completes Initiate Payment, the order must advance past the Payment Received intermediate message catch event so the XOR gateway condition can be evaluated within 5 minutes.
question: Which zbctl publish message command advances the instance past the Payment Received intermediate message catch event in this case?

options.text: The command ./bin/zbctl --insecure publish event payment-received --orderId 1234 publishes a typed event whose orderId attribute matches the instance and advances the instance past the Payment Received catch event for the bank's order-process model

The command ./bin/zbctl --insecure publish message order-received --correlationKey 1234 publishes a message whose correlation key matches orderId 1234 and advances the instance past the Payment Received catch event for the bank

The command ./bin/zbctl --insecure correlate signal payment-received --businessKey 1234 correlates a signal whose business key matches orderId 1234 and advances the instance past the Payment Received catch event in the order-process model for the bank

The command ./bin/zbctl --insecure publish message payment-received --correlationKey 1234 publishes a message whose correlation key matches orderId 1234 and advances the instance past the Payment Received catch event in the order-process model

b.text: Incorrect. The documented message name is payment-received, not order-received; the message name on the published message must match the catch event's message reference in the order-process model to correlate.

d.text: Correct. The guide shows ./bin/zbctl --insecure publish message payment-received --correlationKey 1234, which correlates the payment-received message with the instance carrying orderId 1234 and advances it past the Payment Received intermediate catch event.

a.text: Incorrect. zbctl uses publish message with a message name and a correlation key, not a publish event subcommand with arbitrary attributes; the catch event is a message catch event and correlates by correlation key.

c.text: Incorrect. The Payment Received element is a message catch event correlated through zbctl publish message and a correlation key, not a signal correlated through a businessKey parameter in Camunda 8.8.

explanation: The Advance an instance to an XOR gateway section uses ./bin/zbctl --insecure publish message payment-received --correlationKey 1234 to correlate a payment-received message with the running instance carrying orderId 1234 and advance it past the Payment Received intermediate message catch event.

scenario: Nimbus Insurance teaches new operators the concept of advancing an order-process instance through Camunda 8.8 by completing the Initiate Payment service task at the insurer, with 3 daily training cohorts and 45 minutes allocated per session for hands-on practice using community-supported zbctl on the order-process model.
question: Which concept correctly describes how the order-process instance is advanced to its XOR gateway in Camunda 8.8?

options.text: The instance is advanced by deploying a new BPMN version that auto-completes the Initiate Payment task using a built-in handler shipped with the engine, without any job worker subscribing to the task type on the broker for the insurer

The instance is advanced by starting a job worker that subscribes to the Initiate Payment job type and completes activated jobs so the token moves through the service task toward the XOR gateway on the broker for the insurer

The instance is advanced by Operate calling an internal task completion API on the Initiate Payment task using the operator's signed-in credentials, with no external worker subscribing to the job on the broker for the insurer's instance

The instance is advanced by setting the orderValue variable to 100 in Operate so the XOR gateway evaluates true and the Initiate Payment task auto-completes through the gateway condition on the broker for the insurer's instance

c.text: Incorrect. Operate does not complete service tasks through an internal API; the documented step starts a job worker via zbctl that subscribes to the Initiate Payment job type and completes activated jobs to advance the instance.

a.text: Incorrect. The engine does not auto-complete service tasks through a built-in handler shipped with a redeploy; the documented approach is to start a job worker with zbctl that handles the Initiate Payment job type.

b.text: Correct. The Advance an instance to an XOR gateway section creates a job worker with zbctl that subscribes to the initiate-payment job type and completes activated jobs, moving the token through Initiate Payment toward the XOR gateway.

d.text: Incorrect. Setting orderValue does not complete the Initiate Payment service task; the task remains active until a job worker activates and completes its job, regardless of the XOR gateway condition value on the model.

explanation: Advancing the order-process instance to its XOR gateway relies on a job worker that subscribes to the Initiate Payment job type and completes activated jobs, which is the concept the Operate guide demonstrates with zbctl create worker initiate-payment --handler cat.

scenario: Apex Banking runs Camunda 8.8 for a KYC process generating 1600 tasks per day for human handlers. Before the team rolls Tasklist out to 30 users, the lead wants to confirm how the introduction page describes the trigger that surfaces a task to a user, and which BPMN element the docs tie this trigger to.
question: How does the Tasklist introduction page describe when a user is notified in Tasklist and which BPMN element triggers it?

options.text: Tasklist users are notified in Tasklist when they are assigned a task as you model and run business processes using BPMN, where the relevant element is a user task in the process model
Tasklist users are notified directly by Modeler whenever a BPMN script task they own raises an incident, and Tasklist itself only mirrors that incident state across handlers
Tasklist notifies users when an Operate operation completes against a service task on the model, and any subsequent claim of work is then routed through the Tasklist queue for that bpmnProcessId
Tasklist users are only notified when a Console alert is forwarded by the orchestration cluster, where each alert is tied to a single BPMN message event that fires for a tenant

c.text: Incorrect. The trigger is not the completion of an Operate operation on a service task; the introduction page ties the user notification to assignment of a BPMN user task in the running process.

d.text: Incorrect. The trigger is not a Console alert forwarded by the cluster, and it is not tied to a BPMN message event; it is the assignment of a user task in a running process instance.

a.text: Correct. The introduction page states that as you model and run business processes using BPMN, users are notified in Tasklist when they are assigned a task and run the work required for that specific task.

b.text: Incorrect. The trigger is not a script-task incident raised via Modeler; the user notification is anchored to the BPMN user task element in a running business process.

explanation: Tasklist's introduction explicitly ties user notification to BPMN user tasks: as you model and run business processes using BPMN, users are notified in Tasklist when they are assigned a task. The same paragraph links to the user tasks documentation as the corresponding BPMN element type.

scenario: Forge Manufacturing routes 1800 tasks per week to Tasklist in Camunda 8.8 for quality checks on the production line. A line lead opens the Tasks page and wants to sort the queue so the items he should start working on next, according to the latest planned start time, appear at the top of the list.
question: Which Tasklist ordering option matches the line lead goal in Camunda 8?

options.text: Order the queue by follow-up date; this date defines the latest time a Tasklist user should start working on a task according to the documented ordering options
Order the queue by creation date, which lists the oldest tasks first and does not represent any planned start time for the assignee in the documented ordering options
Order the queue by priority, which defines the urgency of a task in relation to other tasks waiting in the same Tasklist queue for assignment to a user
Order the queue by due date, which provides the deadline for when the task should be finished according to the documented ordering options available in Tasklist

a.text: Correct. The Tasklist ordering page states the follow-up date defines the latest time a user should start working on a task, which matches the line lead goal exactly.

d.text: Incorrect. Due date represents the deadline for when the task should be finished, not the latest planned start time, so it does not match the documented start-time signal.

c.text: Incorrect. Priority orders tasks by relative urgency among each other, but it does not encode a planned start date; two equally urgent tasks may have different follow-up dates.

b.text: Incorrect. Creation date orders the queue by task age, which has no relation to a planned start time and is not the documented ordering option for when work should begin.

explanation: Tasklist can order the queue by creation date, due date, follow-up date, or priority. The follow-up date specifically defines the latest time a user should start working on a task.

scenario: Helix Logistics runs Camunda 8.8 SaaS and processes 12000 shipments per day. An on-call operator opens Operate, clicks a deployed process name from the Process Instances by Name panel, and needs to cancel one specific stuck running instance directly from that process view without leaving the page.
question: Which action on the Processes tab cancels that single running process instance for the operator?

options.text: Clicking the version selector on the Processes tab cancels every running instance bound to the older versions of that deployed process model
Clicking a bulk cancel button at the top of the Processes tab cancels every running instance of the selected deployed process in one Operate action
Clicking the cancel icon under Operations next to the instance row on the Processes tab cancels that single running process instance
Clicking the deployed process name in the Process Instances by Name panel a second time cancels the most recently started running instance

b.text: Incorrect. The source describes cancelling a single running process instance from the Processes tab by clicking a per-row cancel icon under Operations, not by using a bulk cancel control that acts on every running instance of the deployed process.

a.text: Incorrect. The version selector filters the Processes view by process definition version; it does not cancel any running instance of older versions of the deployed process model.

c.text: Correct. The source states that from the Processes tab an operator can cancel a single running process instance by clicking the cancel icon under Operations next to the process instance row.

d.text: Incorrect. Clicking the deployed process name in the Process Instances by Name panel navigates to the process view that lists running instances; it does not cancel any running process instance.

explanation: On the Processes tab opened from the Process Instances by Name panel, each running process instance row exposes an Operations column with a cancel icon. Clicking that icon cancels that one running instance for the operator without affecting other running instances of the same deployed process definition.

scenario: Stellar Logistics is onboarding 12 new operators to Camunda 8.8 Operate for a fleet that processes 9000 shipments per day. Before the operators can practice basic Operate navigation on the sample order-process model, the team lead wants to confirm the documented prerequisites that the userguide states up front.
question: Which prerequisites does the basic Operate navigation section assume have already been completed?

options.text: A BPMN process has been deployed to a Camunda 7 engine and at least 1 process instance has been created on that engine before any Operate navigation is possible from the dashboard
A DMN decision has been deployed to Zeebe and at least 1 evaluated decision instance exists, so Operate can render the dashboard cards needed for navigation
A Web Modeler project has been created and shared with at least 5 collaborators, so Operate can show the deployed processes by name and version on the dashboard
A BPMN process has been deployed to Zeebe and at least 1 process instance has been created on the same cluster, so the operator has something to inspect in Operate

d.text: Correct. The basic-operate-navigation page opens with a note stating that the section and the following one assume you have deployed a process to Zeebe and created at least one process instance; otherwise the dashboard panels and instance views have nothing to display.

a.text: Incorrect. Camunda 8 Operate reads state from the Zeebe broker on the cluster; the prerequisite cited by the docs is a deployment to Zeebe, not to a Camunda 7 engine.

b.text: Incorrect. The prerequisite called out in the note is a deployed process and a running process instance; DMN decisions and decision instances are not what this section assumes.

c.text: Incorrect. Web Modeler collaborators are not mentioned as a prerequisite for Operate navigation; the requirement stated in the note is a deployed process plus at least one running instance.

explanation: The introduction to basic Operate navigation explicitly tells readers it assumes they have deployed a process to Zeebe and created at least one process instance, and points unfamiliar readers to the guides section. The same order-process.bpmn model is then reused throughout the subsequent navigation steps.

scenario: Aurora Insurance runs Camunda 8.8 Operate for a claims platform handling 4000 claims per day. An operator opens Operate, sees the dashboard, and wants to drill from the deployed processes overview into a specific deployed process and then cancel exactly 1 misbehaving running instance directly from that view.
question: Which sequence on the Operate dashboard matches the documented steps for viewing a deployed process and cancelling a single running instance?

options.text: Open the Incidents by Error panel, click the error type to see all related instances, then use the cancel control that appears at the top of the resulting filtered list
Open the Decisions by Name panel on the dashboard, click the related decision, then use the cancel icon next to the linked process instance shown beside the decision
Use the Process Instances by Name panel on the dashboard, click the process name to open its model with running instances, then click the cancel icon under Operations on the Processes tab
Open the Operations panel, filter by the bpmnProcessId, then click the cancel icon next to the instance row from inside the Operations side panel view

d.text: Incorrect. The View a deployed process flow starts from the Process Instances by Name panel on the dashboard, not from the Operations panel; the cancel icon lives next to the instance under Operations on the Processes tab.

c.text: Correct. The userguide instructs operators to use Process Instances by Name on the dashboard, click the deployed process name to open the model and running instances, then click the cancel icon under Operations next to the instance on the Processes tab.

a.text: Incorrect. The Incidents by Error panel is a separate dashboard entry point; the documented View a deployed process flow goes through Process Instances by Name and uses the per-instance cancel icon under Operations.

b.text: Incorrect. The Decisions by Name panel is on the dashboard but is not the entry point used in View a deployed process; the documented path uses Process Instances by Name and the per-instance cancel icon.

explanation: The View a deployed process section walks the user through three steps: locate the process in the Process Instances by Name panel on the dashboard, click its name to open the model and the list of running instances, and finally use the cancel icon under Operations next to a single running instance on the Processes tab.

scenario: Tessera Insurance runs Camunda 8.8 SaaS for 7500 policies per day. A platform operator opened the Processes page in Operate, clicked Delete on a finished claim instance, but skipped the confirmation dialog by mistake. The team wants to know what the documented step-by-step flow guarantees about confirmation and visibility before the deletion is considered complete.
question: What does the Processes-page delete flow require between clicking Delete and the deletion being trackable by the operator?

options.text: A confirmation dialog must be acknowledged by clicking Delete a second time, after which the deleted instance is visible in the Operations panel on the right side of the screen
An additional Tasklist approval task must be claimed and completed by a separate user before the cluster persists the deletion of the finished process instance
A 24 hours grace period must elapse in the cluster retention queue, after which the operator manually re-clicks Delete from the Operations panel to commit the irreversible removal
A Console alert must be acknowledged by an organization administrator within 10 minutes before the operation is forwarded from Operate to the cluster for execution

c.text: Incorrect. There is no documented 24 hours grace period or re-click step in the Processes-page delete flow; the userguide simply requires confirmation in the dialog and then tracks the operation in the Operations panel.

b.text: Incorrect. The delete flow does not route through Tasklist; the operator confirms the deletion themselves in the Operate dialog and views the result in the Operations panel.

a.text: Correct. The userguide describes step 3 as confirming the delete operation by clicking Delete in the dialog, and step 4 as viewing the deleted process instance in the Operations panel on the right side of the screen, giving a clear confirm-then-track pattern.

d.text: Incorrect. There is no Console alert acknowledgement step in the documented Processes-page delete flow; the confirmation is local to Operate and the result is tracked in the Operations panel.

explanation: The Processes-page delete flow inserts a confirmation dialog between the initial Delete click and the persisted deletion: after the operator clicks Delete on a finished instance row, they must click Delete again in the dialog to confirm, and the resulting deleted instance then appears in the Operations panel on the right side of the screen for tracking.

scenario: Granite Logistics runs Camunda 8.8 with 9000 shipments per day. A new junior operator opens the Operate Processes page and only sees a mixed list of running and finished instances. Before any Delete button appears for finished work, the operator needs to know the very first action the userguide tells them to take on this page.
question: Which first action does the Processes-page delete flow tell the operator to perform before clicking Delete on a finished process instance?

options.text: Apply the Finished Instances filter by checking the appropriate box on the Processes page, so that finished instances are listed and a Delete button is reachable per row
Apply a Tenants filter using the tenant ID column on the Processes page, so that the Delete button becomes available for finished instances of that tenant only
Apply the Incidents filter and then expand each incident row on the Processes page, so that the Delete control appears next to the underlying finished instance
Apply a bpmnProcessId filter and a 90 days lookback range on the Processes page, so that the Operations panel exposes a bulk Delete control over the result set

a.text: Correct. Step 1 of the Processes-page delete flow tells the operator to apply the Finished Instances filter by checking the appropriate box on the Processes page; only then does the documented per-row Delete button become the next step.

d.text: Incorrect. The first documented action is the Finished Instances filter checkbox; the flow does not mention a 90 days lookback range or a bulk Delete control in the Operations panel.

c.text: Incorrect. The documented entry point is the Finished Instances filter, not the Incidents filter; expanding incident rows is part of monitoring operation status, not the delete-finished-instances flow.

b.text: Incorrect. The userguide does not gate the Delete button behind a Tenants filter; step 1 is the Finished Instances checkbox on the Processes page.

explanation: The very first step in Delete process instance from Processes page is to apply the Finished Instances filter on the Processes page by checking its checkbox. Without that filter the Delete control documented for the subsequent steps is not the focus of the flow, so the userguide leads with this filter action.

scenario: Vanta Insurance evaluates Operate in Camunda 8.8 for a claims platform handling 5500 claims per day. The architecture team must explain to auditors what the instance-detail delete flow guarantees once the confirmation dialog is accepted, and whether the deleted instance remains accessible to the operator for any further review.
question: What does the documented note on the instance-detail delete flow state about post-deletion access to the process instance?

options.text: The deleted process instance moves to an archived tab on the instance detail page where it stays accessible for 30 days before final purge from the cluster
The userguide warns to use caution because the process instance is now deleted and the operator may not access it again from Operate after the confirmation dialog is accepted
The deleted process instance is exported automatically as a JSON record and attached to the Operations panel so that auditors can still open its variables and history later
The deleted process instance remains visible in Operate but is marked with a strikethrough icon on the instance detail page until a cleanup job removes it overnight

a.text: Incorrect. There is no 30 days archived tab in the documented instance-detail delete flow; the note tells the operator they may not access the instance again after deletion.

c.text: Incorrect. The userguide does not describe an automatic JSON export to the Operations panel; the note simply warns that the instance is gone after confirmation.

d.text: Incorrect. The instance is not kept visible with a strikethrough; the note explicitly warns that the operator may not access the deleted process instance again.

b.text: Correct. The Delete process instance from instance detail page section includes a note that tells the operator to use caution because the process instance is now deleted and they may not access it again, after the confirmation dialog is accepted.

explanation: After confirming deletion from the instance detail page, the userguide displays a cautionary note: the process instance is now deleted, and the operator may not access it again. The deleted notification is shown to make this irreversible step explicit before any further navigation in Operate.

scenario: Quanta Payments operates Camunda 8.8 for a wallet platform with 22000 transactions per day. A support engineer opens the Processes page and wants to use the instance-detail delete flow instead of the Processes-page row delete. The team needs to recall which exact control on the instance row routes them to that detail page in the documented sequence.
question: Which exact click on the Processes page does the instance-detail delete flow specify to navigate from a finished instance to its detail page?

options.text: Click the row count number in the Finished Instances header card, which navigates to a list page that aggregates instance detail for the filter set
Click the Process Instance Key of the finished process instance, which navigates the operator to the instance detail page where the Delete button is shown
Click the bpmnProcessId link in the breadcrumb above the Processes page, which loads the process definition page where instance detail can be opened
Click the Edit Variables icon on the instance row, which auto-loads the variables tab of the instance detail page in a second browser tab

d.text: Incorrect. The instance-detail flow does not navigate via an Edit Variables icon; the userguide tells the operator to click the Process Instance Key on the finished instance to open instance detail.

b.text: Correct. After applying the Finished Instances filter, step 2 of the instance-detail flow tells the operator to navigate to the instance detail page by clicking the Process Instance Key of the process instance they want to delete.

a.text: Incorrect. The documented navigation is per-instance via the Process Instance Key, not via an aggregate header card count or a list page jump.

c.text: Incorrect. There is no documented breadcrumb route via bpmnProcessId; the instance-detail flow opens the detail page through the Process Instance Key on the finished instance row.

explanation: In the Delete process instance from instance detail page flow, after applying the Finished Instances filter on the Processes page, the operator clicks the Process Instance Key of the chosen finished process instance to open its instance detail page, where the Delete button and the confirmation dialog then complete the deletion.

scenario: Crescent Insurer runs Camunda 8.8 with 5 clusters and routes 4500 claims per day through a Decision Requirements Diagram that contains several decision tables and literal expressions. An operator navigates to the Decisions page in Operate and prepares to delete one decision definition that belongs to that DRD.
question: Which resource scope gets removed when an operator deletes that decision definition from the Decisions page?

options.text: Only the selected decision definition is removed; every other decision table or literal expression in the same DRD remains deployed
Only the selected decision definition and the literal expressions inside it are removed; other decision tables in the DRD remain
The whole DRD that contains the selected decision definition is removed, including every other decision table and literal expression in it
Only the version of the selected decision definition is removed; the DRD itself remains and other decision tables stay deployed

a.text: Incorrect. The docs state that deleting a decision definition deletes the DRD, which also takes down every other decision table and literal expression that is part of the same DRD.

d.text: Incorrect. The delete operation does not stop at the selected version; the docs say it removes the whole DRD that contains the decision definition the operator picked.

b.text: Incorrect. The deletion is not scoped to literal expressions only; the docs state that every other decision table and literal expression in the same DRD is also removed.

c.text: Correct. The Decisions page docs say deleting a decision definition deletes the DRD that contains it, so every other decision table and literal expression that is part of the DRD is removed at the same time.

explanation: Deleting a decision definition on the Decisions page deletes the entire DRD that contains it; every other decision table and literal expression that is part of that DRD is removed alongside it.

scenario: Halcyon Bank deploys a single version of a pricing decision in Camunda 8.8 that drives 18000 loans per day across 24 microservices. An architect proposes deleting that only version from the Decisions page in Operate so the team can replace it with a new model.
question: What risk does the documentation specifically associate with deleting the only existing version of that decision definition?

options.text: The deletion can result in process incidents on running process instances that reference the now-missing decision version
The deletion can result in the cluster broker rejecting every new deployment until a replacement decision is uploaded
The deletion can result in immediate cancellation of every running process instance that has ever called the deleted decision
The deletion can result in a rollback of the deleted decision version once dependent processes raise a failure event

a.text: Correct. The Decisions page docs warn that deleting the only existing version of a decision definition could result in process incidents on process instances that still reference the now-missing version of the decision.

c.text: Incorrect. The docs do not state that running process instances are cancelled outright by the delete; the documented consequence is process incidents on those instances, not blanket cancellation.

d.text: Incorrect. There is no automatic rollback of the delete operation; the docs only mention the risk of process incidents on instances that reference the removed decision version.

b.text: Incorrect. The docs do not say the cluster broker blocks future deployments; the documented impact is process incidents on instances that still reference the removed decision version.

explanation: When operators delete the only existing version of a decision definition, processes that still reference it can raise incidents at runtime; the Decisions page docs flag this as a specific documented risk.

scenario: Lattice Logistics runs Camunda 8.8 with 4 clusters and routes 7000 shipments per day through a DRD that bundles several decision tables and literal expressions. An operator opens the Decisions page in Operate, filters by name and version, and deletes one decision definition from that DRD.
question: Which other resources inside the same DRD does this single deletion remove?

options.text: Every other decision table inside the DRD is moved to a new DRD, while only literal expressions of the deleted definition are removed
Every other decision table inside the DRD is kept, but the literal expressions belonging to the deleted definition are removed
Every other decision table and literal expression inside the DRD is kept and re-linked to the most recent surviving decision definition
Every other decision table and literal expression that is part of the same DRD is removed alongside the selected decision definition

b.text: Incorrect. The Decisions page docs say every other decision table in the same DRD is also deleted; scoping the removal to only literal expressions of the picked definition does not match the documented impact.

d.text: Correct. The Decisions page docs state that deleting a decision definition deletes the DRD, removing all other decision tables and literal expressions that are part of the same DRD alongside the selected definition.

a.text: Incorrect. The delete operation does not move resources to a new DRD; the entire DRD that contained the picked definition is removed in one step, including its other decision tables and literal expressions.

c.text: Incorrect. Surviving resources are not re-linked to another definition; the docs say the whole DRD goes away, so the other decision tables and literal expressions are removed with it.

explanation: On the Decisions page, deleting a decision definition removes the DRD it belongs to, taking every other decision table and literal expression that is part of that DRD with it in the same operation.

scenario: Atlas Insurance runs Camunda 8.8 with 4500 policies per day. A platform engineer is asked to delete an old onboarding process definition version from Operate, but the previous release still has 12 running instances on that exact version on the cluster. The team wants to know what the userguide says about deleting a process definition that still has running instances.
question: What does the Processes-page delete-resources flow specify when the selected process definition version still has running instances?

options.text: The selected process definition version is deleted, and any running instances on it are paused for 30 minutes while a background migration job moves them to the next deployed version on the cluster

The selected process definition version must have no running instances before deletion; otherwise the userguide says it is not possible to delete the definition, and the user must cancel or resolve those instances first
The selected process definition version is allowed to have up to 50 running instances, which Operate moves to the latest deployed version automatically when the Delete button is clicked
The selected process definition version is deleted in place and any running instances on it are converted to incidents in Operate so an operator can resolve them after deletion completes

b.text: Correct. The note on Delete process definition from Processes page states that the selected process definition version must have no running instances; otherwise it is not possible to delete the definition. The note points the operator to cancel or resolve running instances first using basic Operate navigation.

c.text: Incorrect. There is no documented threshold like 50 running instances, and Operate does not auto-migrate instances to the latest version when Delete is clicked; the deletion is blocked while running instances exist.

d.text: Incorrect. Running instances are not converted to incidents on deletion; the userguide simply blocks deletion until the operator cancels or resolves the running instances themselves.

a.text: Incorrect. There is no 30 minutes pause-and-migrate behaviour described in the docs; deletion of the process definition version is gated on the version having no running instances.

explanation: Delete process definition from Processes page wraps the version filter and Delete button in a guardrail note: the chosen process definition version must have no running instances. Otherwise deletion is blocked, and the operator must first cancel or resolve those running instances via basic Operate navigation.

scenario: Nimbus Healthcare runs Camunda 8.8 for an admissions platform handling 1200 records per day. A compliance officer asks the operator to permanently retire 1 deprecated process definition version that is no longer referenced. The officer wants a precise list of what will be removed when the operator confirms the Processes-page delete dialog.
question: Which set of artefacts does the userguide say will be impacted when a process definition is deleted from the Processes page?

options.text: Only the deployment record is removed and the underlying instances remain visible under an archived tab in Operate, with Tasklist tasks demoted to claimed-only state pending administrator review

Only the BPMN XML for the chosen version is removed from Operate, while finished instance history, related decision instances and any user tasks in Tasklist remain available for at least 90 days for audit

All finished process instances of the deleted definition are removed, related decision and process instances referenced by them are removed, and any user tasks in Tasklist from this definition are deleted
All running process instances are first migrated to the next available version, finished instances are kept as read-only, and Tasklist user tasks are reassigned to a default queue after deletion

b.text: Incorrect. The note does not preserve finished history or Tasklist tasks for 90 days; finished instances of the deleted definition are removed along with related decision and process instances and any user tasks in Tasklist.

c.text: Correct. The userguide note states that deleting a process definition removes the definition's finished process instances, all decision and process instances referenced by the deleted process instances, and any user tasks that belong to the definition from Tasklist.

d.text: Incorrect. The delete flow is blocked while running instances exist; there is no automatic migration to a next version, and finished instances are removed rather than kept read-only.

a.text: Incorrect. The userguide describes a permanent removal of finished instances and related artefacts, not an archived tab or a demoted Tasklist state.

explanation: The Processes-page delete note lists three permanent impacts of deleting a process definition: the definition's finished process instances are removed, decision and process instances referenced by those deleted process instances are also removed, and any user tasks in Tasklist that belong to the definition are deleted.

scenario: Echo Banking runs Camunda 8.8 for an internal back-office platform with 2000 transactions per day. A new operator preparing onboarding notes wants a single-sentence summary of which page in Operate is used to start the documented Delete process definition flow and which kind of filter is applied on that page first.
question: Which page in Operate and which filter combination does the Delete process definition flow use as its starting point?

options.text: The Processes page, filtered by process name and version, where the Delete button at the top right then opens the confirmation dialog for the selected process definition version
The Dashboard page, filtered by Operations type and time range, where the Delete control then appears in the Operations panel for any deployment created by the current user
The Decisions page, filtered by decision name and DMN version, where the Delete control then appears at the top right of the filtered detail view of the chosen definition
The Console cluster detail page, filtered by deployment id, where the Delete control routes the request through the Administration API to remove the process definition from the cluster

c.text: Incorrect. The Decisions page and DMN version filter belong to the decision-definition delete flow, not the process-definition delete flow described in Delete process definition from Processes page.

b.text: Incorrect. The Dashboard page is not the starting point; the userguide opens this flow on the Processes page with a process name and version filter.

a.text: Correct. Step 1 of the Processes-page flow tells the operator to open the Processes page and select a specific process version by filtering by process name and version; the Delete button at the top right then opens the confirmation dialog.

d.text: Incorrect. The delete flow is described inside Operate on the Processes page, not via a Console cluster detail page or the Administration API as the starting point.

explanation: Delete process definition from Processes page starts on the Operate Processes page, where the operator filters by process name and version to select the specific definition version. The Delete button at the top right then opens the confirmation dialog before the deletion is executed.

scenario: Beacon Logistics runs Camunda 8.8 for a routing platform with 4000 shipments per day. A team lead wants to retire an unused process definition version from Operate and asks the operator how progress of the delete is tracked once the confirmation dialog has been accepted on the Processes page.
question: Where does the Processes-page delete-resources flow tell the operator the progress of the delete operation can be seen?

options.text: In a Tasklist row created for the operator after confirmation, where the delete operation is completed by claiming and completing the task within 1 hour
In an audit log section on the Console cluster detail page, where every delete operation is recorded by deployment id with a separate tab for the operator role
In a toast notification at the top of the Operate dashboard that automatically disappears after 30 seconds once the delete operation reaches the cluster broker
In the Operations panel on the right side of the screen in Operate, where the progress of the delete operation appears as a tracked entry after the confirmation dialog

c.text: Incorrect. The userguide does not rely on a 30 seconds toast notification on the dashboard; the documented progress view is the Operations panel on the right side of the screen in Operate.

b.text: Incorrect. Console is not the documented location for the progress of this Operate delete operation; the flow keeps the operator inside Operate and tracks progress in the Operations panel.

a.text: Incorrect. The delete flow is performed inside Operate and is not routed through Tasklist; the operator watches progress in the Operations panel on the right side of the screen.

d.text: Correct. Step 4 of Delete process definition from Processes page states that the progress of the delete operation can be seen in the Operations panel on the right side of the screen in Operate, after the operator confirms the dialog.

explanation: After confirming the delete on the Processes page, the operator tracks progress in the Operations panel on the right side of the Operate screen. That panel records the delete operation and shows its progression, providing a single place in Operate to observe the lifecycle of the delete request.

scenario: Catalyst Banking runs Camunda 8.8 for a loan platform handling 3500 loans per day. A risk operator wants to delete an obsolete decision definition version from Operate, but a colleague warns that the deletion will affect more than just the chosen decision. The team wants to know which DRD-level impacts the userguide documents for this action.
question: Which set of impacts does the Decisions-page delete-resources flow attribute to deleting a decision definition?

options.text: The DRD is deleted only if it has been deployed less than 30 days ago, after which a new evaluation against the same bpmnProcessId silently uses the previous version of the decision instead
Only the metadata header of the chosen decision is removed, while the underlying DMN XML is kept on the cluster and the DRD continues to evaluate from the previous version of the definition
Only the chosen decision table is removed from the DRD, while literal expressions inside the same DRD remain available so existing process incidents on the cluster can still resolve against them

The DRD that contains the chosen decision is deleted, including all decision tables and literal expressions in that DRD, and deleting the only existing version of a decision definition could result in process incidents

c.text: Incorrect. The userguide states that deleting a decision definition removes the whole DRD that contains it, including all other decision tables and literal expressions in that DRD, not only the chosen decision.

b.text: Incorrect. There is no documented metadata-only deletion that keeps DMN XML on the cluster; the note states the DRD is deleted entirely along with its tables and literal expressions.

d.text: Correct. The note on Delete decision definition from Decisions Page states that deleting a decision definition deletes the DRD, including all decision tables and literal expressions that are part of the DRD, and warns that deleting the only existing version of a decision definition could result in process incidents.

a.text: Incorrect. The docs do not gate deletion on a 30 days deployment age, and they do not describe a silent fallback to the previous version of the decision after deletion.

explanation: Delete decision definition from Decisions Page warns that deleting a decision definition deletes the DRD that contains it, removing all other decision tables and literal expressions in that DRD. The note also flags that deleting the only existing version of a decision definition could result in process incidents in Operate.

scenario: Sable Insurance runs Camunda 8.8 with 1800 policies per day, and an operator needs a quick reference card for the very first step of the Decisions-page delete-resources flow. The card should describe the page, the filter dimensions and the position of the Delete control with no extra steps.
question: Which combination matches the first two documented steps of Delete decision definition from Decisions Page?

options.text: Open the Decisions page and filter by decision name and version, then click the Delete button at the top right to open the confirmation dialog for the selected version
Open the Processes page and filter by the bpmnProcessId that uses the decision, then click the Delete control next to the linked decision row in the right-hand panel
Open the Operations panel and select a Decisions filter, then click the Delete link inside the panel header to remove the selected decision definition from the cluster index
Open the Console DRD viewer and filter by DRD id, then click the Delete control in the Console toolbar to schedule a cluster-wide removal of the chosen decision definition

c.text: Incorrect. The Operations panel tracks operations; the first two documented steps are on the Decisions page with a name plus version filter and a Delete button at the top right.

a.text: Correct. Step 1 of Delete decision definition from Decisions Page is to select a specific decision version on the Decisions page by filtering by decision name and version, and step 2 is to click the Delete button at the top right which opens the confirmation dialog.

b.text: Incorrect. The decision delete flow starts on the Decisions page, not on the Processes page using a bpmnProcessId filter for a linked decision.

d.text: Incorrect. The flow lives inside Operate on the Decisions page, not in a Console DRD viewer or toolbar.

explanation: Delete decision definition from Decisions Page opens on the Decisions page in Operate. The operator filters by decision name and version to select the specific version, then clicks the Delete button at the top right, which opens the confirmation dialog before the actual deletion of the DRD-bound definition is executed.

scenario: Vertex Banking runs Camunda 8.8 with 7000 loans per day across 4 DRDs. Before approving a runbook for retiring old decision definitions, a senior operator wants a precise statement of the relationship between deleting a single decision definition and the wider DRD it belongs to in Operate.
question: Which statement correctly describes the DRD-level effect of deleting a decision definition from the Decisions page?

options.text: Deleting a single decision definition keeps the surrounding DRD intact and simply removes that one decision table, while every other decision table and literal expression in the DRD remains available for evaluation

Deleting a single decision definition triggers an automatic version bump for the surrounding DRD on the cluster, and the previous DRD continues to be evaluated for 30 days as a fallback for ongoing process instances
Deleting a single decision definition deletes the DRD that contains it, so all other decision tables and literal expressions inside that DRD are also deleted along with the chosen decision
Deleting a single decision definition has no impact on the DRD; the DRD is owned by Modeler and the operator must redeploy a modified DRD from Modeler to remove a decision table

a.text: Incorrect. The userguide note explicitly states the deletion removes the DRD that contains the chosen decision, so other decision tables and literal expressions in that DRD are removed as well.

b.text: Incorrect. The docs do not describe an automatic DRD version bump or a 30 days fallback evaluation; the DRD itself is removed.

d.text: Incorrect. The delete action in Operate is not a no-op against the DRD; it removes the DRD that contains the chosen decision definition.

c.text: Correct. The note on Delete decision definition from Decisions Page states that deleting a decision definition deletes the DRD which contains it, so all other decision tables and literal expressions that are part of that DRD are also deleted.

explanation: Operate treats deletion of a decision definition as deletion of its containing DRD. The note records that all decision tables and literal expressions that are part of the DRD are deleted alongside the chosen decision, which is a wider blast radius than just the targeted decision table.

scenario: Forge Insurance runs Camunda 8.8 for an underwriting platform with 2200 policies per day. A senior operator is preparing a checklist for retiring old decision definitions and wants to recall the exact risk that the userguide highlights when deleting the only existing version of a decision definition from the Decisions page.
question: Which risk does the Delete decision definition from Decisions Page note specifically associate with deleting the only existing version of a decision definition?

options.text: Deleting the only existing version of a decision definition forces a 24 hours read-only window on the surrounding DRD before any other operation can be performed in Operate
Deleting the only existing version of a decision definition could result in process incidents on running process instances that still reference that decision in their model
Deleting the only existing version of a decision definition silently switches all dependent process instances to a hard-coded default outcome on the cluster, with no incident raised to the operator
Deleting the only existing version of a decision definition requires the operator to deploy a replacement DRD within 10 minutes from Modeler, otherwise Operate auto-restores the deleted version

b.text: Correct. The userguide note states that deleting the only existing version of a decision definition could result in process incidents, because dependent process instances no longer find a decision to evaluate against on the cluster.

c.text: Incorrect. The docs do not describe a silent default outcome; the documented risk is that the deletion could result in process incidents.

a.text: Incorrect. There is no documented 24 hours read-only window after deletion; the docs flag the risk of process incidents instead.

d.text: Incorrect. The userguide does not describe a 10 minutes replacement window or an auto-restore behaviour; the call-out is about the risk of process incidents.

explanation: Inside the same note that explains DRD-level impact, the userguide highlights a second risk: deleting the only existing version of a decision definition could result in process incidents. This makes it explicit that operators should keep at least one usable version of a decision definition available on the cluster.

scenario: Meridian Logistics is rolling out Camunda 8.8 SaaS for a routing platform with 3 clusters. The platform engineer needs to expose the Zeebe, Operate, Tasklist and orchestration cluster REST API to 6 internal microservices, and wants to know which Console capability is documented for managing the credentials those microservices will use.
question: Which Camunda Console capability does the introduction page list for issuing credentials that microservices use to call an orchestration cluster?

options.text: Managing API clients for the orchestration cluster, which the introduction page lists as a way to interact with Zeebe, Operate, Tasklist and the orchestration cluster REST API from external callers
Configuring Identity tenant mappings against external IdPs on the orchestration cluster, where the engineer maps groups to roles before any microservice can authenticate to Zeebe at all
Editing the Zeebe broker configuration YAML in Console, where the engineer adds per-microservice basic-auth users before the cluster accepts any gRPC or REST traffic from them
Subscribing to operation events in Optimize, where the engineer issues short-lived event tokens that each microservice uses to authenticate calls to the orchestration cluster

b.text: Incorrect. The introduction page lists Manage API clients as the relevant Console capability for orchestration cluster credentials; Identity tenant mappings are not the action highlighted in this section.

a.text: Correct. The introduction lists Manage API clients as a way to interact with an orchestration cluster and its components, including Zeebe, Operate, Tasklist and the REST API, making it the documented Console capability for these microservice credentials.

c.text: Incorrect. The introduction does not describe editing Zeebe broker YAML from Console as the mechanism for microservice credentials; it points to Manage API clients on the orchestration cluster.

d.text: Incorrect. Optimize event subscriptions are not the documented credential mechanism; the introduction names Manage API clients as the capability for orchestration cluster access.

explanation: The Camunda Console introduction page explicitly lists Manage API clients as a capability for interacting with an orchestration cluster and its components, including Zeebe, Operate, Tasklist and the orchestration cluster REST API. That single bullet is the documented mechanism for issuing the credentials a microservice would use.

scenario: Atlas Bank runs Camunda 8.8 SaaS with 12 clusters spread across 4 teams of 40 developers. The platform group wants its Terraform pipeline to create and delete clusters programmatically rather than have a human click around Console every release, and also needs to manage API clients and IP allowlists for each cluster from the same automation surface.
question: Which Console capability lets the bank manage cluster lifecycle programmatically from a Terraform pipeline?

options.text: The Administration API REST clients in Console let the bank create and delete clusters programmatically from a Terraform pipeline
The IP allowlist clients in Console let the bank create and delete clusters programmatically from a Terraform pipeline
The Orchestration Cluster API clients in Console let the bank create and delete clusters programmatically from a Terraform pipeline
The alert webhook clients in Console let the bank create and delete clusters programmatically from a Terraform pipeline

a.text: Correct. Per docs, Console exposes Administration API clients (REST) that operators use to manage clusters programmatically, which fits a Terraform-driven create-and-delete-cluster workflow for the bank.

c.text: Incorrect. Orchestration Cluster API clients interact with Zeebe, Operate, Tasklist, and the REST API of a running cluster; they are not the surface for creating or deleting clusters themselves.

b.text: Incorrect. IP allowlists restrict access to an existing cluster; they do not create or delete clusters and would not be used by a Terraform pipeline for cluster lifecycle automation.

d.text: Incorrect. Alerts in Console notify when workflow errors occur; they neither create nor delete clusters and have no role in driving cluster lifecycle from Terraform.

explanation: Console exposes Administration API clients (REST) that let operators create and delete clusters and perform other lifecycle tasks programmatically, which is what the bank needs to drive cluster lifecycle from a Terraform pipeline.

scenario: Wren Healthcare runs Camunda 8.8 for an intake platform handling 900 records per day. A new operator opens Tasklist for the first time and asks what role Tasklist plays in the Camunda 8 platform compared to Operate, before any further configuration of user tasks is attempted on the cluster.
question: Which description matches the Tasklist introduction page's framing of what Tasklist is and what it does?

options.text: Tasklist is a ready-to-use application to rapidly implement business processes alongside user tasks, used to orchestrate human workflows and reduce time-to-value with an out-of-the-box interface
Tasklist is a monitoring tool used to inspect Zeebe broker partitions and replication lag, designed to complement Operate when investigating performance issues across clusters
Tasklist is a deployment dashboard used to push BPMN and DMN files to the cluster, designed to replace Web Modeler deploy actions for organizations that do not run Web Modeler at all
Tasklist is a script editor used by operators to author DMN tables, where each evaluated decision is then routed through a built-in queue to the appropriate human handler in the organization

a.text: Correct. The Tasklist introduction defines Tasklist as a ready-to-use application to rapidly implement business processes alongside user tasks, used to orchestrate human workflows and reduce time-to-value with an out-of-the-box interface.

d.text: Incorrect. The Tasklist introduction does not describe Tasklist as a DMN script editor; it presents Tasklist as a ready-to-use application focused on user tasks and human workflows.

c.text: Incorrect. Tasklist is not framed as a BPMN or DMN deployment dashboard in the introduction page; that responsibility sits with Modeler and the deployment surfaces around Zeebe.

b.text: Incorrect. Tasklist is not a broker monitoring tool; the introduction page frames it as a ready-to-use application alongside user tasks for human workflows.

explanation: The Tasklist introduction opens with a precise framing: Tasklist is a ready-to-use application to rapidly implement business processes alongside user tasks. It is positioned as a way to orchestrate human workflows, reduce time-to-value, and notify users when they are assigned a task to complete.

scenario: Lattice Insurance processes 8000 claims per day on Camunda 8.8 SaaS. The insurer wants a branded customer-facing claim intake screen that matches the rest of its portal instead of the stock Tasklist interface, while keeping BPMN user tasks as the underlying work assignment model and Tasklist still available internally for 200 adjusters.
question: Which path lets the insurer build a custom UI while keeping BPMN user tasks as the work source?

options.text: Embed the Tasklist UI inside an iframe and override the CSS with custom branding for the insurer customer-facing claim app
Replace the BPMN user task element with a Camunda 7 human task and connect the custom UI to the legacy REST engine
Generate a SwaggerUI client off the Tasklist GraphQL schema and treat it as the production customer-facing UI for claims
Build a fully custom application against the Orchestration Cluster API and consume BPMN user tasks from it as the work source

a.text: Incorrect. The docs do not present iframe-embedding the stock Tasklist UI as the documented extension path; they point users to a fully custom application built against the Orchestration Cluster API.
