d.text: Correct. Per docs, users are not limited to the Tasklist UI and can build a fully custom application using the Orchestration Cluster API while BPMN user tasks continue to drive the work assignment.

b.text: Incorrect. Camunda 8 user tasks are BPMN elements served by Zeebe; Camunda 7 human tasks belong to a separate legacy engine and are outside the Camunda 8 Tasklist product.

c.text: Incorrect. The Tasklist documentation directs custom UIs to the Orchestration Cluster API rather than to a GraphQL schema treated as the production customer-facing endpoint.

explanation: Tasklist ships a ready-to-use UI but the docs explicitly state users are not limited to it; teams can extend their case or build a fully custom application against the Orchestration Cluster API while BPMN user tasks continue to drive the assignment of work.

scenario: Ridge Insurance runs Camunda 8.8 with 3500 claims per day routed through BPMN user tasks. The team has 4 internal claim handlers using the Tasklist user interface and 2 partner-facing portals that want fully custom task UIs. They need to know which surface the introduction page documents for those custom applications.
question: Which extension surface does the Tasklist introduction page document for teams that want fully custom task applications instead of the built-in Tasklist UI?

options.text: Teams use a Webhook subscription from Console to forward every user task event to an external system, where the external system renders the entire custom task user interface to end users
Teams use a Tasklist-specific GraphQL endpoint inside Operate to build any fully custom task application, while the built-in Tasklist user interface remains the only sanctioned end-user front end
Teams use a Modeler plugin SDK to render custom task user interfaces directly inside the BPMN diagram view, while the Tasklist back end continues to assign work to those embedded views
Teams use the Orchestration Cluster API to extend the case or build a fully custom application, in addition to the built-in Tasklist user interface that ships out of the box with Camunda 8

d.text: Correct. The Tasklist introduction states that users are not limited to using the Tasklist user interface and can extend their case or build a fully custom application using the Orchestration Cluster API, alongside the ready-to-use UI.

b.text: Incorrect. The introduction does not describe a Tasklist-specific GraphQL endpoint inside Operate; it points teams at the Orchestration Cluster API for custom applications.

a.text: Incorrect. The introduction does not describe a Console webhook subscription for forwarding user task events; the documented extension surface is the Orchestration Cluster API.

c.text: Incorrect. The introduction does not describe a Modeler plugin SDK for custom task UIs; teams build fully custom applications via the Orchestration Cluster API.

explanation: The Tasklist introduction explicitly tells readers they are not limited to the Tasklist user interface and points at the Orchestration Cluster API as the way to extend the case or build a fully custom application. The page also links to the frontend developer documentation for task applications.

scenario: Vertex Logistics runs Camunda 8.8 SaaS for 6000 shipments per day. The platform team triggers 5 batch retry actions per week to recover stuck deliveries. After each batch, operators open the Processes view in Operate, expand the operations panel, click an operation ID, and want to see which affected process instances completed that operation successfully.
question: Which column does Operate add to the process instances list once an operation is selected from the operations panel?

options.text: A column named Incident State is added to the process instances list and indicates the result of the selected operation
A column named Batch Result is added to the process instances list and indicates the result of the selected operation
A column named Operation State is added to the process instances list and indicates the result of the selected operation
A column named Job Status is added to the process instances list and indicates the result of the selected operation

a.text: Incorrect. The docs name the new column Operation State; there is no Incident State column added to the process instances list when an operation is selected from the operations panel.

d.text: Incorrect. Job Status is not a column added by selecting an operation; jobs are surfaced inside individual instance views, not added to the filtered process instances list by this action.

c.text: Correct. Per docs, after selecting an operation from the operations panel, a new column named Operation State is added to the process instances list indicating each instance's current operation state.

b.text: Incorrect. Batch Result is not the column name used by Operate; the docs explicitly call the column added to the process instances list Operation State.

explanation: After selecting an operation from the operations panel in the Processes view, the docs describe a new column named Operation State being added to the process instances list and showing the current state of the selected operation per instance.

scenario: Pillar Bank runs Camunda 8.8 SaaS for 12000 loans per day. A newly onboarded operator opens the Processes view in Operate, expands the operations panel for the first time on a clean tenant where no cancel, retry, migration, or modification action has ever been started, and wonders what the empty operations panel should display.
question: What does the operations panel show when no cancel, retry, migration, or modification action has yet been started?

options.text: The panel displays the cluster job worker activity feed so the operator can pick a stuck job and act on it from there
The panel displays the last 5 operations from the prior Operate version that were migrated forward from a Camunda 7 setup
The panel displays a default seed list of system-generated operations created automatically by Zeebe at deploy time
The panel displays the text No operations have been created yet. and stays empty until an operator initiates an operation

d.text: Correct. Per docs, if no operations exist the operations panel shows the text No operations have been created yet. and remains empty until an operator initiates a cancel, retry, migration, or modification action in the cluster.

c.text: Incorrect. Zeebe does not seed system-generated operations at deploy time; the operations panel only lists operator-initiated cancel, retry, migration, or modification actions.

b.text: Incorrect. Operate does not import operations from a Camunda 7 engine; the panel reflects only operations created by operators in the current Camunda 8 cluster.

a.text: Incorrect. The operations panel lists operator-initiated cancel, retry, migration, and modification actions on process instances; it is not a job worker activity feed.

explanation: On a tenant where nothing has yet been started, the Operate docs say the operations panel shows the text No operations have been created yet., and entries appear only after an operator triggers a cancel, retry, migration, or modification action.

scenario: Quantum Insurance runs Camunda 8.8 with 8000 claims per day. A platform operator has just triggered a batch of cancel, retry, migration and modification operations from Operate and now needs to monitor exactly which of these have failed. The team wants to know how Operate visualises operation state and failures on the Processes view for these mixed operations.
question: How does the monitor-operation-status page describe the Operate workflow for viewing operation state and failure details for a chosen operation?

options.text: The operator opens the Tasklist user task queue, claims the matching automated cleanup task, and a built-in script runs that retries every failed operation in the batch and emails a summary to the on-call engineer

The operator opens the Processes view in Operate, expands the Operations panel, selects an operation ID, and a new Operation State column appears on the instance list, with failed rows marked red and expandable to show the error message

The operator opens Optimize, filters by operation type, and reads aggregated failure ratios per operation against 7 days of historical data, then drills into individual operations from the Optimize report
The operator opens the Console alerts page, selects the alert ID for the operation batch, and reads per-operation status from a Console-only audit log that is not shown anywhere inside the Operate UI

c.text: Incorrect. Optimize is not the documented surface for monitoring Operate operation status; the userguide drives this workflow entirely from the Processes view and Operations panel in Operate.

d.text: Incorrect. Console alerts are not the documented monitor for operation state; the workflow uses the Operate Processes view and a side Operations panel.

b.text: Correct. The userguide says the operator goes to the Processes view in Operate, expands the Operations panel, selects an operation by clicking its ID so it becomes a filter, and an Operation State column appears on the instance list; failed operations mark the row red and expanding it shows the related error message.

a.text: Incorrect. Tasklist is not used to monitor or retry Operate operations; the documented flow runs in the Operate Processes view and Operations panel.

explanation: Monitor operation status in Operate drives off the Processes view: the operator expands the Operations panel via the Expand Operations icon, picks an operation ID which becomes a filter, and an Operation State column appears on the process-instance list. Failed operations mark the row red, and expanding the row reveals the error message related to that selected operation.

scenario: Vertex Insurance runs Camunda 8.8 SaaS with 6000 policies per day. After retrying 240 stuck process instances from the Processes view, the operator selects that retry operation from the operations panel and wants to track per-instance progress as the engine works through the selection without leaving Operate or scripting a custom dashboard.
question: Which column appears on the processes instances list once an operation is selected from the operations panel?

options.text: A column named Incident Owner is added to the list, displaying which operator initially raised the operation for each affected instance
A column named Operation State is added to the processes instances list, indicating the current state of the operation for each instance
A column named Retry Backoff is added to the list, showing the next scheduled retry time for the selected operation on each instance
A column named Job Worker Health is added to the list, surfacing each worker's last successful poll timestamp for the running instances

d.text: Incorrect. Selecting an operation from the operations panel does not add a worker health column; that signal lives in cluster health pages, not the processes instances list.

b.text: Correct. After selecting an operation from the operations panel, Operate adds an Operation State column to the processes instances list, indicating the current state of the operation for each instance.

c.text: Incorrect. There is no Retry Backoff column added when an operation is selected; the processes list surfaces the operation state for each instance, not the next retry schedule.

a.text: Incorrect. There is no Incident Owner column added by selecting an operation; the operations panel filter contributes an Operation State column, not an audit field for the originating operator.

explanation: When an operation is selected from the operations panel, the operations panel filter applies and Operate adds an Operation State column to the processes instances list so the operator can see, per instance, the current state of the selected operation across the affected process instances.

scenario: Catalyst Fintech runs Camunda 8.8 SaaS for 4000 loans per day. A newly onboarded operator opens Operate, clicks Processes in the top navigation, and clicks the Expand Operations icon on a tenant where no retry, cancel, migration, or modification action has yet been started across the cluster.
question: What does the operations panel display when no operations have been created yet?

options.text: It shows the text Awaiting first operation, and the cluster populates the list as soon as the broker reports any job worker activity
It shows the text Operations panel disabled, and the feature is unlocked only after the organization purchases the batch operations add-on tier
It shows the text No operations available, and the panel is only enabled for tenants with at least 100 running process instances visible
It shows the text No operations have been created yet, and the panel stays empty until an operator initiates a cancel, retry, migration, or modification

a.text: Incorrect. The empty operations panel is not driven by broker job worker activity; nothing populates the list except operator-initiated cancel, retry, migration, or modification actions.

c.text: Incorrect. There is no minimum instance threshold; the operations panel is always available, and the empty-state text shipped by Operate is different from this distractor.

d.text: Correct. When no operations exist, the operations panel shows the text No operations have been created yet, and stays empty until an operator triggers a cancel, retry, migration, or modification action.

b.text: Incorrect. The operations panel is part of Operate at every tier and is not gated by an add-on purchase; the empty-state text differs from this distractor as well.

explanation: Operate's monitor operation status guide is explicit that when no operations exist, the operations panel shows the text No operations have been created yet, and the panel begins listing entries only after an operator initiates a cancel, retry, migration, or modification.

scenario: Beacon Bank runs Camunda 8.8 SaaS with 8000 transactions per day. The operator started a cancel operation across 50 process instances; one row now appears marked red in the processes instances list, and the operator wants to inspect why that single instance's cancel operation failed without leaving Operate.
question: How can the operator surface the error message tied to that specific failed operation in the processes instances list?

options.text: Switch to the Tasklist view, locate a user task on the failed process instance, and read the error message from the task assignment detail panel
Expand the red-marked process instance row in the processes instances list to reveal the error message related to the selected failed operation
Restart the broker pod for the affected partition, then re-run the cancel operation and check Optimize for the persisted error event from the operation
Open the cluster log in the SaaS console, copy the trace id from the canceled instance, and search the Elasticsearch index for the cause of the failure

d.text: Incorrect. Operate exposes the failed operation's error message directly on the red-marked row; a separate trip through the SaaS console and Elasticsearch is not required to find it.

a.text: Incorrect. Tasklist exposes user task details, not operation error messages; the failed cancel operation's error is shown by expanding the red row in the processes instances list.

c.text: Incorrect. Restarting the broker partition or going to Optimize is unrelated to inspecting a failed operation; Operate already surfaces the error message inline in the processes list.

b.text: Correct. When an operation fails, the related process instance row is marked red and the operator can expand the row to see the error message tied to the selected operation in the processes instances list.

explanation: Per the Operate monitor operation status documentation, a failed operation marks the related process instance row red in the processes instances list, and expanding the row reveals the error message tied to the previously selected operation.

scenario: Apex Logistics runs Camunda 8.8 SaaS for 12000 shipments per day. A new platform engineer is documenting which operator-initiated actions the Operate operations panel exposes for monitoring and investigation across the cluster, and is reviewing the official Operate guide to enumerate them.
question: Which set of operations does the Operate operations panel track for monitoring and investigation?

options.text: Inviting new users, rotating API client credentials, and editing Identity role mappings across the cluster's tenants from the operations panel
Deploying BPMN files, registering job workers, and scaling cluster partitions across the Zeebe brokers running in the cluster setup
Authoring forms in Web Modeler, publishing DMN diagrams, and starting new process instances from the Operate run button on the diagram
Canceling and retrying process instances, process instance migration, and process instance modification operations across the cluster

d.text: Correct. Operate's monitor operation status guide states that the operations panel covers canceling and retrying process instances, process instance migration, and process instance modification operations in a single panel.

b.text: Incorrect. Broker deployment, worker registration, and partition scaling are cluster-level concerns surfaced by Console and broker logs, not by the Operate operations panel.

c.text: Incorrect. Form authoring and DMN publishing belong to Web Modeler; starting new instances is not what the operations panel monitors, which is dedicated to cancel, retry, migration, and modification.

a.text: Incorrect. User invitations and API client rotations live in Identity and Console; the Operate operations panel monitors cancel, retry, migration, and modification operations only.

explanation: The Operate monitor operation status guide says the operations panel monitors and lets you investigate operations you start, including canceling and retrying process instances, process instance migration, and process instance modification.

scenario: Orbit Fintech runs Camunda 8.8 SaaS for 18000 transactions per day across 6 microservices. Platform architects are mapping which Camunda 8 web app owns which job: active-instance monitoring and troubleshooting of process execution versus user task assignment, analytics dashboards, or cluster lifecycle management for the fintech production environment.
question: Which Camunda 8 component is the tool for monitoring and troubleshooting process instances running in Zeebe?

options.text: Tasklist is the tool for monitoring and troubleshooting process instances running in Zeebe across the cluster
Operate is the tool for monitoring and troubleshooting process instances running in Zeebe across the cluster
Console is the tool for monitoring and troubleshooting process instances running in Zeebe across the cluster
Optimize is the tool for monitoring and troubleshooting process instances running in Zeebe across the cluster

a.text: Incorrect. Tasklist is the ready-to-use application for BPMN user task assignment and completion; it does not own process instance monitoring or operational troubleshooting of running executions.

c.text: Incorrect. Console manages clusters, API clients, alerts, IP allowlists, and the organization; it does not provide per-instance monitoring or troubleshooting of process execution in Zeebe.

d.text: Incorrect. Optimize focuses on analytics, KPIs, and dashboards built on process data; troubleshooting active process instances in Zeebe belongs to a different web app in the Camunda 8 product.

b.text: Correct. Per docs, Operate is the tool for monitoring and troubleshooting process instances running in Zeebe, including resolving incidents, updating variables, and modifying or batch-moving active instances.

explanation: The Operate docs open by stating that Operate is the tool for monitoring and troubleshooting process instances running in Zeebe, with extra capabilities such as resolving incidents, updating variables, and modifying or batch moving active instances.

scenario: Vanta Payments, a fintech running a Camunda 8.8 proof of concept for 10000 transactions per day, plans to ship Operate alongside Zeebe. The architect needs to confirm exactly where Operate may legally run before the team signs the rollout plan for production clusters against the Camunda 8 commercial offering.
question: Which statement correctly describes how Operate is licensed and made available with Camunda 8.8?

options.text: Operate is freely licensed for both production and non-production use through a perpetual open-source community license shipped together with each Zeebe broker.
Operate is available only as a 30-day evaluation under the trial license, after which non-production access is also blocked until the cluster is upgraded to a paid plan.
Operate is licensed only under a paid Enterprise subscription and cannot be used in any environment without first signing a full Camunda 8 commercial plan.
Operate is offered under the Operate trial license for use in non-production environments only, with production use available through the Camunda 8 offering.

c.text: Incorrect. The Operate trial license explicitly permits use without a paid plan, but only for non-production environments; a paid Camunda 8 plan is required for production use, not for any use at all.

d.text: Correct. The docs state that Operate is available under the Operate trial license with no time or feature restrictions as long as it is used in non-production environments, and is available for production use with support in the Camunda 8 offering.

a.text: Incorrect. Operate is not shipped under a perpetual open-source community license; it is offered under the Operate trial license for non-production use and through the commercial Camunda 8 offering for production.

b.text: Incorrect. The Operate trial license places no restriction on the length of the evaluation period or on the available feature set, so a 30-day expiry on non-production use does not apply.

explanation: Operate is offered under the Operate trial license for non-production use with no time or feature limits, and is available for production use with support as part of the Camunda 8 commercial offering.

scenario: Iris Insurance runs Camunda 8.8 SaaS with 9000 policies per day. A new platform engineer is reading the Operate introduction and needs to recall which engine Operate is bound to for monitoring and troubleshooting the process instances running across the company's tenants in the cluster.
question: Which Camunda 8 engine does Operate monitor and troubleshoot process instances for?

options.text: The Camunda 7 process engine running on the JVM with persistence in a relational database such as PostgreSQL or Oracle behind the cluster
The Optimize analytics engine that ingests historical process events from the Elasticsearch cluster behind Camunda 8 and serves them as reports
The Identity authorization engine that issues OAuth tokens for the API clients used by Operate and the rest of Camunda 8 from the cluster
The Zeebe distributed workflow engine that runs the BPMN process instances and powers the rest of Camunda 8 SaaS across the cluster

a.text: Incorrect. Camunda 7 is a separate product family; Operate in Camunda 8 monitors and troubleshoots process instances on the Zeebe distributed workflow engine, not on the Camunda 7 process engine.

d.text: Correct. The Operate introduction states that Operate is a tool for monitoring and troubleshooting process instances running in Zeebe, the distributed workflow engine that powers Camunda 8 SaaS and Self-Managed.

b.text: Incorrect. Optimize is a separate analytics product for reports and KPIs; Operate monitors process instances on Zeebe, not on an Optimize analytics engine.

c.text: Incorrect. Identity is the OAuth authorization service for the platform; Operate monitors process instances on Zeebe, not on Identity, which only issues tokens for API clients.

explanation: The Operate introduction is explicit that Operate is a tool for monitoring and troubleshooting process instances running in Zeebe, the distributed workflow engine at the heart of Camunda 8.

scenario: Sable Bank runs Camunda 8.8 SaaS with 6500 loans per day. A wrong service task absorbed the token on a single in-flight loan instance, and the platform engineer needs to allow execution to continue on that one active instance without canceling and re-creating it from scratch.
question: Which Operate capability allows execution to continue on a single active process instance?

options.text: Process instance modification on the single active instance, which lets the operator move the token, activate elements, or terminate scopes on the instance
Process instance migration on the single active instance, which switches the instance from the current process version to a newer process version of the BPMN
Process instance batch retry on the single active instance, which re-runs the latest failed job worker handler for that instance in a tight retry loop in the cluster
Process instance redeployment on the single active instance, which republishes the BPMN file and rewires the token to the start event of the same active instance

a.text: Correct. The Operate introduction calls out that Operate can modify an active process instance to allow execution to continue, via process instance modification on that single instance, alongside other key operations.

b.text: Incorrect. Process instance migration switches the instance to a different process definition version; it does not, by itself, move a stuck token on the same version to let execution continue.

c.text: Incorrect. There is no instance-scoped tight retry loop construct in Operate; per-instance unblocking is performed through process instance modification, not through repeated job retries on one instance.

d.text: Incorrect. Operate does not redeploy a BPMN file per instance to rewire the token; per-instance unblocking is achieved by process instance modification on the single active instance.

explanation: Per the Operate introduction, one of Operate's key operations is to modify an active process instance to allow execution to continue, which is performed via process instance modification on the affected instance.

scenario: Zenith Insurance runs Camunda 8.8 SaaS for 3000 claims per day. After a deployment bug routed 800 claim instances down the wrong branch, the lead operator wants to move all 800 active claim instances to the correct flow node in one bulk operation rather than touching each instance individually.
question: Which Operate capability lets the operator move many active process instances to a different flow node at once?

options.text: Batch move modification, which moves many active process instances to the correct flow node in a single bulk batch modification operation from Operate
Process instance migration on the 800 claim instances, which switches each instance to a new BPMN version where the misrouted branch is fixed at the source
Per-instance modification, applied to each of the 800 claim instances one at a time, replays the token at the correct flow node for every instance separately
Selections-based cancel, which terminates the 800 misrouted claim instances so that a fresh instance starts again from the correct branch in the BPMN model

c.text: Incorrect. Single-instance modification works one at a time and is not the bulk path; the Operate introduction explicitly lists batch move of active process instances as a separate operation.

d.text: Incorrect. Canceling and restarting loses each instance's state and is not the path the Operate introduction names for moving many active instances to a different flow node together.

b.text: Incorrect. Migration is meant for switching to a new process version, not for moving the token between flow nodes within a process; bulk token moves are batch move modifications.

a.text: Correct. The Operate introduction lists batch move of active process instances as a key operation that lets the operator move many active process instances together to allow execution to continue.

explanation: The Operate introduction explicitly lists batch move of active process instances as a distinct key operation in Operate, intended for moving many active instances to a different flow node in a single bulk modification.

scenario: Aurora Bank runs Camunda 8.8 with 12000 loans per day and discovers that a faulty gateway condition routed 4500 loan instances down the wrong process branch. Operations wants to move all 4500 affected instances back onto the corrected service task in a single coordinated action, rather than fixing one instance at a time.
question: Which statement correctly distinguishes batch modification from process instance modification in Operate?

options.text: Batch modification activates a single element on one active process instance, while process instance modification moves a large set of stuck instances across flow nodes in one bulk pass.
Batch modification edits variables on many process instances at once, while process instance modification is limited to terminating element instances on a single active process instance.
Batch modification and process instance modification are aliases of the same Operate feature, with batch reserved for SaaS clusters and instance modification reserved for Self-Managed clusters.
Batch modification moves multiple selected process instances to the correct flow node in one operation, while process instance modification covers a single active instance with extra options.

a.text: Incorrect. The docs reverse the directions described here: batch modification is the bulk path that moves multiple selected instances together, and process instance modification is the single-instance path that also offers activating an element.

b.text: Incorrect. Batch modification does not edit variables; it moves selected process instances to a different flow node. Process instance modification is the single-instance flow and offers options like activating or terminating an element instance.

d.text: Correct. The docs describe batch modification as selecting multiple process instances and moving them to the correct flow node in a single operation, and direct users to process instance modification when only one active instance needs additional options such as activate or terminate.

c.text: Incorrect. The two flows are not SaaS-versus-Self-Managed variants of the same feature; they target different scopes, single instance versus a selected batch of instances on the same source flow node.

explanation: Batch modification is the Operate path used when multiple process instances need to be moved to the correct flow node in one operation; a single active instance is handled through process instance modification, which also offers extra options like activating or terminating an element instance.

scenario: Helios Logistics processes 8200 shipments per day in Camunda 8.8 SaaS. After a bad release, 350 shipment instances are parked on a wrong validation task. An operator selects them in the process instance list view and clicks Move, then expects the UI to clearly confirm that batch modification mode is now active before any apply step is taken.
question: Which visual indicators does Operate show in the list view after the operator clicks Continue on the information modal?

options.text: A green checkmark column replaces the toolbar, and the Move action becomes unavailable until each selected instance has been approved one by one.
An information modal remains pinned on screen and blocks any further selection changes until the Apply Modification action is finally confirmed.
A blue border with a blue banner at the top and two buttons at the bottom for applying or exiting modifications becomes visible across the list view.
A red border with a red banner at the top and one End Batch button at the bottom that discards all selected moves immediately on click.

d.text: Incorrect. The docs specify that batch modification mode is represented by a blue border and a blue banner with two buttons at the bottom (apply or exit), not a red border with a single End Batch button.

a.text: Incorrect. There is no per-instance approval gate or green checkmark column; once the operator clicks Continue, the toolbar remains and a blue banner plus border indicates batch mode is active.

b.text: Incorrect. The information modal closes when the operator clicks Continue; the UI then shows a blue border and banner indicating batch modification mode and selection can continue freely.

c.text: Correct. The docs state that entering batch modification mode is represented by a blue border, including a blue banner at the top and two buttons at the bottom for applying or exiting modifications.

explanation: After clicking Continue on the information modal, Operate enters batch modification mode and signals this with a blue border and a blue banner at the top, plus two buttons at the bottom of the list view for applying or exiting the modification.

scenario: Zenith Insurance runs Camunda 8.8 with 4200 policies per day. After a faulty boundary configuration, an operator selects 250 stuck policy instances and tries a batch Move on a mixture of element types including boundary events, an embedded subprocess, and an element with multiple running scopes, and expects Operate to refuse certain element types.
question: Which set of elements cannot be used as the source of a batch Move modification in Operate?

options.text: Service tasks with attached timer boundaries, message intermediate catch events, and elements inside an embedded subprocess scope are the only element types that cannot be moved.
Start events, boundary events, events attached to event-based gateways, a subprocess itself, and elements that currently have multiple running scopes cannot be moved.
User tasks, business rule tasks, call activities, and any element inside a transaction subprocess scope are the element types that cannot be used as a batch Move source.
Sequence flows, message flows, gateways, and intermediate throw events are the element types that cannot be used as the source of a batch Move once batch mode is active.

b.text: Correct. The docs list the elements that block a batch Move: start events, boundary events, and events attached to event-based gateways, a subprocess itself, and elements with multiple running scopes; these element types cannot be moved through batch modification.

a.text: Incorrect. The docs do not single out service tasks with attached timers or message catch events as blocked; the blocked types are start events, boundary events, event-based gateway events, subprocesses, and elements with multiple running scopes.

c.text: Incorrect. User tasks, business rule tasks, and call activities are valid Move sources; only start events, boundary events, event-based gateway events, subprocesses themselves, and elements with multiple running scopes are blocked.

d.text: Incorrect. Sequence flows, message flows, and gateways are not flow nodes that the batch Move targets at all; the documented blockers are start events, boundary events, event-based gateway events, a subprocess itself, and elements with multiple running scopes.

explanation: Operate batch Move modifications are blocked on start events, boundary events, events attached to event-based gateways, a subprocess itself, and elements that currently have multiple running scopes; other regular flow nodes can serve as Move sources.

scenario: Forge Bank runs Camunda 8.8 SaaS for 5000 transactions per day. The operator has picked the affected source flow node on the process diagram and is now selecting 40 process instances stuck on that faulty service task; the operator wants to know which action will appear in the process instance toolbar.
question: Which action appears in the process instance toolbar once instances are selected for batch move?

options.text: A Migrate action appears in the toolbar, which would take the operator to the migration view for the selected process instances on the cluster
A Modify action appears in the toolbar, which opens the single-instance modification view for the first selected process instance in the list
A Move action appears in the toolbar, which becomes available as soon as the operator selects process instances from the processes list in Operate
A Retry action appears in the toolbar, which would ask the engine to re-execute the last failed job for the selected process instances at once

a.text: Incorrect. Migrate is the entry action for the process instance migration flow, not for batch modification; the batch modification flow exposes a Move action in the toolbar instead.

d.text: Incorrect. Retry replays failed jobs on selected instances but is not the action exposed by the batch modification flow; that flow surfaces a Move action in the toolbar.

c.text: Correct. Per the batch modification guide, as the operator selects process instances, the process instance toolbar appears and the Move action becomes available, which starts the batch move flow.

b.text: Incorrect. The single-instance Modify action is for individual process instance modification, not the batch move flow; the batch flow uses the Move action that appears in the toolbar on selection.

explanation: As the operator selects instances on the processes list, the process instance toolbar appears and the Move action becomes available; clicking Move is the documented entry point for the batch modification flow in Operate.

scenario: Aurora Insurance runs Camunda 8.8 SaaS with 9000 policies per day. The operator entered batch modification mode and selected 80 process instances, but realized the wrong source flow node was picked and wants to drop the current selection without leaving the mode itself to start over with a different set.
question: How can the operator drop all currently selected instances without leaving batch modification mode?

options.text: Click the Discard option in the process instance toolbar, or simply clear the current selection in the list while staying inside batch modification mode
Click Apply Modification below the process instance list view, which commits an empty modification so that the current selection is dropped without changes
Click Move once more in the toolbar, which restarts the batch flow and resets the current selection to an empty state inside the same batch modification mode
Click Exit below the process instance list view, which clears the current selection and returns the view to its prior state outside batch modification mode

d.text: Incorrect. Clicking Exit leaves batch modification mode entirely; the documented in-mode discard path is the Discard option in the toolbar or clearing the selection directly.

b.text: Incorrect. Apply Modification commits the batch operation to the engine; it is not the documented way to discard the current selection while staying in batch modification mode.

a.text: Correct. The batch modification guide explicitly says the operator can discard all currently selected instances by clicking the Discard option in the process instance toolbar or by clearing the current selection.

c.text: Incorrect. There is no documented Move-as-reset behavior; in-mode discard is achieved through the Discard option in the toolbar or by clearing the selection on the processes list.

explanation: While in batch modification mode, the operator can continue to refine the selection and can discard all currently selected instances either through the Discard option in the process instance toolbar or by clearing the current selection.

scenario: Meridian Insurance runs Camunda 8.8 SaaS with 11000 claims per day. After entering batch modification mode and selecting 60 process instances stuck on the wrong service task, the operator now needs to indicate where the tokens should move to and then commit the operation through the documented Operate flow.
question: Which sequence does the operator follow to move the selected instances to a target flow node and commit?

options.text: Type the target flow node id into the process instance toolbar, click Move once more, and click Confirm in the appearing migration summary modal to commit the change
Click Apply Modification first, then choose the target flow node from the appearing dropdown, and finally click Continue in the confirmation modal to commit
Click Exit, switch to single-instance modification on each selected instance, and click Apply individually for every selected process instance in the original cluster
Select the target flow node on the diagram, click Apply Modification below the list, and click Apply in the confirmation summary modal to start the batch operation

b.text: Incorrect. The target flow node is picked on the diagram before Apply Modification, and the summary modal uses an Apply button; ordering and labels in this option do not match the documented flow.

d.text: Correct. The batch modification guide says: select the target flow node, click Apply Modification below the list, and click Apply in the confirmation summary modal to start the batch modification operation.

a.text: Incorrect. The target flow node is picked on the diagram, not typed into the toolbar; the summary modal commits via an Apply button as part of batch modification, not via Confirm on a migration modal.

c.text: Incorrect. The bulk path applies a single batch operation across the selection through Apply Modification and Apply on the summary modal; falling back to single-instance modification defeats the bulk flow.

explanation: After selecting the target flow node on the diagram, the operator clicks Apply Modification below the process instance list view, and then clicks the Apply button in the confirmation summary modal to start the batch modification operation.

scenario: Granite Bank runs Camunda 8.8 SaaS with 8500 transactions per day. After selecting the target flow node and clicking Apply Modification on 150 process instances, the operator now faces a confirmation modal that summarizes the selected modifications before any change is persisted on the cluster.
question: Which button in the confirmation modal actually starts the batch modification?

options.text: The Confirm button starts the batch modification operation, paired with typing the word MODIFY into a confirmation text box before the operator can submit
The Move button starts the batch modification operation; it is the same button used earlier to enter batch modification mode from the process instance toolbar
The Apply button in the summary modal starts the batch modification on the selected process instances at the chosen target flow node for the operation
The Continue button starts the batch modification operation; it is the same button that earlier confirmed entering batch modification mode from the information modal

d.text: Incorrect. Continue is used on the earlier information modal that confirms switching into batch mode; the summary modal that starts the operation uses an Apply button instead.

a.text: Incorrect. There is no MODIFY confirmation text box on the batch modification summary modal; that pattern belongs to the migration confirmation flow, not batch modification.

c.text: Correct. The batch modification guide says the operator clicks the Apply button on the summary modal to start the batch modification on the selected process instances at the chosen target flow node.

b.text: Incorrect. The Move button is the toolbar entry to batch mode; the actual commit happens through Apply Modification and Apply in the summary modal, not by clicking Move again.

explanation: Per the batch modification guide, after clicking Apply Modification a confirmation modal appears that summarizes the modifications, and clicking the Apply button on that summary modal starts the batch modification operation.

scenario: Wren Logistics runs Camunda 8.8 SaaS for 6000 shipments per day. The operator entered batch modification mode to move 25 selected instances but realized the wrong source flow node was picked and wants to leave the mode at any time without applying any of the pending changes to the cluster.
question: How can the operator leave batch modification mode without applying any of the pending changes?

options.text: Wait for the batch modification mode session to time out automatically after a fixed idle period, at which point all pending changes are dropped and the mode exits
Click Exit below the process instance list view at any time, which leaves batch modification mode without applying the pending changes to the selected instances
Click Apply Modification with an empty target, which the engine then treats as a no-op and quietly drops the operator out of batch modification mode in Operate
Refresh the browser tab on the Processes view, which abandons the current batch session and returns the view to its prior selection cleanly across the cluster

d.text: Incorrect. Operate exposes a documented Exit control for leaving batch modification mode; relying on a browser refresh is not the documented way to leave the mode without applying changes.

b.text: Correct. The batch modification guide explicitly states the operator can exit the modification mode at any time by clicking the Exit button below the process instance list view.

c.text: Incorrect. Apply Modification commits the batch operation through the summary modal; it is not a no-op escape hatch. The documented exit path is the Exit button below the list view.

a.text: Incorrect. There is no documented idle timeout that auto-exits batch modification mode; the operator leaves the mode explicitly by clicking the Exit button below the list view.

explanation: Per the batch modification guide, the operator can exit the modification mode at any time by clicking the Exit control below the process instance list view, which leaves batch modification mode without applying any pending changes.

scenario: Nimbus Bank runs Camunda 8.8 with 4500 loan instances of orderProcess version 3, some completed, some active, some in incident state, and some terminated. The operations team selects all of them from the process instances list and clicks Migrate to move the cohort to version 4.
question: Which subset of the selected process instances does Operate include in the migration plan?

options.text: All process instances of the source version participate, including completed and terminated ones, so the plan covers the full 4500-instance cohort selected from the list.
Only the process instances currently sitting on a flow node that has been mapped in the bottom panel participate; idle instances are filtered out of the plan.
All selected instances participate, but the completed and terminated ones are flagged for the operator to manually review after the migration finishes.
Only running process instances, meaning those in active or incident state, participate; all other selected instances are ignored and not part of the migration plan.

a.text: Incorrect. Operate does not migrate completed or terminated instances; they are ignored even when the operator selects them in the list. Only running instances reach the migration plan.

d.text: Correct. The migration operation is restricted to running process instances in active or incident state; any other selected instances are excluded from the plan and ignored by the operation.

b.text: Incorrect. Migration eligibility is based on instance lifecycle state, not on the current flow node position; any running instance of the source version qualifies regardless of where its token sits.

c.text: Incorrect. Non-running instances are silently ignored, not flagged for follow-up; the documented behavior is that they simply do not participate in the migration plan.

explanation: In Camunda 8.8 Operate, the migration operation only applies to running process instances, defined as those in an active or incident state. Completed, canceled, or terminated instances selected from the list are silently excluded from the migration plan.

scenario: Iris Insurance runs Camunda 8.8 with 800 policies per day on policyProcess. An operator selects 3 active instances of version 2 from the process instances list, clicks Migrate, and lands on the migration view to begin mapping flow nodes for the migration to version 3.
question: Which layout of three areas does the Operate migration view present to the operator?

options.text: The source process diagram is on the top left, the target process diagram is on the bottom left, and the flow node mapping list runs down the entire right side of the view.
The source process diagram fills the top of the view, and the bottom is split between the target process diagram on one side and a flow node mapping table on the other.
The source process diagram is on the top left, the target process diagram is on the top right, and the flow node mapping list sits in the bottom panel of the migration view.
A list of running source instances is at the top, the target process diagram is at the bottom left, and the flow node mapping list sits in the bottom right of the view.

c.text: Correct. The Operate migration view shows the source process diagram on the top left, the target process diagram on the top right, and the flow node mapping list in the bottom panel, the three areas documented for the view.

a.text: Incorrect. The target diagram sits beside the source diagram on the top right, not below it; the flow node mapping list is the bottom panel, not a right-side rail of the migration view.

d.text: Incorrect. The running instances were selected on the previous screen; the migration view itself shows source and target diagrams side by side on top and the mapping panel below them.

b.text: Incorrect. The source and target diagrams share the top area side by side, top left and top right respectively, and the bottom panel is dedicated to the flow node mapping list alone.

explanation: Operate's migration view is structured into three areas. The source process diagram occupies the top left, the target process diagram occupies the top right, and a flow node mapping list sits in the bottom panel where operators bind each source flow node to a target flow node.

scenario: Tessera Logistics runs Camunda 8.8 SaaS with 5200 shipments per day. A platform engineer is selecting 300 process instances of orderProcess for migration to a new version and wants to recall which instance states make an instance eligible to be part of the migration plan in Operate.
question: Which process instance states are eligible to be migrated by the Operate migration plan?

options.text: Active and incident instances only, while completed, canceled, and other non-running instances are ignored by the migration plan in Operate as documented
Active and completed instances, allowing the migration to backfill historical reporting in Optimize for the target version of the process on the cluster
Canceled and incident instances only, with the migration view treating them as recoverable while leaving fully active instances alone on the cluster setup
Completed and canceled instances only, with Zeebe rewriting their historical record into the target process version at migration time across the cluster

d.text: Incorrect. Completed and canceled instances cannot be migrated; the migration view documents that only running instances, meaning active or incident, are eligible to be part of the migration plan.

b.text: Incorrect. Completed instances are not migrated; only running instances, meaning active or incident, are eligible, so historical instances are ignored by the migration plan in Operate.

c.text: Incorrect. Canceled instances are not eligible for migration; the migration plan is restricted to running instances, which the docs define as either active or in an incident state.

a.text: Correct. The migration guide states it is only possible to migrate running process instances, meaning instances in an active or incident state, and that all other instances are ignored.

explanation: The Operate process instance migration guide explicitly says that only running process instances, meaning instances in an active or incident state, are part of the migration plan; all other instances such as completed or canceled are ignored.

scenario: Stellar Bank runs Camunda 8.8 SaaS for 17000 transactions per day. A new operator opens the migration view for the first time on a payment process and is greeted by three distinct areas; the team lead wants the operator to learn the documented layout before performing any real migrations on the cluster.
question: Which three areas does the Operate migration view present to the operator on screen?

options.text: The source process diagram in a left tab, the target process diagram in a right tab, and a job worker activity feed across the bottom of the migration view setup
A target process diagram on the left, the source process diagram on the right, and an operations panel along the bottom of the migration view in Operate
The source process diagram at the top left, the target process diagram at the top right, and a flow node mapping panel along the bottom of the migration view
A BPMN editor at the top, a DMN editor at the bottom, and a side panel listing the API tokens needed to start the migration operation on the cluster setup

b.text: Incorrect. The source is on the top left and the target on the top right in the migration view, not the other way around, and the bottom is a flow node mapping panel, not the operations panel.

d.text: Incorrect. The migration view is not a BPMN or DMN editor; it surfaces the source and target diagrams plus a flow node mapping panel, not a side list of API tokens to start the operation.

c.text: Correct. The migration guide describes the migration view as having three areas: the source process diagram at the top left, the target process diagram at the top right, and the flow node mapping panel at the bottom.

a.text: Incorrect. The diagrams are shown side by side at the top, not in left and right tabs, and the bottom area is a flow node mapping panel, not a job worker activity feed for the cluster.

explanation: Per the migration guide, the migration view has three areas: the source process diagram at the top left, the target process diagram at the top right, and the flow node mapping panel along the bottom of the view.

scenario: Lattice Insurance runs Camunda 8.8 SaaS with 6800 claims per day. The operator entered the migration view from 120 active instances of claimsProcess version 3 and now needs to set the destination process and version before mapping any flow nodes from the source to the target on the cluster.
question: How does the operator pick the destination process and version in the migration view?

options.text: Drag the source process diagram tile onto the target diagram tile, which auto-selects the latest deployed version of the same BPMN file in the cluster setup
Pick the destination from the filters panel on the left, then click Bump Version to move the selection to the next published BPMN version on the cluster setup
Enter the target process into the Target box and pick a version from the dropdown, defining where the selected source instances are migrated to in Operate
Type the target version number into a dialog in the operations panel; the destination process id is then inferred from the source selection on the cluster setup

c.text: Correct. The migration guide says the operator enters a target process into the Target box and selects a version from the dropdown, which defines where all selected process instances are migrated to in Operate.

a.text: Incorrect. There is no documented drag-and-drop between the source and target diagrams; the destination is chosen via the Target box and version dropdown in the migration view, not by dragging tiles.

b.text: Incorrect. The filters panel chooses the source process and source version, not the destination; the destination is picked through the Target box and version dropdown in the migration view itself.

d.text: Incorrect. The destination process is explicit, not inferred, and is set through the Target box and version dropdown in the migration view, not through a dialog in the operations panel.

explanation: Per the migration guide, the operator types the target process into the Target box and chooses a version from the dropdown; this combination defines the process and version that the selected source instances are migrated to.

scenario: Vanta Bank runs Camunda 8.8 SaaS with 22000 transactions per day. The operator is migrating 450 active instances of paymentProcess from version 4 to version 5 and now needs to indicate which target flow node each source service task should map to in the migration view of Operate on the cluster.
question: How does the operator declare which target flow node each source flow node maps to in the migration view?

options.text: Type a FEEL expression in the mapping panel that resolves to the target flow node id at runtime when the migration operation is started on the affected cluster
Use the dropdowns in the bottom flow node mapping panel to pick a target flow node for each source flow node that should be part of the migration plan in Operate
Click each source flow node in the diagram; the migration view then auto-maps it to a target flow node with a matching BPMN id without any operator action being needed
Drag each source flow node onto a target flow node directly in the diagrams; the migration view then records the mapping in the bottom panel automatically for the operation

d.text: Incorrect. The migration view does not record mappings via drag-and-drop on the diagrams; the documented mechanism is the dropdowns in the bottom flow node mapping panel for each source.

a.text: Incorrect. There is no FEEL-based runtime resolution of target flow nodes during migration; the operator uses the dropdowns in the bottom mapping panel to choose each target explicitly.

c.text: Incorrect. There is no auto-map-on-click behavior in the migration view; each source flow node is mapped explicitly via the dropdowns in the bottom flow node mapping panel.

b.text: Correct. The migration guide says the operator uses the dropdowns in the bottom flow node mapping panel to select a target flow node for each source flow node that should be part of the migration.

explanation: In the migration view, the bottom flow node mapping panel lists each source flow node and exposes dropdowns; the operator selects, for each source flow node that should be part of the migration, the matching target flow node.

scenario: Orion Insurance runs Camunda 8.8 SaaS with 9500 policies per day. Before confirming a migration of 75 active instances of orderProcess, the operator wants to double-check how the source flow node Check payment is visually mapped to its counterpart on version 2 of orderProcess in the migration view.
question: How can the operator visualize how an individual source flow node is mapped to its target in the migration view?

options.text: Hover over the Migrate button in the toolbar; this surfaces a tooltip with the full list of source-to-target mappings for the operation across the migration plan
Click a flow node on the diagram, or click a source flow node row in the bottom panel, to see how that flow node is currently mapped to the target in the view
Open the operations panel after starting the migration, where the operator can confirm or reject each per-flow-node mapping individually for the migration plan
Switch to Optimize and run the Migration Mapping report, which lists every source-to-target mapping for the selected process versions in the cluster setup

c.text: Incorrect. Per-flow-node confirmation does not happen via the operations panel after starting the migration; the mappings are inspected in the migration view itself before starting.

d.text: Incorrect. Optimize is analytics-only; the migration view itself surfaces how a specific source flow node maps to its target via the diagram and the bottom panel before the migration starts.

b.text: Correct. The migration guide says the operator can click a flow node on the diagram or click a source flow node row in the bottom panel to see how that flow node is mapped to the target.

a.text: Incorrect. There is no documented Migrate-button tooltip listing all mappings; mappings are inspected in the diagram and the bottom flow node mapping panel of the migration view itself.

explanation: Per the migration guide, the operator can preview how an individual source flow node is mapped to its target by clicking the flow node on the diagram or by clicking the source flow node row in the bottom mapping panel.

scenario: Atlas Bank runs Camunda 8.8 SaaS for 21000 transactions per day. The operator has reviewed the migration preview for 320 active loanProcess instances moving from version 6 to version 7 and is now ready to perform the final confirmation step before any tokens are moved on the cluster by Operate.
question: Which final confirmation step does Operate require to start the migration operation?

options.text: Click Apply Modification, accept the confirmation summary modal, and click Apply once more on the summary modal to start the migration operation on the cluster
Click Migrate in the toolbar, then click Continue in the appearing information modal, and click Apply Migration in the summary modal to start the migration operation
Click Next, then click Save Plan, and finally click Start Operation in the operations panel to start the migration operation on the affected process instances
Click Confirm to review the plan, type the word MIGRATE into the text box, and click Confirm again to start the migration operation on the affected instances

d.text: Correct. The migration guide says the operator clicks Confirm to review the migration, types MIGRATE into the text box, and clicks Confirm again to start the migration operation on the selected instances.

a.text: Incorrect. Apply Modification and Apply on a summary modal is the commit pattern for batch modification, not migration; migration uses Confirm plus typing MIGRATE in the text box.

b.text: Incorrect. Migrate enters the migration view at the start of the flow, not the final commit; the documented final step is Confirm plus typing MIGRATE plus a second Confirm.

c.text: Incorrect. There is no Save Plan button or operations-panel Start Operation control in migration; the documented final step is Confirm, type MIGRATE, and Confirm again to start.

explanation: Per the migration guide, the operator clicks Confirm to review the migration, then types the word MIGRATE into the text box and clicks Confirm a second time to start the migration operation on the selected process instances.

scenario: A logistics operator at Atlas Logistics runs Camunda 8.8 across 800 shipments per day and queues a cancel-all modification on a single flow node nested in a multi-instance body that currently holds 1200 running tokens for a shipment-fanout pattern. Before applying, the operator needs to confirm how many tokens a single such modification can affect in this version so the team can plan repetitions.
question: How many tokens can a single cancel-all or move-all modification affect on a flow node nested in a multi-instance body?

options.text: Up to 100 tokens can be affected per modification, and the operator must repeat the cancel or move modification on the same flow node until all remaining running tokens have been handled
Up to 500 tokens can be affected per modification, and the operator must repeat the cancel or move modification on the same flow node until all remaining running tokens have been handled
Up to 250 tokens can be affected per modification, and the operator must repeat the cancel or move modification on the same flow node until all remaining running tokens have been handled
Up to 1000 tokens can be affected per modification, and the operator must repeat the cancel or move modification on the same flow node until all remaining running tokens have been handled

a.text: Incorrect. The documented technical limit is 500 tokens per cancel-or-move modification on a flow node nested in a multi-instance body, not 100. Only 500 tokens are affected per repetition until all have been handled.

c.text: Incorrect. The documented technical limit is 500 tokens per cancel-or-move modification on a flow node nested in a multi-instance body, not 250. Only 500 tokens are affected per repetition until all have been handled.

d.text: Incorrect. The documented technical limit is 500 tokens per cancel-or-move modification on a flow node nested in a multi-instance body, not 1000. Only 500 tokens are affected per repetition until all have been handled.

b.text: Correct. The docs specify that when cancelling or moving all tokens within a single flow node instance nested in a multi-instance body, a maximum of 500 tokens can be affected per modification, and operators repeat the modification until all tokens are handled.

explanation: Cancelling or moving all tokens within a single flow node nested in a multi-instance body is capped at 500 tokens per modification in this version. Operators repeat the modification on the same flow node, or use the Orchestration Cluster API with a custom filter for finer control.

scenario: At Forge Bank, an operator running Camunda 8.8 Operate opens a single process instance carrying 12000 euros of loan disbursement and needs to begin making modifications. The operator wants a clear visual confirmation that the instance view has switched into a mode where pending changes can be staged before being committed.
question: How does the operator enter modification mode for this process instance, and what changes in the Operate UI when it is active?

options.text: The operator picks Edit Mode from a right-click menu on the process diagram, and the diagram switches to a grey background with a single Save button shown in the top toolbar
The operator opens the process instance settings panel and toggles a Modification switch, with no other visible UI change beyond the toggle remaining in the on position
The operator runs a modify-instance command from the Operate command palette, which opens a separate diff modal where each pending change is staged in a JSON editor view
The operator clicks the modify icon on the process instance header, after which Operate shows a blue banner at the top and footer buttons for applying or discarding the pending modifications

d.text: Correct. The docs describe entering modification mode by clicking the modify icon on the process instance header; the UI then shows a blue banner at the top and two buttons at the bottom for applying or discarding modifications.

a.text: Incorrect. Operate does not enter modification mode through a right-click Edit Mode menu on the diagram, and there is no grey background or single Save button described in the modification flow.

b.text: Incorrect. There is no settings-panel Modification toggle in Operate, and the docs explicitly call out a blue banner plus apply or discard buttons rather than a silent toggle change.

c.text: Incorrect. Modification mode is entered from the process instance header icon and stages changes inline in the instance history, not from a command palette into a separate JSON diff modal.

explanation: Modification mode is entered from the modify icon on the process instance header. Operate then shows a blue banner at the top of the view and two footer buttons for applying or discarding the pending modifications that the operator stages.

scenario: Aurora Insurance is running Camunda 8.8 and an operator has 250 claim instances paused at a Review Claim service task that is no longer needed for one specific instance. The operator opens that single instance in Operate modification mode and wants to remove every running token currently sitting on Review Claim before applying the change.
question: Which sequence in modification mode cancels all running tokens on the Review Claim flow node for this instance?

options.text: The operator selects the Review Claim flow node in the diagram and picks Cancel instance from its dropdown, and the cancellation appears as a pending modification in the instance history
The operator highlights Review Claim and chooses Move instance, then leaves the target empty so that the running tokens are dropped rather than moved to another flow node in the diagram
The operator selects the Review Claim flow node and picks Skip token, after which Operate immediately removes the token from the instance without any pending modification entry being created
The operator opens the variables panel, clears the value that fed Review Claim, and the engine then automatically retires every running token sitting on that flow node for the instance

a.text: Correct. The docs say that to cancel all running tokens on a flow node, the operator selects the flow node and clicks Cancel instance from the dropdown; the cancellation is then visible as a pending modification reflected in the instance history.

c.text: Incorrect. There is no Skip token action in the documented modification flow, and modifications are always staged as pending entries before they are applied, not removed immediately.

d.text: Incorrect. Cancelling tokens on a flow node is an explicit modification action in Operate; clearing a variable value does not automatically retire running tokens sitting on a service task.

b.text: Incorrect. The Move instance action requires choosing a target flow node to receive the tokens; leaving the target empty is not how tokens are cancelled in the documented flow.

explanation: To cancel all running tokens on a single flow node, the operator selects the flow node in modification mode and clicks Cancel instance from its dropdown. The pending cancellation is then reflected in the instance history before being applied.

scenario: Pinnacle Marketplace runs Camunda 8.8 and an operator needs to relocate all running tokens currently sitting on the Quote Shipping flow node to the Confirm Order flow node on one specific instance carrying a 4200 euro order, while keeping the other 6000 active instances untouched.
question: Which combination of single-modification actions is documented as equivalent to the Move instance modification on the source flow node?

options.text: A Move instance modification cannot be decomposed and is described as a primitive operation that has no equivalent expressed through other modification-mode actions on the diagram
A Cancel modification on the source flow node combined with an Add modification on the target flow node produces the same result as a single Move instance modification according to the documentation
A Cancel modification on the source flow node alone is described as equivalent to Move instance;  the engine automatically restarts a token at the next downstream flow node
Two consecutive Add modifications, the first on the source and the second on the target, are described as the equivalent decomposition of a Move instance modification in the docs

b.text: Correct. The docs explicitly state that the move operation is equivalent to the combination of Cancel and Add modifications; the modifications described before the move section can also be achieved with one single move modification.

d.text: Incorrect. Two Add modifications would introduce extra tokens rather than moving tokens away from the source flow node, so this is not the documented decomposition of Move instance.

c.text: Incorrect. A Cancel modification on the source alone removes tokens without seeding any at the target; the engine does not automatically restart a token at the next downstream flow node.

a.text: Incorrect. The docs state explicitly that the move operation is equivalent to the combination of Cancel and Add, so Move instance is documented as decomposable rather than primitive.

explanation: The documentation calls out that the Move instance modification is equivalent to a Cancel on the source flow node combined with an Add on the target flow node; either form may be used to relocate running tokens within a single process instance.

scenario: Crescent Fintech runs Camunda 8.8 and uses modification mode on one Camunda Operate instance worth 9800 dollars in pending settlement. The operator has just staged an Add modification that creates a new scope, and now needs to attach a fraudCheckRequired variable to that newly generated scope before applying any of the pending changes.
question: Which sequence adds a variable to a newly generated scope inside modification mode?

options.text: The operator stops modification mode briefly, opens the variables panel on the parent instance and adds the variable there, then restarts modification mode so the new scope inherits the variable on apply
The operator opens the source BPMN file in Web Modeler, declares the variable in a new scope, redeploys the model, and Operate then mirrors the variable into the newly generated scope of the instance
The operator picks the new scope and chooses Inject Variable from a context menu, which writes the variable straight into the engine state and bypasses the pending modifications summary view
In the instance history the operator selects the new scope, clicks Add Variable in the variables panel, fills Name and Value, and blurs the field so that the entry joins the pending modifications

d.text: Correct. The docs say the operator selects the new scope from the instance history, clicks Add Variable in the variables panel, fills Name and Value, and once the field is blurred with valid values the new variable joins the pending modifications.

a.text: Incorrect. Variables on a newly generated scope are configured inside modification mode on that scope itself, not by exiting modification mode and editing the parent instance variables.

b.text: Incorrect. Adding a variable to a new scope is an Operate modification action; redeploying the BPMN file from Web Modeler does not retroactively inject variables into existing scopes.

c.text: Incorrect. There is no Inject Variable context-menu action; the documented flow uses Add Variable, and the entry is staged as a pending modification rather than written straight into engine state.

explanation: When modification mode creates a new scope, the operator selects that scope in the instance history, clicks Add Variable in the variables panel, fills Name and Value, and blurs the field. The valid entry then joins the pending modifications before being applied.

scenario: A Junction Retail operator running Camunda 8.8 has staged 5 modifications on one open process instance worth 320 euros of returns. Before applying anything the operator wants to bring up a single screen that lists every pending modification on this instance so they can review the change set together.
question: Which action in modification mode opens the summary modal listing every pending modification on the instance?

options.text: The operator selects each individual flow node with a pending change, and a side panel automatically refreshes to render only those modifications that touched the currently selected flow node
The operator presses an Undo button repeatedly until each change is briefly described in a toast, which lets the operator inspect the full pending list before re-staging the changes again
The operator opens the Operate global notifications drawer at the top of the page, where a per-instance section accumulates the staged modifications as the operator stages each one
The operator clicks the Apply Modifications button in the footer of modification mode, and Operate opens a modal where all pending modifications staged on the instance can be reviewed together

d.text: Correct. The docs say that to display pending modifications, the operator clicks Apply Modifications in the footer; a modal then opens where all of the modifications on the instance can be seen and managed before commit.

c.text: Incorrect. The pending modifications summary is opened from the modification footer, not from a global notifications drawer at the top of the Operate page.

a.text: Incorrect. The summary modal lists every pending modification together; there is no documented side panel that scopes the view down to a single selected flow node.

b.text: Incorrect. Undo from the modification footer reverses the latest modification rather than opening the pending summary, and it is not described as a way to enumerate the full pending list.

explanation: Clicking Apply Modifications in the modification-mode footer opens the summary modal. The modal lists every pending modification staged on the instance and lets the operator review and act on them before committing.

scenario: Atlas Logistics runs Camunda 8.8 Operate and an operator has the pending modifications modal open on a single shipment instance covering 75 shipments. The operator wants to delete one specific pending modification from the list, double-check an edited variable in a viewer, and then close the modal without committing yet to keep staging.
question: Which set of actions inside the pending modifications modal supports this review workflow?

options.text: Inside the modal the operator clicks a delete icon next to a modification, opens a JSON or Diff Viewer for an added or edited variable, and clicks a cancel control to close the modal and keep staging

Inside the modal the operator can delete entries only after committing the change set at least once, and the JSON or Diff Viewer is reachable solely through the global Operations side panel of the page

Inside the modal the operator can only apply all pending modifications or discard them all at once, so deleting individual entries or previewing variables before commit is not documented as a supported action
Inside the modal the operator must click a Continue editing button to remove items, while inspection of added or edited variables happens on a completely separate modal opened from the diagram view

a.text: Correct. The docs list four actions in the summary modal: deleting a modification with a Delete Icon, viewing an added or edited variable in a JSON or Diff Viewer, cancelling and closing the modal to continue staging, and applying the modifications to exit.

c.text: Incorrect. The modal supports per-entry deletion and a JSON or Diff Viewer for added or edited variables, not just an all-or-nothing apply or discard control on the staged set.

d.text: Incorrect. There is no Continue editing button documented for removing items; deletion uses a Delete Icon and variable inspection happens inside the same summary modal as the rest of the actions.

b.text: Incorrect. Entries can be deleted before committing, and the JSON or Diff Viewer for added or edited variables is exposed inside the summary modal rather than through the global Operations side panel.

explanation: The pending modifications modal lets the operator delete individual modifications with a Delete Icon, view added or edited variables in a JSON or Diff Viewer, cancel and close to keep staging, or apply the modifications to exit modification mode.

scenario: Iris Fintech runs Camunda 8.8 Operate and the operator opens modification mode on a single process instance worth 7300 dollars of pending settlement. The operator is trying to either add a token to a boundary event or move a token onto a start event, but the modification mode rejects both actions on the diagram for this instance.
question: Which modification actions are documented as non-supported on the elements the operator selected?
